NOTE: This Draft is intended to illustrate the Project Committee’s vision for the guide.
62
63
The document is organized by Objective and Strategy. There are eight Objectives each of which
64
contains four to six strategies. In the printed document (which includes everything prior to Chapter 4),
65
the Objectives are in Chapter 2 (Objective 1) and Chapter 3 (Objective 2-8). The CD will contain all of
66
this material along with Chapter 4 which will have detailed material on all the Strategies contained in
67
the eight Objectives. Additional appendices (as needed) will also be on the CD only.
68
69
The Objectives have been color-coded (each Objective and all its included Strategies are a different
70
color). The colors are also shown in the Table of Contents. The colors are preliminary and conceptual
71
and are specifically designed to make this 90% review draft easier to follow and understand. The final
72
document may not use color in the same way.
73
74
Some of the graphics are meant to be navigational in nature and have links that take you either to the
75
Strategies themselves or to sections within the detailed Chapter 4 write-ups. These are the Objective
76
Graphics and the Roadmap Graphics.
77
78
The “Objective Graphics” are for navigational purposes and will have workable links on the CD to each
79
of the Strategies included in that Objective. Some of the links are operable in this draft.
80
81
The “Roadmap Graphics” graphics are for navigational purposes and will have workable links to the
82
sections within the specific Strategy’s detailed write-up in Chapter 4. Some of the links are operable in
83
this draft.

Foreword: Why this Guide was Written
304
All new buildings and major renovations must fulfill a variety of requirements related to building
305
function, codes and standards, and environmental and community impacts. Among these requirements,
306
indoor air quality (IAQ) is generally addressed through compliance with minimum code requirements
307
based on industry consensus standards such as ASHRAE Standard 62.1. IAQ affects occupant health,
308
comfort and productivity and in some cases even usability; which in turn can have significant economic
309
impacts for building owners and occupants. While owners and building professionals understand that
310
IAQ is important, they may not appreciate the impacts of routine design and construction decisions and
311
often fail to integrate IAQ issues into the design process early enough to optimize design decisions. In
312
addition, they may assume that achieving a high level of IAQ is associated with premium costs and
313
novel or even risky technical solutions. On the other hand, some attempt to provide improved IAQ
314
through measures such as increased outdoor air ventilation rates or specification of lower emitting
315
materials without an integrated approach to IAQ or a sound technical understanding of these measures.
316
At the same time, fundamental IAQ issues such as moisture, construction and commissioning, and
317
outdoor air quality are not adequately addressed, leading to potentially severe IAQ problems.
318
Fortunately, information exists to achieve better IAQ without incurring excessive costs or employing
319
practices that are beyond the current capabilities of the building professions. This document, which is
320
the result of a collaborative effort of six leading organizations in the building industry∗, has been
321
developed to improve IAQ in commercial and institutional buildings by accelerating the adoption of best
322
practices for design, construction and commissioning that have proven successful in other building
323
projects.
324
325
The guide focuses on improving IAQ in new construction and major renovations. It builds on existing
326
knowledge to identify practical design strategies, construction practices and commissioning or quality
327
control processes that can improve IAQ at low or no incremental cost. This focus on improved practice
328
emphasizes eight areas:
329
• Better integration of the professions as early in the design and construction process as possible;
330
• Controlling moisture in building assemblies;
331
• Limiting outdoor contaminant entry;
332
• Controlling moisture and contaminants in ventilation systems;
333
• Limiting contaminants from indoor sources;
334
• Managing contaminants from building equipment and activities,
335
• Providing effective ventilation and air cleaning; and
336
• Employing advanced ventilation approaches where appropriate.
337
338
The guide addresses the commercial and institutional buildings covered by ASHRAE Standard 62.1
339
Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality. While that standard contains minimum requirements
340
intended for adoption or reference by building codes, this guide supports a higher level of performance.
341
It is written primarily for the architects, design engineers, product specifiers and contractors who can
342
apply the recommended practices to their projects. Building owners, developers and other decision
343
makers may also use it to direct the work of these professionals, while facility managers and building
344
operators may use the guide to understand the IAQ implications of existing systems and O&M practices.
345
Other key audiences are the organizations that provide sustainable building rating programs and those
346
∗ American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE), American Institute of Architects
(AIA), Building Owners and Managers Association International (BOMA), U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA),
Sheet Metal and Air Conditioning Contractors’ National Association (SMACNA) and U.S. Green Building Council
(USGBC).
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 11
Return to TOP
who conduct training for building professionals. Finally, building tenants may use the guide to create
347
market demand for higher quality indoor environments.
348
349
350
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 12
Return to TOP
Message to Building Owners
351
Indoor air quality is one of many issues that building owners and developers must address to provide
352
buildings that meet their intended needs. While building occupants do sometimes complain about poor
353
IAQ, it is not always on the top of their list of concerns. So why should you worry about IAQ when you
354
have so much else to worry about?
355
356
There are two basic reasons for building owners and developers to be interested in IAQ. On the positive
357
side, better IAQ leads to more productive and happier occupants. In commercial real estate this is tied
358
directly to return on investment and bottom-line economics, while in schools and institutional buildings
359
it is tied to achievement of learning outcomes and organizational mission. While it is hard to put firm
360
numbers on these benefits, there is increasing evidence of measurable productivity increases and
361
reduced absentee rates in spaces with better IAQ. In considering the economics of IAQ, it is important to
362
note that the salaries of building occupants are the largest cost associated with building operation,
363
dwarfing energy costs by a factor of 50 or even 100. On the negative side, IAQ problems that get out of
364
hand can be quite costly in terms of lost work time, lost use of buildings, expensive building or
365
mechanical system repairs, legal costs and bad publicity. While extreme IAQ problems are rare, they do
366
occur and the consequences can be dramatic. Less extreme problems are more common and can erode
367
occupant productivity, occupancy and/or rent levels and incur costs for smaller legal disputes and repairs.
368
369
This document presents a wealth of practical information on how to design and construct buildings with
370
better IAQ without making large financial investments or using untested technologies. While the guide
371
is full of information on design and construction to control moisture, reduce contaminant entry and
372
provide effective ventilation, probably the most important message for the owner/developer is to get
373
IAQ on the table at the very beginning of the development and design processes. Adding IAQ to the
374
earliest discussions with the architect and the rest of the project team will make it easier and more
375
effective to provide good IAQ at lower or even no added cost.
376
377
378
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 13
Return to TOP
Executive Summary: The Message of this Guide
379
Indoor air quality (IAQ) is one of many factors that determine building functionality and economics.
380
IAQ affects building occupants and their ability to conduct their activities; creates positive or negative
381
impressions on customers, clients and other visitors to a building; and can impact the ability to rent or
382
sell building space. When a building’s IAQ is good, it will be a more desirable place to work, to learn, to
383
conduct business or to rent. When IAQ is poor, the negative outcomes vary from relatively minor
384
unpleasantness to serious outcomes such as occupant health problems, vacant space, extended periods of
385
building closure, major repair costs and expensive legal actions.
386
387
While it is hard to put firm numbers on the benefits of good IAQ, there is increasing evidence of
388
measurable productivity increases and reduced absentee rates in spaces with better IAQ. In considering
389
the economics of IAQ, it is important to note that the salaries of building occupants are the largest
390
building operation cost, dwarfing energy by a factor of 50 or even 100. Small decreases in occupant
391
productivity due to poor IAQ, even a few extra days off per year, will cost more than the money that
392
might be saved by putting off important maintenance activities or by actions to reduce energy for
393
heating and cooling that significantly degrade IAQ.
394
395
What are the IAQ problems in buildings?
396
The information in this guide is based on the IAQ problems that have been occurring in commercial and
397
institutional buildings for several decades and the authors’ experience in investigating, resolving and
398
avoiding these problems. These problems fall into several major categories, which are used to organize
399
the information contained in this guide.
400
401
• Failure to address IAQ during design and construction
402
Many IAQ problems are the result of IAQ not being considered as a key issue at the very beginning
403
of the design process. Too often, designing for IAQ is considered only a matter of complying with
404
the minimum ventilation rates in buildings codes, and the IAQ implications of many other design
405
decisions are not recognized. These decisions are covered in more detail later in the guide but
406
include site selection, building orientation, location of outdoor air intakes, and basic decisions on
407
how the building with be heated, cooled and ventilated. Efforts to achieve high levels of building
408
performance too often neglect IAQ at the beginning of the design process, leading to decisions that
409
may negatively impact IAQ and missed opportunities to include good IAQ among the building
410
performance goals.
411
412
Another key factor that favorably impacts IAQ is a serious commitment to a comprehensive
413
commissioning process that starts in the design phase and continues into occupancy and to design
414
choices that make a building easier to operate and maintain over the long term. Examples of the
415
latter include mechanical room layouts with adequate access to system components for inspection
416
and maintenance and selection of building materials that are easier to clean.
417
418
• Moisture in building assemblies
419
Many notable cases of building IAQ problems have been associated with excessive levels of
420
moisture in building assemblies, particularly in the building envelope. Such situations can lead to
421
mold growth that can be very difficult to fix without major renovation efforts and costs. The
422
moisture problems arise for a variety of reasons, including roof leaks, rain penetration through leaky
423
construction details at windows and other sites, envelope design and construction defects such as
424
low permeability wall coverings in hot and humid climates, and poor building pressure control.
425
These problems are largely avoidable, but require an understanding of the fundamentals of building
426
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 14
Return to TOP
moisture dynamics and attention to detail in envelope design and construction and in mechanical
427
system selection, installation, balancing and operation.
428
429
• Poor outdoor air quality
430
As noted earlier, the traditional means to deal with IAQ is through outdoor air ventilation. Building
431
ventilation rates are generally based on building codes and industry standards, such as ASHRAE
432
Standard 62.1, with the ventilation air provided to dilute contaminants generated by building
433
occupants and their activities and building materials and furnishings. While dilution can be an
434
effective means of indoor contaminant control, it assumes that the outdoor air is cleaner than the
435
indoor air. In many locations and for many contaminants this is not the case, and outdoor air
436
ventilation can actually make IAQ worse. Poor outdoor air quality includes regionally elevated
437
outdoor contaminant levels as well as local sources such as motor vehicle exhaust from nearby
438
roadways and contaminants generated by activities in adjacent buildings. Some programs to
439
encourage higher levels of building performance recommend increasing outdoor air ventilation rates,
440
but such recommendations need to consider the potential impacts of poor outdoor air quality.
441
ASHRAE Standard 62.1 requires the assessment of outdoor air quality in the vicinity of a building
442
and requires outdoor air cleaning under some circumstances. This guide covers outdoor air quality in
443
more detail given the general reliance on outdoor air ventilation for IAQ control.
444
445
• Moisture and dirt in ventilation systems
446
Dirt accumulation in ventilation systems, combined with poor management of water, can lead to
447
biological growth in the airstream and serious IAQ problems. These conditions generally result from
448
inadequate levels of particle filtration, poor filter maintenance, and problems with cooling coil
449
condensate drainage, humidifiers, or control of rain and snow entry. ASHRAE Standard 62.1
450
contains several requirements related to dirt and moisture management in ventilation systems, and
451
this guide addresses the topic in more detail given the seriousness of the problems that can result.
452
453
• Indoor contaminant sources
454
Many IAQ problems are associated with indoor contaminant sources that are unusually strong or
455
otherwise cannot be handled by typical or code-compliant levels of outdoor air ventilation. Many
456
contaminants are released by normal building materials and furnishings, especially when new, and
457
also by materials and substances brought into the building during operation. Unusual, unexpected or
458
atypically high contaminant emissions from indoor sources are associated with many IAQ problems,
459
and this guide speaks to the issue of material selection, cleaning and other indoor source issues.
460
461
• Contaminants from indoor equipment and activities
462
The wide range of occupancies and activities in commercial and institutional buildings involve many
463
different types of equipment and activities, and IAQ problems have resulted from improper
464
equipment operation, inadequate exhaust ventilation and poor choices of materials used in some of
465
these activities. A prime example of the latter is improper selection and use of certain cleaning
466
products.
467
468
• Inadequate ventilation rates
469
While building codes and standards have addressed outdoor air ventilation for decades, many
470
buildings and spaces are poorly ventilated, which increases the likelihood of IAQ problems. There
471
are a variety of reasons for inadequate ventilation rates including lack of compliance of the design
472
with applicable codes and standards, installation or maintenance problems that lead to the design
473
ventilation rate not being achieved in practice, or space use changes that occur without a
474
determination of the need for updated ventilation rates. System level outdoor air intake rates may be
475
adequate, but air distribution problems can lead to certain areas in the building being poorly
476
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 15
Return to TOP
ventilated. While ASHRAE Standard 62.1 covers the determination of design ventilation rates,
477
problems can still result and additional guidance is provided in this document to help avoid these
478
potential problems.
479
480
• Ineffective filtration and air cleaning
481
Filtration and air cleaning are effective means of controlling many indoor air pollutants, particularly
482
those associated with poor outdoor air quality. However, their effectiveness depends on proper
483
selection and installation and timely maintenance during building occupancy. Serious IAQ problems
484
do result from improper selection or maintenance of filtration and air cleaning systems.
485
486
Based on the known causes of IAQ problems discussed above, this guide is organized around the
487
following eight strategies for improving building IAQ:
488
489
1. Manage the Process to Achieve Good IAQ
490
2. Control Moisture in Building Assemblies
491
3. Limit Entry of Outdoor Contaminants
492
4. Control Moisture and Contaminants Related to Mechanical Systems
493
5. Limited Contaminants from Indoor Sources
494
6. Contain and Exhaust Contaminants from Building Equipment and Activities
495
7. Reduce Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation and Air Cleaning
496
8. Apply More Advanced Ventilation Approaches
497
498
This Guide has been written to help building owners, design professionals and others understand the
499
benefits of good IAQ and the risks of common IAQ problems, and to appreciate the design and
500
construction decisions and processes that are available to realize the benefits while managing the risks.
501
502
503
504
505
506
507
508
509
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 16
Return to TOP
Chapter 1 Introduction
510
511
Indoor air quality (IAQ) is one of many factors that determine building functionality and economics.
512
IAQ affects building occupants and their ability to conduct their activities; creates positive or negative
513
impressions on customers, clients and other visitors to a building; and can impact the ability to rent
514
building space. When a building’s IAQ is good, it will be a more desirable place to work, to learn, to
515
conduct business or to rent. When IAQ is poor, the negative outcomes vary from relatively minor
516
unpleasantness to more serious outcomes such as occupant health problems, vacant space, extended
517
periods of building closure, major repair costs and expensive legal actions.
518
519
While controlling indoor pollutant levels and providing adequate ventilation and thermal comfort have
520
motivated the design and use of buildings for centuries, awareness of and concerns about indoor air
521
quality have increased in recent decades. However, in most cases IAQ is still not a high priority design
522
or building management concern compared to function, cost, space, aesthetics, and other attributes such
523
as location and parking. But given the very real benefits of good IAQ, the potentially serious
524
consequences of poor IAQ, and the ability to design, construct and operate buildings with good IAQ
525
using existing knowledge without incurring significant additional costs1,2, building owners, designers
526
and other professionals need a better appreciation of the importance of and means for providing good
527
IAQ in their buildings. This document is intended to provide both an understanding of the critical design
528
and construction issues related to IAQ and specific information on how to address those issues through a
529
number of practical measures.
530
531
532
How to use this guide
533
The guide is organized to help you meet important IAQ objectives. The following brief tour of the
534
document can help you decide where to start.
535
536
Chapter 1: Introduction: How to Improve IAQ in Your Building
537
If you’re not sure what constitutes good IAQ or even why IAQ really matters for building owners and
538
occupants, Chapter 1 is the place to start. It addresses these questions and also provides a brief overview
539
of the critical role of design and construction processes in delivering good indoor air quality, and
540
highlights common causes of IAQ problems in buildings.
541
542
1 In one building, the cost of more than 100 sustainable features including numerous IAQ-related features such as low-
emitting building materials and HVAC systems with numerous IAQ features, accounted for only 2.5 % of the contract cost.
Reference: L. Alevantis; K. Frevert; R. Muller; H. Levin; and A. Sowell. 2002. Sustainable Building Practices in California
State Buildings. In Levin, H., ed., Proceedings of Indoor Air 2002: The 9th International Conference on Indoor Air Quality
and Climate. Monterey, California, June 30-July 5, 2002. Vol. 3 pp. 666-671.
2 In 2001, the State of California selected a bidder for its open office systems furniture contract. The contract included
detailed testing and reporting criteria for VOCs. The bid price was at a comparable cost to what the State was paying for the
previous contract without any VOC or environmental requirements. In addition, at a 1.5 M ft2 California State Office
building complex, low-emitting products used in large quantities were provided at no additional first cost. Reference: L.E.
Alevantis; and H. Levin.2002. "California’s Sustainable Building Efforts in State Government: Indoor Air Quality
Specifications for Open Office Systems Furniture and Building Materials”. Role of Human Exposure Assessment in Urban
Air Quality Management. EUR 21052. Kryzanowski M., Jantunen M., Bartonova A., Oglesby L., Kephalopoulos S., and
Kotzias D. eds. Italy: European Commission, pp. 129-136.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 17
Return to TOP
543
Chapter 2: Integrated Design and Construction
544
Processes for Good IAQ
545
Achieving good IAQ depends at least as much on
546
the design and construction processes employed
547
by the project team as on any particular technical
548
measure. The great majority of IAQ problems
549
are due not to a lack of advanced technologies
550
but to simple failure to consider IAQ adequately
551
during design and construction.
552
553
Chapter 2 outlines key practices that project
554
managers and design team leaders can adopt to
555
enable consistent delivery of quality indoor
556
environments at little or no incremental cost and
557
to avoid IAQ nightmares. These practices start
558
with project planning and conceptual and
559
schematic design, and continue through the entire
560
design process, the construction phase and
561
commissioning.
562
563
564
565
566
567
568
Chapter 3: Design, Construction and Commissioning for IAQ
569
Chapter 3 identifies and briefly describes seven key technical Objectives that must be met to deliver
570
good IAQ:
571
572
○ Control Moisture in Building Assemblies
573
○ Limit Entry of Outdoor Contaminants
574
○ Control Moisture and Contaminants Related to Mechanical Systems
575
○ Limited Contaminants from Indoor Sources
576
○ Contain and Exhaust Contaminants from Building Equipment and Activities
577
○ Reduce Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation and Air Cleaning
578
○ Apply More Advanced Ventilation Approaches
579
580
The Overview and Graphic for each Objective provide an understanding of why each Objective is
581
important for good IAQ. In the electronic document, the Graphic contains links to Strategies that can be
582
employed achieve this Objective.
583
584
585

Chapter 4: Detailed Design, Construction and Commissioning Guidance (Electronic Only)
607
Chapter 4 provides more detailed information to use when you’re ready to work on a specific project.
608
609
Whether you’ve already been burned by moisture problems and want guidance on how to avoid them,
610
are doing your first brownfield project with the potential for vapor intrusion, have been asked to design a
611
green building with low-emitting materials, want to do a better job of controlling outdoor pollutants in
612
an urban location or want to reduce the energy costs due to ventilation, Chapter 4 will help.
613
614
615
616
617
618
What is Good IAQ Anyway?
619
This document refers to the desire to move beyond current practice to provide “Good IAQ.” Given that
620
IAQ involves many contaminants and physical features of the indoor environment, e.g. temperature and
621
relative humidity, it is challenging to define IAQ let alone to determine whether it is good, bad or simply
622
adequate. Nevertheless, this guide is about good IAQ, which begs the question, ‘what do we mean by
623
good IAQ anyway?’ In the context of this guide, good IAQ is the result of diligent compliance with both
624
the letter and intent of ASHRAE Standard 62.1, technically sound and well-executed efforts to meet or
625
exceed these minimum requirements, and the application of good practice in building and system design,
626
construction, commissioning, operation and maintenance throughout the life of a building. The intent of
627
good IAQ is to provide air in which there are no known contaminants at concentrations likely to be
628
harmful, no conditions that are likely to be associated with potentially harmful contaminant levels or
629
with occupant health or comfort complaints, and with which virtually no occupants express
630
dissatisfaction.
631
632
Using the Detailed Guidance on the CD
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 21
Return to TOP
Except under some extreme conditions, it is difficult, if not impossible, to identify indoor air quality as
633
poor, good or exceptional in quantitative terms based solely on contaminant concentrations. The limits
634
of existing knowledge regarding health and comfort impacts of specific contaminants and contaminant
635
mixtures in non-industrial environments and the variations in human susceptibility are two primary
636
reasons for this difficulty. It is reasonable to assume that adherence to today’s minimum standards, i.e.,
637
ASHRAE Standard 62.1, and consistency with good engineering and O&M practice will result in
638
acceptable IAQ. However, current practice is not always consistent with minimum standards or good
639
practice, and many building owners and practitioners desire to achieve better than acceptable IAQ.
640
These are the two primary motivations for the development of this guide.
641
642
Why IAQ Matters
643
IAQ directly affects occupant health, comfort and productivity. Well-established, serious health impacts
644
resulting from poor IAQ include Legionnaire’s Disease, heart disease and lung cancer from secondhand
645
smoke, and carbon monoxide poisoning. More widespread health impacts include increased allergy and
646
asthma from exposure to indoor pollutants (particularly those associated with building dampness and
647
mold), colds and other infectious diseases that are transmitted through the air, and “sick building
648
syndrome” symptoms due to elevated indoor pollutant levels as well as other indoor environmental
649
conditions. These more widespread impacts have the potential to affect large numbers of building
650
occupants and are associated with significant costs due to healthcare expenses, sick leave and lost
651
productivity. Fisk (2000)3 and Mendell at al (2002) 4 estimated that the potential reductions in health
652
costs, reduced absenteeism and improvements in work performance from providing better IAQ in non-
653
industrial workplaces in the U.S. were worth tens of billions of dollars annually. More detailed analyses
654
by Fisk and Seppanan (2007)5 and Wargocki et al. (2006)6 indicate that increases in ventilation rates and
655
better control of temperatures have the potential to improve office and school work performance by
656
several percentage points. Much of the related research is reviewed in a recently released web site
657
at http://eetd.lbl.gov/ied/sfrb/sfrb.html. Despite these significant impacts, many building design and
658
construction decisions are made without an understanding of the potentially serious consequences of
659
poor IAQ and without benefit of the well-established body of knowledge on how to do better.
660
661
The costs of poor IAQ can be striking. There have been many lawsuits associated with IAQ problems,
662
though most are settled with no financial details released. However, some of these cases have involved
663
legal fees and settlements exceeding $10 million. A conservative estimate puts the lower bound of
664
litigation costs during the early 2000s well over $500 million annually.7 A few notable cases in the
665
public record are highlighted below, but there have been and continue to be many others:
666
• In 1995, Polk County Florida, recovered $47.8 million in settlements against various companies
667
involved in the construction of the county courthouse (including $35 million from the general
668
contractors' insurer), due to moisture and mold problems associated with a range of building
669
envelope problems. The original construction cost for the building was $35 million, but $45
670
million was spent to replace the entire building envelope, clean up the mold and relocate the
671
court system.
672
3 Fisk, W.J. (2000) Health and productivity gains from better indoor environments and their relationship with building energy
efficiency. Annual Review of Energy and the Environment 25(1): 537-566.
4 Mendell et al, 2002. Improving the Health of Workers in Indoor Environments: Priority Research Needs for a National
Occupational Research Agenda Am J Public Health. 2002;92:1430–1440)
5 Fisk WJ and Seppanen O (2007) Providing better indoor environmental quality brings economic benefits. Proceedings of
REHVA World Congress - CLIMA 2007 WellBeing Indoors. Paper A01. Published by FINVAC, Helsinki.
6 Wargocki, P., et al., "Indoor climate and productivity in offices: how to integrate productivity in life cycle analysis of
building services", in REHVA Guidebook No. 6. Federation of European Heating and Air Conditioning Associations. 2006b:
Brussels, Belgium.\
7 Levin, H. 2005. National Expenditures for IAQ Problem Prevention or Mitigation. LBNL Report 58694.
http://eetd.lbl.gov/ied/pdf/LBNL-58694.pdf
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 22
Return to TOP
• Occupants of a courthouse in Suffolk County Massachusetts received a $3 million settlement in
673
1999 following a series of IAQ problems associated with a combination of inadequate ventilation
674
and fumes from a waterproofing material applied to the occupied building.
675
676
While the highlighted cases occurred in government buildings, there are numerous IAQ problems in
677
private sector buildings that tend to be settled out of court and are therefore not in the public record.
678
Recent and current cases involve a range of problems and potentially large dollar values, including the
679
following:
680
• Depressurized buildings in warm humid climates leading to condensation, exacerbated by low
681
permeability indoor wall coverings.
682
• Water leakage through the building envelope caused by the lack of back-up drainage planes,
683
inadequately designed and installed window flashings, and leaky penetrations through the
684
envelope associated with complex architecture.
685
• Water leakage into wall cavities and building risers associated with failure of plumbing lines and
686
assemblies.
687
• Extensive mold growth in air handling units and HVAC ducts.
688
• Water and mold damage to finishing and construction materials stored outdoors without
689
protection from rain and snow.
690
• Defective air barriers or large openings in envelope construction resulting in massive amounts of
691
unconditioned air entering air-conditioned buildings.
692
693
On the other hand, it is becoming clear that good IAQ can more than pay for itself by improving
694
occupant performance,8 reducing symptoms and sick leave, and making space more attractive to tenants.
695
In terms of productivity, occupant salaries are by far the largest cost associated with building operation,
696
dwarfing energy consumption by as much as a factor of 100. Improved student performance in schools
697
has long-term economic implications as well. Research shows that good IAQ improves both worker
698
productivity and student performance. For example:
699
700
• A laboratory study showed that performance of office tasks increased by just over 1 % for each 10 %
701
decrease in the percentage of people who were dissatisfied with the air quality in the test space.9
702
• A study of the performance of schoolwork by children found significant increases in the rate at
703
which learning tasks were performed as a result of IAQ improvements due to increased outdoor air
704
supply rates. 10
705
706
Clearly the benefits of good IAQ can be significant. These are recognized as part of today’s focus on
707
healthy and sustainable buildings. In addition, insurance companies are starting to recognize these
708
benefits by offering reductions in premiums when certain IAQ improvements are implemented.
709
710
Importance of the Design and Construction Process
711
While there is plenty of information and experience to achieve good IAQ in commercial and
712
institutional buildings, it doesn’t happen automatically. It takes a level of awareness and commitment
713
that isn’t typical in most projects, including an effort to make IAQ part of the design at the very
714
beginning of the project. There are two primary reasons to include IAQ considerations in the earliest
715
stages of project planning: avoiding problems that occur when IAQ is treated as an afterthought, when it
716
8 Seppanen, O.A., Fisk, W.J. 2006. Some Quantitative Relations between Indoor Environmental Quality and Work
Performance or Health. HVAC&R Research, 12(4): 957-973.
9 Wargocki, P., et al. 2000. Pollution Source Control and Ventilation Improve Health, Comfort and Productivity. Proceedings
of Cold Climate HVAC ‘2000. Sapporo. 445-450.
10 Wargocki, P., Wyon, D.P. 2007. The Effects of Outdoor Air Supply Rate and Supply Air Filter Condition in Classrooms on
the Performance of Schoolwork by Children (RP-1257). HVAC&R Research, 13(2): 165-191.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 23
Return to TOP
is often too late to do much about it, and allowing the consideration of some alternative design concepts
717
that involve decisions at the beginning.
718
719
Incorporating IAQ at the very beginning of the conceptual design of a building gets a number of key
720
issues before the design team, enabling them to make informed decisions that will affect the project
721
through the construction and occupancy phases. These issues and decisions are addressed in more detail
722
in this document but include the owner’s expectations for IAQ in the building (minimum or higher
723
performance), potential contaminant sources in nearby buildings and facilities, the types of activities
724
expected to occur in the building (and the contaminants that might be associated with these activities),
725
the characteristics of the occupants (e.g. age, health status or short term visitors that may have very
726
different expectations than occupants who will remain in the building for a long time), and the approach
727
to be used to heat, cool and ventilate the building. If these considerations are not addressed until after the
728
building layout is defined, the ventilation system type selected and the ventilation rate design
729
calculations are complete, it will be difficult if not impossible to accommodate the particular needs of
730
the building, its owner and its occupants.
731
732
Many design decisions that can lead to poor IAQ are made in the early phase of the design that are
733
difficult to correct later on and which can be avoided if IAQ is put on the table as a key design issue at
734
the start. These include inadequate space for mechanical equipment, limiting access for inspection and
735
maintenance; and selection of interior finishes that can lead to high levels of VOC emissions or lead to
736
moisture problems in the building envelope.
737
738
Making IAQ part of the initial discussion of design goals, on par with building function, image and
739
energy use, allows consideration of high-performance design concepts that can support good IAQ,
740
energy-efficiency and other important design goals. Examples include mechanical systems that separate
741
outdoor air ventilation from space conditioning, the application of natural ventilation, high efficiency air
742
cleaning in conjunction with lowered ventilation rates, and the selection of low-emitting materials based
743
on sound technical consideration of the options.
744
745
Making a commitment to good IAQ at the beginning of a project and maintaining that focus through
746
design, construction, commissioning will result in a building that is more successful in meeting its
747
design goals and achieving the desired level of performance throughout its life.
748
749
What are the IAQ problems in buildings?
750
The information in this guide is based on the IAQ problems that have been occurring in commercial and
751
institutional buildings for several decades and the authors’ experience in investigating, resolving and
752
avoiding these problems. These problems fall into several major categories, which are used to organize
753
the information contained in this guide.
754
755
• Failure to address IAQ during design and construction
756
Many IAQ problems are the result of IAQ not being considered as a key issue at the very beginning
757
of the design process. Too often, designing for IAQ is considered only a matter of complying with
758
the minimum ventilation rates in buildings codes, and the IAQ implications of many other design
759
decisions are not recognized. These decisions are covered in more detail later in the guide but
760
include site selection, building orientation, location of outdoor air intakes, and basic decisions on
761
how the building with be heated, cooled and ventilated. Efforts to achieve high levels of building
762
performance too often neglect IAQ at the beginning of the design process, leading to decisions that
763
may negatively impact IAQ and missed opportunities to include good IAQ among the building
764
performance goals.
765
766
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 24
Return to TOP
Another key factor that favorably impacts IAQ is a serious commitment to a comprehensive
767
commissioning process that starts in the design phase and continues well into occupancy and design
768
choices that make a building easier to operate and maintain. Examples of the latter include
769
mechanical room layouts with adequate access to system components for inspection and
770
maintenance and the selection of building materials that are easier to clean.
771
772
• Moisture in building assemblies
773
There have been many notable cases of building IAQ problems associated with excessive levels of
774
moisture in building assemblies, particularly in the building envelope. Such situations can lead to
775
mold growth that can be very difficult to fix without major renovation efforts and costs. The
776
moisture problems arise for a variety of reasons, including roof leaks, rain penetration through leaky
777
windows and other sites, envelope design and construction defects such as low permeability wall
778
coverings in hot and humid climates, and poor building pressure control. These problems are largely
779
avoidable, but require an understanding of the fundamentals of building moisture and attention to
780
detail in envelope design and construction and in mechanical system selection, installation and
781
operation.
782
783
• Poor outdoor air quality
784
As noted earlier, the traditional means to deal with IAQ is through outdoor air ventilation. Building
785
ventilation rates are generally based on building codes and industry standards, such as ASHRAE
786
Standard 62.1, with the ventilation air provided to dilute contaminants generated by building
787
occupants and their activities. While dilution can be an effective means of indoor contaminant
788
control, it assumes that the outdoor air is cleaner than the indoor air. However, in many locations and
789
for many contaminants, this is not the case and outdoor air ventilation can actually make IAQ worse.
790
Poor outdoor air quality include regional issues of elevated outdoor contaminant levels as well as
791
local sources such as motor vehicle exhaust from nearby roadways and contaminants generated by
792
activities in adjacent buildings. Some programs to encourage higher levels of building performance
793
recommend increasing outdoor air ventilation rates, but such recommendations need to consider the
794
potential impacts of poor outdoor air quality. ASHRAE Standard 62.1 requires the assessment of
795
outdoor air quality in the vicinity of a building and requires outdoor air cleaning under some
796
circumstances. This guide covers outdoor air quality in far more detail given the general reliance on
797
outdoor air ventilation for IAQ control.
798
799
• Moisture and dirt in ventilation systems
800
Dirt accumulation in ventilation systems, combined with poor management of water, can lead to the
801
biological growth in the airstream and serious IAQ problems. These conditions generally result from
802
inadequate levels of particle filtration, poor filter maintenance, and problems with cooling coil
803
condensate drainage. ASHRAE Standard 62.1 contains several requirements related to dirt and
804
moisture management in ventilation systems, and this guide addresses the topic in more detail given
805
the seriousness of the problems that can result.
806
807
• Indoor contaminant sources
808
Many IAQ problems are associated with indoor contaminant sources that are unusually strong or
809
otherwise cannot be handled by typical or code-compliant levels of outdoor air ventilation. Many
810
contaminants are released by normal building materials and furnishings, especially when new, and
811
also by materials and substances brought into the building during operation. Unusual, unexpected or
812
atypically high contaminant emissions from indoor sources are associated with many IAQ problems,
813
and this guide speaks to the issue of material selection, cleaning and other indoor source issues.
814
815
• Contaminants from indoor equipment and activities
816
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 25
Return to TOP
The wide range of occupancies and activities in commercial and institutional buildings involve many
817
different types of equipment and activities, and IAQ problems have resulted from improper
818
equipment operation, inadequate exhaust ventilation and poor choices of materials used in some of
819
these activities. A prime example of the latter is improper selection and use of certain cleaning
820
products.
821
822
• Inadequate ventilation rates
823
While building codes and standards have addressed outdoor air ventilation for decades, many
824
buildings and spaces are poorly ventilate, which increases the likelihood of IAQ problems. There are
825
a variety of reasons for inadequate ventilation rates including lack of compliance of the systems
826
design with applicable codes and standards, installation or maintenance problems that lead to the
827
design ventilation rate not being achieved in practice, or space use changes that occur without a
828
determination of the need for updated ventilation rates. System level outdoor air intake rates may be
829
adequate, but air distribution problems can lead to certain areas in the building being poorly
830
ventilated. While ASHRAE Standard 62.1 covers the determination of design ventilation rates,
831
problems can still result and additional guidance is provided in this document to help avoid these
832
potential problems.
833
834
• Ineffective filtration and air cleaning
835
Filtration and air cleaning are effective means of controlling many indoor air pollutants, particularly
836
those associated with poor outdoor air quality. However, their effectiveness depends on proper
837
selection and installation and timely maintenance during building occupancy. Serious IAQ problems
838
do result from improper selection or maintenance of filtration and air cleaning systems.
839
840
Based on the known causes of the IAQ problems discussed above, this guide is organized around eight
841
strategies for improving building IAQ and contains a wealth of information on how to improve IAQ
842
through their implementation.
843
844
845
846
847
848
849
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 26
Return to TOP
Chapter 2 Integrated Design and Construction Process for Good IAQ
850
851
NOTE: The intention of this section is to focus on the overall process issues that are particularly
852
relevant to achieving good IAQ. Its ultimate content will need to reflect the rest of the document and be
853
linked to sections in it. The reviewer should identify errors and omissions, focus on general content,
854
focus, and style. It might not be the most productive use of time to review it in detail or make detailed
855
editorial suggestions.
856
857
Overview
858
The indoor environment results from the dynamic interactions between buildings, their surrounding
859
environments, and their occupants and contents. The project design team leader must see the whole
860
picture to integrate the activities of all the team members in order to achieve a result that effectively
861
addresses all the issues.
862
863
Every project architect knows that there are many trade-offs among the various alternative design
864
solutions and the optimization of each of the many aspects of building design. The key elements
865
affecting IAQ are the location and site, the envelope, the interior material selection, the ventilation and
866
air cleaning schemes, and the operability and maintainability of the completed building. Besides
867
controlling intrusion of air pollutants from outdoors, the envelope must also exclude moisture
868
penetration. But the optimum envelope design also addresses thermal performance of the building and
869
illumination of the interior, while the optimal ventilation design addresses thermal performance and
870
energy consumption.
871
872
There are some very good reasons to focus on IAQ as part of the design process
873
1. Provide a higher quality building with an indoor environment that will enable its owners and/or
874
tenants to achieve their goals and the purposes for which the building was constructed.
875
2. Support and sustain the health and activities of the occupants.
876
3. Reduce the risks of IAQ problems resulting in costly vacancies or evacuations, repairs, or major
877
materials and equipment replacement.
878
879
Major factors that can or should be addressed in design that will contribute to good IAQ
880
1. Source Control – Identify and control (eliminate, minimize, or mitigate) outdoor and indoor
881
sources of contaminants and moisture.
882
• Limit Contaminants from Indoor Sources
883
• Limit Entry of Outdoor Contaminants
884
• Control Moisture in Building Assemblies
885
886
2. Adequate Ventilation and Exhaust – Ensure adequate ventilation for the expected occupancy
887
and use of the building and the anticipated pollutant generating activities. Use exhaust to remove
888
contaminants before they can mix widely with building air.
889
• Reduce Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation and Air Cleaning
890
• Contain and Exhaust Contaminants from Building Equipment and Activities
891
• Control Moisture and Contaminants related to Mechanical Systems
892
• Apply More Advanced Ventilation Approaches
893
894
3. Ease of Operation and Maintenance – Provide for a building that can be easily operated,
895
cleaned and maintained.
896
897
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 27
Return to TOP
Relationship of Design to IAQ in the Occupied Building
898
Designers do not control what occurs in a building after it is completed and occupied. However,
899
designers can greatly enhance the potential for good IAQ over the long term by achieving the three
900
overall goals above and by designing for:
901
• Flexible operation, adaptability to future changes in use, equipment
902
• Good access to equipment, systems, and their controls
903
• Ease of maintenance with non-polluting materials..
904
905
Late Lessons Learned
906
Thirty years ago, very few designers considered IAQ at all. During the hasty rush to conserve energy in
907
the 1970s, efforts were made to tighten buildings and reduce outdoor air ventilation. But during the prior
908
two or three decades, a multitude of new materials were introduced and replaced materials that had been
909
tested and used over many decades if not centuries. Gypsum wallboard, pressed wood products, carpets,
910
vinyl flooring and wall coverings, fiberglass insulation and ceiling tiles, and a host of other building
911
materials were introduced and came to dominate modern buildings. Meanwhile, building occupants also
912
acquired new, modern furnishings and consumer products whose contents include abundant plastics, fire
913
retardants, pesticides, particle board, and other household chemicals. The combination of reduced
914
ventilation and the products of normal human metabolism plus emissions from these new materials and
915
products resulted in unprecedented concentrations of many pollutants that had not been present or
916
problematic previously.
917
918
There are several other very significant trends that contribute to IAQ problems. These include:
919
1. Increased population in hot-humid areas and increased use of air conditioning, with attendant
920
problems of moisture and mold in building cavities and mechanical systems.
921
2. Increased populations in cold, dry areas.
922
3. Owner/ occupants expectations, requiring tight temperature control to increase productively,
923
sales of consumable products and activities etc e.g. the Savannah open ventilation concept is not
924
deemed acceptable mainly for commercial reasons
925
4. Increased population in urban areas, and increased IAQ problems due to poor outdoor air quality
926
and contaminated sites.
927
5. More complex HVAC systems and controls, which make it possible to better control the
928
environment with lower energy use but also offer more ways to screw up.
929
930
Experience in buildings with IAQ problems has been a major source of our present understanding of
931
what must be done in design, construction, operation, and use of a building. Many of the causes of IAQ
932
problems recur frequently and this fact should encourage improved design practices to achieve good
933
IAQ. The most commonly identified causes of IAQ problems are moisture, insufficient ventilation, and
934
introduction of pollutants from building materials and other modern products of convenience, pleasure,
935
or even personal hygiene. Outdoor air pollutants brought in by ventilation systems or entering by
936
infiltration through small cracks or even large openings are also associated with many indoor pollution
937
problems.
938
939
In terms of design process failures, the failure to integrate the work and the designs of the various
940
members of the design and the construction team often gives rise to IAQ problems as well as many
941
others. Simply by considering IAQ throughout the design process and addressing IAQ issues
942
consistently and effectively, good indoor air quality can become a routine part of good design practice.
943
If anything, defensive design practice with respect to IAQ would reduce the number of IAQ-related
944
claims and lawsuits that design professionals have faced during the past 25 years.
945
946
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 28
Return to TOP
Since the late 1970s, large numbers of buildings have been plagued by indoor air quality problems.
947
Researchers and investigators have documented the causes of many of the problems and a review of
948
their findings is valuable. These findings are summarized in the Table 2.1 (below).
949
950
When IAQ consultants and experts investigate problems, somewhere in excess of 80% of them are
951
identified as having been easily avoided by a small amount of forethought and attention. A significant
952
fraction of these can be addressed in the design process, but that requires a continual focus on IAQ
953
throughout.
954
955
Table 2.1 – Risk factors and their role in causing occupant symptoms and illness
956

Secondary Risk Factors

Air exchange rate

Air distribution
Exhaust
Reduced dilution of
contaminants
Increased exposures to chemical,
physical, and biological
contaminants

Furnishings

irritants, toxins

(elevated)

957
When IAQ consultants and experts investigate problems, a very large fraction of them are identified as
958
having been easily avoided by a small amount of forethought and attention. A significant fraction of
959
these can be addressed in the design process, but that requires a continual focus on IAQ throughout
960
design. This is best achieved in a fully integrated design process in which the design team leader (e.g.,
961
Project Architect) establishes IAQ as a high priority and requires all team members to communicate
962
their concerns. Although many IAQ problems manifest during occupancy, these can also be prevented
963
or minimized by thoughtful design and careful attention to construction and operation.
964
965
Research has consistent found higher rates of SBS in mechanically-ventilated buildings than in naturally
966
ventilated ones, and higher in buildings with air-conditioning than in those without. It is not clear why
967
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 29
Return to TOP
this is the case since ventilation rates are generally higher in mechanically-ventilated buildings and
968
outdoor air is filtered. The suggested explanation is that there are common but avoidable problems in
969
mechanically-ventilated and air-conditioned buildings. More careful attention to the design details and
970
in their implementation in construction and operation can take advantage of the potential benefits of
971
mechanical ventilation where it is most appropriate or necessary.
972
973
Researchers also consistently reported finding generally lower symptom prevalence in buildings where
974
occupants are able to open the windows. This is consistent with the views of many experts that giving
975
occupants some measure of control over their environment will increase their satisfaction with the
976
environment and decrease the symptom reporting rate. This control can apply to ventilation, thermal
977
conditions, illumination, and even the acoustic environment.
978
979
SIDEBAR:
980
Mark Mendell and his colleagues at LBL examined data from a survey of 100 from a
981
stratified sample of randomly selected office buildings in the U.S. The reported the
982
following:
983
“Among factors of HVAC design or configuration:
984
985
Outdoor air intakes less than 60 m above the ground were associated with substantially
986
increased odds of most symptoms assessed (ORs up to 2.7\*).
987
988
Among factors of HVAC condition, maintenance, or operation: the presence of
989
humidification systems in good condition was associated with reduction in skin
990
symptoms (OR=0.5), whereas the presence of humidification systems in poor condition
991
was associated with increases in upper respiratory symptoms, eye symptoms,
992
fatigue/difficulty concentrating, and skin symptoms (ORs=1.5 to 1.7).
993
994
Lack of regularly scheduled inspections for HVAC components was associated with
995
increased eye and upper respiratory symptoms (ORs=2.2, 1.6).
996
997
Less frequent cleaning of cooling coils or condensate drain pans was associated with
998
increased headache and eye symptoms (ORs=1.5, 1.5).
999
1000
Ventilation for 10-12 hours per day (relative to more) was associated with a general
1001
slight increase in symptoms, especially fatigue/difficulty concentrating (OR=1.3).
1002
Several specific associations opposite of predicted were found, such as with cleanliness
1003
of air handler components and operational condition of air handling components.”
1004
1005
\*Note: OR=Odds Ratio, the relative increase in risk relative to the baseline. For
1006
example, an odds ratio of 2.5 would mean that there is a 2-1/2 times higher probability
1007
of the symptom or illness.
1008
1009
Having learned from past mistakes, the future promises better air quality, more satisfied clients, fewer
1010
callbacks and lawsuits, and more success for design and construction professionals.
1011
1012
Major Elements of Design and Relevant IAQ Considerations
1013
While design is fundamentally an iterative process where all aspects are considered in increasing detail
1014
as the design process evolves, there are some key considerations to achieve good IAQ that are associated
1015
with specific phases of the process and elements of the design. The major elements of design most
1016
relevant to IAQ and the associated considerations are shown in Table 2.2.
1017
