IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 31
Return to TOP
1020
Best Practices for Good “IAQ by Design”
1021
This section describes the top 10 practices to ensure good IAQ (and/or reduce the risk of IAQ problems)
1022
in a wide range of building types and project scales.
1023
1024
1. Include IAQ in Project Planning
1025
There are two major aspects to project planning that are critical to achieving good IAQ: the project
1026
goals and the project schedule, as described below.
1027
1028
a. Plan for adequate time for project completion, flush out, and full commissioning.
1029
Projects are often rushed toward the end of the construction process leading to compromises
1030
in terms of completing the punch list prior to occupancy. It is also common for planned or
1031
intended flush-out before occupancy to be shortened or eliminated altogether due to the need
1032
for building occupancy at a given date. Therefore, it is important to provide adequate time in
1033
the project schedule and, perhaps, to include incentives for timely completion, penalties for
1034
late completion, and alternatives to occupancy in an incomplete building.
1035
1036
b. Establish project IAQ Goals along with other design goals and program requirements.
1037
Project IAQ goals and standards or guidelines should be established and adopted as part of
1038
the building program and schematic design phase. Absent such goals, it is likely that IAQ
1039
issues will be ignored or only casually addressed during the design and construction process.
1040
A clear set of goals and standards allow everyone to work off the same assumptions and
1041
provide the basis for evaluating the success of the process.
1042
1043
1044
2. Address IAQ design considerations throughout the design and construction process.
1045
More often than not, IAQ problems are the result of the simple fact that obvious sources of potential
1046
problems were not considered or ignored during design. Examples abound and once recognized,
1047
become obvious: locating ventilation system outdoor air intakes near obvious sources of pollution
1048
(trash storage or dumpsters; passenger or freight loading areas); building on contaminated or wet
1049
land (landfills, industrial or agricultural sites, marshes, high water table, or flood planes; not
1050
coordinating different specialty consultants or contractors; inadequate documentation of building
1051
design intent or operational procedures, etc. When the IAQ implications of design decisions are an
1052
integral part of the process and all members of the design team are tasked with identifying and
1053
responding to them, the likelihood of these common errors and others like them is drastically
1054
reduced. (See additional discussion of Conceptual Design later in this chapter [or in a separate
1055
section].)
1056
1057
This is as important at the very beginning of the process as it is just before occupancy. Decisions
1058
made early on typically impose constraints on decisions made later, so failing to consider the IAQ
1059
implications of these early decisions can limit the ability of all members of the design team to
1060
provide the best solutions. At the end of the process, before occupancy, the cleaning and preparation
1061
of the building for occupancy is often conducted under severe time constraints resulting in
1062
compromises on indoor air quality. Sufficient time for TAB and commissioning as well as for flush
1063
out of the building and proper cleaning are all required to achieve good indoor quality.
1064
1065
3. Integrated design: Facilitate communication among design disciplines and consultants and
1066
coordinate their mutually-important interactions to achieve their separate goals.
1067
“Integrated design” has become one of the buzzwords of the decade. Everybody uses the term,
1068
everybody says it’s necessary and wonderful, and the implication is that it isn’t being done or done
1069
well enough. While the latter is often true, the former is never true. It is always done: building
1070
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 32
Return to TOP
designs can’t be completed and constructed if the design is not integrated. The question is how and
1071
how well it is integrated. The issue is that there may be solutions to separate parts of the design,
1072
separate aspects of the building that don’t work well together or that even conflict.
1073
1074
Why Integrated Design is Unavoidable
1075
It is impossible to separate the design solutions that address ventilation and those that address climate,
1076
weather, and outdoor air quality. Thermal conditions indoors affect the quality of the indoor air, and
1077
ventilation with outdoor air almost always impacts indoor thermal conditions. The building envelope
1078
will strongly affect thermal performance of the building and local thermal comfort for those who are
1079
near exterior walls, windows, or doors. Cold exterior wall surfaces not only affect occupants but also
1080
affect the potential for condensation and problems related to moisture and mold on or in the walls.
1081
Warm or hot exterior walls can result in significantly elevated emission rates for chemicals such as
1082
formaldehyde that might be components of insulation or pressed wood products in the walls.
1083
1084
Occupants tend to dress according to outdoor climate conditions, so thermal comfort design goals and
1085
assumptions must be made with respect to the seasons of the year not only in terms of the thermal gains
1086
and losses through the envelope and the requirements for conditioning ventilation air, but they must also
1087
consider the conditions that will be comfortable given occupants’ clothing.
1088
1089
Illumination provided through exterior windows can provide illumination and higher perceives occupant
1090
comfort and well being at no cost but can also be accompanied by thermal gains or losses that must be
1091
addressed by thermal conditioning system or by passive/ active control such manual/ automatic shading
1092
devices. Electrical lighting is always accompanied by some heat gain which may not be undesirable in
1093
cold weather conditions even though it may not be the most effective or efficient way to provide thermal
1094
conditioning of the occupied space. But in a cooling dominated or mixed climate, the heat gain from
1095
electrical illumination sources is accompanied by an energy penalty for eliminating or counteracting the
1096
waste heat from light sources.
1097
1098
Many more examples can be cited regarding the interactions between ventilation, indoor and outdoor air
1099
quality, thermal conditions, climate, weather, illumination, and acoustics. It is simply impossible to
1100
achieve an energy efficient, comfortable, and healthy building without addressing all these issues.
1101
Addressing them separately is often fraught with conflicting or counter-productive solutions to the
1102
separate problems. Addressing them as an integrated design team is the most effective way to optimize
1103
the economic and environmental performance of the completed building.
1104
1105
Doing Integrated Design
1106
An effective way to accomplish a well-integrated design is to start the project design with a gathering of
1107
all the important design team members discussing potential solutions and the advantages and
1108
disadvantages of each from the perspective of each member’s design discipline or specialty.
1109
Brainstorming design solutions from the beginning as a team will allow each team member to
1110
understand more about the concerns of the others, will provide personal knowledge of all the individuals
1111
on the team, and will the groundwork for communication and collaboration throughout the process. The
1112
project architect can then call or even expect each member to interact as necessary with other team
1113
members when problems or solutions arise that require resolution of competing interests and specialties.
1114
1115
The problem of integrating the separate aspects of a building design isn’t particularly new. Up to the
1116
1900’s (prior to the advent of building specialist) architects/ master builders were trained engineers and
1117
their designs incorporated both architectural notions as well as engineering fundamental e.g. Roman
1118
Baths with in-floor heating, St Paul Cathedral with its thermal mass/ volume assisting the buildings
1119
natural/ forced ventilation systems etc. Reyner Banham has documented this evolution nicely in his
1120
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 33
Return to TOP
book, The Architecture of the Well-tempered Environment.11 Richard Rush, formerly senior editor of the
1121
now defunct journal, Progressive Architecture,12 edited a book on integrated design in the mid-
1122
1980s. .Published by the AIA where Rush worked while preparing the book, The Building Systems
1123
Integration Handbook was one of a long series of publications advocating integrated design.
1124
1125
Because the forces that combine to produce good or poor indoor air quality are so many, so diverse, and
1126
so often the responsibilities of a wide range of design team members, integration of the design is
1127
particular important to achieve good IAQ. Without successfully integrated ventilation system design
1128
with the design of the building envelope, illumination, acoustic control, and many other fundamental
1129
aspects of a building, IAQ problems are more likely to occur. In contrast, when the design of all aspects
1130
that affect indoor environmental quality are well integrated, a more economical, energy-efficient,
1131
comfortable, and healthy building is the result.
1132
1133
Avoiding miscommunication or facilitating communication where it may not normally occur among
1134
members of the design and the construction teams are the most critical steps toward achieving good
1135
indoor air quality. The interests of the space planners often include optimizing usable space, leading to
1136
insufficient consideration of supporting services necessary to good building operation and maintenance.
1137
This can lead to insufficient space allocation for mechanical equipment resulting in compromises on the
1138
equipment quality or performance. Whether it is supply or return air ducts, filter banks, or the main
1139
HVAC units themselves, compromises on the space provided can easily result in poor system
1140
performance and poor IAQ as well as poor economic and energy performance. Interior designers tend to
1141
be more concerned about the appearance of their materials selections than on the composition of the
1142
materials. Emissions from materials or the materials that are required to clean and maintain building
1143
surfaces can be a major source of indoor pollutants. Only by considering both the appearance and the air
1144
quality implications of material choices can both design goals be best achieved.
1145
1146
SIDEBAR: Capturing the Synergy of Integrated Design Solutions
1147
The researchers who prepared the section on buildings’ potential role in the mitigation
1148
of greenhouse gas emissions (GHG) for the Nobel Prize winning 2007 IPCC report on
1149
global climate change identified buildings as the sector with the largest potential
1150
reduction in emissions without the development of new technology or the
1151
implementation of regulations or market incentives not already in place.\*. They
1152
identified the potential for even larger reductions in GHG emissions through the
1153
synergy of implementing various energy use reduction measures in concert rather than
1154
in isolation.
1155
1156
Nearly 80% of the potential reductions identified by the researchers can be made at a
1157
cost saving (or negative cost) to the owner. Many of the potential savings are related to
1158
ventilation and thermal conditioning of buildings, to closely related functions that
1159
should be conceived and articulated together in the design proves.
1160
1161
The IPCC researchers identified market barriers to energy efficiency and indoor
1162
environmental quality shown as adapted from their work in Table 2-3 below.
1163
1164
Table 2-3. Market barriers to energy efficiency and good IAQ
1165
Limitations of the traditional building design process:
1166
11 Banham, Reyner, 1984. The Architecture of the Well-tempered Environment, Second Edition. University of Chicago Press.
12 When it ceased publication, Progressive Architecture was incorporated editorially into the journal of the American Institute
of Architects, now called “Architect,” The annual PA Design Awards were one of the prestigious design awards that
architects sought.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 34
Return to TOP
• Minimizing energy use requires optimizing the system as a whole
1167
• In contrast, the typical design process is linear and sequential
1168
• Integrated design process (IDP) helps to achieve 35-50%energy savings at little to
1169
no incremental upfront cost
1170
1171
Source: Adapted from IPCC WGIII Assessment Report: Chapter 6. Mitigation options in buildings as
1172
presented by: Mark D. Levine Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory and Diana Ürge-Vorsatz Central
1173
European University
1174
1175
\* UrgeVorsatz et al, 2007. Mitigating CO2 emissions from energy use in the world's buildings. Building
1176
Research & Information: 35(4), 379–398 and, IPCC, 2007. The Fourth Assessment Report. Geneva:
1177
World Health Organization..Working Group III, chapter 6, Residential and Commercial Buildings.
1178
Accessed 17 June 2008 at http://www.ipcc.ch/pdf/assessment-report/ar4/wg3/ar4-wg3-chapter6.pdf .
1179
1180
1181
Integrate design throughout the process
1182
By addressing problems in a holistic fashion with an integrated design team, many synergies result. But
1183
this integration is most effective when commenced at the beginning of the process and maintained
1184
through to final occupancy. Pollution or heat loads reduced by the designers of the interior and lighting
1185
systems reduce the burden on ventilation system designers to remove the pollutants with high air
1186
exchange rates and to reduce the thermal loads with added conditioning (heating, cooling, or moisture
1187
control). Thus, while reducing waste heat by selection of more efficient illumination devices and use of
1188
controls to reduce unnecessary operation, electric consumption is reduced as well as waste heat. In
1189
commercial buildings and many other building types, during the cooling season and in some climates
1190
throughout the year, much of the load on air-conditioning systems is imposed by the waste heat from
1191
inefficient and wasteful illumination.
1192
1193
Ventilation aimed at supplying only what is needed when and where it is needed is much like a lighting
1194
system that only operates when people are present and require the illumination and where daylight
1195
cannot provide sufficient illumination for the activities being conducted. Thus, when the ventilation and
1196
illumination design teams members collaborate, there is an even larger saving then when they work in
1197
isolation. Sensors that detect the presence of occupants are effective in control of the delivery of
1198
electrical illumination or modulate daylight entry and control of ventilation and thermal conditioning
1199
systems.
1200
1201
Different project delivery mechanisms result in diverse relationships among design team members, but
1202
the lead designer (architect or other manager of the design and construction processes) needs to
1203
orchestrate the direct interactions of the various design team members whose responsibilities and
1204
specialized skills affect each other’s work or overlap in terms of the building systems and components or
1205
operational protocols for which they are responsible. In terms of IAQ, these include the architect and the
1206
major engineering consultants (MEP, Structural, Electrical/Lighting, Acoustical, and Site Planning/Civil
1207
Engineering/Landscape). While it may not be obvious how these diverse professional disciplines affect
1208
each other and how their collective considerations and decisions impact IAQ, it is not always an integral
1209
part of the design process to ensure good communications and coordination. In addition, the individual
1210
responsible for leading the commissioning process and even those writing specifications and leading
1211
value engineering analysis and decision-making all impact various disciplines and the building design
1212
and operational features that determine IAQ.
1213
1214
Major Interactions
1215
Understanding and addressing interactions among different building components and systems are keys
1216
to successful IAQ design and energy and cost efficient buildings. An important example is that of the
1217
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 35
Return to TOP
various building features that are critical to providing adequate ventilation with clean outdoor air. See
1218
sidebar – “Interactions”
1219
1220
SIDEBAR: “Interactions”
1221
All of the relevant elements of HVAC system design have the potential to affect the
1222
others. It is obvious that mechanical system selection will affect the requirements for
1223
and location of the mechanical room and the space that must be allocated to equipment
1224
and to ductwork and piping connected to it. The type of filtration and air cleaning
1225
needed will depend on both the outside air quantity and quality and can be affected by
1226
the location of OA intakes or separation distances from cooling towers and exhausts. A
1227
complete commissioning process is like the test drive of a car that has been assembled
1228
in a factory; each of the parts may have been tested, but until the whole system is
1229
operated under various modes, it is not possible to detect conflicts or malfunctions that
1230
result from the many interactions among the parts and their separate and overlapping
1231
functions.
1232
1233
Scheduling
1234
Integrated design applies not only to the building components but also to the scheduling of the design
1235
and construction processes. It is common for buildings to be finished in a hurry-up fashion as the
1236
intended completion date looms near and the construction is behind schedule. Premature occupancy in
1237
relation to the off-gassing of new materials, proper clean-up of construction debris, and incompleted
1238
system adjusting and balancing has accounted for a large number of IAQ problems in new buildings.
1239
The scheduling of sufficient time for a reasonable flush out schedule after punch list items are complete
1240
is an effective way to reduce the likelihood of problems upon initial occupancy. Remember that most
1241
new construction materials, finishes, and furnishings emit the vast majority of their VOC emissions
1242
during the earliest portion of their service lives, generally in the hours, days, and weeks after installation.
1243
By moving into a building without sufficient flush out – airing out the building with outdoor air
1244
continuously for several days or even a week or two – the newly arrived occupants will be exposed to
1245
the worst possible conditions. Additional airing out can often continue during the early occupancy
1246
period to minimize the concentrations of chemicals emitted from the new materials.
1247
1248
4. Practice proactive moisture exclusion and protection design
1249
Moisture protection is not just another specification section. It is an essential component of a well
1250
thought out, healthy building. It concerns the envelope as well as all wet systems and their
1251
surroundings.
1252
1253
It has come to be well-known that moisture in buildings is associated with increased rates of asthma
1254
and allergic responses among occupants. Moisture control is essential, as any designer is well aware.
1255
However, changes in building materials, assemblies, equipment, and ventilation have often resulted
1256
in failed moisture protection.
1257
1258
Extensive and detailed guidance is provided in Chapter 4 of this Guide. A few simple rules for the
1259
design process need to be followed and attention to their execution has to be diligent from design
1260
concept through acceptance. The rules vary by climate and building construction materials, but the
1261
principles are consistent.
1262
1263
5. Ensure adequate ventilation and effective thermal control during all occupied periods
1264
A major cause of IAQ failure is inadequate or improper ventilation. Delivering what is needed when
1265
and where it is needed is the key to controlling indoor pollutant concentrations while minimizing the
1266
impacts of ventilation equipment and operational costs and wasted energy. The two components of
1267
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 36
Return to TOP
adequate ventilation are determining the necessary quantity and ensuring that it is delivered where it
1268
is needed. The amount needed depends on the rate of pollutant generation indoors by the building
1269
itself, the occupants and the contents. The quality of the outdoor air is an important consideration in
1270
the determination of how much air to bring or let into a building as well as to what filtration and air
1271
cleaning might be appropriate and necessary. Delivering the air from mechanical ventilation to the
1272
individual spaces where it is needed is often fraught with problems which if not addressed will result
1273
in wasted energy consumption and poor indoor air quality. By carefully sizing the system and
1274
attending to it throughout the design and construction process including proper commissioning of the
1275
completed system, most problems can be avoided.
1276
1277
Until the mid-80s, designers, mostly mechanical engineers, considered ventilation systems almost
1278
exclusively as a way of controlling the thermal environment, dry bulb temperature and for humid
1279
outdoor climates or indoor environments, to control humidity. It was only the promulgation of
1280
ASHRAE’s Standard 62, first published in 1973, that contained any requirements for changing the
1281
air in a building and exhausting spaces such as kitchens and bathrooms where pollutants were
1282
expected to be generated. Focusing only on the quantity of outdoor air without regard to its quality
1283
or to its delivery to the breathing zone of each occupied space has increased the attention to
1284
mechanical systems or natural ventilation designs as means of improving IAQ. Meanwhile, at the
1285
same time as air-conditioning has become nearly ubiquitous in non-residential buildings and in many
1286
residential buildings as well, increasingly it has been associated with increased incidents of building
1287
related symptoms and illnesses. The discovery of Legionnaire’s disease in a Philadelphia hotel in
1288
1976 raised awareness of the potential for HVAC systems to actually be both the source and the
1289
delivery mechanism for dangerous pollutants. As efforts to conserve energy led to reduced
1290
ventilation rates, even more building occupants complained of discomfort and sickness that
1291
researchers have attributed to their exposures indoors. Now we know that mechanical systems can
1292
both reduce and increase IAQ problems, depending on how well they are designed, built, maintained,
1293
and operated. Integration of the HVAC or natural ventilation design into the basic concept of the
1294
building can improve ventilation performance and eliminate many causes of IAQ problems. Finally,
1295
full integration of the concept of the building operations and the control o fthe mechanical system
1296
into the ventilation design is necessary as the control and the system are often insufficiently
1297
integrated in the design process.
1298
1299
[Add text on thermal control and its relation to ventilation]
1300
1301
6. Focus on “IAQ by design” during the Conceptual and Schematic Design Phases
1302
The most important decisions with respect to overall building performance – energy, IAQ,
1303
illumination, acoustics, and even economics – are made at the earliest stages of the design process.
1304
Among the most important considerations are overall building shape, location and orientation on the
1305
site, location of openings through which light and air will enter and be supplemented by building
1306
services (mechanical, illumination), ventilation patterns within the building, human and vehicle
1307
circulation on site and within the building,, major structural and finish materials, and the process for
1308
ensuring delivery of the design intent. Once these are determined, efforts to optimize building
1309
performance is akin to the retrofit of an existing building. Trade-offs among priorities and integrated
1310
solutions to fundamental design challenges are no longer as easy or even feasible at all. Therefore,
1311
rather than thinking of ventilation as an after-the-fact application of mechanical equipment to a shell
1312
and core concept, it should be an integral part of the development of the shell and core concept itself.
1313
Illumination similarly can be thought of as being applied to an overall concept or as part of the
1314
considerations that determine the shape and layout of the building and the location of its translucent
1315
openings to the outside. Acoustics should not be retrofitted on a ventilation system but the system
1316
itself should be designed to reduce the need to apply additional measures to control mechanical
1317
equipment noise.
1318
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 37
Return to TOP
1319
Considerations for the conceptual and schematic design phase are included in Table 2-2. This table is
1320
intended only to highlight some of the major design considerations that should be included in the
1321
conceptualization of the building and its schematic design. Many formal considerations will govern
1322
these processes as well codes, real estate considerations, and others. But if there is sufficient
1323
attention to the IAQ implications during the decision process for these major of these in the early
1324
design process, the design development and construction document phases can simply supply the
1325
details and refine these decisions rather than having to fight or compensate for them. All of these
1326
items are addressed in greater detail in later sections of this guide.
1327
1328
7. Carefully select building materials and finishes to avoid potential sources of indoor air pollutants
1329
The best way to reduce the potential build-up of pollutants indoors is to eliminate or reduce their
1330
sources indoors. Ventilation is simply a means to remove what can’t be eliminated. People are a
1331
source of pollutants, so there will always be a need for some ventilation to remove bioeffluents
1332
(human metabolic products).
1333
1334
A vast number of different materials are used in a building’s construction, finishing and furnishing.
1335
Many studies have clearly shown that certain of these materials can have a large impact on the
1336
quality of indoor air due to their emissions of chemical and particulate contaminants. A designer thus
1337
has the option of exercising “source control” and eliminating key contaminant sources through
1338
careful selection of materials used indoors. This highly effective strategy will minimize the need for
1339
costly, and inefficient use of increased ventilation to dilute contaminants that could have been
1340
avoided. Although specific IAQ guidelines for individual contaminants remain sparse, labeling
1341
systems for materials and products are steadily improving, and effective choices can be made that
1342
will lead to improved indoor air. No designer should need to use strong pollution emitting sources
1343
given the availability of cleaner products now on the market.
1344
1345
In selecting low-emission materials, attention must also be paid to their long-term durability and
1346
ease of maintenance and cleaning. Since emissions tend to be greatest during the early portion of a
1347
product’s life cycle (particularly if installation involves use of adhesive materials), the need for
1348
frequent replacement should be avoided. Cleaning compounds used indoors can have a dramatic
1349
impact on contaminant levels, thus materials surfaces that are easy to clean with minimal use of
1350
strong solvents should be specified whenever possible.
1351
1352
There is extensive guidance on source control in terms of building material selection, cleaning and
1353
maintenance products, and other indoor pollutant sources. What is often overlooked is that it is
1354
extremely cost effective to prevent pollution rather than trying to remove it once it gets into the
1355
building. Many types of guidance are available and no designer should need to use strong pollution
1356
emitting sources with the present availability of cleaner, less-polluting products now on the market.
1357
1358
8. Include consideration of the cleaning, maintenance and replacement of interior finish materials
1359
as well as mechanical and other building equipment when these items are selected and specified
1360
from design concept through design development and construction drawings.
1361
1362
Most commercial, educational, retail, and public assembly buildings are cleaned when they are not
1363
under normal occupancy. As a result, ventilation is often turned off. So, the janitor brings the 5-
1364
gallon can in full, uses the product on floors or other surfaces, and takes an empty can away. Yes
1365
most products today that dry fairly quickly….
1366
1367
1368
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 38
Return to TOP
Janitorial closets, for example, are critical to proper cleaning, yet it is not uncommon to find them to
1369
distant from the spaces they serve or to find that they lack critical elements including ventilation and
1370
protection against the undesirable consequences of chemical leakage spills
1371
1372
9. Managing construction and renovation to preserve IAQ
1373
[To be completed]
1374
1375
10. Complete commissioning to ensure delivery of the design intent
1376
Commissioning the envelope, ventilation system (mechanical or passive) and the thermal control
1377
system are essential to achieving good IAQ. Commissioning should include providing high quality
1378
O&M documentation & training.
1379
1380
Effective commissioning ensures adequate communication of design intent to all team members as
1381
well as to construction and operation personnel. It begins with documentation of the project goals
1382
and should include IAQ. A plain language narrative of the intent of the design with respect to
1383
achievement of IAQ including ventilation in all operational modes will assist in achieving the goals.
1384
1385
A list of the major topics that should be included in the documentation for commissioning is
1386
presented in Table 2-3 “Commissioning topics” below.
1387
1388
[Additional text needed here]
1389
1390
1391
Table 2.3 Cx Topics to be Addressed [Table to be developed]
1392
1393
1394
1395
1396
1397
1398
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 39
Return to TOP
1399
OBJECTIVE 1 – MANAGE THE PROCESS TO ACHIEVE GOOD IAQ
1400
1401
1402
The processes used to deliver a project are as essential to the achievement of good IAQ as the technical
1403
strategies employed.
1404
1405
Make good IAQ an explicit priority. Every building project entails hundreds of decisions and trade-offs.
1406
If the owner and design team leader do not make IAQ an explicit priority, it can easily be overlooked
1407
when decisions are made that have a determining effect on it. Many IAQ problems occur because
1408
obvious sources of potential problems simply were not considered.
1409
1410
Consider IAQ from conceptual design. The most important decisions with respect to overall building
1411
performance – energy use, IAQ, illumination, acoustics, and even economics – are made at the earliest
1412
stages of the design process. Among these are:
1413
1414
o Overall building shape, location and orientation;
1415
o Location of openings;
1416
o Ventilation patterns within the building;
1417
o Human and vehicle circulation on site and within the building; and
1418
o Major structural and finish materials.
1419
1420
Once these are determined, efforts to optimize building performance are akin to retrofitting an existing
1421
building. Trade-offs among priorities and integrated solutions to multiple design challenges are no
1422
longer as easy, if even feasible at all. A key example is the selection of ventilation systems. Rather than
1423
thinking of ventilation as an after-the-fact application of mechanical equipment to a pre-determined shell
1424
and core concept, design teams should think of it as an integral part of the development of the shell and
1425
core concept. This will assure that there is sufficient space in the right locations for ventilation systems
1426
that can efficiently meet the building’s needs for air cleaning, dilution and moisture control.
1427
1428
Integrate the design across disciplines. Design team members tend to focus on their individual goals,
1429
but economical achievement of good IAQ – and other performance goals, too – is best accomplished
1430
through joint optimization. The design team leader should facilitate communication and coordination to
1431
achieve IAQ goals. For example, while putting both the loading dock and outdoor air intake on the back
1432
of a building may meet the architect’s aesthetic goals, moving the outdoor air intake to the roof will
1433
greatly reduce the potential to bring truck exhaust into the building through the air handler. Similarly,
1434
while interior designers are focused on the appearance of interior finishes, consideration of their
1435
moisture transmission characteristics, VOC emissions and cleaning requirements can reduce the
1436
potential for IAQ problems.
1437
1438
Manage construction to preserve IAQ. Moisture and dirt that get into building materials and systems
1439
during construction can defeat the best design. Making and enforcing a construction phase IAQ plan is
1440
essential to long term IAQ, as well as to the protection of occupants in buildings undergoing renovation.
1441
1442
Commission to assure that the design intent is met. Like any product, q building need a quality
1443
assurance process to meet its performance goals. Commissioning should start in the project planning
1444
stage when problems can be corrected at the lowest cost, and should cover those systems that are critical
1445
to achieving the owner’s project requirements.
1446
1447
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 40
Return to TOP
Establish a project schedule that facilitates good IAQ. Time is of the essence in every construction
1448
project. Yet failure to allow adequate time for critical steps such as drying of construction materials
1449
(e.g., concrete), flushing initial high emissions from finishes (e.g., paint, carpets) and balancing, controls
1450
and commissioning work critical to HVAC system performance can result in IAQ problems. Moreover,
1451
contracts that reward early completion can unintentionally invite actions such as installation of drywall
1452
or insulation before the building is dried in, leading to moisture damage and mold problems.
1453
1454
Give facilities staff the tools to succeed. Facilities staff need to understand the intended operation of
1455
building systems and their maintenance requirements in order to achieve and preserve the intended
1456
performance. Facilities staff should be involved in the process as early as possible. O&M
1457
documentation and training should describe the intended operation of building systems, not just
1458
components. It should also provide clear guidance on recommended frequencies of inspections, what to
1459
look for, and recommended timing of periodic maintenance.
1460
1461
Establishment of explicit IAQ requirements, consideration of IAQ at conceptual design, and integrated
1462
design and construction are discussed in the main body of Chapter 2. Further information of specific
1463
strategies is discussed in this section:
1464
1465
• Strategy 1.1 – Commission to Assure that the Owner’s IAQ Requirements are Met
1466
• Strategy 1.2 – Select HVAC Systems to Improve IAQ and Reduce the Energy Impacts of Ventilation
1467
• Strategy 1.3 – Establish a Project Schedule that Facilitates Achievement of Good IAQ
1468
• Strategy 1.4 – Manage Construction and Renovation to Preserve IAQ
1469
• Strategy 1.5 – Provide High Quality O&M Documentation and Training Focused on IAQ
1470
1471
1472
1473

1485
1486
Overview Information
What is Commissioning and Why Is It Needed?
1487
Few manufacturers today would consider producing a product without a formal quality control process.
1488
Yet the majority of buildings are built without the use of systematic quality control procedures. As a
1489
result, buildings may be turned over with undetected deficiencies and key assemblies or systems may
1490
fail to function as intended. Indoor air quality may suffer due to any number of problems in design,
1491
material and equipment selections or construction. To address these problems a growing number of
1492
building owners are incorporating commissioning, a quality-focused process used to achieve successful
1493
construction projects (ASHRAE 2005).
1494
1495
Start Commissioning at Project Inception
1496
It is a common misconception that commissioning is a post-construction process. In fact,
1497
commissioning should start in the pre-design phase to maximize its effectiveness and cost-effectiveness.
1498
During this phase, the owner should select a commissioning authority (CA) and establish the
1499
commissioning scope and budget. The design team’s responsibilities related to commissioning should
1500
be defined in their agreements with the owner.
1501
1502
During pre-design, the CA should help the owner identify and make explicit all functional requirements
1503
for the project. For example, every owner expects his building to be free of condensation and mold
1504
problems, be properly ventilated, and provide good-quality ventilation air, but these Owners Project
1505
Requirements (OPRs) can get lost in the shuffle and fail to be met if they are not explicitly stated and
1506
tracked throughout the project.
1507
1508
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 43
Return to TOP
The CA should provide input to the project schedule to assure that it accommodates the steps necessary
1509
to achieve the owners IAQ requirements. This input may include, for example, the timing of inspections
1510
that must be made while key assemblies are still open, or proper sequencing of work to avoid moisture
1511
damage.
1512
1513
Commission the Design
1514
It is much easier and cheaper to correct deficiencies on paper during design than in the finished building
1515
after construction.
1516
1517
During conceptual design, the design team should document the concepts, calculations, decisions, and
1518
product selections used to meet the Owner’s Project Requirements and applicable codes and standards.
1519
The CA plays an important role in reviewing this Basis of Design (BoD) to determine whether it will
1520
meet the owner’s requirements. The CA should continue to review the design development and the
1521
construction documents to assure that they will fulfill the owner’s needs.
1522
1523
The CA should also provide construction checklists, functional testing requirements, and systems
1524
manual requirements and O&M training requirements for inclusion in the specifications, so that the
1525
contractors can understand and budget their role in the commissioning process.
1526
1527
Commission the Construction
1528
During construction the CA should monitor work to assure that it does not compromise the owner’s
1529
project requirements. This includes review of submittals to assure that they are consistent with the OPR
1530
and BOD and provide for commissioning needs. It also includes early and repeated observation of key
1531
aspects of construction to assure that the owner’s requirements are not compromised. This may include,
1532
for example, checking the continuity of drainage planes and air barriers while walls are under
1533
construction, or checking that access is preserved as HVAC equipment and later services are installed.
1534
1535
Usually the CA also provides the installation and start-up checklists that are executed and signed by the
1536
contractors, and spot checks them after completion. Often the CA verifies a statistical sample of the
1537
balancing report by observing the balancer as he conducts repeated measurements.
1538
1539
Test for Acceptance
1540
The CA oversees and documents functional tests that determine the ability of building assemblies and
1541
systems to meet the owner’s project requirements. These may include, for example, testing of all
1542
HVAC system control system sequences of operation or performance testing of air barriers or drainage
1543
planes.
1544
1545
Systems Manual & O&M Training
1546
O&M manuals are often massive, and can lack key information while being laden with material that
1547
does not apply to the project. O&M training is often a cursory afterthought. The CA may define the
1548
requirements for a systems manual and O&M training and verify their delivery.
1549
1550
1551
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 44
Return to TOP
1552

1554
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

Case Study Example
1556
1557
Theater AHU-1
Space CO2 Sensors

500
1000
1500
2000
7/30 12:00
7/30 18:00
7/31 0:00
7/31 6:00

7/31 18:00

8/1 6:00
8/1 12:00
north CO2

1558
Figure 1.1-A
Figure 1.1-B
1559
1560
Poor ventilation in an extensively renovated theater led to multiple patron complaints of stuffiness.
1561
Several factors caused the problem:
1562
1563
1) Outdoor air flow was controlled to maintain the average CO2 at 900 ppm, but one of the two CO2
1564
sensors (pink) consistently read near zero, so the average CO2 level appeared to the control system to be
1565
half its actual value.
1566
2 ) The upper end of the reset range on outdoor air flow was 3,000 cfm instead of 7,200 cfm due to
1567
improper application of control logic.
1568
3) The outside air flow measuring station read about 1,100 cfm with the fan off.
1569
1570
As a result of these problems, the actual minimum outside air flow did not go above about 2,000 cfm.
1571
The problem was not diagnosed until several years after the renovation when the building was retro-
1572
commissioned.
1573
1574
1575
1576
1577

1579
Energy Impacts of Ventilation

1581
1582
Overview Information
In mechanically ventilated buildings, HVAC systems can have a significant effect on indoor air quality
1583
(IAQ), energy use and occupants well being. Studies have shown that Poor indoor air quality has been
1584
directly linked to inappropriate HVAC system design/ selection and HVAC system operation (William J.
1585
Fisk 2000 presentation “Health and Productivity Gains from Better Indoor Environments and Their
1586
Implications”)
1587
1588
1589
Two cross-sectional studies of more than 11,000 workers in 107 buildings in Europe also found
1590
increases in perceived productivity, fewer illness symptoms, and less absenteeism in buildings that
1591
provided workers with control over temperature and ventilation conditions, compared to a control
1592
group (study reference Preller 1990 study)
1593
1594
In addition to the measurable IAQ factors such as CO2, temperature and humidity, the HVAC system
1595
design should also consider human factors such as personal control over the environment. It’s been
1596
shown in a number of studies, such as in the Wyon (1996) study that ventilated buildings with
1597
enhanced occupant control of the indoor environment have lower reported rates of the symptoms of sick
1598
building syndrome and consequently fewer reported complaints relating to indoor air quality
1599
HVAC system selection should always consider indoor air quality, energy consumption, and
1600
environmental benefit as well as all of the inter-related building systems. Optimizing the design and
1601
benefits requires that the project engineers and architect address these issues early in the concept design
1602
phase and continually revise subsequent decisions throughout the remaining design process.
1603
It is also essential that the HVAC system designer communicate to owners the need to implement an
1604
effective commissioning process and preventative maintenance programs.
1605
Many factors enter into the selection of the HVAC system, including outdoor climate conditions, indoor
1606
environmental conditions, site constraints, budget limitations, end-user environmental criteria,
1607
operational/ maintainability and energy targets, etc. The project design team must address all of these
1608
issues in the HVAC system selection process. Some specific IAQ requirements and considerations that
1609
may be critically affected by the choice of HVAC system type are included in the table:

Case Study Examples
Gulf Islands Operations Centre – Sidney, British Columbia
1617
1618
1619
Figure 1.2-A
Figure 1.2-B
1620
1621
Architect: Larry McFarland Architects Ltd.; Mechanical Engineer: Stantec Consulting Ltd.
1622
Client: Public Works and Government Services Canada
1623
Size: 1050 m²
1624
LEED® Status: Certified Platinum
1625
1626
This is the first project in Canada to achieve the LEED Platinum certification level. The redevelopment
1627
is designed to accommodate Parks Canada and other associated user group vessels. The three level
1628
11300f² (1050m²) Operations Building consists of office space, a small laboratory, library, lockers and
1629
two sleeping quarters. An occupancy level of approximately 35 people is expected for the building.
1630
Extensive application of products and practices improved the indoor environmental quality for
1631
occupants. The project utilizes a mixed mode ventilation system to achieved outstanding energy cost
1632
savings of 75% with an energy intensity savings of 49% and exceptional indoor air quality and water use
1633
efficiency with over 60% reduction of potable water use. An ocean-based heat pump system provides
1634
heating and domestic hot water, and a rainwater collection system is used for marine wash-water and
1635
sewage conveyance.
1636
1637
1638
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 49
Return to TOP
1639
Philip Merrill Environmental Center
1640
Source: Heerwagen and Zagrreus (2005)
1641
1642
1643
Figure 1.2-C
1644
1645
As the first LEED® Platinum building in the United States, the Philip Merrill Environment Center is at
1646
the leading edge in sustainability practices. The Merrill Center building is described as a combination of
1647
space-age technology and age-old techniques.
1648
The building has a raft of enhanced Indoor Environment Quality (IEQ) features, including high levels of
1649
occupant control, abundant natural lighting and low VOC emission material usage.
1650
The occupants of this building were surveyed and interviewed about the IEQ and its effect on
1651
psychosocial and productivity-related factors. Key positive findings of this survey showed that
1652
occupants were highly satisfied with the Merrill Center building as a whole, and that intangibles such as
1653
pride in the building and aesthetics contributed to high levels of morale, well-being and a sense of
1654
belonging at work. Acoustical conditions were the most negatively rated, primarily due to distractions
1655
from people talking and loss of speech privacy associated with the highly open environment.
1656
1657
From “The human factors of sustainability: a post-occupancy evaluation of the Philip Merrill
1658
Environmental Center”; http://www.cbe.berkeley.edu/research/pdf\_files/SR\_CBF\_2005.pdf
1659
1660
1661
Negative Case Study Examples:
1662
Four benchmark cases of indoor air quality litigation include:
1663
1664
1) Call vs. Prudential
1665
This was the first major IAQ-related case argued before a jury. Although the case was settled (for a sum
1666
rumored to be in the multimillion dollar range) before a verdict was reached, Call generated important
1667
IAQ related law. Claims by the plaintiffs charged acts of negligence:
1668
1669
2) DuPage County Courthouse
1670
A courthouse located in DuPage County, Illinois, was the cause of a number of suits involving hundreds
1671
of litigants. In 1991, shortly after law enforcement and judicial staff began to occupy the building,
1672
occupants began to suffer from symptoms characteristic of sick building syndrome (SBS). Headaches,
1673
nausea, dizziness, respiratory irritation, and other symptoms were all reported. In March of 1992 several
1674
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 50
Return to TOP
building occupants were removed by ambulance, and the building was temporarily evacuated.
1675
1676
In late 1992 a number of the building's occupants filed a personal injury lawsuit against the architect,
1677
contractors, and HVAC contractors, alleging that their illnesses were caused by the design of the
1678
ventilation system and the presence of volatile organic compounds.
1679
1680
The county then sued the architect and contractors, seeking $3 million for fixing the building's
1681
ventilation system.
1682
1683
Because the county workers' compensation program was self-funded, private insurers were not directly
1684
affected, but the publicity generated by the problems in this building and others provided incentive to
1685
insurers and others to take sick building complaints more seriously.
1686
1687
3) Polk County Courthouse
1688
A new Polk County, Florida, courthouse was found to cause a number of cases of sick building
1689
syndrome, allegedly because of fungi or other microbiological agents breeding in its HVAC system.
1690
1691
The county sued several parties including the building's general contractor, and in June of 1995 a jury
1692
ordered Reliance Insurance Company to pay $25.8 million in damages. The final settlement was for $35
1693
million due to accrued interest over a long appeals process.
1694
1695
4) Martin County Courthouse
1696
A courthouse in Martin County, Florida, which was constructed in 1989 experienced IAQ problems
1697
resulting in rehabilitation of the facility which exceeded $24 million. This was in large part because the
1698
south Florida humidity supported the growth of molds and fungi in building materials including vinyl
1699
wallpaper.
1700
1701
The cost of rehabilitation exceeded the cost of construction,which was $11 million, and the country had
1702
to relocate its employees for two years while the work was being done
1703
1704
Information from Building Air Quality web site : http://www.baq1.com/newsletter.php?id=13
1705
1706
1707
1708

1712
1713
Overview Information
Establishing a good project construction schedule will help ensure the achievement of good IAQ for the
1714
building project. A building owner can have the best intentions, the design team can provide a great
1715
building design and the contractors can do their best execution, but if the schedule is too compressed and
1716
IAQ is not taken into account in sequencing tasks and project management the IAQ of the finished
1717
project may not meet expectations. Installing construction materials that can absorb moisture before the
1718
building is dried in, storing and/or exposing of construction materials to the incorrect elements prior to
1719
installation, and starting and operating of HVAC equipment for temporary heating or cooling during
1720
construction are just a few project schedule related items that will jeopardize the Indoor Air Quality of
1721
the final building project. A good IAQ plan coupled with proper scheduling and sequencing of the
1722
construction project will help ensure achievement of good IAQ.
1723
1724
The schedule must allow for adequate time to complete the construction activities and properly sequence
1725
them. Involve the design and construction team early in the scheduling process. This could include
1726
input from the construction team during the design phase to help with sequencing and duration of
1727
construction activities. This could also develop into proper construction material selection to match the
1728
sequencing and scheduling of the construction activities. A thought to keep in mind is that typically an
1729
accelerated project schedule leads to poor achievement of good project IAQ.
1730
1731
1732

1739

1740

1758
1759
Overview Information
Events that occur during construction can negatively impact indoor environmental quality throughout
1760
the life of a building. The failure to protect the building and building materials from water can result in
1761
damaged or compromised building materials and/or microbial growth. It is essential that a Water
1762
Management Plan be developed and implemented to address water sources, both natural and man-made,
1763
during the construction process. Intrusion of rain, snow, or fog can result in significant microbial
1764
growth. Additional sources of water problems include groundwater and runoff, construction water
1765
sources, and wet building materials (concrete, fire proofing, cellulose insulation, etc). The Water
1766
management Plan should define approaches to both prevent such intrusions and procedures for
1767
responding to water events that cannot be prevented. The Plan should also address a process for
1768
documenting that wet building materials have been adequately dried to prevent microbial growth.
1769
Materials that experience mold growth (visibly moldy) should be cleaned using appropriate controls or
1770
replaced.
1771
Another important consideration is effective housekeeping to reduce potential sources of odors or
1772
nutrients for microbial growth. Protecting the HVAC system components during transportation, storage
1773
and during installation is particularly important. A contaminated HVAC system can support microbial
1774
growth under conditions of normal operation, which can significantly impact IAQ.
1775
Construction, including renovations, within occupied buildings present additional challenges to
1776
maintaining adequate indoor air quality in the spaces that continue to be occupied. Processes for
1777
containing (isolating) the construction activities to protect the occupants are essential considerations, as
1778
are efforts to minimize contaminant sources.
1779
1780
Any construction activity which produces odor or dust can be considered a source of air pollutants and a
1781
potential contributor to IAQ problems. Airborne contaminants generally originate from construction
1782
activities in the following ways: The disturbance of building materials during repair or demolition
1783
creates dust. Particles are typically airborne during initial work, then settle out on surfaces, only to
1784
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 55
Return to TOP
become airborne again during later activity or disturbance (e.g., cleaning). Airborne dust may also be
1785
odorous or include microbial contaminants. Therefore, appropriate dust control measures and effective
1786
housekeep are important measures for maintaining acceptable IAQ.
1787
1788
Another source of air pollutants are emissions from products used in the construction process. These
1789
include coatings, adhesives, building materials which are applied wet, cleaning agents, etc. Appropriate
1790
selection and control of these products is an important consideration, particularly in an occupied facility.
1791
1792

Chapter 3 Overview Information for Design, Construction, and
1869
Commissioning for IAQ
1870
1871
Part I of the Guide provides a convenient summary of the key elements of design for IAQ. These are
1872
grouped into six goals:
1873
1874
• Objective 2 – Control Moisture in Building Assemblies
1875
• Objective 3 – Limit Entry of Outdoor Contaminants
1876
• Objective 4 – Control Moisture and Contaminants related to Mechanical Systems
1877
• Objective 5 – Limit Contaminants from Indoor Sources
1878
• Objective 6 – Capture and Exhaust Contaminants from Building Equipment and Activities
1879
• Objective 7 – Reduce Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation and Air Cleaning
1880
• Objective 8 – Apply More Advanced Ventilation Approaches
1881
1882
Introductory text provides an overview of each goal. The accompanying graphic identifies major
1883
objectives related to that goal. In the electronic document, each Objective in the graphic is a link to
1884
further information about that topic.
1885
1886
For each Strategy, the chapter provides a Summary, a Graphical illustration, an Example or Case Study
1887
and a Roadmap Table.
1888
1889
The Summary explains why the issue is important for IAQ, how to determine whether it needs to be
1890
considered for a particular project, and potential design solutions.
1891
1892
The Graphic provides a visual overview of the issue. In the electronic document, it contains links to the
1893
detailed guidance in Chapter 4.
1894
1895
The Examples and Case Studies provide insights into problems and successes from other projects.
1896
1897
The Roadmap Table identifies the elements that need to be addressed in meeting each objective, and can
1898
be used as a checklist in project planning. Each element is identified as:
1899
1900
o Fundamental (F),
1901
o Enhanced (E), or
1902
o Conditional, (C), i.e., dependent on the situation.
1903
1904
In the printed document, page numbers for the relevant Chapter 4 guidance will be listed in the How-to
1905
column. In the electronic document, the table entries will hyper-link to the detailed guidance in Chapter
1906
4 via the page numbers in the How-to column.
1907
1908
1909
1910
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 58
Return to TOP
OBJECTIVE 2 – CONTROL MOISTURE IN BUILDING ASSEMBLIES
1911
1912
1913
Control of moisture is fundamental to good IAQ. Dampness is strongly associated with adverse health
1914
outcomes. Moisture enables growth of microorganisms, breakdown of materials, and other processes
1915
that can cause IAQ problems. Moistures is one of the most common causes of IAQ issues in buildings,
1916
and failure to manage it properly can render more advanced IAQ measures moot.
1917
1918
Good design and construction are prerequisites for a building that resists moisture problems. The site
1919
should be designed so that runoff from rain and snowmelt are prevented from entering the building. The
1920
foundation should be designed to minimize rainwater and ground water intrusion. Exterior walls should
1921
be designed to manage rain water, protecting their inner portions from direct rain and seepage through
1922
the cladding. Roofs should be designed to collect and dispose of rainwater.
1923
1924
Wall and roof systems should be designed to minimize condensation of water vapor on cool surfaces
1925
within the building enclosure or on inner surfaces. HVAC systems should be designed to maintain
1926
building pressures that limit moisture transport into the building enclosure and to control indoor
1927
humidity below levels that may cause moisture problems.
1928
1929
Landscaping and other plantings, and the maintenance of those plantings, should be designed to
1930
minimize application of water, herbicides, and pesticides near the building. Indoor moisture sources
1931
should be limited where possible, and unavoidable sources such as pools or showers should be managed
1932
through exhaust and control of building and space pressures. When indoor areas are unavoidably wet
1933
the design should use materials, equipment and assemblies that can withstand repeated wetting.
1934
1935
Key elements of moisture control in building assemblies are discussed in:
1936
• Strategy 2.1 - Limit Penetration of Liquid Water into the Building Envelope
1937
• Strategy 2.2 - Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces
1938
• Strategy 2.3 - Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
1939
• Strategy 2.4 - Control Indoor Humidity
1940
• Strategy 2.5 - Select Suitable Materials, Equipment & Assemblies for Areas that Are Unavoidably
1941
Wet
1942
• Strategy 2.6 - Consider Impacts of Landscaping and Indoor Plants on Moisture and Contaminant
1943
Levels
1944
1945
Moisture must also be controlled during construction to prevent contamination of the building. This
1946
issue is discussed under Strategy 1.4 - Manage Construction and Renovation to Preserve IAQ.
1947
1948
Avoiding moisture problems also requires proper management of moisture related to mechanical
1949
systems. These issues are discussed in Objective 4 Control Moisture and Contaminants Related to
1950
Mechanical Systems. In particular, air handling systems should be designed to limit entry of rain and
1951
snow, properly drain condensate from cooling coils, control carryover of liquid moisture from cooling
1952
coils and humidifiers, and keep filters and airstream surfaces dry. Piping and plumbing fixtures should
1953
be designed to minimize water leaks and facilitate repair; and ductwork, piping and fixtures should be
1954
designed to limit condensation. These issues are addressed in:
1955
• Strategy 4.1 - Control Moisture and Dirt in Air Handling Systems
1956
• Strategy 4.4 - Control Moisture associated with Piping, Plumbing Fixtures and Ductwork
1957
1958
1959
1960
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 59
Return to TOP

Good design is a prerequisite for a building that resists moisture problems. The site should be designed
1978
so that water from rain and snowmelt are prevented from entering the building. The foundation should
1979
be designed to prevent rainwater and ground water intrusion. Exterior walls should be designed to
1980
manage rain water, protecting their inner portions from direct rain and seepage through the cladding.
1981
Roofs should be designed to collect and dispose of rainwater and snowmelt.

pg #’s
• Recommendations for Building Enclosures
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2002
2003
Overview Information
Overview
2004
Preventing condensation, or more accurately, avoiding increase in the moisture content of building
2005
materials to levels that can support the growth of microorganisms especially molds within the enclosure
2006
and on interior surfaces is fundamental to avoiding health and air quality problems in buildings, as well
2007
as premature deterioration of enclosure components.
2008
2009
The growth or amplification of microorganisms in buildings not only results in biodeterioration of
2010
susceptible construction materials but also leads to the production of allergens and microbial VOCs
2011
( musty odors ) that can affect occupant health and air quality. A complex microbial ecology (e.g., mites
2012
feed on mold ; other organisms feed on mites ) can develop in or on construction materials that are
2013
chronically wet or damp. Allergens associated with molds and arthropods growing in chronically wet
2014
construction niches can enter the indoor environment and pose a risk to sensitive occupants.
2015
2016
The moisture content of building materials increases due to water vapor transport across enclosure
2017
assemblies, either due to infiltrating, exfiltrating, or convecting air in contact with surfaces that have a
2018
temperature lower than the dew-point of the air coming in contact with the surface, and/or by diffusion
2019
due to a difference in water vapor pressure across the assembly, or by capillary transport through the
2020
microscopic voids in building materials.
2021
Properly designed enclosure assemblies that have greater drying potential than wetting potential, and
2022
that achieve a moisture balance over time are not always implemented, and many building designs do
2023
not get scrutinized for appropriate enclosure design.
2024
2025
Thermal bridges in the form of highly conductive materials that penetrate the insulated enclosure can
2026
drop temperatures of surfaces indoors to levels promoting condensation
2027
Building enclosures are often designed without a proper understanding of the performance of the
2028
assembly when subjected to the exterior weather and interior boundary conditions. Code requirements
2029
may even impose solutions that are problematic such as requiring vapor retarders prescriptively, and
2030
water resistive barriers that may be too vapor permeable under certain conditions. Prescriptive criteria
2031
in codes are slowly being improved, but the substitution of a single material in an assembly can radically
2032
change how the assembly performs over time.
2033
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 62
Return to TOP
Building enclosures should be designed by a knowledgeable design professional using design tools
2034
referenced in the ASHRAE Handbook-Fundamentals, in order to avoid the likelihood of moisture-
2035
related problems.
2036
2037
Above Grade Walls, Roofs
2038
Control Air Leakage
2039
Condensation of moisture from exfiltrating air in northern climates, and from infiltrating hot humid air
2040
in southern climates, can cause condensation and mold growth that can promote health problems and
2041
premature building deterioration. Unlike the moisture transport mechanism of diffusion due to a vapor
2042
pressure difference, air pressure differentials can transport hundreds of times more water vapor through
2043
air leaks in the envelope over the same period of time (Quirouette, 1986). This water vapor can
2044
condense within the envelope in a concentrated manner, depending on the pathway, if the enclosure
2045
includes surfaces below the dew-point of the air.
2046
2047
Control convection
2048
Air in contact with cool surfaces will be cooled down. Cool air is heavier than warm air and will drop
2049
down, being replaced by warmer air carrying moisture, promoting condensation on the cool surfaces due
2050
to a “convective loop”. Eliminating and avoiding air spaces adjacent to cool surfaces is key to
2051
preventing condensation due to convection.
2052
2053
Control Surface Temperature and Diffusion
2054
Exterior enclosure assemblies need to reduce the likelihood of condensation due to contact with air that
2055
has a dew-point temperature higher than the temperature of the surface of interest, through the use of
2056
materials with appropriate vapor retarding qualities for the conditions considered, and based on their
2057
conductive properties. Consider local exterior weather data, interior design conditions, and material
2058
properties.
2059
2060
Design
2061
Design for airtightness of the enclosure
2062
Include a continuous air barrier system in the building enclosure:
2063
Select a material in each opaque wall, floor and roof assembly that meets a maximum air permeance of
2064
0.02 L/s.m2 @ 75 Pa (0.004 cfm/ft2 @ 0.3” w.g) and join it together with tapes, sealants etc., into an
2065
assembly; join the air barrier layer of each assembly with the air barrier layer of adjacent ones, and to all
2066
fenestration and doors, until all enclosure assemblies (six-sided box) are interconnected and sealed. Seal
2067
all penetrations of the air barrier layer. The airtight layer of each assembly will support the entire air
2068
pressure of wind, stack effect and HVAC fans. Ensure that the airtight layer is structurally supported
2069
and can support the maximum positive and negative air pressures it will experience, without rupture,
2070
displacement or mechanical damage. Stresses must be safely transferred to the structure.
2071
2072
Convection
2073
Air gaps adjacent to cool or cold surfaces can promote convection within a wall assembly. Cold air is
2074
heavier and drops, pulling in warm humid air to replace it and deposit moisture on the cold surface.
2075
This is especially true in vertical or sloping assemblies. The colder side can be the sheathing in colder
2076
climates or the interior drywall in warmer climates. Eliminating the air space on one or other side of the
2077
insulation can be effective in preventing these convective loops. Fibrous insulation, however, which is
2078
mostly air, can also promote these convective loops.
2079
2080
Diffusion
2081
A vapor retarder with appropriate permeance for the application should be placed on the predominantly
2082
high vapor pressure side of the assembly. To design assemblies for appropriate diffusion control,
2083
hygrothermal analysis using either the steady state dewpoint or Glaser method described in the 2005
2084
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 63
Return to TOP
ASHRAE Handbook – Fundamentals, Chapter 23 or by using a mathematical model that simulates
2085
transient hygrothermal conditions (such as WUFI, hygIRC, or Delphin). Users of such methods must
2086
understand their limitations, and interpretation of the analysis results should be done by a trained person
2087
to reasonably extrapolate field performance approaching the design results. The International Energy
2088
Agency Annex 24 (Hens, 1990) has established that a surface humidity of 80% represents a reasonable
2089
threshold for designers to achieve a successful building enclosure assembly for temperatures between
2090
40oF – 120oF (5oC-50oC).
2091
2092
Window and Skylight Selection
2093
Fenestration should be selected carefully by designers to avoid condensation. Fenestration is selected
2094
taking into account the interior boundary conditions, exterior weather conditions, and, from a chart
2095
developed by AAMA, the appropriate Condensation Resistance Factor (CRF) for the window or skylight.
2096
Thermally broken units that minimize the amount of exterior metal exposed to cold usually perform best.
2097
The edge spacer of the insulating glass unit is usually the most conductive (and coldest) location in an
2098
assembly. A new generation of “warm-edge” spacers that include thermally broken aluminum spacers,
2099
stainless steel spacers and non-metallic glass-fiber reinforced plastic spacers are increasingly being used
2100
and improve the thermal performance of fenestration. Window and skylight manufacturers generally
2101
can provide NFRC simulations using the software THERM that show how a specific selection of
2102
window, spacer and glass, with various gaseous fill will perform. It is also important to note that some
2103
non-metal windows that have an improved U-factor may have a worse CRF than a metallic thermally
2104
broken window. Custom designs are often required to be verified using the THERM and WINDOW
2105
software, and validated by physical laboratory testing as well.
2106
2107
Below Grade Walls and Slabs on and Below Grade
2108
Deep ground temperature in a locale is not unlike the average annual temperature, with local variations
2109
due to shading from vegetation, elevation or proximity to the coast. Comparing the annual average
2110
temperature with the August dew-point temperature of the air is a good indication of whether mold will
2111
grow on slabs and walls of below grade structures. Concrete is highly conductive and will become very
2112
similar to ground temperature and potentially become a likely condensation surface. Insulating outside
2113
the concrete is the best choice for keeping the concrete above the dew-point of the air. In termite-
2114
infested areas, select rigid insulation that has termiticides included; that renders poisoning the soil
2115
unnecessary. Insulating under slabs with a vapor retarder on top in intimate contact with the slab is the
2116
best strategy for a dry slab.
2117
Insulating on the inside of below-grade walls is possible, but it is best to insulate using adhered rigid
2118
insulation so as to avoid convection through fibrous insulation.
2119
2120
2121
2122
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 64
Return to TOP
2123
2124
Figure 2.2-A Comparing a location’s average annual temperature with the
2125
August dew-point of the air is a good indication of whether below-grade
2126
structures may cause condensation and mold.
2127
2128
2129
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 65
Return to TOP
2130
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
2131
2132
2133
Case Study Example
Thermal bridges, due to conductive materials that penetrate or interrupt the thermal insulation layer,
2134
cause a drop in temperature of the interior surface in cold climates. This can cause condensation and
2135
mold growth. It can also cause deposition of particulates onto the cold surfaces due to convective loops
2136
caused by the temperature differences called “ghosting”. In the building shown below in a cold climate,
2137
interior humidity, candle smoke and thermal bridging combined to cause ghosting of the steel studs. (
2138
2139
2140
Figure 2.2-B THERM study showing temperature of studs (Anis, 2007)
2141

2142
Figure 2.2-C Steel Studs Form Thermal Bridges Lowering Temperature of Interior Surfaces
2143
2144

2145
2146
2147
2148
2149
2150
Strategy 2.3 – Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
2151
2152
Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
F
E
How-to
• Assessment
o

9

9

9

Pressure differential mapping
9
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2153
2154
Overview Information
Proper building pressurization is required to limit moisture and contaminant transfer across the building
2155
envelope. Building pressurization is the static pressure difference between the interior pressure and the
2156
exterior (atmospheric) pressure of a building. This static pressure difference will influence how much
2157
and where exfiltration and infiltration will occur at the building envelope. Many HVAC systems are
2158
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 67
Return to TOP
designed to achieve an overall building pressurization at an average of 0.02 inches (4.98 Pa) differential
2159
(across the building envelope). The target pressure differential of 0.02 inches can fluctuate due to
2160
changing weather conditions, wind load, and HVAC system operation.
2161
2162
2163
Figure 2.3-A (Source: ASHRAE Fundamentals 2005 page 27.1)
2164
2165
Positive building pressure is a major factor in the following situations; mechanically cooled buildings in
2166
hot humid climates for reduced infiltration and control of condensation and mold growth, buildings
2167
maintained at low temperatures relative to outdoor temperatures (refrigerated warehouses, ice arenas,
2168
etc) for reduced infiltration and control of condensation and mold growth and buildings in areas with
2169
poor outdoor air quality to control the infiltration of the outdoor air contaminants.
2170
2171
Buildings in cold climates are typically designed for a neutral pressure. If the building were positively
2172
pressurized in a cold climate, it must be noted that the resulting exfiltration of relatively moist air during
2173
the heating season can cause condensation, mold in the building envelope and building deterioration
2174
problems. Similarly, humid spaces (e.g., natatoriums, shower rooms, spas, kitchens, indoor gardens,
2175
humidified buildings or areas such as health care facilities, museums, and musical instrument storage
2176
and performance areas) are of extra concern in cold climates and need to be slightly negative in pressure
2177
relative to outdoors to reduce the risk of condensation and mold in the building envelope. A discussion
2178
on mixed climates can found in the Assessment section under Climatological Requirements (see
2179
Roadmap Table for links to section).
2180
2181
Buildings are often treated as if they are one large compartment. In reality the one large compartment is
2182
typically comprised of many smaller compartments or spaces. The static pressures will differ from one
2183
space to another due to stack effect, changing wind direction, climate changes, HVAC system operation,
2184
etc. It is important that these factors be considered for proper compartmentalization and/or HVAC
2185
system control and system segregation. For example having the building positively pressurized overall
2186
does not mean every perimeter space is positively pressurized, especially if the HVAC system utilizes a
2187
return air plenum and the return air is not ducted to each space. These return plenums create bands of
2188
negative pressure on each floor.
2189
2190
When designing for proper building pressurization, the envelope leakage is often overlooked. The
2191
amount of envelope leakage can drastically change the required outside air (supply air) quantity to
2192
maintain a positively pressured building. For example, using typical air leakage rates for office
2193
building envelopes, values per unit wall area at 0.30 in. of water range from 0.10, 0.30 and 0.60 cfm/ft2
2194
for tight, average, and leaky walls respectively (Tamura and Shaw 1976a).
2195
2196
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 68
Return to TOP
The simple assumption that outside air quantity exceeds exhaust air quantity typically does not assure
2197
that the building will be positively pressurized. Envelope leakage, wind load, climatological
2198
requirements, building size/dimensions, building orientation, compartmentalization, and building usage
2199
must all be assessed. The more complex the building including layout, building height, architectural
2200
features, HVAC system type and usage, etc, the more difficult proper building pressurization attainment
2201
will be.
2202
2203
In addition to the proper volume of air being provided for proper building pressurization, the distribution
2204
of the air within the building spaces must be addressed. The makeup air must be provided in the correct
2205
areas or spaces to help overcome depressurization due to stack effect and/or wind effect.
2206
2207
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
2208
2209
2210
2211
2212
2213

Page 69
Return to TOP

2215

2216

Strategy 2.4 – Control Indoor Humidity
2227
2228
Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
F
E
How-to
•
Principles of Indoor Condensation
o
What can go wrong?
9
pg #’s
•
Integrated Design Process
9
pg #’s
•
