9
pg #’s
o
Small Packaged Systems
9
pg #’s
o
No Humidity Controller Needed
9
pg #’s
•
Special Spaces
9
pg #’s
•
Dedicated Dehumidification systems
9
pg #’s
•
Humidity Design Considerations for Specific Spaces
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2229
2230
Overview Information
Principles of indoor condensation
2231
It may be common to say that the dew falls, but more correctly, it condenses from the surrounding air.
2232
This happens because cold air can hold less water in vapor form than hot air. When air cools enough, it
2233
can no longer hold all its water vapor, and the vapor turns back into a liquid (condenses). The
2234
temperature at which this happens is a called the dew point and may occur on a cold surface
2235
2236
Every air condition has a unique dew point temperature, and when the air is cooled to this temperature,
2237
condensation will result. The psychrometric chart (called "psych" chart for short) is a two-dimensional
2238
graph of air conditions that can be very useful for analyzing, visualizing and understanding moist air
2239
conditions and processes. Use of the chart is discussed in the Principles of Indoor Condensation section
2240
of the detailed information in Chapter 4.
2241
2242
What can go wrong and how to solve it
2243
Conditions that can lead to moisture problems include hot humid climates, especially if space pressure is
2244
not controlled, large indoor moisture sources, unusually cold surfaces, continuous ventilation with non-
2245
continuous conditioning and changes in temperature and humidity indoors, such as when systems are
2246
turned off. Each of these can contribute to the risk of condensation by increasing indoor humidity in the
2247
presence of surfaces below the dew point.
2248
2249
Air conditioning system designers often choose indoor conditions like 50 or 60% relative humidity and
2250
design a system to handle the peak sensible load. However, more frequently, systems operate at part
2251
load when they may not do enough dehumidification. ASHRAE Standard 62.1 now requires that
2252
designers consider the dehumidification performance of the system at a “humidity challenge” condition
2253
with high latent, low sensible load.
2254
2255
Indoor humidity can be controlled in many designs without dehumidifiers or humidity sensors, by using
2256
strategies such as dedicated outdoor air systems, energy recovery ventilation, hot gas reheat and variable
2257
volume control (VAV), as opposed to cycling control or temperature reset. In the latter strategy, varying
2258
air flow through the cooling coil in order to control capacity instead of raising the coil discharge
2259
temperature retains the moisture removal capacity of the system at part load.
2260
2261
Some of these strategies may not be available with smaller packaged cooling units, and may be available
2262
with an upgrade of the HVAC equipment or a change of the system type.
2263
2264
2265
2266
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 71
Return to TOP
Dedicated dehumidification systems
2267
Dedicated humidity control systems may be required in spaces that are underground, in swimming and
2268
bathing areas, kitchens, or where large volumes of unconditioned humid outdoor air enters, for instance
2269
by door opening or other forms of infiltration. Dehumidifiers may be based on the refrigerant cycle or
2270
use a desiccant. The latter is a material that is hygroscopic (attracts water) that passively removes water
2271
vapor from an air stream and then must be regenerated by heating to drive off the water.
2272
2273
In showers, natatoriums and cooking areas, it is accepted that humidity will be high, condensation will
2274
occur and consequently surfaces are inorganic and cleanable. Moisture can be kept out of less moisture-
2275
tolerant parts of the building by keeping spaces with high humidity at negative pressure.
2276
2277
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4

2279
To be added

Strategy 2.5 – Select Suitable Materials, Equipment and Assemblies for Unavoidably
2300
Wet Areas
2301
Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
F
E
How-to
•
Indoor Areas Subject to Repeated Wetting
9
pg #’s
•
Problems Associated with Wet Materials
9
pg #’s
•
Materials susceptible to moisture damage
9
pg #’s
•
Selection of Moisture-resistant Materials
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C= Contingencies
2302
2303
Overview Information
As stated by Latta in 1962: “the harmful effects of water on building materials can hardly be over-
2304
emphasized” and without this influence “the construction of durable buildings would be greatly
2305
simplified”. More recently, the concept of the “4Ds” of moisture control in buildings was advanced
2306
(CMHC 1998): Deflection (don’t let the moisture in), Drainage (give moisture a means of escape),
2307
Drying (facilitate air movement/breathing to remove moisture), and Durability (mold and corrosion
2308
resistance of materials susceptible to wetting).
2309
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 72
Return to TOP
2310
Strategies 2.1 through 2.4 of this guide focus on building system design to accomplish the first three D’s.
2311
The best design efforts cannot prevent occasional wetting incidents, and certain indoor activities
2312
intentionally lead to damp conditions and materials. The main focus of this Strategy thus lies with the
2313
durability aspect of materials subjected to periodic wetting episodes within buildings. This section will
2314
identify building areas that may be subject to repeated wetting, review the properties of materials that
2315
enable moisture-resistance or tolerance, and finally describe those materials suitable for wet locations.
2316
2317
This topic is an important one in the area of IAQ, as witnessed by growing concern regarding the impact
2318
of damp spaces on occupant health, and specifically the involvement of damp materials in this regard.
2319
This is highlighted in the recent report on Damp Indoor Spaces and Health (IOM, 2004) which
2320
concludes that “studies should be conducted to evaluate the effect of the duration of moisture damage of
2321
materials and its possible influence on occupant health and to evaluate the effectiveness of various
2322
changes in building designs, construction methods, operation, and maintenance in reducing dampness
2323
problems”.
2324
• Warning regarding materials which combine moisture resistant and non-resistant layers (e.g., vinyl-
2325
coated wallboard) highly susceptible to mould growth when subjected to wetting
2326
• Avoid use of suspended ceilings in zones with high humidity (unconditioned space susceptible to
2327
condensation)
2328
2329
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4

Figure 2.5-A [Concept: Highlight washroom/locker area with Magnifying glass revealing
2331
blistered paint on wall and Janitor w wet-mop]

Plants as Air Cleaners
C

•
Plants and Moisture
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2350
2351
Overview Information
There are potential advantages and disadvantages associated with the presence of plants (e.g., green
2352
roofs, living facades, living walls, etc.) as a component of the building envelope, and on walls or other
2353
locations in the occupied space. For example, as part of their physiology, plants emit water molecules
2354
into the air (process of transpiration) thus providing for evaporative cooling but also elevating the
2355
relative humidity. There is speculation that plants reduce levels of some pollutants such as VOCs.
2356
However, there is also literature showing that large numbers of indoor plants elevate the levels of mold
2357
spores in the air. Finally, the moisture needed for plant growth (e.g., in green roofs, in living walls, and
2358
in potted soil) is also sufficient to allow fungi and bacteria to grow in the same substrate or building
2359
material (See Photo #1).
2360
2361
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
2362
2363
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 74
Return to TOP
2364
Case Study Example
2365
2366
Figure 2.6-A A tree sapling grows from the upper portion of a window. The roots of this plant are
2367
obtaining moisture (see discussion on water activity) and nutrients from the envelope infrastructure.
2368
(Photo courtesy of Phil Morey)
2369
2370
2371
2372
2373
2374
2375
2376
2377
2378
2379
2380
2381
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 75
Return to TOP
OBJECTIVE 3 – LIMIT ENTRY OF OUTDOOR CONTAMINANTS
2382
2383
2384
Limiting the entry of outdoor contaminants is key to establishing good control of indoor air quality.
2385
Outdoor contaminants may include particles or gases in outdoor air, contaminants in the soil, and
2386
pesticides and landscaping chemicals applied around the building. These contaminants may be
2387
introduced into the building through a variety of pathways, including envelope penetrations
2388
(doors/windows), HVAC intakes, unintended infiltration due to negative building pressures, and
2389
occupant activities.
2390
2391
In many areas of the U.S., outdoor air pollution exceeds the National Ambient Air Quality Standards
2392
(NAAQS) established by the Environmental Protection Agency. Even in areas where air quality is
2393
generally good, pollution may be high at specific sites or during certain periods due to local sources.
2394
The site and building can also include sources of airborne contaminants such as vehicle exhaust from
2395
on-site access roads and parking facilities, building exhaust from toilet rooms, kitchens, labs, etc., and
2396
cooling towers. These contaminants enter buildings in ventilation air and through infiltration.
2397
2398
Where contaminants in outdoor air are present in high concentrations, adopting best practices to reduce
2399
their entry can substantially improve IAQ. These practices include locating the building, its operable
2400
openings and outdoor air intakes to limit contaminant entry; using enhanced efficiency filtration to
2401
remove respirable particles; using gas phase air cleaning to remove gaseous contaminants; and
2402
maintaining positive building pressures to reduce infiltration of untreated contaminants.
2403
2404
Another important but not widely recognized source of contaminants is intrusion of gases or vapors
2405
from the soil. There are hundreds of thousands of contaminated or potentially contaminated sites in the
2406
U.S., and any project built on or near a previously used site should screen for potential vapor intrusion.
2407
Where soil contaminants cannot be removed or treated in-situ immediately, vapor intrusion can be
2408
reduced by installation of active soil depressurization systems combined with gas vapor barriers. Radon,
2409
a naturally occurring radioactive gas that is the second leading cause of lung cancer in the U.S., is
2410
another important contaminant in soils. Mitigation strategies for radon are very similar to those for
2411
vapor intrusion.
2412
2413
Additional best practices to control entry of outdoor contaminants include use of well-designed track-off
2414
systems at building entrances, building design and construction to exclude pests, and landscape design
2415
that minimizes the need to apply herbicides and pesticides near buildings.
2416
2417
Elements in the control of outdoor contaminants discussed in this Objective are:
2418
• Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outside Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
2419
• Strategy 3.2 – Control Entry of Radon
2420
• Strategy 3.3 – Control Intrusion of Vapors from Subsurface Contaminants
2421
• Strategy 3.4 – Provide Effective Track-off Systems at Building Entrances
2422
• Strategy 3.5 – Design and Build to Exclude Pests
2423
2424
To limit entry of outdoor contaminants; design, construction, and commissioning must also address
2425
issues discussed elsewhere in the guide including:
2426
• Chapter 2 Integrated Design and Construction Process for Good IAQ to select the site, locate the
2427
building on the site, and locate operable openings in a manner that limits introduction of outdoor
2428
contaminants.
2429
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 76
Return to TOP
• Strategy 7.4 Provide Particle Filtration and Gas Phase Air Cleaning Consistent with Project IAQ
2430
Objectives.
2431
• Strategy 2.3 Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
2432
• Strategy 4.5 Control Legionella in Water Systems
2433
• Strategy 2.6 Consider Impacts of Landscaping and Indoor Plants on Moisture and Contaminant
2434
Levels
2435
2436
2437
2438
2439
OBJECTIVE 3 GRAPHIC
2440
2441
2442
2443
2444
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 77
Return to TOP
Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outdoor Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
2445
2446
Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
F
E

ASHRAE Recommendations

pg #’s

9

9

9
pg #’s
•
Special Considerations for Packaged HVAC Units
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2447
2448
Overview Information
Outside air enters a building through its air intakes. In mechanically ventilated buildings, these air
2449
intakes are part of the HVAC system, and in naturally ventilated buildings these intakes can be either
2450
operable windows or other openings in the building’s envelope.
2451
2452
Research to date has shown that the quality of the outside air provided in a building greatly affects the
2453
quality of the indoor air. Therefore, besides evaluating the quality of the outdoor air in the area where a
2454
building is located, it is equally important to assess the presence of any local contaminant sources and
2455
design the location of the air intakes in such a way as to minimize re-entrainment of airborne pollutants
2456
emitted by these sources.
2457
2458
Mechanical codes (such as the IMC, IPC, UMC, and UPC) have some requirements for locations of
2459
building intakes. However, in most cases these requirements are very limited and do not reflect the
2460
“standard of care” that a designer should follow.
2461
2462
Table 5.1 of ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 lists minimum separation distances between air intakes and
2463
specific contamination sources. Although the list does not cover all possible sources, it does give the
2464
designer a guiding tool. Appendix F of Standard 62.1 allows the designer to calculate distances from
2465
sources other than the ones listed in Table 5.1. The distances listed in 5.1 should be considered as
2466
design minimums.
2467
2468
Outdoor air intakes should be located at least twenty five feet away from plume discharges and upwind
2469
of cooling towers, evaporative condensers and fluid coolers. In addition, outdoor air intakes should be
2470
located at least 15 feet away from intakes or basins of cooling towers, evaporative condensers and fluid
2471
coolers. Buildings designed with smaller separation distances greatly increase the liability of the
2472
designer and place the building occupants in immediate danger of contracting Legionnaires' Disease as
2473
well as various other health effects from inhaling pollutants emitted by the chemicals used to treat the
2474
cooling tower water. There is strong evidence both in the scientific literature as well as in the form of
2475
recommendations by expert organizations on separation distances between cooling towers and building
2476
air intakes.
2477
2478
All nearby potential odor or contaminant sources (such restaurant exhausts, emergency generators) and
2479
prevailing wind conditions need to be evaluated. Location of plumbing vents in relationship to outdoor
2480
air intakes in high-rise buildings may require additional analysis. Model Codes such as the IMC and the
2481
UMC, require a 3 to 10 foot separation distance between terminations of vents carrying non-explosive or
2482
flammable vapors, fumes or dusts and building air intakes. In the case of plumbing vents, the IPC and
2483
UPC require a 2 to 10 foot separation distance. For the healthcare industry, the Guidelines for Design
2484
and Construction of Health Care Facilities require separation distances of 25 feet between building
2485
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 78
Return to TOP
intakes and plumbing vents, exhaust outlets of ventilating systems, combustion equipment stacks, areas
2486
that may collect vehicular exhaust or other noxious fumes. However, these guidelines allow the 25 foot
2487
separation distance to be reduced to 10 feet if plumbing vents are terminated at a level above the top of
2488
the air intake.
2489
2490
Therefore it is clear that due to wind effects around buildings and multiple other local variables,
2491
establishing minimum separation distances for each source is impossible. Each design case must be
2492
evaluated based on local conditions and variables and the designer ultimately needs to exercise
2493
professional judgment. In some cases, advanced calculations and/or modeling may be required such as
2494
wind tunnel analyses with scale models, computer simulations, and CFD analyses.
2495
2496
In packaged HVAC units, the vent of the flue gases should be extended at least 10 inches or more above
2497
the top of the unit to minimize flue gas re-entrainment. In units where the intake and exhausts are in
2498
close proximity, dilution of building exhaust air in the economizer mode is significantly less than the
2499
dilution of flue gas in the heating mode. Packaged HVAC units should be located so that their air
2500
intakes and exhausts are directed away from large obstructions.
2501
2502
2503

2510
entrainment (shown is a close up of the indirect evaporative cooler at the outdoor air intake)

• O&M of Radon Control Systems

pg #’s
• Radon Measurements
C
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2524
2525
Overview Information
Radon is a radioactive gas formed from the decay of uranium in rock, soil and groundwater. Exposure
2526
to radon is the second leading cause of lung cancer in the U.S. after cigarette smoking, and is
2527
responsible for about 10 to 14% of lung cancer deaths (NAS 1999).
2528
2529
Radon most commonly enters buildings in soil gas that is drawn in through joints, cracks, penetrations
2530
or pores in concrete masonry units (CMUs) when the building is at negative pressure relative to ground.
2531
The potential for high radon levels varies regionally, with additional variation from building to building
2532
in the same region and even from room to room in the same building.
2533
2534
Design for control of radon entry includes three components:
2535
• Active soil depressurization (ASD). An ASD system uses a suction fan(s) to draw radon from the
2536
sub-slab and discharge it where it can be harmlessly diluted to background levels. Installation of a
2537
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 81
Return to TOP
permeable sub-slab layer (aggregate) allows the negative pressure field created by a given fan to
2538
extend over a greater sub-slab area.
2539
• Sealing of radon entry routes, including ground-contact joints, cracks and penetrations and below-
2540
grade CMU walls.
2541
• Use of HVAC systems to maintain positive building pressure in ground-contact rooms and to
2542
provide dilution ventilation.
2543
2544
The ASD system and sealing of radon entry routes are easier and cheaper to implement during
2545
construction than they are as a retrofit. In addition, many elements of a radon-control system are also
2546
useful to reduce intrusion of vapors from brownfield sites and to reduce penetration of liquid water and
2547
water vapor into below grade building assemblies.
2548
2549
The design team should review radon potential, synergies of radon control techniques with design for
2550
other IAQ issues, and new construction vs. retrofit costs with the owner to determine whether to
2551
implement radon control measures.
2552
2553
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
2554
2555
2556
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 82
Return to TOP
2557
Case Study Example
2558
2559
Figure 3.2-A
2560
2561
This 4-story owner-occupied office building is located in an urban community in the Southeastern U.S.
2562
The site has shallow topsoil above granite and is in an identified radon region. At the time of the
2563
investigation described, the building was eight years old. It has an open atrium lobby that penetrates all
2564
floors. Each floor is served by an autonomous air handler and ventilation and make-up air is introduced
2565
from the roof through a common shaft to each mechanical room. The return air on each floor flows to
2566
the lobby atrium, entering each floor’s mechanical room through transfer grilles from the lobby wall.
2567
The lower floors are open plan office space and the upper floor is devoted to the company cafeteria and
2568
the executive dining room. The lobby is decorated with architectural plantings including a large and
2569
valuable ficus tree several stories tall.
2570
2571
An investigation was undertaken in response to a complaint of regular, repeated occurrences of
2572
headaches and nausea reported by over 50% of the occupant population every other Friday afternoon
2573
after 2:00 p.m. Air testing conducted on a "problem" Friday revealed elevated VOC levels (above 5000
2574
μg/m3) and high fungal counts (greater than 3 times outdoor levels). The surprise to the investigators
2575
was that radon levels were also elevated to over 3 times the action level. At that time, the investigation
2576
team had reviewed the airflow data from specifications and the TAB report, but had taken no airflow
2577
measurements.
2578
2579
The report and findings were submitted by the investigation team two weeks later on another Friday
2580
afternoon. At this time, it was obvious that fish was being served in the cafeteria because the distinctive
2581
odor was present on all floors of the building, clearly migrating from the fourth floor kitchen. Based on
2582
this obvious evidence of contamination cross-over between floors, the kitchen area and operating
2583
practices were investigated. It was found that the kitchen closed at 2:00 p.m. on Friday afternoon, and
2584
every other Friday pesticide treatment was applied. The pesticide aerosol quickly migrated throughout
2585
the building because of the lobby atrium’s negative pressurization and was drawn onto all floors in the
2586
return air. The negative atrium pressure was also depressurizing the soil in which the Ficus tree was
2587
planted. The team learned that the root system penetrated the concrete slab because of the size of the
2588
tree. Thus, there was no air barrier between the soil and the space. The depressurization and lack of an
2589
air barrier was drawing radon into the building. The soil was also the source of the high fungal counts.
2590
2591
Once the causes of these problems were determined, they were easily solved through:
2592
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 83
Return to TOP
-- addition of localized exhaust in the kitchen with integral make-up air,
2593
-- rescheduling of pesticide application to off-hours,
2594
-- provision of additional fan powered outdoor air delivered directly to each floor’s mechanical room,
2595
-- balancing of outdoor air quantities to deliver a larger quantity to the ground floor zone.
2596
2597
The atrium lobby pressure relative to outside was about -50 to – 60 Pa before the modifications and
2598
increased to 0 to 8 Pa after. This was sufficient to bring the level of radon right at the source (the ficus
2599
tree root area) down below the action level. The presenting problem of headaches and nausea related to
2600
the pesticide application was resolved as well. Had the building not had an IAQ problem that was
2601
causing acute symptoms, the elevated radon levels likely would never have been detected and resolve.
2602
2603
(Photos courtesy of Barney Burroughs)
2604
2605
2606
2607
2608
Strategy 3.3 – Control Intrusion of Vapors from Subsurface Contaminants
2609
2610

pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2611
2612
Overview Information
What is Vapor Intrusion? Vapor intrusion is “the migration of a …[chemical of concern] vapor from
2613
a subsurface soil or groundwater source into the indoor environment of an existing or planned structure"
2614
(ASTM 2008). Although vapors can intrude into buildings from naturally occurring subsurface gases,
2615
most guidance, standards and regulations are specific to vapors from environmental contaminants.
2616
Contaminants can be present below ground due to accidental spills, improper disposal, leaking landfills
2617
or leaking underground or above ground storage tanks (Tillman & Weaver 2005). Some of the most
2618
widespread present and past land uses associated with chemicals of concern include gas stations, dry
2619
cleaners, former industrial sites that used vapor degreasers or other parts cleaning chemicals, and
2620
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 84
Return to TOP
manufactured gas plants (ASTM 2008). There are hundreds of thousands of contaminated or potentially
2621
contaminated sites in the U.S. (EPA 2002). Vapor intrusion can occur even though a site has undergone
2622
or is undergoing remediation. Moreover, the potential for vapor intrusion is not limited to the sites
2623
where contaminants were originally released, since contaminants reaching the water table can travel at
2624
least several miles in contaminated groundwater plumes, and contaminant vapor can travel shorter
2625
distances through the vadose zone above the water table.
2626
2627
Why Is It a Concern? Vapor intrusion is a concern primarily because of the potential for chronic
2628
health effects from long term exposure to low contaminant concentrations, although in extreme cases
2629
vapor concentrations can be high enough to cause acute health effects or explosion hazards (EPA 2002).
2630
The chemicals of concern for vapor intrusion are those that are sufficiently volatile to migrate through
2631
the soil as a vapor and sufficiently toxic that they may adversely affect human health (EPA 2002).
2632
These include many volatile organic compounds, some semi-volatile organic compounds, and some
2633
inorganic substances such as mercury.
2634
2635
Standards, Guidance and Regulations: The potential for intrusion of vapors from these sites into
2636
buildings was not widely recognized by U.S. regulators until the 1990s (IRTC 2007) and both the
2637
science and the regulatory environment are still evolving. ASTM (2008) recently develop a national
2638
standard for assessment of vapor intrusion on properties involved in real estate transactions. The
2639
standard uses a tiered approach to allow properties with a low risk of vapor intrusion to be screened out
2640
quickly and at relatively low cost. The Interstate Technology & Regulatory Council published guidance
2641
on both assessment and mitigation in 2007.
2642
2643
Screening and assessment of the potential for vapor intrusion must follow the regulations, policies and
2644
guidance of the relevant jurisdiction and must be performed by a qualified professional, typically an
2645
environmental consultant outside the primary design team. Obtaining guidance from the appropriate
2646
regulatory agency early in the process is the best way to avoid problems and assure a successful project.
2647
2648
Site Remediation: In the long term, the best remedy for contaminated sites is to remove the
2649
contaminant source or to perform treatments in place to reduce contaminant levels. Some remediation
2650
techniques may reduce or eliminate soil gas migration substantially in a relatively short time, rendering
2651
building mitigation measures unnecessary. Some other technologies are longer term strategies that may
2652
take many years to reduce the contaminant source to a level where vapor intrusion is no longer a concern.
2653
In still other cases, cleanup may be on hold for various reasons. Where longer term cleanup strategies
2654
are used or cleanup is on hold, institutional controls and/or building mitigation are necessary as interim
2655
strategies.
2656
2657
Institutional Control: Institutional controls are “legally enforceable conditions placed on a property to
2658
reduce the likelihood of exposure to unacceptable levels of contaminants…” (ASTM 2008). They may
2659
include such measures as zoning restrictions, requirements that vapor intrusion mitigation systems be
2660
preemptively installed in new construction, requirements that source remediation systems or building
2661
mitigation systems be periodically inspected or requirements that contaminant levels be periodically
2662
monitored. (ITRC 2007).
2663
2664
Building Mitigation Systems: Building mitigation systems are systems that reduce intrusion of vapors
2665
into buildings.
2666
2667
• Qualifications for Design and Installation: These systems must be designed and installed by parties
2668
who meet the qualifications of the regulatory agency having jurisdiction and who have relevant
2669
expertise. Very few design teams or contractors have this expertise in-house, so most rely on
2670
specialized environmental consultants and mitigation contractors. Requirements for design,
2671
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 85
Return to TOP
installation and performance testing of vapor intrusion mitigation systems vary widely by jurisdiction,
2672
so it is important to involve the appropriate regulatory agency early in the design process.
2673
2674
• When to Address VI: Where the need for mitigation is a possibility, it is cheaper and more effective
2675
to incorporate the system in the original design and construction, rather than retrofit it after the
2676
building is built and vapor concentrations are tested. The need for vapor intrusion control should be
2677
considered early in design, when other design elements may be modified to potentially eliminate the
2678
need or reduce its cost.
2679
2680
• Comparison with Radon Control: Vapor intrusion mitigation technologies are largely adapted from
2681
radon control strategies. Bringing indoor vapor concentrations below action levels commonly
2682
requires reductions of two or three orders of magnitude (and sometimes more), in contrast to the one
2683
to two orders of magnitude typically required to bring radon concentrations below action levels. To
2684
achieve these larger reductions, it may be necessary to combine several mitigation techniques or to
2685
design and install systems to exacting standards.
2686
2687
• Active Soil Depressurization (ASD): Active soil depressurization (sub-slab or sub-membrane) is
2688
generally the most reliable vapor intrusion control technology. ASD systems use one or more suction
2689
fans to depressurize the soil in contact with the building. This assures that the predominant direction
2690
of air leakage across those portions of the building envelope in contact with the ground is from the
2691
building into the soil, rather than from the soil into the building. Soil gas is drawn through vent risers
2692
by the suction fan(s) and released above the building where it can be diluted by ambient air. ASD
2693
systems should be combined with sealing of joints, cracks and penetrations and sealing of below
2694
grade walls. In new construction, ASD is usually combined with two additional elements to enhance
2695
its effectiveness: a “venting layer” of gas-permeable aggregate and/or a network of perforated pipe;
2696
and a gas vapor barrier above the venting layer. Where the amount of vapor intrusion that will occur
2697
is uncertain, it can make sense to install a passive venting system with a gas vapor barrier and add the
2698
fan(s) to convert to ASD if post-construction testing shows it to be necessary. Passive systems
2699
should always be designed to be readily converted to active systems. In general, use of gas vapor
2700
barriers alone without passive venting is not recommended.
2701
2702
• Building Pressurization: Building pressurization may be the best choice for sites with wet or low
2703
permeability soils where ASD is not effective. It may also be a viable alternative to ASD for other
2704
buildings if the HVAC system has the capability to reliably maintain positive pressures in all ground-
2705
contact rooms. However, pressurization for vapor intrusion control requires a level of quality
2706
assurance in design, construction, operation and maintenance that goes beyond that associated with
2707
building pressurization control for most other purposes. It is also likely to increase energy use more
2708
than an ASD system. In cases where the building owner is not the party legally responsible for
2709
mitigation, the owner may be reluctant to employ the HVAC system for vapor intrusion mitigation
2710
control. For these reasons, building pressurization has not been widely used as a vapor intrusion
2711
mitigation strategy to date (Folkes, pers. comm.. 2008).
2712
2713
• Other Techniques: Positive sub-slab pressurization, indoor air treatment and “intrinsically safe
2714
building design” may be viable alternative or supplemental mitigation techniques in certain
2715
circumstances.
2716
2717
2718
2719
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 86
Return to TOP
2720
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
2721
2722
2723
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 87
Return to TOP
2724
Case Study Example
Genzyme Headquarters at Kendall Square: Cambridge, MA
2725
2726
2727
Figure 3.3-A
2728
2729
Kendall Square is a 10 acre mixed use development consisting of approximately 1.3 million sq. ft. of
2730
new construction combined with two underground parking structures. The primary focus of the
2731
development is life science offices and R&D. The development is located within the limits of a former
2732
Manufactured Gas Plant (MGP) site, making the project one of the largest brownfield redevelopments
2733
undertaken in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. (EPA estimates that there are 3,000 to 5,000 sites of
2734
former manufactured gas plants in the U.S.).
2735
2736
The remediation program consisted of a combination of mass excavation with off-site disposal and in
2737
situ stabilization of the MGP residual wastes. The primary contaminants of concern (COC) were sub-
2738
surface coal tars and related products. The majority of the remediation was completed in the fall of
2739
2001.
2740
2741
Two multi-level life science buildings were later constructed, each approximately 300,000 sq. ft. The
2742
buildings are located within an area of the site that was remediated using in situ stabilization, so some
2743
residual organic compounds are present in the sub-surface soils. To mitigate the threat of migration of
2744
COC's into the buildings, the environmental engineers for the site designed a comprehensive vapor
2745
control system including a state of the art low profile venting system and an impermeable vapor
2746
membrane beneath the slab. Over 75,000 sq. ft. of membrane was applied by a manufacturer-certified
2747
installer. Use of a spray-applied membrane simplified the difficult attachments onto the numerous grade
2748
beams, footings, pile caps, electrical conduits, VCS pipes and plumbing pipes
2749
2750
Case study courtesy of CETCO Liquid Boot Co
2751
2752
2753
2754
2755
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 88
Return to TOP
2756
Strategy 3.4 – Provide Track-off Systems at Entrances
2757
2758
2759
Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
F
E
How-to
•
Contaminants Tracked into Buildings by Occupants
9
pg #’s

Paving
9

pg #’s

9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2760
2761
Overview Information
The best way to reduce the need for building cleaning (and therefore minimize the generation indoors of
2762
contaminants associated with cleaning products) is to prevent dirt from getting into the building.
2763
Installation of effective dirt track-off systems at all entranceways (including those from loading docks,
2764
receiving areas and garage entrances) is therefore an essential component of a building’s IAQ strategy.
2765
2766
Track-off systems start with the approach to building entrances. In order to facilitate dirt removal from
2767
footwear, textured paving should be selected rather than smooth surfaces (City of New York, 1999).
2768
Any plantings bordering walkways should not drop flowers or berries that can be tracked into the
2769
building.
2770
2771
Proper design of dirt track-off/entry mat systems must include specific combinations of mat materials,
2772
textures, and lengths. General guidance is shown below
2773
(http://www.epa.gov/iaq/schooldesign/controlling.html#Entry%20Mat%20Barriers, US EPA, accessed
2774
July 21, 2008)
2775
2776
Table 3.4-A Dirt Track-off / Entry Mat System Specifications
2777
Scraper Mat
Absorption Mat
Finishing Mat
Minimum of 6 feet long, and
located outside the entry
doors. Removes the bulk of
dirt and snow with some
form of knobby or
squeegee-like projections.
Generally the higher the
projection, the better the
cleaning and holding
capacity. Surface mats are
typically made of nitrile
rubber. Recessed grating
systems contain a deep well
that will hold more dirt and
moisture between cleanings,
but will have a higher first
cost.
Minimum of 6 feet long, and
located just inside the entry
doors. Generally made from
nylon or combinations of
nylon and heavily textured
piles of polypropylene that
can perform both a scraping
action and a moisture
wicking action.
Minimum of 8 feet long, and
follows immediately after
the absorption mat.
Generally made from
polypropylene with a course
fiber surface that will both
capture and hold any
remaining particles or
moisture.
2778
2779
Entry mat system design for a particular building should be tailored to the climate zone:
2780
• Snowy climates require greater lengths of scraper mat;
2781
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 89
Return to TOP
• Rainy locations need longer adsorption mats, and
2782
• Muddy locations need more of all mat types (US EPA Tools for Schools).
2783

2834
OBJECTIVE 4 – CONTROL MOISTURE AND CONTAMINANTS RELATED TO
2835
MECHANICAL SYSTEMS
2836
2837
2838
Mechanical systems play an important role in providing good indoor air quality (IAQ) through
2839
ventilation, air cleaning, and comfort conditioning. However, since many mechanical systems carry
2840
water or become wet in operation, they can also amplify and distribute microbial contaminants. In
2841
addition they can be sources of particulate contaminants. Proper design and construction reduce the risk
2842
that mechanical systems will cause IAQ problems.
2843
2844
Moisture and dirt in air handling systems provide an environment for microbial growth. Air handling
2845
systems should exclude moisture or promptly drain it away. Outdoor air intakes should be located and
2846
louvers designed to limit entry of rain and snow. Drains should remove rain and snow that do enter.
2847
Cooling coils and humidifiers should be designed to avoid carryover of liquid moisture beyond the drain
2848
pan. Drain pans should be sloped to drain moisture away promptly and should be made of corrosion-
2849
resistant materials. Traps should be designed to provide sufficient water depth to drain properly against
2850
fan static pressure. Filters should be located where they will not be wetted by entrained rain or snow,
2851
cooling coil condensate, or moisture from humidifiers. Porous duct linings should not be used in areas
2852
with any moisture sources. The potential for microbial growth can be significantly reduced by using
2853
higher efficiency filtration (MERV 13 or higher) to keep systems clean.
2854
2855
Airstream surfaces should be smooth and non-porous. Porous interior duct insulation and other rough
2856
or porous surfaces should be avoided or limited to areas where necessary for acoustical attenuation.
2857
Porous or rough materials accumulate dirt, providing sites for microbial growth, and cannot be cleaned.
2858
Materials that can be eroded should not be used in air handling systems, since they can be sources of
2859
particulate contaminants.
2860
2861
Condensation on exterior surfaces of mechanical systems should be avoided. Air handlers and
2862
ductwork carrying cold air and piping carrying chilled water or service water should have insulation and
2863
vapor barriers sufficient to prevent condensation. Design should consider humidity levels that may
2864
occur during unoccupied hours or in chaseways, plenums and exterior building cavities, as well as those
2865
that occur during occupied hours in usable space.
2866
2867
Heating, cooling and service water piping and fixtures should be designed, assembled and tested to
2868
reduce potential for leaks.
2869
2870
Sufficient access should be provided to allow periodic inspection, cleaning and repair of air handlers,
2871
ductwork, and piping systems.
2872
2873
Cooling towers and evaporative condensers should be located downwind of and as far as possible from
2874
outside air intakes, operable windows, and outdoor public areas. In addition, they should be located
2875
away from sources of organic matter that could serve as a food source for Legionella. Mechanical
2876
filtration devices should be provided to help remove dirt and organic matter from the cold water basin.
2877
The system should be designed to facilitate regular water treatment, and the basin and drift eliminators
2878
should be designed to facilitate regular visual inspection and cleaning. Service hot water (SHW) storage
2879
tanks and distribution systems should be kept at temperatures high enough to minimize growth of
2880
Legionella. Distribution systems should also be designed to reduce areas where lukewarm water can
2881
stagnate and provide an environment favorable to its multiplication. SHW systems should be designed
2882
to enable periodic thermal or chemical disinfection.
2883
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 92
Return to TOP
2884
These issues are discussed in the following Strategies:
2885
• Strategy 4.1 – Control Moisture and Dirt in Air Handling Systems
2886
• Strategy 4.2 – Select and Install Airstream Surfaces that are Resistant to Erosion and Deposition
2887
• Strategy 4.3 – Control Moisture Associated with Piping, Plumbing Fixtures, and Ductwork
2888
• Strategy 4.4 – Facilitate Access to HVAC Equipment for Inspection, Cleaning and Maintenance
2889
• Strategy 4.5 – Control Legionella in Water Systems
2890
2891
A number of Strategies discussed under other Objectives are also important in preventing IAQ problems
2892
related to mechanical systems.
2893
o Dirt and moisture should be kept out of air handling systems during construction to prevent
2894
contamination that may cause IAQ problems upon occupancy.
2895
o Outdoor air intakes should be located to limit introduction of dirt. Intakes higher on the building are
2896
generally preferable to intakes at or below grade in this regard.
2897
o Higher efficiency filters reduce dirt in air handling systems and thereby reduce food sources for
2898
microorganisms, reducing the risk that the air handling system will be a source of IAQ problems.
2899
o The potential for condensation on plumbing fixtures containing cold water (e.g., toilets) should be
2900
anticipated and potential for microbial growth limited by using adjacent finish materials that can
2901
withstand repeated wetting and are easy to clean.
2902
o Envelope systems should be properly designed and constructed and building pressurization
2903
maintained to limit penetration of humid outdoor air into building cavities where moisture could
2904
condense on cool surfaces, including ductwork and cold water piping.
2905
2906
These issues are discussed in the following Strategies:
2907
• Strategy 1.4 – Manage Construction and Renovation to Preserve IAQ
2908
• Strategy 7.5 – Provide Particle Filtration and Gas Phase Air Cleaning Consistent with Project IAQ
2909
Objectives
2910
• Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outside Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
2911
• Strategy 2.5 – Select Materials, Equipment, and Assemblies that Can Withstand Repeated Wettings in
2912
Areas that are Unavoidably Wet
2913
• Strategy 2.2 – Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces
2914
• Strategy 2.3 – Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
2915
• Strategy 1.5 – Provide High Quality O&M Documentation and Training Focused on IAQ
2916
2917
2918
2919
2920
2921

2929
Strategy 4.1 – Control Moisture and Dirt in Air Handling Systems
2930
2931

2932
2933
Overview Information
Fungi and bacteria are normally present on most interior surfaces in buildings including surfaces in
2934
HVAC system components. These microorganisms become problematic to IAQ when they amplify or
2935
grow on surfaces with growth sometimes becoming manifest visually. The growth of microorganisms in
2936
HVAC systems can result in malodors, building related symptoms (e.g., nasal and throat irritation) and
2937
in rare cases building related illnesses such as humidifier fever and hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
2938
2939
The growth of fungi and bacteria in HVAC components is often associated with poor design for
2940
moisture control and with build-up of dust and dirt (food for microorganisms on damp or wet surfaces).
2941
Implementation of design strategies that limit moisture and dirt accumulation in HVAC components will
2942
lessen the risk of fungal and bacterial growth on HVAC component surfaces.
2943
2944
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 95
Return to TOP
2945
Graphical Roadmap to Detail Information in Chapter 4
2946
2947
2948
Case Study Example
2949
Figure 4.1-A This air handling unit was poorly designed and maintained.
2950
Access to the pan was achieved only after removal of more than 10 fasteners.
2951
The drain pan outlet was not flush with the bottom of the pan.
2952
Smooth and Cleanable
Surfaces

Return to TOP
The tan-yellow mass in the pan is a biofilm consisting of a gelatinous mass of bacteria,
2953
fungi, and protozoa. This plenum was opened up for inspection and cleaning because
2954
of malodor complaints from occupants in zones being provided with ventilation air from
2955
the air-handling unit. (Photo courtesy of Phil Morey)
2956
2957

Strategy 4.2 – Select and Install Airstream Surfaces that are Resistant to Erosion and
2962

Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
2964
2965
Overview Information
In any air handling system it is common and expected that cleaning and filtration of the air will be
2966
applied to the maximum extent physically and economically possible. However, even though the air is
2967
cleaned and filtered at the air handling unit as much as possible, the air that is finally delivered to the
2968
spaces within the building can only be as clean as the interior of the duct systems through which it
2969
travels.
2970
2971
Air duct systems can be constructed from a number of different materials. Regardless of the material
2972
that is used to construct the air distribution path, the surfaces of these air duct systems present numerous
2973
places for air to contact dirt and dust particles as well as bacteria and other microbial organisms. Any air
2974
system should be designed and installed to minimize the number of places in joints, bends and fittings
2975
where dirt, moisture and other particles can accumulate. The surfaces of the system that distributed air
2976
can contact should be smooth and as free as possible of uneven and porous areas. Before any system is
2977
put into use all interior surfaces should be thoroughly cleaned of dirt and surface oils. Air duct systems
2978
should then be cleaned at regular intervals as part of a preventative maintenance program.
2979
2980
Duct systems that are constructed of metal (typically, steel or aluminum) are usually insulated, either
2981
externally or as an internal liner, by flexible or rigid board thermal insulation materials. Internal
2982
insulation is typically used to provide enhanced acoustical noise control from the air systems. However,
2983
because of the potential of additional sources of contamination to the airstream from these materials
2984
when installed internally, the external insulation is preferred. If the use of internal duct liner is
2985
unavoidable care should be taken at every step of its design and installation to reduce the potential of the
2986
liner to contribute to airstream contamination. The proper material should be selected. Closed cell foam,
2987
or a rigid thermal board material, is currently thought to be less susceptible to deterioration than
2988
insulation made of fiberglass. In any material, however, the surfaces should be as smooth as possible,
2989
and the surface should be coated to reduce porous areas of the surface and provide an anti-microbial
2990
material to control the growth of fungi and bacteria.
2991
2992
All liner should be installed following good design specifications, applicable Standards and Guidelines
2993
from SMACNA and NAIMA, and all manufacturers’ instructions. All edges should be sealed and all
2994
gaps between sections of insulation should be eliminated.
2995
2996

3014
3015
Overview Information
Because of condensation, mold growth may occur on cold water piping or cold air supply ducts with
3016
inadequate thermal insulation or a failed vapor retarder. Liquid water from condensation drips can
3017
damage materials nearby such as ceiling tiles, composite wooden materials, and paper-faced wallboard
3018
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 98
Return to TOP
located below or adjacent to the piping or ducts. Leaks from poorly designed plumbing within walls or
3019
risers may go unnoticed until damage including mold growth becomes evident in occupied spaces.
3020
Implementation of design strategies that limit condensation on cold water piping and ducts and that
3021
reduces the likelihood of leaks from piping hidden in building infrastructure, will lessen the risk of mold
3022
growth in buildings.
3023
3024
3025

3026
3027
3028

3031
location. Mold growth is present on pipe jacketing. Condensation was associated with the
3032
infiltration of warm humid air into above ceiling spaces and resulting unexpectedly high dew
3033
points in this unconditioned space. (Photo courtesy of Phil Morey)

3040
Maintenance

Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
3043
3044
Condensation drips
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 100
Return to TOP
Overview Information
Access for periodic inspection, routine maintenance, and cleaning of air handling equipment is essential
3045
for maintaining acceptable indoor air quality. Assessing “soiling” of the HVAC system, monitoring the
3046
effectiveness of air-cleaning, and validating the performance of monitors and sensors has become an
3047
increasingly important aspect of building operation and maintenance. It is important to conserve
3048
(assure) access to HVAC system components just like access to the electrical panels and components are
3049
protected. Failure to design and construct systems that facilitate access may significantly compromise
3050
indoor air quality. It is well recognized that routine maintenance can be significantly compromised by
3051
above ceiling installations or locations requiring ladders or other equipment for access. In addition,
3052
systems that can be accessed without special tools are more likely to receive the appropriate inspection
3053
and maintenance.
3054
3055
Access considerations begin with the design of the mechanical room to accommodate the AHU and
3056
other HVAC system components. Design and selection of the HVAC system itself can limit access and
3057
maintenance problems. For example, choosing a central system rather than a multiple fan-coil system
3058
reduces the components needing access for inspection and maintenance. The mechanical room design
3059
must position the HVAC system components to assure ease of access. Low ceilings, cramped spaces, or
3060
difficult access will inhibit appropriate access and maintenance. This must be avoided. It is also
3061
important recognize that access doors to the mechanical room can impact performance by allowing the
3062
introduction of local contaminants. Access doors should remain closed except as required.
3063
3064
3065
Figure 4.4-B Typical access points at the air-handling unit. (Source: 62.1 User’s Manual)
3066
3067
AHSRAE Standard 62.1-2007 provides fairly comprehensive requirements for assuring appropriate
3068
access to all components of the HVAC system including the air-handling unit, air distribution system,
3069
controllers, and sensors. In addition, most manufacturers provide guidance on the required service
3070
clearances. It is essential that such clearances take into account door swings and space for personnel to
3071
access, stand, and move tools and materials out. Failure to provide appropriate access can result in
3072
inadequate maintenance of the system.
3073
3074
Even with the best designs, appropriate access can be compromised during the construction and
3075
installation of any or all of the HVAC system components. In addition, installation of other building
3076
systems, such as plumbing, electrical systems, and telecommunication systems, can result in obstruction
3077
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 101
Return to TOP
that either complicate or obstruct direct access. Careful stewardship during the construction phase is
3078
essential to assuring that clear access is maintained.
3079
3080
Another risk associated with providing access to the HVAC system is the potential of air leakage from
3081
or into the system. Leakage of cooled air from the system can result in condensation, which can result
3082
in microbial growth within the HVAC system or on other building materials. Conversely, leakage on
3083
the negatively pressurized sections of the system can result in the infiltration of contaminants from the
3084
surrounding spaces and these contaminants may degrade the indoor air quality in the occupied spaces
3085
serviced by the system.
3086
3087
Any modifications or addition of access doors may also impact long-term performance and indoor air
3088
quality. For example, if new access doors are required for the remediation of mold growth within the
3089
system, these doors must be installed in conformance with SMACNA requirements to assure adequate
3090
prevention of leakage
3091
3092
3093
Case Study Example
3094
Figure 4.4-A
3095
3096
Activities by other trades can compromise access as in this example where the full
3097
opening of an appropriately sized access door is blocked by chill-water piping that was
3098
installed after the AHU
3099
3100
3101
3102

3111
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

3112
Overview Information
Legionella are bacteria normally present in aquatic environments such as rivers and lakes. These
3113
bacteria can also be present in man-made water systems, multiply or grow in these systems and cause
3114
illnesses known as Legionnaire’s disease and Pontiac Fever. Legionnaire’s disease is a lung infection
3115
caused by inhalation of mist or water droplets containing the bacterium. Legionnaire’s disease can be
3116
fatal. Approximately 18,000 cases of Legionnaire’s Disease occur annually in the United States (1).
3117
Case fatality rates are approximately 20% for community-acquired Legionnaire’s disease and 20-40%
3118
for hospital-acquired disease which translates into an annual fatality total of 4,000 to 5,000 (1,2).
3119
Legionella that can cause Legionnaire’s disease can grow in cooling towers, potable water systems
3120
including showers and sinks, whirlpool spas, humidifiers, vegetable misters, decorative water fountains,
3121
etc.
3122
3123
Graphical Roadmap to Detail Information in Chapter 4
3124
3125
3126
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 104
Return to TOP
3127
Case Study Example
3128
3129
3130
Figure 4.5-A The drift from this cooling tower travelled approximately 100 meters and was
3131
implicated as the source of Legionnaires disease among three people in a nearby building. The
3132
water in the cooling tower basin, which had been treated with biocide and appeared quite clean,
3133
was heavily colonized by Legionella pneumophila serogroup 1 (See additional case study details
3134
in Chapter 4). (Photo courtesy of J. Stout)
3135
3136
3137
3138
3139
3140
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 105
Return to TOP
3141
OBJECTIVE 5 – LIMIT CONTAMINANTS FROM INDOOR SOURCES
3142
3143
3144
Indoor air contaminants generated within buildings can be broken into two major classes: particles and
3145
gases. Often considered separately, they are actually inter-related: gas-phase pollutants can be absorbed
3146
onto particles (and potentially re-released); while certain volatiles can form fine particles in the presence
3147
of oxidizing agents.
3148
3149
Solid particles generated within buildings can emanate from equipment, such as printers and copiers;
3150
from occupants through clothing fiber, skin shed, coughing, and sneezing; and from erosion or wear of
3151
fibrous or fleecy materials, such as duct lining or carpeting. Sometimes particles and gases are emitted
3152
by a common source, such as cigarette smoking.
3153
3154
Gas phase indoor contaminants (both volatile and semi-volatile) can emanate from building materials,
3155
finishes, or furnishings; or from occupants (e.g., bioeffluents, fragrances) or indoor activities. They are
3156
typically present as complex mixtures. The adverse impact on IAQ can vary significantly from one
3157
compound (or mixture) to the next. Our understanding of these impacts is steadily evolving.
3158
3159
In addition, indoor particulate and gaseous contaminant loads can echo outdoor loads if negative
3160
building pressures allow significant infiltration or if filtration or gas-phase cleaning of outdoor air is
3161
inadequate.
3162
3163
Strategies for control of contaminants generated indoors include source control, capture and exhaust,
3164
filtration and air cleaning, and dilution ventilation. Source control is the focus of this Objective.
3165
Capture and exhaust, dilution ventilation, and filtration are discussed under:
3166
• Objective 6 – Capture and Exhaust Emissions from Building Equipment and Activities
3167
• Objective 7 – Reduce Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation and Air Cleaning
3168
3169
Materials and products used indoors and in building construction can be significant sources of gaseous
3170
contaminants, thus source control through careful product selection has proved to be an effective
3171
strategy for the delivery of quality indoor environments. Just as our knowledge regarding the nature and
3172
impact of these contaminants is evolving, so too are the techniques and protocols for evaluating and
3173
rating air quality impacts of indoor materials. A number of product labeling systems exist with diverse
3174
levels of detail and sophistication.
3175
3176
Several additional strategies can be used to limit the release of gaseous indoor contaminants. These
3177
include:
3178
o Selection of materials with durable finishes which can reduce contaminant release through
3179
maintenance and cleaning activities
3180
o Reduced use of “fleecy” materials that can absorb and re-release contaminants
3181
o Proper curing/conditioning of materials prior to their installation in a building
3182
o Temporary increases in ventilation following initial construction or renovation
3183
o Short-term use of gas-phase filtration following building “flush”
3184
o Use of effective emissions barriers on material surfaces including those that are not obvious from
3185
sight
3186
o Consideration of the impact of local temperature and humidity conditions on gas-phase emissions.
3187
3188
3189
3190
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 106
Return to TOP
Specific Strategies to achieve this Objective are:
3191
• Strategy 5.1 – Control Indoor Contaminant Sources through Appropriate Material Selection
3192
• Strategy 5.2 – Employ Strategies to Limit the Impact of Material Emissions
3193
• Strategy 5.3 – Minimize IAQ Impacts Associated with Cleaning and Maintenance
3194
• Strategy 5.4 – Consider Upper Room Ultraviolet Germicidal Irradiation in Relevant Applications
3195
3196
3197
3198

Additional Information
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
3209
3210
Overview Information
Recent advances in toxicology, indoor chemistry, and the sampling and analysis of indoor contaminants
3211
have contributed to a greater understanding of the nature and impacts of the pollutants that affect
3212
building occupants. In parallel, advancements in the techniques and tools used to determine the
3213
emissions (or “off-gassing”) properties of materials and products used in building construction, finishing
3214
and furnishing have enabled us to more clearly see their chemical “fingerprints” and thus their impact on
3215
the air quality of indoor environments.
3216
3217
Provision of good IAQ requires coordination of many aspects of building design, but a practical first
3218
step is problem avoidance through careful selection of materials with minimal emission of irritating or
3219
harmful compounds. This form of “source control” is an effective means of preventing IAQ problems,
3220
and avoids the high cost of excessive ventilation to dilute avoidable contaminants. Thus building
3221
designers, in addition to specifying material structural, fire, moisture (and mold) resistance properties,
3222
must also carefully consider their chemical emission characteristics. Their long-term durability,
3223
maintenance and cleaning requirements also have significant impact on IAQ, and must therefore be
3224
included in material specifications.
3225
3226
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 108
Return to TOP
Rating systems for assessing the chemical emissions of products are also evolving. Product labels
3227
detailing comprehensive emission properties provide far more information than given by “content”-
3228
based product labels which merely report the weight % of constituent volatile organic compounds
3229
(VOCs). Within emissions-based systems, the Total VOC (or “TVOC”) emission rate, while still widely
3230
reported, is increasingly recognized as a poor indicator of the true impact of any given material. This is
3231
largely due to the great diversity in toxicological impact of individual (or “Target”) VOCs: some pose
3232
serious threats at relatively low levels, while others may be benign at high concentration. Long-term
3233
emissions of semi-volatiles (or “SVOCs”) such as phthalates, pesticides, and flame-retardants are now
3234
recognized as important factors in IAQ problems, and should be considered in the evaluation of material
3235
properties. Actual emission rates vary significantly over time: for a given product, some chemicals may
3236
exhibit rapid decay in emissions (within hours or days), while others may release contaminants at nearly
3237
constant rates for months. The acute or long-term impact of materials can thus be dramatically different,
3238
and should be factored into product assessment. Formation of “secondary” products through “indoor
3239
chemistry” reactions may have key impact on IAQ, thus elimination or reduction of the primary
3240
reactants (such as terpenoids) should be a priority for advanced labeling systems on which designers can
3241
base material selection decisions.
3242
3243
In evaluating emissions impact, materials should be considered as parts of systems whenever possible.
3244
Carpeting is not independent of cushion, adhesives, or subfloor. Wallboard requires primer and paint.
3245
Emissions from the system may be markedly different than from its individual constituents. Materials
3246
subjected to relatively high temperatures (e.g. through solar gains) or high humidity may also have
3247
increased emissions.
3248
3249
IAQ guidelines and standards are sparse. Occupational regulations for air quality do not directly apply to
3250
non-industrial buildings due to differences in the levels and composition of the contaminant species as
3251
well as the nature of the building occupants. Emission “labels” must therefore rely on guidance level
3252
information. Building designers must have a basic understanding of the key issues related to emissions
3253
labeling/reporting prior to specifying building materials, finishes and furnishings. In the absence of
3254
detailed guidance, several basic recommendations can be made concerning material selection for the
3255
diverse range of products that go into building design (Schoen et al 2008).
3256
3257
3258
Figure 5.1-B Source Control: understand potential
3259
emission sources; prevent entry into buildings
3260
3261
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 109
Return to TOP
In general, the following strategies will assist in selecting low-emitting materials for building design:
3262
• Require submission and review of material composition, VOC contents or preferably detailed
3263
emissions properties as condition of acceptance for project (ensure supplier receives detailed
3264
information from manufacturer) prior to material selection.
3265
• Where product-specific emissions data are not available, limit usage of products/materials
3266
generally known to have higher contaminant emissions, including: unfinished composite or
3267
engineered wood products; oil-based architectural coatings and paints; caulks, sealants and
3268
adhesives. Specify use of low-emission resins if required during product manufacture.
3269
• Limit use of porous/fleecy materials including carpeting, fabrics and upholstery to minimize sink
3270
effects and facilitate easier cleaning.
3271
• Select materials that are durable, low-maintenance, and with easily cleanable surfaces. Require
3272
detailed installation, maintenance and cleaning instructions (including required cleaning agent
3273
emission properties) as part of the material specification process. Verify that product installation
3274
practice conforms to specification. Ensure that detailed maintenance and cleaning instructions
3275
are delivered to building owner/operators (refer to additional guidance on Cleaning and
3276
Maintenance that will minimize the IAQ impact of these activities).
3277
• Avoid indoor use of certain PVC-based materials, especially in damp conditions (refer to section
3278
on controlling moisture) where hydrolysis may promote release of harmful secondary emissions.
3279
• Limit the use of lining materials in interior surfaces of ventilation ducts (see Strategy 4.2 - Select
3280
and Install Airstream Surfaces for guidance re engineering of HVAC ducting to minimize noise).
3281
Use low emission cleaning agents to remove any residual oils on the interior surfaces of
3282
ductwork prior to installation. Immediately following manufacture, seal all duct openings and
3283
store in a dry location. Remove seals only just prior to installation to prevent contamination
3284
during construction.
3285
• Fully identify each material or product in the Project Specifications. Prepare a Schedule of
3286
Materials identifying each material by a unique name and symbol, and appearing on Project
3287
Plans. Require that emissions data for all substitutions be submitted for approval; confirm
3288
delivered products meet specifications.
3289
3290
In addition to these material selection practices, refer to the section “Strategies to Limit the Impact of
3291
Material Emissions” to further control the remaining effect of new product emissions on indoor air.
3292

3321
3322
Overview Information
The first and most effective means to reduce the impact of material emissions is to employ appropriate
3323
selection strategies to limit entry of problem materials into the building (source control). However, the
3324
products ultimately used in building construction and furnishing will still have some emissions, and
3325
certain materials with relatively high emissions may be unavoidable due to lack of alternative products
3326
or emission-generating activities. Strategies to reduce the negative impact of these materials/activities
3327
on the indoor environment should be implemented by the building design team.

Case Study Example
Green Housekeeping’ Program at Brooklyn Public Library:
3374
3375
(Source: City of New York, Dept. of Design and Construction (1999): High Performance Building
3376
Guidelines)
3377
3378
Reduction of Toxins: Over 16 hazardous substances have been eliminated from BPL’s cleaning
3379
operations (including butoxyethanol, diacetone alcohol, dipropylene glycol, petroleum distillates,
3380
ethanolamine, ethyl ether, isobutane, isopropanol, methyl ether, naptha, nonyl phenolethoxylate)
3381
3382
Reduction of Cleaning Product: Usage Facility staff estimates a reduction of approximately 50% in the
3383
amount of cleaning product used. This is primarily the result of using a proportioning chemical
3384
dispenser, which premixes cleaners and disinfectants for accurate dilution.
3385
3386

Packaging Waste Reduction: The need for 55-gallon storage drums has been eliminated. In addition to
3387
being bulky and wasteful, these drums were difficult and dangerous to handle.
3388
3389
Improved Efficiency: The proportioning dispenser has been shown to save time because the need for
3390
mixing of products and walking back and forth for water has been eliminated. In addition, BPL staff
3391
believe that this initiative has boosted the morale of the custodial staff, and as a result, productivity has
3392
increased

Strategy 5.4 – Consider Upper Room Ultraviolet Germicidal Irradiation in Relevant
3404
Applications
3405
Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
F
E
How-to
•
UVGI and Infection Control
9
pg #’s
•
UVGI in Buildings
9
pg #’s
•
Safety with UVGI
9
pg #’s
•
Effect on IAQ
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
3406
3407
Overview Information
Droplet nuclei containing the bacterium that causes tuberculosis (Mycobacterium tuberculosis) can
3408
remain suspended in the air for several hours and can be transmitted through a building by the HVAC
3409
system (Nardell and Macher, 1999). Upper room UVGI has been used to control infection, especially in
3410
large, poorly ventilated indoor spaces where other interventions such as increasing HVAC outdoor air
3411
ventilation or raising filtration efficiency are not practical (Nardell, 2002). Photo #1 shows the practical
3412
use of a wall mounted UVGI fixture in a homeless shelter. The UVGI device is designed to limit the
3413
vertical (downward) spread of irradiation so as to protect room occupants. Droplet nuclei from an
3414
infected occupant in the room can migrate on air currents to the upper level of the room to be inactivated
3415
by UVGI. UVGI devices can be installed in air handling units, generally upstream of cooling coils
3416
(Kowalski and Bahnfelth, 2000) and also in free standing air cleaners (Kujundzic et al., 2006). While
3417
constant UVGI irradiation of surfaces (eg., cooling coils, filters, etc.) can kill or inactivate most
3418
microorganisms on those surfaces, the effect of irradiation on IAQ in the building as a whole, is
3419
controversial and a matter of active research (Menzies et al., 1999).
3420
3421
3422
Case Study Example
3423
3424
From Lighting Research Center, Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, pp. 6 (Philip W. Brickner, MD - 212-
3425
604-8025; drpwb@aol.com) - Permission still to be obtained.
3426
3427
3428
The UVGI fixture in the upper wall area provides a plane of 254 NM irradiation (slight upward incline)
3429
in the upper room air. Droplet nuclei containing Mycrobacterium tuberculosis (from an infected
3430
occupant) are inactivated so long as the particle is present for sufficient time in the irradiation plane of
3431
the UVGI fixture. The blue light in the photograph provides a visual indication of the UV irradiation
3432
pattern from the fixture
3433
3434
3435
3436
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 115
Return to TOP
3437
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
3438
3439
3440
3441
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 116
Return to TOP
OBJECTIVE 6 – CAPTURE AND EXHAUST EMISSIONS FROM BUILDING EQUIPMENT
3442
AND ACTIVITIES
3443
3444
3445
IAQ is enhanced when emissions from building equipment and activities are captured and exhausted
3446
close to their source. Best practices include proper venting of combustion equipment, local capture and
3447
exhaust of contaminants from point sources, enclosure and exhaust of areas where more diffuse
3448
contaminants are generated, control of space pressures to minimize transfer from their source to other
3449
areas of the building, and design of exhaust systems to prevent leakage of exhaust air back into occupied
3450
spaces or into air distribution systems.
3451
3452
Some of these issues (such as venting of combustion equipment, provision of toilet exhaust and
3453
ventilation of enclosed parking garages) are addressed by codes.
3454
3455
All combustion equipment, including in-space equipment such as decorative fireplaces, grills, and gas
3456
dryers, should be vented at the source to remove combustion products from the building.
3457
3458
Occupants’ exposure to contaminants such as ozone and VOCs can be reduced by providing exhaust at
3459
the source for high volume copiers and printers, nail stations in salons, art and shop areas in schools, and
3460
similar activities. Properly designed exhaust reduces the exposure of people using these areas as well as
3461
people elsewhere in the space.
3462
3463
Occupants’ exposure to contaminants form more diffuse sources can be reduced by reducing air leakage
3464
sites in the walls, floors, and ceilings of the spaces where they are generated and keeping these spaces at
3465
negative pressure relative to surrounding areas. In mixed use buildings, for example,
3466
compartmentalizing dry cleaners, hair salons, bars, restaurants, copy centers, and other contaminant-
3467
generating spaces and exhausting enough air from the to keep them at a lower pressure than their
3468
neighbors will reduce transfer of contaminants to adjacent spaces. Spaces on floors below residential
3469
units deserve special attention to limit the flow of contaminants upward into the residential units due to
3470
stack effect. Compartmentalizing office spaces in industrial buildings and keeping them at positive
3471
pressure relative to production areas will reduce incursion of industrial contaminants. Medical and
3472
dental procedure rooms must meet stringent pressurization requirements and be designed in accordance
3473
with applicable codes and standards.
3474
3475
In any application where control of relative space pressures is important, space pressure relationships
3476
must be maintained under all operating conditions (variable volume operation, air-side economizer
3477
operation, morning warm-up, etc.), not just under design conditions.
3478
3479
Design and construction should assure that contaminant laden exhaust air is in fact exhausted. Exhaust
3480
fans should be located at the end of the exhaust duct run to keep these ducts at negative pressure,
3481
especially when the ducts carry radon, sub-slab vapors from contaminated sites, or significant
3482
contaminants from building activities or equipment. Where exhaust ducts run through return plenums
3483
or shafts, care should be taken to assure that they will always be at lower pressure than the return
3484
system.
3485
3486
Strategies to meet this Objective include:
3487
• Strategy 6.1 – Properly Vent Combustion Equipment
3488
• Strategy 6.2 – Provide Local Capture and Exhaust for Point Sources of Contaminants
3489
• Strategy 6.3 – Design Exhaust Systems to Prevent Leakage of Exhaust Air into Occupied Spaces or
3490
Air Distribution Systems
3491
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 117
Return to TOP
• Strategy 6.4 – Maintain Proper Pressure Relationships between Spaces
3492
3493
Exhaust must be properly designed to avoid undermining of other IAQ strategies. For instance, exhaust
3494
outlets must be located far enough away from operable windows and outside air intakes to avoid
3495
drawing contaminants back into the building. Selection, design, and operation of exhaust and
3496
ventilation systems must be coordinated to maintain proper building pressurization and space pressure
3497
relationships. Exhausts must avoid creating negative pressures that draw too much moisture into the
3498
building envelope, draw untreated outdoor air into the building, or draw radon or subsurface vapors up
3499
into the building.
3500
3501
These issues are discussed under the following Strategies:
3502
• Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outside Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
3503
• Strategy 1.2 – Select HVAC Systems to Improve IAQ and Reduce the Energy Impacts of Ventilation
3504
• Strategy 2.2 – Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces
3505
• Strategy 2.3 – Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
3506
• Strategy 3.2 – Control Entry of Radon
3507
• Strategy 3.3 – Control Intrusion of Vapors from Subsurface Contaminants
3508
3509
3510
3511
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 118
Return to TOP

Strategy 6.1 – Properly Vent Combustion Equipment
3521
3522
Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
F
E
How-to
• Introduction
9
pg #’s
• Capture and Exhaust of Combustion Products
9
pg #’s
o
Chimney (Natural Draft) Systems

pg #’s

9

• Supply Air for Combustion
9
pg #’s
• Proper Operation and Maintenance of Equipment
9
pg #’s
• Commissioning
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
3523
3524
Overview Information
Combustion occurs whenever a source of fuel combines with oxygen and is burned. Within the enclosed
3525
spaces of a building there are many potential uses of combustion operations, and, consequently many
3526
potential sources of combustion products. During the combustion process a number of gases and
3527
particles are released as by-products. Some of these by-products are potentially harmful to human health.
3528
To avoid exposure to these harmful products, combustion equipment used within a building must be
3529
designed and installed with a proper system of supply and exhaust ventilation.
3530
3531
Indoor combustion requires a properly designed and installed venting and exhaust system. Depending on
3532
the fuel, process and type of equipment being vented, there are several different types of exhaust capture
3533
systems. Regardless of the type of system used, all components of any capture and exhaust system must
3534
be selected to properly function under the conditions of the exhaust air. The system must then be
3535
designed and installed to adequately remove the products of combustion.
3536
3537
Equally as important as the size and operation of the exhaust system to remove combustion products is
3538
the availability of an adequate supply of fresh supply air for combustion. An inadequate amount of
3539
supply air can result in an increase of the harmful products of combustion and can also result in
3540
unwanted negative pressures in the building spaces.
3541
3542
There is always a great potential for contamination from harmful combustion products if the combustion
3543
equipment itself is not functioning correctly. Perform regular inspections of the equipment condition and
3544
operations, and repair or replace any defective equipment as required. It is also recommended that
3545
monitoring of the design and installation of combustion equipment and exhaust and supply duct systems
3546
be included as a part of the building commissioning process.
3547
3548
3549

pressure to surrounding spaces
