Overview Information
ASHRAE 62.1-2007, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality, a contaminant is defined as: “an
3569
unwanted airborne constituent that may reduce acceptability of the air.” By this definition, in the indoor
3570
environment today, contaminants are generated as a part of such processes as cooking, commercial
3571
laundries, scientific procedures and experimentation, generation and reproduction of paper materials,
3572
personal nail treatments, woodworking and metal shop procedures, and areas where chemicals are
3573
utilized extensively (such as natatoriums). The potential impact on the occupants in these spaces and the
3574
surrounding areas includes skin irritations, nose/sinus irritations, objectionable odors, and damage to
3575
interior building construction materials and/or finishes. The effective local capture and exhaust for point
3576
sources of contaminants can significantly minimize the impact of these contaminants on the occupants in
3577
the area in which the contaminant is generated and surrounding occupied spaces.
3578
3579
3580
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4

Return to TOP

3593
Strategy 6.3 – Design Exhaust Systems to Prevent Leakage of Exhaust Air into
3594
Occupied Spaces or Air Distribution Systems
3595
Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
F
E
How-to
• Overview
9
pg #’s
• Effectively seal ductwork to minimize potential for leakage from the
duct system
9
pg #’s

entrainment of exhaust air into fresh air ventilation systems or
adjacent buildings
9
pg #’s
• Maintain exhaust ducts in plenum spaces under a negative pressure.
9
pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
3596
3597
Overview Information
Exhaust systems are required to remove odors and contaminants from indoor environment. Areas which
3598
require exhaust include toilet rooms, soiled laundry storage rooms, pet shops (animal areas) and spaces
3599
where contaminants are generated as a part of such processes as cooking, scientific procedures and
3600
experimentation, generation and reproduction of paper materials, personal nail treatments, woodworking
3601
and metal shop procedures, and areas where chemicals are utilized extensively (such as natatoriums). In
3602
addition, if the building design requires the use of smoke control system(s), then the potential impact of
3603
leakage of the exhaust system can have health or life safety consequences. In order to minimize the
3604
potential impact on the occupants in spaces outside of the area of the odor or contaminant, air must be
3605
effectively exhausted directly from the space. This topic is covered in Strategy 6.2 Provide Local
3606
Capture and Exhaust for Point Sources of Contaminants.
3607
3608
In addition to effectively exhausting the area of odors or contaminants, it is critical that the exhaust air
3609
be conveyed to the outdoors and discharged in a manner to minimize leakage of the exhaust air into
3610
surrounding occupied spaces or air distribution systems.
3611
3612
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

Return to TOP

3629
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 124
Return to TOP
3630
Overview Information
Proper space pressurization is required to minimize moisture and contaminant transfer across adjacent
3631
spaces. Space pressurization is the static pressure difference between the adjacent spaces of a building
3632
with the air moving from higher pressure spaces to lower pressure spaces. This static pressure
3633
difference will influence where exfiltration and infiltration will occur across the adjacent spaces.
3634
Maintaining proper pressure relationships between adjacent spaces is critical to ensure airflow in the
3635
proper direction, from clean spaces to dirty spaces. Many HVAC systems are designed to achieve space
3636
pressurization at an average of 0.05 inches (12.45 Pa) differential.
3637
3638
Case Study Example
3639
3640
Figure 6.4-A (to be added)
3641
3642
3643
3644
Graphical Roadmap to Detail Information in Chapter 4
3645
3646
3647
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 125
Return to TOP
OBJECTIVE 7 - REDUCE CONTAMINANT CONCENTRATIONS THROUGH
3648
VENTILATION, FILTRATION, AND AIR CLEANING
3649
3650
3651
Ventilation with outdoor air is required for all occupied spaces. Inadequate ventilation increases adverse
3652
health effects on occupants and decreases the acceptability of the indoor environment. Filtration and
3653
gas-phase air cleaning (FAC) can remove a substantial fraction of contaminants from outside air and
3654
recirculated air, and can limit accumulation of dirt in air handing systems that provides food for
3655
microorganisms. Contaminants should be reduced as much as possible through the best practices
3656
described under Objectives 1 through 6, with ventilation and air cleaning relied upon to dilute remaining
3657
contaminants and those that cannot be excluded or eliminated, such as human bioeffluents.
3658
3659
Best practices for ventilation meet or exceed the recommendations of ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-
3660
2007, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality, including provision of required zone air flows and
3661
design of ventilation systems to distribute air effectively. Monitoring and control of outdoor air delivery
3662
helps to assure that the design ventilation rates are met at the HVAC system.
3663
3664
Appropriate FAC can remove a substantial fraction of contaminants from incoming outside air,
3665
including regional pollutants such as PM 2.5 and ozone, as well as local contaminants such as vehicle
3666
exhaust or pollen. FAC can also reduce concentrations of indoor contaminants such as dust, mold,
3667
spores, and volatile compounds emitted from furnishings. Higher levels of filtration also limit
3668
accumulation of dirt in air handling systems, reducing food sources for microorganisms that can cause
3669
IAQ problems.
3670
3671
Occupant perception of IAQ is closely correlated to thermal comfort. Thermal comfort conditions must
3672
be considered in any ventilation system design, and the systems providing ventilation and thermal
3673
comfort must be integrated.
3674
3675
Essential elements of ventilation and air cleaning covered in this Objective are:
3676
• Strategy 7.1 – Provide Appropriate Outdoor Air Quantities for Each Room or Zone
3677
• Strategy 7.2 – Continuously Monitor and Control Outdoor Air Delivery
3678
• Strategy 7.3 – Distribute Ventilation Air Effectively
3679
• Strategy 7.4 – Provide Particle Filtration and Gas Phase Air Cleaning Consistent with Project IAQ
3680
Objectives
3681
• Strategy 7.5 – Provide Comfort Conditions that Enhance Occupant Satisfaction
3682
3683
Decisions made very early in design may limit the design teams’ ability to provide good ventilation and
3684
air cleaning. The type of air handling systems chosen can be the controlling factor in the ability to
3685
control space humidity with the required outside air volumes, the ability to accommodate the appropriate
3686
levels of filtration and air cleaning, and the ability to locate outside air intakes away from local
3687
contaminant sources. The benefits of using higher efficiency filtration to keep the air handlers clean
3688
may be lost if HVAC system components are not protected during construction storage or if systems are
3689
operated during construction with lower efficiency filtration.
3690
3691
Guidance provide elsewhere includes:
3692
• Strategy 1.2 – Select HVAC systems to Improve IAQ and Reduce the Energy Impacts of Ventilation
3693
• Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outside Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
3694

3716
3717
Overview Information
Ventilation with outdoor air is required for all occupied spaces. The consequences of severe
3718
underventilation include increases in adverse health effects for the occupants and unacceptable indoor
3719
air quality.
3720
3721
How much outdoor air should be delivered to the breathing zone of each room in this building in the
3722
illustration? The current American national ventilation standard is ANSI/ASHRAE 62.1-2007. It
3723
specifies minimum ventilation rates through the ventilation rate procedure. There are also local building
3724
codes that specify rates but they vary by jurisdiction. A starting point for determining minimum
3725
ventilation required in the USA is ANSI/ASHRAE 62.1-2007. If local codes require more ventilation,
3726
they must be followed. After determining the outdoor air for each zone, the quantity of air for the
3727
ventilation system must be adjusted to account for effectiveness of the air distribution system
3728
(see Strategy 7.3 Distribute Ventilation Air Effectively, and Strategy 8.1 Effectively Distribute
3729
Ventilation Air for Multiple Spaces).
3730
3731

Case Study Example
3735
Figure 7.1-A Two recent studies documented the
3736
associations between ventilation and student attendance
3737
and classroom performance. The first study documented
3738
the associations between classroom attendance in
3739
Washington and Idaho and CO2 concentrations (used as an
3740
indicator for ventilation rates)13. When the difference in
3741
indoor and outdoor CO2 exceeded 1000 ppm, student
3742
absences increased by 10-20%. For classrooms,
3743
concentrations of 1000ppm dCO2 and greater would be
3744
associated with under ventilated spaces.
3745
3746
A second study examined academic performance in a
3747
controlled classroom situation in Denmark where
3748
ventilation and temperature were varied. The results were
3749
that “increasing the outdoor air supply rate and reducing
3750
moderately elevated classroom temperatures significantly
3751
improved the performance of many tasks, mainly in terms
3752
of how quickly each pupil worked (speed) but also for
3753
some tasks in terms of how many errors were committed
3754
(% errors, the percentage of responses that were errors).
3755
The improvement was statistically significant at the level
3756
of P ≤ 0.05. ” 14
3757

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 129
Return to TOP
3768
Overview Information
Accurate monitor and control of outdoor air intake at the air handler is an important aspect of providing
3769
the correct amount of outdoor air to a building. It has been common for designers to design fixed
3770
minimum outdoor air dampers, which does not necessarily provide good control of outdoor air intake
3771
rates, particularly in VAV systems.
3772
3773
In most systems, it is difficult to accurately measure outdoor airflows at the outdoor air dampers during
3774
balancing, commissioning, or operation. As a result, both over- and under-ventilation can commonly
3775
occur. It is estimated that the current amount of energy for ventilating US buildings could be reduced by
3776
as much as 30% if the average minimum outdoor rate is reduced to meet the current standards.
3777
3778
It should also be noted that continuous monitoring of the outdoor rates at the air handler does not
3779
guarantee that the proper amount of ventilation is delivered locally within the building. Poor air mixing,
3780
especially in large buildings, can result in parts of a building receiving less than the minimum amount of
3781
required ventilation.
3782
3783
Accurate measurements of airflows in ducts require careful design, proper commissioning, and ongoing
3784
calibration. Under carefully controlled laboratory conditions, commercially-available airflow sensors
3785
are very accurate. However, laboratory conditions and accuracies cannot be duplicated in the field
3786
3787
The most accurate location to measure airflows is in straight duct runs. Unfortunately, this presents a
3788
challenge to the designer because in most HVAC systems there is insufficient straight duct length due to
3789
space and architectural constraints.
3790
3791
For built up systems with economizers, a dedicated outdoor air intake fan is recommended along with a
3792
separate intake for minimum outdoor air. Large packaged HVAC systems with air economizers can be
3793
ordered with separate intake for minimum outdoor air as well as with monitoring and control of the
3794
minimum outdoor air.
3795
3796
Small packaged HVAC systems units typically do not have continuous measurement of outdoor airflows.
3797
This suggests the need for even greater attention to confirmation of the delivery of design air flow rates
3798
through balancing, commissioning, and periodic re-commissioning. It is recommended that for small
3799
packaged HVAC systems, straight runs of ductwork are designed and installed both in supply and return
3800
airstreams thus allowing accurate measurements of airflows in these airstreams. Outdoor airflows can
3801
then be estimated by deducting the return airflow rate from the supply airflow rate. If possible,
3802
ductwork should be added on the unit’s outdoor air intake to allow for a traverse of outdoor air.
3803
3804
Research has shown that in general better accuracies can be expected when sensors are placed in the
3805
following locations: (a) between the fixed louver blades where the air speeds are more uniform
3806
compared to air speeds downstream of the louvers; or (b) downstream of the louvers and upstream of the
3807
dampers provided that an airflow straightening device is installed between the louvers and the airflow or
3808
pressure sensors. Regardless of whether or not airflow sensors are factory or field-installed, accuracies
3809
of these sensors must be verified with appropriately calibrated equipment at start-up and during
3810
occupancy on regular time intervals.
3811
3812
Indirect methods for measuring minimum outdoor airflows include plenum pressure control and CO2
3813
concentration balance at the mixing plenum. Due to the accuracy problems associated with the indirect
3814
methods, none is recommended. Instead, direct methods are encouraged.
3815
3816
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 130
Return to TOP
The designer needs to make provisions for measurement and verification of the minimum outdoor
3817
airflows during the initial commissioning as well during the ongoing commissioning of a building. Such
3818
provisions include easy access to the airflow sensors, software that can detect sensor (e.g. airflow) and
3819
equipment (damper motor) malfunctions, etc. In addition, the design criteria and occupancy
3820
assumptions need to be listed in a clear format in the operations and maintenance manual. The building
3821
maintenance staff should be encouraged to adjust the minimum amount of outdoor air on regular
3822
intervals (e.g., annually) based on actual maximum occupancy data.
3823
3824
3825

Overview Information
Ventilation only works when the air is delivered to the breathing zone. Different methods of distribution
3863
have different efficiencies. For an inefficient system, quantity of outdoor air at the air handler must be
3864
increased in order to provide the required minimum quantities in the breathing zone

Case Study Example
Zone Air Distribution Effectiveness
3872
The air quantity that must be distributed to a zone varies by the effectiveness of the distribution within
3873
the room. The ventilation air provided to the zone must be consistent with the air required for in the
3874
breathing zone. The zone outdoor air flow is given by the equation
3875
3876
Voz = Vbz/Ez (6-2)
3877
3878
Where:
3879
Voz is the quantity of ventilation air delivered to the occupied zone (cfm)
3880
Vbz is the quantity of ventilation air delivered to the breathing zone (cfm)
3881
Ez is zone air distribution effectiveness
3882
3883
March 2008
ASHRAE 62.1 – 2007
Page 80
Zone Air Distribution
Effectiveness Ez = 1.0
March 2008
ASHRAE 62.1 – 2007
Page 81
Zone Air Distribution
Effectiveness Ez = 0.8
3884
Figure 7.3-A
Figure 7.3-B
3885
Cold air falls and is well mixed
Some hot air stays at the ceiling so
3886
only 80% of the air gets to the breathing zone
3887
3888
Efficient Distribution
3889
Ez ranges from 1.0 to 1.2
3890
• Ceiling supply of cool air
3891
• Ceiling supply of warm air and floor return
3892
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 133
Return to TOP
• Ceiling supply of warm air less than 15°F (8°C) above space temperature and ceiling return provided
3893
that the 150 fpm (0.8 m/s) supply air jet reaches to within 4.5 ft (1.4 m) of floor level.
3894
• Floor supply of cool air and ceiling return provided that the 150 fpm (0.8 m/s) supply jet reaches 4.5
3895
ft (1.4 m) or more above the floor
3896
• Floor supply of cool air and ceiling return, provided low- velocity displacement ventilation achieves
3897
unidirectional flow and thermal stratification
3898
• Floor supply of warm air and floor return
3899
3900
Adequate Distribution
3901
Ez = 0.8.
3902
• Ceiling supply of warm air 15F or more above space temperature and ceiling return
3903
• Makeup supply drawn in on the opposite side of the room from the exhaust and/or return
3904
3905
Inefficient Distribution
3906
Ez < 0.8
3907
• Floor supply of warm air and ceiling return
3908
• Makeup supply drawn in near to the exhaust and/or return location

Project IAQ Objectives
3917

Pre-filtration Considerations

pg #’s
Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
3918
3919
Overview Information
The technologies of enhanced particulate filtration and gas phase air cleaning (FAC) are well developed
3920
and well established with a history of over half a century of application in industrial process control;
3921
clean rooms; healthcare and pharmaceuticals; and special usage buildings, such as museums and
3922
laboratories. However, Filtration and Air Cleaning (FAC) systems have not been widely applied in
3923
general commercial buildings. Thus, this section is to provide the design team with a fuller
3924
understanding and appreciation of the potential of FAC as a tool for attaining enhanced levels of IAQ
3925
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 134
Return to TOP
acceptability. Both particulate and gas phase control are discussed together in this section because much
3926
of the design consideration and mechanical application technology is concurrent and co-mingled.
3927
3928
A full range of particulate filtration and gas phase air cleaners (FAC) is available from a wide selection
3929
of manufacturers. The filter cartridges vary widely in frame methodology and seal mechanisms;
3930
filter/sorption media; cartridge depth and size; the loading characteristics and capacity; surface area
3931
configurations; airflow and pressure drop; and cost. The efficiency or removal performance is a critical
3932
component of the selection of filtration and air cleaning equipment. However, other factors also
3933
influence the overall performance and total value of the FAC system and, thus, should be included in the
3934
selection and specification process.
3935
3936
The application of filtration and air cleaning (FAC) is a balance: of control and extraction requirements;
3937
the physical/mechanical limitations of the air handling system; the characteristics of the contaminants of
3938
concern; and the features of the FAC equipment. The higher the efficiency requirements, generally, the
3939
greater the space, energy, operating and maintenance cost. Because of the latter constraints, the final
3940
selection of equipment should go beyond removal efficiency to include; capacity and life cycle,
3941
maintenance requirements, and life-long pressure drop factors. Because of these factors, life-cycle cost,
3942
including energy requirements, should drive the selection rather than first cost. The subsequent
3943
supportive discussion provides rationale for the selection of specific extraction efficiencies and specific
3944
FAC equipment.
3945
3946
Specific recommendations are provided in the section on General Selection Guidance in the detailed
3947
information for this Strategy; however, early consideration should be devoted to filtration and air
3948
cleaning (FAC) in a number of overall building design aspects. This early consideration is necessary
3949
because the function of FAC equipment is similar to ventilation systems except the FAC system
3950
employs extraction rather than dilution to lessen the concentration of unwanted contaminant burden in
3951
the conditioned space. Thus, it can be applied in conjunction with ventilation to aid and improve the
3952
quality of the ventilation air or as an alternative or direct substitute for ventilation air for economic
3953
reasons. Because of these factors, FAC has inter-activity features that are similar to ventilation,
3954
including the appraisal of the outdoor air, an understanding of internal sources, knowledge of occupant
3955
density and activity, concern for air capture and recirculation efficacy, selection of HVAC equipment,
3956
and concern for energy management issues. Following are typical inter-related areas that trigger early
3957
consideration of FAC by the design team:
3958
• When outdoor ventilation air is unreliable for delivering dependable and effective dilution of
3959
indoor contaminants because of pre-existing content of pollutants, such as ozone or ultra-fine
3960
particulate matter, or when other odor or pollutant generating sources are nearby;
3961
• When outdoor ventilation air is either consistently or seasonally burdened with high heat and
3962
latent loads demanding additional and excessive HVAC equipment capacity and operating
3963
energy consumption;
3964
• When source control tactics, such as material selection and localized exhaust are insufficient to
3965
adequately lower concentrations of contaminants of concern (COC);
3966
• When occupant function or activity generates elevated levels of contaminants of concern (COC)
3967
that cannot be adequately controlled by routine levels of dilution ventilation;
3968
• When internal occupancy patterns include high density and/or wide diversity of space usage
3969
requiring high ventilation levels with related capacity and energy load;
3970
• When owner or tenant needs or expectations require higher levels of contaminant control to
3971
provide enhanced levels of indoor environmental acceptability and conditions;
3972
• When occupants, occupant activities, or contents of the building require enhanced levels of
3973
protection from the deleterious effects of airborne contaminants, whether the contaminants of
3974
concern are microbiological, chemical, or particulate in nature; and
3975
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 135
Return to TOP
• When exhaust systems contain contaminants of concern (COC) having the potential for re-
3976
entrainment or contribution of potential risk to the ambient air.
3977
3978
Application Example: Improved IAQ and Protected Objects in Specialty City Museum. This low
3979
rise building serves the archival offices and laboratories of a museum complex located in a major
3980
southeastern city. The building is less than 10 years old and is used for preservation and restoration of
3981
historically significant photos, documents, and objects d'art that are mostly cellulosic and/or organic-
3982
based in content. Thus, there are a number of odorous, noxious, and potentially hazardous chemicals
3983
used in the various preservation processes employed by the museum professionals. These normally
3984
require a high level of ventilation and dilution to maintain acceptable indoor air quality. However, the
3985
same historical documents are highly susceptible to the contaminants of ambient outdoor urban air. Thus,
3986
the facility HVAC system operates with reduced outdoor air and both the outdoor air and the return
3987
airstreams are treated with filtration and gaseous air cleaning to remove the chemical contaminants from
3988
both the outdoor air and the return air. This results in acceptable indoor environmental conditions for
3989
both the occupants and the valuable stored materials, while reducing both the risk and the cost of
3990
introducing excessive quantities of outdoor air.
3991
3992
The special FAC equipment consists of MERV 6 prefilters and 12" deep medium efficiency (ME)
3993
matrix media imbedded gas phase filter cartridges and MERV 6 final filters and is located in the mixed
3994
air plenum to treat both return and outdoor air sources
3995
3996
3997

3998
3999

Strategy 7.5 – Provide Comfort Conditions that Enhance Occupant Satisfaction
4014

Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
4016
4017
Overview Information
Thermal comfort has links both to occupant perceptions of air quality and studies now suggest that there
4018
are links to occupant productivity as well. Dry bulb temperature is only one parameter out of many that
4019
interact in a complex manner to produce occupant satisfaction. Others include humidity, radiant
4020
temperature, and air movement.
4021
4022
Often in traditional designs, the HVAC designer’s role in achieving comfort conditions begins and ends
4023
at the selection of an indoor design condition and the sizing of a system to provide these at the peak
4024
outdoor conditions.
4025
4026
There are tools for predicting the proportion of people likely to be satisfied by the combination of
4027
comfort factors that include temperature, humidity, air velocity and radiant heat transfer. The most
4028
commonly known tool in the U.S. is the Thermal Comfort Tool, a computer program that is part of
4029
ASHRAE Standard 55. In order to use this tool, the amount of clothing worn by occupants and their
4030
level of metabolic activity must be known (in units of Clos and Mets, respectively).
4031
4032
Use smarter zones and more of them. Rooms and areas having loads that vary over time in patterns that
4033
are significantly different from areas that surround them should have their own conditioning control
4034
loops and thermostats. Each person in control over their own environment is the ideal situation.
4035
4036
“Thermostat wars” can occur. In some cases, removal of occupant direct control by giving it to the
4037
building operator alone may be a reasonable option.
4038
4039
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 137
Return to TOP
The control of humidity at part load is a comfort goal. See the section on this subject, Controlling
4040
Indoor Humidity.
4041
4042
However, air diffusion must be selected so that minimum air velocity conditions in occupied zones are
4043
maintained at low air flow.
4044
4045
Avoiding thermostats that have fixed rather than variable set points and sometimes by allowing
4046
sufficient system capacity
4047
4048
Choose thermostat locations that best represent the conditions that occupants will experience and that
4049
are not confounded by solar radiation or other heat sources.
4050
4051
By (1) mitigating the negative impacts of radiation, (2) taking them into account in system capacity and
4052
control and (3) utilizing the beneficial techniques radiation can play in comfort.
4053
4054
Drafts can occur under windows Overhead forced air has height and temperature limitations.
4055
4056
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

OBJECTIVE 8 – APPLY MORE ADVANCED VENTILATION APPROACHES
4074
4075
4076
Ventilation consumes a significant amount of energy, and in an era of increasing energy costs and
4077
concern about climate change, best practices to reduce the energy impacts of ventilation should be
4078
employed.
4079
4080
Demand controlled ventilation (DCV) uses occupancy sensors, CO2 sensors, or other approaches to
4081
reduce ventilation air quantities when areas served are vacant or have reduced numbers of occupants.
4082
DCV can be particularly cost-effective for spaces that are intermittently occupied such as conference
4083
rooms, or have highly variable occupancies such as auditoriums and theaters.
4084
4085
Natural Ventilation can be a very low energy strategy that provides a pleasant environment in mild
4086
climates with good outdoor air quality. Mixed mode ventilation can provide the benefits of natural
4087
ventilation during mild times of the year in other climates. Meeting ventilation requirements in all
4088
spaces with natural or mixed mode ventilation is a new challenge for most engineers and requires careful
4089
design to be successful.
4090
4091
The IAQ procedure of ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 provides a path to comply with ventilation
4092
standards using lower outside air volumes than those required by the ventilation rate procedure (VRP),
4093
or to provide enhanced IAQ with the same air volumes. It can be particularly cost-effective in
4094
applications requiring large volumes of ventilation air in climates where the energy cost of conditioning
4095
outside air is high.
4096
4097
This Objective provide guidance on:
4098
• Strategy 8.1 – Effectively Distribute Ventilation Air for Multiple Spaces
4099
• Strategy 8.2 – Use Demand-Controlled Ventilation Where Appropriate
4100
• Strategy 8.3 – Use Energy Recovery Ventilation Where Appropriate
4101
• Strategy 8.4 – Use Natural or Mixed Mode Ventilation Where Appropriate
4102
• Strategy 8.5 – Use the ASHRAE Standard 62.1 IAQ Procedure Where Appropriate
4103
4104
Before considering advanced approaches, it is important to understand the basic ventilation and air
4105
cleaning issues described in Objective 7 – Reduce Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation,
4106
Filtration, and Air Cleaning.
4107

4109

4133
4134
Overview Information
Ventilation only works when the air is delivered to the breathing zone. Different methods of distribution
4135
have different efficiencies. For an inefficient system, the quantity of outdoor air at the air handler must
4136
be increased in order to provide the required minimum quantities in the breathing zone. For Multiple
4137
Zone Recirculating Systems, the system will have efficiency (Ev) that must be calculated in order to
4138
determine the quantity of outdoor air at the air handler. This efficiency is for the system and must be
4139
used in addition to the corrections for effectiveness of distributing air to the zone (Ez). The values for
4140
system efficiency can range from 1.0 to 0.3 or less with higher values being more efficient (better).
4141
4142
Case Study Example
For a single zone system or a dedicated outdoor air system (DOAS) the quantity of the air at the outdoor
4143
air intake (Vot) is equal to the air required for the zone(s).
4144
4145
Vot = Voz (6-3)
4146
4147
Vot = Σall zonesVoz (6-4)
4148
4149
For systems that supply multiple zones, the calculations are more complex. The quantity of air required
4150
at the outdoor air intake is driven by the critical zone, however the %outdoor air required at the system
4151
intake (Xs) can be less than the %outdoor air required at the critical zone (Zd critical). The system
4152
ventilation efficiency is given by the equation:
4153
4154
Single Supply Systems Evz = 1+Xs – Zd (A-1)
4155
4156
The multiple space equations were tested in the building illustrated15 and a research project confirmed
4157
that the system intake air fraction does not have to be as large as the fraction of outdoor air at the critical
4158
zone in order to deliver the proper air quantity (Voz) to the zone.
4159
4160
15 A Study of Multiple Space Effects on Ventilation System Efficiency in Standard 62.1 – 2004 and
Experimental Validation of the Multiple Spaces Equation,” Final Report for ASHRAE Research Project
1276, David P. Yuill, Grenville K. Yuill, Andrew H. Coward, March 15, 2007, ASHRAE, Atlanta, GA
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 141
Return to TOP

Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
4171
4172
4173
Overview Information
Demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) is a control strategy that varies the amount of ventilation by
4174
resetting the design outdoor air intake flow set points to an occupied space based on the changing
4175
number of occupants. The goal is to avoid under ventilation (thus poor indoor air quality), as well as
4176
over ventilation (thus wasted energy). It has been estimated that in U.S. commercial buildings, DCV has
4177
the potential to reduce heating and cooling loads by as much as 20% or $0.05 to more than $1 per ft2
4178
annually. However, actual savings can vary widely depending on climate, variability in population
4179
density and occupancy schedule, type of building, whether or not the HVAC system has an economizer,
4180
etc.
4181
4182
The simplest approach to DCV is by controling outdoor air rate in an on-off manner based on a signal
4183
from a room occupancy sensor, time clock, or a light switch. A more sophisticated approach uses a
4184
signal that is proportional to the number of persons in a space to automatically modulate the amount of
4185
outdoor air. Examples of approaches for estimating occupancy include people-counters and carbon
4186
dioxide (CO2) sensors.
4187
4188
Measurement and control of indoor CO2 concentration has been the most popular DCV method because
4189
CO2 sensors and associated controllers are relatively inexpensive and have been shown to correlate well
4190
with people-related contaminant levels in controlled environments. A number of packaged HVAC
4191
equipment manufacturers now offer CO2 sensors and controllers as an option to their equipment. This
4192
method is based on the fact that when the indoor/outdoor CO2 concentration difference is at steady state,
4193
the rate of CO2 generation indoors by occupants is proportional to the number of occupants and their
4194
activity level.
4195
4196
Where DCV Should and Should Not Be Applied?
4197
DCV is most appropriate in densely occupied spaces with intermittent or variable population. For these
4198
spaces, DCV offers the potential for both energy savings and improved indoor air quality. The benefit
4199
of DCV increases with the level of density, transiency and cost of energy.
4200
4201
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 143
Return to TOP
Occupancy categories appropriate for DCV include theaters, auditoriums/public assembly, gyms, some
4202
classrooms, restaurants, office conference rooms, etc. Due to health concerns, densely occupied spaces
4203
with people-related pollutants other than normal bio-effluents, such as waiting areas of health care
4204
facilities, are inappropriate for DCV despite their intermittent or variable population. Densely and
4205
continuously occupied office spaces, such as call centers, are also inappropriate for DCV.
4206
4207
Although the energy conserving benefits of DCV are small in general office building applications and
4208
should not be used in such applications, certain aspects of DCV controls may be beneficial to a building
4209
in ensuring that the proper amount of ventilation is supplied under all operating conditions. For example,
4210
continuous measurement of outdoor airflow rates and indoor CO2 levels can help building personnel
4211
make adjustments to the HVAC system set points thus avoiding large amounts of over ventilation or
4212
under ventilation relative to the applicable code requirements.
4213
4214
Special considerations for CO2-based DCV
4215
CO2-based DCV is required by some building codes. Despite their relatively low cost and short payback,
4216
the CO2-based DCV market has not grown as had been expected (FEMP, 2004). This is partially due to
4217
the limited data on the long-term performance of these sensors. Limited studies have indicated that
4218
there are numerous issues that need to be addressed with further research. Some of the reported issues
4219
with the CO2 sensors relate to the accuracy of the sensors while others relate to maintenance/calibration
4220
and to the inherent sensor lag times. Because the CO2 generation rates have been measured and reported
4221
for sedentary adults (1.2 met units), they should not be applied to children in classrooms. CO2
4222
generation rates for children are not available in the published literature.
4223
4224
The following considerations should be made during the design of a DCV system:
4225
1. In HVAC systems with open plenum returns, CO2 sensors should be located in the room at the
4226
breathing level and not in return air plenums.
4227
2. In HVAC systems with open plenum returns, a sufficient number of sensors should be placed
4228
within a space in order to get an accurate average space CO2 concentration.
4229
3. In HVAC systems with ducted returns, CO2 sensors may be placed in the return air duct, if the
4230
designer can provide calculations showing that the CO2 measurement in the return duct is
4231
equivalent to breathing level average measurements.
4232
4. In all rooms with CO2 sensors, DCV controls should maintain CO2 concentrations (with respect
4233
to the outdoor air CO2 concentration) between the maximum level expected at design population
4234
and the minimum level expected at minimum population.
4235
5. Outdoor air CO2 concentration should be measured continuously using a CO2 sensor located
4236
within four feet of the outdoor air intake. Alternatively, outdoor air CO2 concentration can be
4237
assumed to constant, provided the constant level is conservatively high and based on recent
4238
historical data for the area where the building is located, e.g., 400 ppm or higher.
4239
6. CO2 sensors should be certified by the manufacturer to have an accuracy of no less than 50 ppm
4240
for concentration ranges typically found in HVAC applications (e.g., 400 to 2000 ppm), be
4241
factory and field calibrated, and be certified by the manufacturer to require calibration no more
4242
frequently than once every 5 years while operating under typical field conditions (limited
4243
research to date indicates that field-based calibration should be performed once every one to two
4244
years)
4245
7. Design provisions should be provided for periodic maintenance and calibration. This will assist:
4246
(a) maintenance personnel in properly maintaining the DCV; and (b) health and safety personnel
4247
in validating that the proper amount of ventilation is supplied under all variable occupancy levels
4248
and load conditions
4249
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 144
Return to TOP
4250
4251
Alternatives to CO2-based DCV
4252
Other forms of DCV are based on technologies that can count the number of persons entering and
4253
exiting a space and adjust ventilation accordingly. In it simplest form this is done by estimating the
4254
number of persons during certain time periods and programming the ventilation supply accordingly.
4255
However, new advances in sensing and micro computing technologies may automate this task. Dynamic
4256
infrared imaging hardware and software are emerging technologies with some significant potential
4257
advantages. Some of the potential advantages of these technologies may include reduced signal delay
4258
problems (as discussed above) or drifts typically found in chemical sensors.
4259
4260
DCV in Multiple-Zone Systems
4261
Application of DCV in single-zone systems is fairly straight forward. However, neither Standard 62.1-
4262
2007 nor the associated User’s Manual offer any advice on the design and operation of DCV for systems
4263
that serve multiple spaces.
4264
4265
4266

4290
4291
Overview Information
Energy recovery ventilation is required for certain applications. In other cases energy recovery systems
4292
provide an attractive payback in terms of overall system sizing and reduced operating costs over the life
4293
of the system. In general there are two types of devices: total energy recovery ventilators (ERV) that
4294
transfer heat and moisture between incoming and exhaust air and heat recovery ventilators (HRV) that
4295
do not transfer moisture. Along with generic IAQ concerns about keeping mechanical air delivery
4296
equipment clean, proper application of equipment should address correct selection, sizing, application,
4297
commissioning maintenance.
4298
4299

4300
4301
4302
Case Study Example
In humid climates, there is a challenge in properly ventilating schools and also controlling indoor
4303
humidity. ASHRAE 62.1-200716 requires that relative humidity in a space be limited to less than 65%
4304
during cooling conditions. Using package equipment for air conditioning and ventilation often cannot
4305
provide humidity control under part load conditions. This is because the equipment sensible heat ratio is
4306
16 ANSI/ASHRAE 62.1-2007 Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 147
Return to TOP
0.67 while the part-load sensible heat ratio in classrooms is c loser to 0.40.17 Using an energy recovery
4307
system with latent transfer capability can provide improved humidity control while saving energy. A
4308
recent study of an energy recovery system documented reductions in space humidity with corresponding
4309
energy savings at schools in the Atlanta area.18
4310
4311
4312
Figure 8.3-A Sensible and latent loads for a typical
4313
Classroom in Atlanta, more accurately calculated
4314
4315

Note: F=Fundamental; E=Enhanced; C=Conditional
4323
4324
Overview Information
Natural ventilation systems must be capable of providing sufficient outside air to achieve appropriate
4325
standards of air quality and to provide cooling when needed. Since the cooling capacity of natural
4326
ventilation is limited by the temperature of outdoor air, a key design challenge is to limit heat gains
4327
through good solar control and careful management of the internal gains. Naturally conditioned
4328
buildings do not aim to achieve constant environmental conditions, but take advantage of, and adapt to,
4329
dynamic ambient conditions to provide a comfortable, controllable indoor environment for the
4330
occupants.
4331
4332
Natural ventilation strategies tend to work well with other sustainable practices. For instance, the high
4333
spaces and narrow floor plates necessary for ventilation also work well with day lighting. Naturally
4334
ventilated buildings often take advantage of thermally massive elements such as concrete and masonry
4335
to provide a more stable mean radiant temperature. The massive elements absorb and release heat
4336
slowly. Mass can be used to temper incoming air, especially when using “night flushing,” and can also
4337
moderate mean radiant temperature, which improves comfort. Mass provides a thermal damper, so the
4338
building requires less overall energy to heat and cool.
4339
4340
Very sophisticated natural/ mixed mode ventilation systems are now more common in large buildings,
4341
especially in Japan and Europe. Pressure sensors and motor driven dampers have been used to control
4342
pressures in various parts of the building and to take advantage of stack effect or wind pressure to
4343
deliver ventilation where and when it is needed. These sophisticated ventilation control systems need
4344
considerable care in design and operation as well as end user education.
4345
4346
Key issues for consideration are:
4347
• Delivering sufficient outdoor air to dilute indoor pollutants
4348
• Preventing the entry of undesirable constituents in polluted outdoor air
4349
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 149
Return to TOP
• Controlling airflow through passive or active means requires well-designed systems with
4350
thorough consideration of air flows under the wide range of outdoor weather conditions to which
4351
the building will be subjected
4352
• A satisfactory acoustic environment: natural ventilation openings provide a noise transmission
4353
path from outside to inside, and this may be a determining factor in some building locations. In
4354
addition, naturally ventilated buildings often include large areas of exposed concrete in order to
4355
increase the thermal capacity of the space. Such large areas of hard surface will require careful
4356
attention to achieve a satisfactory acoustic environment for the occupants
4357
• Smoke control: since smoke can follow natural ventilation paths, the integration of the fire safety
4358
strategy must be integrated with the natural ventilation design
4359
• Health and safety: many natural ventilation openings will be at significant heights above floor
4360
level, so the Work at Heights Regulations will be particularly relevant
4361
4362
It should be noted that natural ventilation may not be appropriate in building types with tight
4363
temperature and humidity requirements or in locations which experience prolonged periods of high
4364
humidity, chronic outdoor air pollution, or severe outside weather conditions. On the other hand, there
4365
are large portions of North America which can take advantage of natural ventilation for the whole of the
4366
year or significant portions of the year.
4367
4368
It is generally accepted that natural ventilation will find broader acceptance in mountain or moderate
4369
climate zones, with low humidity and large diurnal temperature swings. For instance, natural ventilation
4370
has been successfully used on the West Coast, where temperature and humidity are moderate also in
4371
northern cooler climates and in other coastal areas where temperatures are moderate and with steady
4372
daytime breezes.
4373
4374
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 150
Return to TOP

4445
4446
Overview Information
The attainment of acceptable indoor air quality is fulfilled through the removal or control of irritating,
4447
harmful, or unpleasant constituents of the indoor environment. The established methods for contaminant
4448
control are: elimination through source control; dilution through ventilation; and extraction through
4449
filtration and air cleaning (FAC). The best known application of the Indoor Air Quality Procedure
4450
(IAQP) is the usage of enhanced filtration and/or gas phase or chemical air cleaning (FAC) as an
4451
alternative to ventilation with outdoor air. However, the IAQP combines all three of these basic control
4452
methods to provide a cost-effective combination of the strengths and value of each to provide the
4453
following multiple benefits:
4454
• It provides a methodology for documenting and predicting the outcome of source control and
4455
rewards reduction tactics by lowering ventilation requirements;
4456
• It lowers the heat, moisture, and pollutant burden of outdoor air by reducing its volume
4457
introduced to the conditioned space;
4458
• The use of enhanced filtration and air cleaning lowers the constituent contaminant concentrations
4459
of Contaminants of Concern (CoC) contained in the outdoor air;
4460
• The use of enhanced FAC lowers the constituent concentration of contaminants of concern
4461
(CoC) created within the conditioned space;
4462
• Because of enhanced FAC, it assures cleaner heat exchange surfaces and more sustainable
4463
energy efficient HVAC systems;
4464
• It lowers system capacity and operating costs by substituting lower cost FAC for a portion of the
4465
outdoor air component.
4466
4467
To attain these outcomes, the IAQP applies a combination of tactics as illustrated in the drawing below.
4468
These are designated on the drawing as: A) Reduction of the outdoor air from that required by the VRP;
4469
B) The inclusion or addition of enhanced FAC; and C) Rigorous scrutiny of surfaces and sources of
4470
contaminant out-gassing in the conditioned space.
4471
4472
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 155
Return to TOP
4473
Figure 8.5-B The usage of the IAQP enables the reduction of the required
4474
outdoor air ventilation component as illustrated in this diagram.
4475
4476
This methodology was first introduced in the original ASHRAE Standard 62 in 1973 and formalized in
4477
Standard 62-89 as an alternate path of compliance to attain acceptable indoor air quality. However, the
4478
IAQP has not been widely used by designers because of its complexity and the additional engineering
4479
rigor required, such as calculations, analysis, and/or testing, along with their related liability. The
4480
occasional usage of the procedure was predominately in areas having high outdoor humidity and heat
4481
loads and in buildings having high density and diversity, such as arenas, schools, auditoriums, theaters,
4482
convention centers, and hotels. In these spaces, the economic advantages provide compelling returns on
4483
the additional design and equipment investment, as illustrated by the case study discussed below. (See
4484
Chapter 4 History of the IAQP for more information about the procedure)
4485
4486
Areas of Interactivity
4487
There is considerable interaction of the application of the IAQP to other areas of the design process.
4488
These include the following:
4489
• The evaluation of sources, components, and concentrations of contaminants of concern in the
4490
outdoor air; (See Strategies in Objective 3 Limit Entry of Outdoor Contaminants)
4491
• The selection and evaluation of the building component materials for their out-gassing and
4492
contaminant source contribution; (See Strategies in Objective 5 Contaminants from Indoor
4493
Sources)
4494
• The determination of filtration needs and the selection of the appropriate filtration and air
4495
cleaning efficacy and equipment type; (See Strategy 7.4 Provide Particle Filtration and Gas
4496
Phase Air Cleaning)
4497
• The evaluation and selection of the ventilation system; (See Strategies in Objective 7 Reduce
4498
Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation and Air Cleaning)
4499
• The selection of HVAC systems and equipment so that enhanced filtration can be installed with
4500
adequate access, space and fan horsepower; (See Strategy 1.2 Select HVAC Systems to Improve
4501
IAQ)
4502
4503
How to Apply the IAQP
4504
The ASHRAE Standard 62-2007 allows four alternative approaches of applying the IAQP, which are
4505
outlined in the following table.
4506
4507
4508
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 156
Return to TOP

4509
Approach
Methodology
Compliance Verification
Mass Balance
Perform steady state calculations using mass
balance equations on CoC to verify similar
outcome equal to or better than Ventilation Rate
Procedure (VRP)
Provide calculations
Similar Successful Buildings

and activity demonstrating and documenting
successful usage of IAQP
Document similar building
selection
Contaminant Monitoring

using testing and subjective evaluation by
occupants to verify attainment of acceptability
Provide testing and
subjective results

details with related
verification details
4510
Application Process of the IAQP
4511
The following chart details the various steps of the IAQP (See Standard 62-2007 and its related User's
4512
Manual). By applying these design tactics, the outdoor air ventilation air requirements may be reduced
4513
substantially. Further discussion and details on each of these design steps is located in Chapter IV Using
4514
the IAQ Procedure)
4515
4516
Table 8.5-B Process for Applying the IAQP
4517
Design Step
Application
Select IAQP alternative
compliance method
See the Chapter IV Use the IAQP for….full title discussion for when is
advisable and advantageous to employ the IAQP.
Determine and select CoC
Investigate and/or select CoC applicable for the project. Requires input from
owner/occupant regardingsite weather and environment data; materials
selection; and space usage, occupancy, and activity. May require air testing or
monitoring to determine.
Establish CoC sources and
strengths
Determine the existing or expected sources, concentrations, and out-gassing
rates of CoC to guide material selection tactics, control strategies, and
efficiency and location requirements of control equipment. (See Std. 62
Appendix B for assistance and guidance.)
Determine Cognizant
Authorities for CoC
Based upon CoC, determine and select the appropriate authority to support the
targets of acceptability, such as EPA, NIOSH, WHO etc. May include owners
having cognizant experience with proprietary CoC used at their site
Select method for
determining acceptability
See above Figure #XXX for methods of establishing compliance and
acceptability.
Determine targets of
acceptability
Select the concentration levels determined to be acceptable and select the
percentage acceptable for perceived occupant panel.
Determine if excess
ventilation is required
Based upon the CoC and its concentrations, determine whether excessive
ventilation beyond normal VRP rates are required. Employ the IAQP to
comply with "excess" ventilation requirements imposed by the owner, codes,
or other standards.
Determine FAC requirement
Dependent upon CoC nature, source, concentration, and out-gassing generation
rate, select, apply, and locate the appropriate FAC control methodology (see
Discussion on Filtration Selection).
Evaluate IAQP for
supplemental needs
Apply FAC to treat selected zones for compliance; or the total building to
attain improved IAQ acceptability; or to supplement the ventilation
effectiveness beyond the VRP rate.
Determine availability of
similar successful building(s)
If this is the selected method of compliance, investigate and document similar
building experience and performance.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 157
Return to TOP
Design Step
Application
Perform mass balance
calculations
If the selected method, perform the appropriate mass equations as detailed in
Appendix D of Standard 62-2007 to determine that the attained results replicate
the normal VRP rate.
Perform compliance methods
Use testing and monitoring of CoC or the acceptability panel to evaluate the
treated space to establish acceptability and compliance.
Documentation
Document each step of the IAQP design process, including the selection
rationale, the CoC and sources and strengths, the compliance method, and the
compliance data.
4518
4519
Graphical Roadmap to Detailed Information in Chapter 4
4520
4521
Case Study Example
This large public assembly building located in a major Southeastern city was constructed in 1991 using
4522
the IAQ Procedure of Standard 62-89. Enhanced filtration was included in the original design because
4523
the facility is located in an urban area with dense auto, truck, and train traffic. The facility also features
4524
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 158
Return to TOP
public events that generate high levels of internal dust and dirt loads such as Monster Truck and tractor
4525
pulling that required enhanced filtration. This filtration component enabled the usage of the IAQ
4526
Procedure that reduced the outdoor air requirement. This yielded the savings of 2350 tons of chiller
4527
capacity and 2.5 million dollars of related construction costs. This totaled 40 million BTU and $800,000
4528
of reduced operating energy costs per year in 1991 dollars, which equals $1,256,000 per year in 2008
4529
dollars. This highly successful installation currently services their MERV 13 bag filters annually and
4530
estimates total accumulated savings to date of over $13,000,000 to $15,000,000, which is more than the
4531
original cost of the building
4532
4533
4534
Figure 8.5-A
