4620
Strategy 1.2 – Select HVAC system(s) to Improve Indoor Air Quality and Reduce the
4621
Energy Impacts of Ventilation
4622
4623
HVAC system IAQ design principles
4624
The design of the building and its mechanical/ electrical systems can have a significant effect on the
4625
buildings IAQ and environmental impact. The 2006 Department of Energy (DOE) “Buildings Energy
4626
Data-book” shows that commercial buildings consume in the order of 30% of the total energy consumed
4627
in the United States. This clearly represents an opportunity for energy savings using proven technologies
4628
and design concepts.
4629
4630
Many factors enter into the selection of the HVAC system for a given project, including the criteria for
4631
indoor environmental conditions (temperature, humidity, air velocity, noise, indoor air quality) and other
4632
important criteria such as space limitations, first costs, operating costs, robustness, life cycle costs,
4633
controllability, and redundancy. The design engineer typically considers various system options and
4634
recommends one or a few system options for consideration by the owner and design team.
4635
4636
For some projects, many different HVAC system types can meet the project IAQ requirements, and the
4637
choice of system type will be driven by other considerations. For other projects, the type of HVAC
4638
system chosen can be more critical to IAQ. In these cases, IAQ requirements should be a key
4639
consideration in the choice of systems.
4640
4641
Integrated design
4642
Integrated building design is a process of design in which the design team works together on seemingly
4643
unrelated aspects of design in a manner that permits synergistic benefits to be realized. The goal is to
4644
achieve high performance and multiple benefits at a lower cost. This process often includes integrating
4645
green design strategies into conventional design criteria. A key to successful integrated building design
4646
is the participation of people from different specialties of design: general architecture, HVAC, lighting
4647
and electrical, interior design, and landscape design. By working together at key points in the design
4648
process, these participants can often identify highly attractive solutions to design needs that would
4649
otherwise not be found. In terms of energy usage, in an integrated design approach, a member of the
4650
integrated design team will calculate energy use and cost very early in the design, informing designers
4651
of the energy-use implications of building orientation, configuration, fenestration, mechanical systems,
4652
and lighting options.
4653
Finding the right building design recipe through an integrated design process can be challenging. At
4654
first, design teams often make incremental changes that are effective and result in a better performing
4655
building—and often at affordable costs. However, continuing to explore design integration opportunities
4656
can sometimes yield incredible results.
4657
Whenever one green design strategy can provide more than one benefit, there is a potential for design
4658
integration. For example, building envelope and lighting design strategies that significantly
4659
reduce HVAC system requirements can have remarkable results. Sometimes the most effective solutions
4660
also have the lowest construction costs, especially when they are part of an integrated design.
4661
The best HVAC design considers all the inter-related building systems while addressing indoor air
4662
quality, energy consumption, and environmental benefit. Optimizing the design and benefits requires
4663
that the project engineers and architect address these issues early in the concept design phase and
4664
continually revise subsequent decisions throughout the remaining design process. It is also essential that
4665
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 163
Return to TOP
designers communicate to owners the need to implement an effective commissioning processes and
4666
preventative maintenance programs.
4667
4668
An integrated approach to building and HVAC system design requires a balanced consideration of
4669
related indoor air quality, budget constraint, usability and environmental footprint impact. The
4670
integrated process requires:
4671
• Assessment of local environmental conditions that will affect the indoor IAQ (weather, pollution
4672
etc).
4673
• A clear understanding of the User/ owner IAQ requirements
4674
• The architectural/ civil features must be designed in concert with selected HVAC system options
4675
(to ensure adequate space allowance for mechanical and electrical equipment and access to
4676
appropriate outside air/ relief air/ exhaust air location etc.)
4677
• Computer modeling of appropriate building system options. The simulation should assess the
4678
interaction between the HVAC system and other related systems such as architectural features,
4679
structural systems (thermal mass), day lighting, artificial lighting, office equipment, building
4680
exhaust requirements etc., in order to optimize design and the operating energy efficiency.
4681
• Use separately-zoned HVAC systems to serve areas with different users, loads, orientations and
4682
hours of operation.
4683
• Program HVAC systems for occupied and unoccupied setback controls.
4684
• Keep computer server rooms and other 24/7 cooling loads on separate cooling systems when
4685
rooms require continuous cooling.
4686
• Locate thermostats or other sensors to cover areas of similar load.
4687
• Design for "diversity of use", assuming that not all spaces will be used at all times, with full
4688
occupancy or at full load.
4689
• Consider distributed air-handling mechanical rooms to reduce the size and complexity of
4690
ductwork systems. Special attention should be paid to noise control.
4691
• Evaluate the potential heat recovery by performing a lifetime-cost analysis.
4692
• Isolate building air intakes, placing them upwind and away from building exhaust air, loading
4693
dock, or parking lot vehicular exhaust air, as well as away from adjacent spray, combustion
4694
gases, sanitary vents, trash storage and other sources of undesirable air contaminants.
4695
• Locate and design air intakes for the best air supply source for the HVAC system and in
4696
favorable direction for full airside economizer mode operation, as well as for minimizing snow
4697
intake in winter.
4698
• Avoid use of ceiling plenums as return air ducts.
4699
• Provide low-leak outside-air dampers, which use separate dampers for minimum outside air and
4700
main outside air functions in order to accurately control minimum outside-air-intake quantities.
4701
Do not use main outside-air damper to set minimum fresh air level.
4702
• Provide modulating dampers to ensure minimum outside air.
4703
• Avoid rooftop units that preclude other roof top uses and add maintenance difficulty which may
4704
result in inefficient operation. Always install air-handling units in accessible locations where
4705
they can be maintained.
4706
4707
More information is provided in the section Chapter 2 Integrated Design and Construction Processes for
4708
Good IAQ.
4709
4710
Energy conservation and environmental impact
4711
Since the middle of the last century, HVAC design solutions generally involved the use of powerful and
4712
sometimes complex, HVAC systems to maintain acceptable thermal comfort and indoor air quality. This
4713
mechanical system approach to achieving thermal comfort and IAQ can consume large quantities of
4714
high grade energy and generate significant quantities of avoidable pollutants.
4715
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 164
Return to TOP
4716
HVAC systems are typically used to deal with both the thermal comfort (temperature/ humidity control)
4717
and the indoor air quality (pollutant control) goals within the occupied spaces. For example a VAV
4718
(variable air volume) reheat system deals with the occupied space thermal gains and losses by changing
4719
the temperature and/ or the quantity of air supplied into the occupied space. In some cases, changing the
4720
quantity and/ or the temperature of the supply air can cause significant indoor air quality and thermal
4721
comfort problems. There is a relatively new trend in HVAC system design to use separate/ decoupled
4722
systems to deal with these two environment design issues. An example of this type of decoupled HVAC
4723
system is the radiant heating and cooling with a separate displacement ventilation system. In this system,
4724
the radiant heating and cooling surfaces provide thermal comfort and the displacement ventilation
4725
provides indoor air quality.
4726
4727
After World War II, and the advent of central air conditioning and sealed building, using natural forces
4728
such as thermal mass, day lighting and natural ventilation was often seen by designers and owners as
4729
archaic design solutions. Today with rising energy costs and the worldwide movement toward buildings
4730
that employ “green” strategies, architects and engineers, mostly in Europe, are using advanced computer
4731
and modeling techniques to refine the physics of heating, cooling, ventilating and IAQ.
4732
4733
This emerging trend is also looking at energy in a different way, giving consideration to the quantity of
4734
energy used in buildings but also the quality of the energy used. An example of this type of design is
4735
using passive/ natural or low grade energy/ waste energy in lieu of fossil fuel/ electrical power. Other
4736
examples of this emerging design trend include the use of natural ventilation, free cooling, day lighting,
4737
mixed mode ventilation, displacement ventilation, thermal mass, water/ air economizers, etc.
4738
4739
The primary difference between present day conventional design and this emerging approach is in the
4740
design process. All members of the design team are required to work within an integrated framework,
4741
thinking about all design decisions within the context of occupant and building safety, thermal comfort,
4742
IAQ and the impact of the design decision upon the environment. A key example of this is the use of
4743
natural and or displacement ventilation. For natural and/or displacement ventilation to work, the
4744
building thermal solar gains and thermal losses have to be minimized to less than 30 Watts/m2.
4745
4746
Despite these trends, HVAC system designers are still often required, for numerous reasons, to use
4747
conventional HVAC systems such as VAV (variable air volume) with reheat, CV (constant volume)
4748
with reheat, FC (fan coil) and packaged HVAC equipment to air condition institutional, commercial and
4749
residential buildings. Clearly these conventional systems have their place and the designers can still
4750
apply low energy and improved indoor air quality principals within their design.
4751
4752
Thermal Comfort expectations
4753
Thermal comfort is a complex mix of physiology, psychology and culture. What is deemed acceptable
4754
will depend on activity and clothing level as well as temperatures, air speeds and humidity. Occupants
4755
who have more control over clothing, activity level and air speed will find a broader range of
4756
temperature and relative humidity acceptable.
4757
4758
Higher indoor temperatures do not necessarily mean that occupants will be less comfortable. In summer,
4759
increased air movement can provide an enhanced perception of thermal comfort, but consideration
4760
should be given to nuisance draughts.
4761
4762
In some areas with lower external night-time temperature, night ventilation strategy can be used to pre-
4763
cool the building structure. This lowers the mean radiant temperature and the indoor air temperature
4764
(because the cooler structure absorbs heat from the indoor air and warm surfaces). By lowering the mean
4765
radiant temperature, better comfort can be achieved although temperature in the space rises.
4766
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 165
Return to TOP
4767
Good HVAC design should also provide occupants with so-called adaptive opportunities to adjust their
4768
situation in response to changing conditions. This might involve:
4769
• using blinds or other moveable shading to cut out direct solar radiation
4770
• providing the opportunity to increase air movement by opening windows or the use of desk fans.
4771
Coupled with less formal dress codes, these opportunities allow occupants to adjust their environment to
4772
suit their own preferences, allowing greater freedom of choice than is usually practical in an air
4773
conditioned environment. However, this does require careful design of the windows, their position and
4774
opening mechanisms, such that occupants can readily adapt their environment.
4775
4776
Another important factor is that recent research has shown that in prolonged spells of warm weather,
4777
people's expectations for comfort change. More information is provided in Strategy 7.5 Provide
4778
Comfort Conditions that Enhance Occupant Satisfaction.
4779
4780
User/ Owner IEQ/ IAQ expectation
4781
Indoor environment quality (IEQ) is a generic term used to describe the physical and perceptual
4782
attributes of indoor spaces. These include the thermal, acoustic and visual properties of the environment,
4783
as well as the indoor air quality (IAQ). In a typical modern North American building, IAQ and IEQ are
4784
generally controlled by the building’s HVAC system making them mutually inclusive.
4785
4786
Building IEQ/ IAQ can have a significant affect on the health, well-being and comfort of building
4787
occupants, which in turn may impact on their productivity at work. IAQ can also have a direct impact on
4788
the integrity/ life of the building structure.
4789
4790
IEQ/ IAQ is characterized by:
4791
• physical/ measurable factors, such as ambient temperature, humidity and ventilation rates
4792
• air pollutant factors, such as pollutant levels and exposure times
4793
• human factors, such as occupant health status, individual sensitivity and personal control.
4794
4795
Major contributors to poor IAQ include:
4796
• inappropriate HVAC system selection
4797
• air/ moisture leaks in the building envelope
4798
• insufficient outside air supplied into the occupied spaces
4799
• inappropriate air intake location (bring in fumes/ pollutants)
4800
• off gassing of building materials
4801
• by products from office equipment
4802
• HVAC system operating performance and maintenance
4803
4804
Other factors that may contribute to poor IAQ include poor cleaning practices, poor moisture control
4805
(e.g. water leaks or persistent damp surfaces), human occupancy effects (e.g. odors), poorly designed
4806
enclosed garages and poor building maintenance.
4807
4808
Improved IEQ/ IAQ have the potential to reduce building maintenance costs, through a reduction in
4809
occupant complaints about IEQ-related issues. According to a study by Federspiel (2001), based on 575
4810
buildings in the USA, nearly one fifth of complaints to facilities managers were related to indoor
4811
environment issues. Federspiel (2001) estimated the potential savings in maintenance costs from
4812
reduced IAQ complaints alone to be $0.0035/ft2 per year. However, this saving needs to be offset
4813
against any increased costs of providing higher quality IEQ, such as increased maintenance cost.
4814
4815
4816
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 166
Return to TOP
Regional/ local and project Specific IAQ issues
4817
Regional prevailing conditions such as poor outside air quality, high humidity, cold/ hot outside
4818
temperatures, snow drifting, winds, etc. must be considered as part of the HVAC selection process.
4819
Dealing with these conditions may require special design considerations such as:
4820
4821
• Building Pressurization Control
4822
Positive building pressurization is critical to IAQ in the following applications:
4823
 Mechanically cooled buildings, particularly in warm humid climates (Figure XXX) - to
4824
reduce risk of condensation and mold growth in building cavities
4825
 Refrigerated buildings (refrigerated warehouses, ice arenas) in most climates – to reduce
4826
risk of condensation and mold growth in building cavities (not covered by this manual)
4827
 Any building located in an area with poor outdoor air quality (Figure 2) – to reduce risk of
4828
infiltration of untreated air
4829
 Buildings in cold climates to prevent condensation in building envelopes
4830
 Regional locations with high outside humidity, the building needs to be positively
4831
pressurized to ensure that the outside air does not penetrate the building envelope. The
4832
following maps are available from EPA showing Humid areas were buildings should have
4833
positive building pressurization to minimize risk of mold growth in building cavities and
4834
areas with poor air quality should also have positive building pressurization to minimize
4835
infiltration of untreated air.
4836
4837
Figure 1.2-D Humid areas should have positive
building pressurization to minimize risk of mold
growth in building cavities. (humid area outline to
be added)
Figure 1.2-E. Areas with poor air quality should have
positive building pressurization to minimize infiltration
of untreated air. This map shows areas that do not
attain National Ambient Air Quality Standards, but
other locations may have poor air quality in the
immediate vicinity of a particular project.
4838
The following types of HVAC systems are less likely to provide positive building pressurization and
4839
therefore more likely to be unsuitable for applications requiring positive pressure unless the
4840
recommended solutions are implemented:
4841
4842
System type
Causes of poor building
pressurization
Possible solutions
PTACs
PTHPs
FCUs
No outside air, or lack of positive control of
outside air volume
Provide separate conditioned make-up
air system, ducted to each exterior
space.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 167
Return to TOP
System type
Causes of poor building
pressurization
Possible solutions
UVs
Systems relying on continuous
exhaust to ventilate building by
infiltration or through passive air
inlets.
Negative building pressure is intrinsic to
design
Provide separate conditioned make-up
air system, ducted to each exterior
space, and use intermittent exhaust.
Systems with plenum returns
Depressurization of building envelope in
plenum area
Use ducted returns
Natural ventilation systems
Negative building pressure is required to
draw ventilation air in
Use systems in dry climates and in areas
with good outdoor air quality.
If used as part of a mixed mode system
in other areas, control natural ventilation
openings to prevent opening at times of
high humidity or low outdoor air quality.
Key: PTAC = packaged terminal air conditioner; PTHP = packaged terminal heat pump; FCU = fan coil unit; UV = unit ventilator
4843
4844
More information is provided in the section: Strategy 2.3 Maintain Proper Building Pressurization.
4845
4846
• Space Humidity Control
4847
Space humidity control is critical to IAQ in the following applications:
4848
 All buildings in climates with a significant amount of humid weather (Figure ###).
4849
 A space with large latent loads relative to sensible loads, such as below grade spaces,
4850
natatoriums and shower rooms (not covered in this guide).
4851
 Buildings with chilled beams, chilled ceilings or other sensible-only in-space cooling.
4852
 Buildings in cold climates with mechanical humidification.
4853
4854
4855
4856
Figure 1.2-F. Humid areas requiring space humidity control to minimize risk of mold growth in buildings.
4857
(humid area outline to be added – probably different from Figure 1.)
4858
4859
4860
4861
The following types of systems are less likely to provide proper humidity control and therefore more
4862
likely to be unsuitable for use in climates with a substantial number of humid days.
4863
4864
4865
4866
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 168
Return to TOP
System type
Causes of poor building
humidity control
Possible solutions
PTACs
PTHPs
FCUs (DX)
Constant volume operation satisfies
space temperature by cycling
cooling on and off or resetting
discharge air temperature and does
not provide adequate moisture
removal at part load conditions.
Provide separate conditioned make-
up air system ducted to each
exterior space, and use these
systems for space loads only.
(sensible cooling only??)
UVs
As above, but more so because
typically used in applications with
high minimum outdoor air flow rates
and high indoor latent loads.
Select UV with
•
Reheat (follow
requirements of ASHRAE
Standard 90.1) and space
RH control
•
Air-to-air enthalpy
recovery, or
•
Both
Properly size unit. Where does this
fit? –delete?
Constant volume RTUs, AHUs
without reheat
As above
Provide RTU or AHU with
•
Reheat (follow
requirements of ASHRAE
Standard 90.1) and space
RH control
•
Air-to-air enthalpy
recovery, or
•
Both
Properly size unit.
Natural ventilation systems
No ability to control humidity.
Use systems in dry climates.
If used as part of a mixed mode
system in other areas, control
natural ventilation openings to
prevent opening at times of high
humidity.
4867
Systems that do not provide separate humidity control should not be used in spaces with large latent
4868
loads and small sensible loads.
4869
4870
Buildings with chilled beams, chilled ceilings or other sensible-only in-space cooling must have controls
4871
that keep the sensible cooling units above the dew point temperature of the room air.
4872
4873
More information is provided in Strategy 2.4 Control Indoor Humidity.
4874
4875
• Particle Filtration Control
4876
Medium to high efficiency particle filtration is critical to IAQ in the following applications:
4877
 Buildings in areas with high levels of particulate matter in outdoor air (Figure ###)
4878
 Buildings where control of infectious agents is a concern (not covered in this guide)
4879
 Buildings or portions of a building requiring special control of the particular matter
4880
 Buildings where owners want to achieve enhanced IAQ
4881
4882
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 169
Return to TOP
Figure 1.2-G Areas with high levels of particulate matter in outdoor air. This map shows areas that do not attain
National Ambient Air Quality Standards for PM10 (left) or PM 2.5 (right), but other locations may have poor air
quality in the immediate vicinity of a particular project.
4883
HVAC System Options and General IAQ Considerations
4884
The following types of systems are less likely to be able to provide medium to high efficiency particle
4885
filtration and therefore more likely to be unsuitable for use in areas with poor outdoor air quality or
4886
where enhanced indoor air quality is desired.
4887
4888
System type
Causes of poor particle
filtration
Possible solutions
PTACs
PTHPs
UVs
These units have filter racks
designed for filters 1” deep or less,
and the unit fans have very limited
capacity to overcome static
pressures, so they are unable to
accommodate filters with MERV
levels beyond about 6 (???)
Select alternative system types.
Standard FCUs
Standard RTUs
These units have filter racks
designed for filters 2” deep or less,
and some unit fans have very limited
capacity to overcome static
pressures, so they are unable to
accommodate filters with MERV
levels beyond about 8
Select custom or specialized units
designed for higher levels of
filtration.
Natural ventilation
Openings typically unfiltered.
Limited ability to overcome static
pressure drop of medium to high
efficiency filters.
Use systems in areas with low levels
of outdoor particulate matter.
4889
More information is provided in Strategy 7.4 Provide Particle Filtration and Gas Phase Air Cleaning
4890
4891
A brief description of some of the available HVAC systems is as follows:
4892
4893
CV (constant air volume) with or without reheat
4894
Constant volume-variable temperature systems provide a zone with a fixed airflow rate. In most HVAC
4895
systems, the air volume is usually based on the design cooling load for the given zone. The ducting and
4896
air handling system are sized to match the heat gain from equipment, lights, exterior, and people. Air
4897
temperature can be varied in the air handling unit to meet the sensible heating or cooling requirement of
4898
the zone.
4899
4900
The total building supply air volume is based on the sum of all the zones. Even on a hot design day, not
4901
all zones need full cooling at the same time. Because the system cannot vary the supply air volume to
4902
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 170
Return to TOP
each zone, some zone air handling systems will have to supply warmer air to maintain the space set-
4903
point. Since there is no diversity in supply air volume, significantly more air is circulated than required
4904
to meet the cooling load, even at design conditions.
4905
4906
• IAQ Considerations – ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air
4907
Quality, provides a procedure for calculating the minimum outdoor air volume for a system
4908
serving multiple zones. Ventilation air is typically introduced at the air handling unit. Ventilation
4909
air for the space can be set at the minimum outdoor air rate because the unit is dedicated to a
4910
particular zone. Dehumidification can be a concern. Since the supply air temperature is
4911
modulated to meet the sensible cooling load only, humidity from ventilation air may not be
4912
removed, resulting in humid air being distributed throughout the building.
4913
4914
Constant volume-variable temperature systems are good for maintaining the correct amount of
4915
outdoor air for each zone but generally very poor energy performers and not recommended for
4916
Multizone applications.
4917
4918
4919
Figure 1.2-H
4920
4921
VAV (variable air volume) with reheat
4922
Variable air volume (VAV) with reheat systems provide conditioned air to each zone at a constant
4923
temperature, typically 55°F. The amount of air varies to match the heat gain from equipment, lights,
4924
exterior and people. At part load conditions, VAV systems supply only the necessary amount of
4925
conditioned air to each zone, saving significant fan energy. A damper (such as a pre-manufactured VAV
4926
box) adjusts air flow at each zone. A temperature sensor located in the space adjusts the damper to
4927
maintain the room temperature setting. When more dampers close, the duct system static pressure
4928
increases. The primary supply fan adjusts to maintain duct static pressure using discharge dampers or
4929
variable frequency drives (VFDs).
4930
A variation on a fixed supply air temperature is “supply air temperature reset” control which re-sets the
4931
supply air temperature to satisfy the zone calling for maximum cooling. This configuration decreases the
4932
energy wasteful reheat capacity.
4933
4934
• IAQ Considerations – ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air
4935
Quality, has a procedure for calculating the minimum outdoor air volume for a system serving
4936
multiple zones.
4937
4938
VAV systems present unique challenges in maintaining minimum outdoor air quantities:
4939
• Minimum outdoor air quantities are typically fixed. VAV systems vary the amount of
4940
supply air. As the supply air decreases, the percentage of outside air in the supply air
4941
stream must increase. Direct measurement of outside air quantities, can help maintain
4942
minimum outdoor air quantities throughout the operating range of a VAV system.
4943
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 171
Return to TOP
• Different zones need different amounts of outside air, but centralized air handling units
4944
provide only one outdoor air ratio.
4945
4946
4947
Figure 1.2-I
4948
4949
Fan Powered Boxes
4950
A variation of VAV is either series or parallel fan powered boxes. The diagram below shows a typical
4951
series fan powered box system. The fans in series boxes operate all the time and provide constant air
4952
volume to the space. The temperature supplied to the space is varied by mixing primary air with return
4953
air as required to meet the cooling load.
4954
4955
4956
Figure 1.2-J
4957
4958
Advantages to Series fan powered boxes are:
4959
o It is possible, even at design load conditions, to introduce plenum air into the series box
4960
so that 55°F enters the space even though 49°F primary air was distributed through the
4961
building. This reducing of the primary supply air temperature can significantly reduce the
4962
size of the air handling plant and distribution ductwork. This approach requires special
4963
attention to ensure that minimum ventilation rates are maintained at all times.
4964
o Constant air volume to the space ensures proper diffuser operation over all operating
4965
conditions.
4966
o Plenum air is used as reheat which is a form of heat recovery.
4967
o Overhead heating is easily done.
4968
o During unoccupied hours, only a local series box needs to be started to heat or cool a
4969
zone as opposed to starting up the main air handling unit with standard VAV.
4970
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 172
Return to TOP
4971
Disadvantages include:
4972
• The capital cost of the boxes
4973
• Using plenum air is not recommended due to entrainment of particular and gaseous matter.
4974
• The operational and energy cost of the fans in the boxes
4975
• Sound
4976
• Maintenance
4977
4978
Another fan powered box configuration is the parallel arrangement. In cooling mode, the box behaves
4979
like a typical VAV box. In the heating mode, the fan operates providing constant volume. The
4980
temperature is varied by mixing plenum air with primary air to meet the space conditions or by a heating
4981
coil mounted in the box.
4982
The following diagram is for a parallel fan powered box arrangement
4983
4984
4985
Figure 1.2-K
4986
4987
Advantages to parallel fan powered boxes are:
4988
• Box fans only operate when necessary.
4989
• Constant air volume to the space during heating ensures proper diffuser operation.
4990
• Plenum air is used as reheat which is a form of heat recovery.
4991
• Overhead heating is easily done.
4992
• During unoccupied hours, only a local series box needs to be started to heat or cool a zone as
4993
opposed to starting up the main air handling unit with standard VAV
4994
4995
Disadvantages include:
4996
• The capital cost of the boxes.
4997
• Using plenum air is not recommended due to entrainment of particular and gaseous matter.
4998
• The operational and energy cost of the fans in the boxes.
4999
• Sound; in particular, the starting and stopping of the fans in the ceiling plenum adds; and
5000
• Maintenance.
5001
5002
DD (Dual Duct system) dual fan
5003
Dual-duct/dual-fan systems are a variation of the more traditional dual-duct system. There is a dedicated
5004
cooling air handling unit and a dedicated heating air handling unit. Both units are variable air volume
5005
(VAV). Each unit is ducted to mixing boxes for each zone. The cooling air handling unit provides each
5006
zone mixing \ box with conditioned air at a constant temperature (typically 55°F). The amount of air is
5007
varied to match the heat gain from equipment, lights, exterior and people loads. At part load conditions,
5008
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 173
Return to TOP
the mixing box only supplies the minimum amount of conditioned air necessary to each zone, resulting
5009
in significant fan energy savings. In heating mode, the mixing box reduces the cold air volume to
5010
minimum and then modulates the hot air volume to meet the space conditions. As the mixing boxes
5011
modulate the hot and cold air, the duct system static pressures change. The supply fans are then
5012
modulated to maintain duct static pressure either by discharge dampers, inlet guide vanes, or variable
5013
frequency drives (VFDs).
5014
5015
• IAQ Considerations – ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air
5016
Quality, provides a procedure for calculating the minimum outdoor air volume for a system
5017
serving multiple zones. Ventilation air is introduced at the cooling air handling unit. Two issues
5018
occur with VAV systems. First, as the total supply air to a space is reduced, the percentage
5019
ventilation air is also reduced. The central air handling system must be able to maintain a fixed
5020
amount of outdoor air while varying the supply air volume. Secondly, different zones will
5021
require different percentages of ventilation air, but the centralized air handling unit can provide
5022
only one outdoor air ratio.
5023
5024
5025
Figure 1.2-L
5026
5027
Multi zone systems
5028
Multi-zone systems provide each zone with constant volume-variable temperature air. The air
5029
temperature is varied to meet the heat gain from equipment, lights, exterior and people loads. The air
5030
temperature can also be raised to provide heating to the zone, either by a heating coil in the air handling
5031
unit or in each of the zone duct systems.
5032
.
5033
A modification of the two-deck system is the three-deck system, with the third deck being bypass air
5034
(return air with ventilation air introduced). The decks are arranged in such away that when the zone is
5035
calling for heating, bypass air and hot deck air are mixed to meet the zone requirements. In cooling, air
5036
from the cooling deck and bypass air mix to meet the cooling load. The result: no simultaneous heating
5037
and cooling.
5038
5039
• IAQ Considerations – ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air
5040
Quality, provides a procedure for calculating the minimum outdoor air volume for a system
5041
serving multiple zones. Ventilation air is introduced at the air handling unit. Each zone receives
5042
constant air flow with a fixed percentage of ventilation air. Different zones, however, will
5043
require different percentages of ventilation air, but the centralized air handling unit can provide
5044
only one outdoor air ratio.
5045
5046
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 174
Return to TOP
5047
Figure 1.2-M
5048
5049
Fan coil Systems
5050
Fan coils have a fan and one coil (two-pipe) or two coils (four-pipe) in small units distributed
5051
throughout the building. Fan coils are dedicated to each zone and are matched to the zone design load.
5052
Four-pipe fan coil systems allow some zones to be heated while other zones are being cooled. Two-pipe
5053
fan coil systems cost less, but are less flexible. The supply air temperature varies to meet the heating or
5054
cooling needs of the zone.
5055
5056
• IAQ Considerations – Fan coil systems usually need a dedicated ventilation system to supply
5057
each zone with ventilation air. ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 provides procedures for calculating
5058
ventilation rates to maintain minimum acceptable indoor air quality. For cooler climates, the
5059
ventilation air must be tempered. It is possible not to condition the ventilation air, depending on
5060
the design wet bulb temperature, and add the ventilation cooling load to the fan coil load. This
5061
can reduce the capital cost; however, when a two-position valve cycles off in the fan coil,
5062
unconditioned air enters directly into the zone and can create a humidity problem. Precooling the
5063
ventilation air in the ventilation unit reduces the load on the fan coil and helps avoid
5064
dehumidification problems.
5065
5066
Ventilation systems are often ducted directly to the return air connection of the fan coils. By
5067
balancing the ventilation system during commissioning, proper ventilation rate to each zone is
5068
provided.
5069
5070
5071
Figure 1.2-N
5072
5073
5074
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 175
Return to TOP
Vertical Self-Contained Systems
5075
Vertical self-contained systems are based on specially designed air handling systems spread throughout
5076
the building with integral air conditioning. Each unit has multiple water-cooled DX refrigeration
5077
circuits. Part load humidity control may be a concern, depending on the unit/system configuration.
5078
5079
Air distribution can be constant volume or variable air volume (VAV). VAV is the most common for
5080
office applications.
5081
5082
• IAQ Considerations – ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 provides procedures for calculating
5083
ventilation rates to maintain minimum acceptable indoor air quality. If the vertical self-contained
5084
units have air-side economizers, ventilation air can be introduced at the air handling unit.
5085
5086
When water-side economizers are used, a dedicated ventilation unit is often utilized. The ventilation unit
5087
can be placed on the roof and deliver the correct amount of ventilation air through a shaft to each floor’s
5088
mechanical room.
5089
5090
Energy recovery in the ventilation unit is possible and greatly improves the operating cost of the
5091
building.
5092
5093
5094
Figure 1.2-O
5095
5096
Dedicated outside air systems
5097
A dedicated outside air system normally splits the buildings ventilation (IAQ) requirements from the
5098
thermal comfort requirements. This is achieved by having a dedicated air handling unit supply fixed
5099
amounts of outside air into all of the occupied zones and use independent terminal sensible cooling and
5100
heating units to deal with the space thermal loads.
5101
5102
The dedicated outside air handling unit normally uses 100% outside air and the air handling unit can be
5103
used to humidify, dehumidify, filter, heat reclaim, preheat and cooling the outside air before supplying
5104
the air into the occupied zones.
5105
5106
Carbon dioxide sensors can be used to reduce the amount of outside air delivered to an occupancy zone
5107
as the number of people in the zone decreases.
5108
5109
Dedicated outside air systems are the most effective means available for ensuring delivery of designed
5110
or desired quantities of outdoor air.
5111
5112
5113
5114
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 176
Return to TOP
5115
Figure 1.2-P
5116
5117
HVAC system selection procedure
5118
System choice should be informed in that there may not be one clear preferred HVAC system for any
5119
given application. HVAC system selection cannot be done in a vacuum and should be done as part of the
5120
integrated design process. As stated above, system comparison analysis needs to be undertaken for every
5121
project considering the project constrains such as:
5122
• Budget constraints both in terms of first and operating costs
5123
• Project location
5124
• Project use
5125
• Client/ end user expectations, etc.
5126
• Required flexibility
5127
• Building constraint, such as floor space for equipment, maintenace space, plenum space, roof
5128
space.
5129
• Heating and cooling loads
5130
• Smoke control.
5131
• Ability to add zones and plant capacity
5132
• Maintenance cost etc
5133
5134
Some HVAC selection criteria are YES/NO or gauged e.g. one system may be slightly better then
5135
another, but both system options will meet the basic criteria. The following table is a sample analysis
5136
done for an office building for a particular set of project circumstances:

5139
5140
Mixed-mode ventilation (See also Strategy 8.4 Use Natural / Mixed Mode Ventilation)
5141
In various parts of the world, designers are using natural ventilation or a combination of natural and
5142
mechanical ventilation systems to provide the required building IEQ. The combination of natural and
5143
mechanical ventilation is called mixed-mode ventilation. Mixed-mode ventilation recognizes that not all
5144
parts of a building have to be treated in exactly the same way. Different strategies may be applied to
5145
different parts of a building, or at different times. This is discussed in greater detail in CIBSE AM13
5146
(22).
5147
5148
The various approaches to mixed-mode ventilation are described below. These approaches are not
5149
mutually exclusive, in other words, several of them can be combined in a single building.
5150
1. Contingency mixed-mode: where flexibility of space is required, it is important to 'design-in' the
5151
potential to upgrade the services so that additional cooling can be installed to meet tenant
5152
requirements or the changing climate. This provision will include space allowances for
5153
additional distribution systems incorporated into floor and/or ceiling voids. The cost of the
5154
additional flexibility will need to be set against the savings in initial and operating costs accruing
5155
from the avoidance of unnecessary air conditioning.
5156
5157
2. Zoned mixed-mode: recognizes that different parts of any building will have different uses. Air
5158
conditioning is provided only in certain parts of the building that have the greater need. For
5159
instance in areas of lower heat gain, or those that are occupied for short periods, heating and
5160
natural ventilation only could be provided. Such an approach relies on the requirements of the
5161
individual spaces being reasonably constant over the life of the building. Such an approach can
5162
also create tensions, if one group of occupants feels that another group has been provided with
5163
what they believe is a better working environment.
5164
5165
3. Changeover mixed-mode: recognizes that the cooling requirements of any space will vary from
5166
season to season. An example of changeover mixed-mode would be to use mechanical
5167
ventilation in extreme weather conditions (hot and cold), but rely on natural ventilation in milder
5168
weather. This reduces the problem of cold draughts in winter, and allows the use of mechanical
5169
night ventilation for precooling in hot summer periods.
5170
5171
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 179
Return to TOP
4. Concurrent mixed-mode: provides mechanical ventilation and natural conditioning
5172
simultaneously. The mechanical system is designed to provide the fresh air requirement, with
5173
additional ventilation by opening windows to provide summer cooling. The mechanical system
5174
can also provide night ventilation with the windows closed, which avoids the associated security
5175
problem. If mechanical cooling is also provided, care should be taken to avoid excess ventilation
5176
in very hot weather.
5177
5178
Displacement ventilation systems
5179
Displacement ventilation uses the natural buoyancy of warm air to provide improved ventilation. First
5180
developed for use in industrial buildings, displacement ventilation now enjoys an increasing percentage
5181
of market share for many applications throughout the world. It is viewed as proven technology in
5182
Scandinavian countries, where it has been in use since the 1960’s. In North America, interest in this type
5183
of system is rapidly growing.
5184
5185
Displacement ventilation differs from conventional HVAC systems in several important ways.
5186
Conventional overhead air disribution systems can be thought of as “mixing” systems. Air is supplied at
5187
the ceiling at a relatively high velocity and at a temperature about 20°F below the desired room
5188
temperature. The supply air mixes with the room air to provide a nearly uniform temperature. Because
5189
of the mixing effect, used room air recirculates, resulting in relatively low ventilation effectiveness. In a
5190
perfect conventional system, the air being exhausted from the space has the same temperature and
5191
contaminant concentration as the room air. In contrast, displacement ventilation systems supply air at or
5192
near the floor level at a low velocity and at a temperature only slightly below the desired room
5193
temperature. The cooler supply air “displaces” the warmer room air, creating a zone of fresh, cool air at
5194
the occupied level. Heat and lighter contaminants produced by activities in the space rise to the ceiling
5195
where they are exhausted from the space. The air being exhausted from the space has a higher
5196
temperature and higher contaminant concentration than the room air.
5197
5198
5199
Figure 1.2-Q
5200
5201
5202
At the air handler, the displacement system is similar to the conventional system. There is, however, one
5203
important difference. The displacement system may require the uses a face and bypass or reheat
5204
configuration at the cooling coil if/ when humidity control is required at the higher supply temperature
5205
e.g. when necessary, a portion of the supply air is dehumidified, then mixed with the bypass air to
5206
provide the required supply air temperature and humidity.
5207
5208

5253
Strategy 1.3 – Establish a Project Schedule that Facilitates Achievement of Good IAQ
5254
5255
5256
Overview
5257
Establishing a good project construction schedule will help ensure the achievement of good IAQ for the
5258
building project. A building owner can have the best intentions, the design team can provide a great
5259
building design and the contractors can do their best execution, but if the schedule is too compressed,
5260
IAQ is not taken into account in sequencing tasks and project management the IAQ of the finished
5261
project may not meet expectations. Installing construction materials that can absorb moisture before the
5262
building is dried in, storing and/or exposing of construction materials to the incorrect elements prior to
5263
installation, and starting and operating of HVAC equipment for temporary heating or cooling during
5264
construction are just a few of the items that will jeopardize the Indoor Air Quality of the final building
5265
project. A good IAQ plan coupled with proper scheduling and sequencing of the construction project
5266
will help ensure achievement of good IAQ.
5267
5268
The schedule must allow for adequate time to complete the construction activities and properly sequence
5269
them. Involve the design and construction team early in the scheduling process. This could include
5270
input from the construction team during the design phase to help with sequencing and duration of
5271
construction activities. This could also develop into proper construction material selection to match the
5272
sequencing and scheduling of the construction activities. A thought to keep in mind is that typically an
5273
accelerated project schedule leads to poor achievement of good project IAQ.
5274
5275
Building Conception
5276
Early Planning & Organization
5277
The day the owner determines the need for a building is the day the building project scheduling should
5278
start. Starting earlier in the planning phases is a critical element in coupling proper IAQ management
5279
with the scheduling and sequencing of the building project. The budget need to be set, the required time
5280
of building occupancy needs to be determined, and the project team (Design professionals, contractors,
5281
TAB, Cx) needs to be selected and assembled. The earlier the project team is assembled the earlier
5282
schedule input from all team members is available. This could help alleviate the guessing game and
5283
“rule of thumbs” used in the scheduling process. Every project is different and has its own challenges
5284
and having the experience and knowledge of the COMPLETE project team early will aid in the
5285
scheduling process and attainment of good project IAQ.
5286
5287
Involve the design and construction team early in the scheduling process. This could include input from
5288
the construction team during the design phase to help with sequencing and duration of construction
5289
activities. This could also develop into proper construction material selection to match the sequencing
5290
and scheduling of the construction activities; eliminating long lead time products for a short construction
5291
schedule or selecting the proper type of materials that will accommodate the required sequence of
5292
construction activities. As shown in figure xx, the earlier the decisions are made in the project phase the
5293
more influence the decision will have and the cost of action or solution will be minimized.
5294
5295
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 182
Return to TOP
5296
Figure 1.3-E: Project Phases of Influence
5297
5298
Project Incentives/Goals
5299
Make sure that all project incentives/goals promote scheduling that helps achieve good IAQ. The
5300
project team members must understand the importance of project IAQ and the effects the construction
5301
process has on attaining good IAQ. The project incentive of offering a monetary bonus for early
5302
completion of a project could impact the IAQ of the project. A main focus becomes completing a
5303
project early to obtain the monetary bonus and improper sequencing of construction activities could
5304
occur to expedite construction and completion of the project. An on time project is not always a quality
5305
project. Any project incentive/goal must be carefully evaluated to make sure all aspects of attaining the
5306
goals does not adversely affect the project IAQ.
5307
5308
Schematic Design
5309
5310
Design Development
5311
Construction Products/Materials Selection
5312
Design and utilized construction products/materials that will aid in proper construction scheduling. If
5313
certain areas or portions of the building are required to be constructed before the building can be dried in,
5314
make sure that the construction methods and materials selected are capable of withstanding the exposure
5315
to the temperature and moisture elements. Having to install drywall shafts; installing duct sound
5316
attenuators or any hydroscopic material that can retain moisture before the building is dried in can create
5317
mold issues. Think about sequencing and constructability while designing. Make sure that products or
5318
materials that are selected have lead/delivery times that will accommodate the construction schedule. If
5319
products or materials are selected and wetting of the products/materials occurs, then adequate drying
5320
time should be provided for in the schedule. In addition, make sure that any alternative product/material
5321
selections are “equal to”, in all regards, to the base selection to prevent any quality or functional issues
5322
from occurring.
5323
5324
Equipment Access and Installation logistics
5325
Proper design and layout of the building is required to allow for equipment access and installation
5326
logistics. If there is building equipment (HVAC, food service, transportation, etc.) that will be utilized
5327
in the building, make sure that the equipment can be installed in the building when the equipment will
5328
be protected from exposure to the elements. Having to install equipment early on a project due to
5329
physical limitations, no access or paths available once walls, ceilings or roof are installed, will expose
5330
the equipment to the elements that can damage the equipment and/or promote bacterial growth. This
5331
can also create issues for maintenance and replacement of the equipment at a later date. If there are no
5332
alternatives to installing equipment early make sure that the equipment is protected from the elements.
5333
Think about sequencing and constructability while designing.
5334
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 183
Return to TOP
5335
Phasing of Projects
5336
Phasing of new construction projects and remodels can be very challenging when trying to maintain the
5337
project IAQ goals. If proper isolation of the areas to be occupied and the areas under construction are
5338
not addressed, then exposure of the occupied areas to construction activities (noise, dust, moisture, odors,
5339
building not dried in completely, etc.) could lead to long term IAQ issues with the building by
5340
introducing lingering contaminants like mold and by sensitizing occupants. Proper construction and
5341
sealing of the areas or possibly proper space pressurization should be utilized to help isolate the phased
5342
areas. See Strategy 6.4 Maintain Proper Pressure Relationships between Spaces.
5343
5344
Completion of the HVAC systems must match the rest of the schedule in the phased areas. The
5345
complete HVAC duct system and piping system must be able to be installed and operated for the
5346
occupied areas without damage or contamination (operation of partly loaded systems, excessive system
5347
pressures, exposure to construction activities, etc.) to the HVAC systems. A single HVAC system (one
5348
air handling unit for example) serving a multiple phased project would typically have one common
5349
return air system for the entire building/system. If one area is to be occupied, the unit will be put into
5350
operation and the unit will be exposed, through the return air path, to areas still under construction. In
5351
addition, it must be verified that the system can be operated partially loaded. Piping systems must also
5352
be addressed. The chilled water and heating hot water systems must be able to be completed or isolated
5353
and allowed to be operated partially loaded. Plumbing systems (sewer, domestic water, venting, etc.)
5354
must be able to be piped and connected per the phased areas allowing for a functional system that is
5355
partially installed.
5356
5357
HVAC system selection, building material selection and construction procedures to be utilized should all
5358
be reviewed when phasing a project is to be considered. Some project activities might not be able to be
5359
completed due to the phasing of the project. The HVAC test and balance work requires that the HVAC
5360
system is complete and functional to be able to be properly balance the system. The bid specifications
5361
and drawings should clearly identify the areas to be phased and the sequence of phasing and occupation
5362
of the building.
5363
5364
Construction Documents
5365
IAQ Schedule Requirements
5366
IAQ needs to be a consideration in the sequencing and scheduling part of the specification and on the
5367
required project submittal of the schedule. This could be in the form of an IAQ project plan addressing
5368
items that could affect project IAQ during the construction process. All IAQ items and issues can be
5369
addressed and applied to the construction sequencing and scheduling process. Once the IAQ items are
5370
applied to the project schedule, they can be reviewed and updated at project construction meetings.
5371
Project IAQ should become a construction meeting agenda item.
5372
5373
Construction
5374
Sequencing of Construction Activities
5375
The schedule must allow for proper sequencing of the construction activities and allow time for all
5376
phases/trade of construction to happen. Give each trade ample time to complete their tasks. Proper
5377
construction sequencing to prevent moisture, mold and contamination problems is possible. A good
5378
example of improper sequencing would be installation of construction materials (dry wall, insulation)
5379
that can absorb and retain moisture before the building is dried in or operating the permanent HVAC
5380
equipment during construction contaminating the HVAC system with dirt and debris. Environmental
5381
conditions that will minimize the effects of moisture damage and contamination during construction
5382
should be established by the design team and stated in the contract documents. Such conditions include
5383
outlining minimum thresholds of acceptance for vapor retarder, gypsum wallboard, and interior finish
5384
installation, protection of installed products during construction, as well as for mechanical systems
5385
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 184
Return to TOP
functionality. In addition. once construction is complete, to safeguard against future moisture and mold
5386
related problems, the design team should review the information for certification of compliance with the
5387
contract design documents and overall satisfactory performance of the architectural and mechanical
5388
building systems. (Odom, DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 Mold and Moisture Prevention, Washington,
5389
DC: National Council of Architectural Registration Boards)
5390
5391
Protect the finished products during construction. Once construction is complete allow for airing and
5392
flush out of furniture, carpets, walls, etc.
5393
5394
Schedule Compression
5395
Schedule compression can adversely affect good project IAQ. The schedule must allow for proper and
5396
adequate time to complete the construction activities. If the project schedule has been delayed, careful
5397
consideration must be made when trying to compress the schedule to meet the contract completion date.
5398
The project IAQ and quality of construction can be compromised if the process at end of project is
5399
compressed. Get project IAQ on the project construction meeting agenda to keep all project team
5400
members involved with project IAQ. Whenever schedule compression is discussed or implemented,
5401
questions and concerns need to be raised on the implications and effects the schedule compression could
5402
have on project IAQ.
5403
5404
[Note: Insert Examples of what can go wrong.]
5405
5406
Storage of Construction Materials
5407
Construction materials should be stored so that the materials are not exposed to the elements.
5408
5409
Staged entry of materials for Magee - Stage entry of materials/activities: delay installation of fleecy
5410
absorptive materials until relatively high emission activities (e.g. Painting/staining; use of caulks,
5411
sealants, adhesives) have been completed.
5412
5413
Operation of Permanent HVAC Equipment during Construction
5414
Operating the permanent HVAC equipment during construction should be avoided. Introduction of
5415
moisture, dirt and debris into the HVAC system occurs when the permanent HVAC system is utilized
5416
during construction. Temporary HVAC equipment should be utilized during the construction phase for
5417
all heating, ventilation, cooling and dehumidification purposes. The project construction site needs to be
5418
kept at correct tempered conditions to allow for proper construction procedures. If the construction
5419
environment is kept at the proper conditions; cleanliness, temperature, and humidity, the worker will be
5420
more productive, the construction materials will perform as specified (adhesives, paints, drywall, etc.),
5421
the quality of the construction will be improved and the IAQ for the project will be improved. Strategy
5422
1.4 Manage Construction and Renovation to Preserve IAQ and SMACNA technical papers have more
5423
information regarding the operation of permanent HVAC equipment during construction.
5424
5425
Inspection Access
5426
Allow time for inspection of construction assemblies and proper access for the inspections. Do not
5427
cover up joint details, piping, etc. prior to testing and/or inspections. Examples include pipe leaks, wall
5428
leaks, duct not insulated or ducts not sealed or connected.
5429
5430
Post Construction
5431
Building Flush Out
5432
Flush out of a phased project is difficult. Tailor the flush out to the schedule of when activities with an
5433
impact on IAQ are performed. For instance, when adhesives are utilize or paint has been applied or
5434
furniture has been moved in, the flush out should occur to remove the harmful chemicals. The flush out
5435
can also base it on emissions profile of materials, if this data is part of the design / submittals.
5436
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 185
Return to TOP
5437
The required time for flush out must be determined. If the flush out is based on 100% outside air, the
5438
HVAC equipment must be sized to treat the outside air (dehumidification, heating, cooling, etc.) and
5439
prevent any environmental condition concerns inside the building. If the HVAC equipment cannot
5440
accommodate 100% outside air due to the loads, then the flush out time must be extended utilizing a
5441
smaller percentage of outside air. As noted in “Hensley Field Operations Center Construction Indoor
5442
Air Quality” by Michael Kawecki the HVAC system design was based on 30% outside air and the two
5443
week USGBC LEED flush out time (based on 100% outside air) would have been extended to ten weeks
5444
due to the HVAC equipment sizing. Also, if a water side economizer is utilized there is no means for
5445
100% OA and the flush out cycle will be extended.
5446
5447
Retrofits and Remodels
5448
Coordination and communication with the building owner is imperative. The scheduling of construction
5449
activities that will create sound issues, odor issues or particulate matter issues should occur when it will
5450
not disturb the building occupants. Notify the occupants well in advance of the start of construction
5451
activities and provide them a way to report any IAQ issues. Have a plan in place to respond to any
5452
reported IAQ issues.
5453
5454
5455
5456
5457
5458
5459
5460

Page 186
Return to TOP
5461

Strategy 1.5 – Provide High Quality O&M Documentation and Training Focused on IAQ
5506
5507
