OBJECTIVE 2 – CONTROL MOISTURE IN BUILDING ASSEMBLIES
5522
5523
5524
Key elements of moisture control are:
5525
5526
• Strategy 2.1 – Limit Penetration of Liquid Water into the Building Envelope
5527
• Strategy 2.2 – Limit Condensation of Water Vapor within the Building Envelope & on Interior
5528
Surfaces
5529
• Strategy 2.3 – Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
5530
• Strategy 2.4 – Control Indoor Humidity
5531
• Strategy 2.5 – Select Materials, Equipment, and Assemblies that can Withstand Repeated Wettings
5532
in Areas that are Unavoidably Wet
5533
• Strategy 2.6 – Consider Impact of Landscaping and Indoor Plants on Moisture and Contaminant
5534
Levels
5535
5536
5537
5538
See also these closely related Strategies:
5539
5540
• Strategy 1.4 – Manage Construction and Renovation to Preserve IAQ
5541
• Strategy 4.1 – Control Moisture and Dirt in Air Handling Systems
5542
• Strategy 4.4 – Control Moisture Associated with Piping and Plumbing Fixtures
5543
5544
5545
5546
5547
5548
5549
5550
5551
5552
5553
5554
5555
[REMAINDER OF PAGE LEFT INTENTIALLY BLANK]
5556
5557
5558
5559
5560
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 189
Return to TOP
5561
Strategy 2.1 - Limit Penetration of Liquid Water into the Building Envelope
5562
5563
[Editor’s Note: Strategy not yet updated form 60% Draft ]
5564
5565
Design teams must understand the problems that water causes in buildings and the dynamics of moisture
5566
sources, moisture mitigation, and moisture control. That knowledge must be reflected in the design
5567
documents, building drawings, and specifications. Good design is a prerequisite for a building that
5568
resists moisture problems; however, good design alone is not enough. The design must be implemented
5569
correctly during construction. Moisture in buildings is a major contributor to mold growth, unhealthy
5570
buildings, and poor indoor air quality. Wetting of building walls and rainwater leaks are major causes of
5571
water infiltration. Preventive and remedial measures include rainwater tight detail design; selection of
5572
building materials with appropriate water transmission characteristics; and proper field workmanship
5573
quality control. This strategy provides guidance on design and construction to protect buildings from
5574
penetration of liquid water – rain, snowmelt, runoff and groundwater.
5575
5576
Site Drainage
5577
Water from rain or snowmelt can infiltrate a building, damaging the structure and its contents. Properly
5578
designed site drainage avoids damage to the building and the potential need for costly remediation. The
5579
site drainage design must create a controlled condition to help move water away from the building(s).
5580
Unnecessary or large impervious surfaces should be avoided. The use of alternative, relatively
5581
permeable paving materials (e.g. porous pavement, modular porous pavers, gravel, cobbles, wood,
5582
mulch, brick, natural stone) will allow more water to infiltrate, thus reducing the size and cost of
5583
systems to manage runoff (water that does not infiltrate into the soil). Grading can be used to slow
5584
runoff velocity and achieve a more balanced infiltration rate. Topography helps to determine the
5585
amount, direction, and rate of runoff. Ensure site drainage is moving away from the building(s), water is
5586
not allowed to pond at low points or in low areas (unless planned for), and the finished floor is elevated
5587
enough so that water will not back up into the building if the drainage systems are blocked.
5588
5589
Identify and design water runoff management approaches appropriate for the site’s characteristics.
5590
Potential approaches to use include infiltration control methods such as swales or infiltration trenches
5591
and retention or detention control methods such as wet or dry ponds.
5592
5593
5594
5595
Figure 2.1-B
5596
5597
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 190
Return to TOP
5598
Foundation Design
5599
Foundations are vulnerable to penetration of liquid water for a number of reasons: water from rain and
5600
plumbing leaks is drawn by gravity to building foundations; basements are often finished and use details
5601
that put materials vulnerable to mold in contact with concrete (or sometimes wood) that is likely to get
5602
wet; crawl spaces and basements have more problems because they are holes in the ground and have
5603
more extensive contact with soil than do slab-on-grade foundations.
5604
5605
Many moisture problems can be avoided by properly designing the foundation. Moisture problems
5606
associated with improperly designed foundations can be difficult and expensive to identify and fix, can
5607
facilitate growth of mold that create the potential for health problems, and can be a liability for building
5608
owners.
5609
5610
The foundation should be designed to prevent rainwater and ground water intrusion. Whether the
5611
building has a slab-on-grade foundation, crawl spaces or a basement, plan the surrounding slope to
5612
divert water away from the building. Design below-grade drainage systems to divert water away from
5613
the foundation and specify capillary breaks to keep water from wicking through the foundation to
5614
moisture-sensitive materials (e.g. wooden framing and paper-covered gypsum board).
5615
5616
Items that should be included in the plans are: maximum rainfall or snow melt assumptions; drainage
5617
surface areas including shapes, slopes, superstructures, or other obstructions; estimated water flows; and
5618
the location of all conduits.
5619
5620
5621
Figure 2.1-C
5622
5623
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 191
Return to TOP
5624
Wall Design
5625
Moisture control is an important aspect of designing an integrated building envelope. Failure to
5626
properly design walls to manage moisture and to integrate moisture management system features with
5627
those of other building envelope components, such as the roof and foundation, can lead to serious
5628
moisture-related damage and mold growth. Solving problems resulting from poorly designed walls can
5629
necessitate the replacement of multiple building components leading to high repair costs. This strategy
5630
deals with control of liquid water. Control of moist air and related condensation is covered in Strategy
5631
2.2 Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces.
5632
5633
Exterior walls should be designed to manage rain water, protecting their inner portions from direct rain
5634
and seepage through the cladding. A pen test should be used to verify the continuity of the drainage
5635
plane from the intersection with the roof, through flashings, and around penetrations to the foundation.
5636
Sectional views should be provided to illustrate continuity of the drainage plane from the roof assembly
5637
through the above and below grade walls to the footings. Two dimensional sections should be provided
5638
for intersections with two components (for example, the intersection of a wall with the roof of a lower
5639
section of the building, or with a plaza) and three dimensional sections for intersections with three or
5640
more components. Details should be provided for all penetrations including windows, doors, piping
5641
penetrations, etc.
5642
5643
5644
Figure 2.1-D
5645
5646
Roof and Ceiling Assembly Design
5647
Improper detailing of the roof and ceiling assemblies may result in unwanted water intrusions leading to
5648
damage to the building and its contents. Failure to properly design the roof can lead to the need for
5649
more frequent costly roof maintenance or repair and a decreased lifespan of the building structure.
5650
5651
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 192
Return to TOP
In order to collect and dispose of rainwater, slope the roof to drain rainwater toward collection and
5652
disposal sites and design the roof drainage systems with sufficient runoff-handling capacity. This can be
5653
achieved by installing external gutter and downspout drainage systems or internal roof drainage systems.
5654
5655
Design penetrations, parapets, and roof and wall intersections to prevent the entry of rainwater.
5656
Drainage layers must maintain integrity at joints and penetrations, where the enclosure is the most
5657
susceptible to moisture problems.
5658
5659
Drawings and specifications must detail: joints between pieces of roofing (e.g., how shingles overlap,
5660
methods for sealing seams in membrane roofs); joints in the inner layer of moisture protection (e.g., how
5661
roofing paper overlaps), if used in the section; and continuity of roofing and, if present, the inner layer
5662
of moisture protection with flashing for curbs, skylights, pipes, ducts, hatches, and other roof
5663
penetrations.
5664
5665
In climate zones 5, 6, and 7, control ice dams. Ice dams are formed when melted snow runs down a
5666
sloped roof to an overhang that is not over conditioned space. Meltwater freezes on the cold roof edge
5667
and forms a dam. The trough behind the ice dam allows subsequent snow-melt to pool behind it. Water
5668
then backs under the shingles and runs down through the wall or ceiling. Ice dams are usually caused by
5669
warm air that bypasses the roof insulation and warms the roof deck above the freezing temperature. To
5670
minimize formation of ice dams:
5671
• Provide a carefully detailed, tested air barrier in the roof system,
5672
• Drain un-vented, low-slope roofs to interior drains,
5673
• Insulate un-vented roofs that slope to eave drainage with spray or board foam (Note: Special
5674
attention must be paid to air sealing foam board in this situation.), and
5675
• Use vented roofs that slope to eave drainage.
5676
5677
5678
Figure 2.1-E
5679
5680
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 193
Return to TOP
5681
Construction
5682
Verify that the design is executed correctly during construction by implementing a quality control
5683
program. The requirements of and responsibility for providing, reviewing, and accepting submittals,
5684
shop drawings, proposed solutions, and scheduled inspections and testing must be clearly presented in
5685
the design documents.
5686
5687
Construction-phase storm water management controls should be implemented to keep the site dry during
5688
construction and to ensure that the site drainage design features are constructed correctly and operating
5689
properly. (See Strategy 1.4 Manage Construction and Renovation to Preserve IAQ).
5690
5691
Verification
5692
Verify that all requirements have been included in the building design by reviewing the plans for the
5693
appropriate sectional and detail drawings.
5694
5695
Verify that the requirements are properly executed by implementing a quality control program.
5696
5697
Operation and Maintenance
5698
Water management systems should be maintained properly after construction and building occupancy.
5699
5700
5701
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 194
Return to TOP
5702
Strategy 2.2 - Limit Condensation of Water Vapor within the Building Envelope and on
5703
Interior Surfaces
5704
5705
Design
5706
Airtightness Design Requirements
5707
The design of building enclosures to avoid/reduce infiltration and exfiltration requires air retarder (air
5708
barrier) technology be utilized; this relies upon the respect of and implementation of four design
5709
requirements:
5710
• Maximum acceptable air permeance of materials, assemblies and the whole building.
5711
• Continuity
5712
• Structural support
5713
• Durability
5714
5715
5716
Air Barrier Design Requirements
5717
Primarily adapted from the WBDG Paper “Air Barrier Systems in Buildings” by Wagdy Anis, FAIA
5718
5719
1. Introduction
5720
The problems created by infiltration and exfiltration in buildings are reviewed below, and the design
5721
considerations of an air barrier system to control the problems are reviewed; the paper explains the air
5722
pressures on buildings, the fundamentals of controlling those pressures, air barrier material requirements,
5723
combination “air and vapor barriers,” and the required properties of air barriers systems. Specific
5724
designs will be reviewed, and warm-side air and vapor barriers vs. cold-side air barrier systems
5725
compared. The complexities of the “airtight drywall approach,” or “ADA,” (Lstiburek and Lischkoff,
5726
1986) will be discussed. Finally, the paper will review roof air barrier concepts.
5727
5728
2. Description
5729
Infiltration and exfiltration of air in buildings have serious consequences, because they are uncontrolled;
5730
the infiltrating air is untreated and can therefore entrain pollutants, allergens and bacteria into buildings.
5731
The accompanying change in air pressures can disrupt the delicate pressure relationships between spaces
5732
that HVAC systems create by design, in buildings such as hospitals, where patients’ very lives may
5733
depend upon maintaining those relationships, and labs, where pollutant control is essential. Disrupted
5734
air pressure relationships can move pollutants from spaces where they should be contained, into other
5735
spaces where they are not desired. For example, pollutants can move from such areas as storage rooms
5736
or garages under buildings into living or working spaces and cause indoor air quality problems. Another
5737
serious consequence of infiltration and exfiltration through the building envelope is the condensation of
5738
moisture from the exfiltrating air in northern climates, and from infiltrating hot humid air in southern
5739
climates, causing mold growth, decay and corrosion that cause health problems and premature building
5740
deterioration. Unlike the moisture transport mechanism of diffusion, air pressure differentials can
5741
transport hundreds of times more water vapor through air leaks in the envelope over the same period of
5742
time (Quirouette, 1986). This water vapor can condense within the envelope in a concentrated manner,
5743
wherever those air leaks may be (Figure1).
5744
5745
Air leaks through the building enclosure can take one of several forms:
5746
1.
Orifice flow.
5747
2.
Diffuse flow.
5748
3.
Channel flow.
5749
5750
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 195
Return to TOP
Orifice flow occurs when the air entry and exit are in a linear pathway, such as in the crack between a
5751
window rough opening and its frame (Figure 2.2-D).
5752
5753
5754
Figure 2.2-D
5755
5756
Diffuse flow happens when materials are used in the envelope that are ineffective in controlling air
5757
infiltration and exfiltration due to many cracks or their high permeance to air, such as fibrous insulation
5758
or uncoated concrete block.
5759
5760
Channel flow is probably the most common and serious of all types of air leaks and is shown in Figure
5761
2.2-E The air entry point and exit point are distant from each other, giving the air enough time to cool
5762
below its dewpoint and deposit moisture in the building enclosure.
5763
5764
5765
5766
5767
5768
5769
5770
5771
5772
5773
5774
Figure 2.2-E Channel Flow
5775
5776
Lastly, air infiltration and exfiltration are the cause of unnecessary energy consumption in buildings due
5777
to the added heating and cooling loads and the additional humidification or dehumidification needed.
5778
5779
Air Pressures that Cause Infiltration and Exfiltration
5780
There are three major air pressures on buildings that cause infiltration and exfiltration:
5781
• Wind Pressure
5782
• Stack Pressure (sometimes called chimney effect, or buoyancy)
5783
• HVAC Fan Pressure
5784
VAPOR BARRIER
CONCENTRATED
CONDENSATION
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 196
Return to TOP
5785
Wind Pressure
5786
The average annual wind pressure on buildings is of significance in calculating energy or moisture-
5787
related air leakage in buildings. When averaged out over the course of the year, it is about 10-15 mph
5788
(0.2 – 0.3 psf) (10 – 14 Pa) in most locations in North America. (http://irc.nrc-cnrc.gc.ca/bsi/86-
5789
2\_E.html) Wind pressure tends to pressurize a building positively on the façade it is hitting, and as the
5790
wind goes around the corner of the building it cavitates and speeds up considerably, creating especially
5791
strong negative pressure at the corners and less strong negative pressure on the rest of the building walls
5792
and roof (Figures 2.2-F and 2.2-G), (Building Science For a Cold Climate, Hutcheon and Handegord,
5793
1983).
5794
5795
5796
Figure 2.2-F
Figure 2.2-G
5797
5798
Stack Pressure
5799
Stack pressure is caused by a difference in atmospheric pressure at the top and bottom of a building due
5800
to the difference in temperature, and therefore, a difference in the weight of the columns of air indoors
5801
vs. outdoors in the winter. Stack effect in cold climates can cause infiltration of air at the bottom of the
5802
building and exfiltration at the top, as seen in Figure 2.2-H. The reverse occurs in warm climates with
5803
air conditioning.
5804
5805
5806
S TACK EFFECT
HVAC FAN EFFECT
WIND EFFECT
COMBINED EFFECT
5807
Figure 2.2-H (source Wagdy Anis)
5808
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 197
Return to TOP
5809
Fan Pressure
5810
Fan pressure is caused by HVAC system pressurization, usually positively, which is fine in warm
5811
climates but can cause incremental envelope problems to wind and stack pressures in heating climates.
5812
HVAC engineers tend to do this to reduce infiltration (and with it, pollution) and disruption of the
5813
HVAC system design pressures relationships. Figure 2.2-I shows each of these pressures separately and
5814
a combined diagram.
5815
5816
5817
5818
Figure 2.2-I (source Wagdy Anis)
5819
5820
The US Department of Energy reports that up to 40% of the energy used by buildings for heating and
5821
cooling is lost due to infiltration (Pollock, 2001). Using CONTAMW/TRNSYS modeling shows that in
5822
newer buildings infiltration is responsible for about 25% of the heating load and 4% of the cooling load
5823
(Emmerich, and Persily, 1998). To control air infiltration and exfiltration in buildings, a conceptual
5824
approach to airtightening is needed, called an air barrier “system.” The Canadian National Building
5825
Code, and more recently, the Commonwealth of Massachusetts’ building code, take this comprehensive
5826
and conceptual approach, namely requiring an air barrier system in the building envelope as a code
5827
requirement. A continuous air barrier system is the combination of interconnected materials, flexible
5828
sealed joints and components of the building envelope that provide the airtightness of the building
5829
enclosure and separations between conditioned and unconditioned spaces (http://irc.nrc-
5830
cnrc.gc.ca/bsi/86-3\_E.html) (Figure 2.2-J), (Lux and Brown, 1986).
5831
5832
Air Barrier Systems
5833
The concept is to select and target a component of the wall or roof that is air impermeable and to
5834
deliberately make it an airtight “assembly” by sealing the joints and penetrations. This assembly of
5835
materials is connected to adjacent assemblies and components such as windows, doors and the
5836
foundation and roof air barrier component. The air barrier system above grade is also connected to the
5837
foundation walls, and basement slabs to complete the air barrier system of the building. Air tightening
5838
below grade walls and slabs prevents entry of organic gases such as radon, and pollutants from
5839
agricultural activities and brownfields, due to depressurization of spaces with their enclosure in contact
5840
with the soil.
5841
5842
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 198
Return to TOP
5843
Figure 2.2-J
5844
5845
The important features of an air barrier system in a building are: Continuity, Structural Support, Air
5846
impermeability and Durability.
5847
5848
Continuity
5849
To ensure continuity, each component serving its role in resisting infiltration such as a wall or a window
5850
or a foundation or a roof must all be interconnected, to prevent air leakage at the joints between
5851
materials, components, assemblies and systems.
5852
5853
Structural support
5854
Effective structural support requires that any component of the air barrier system must resist the
5855
structural loads that are imposed on that component by wind, stack and chimney effect, without rupture,
5856
displacement or undue deflection. This load must then be safely transferred to the structure. Design
5857
consideration must determine adequate resistance to these pressures by fasteners, adhesives, etc.
5858
5859
Air impermeability
5860
Materials chosen to be part of the air barrier system should be chosen with care to avoid selecting
5861
materials that are too air-permeable, such as fiberboard, perlite board and uncoated concrete block. The
5862
air permeance of a material is measured using ASTM E 2178 test protocol and reported in Litres/Second
5863
per Square Meter at 75 Pa pressure (cfm/sf at 0.3” w.g or 1.57 psf). The Canadian and Massachusetts
5864
codes consider 0.02 L/s.M2 75Pa (0.004 cfm / sf 1.57 psf ), which happens to be the air permeance of a
5865
sheet of ½” unpainted gypsum wall board, as the maximum allowable air leakage for a material that can
5866
be used as part of the air barrier system for the opaque enclosure. This is more airtight than the
5867
requirements for windows and curtain-wall, but it must be remembered that windows and curtain wall
5868
are assemblies of materials and also these materials are more resistant to damage due to condensation
5869
than ordinary building materials. It is to be expected that when fairly airtight materials are assembled
5870
together by sealing, taping screws, etc., that the assembly will leak more air than the original material
5871
that is being used as the basic material; also, when these assemblies are joined together into a whole
5872
building, that building enclosure will leak more air than the individual assemblies joined together in the
5873
MATERIAL A
MATERIAL A
MATERIAL A
MATERIAL B
MATERIAL B
MATERIAL B
J
O
I
N
T
S
ASSEMBLY A
J
O
I
N
T
S
J
O
I
N
T
S
ASSEMBLY B
AIR BARRIER SYSTEM
AIR BARRIER SYSTEM CONCEPTUAL DIAGRAM
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 199
Return to TOP
first place; so in order to achieve a reasonable end result, the basic materials selected for the air barrier
5874
must be quite air-impermeable.
5875
5876
Durability
5877
Materials selected for the air barrier system must perform their function for the expected life of the
5878
structure; otherwise they must be accessible for periodic maintenance, such as elastomeric paint coatings
5879
on concrete block.
5880
5881
In summary, air barrier system code requirements may require:
5882
• A continuous plane of airtightness must be traced throughout the building envelope with all
5883
moving joints made flexible and sealed.
5884
• Air Permeance Compliance Alternatives:
5885
o The air barrier material in an assembly of the opaque envelope must have an air
5886
permeance not to exceed 0.004 cfm / ft2 at 0.3” wg (1.57 psf) [0.02 L/s\*m2 @ 75 Pa]
5887
when tested in accordance with ASTM E2178.
5888
o An air barrier assembly must have an air permeance not to exceed 0.2 L/s\*m2 @ 75 PA
5889
(0.04 cfm/ ft2@1.57 psf), when tested according to ASTM E 2357 or ASTM E 1677.
5890
The registered design professional shall determine the test air pressures adequate to
5891
simulate design conditions for the location of the project.
5892
o The whole building’s air leakage rate must not exceed 2 L/s.M275 PA (0.4 cfm/ sf 1.57
5893
psf) when tested according to ASTM E779.
5894
• The air barrier system must be able to withstand the maximum design positive and negative air
5895
pressure and must transfer the load to the structure.
5896
• The air barrier must not displace under load or displace adjacent materials.
5897
• The air barrier material used must be durable or able to be maintained.
5898
• Connections between roof air barrier, wall air barrier, window frames, door frames, foundations,
5899
floors over crawlspaces, and across building joints must be flexible to withstand building
5900
movements due to thermal, seismic, moisture content changes and creep; the joint must support
5901
the same air pressures as the air barrier material without displacement.
5902
• Penetrations through the air barrier must be sealed.
5903
• An air barrier must be provided between spaces that have either significantly different
5904
temperature or humidity requirements.
5905
• Lighting fixtures are required to be airtight when installed through the air barrier
5906
• To control stack pressure transfer to the envelope, stairwells, shafts, chutes and elevator lobbies
5907
must be decoupled from the floors they serve by providing doors that meet air leakage criteria for
5908
exterior doors, or the doors must be gasketed (Figure 2.2-K).
5909
• Functional penetrations through the envelope that are normally inoperative, such as elevator
5910
shaft louvers and atrium smoke exhaust systems, must be dampered with airtight motorized
5911
dampers connected to the fire alarm system to open on call and to fail in the open position
5912
(Massachusetts Energy Code for Commercial Buildings, 780 CMR 13, 2001).
5913
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 200
Return to TOP
5914
Figure 2.2-K (source Wagdy Anis)
5915
5916
In addition, other pressure differentials within buildings should be controlled by the following methods:
5917
• Compartmentalizing and sealing garages under buildings with airtight walls to vestibules with
5918
weather-stripped doors at building access points.
5919
• Compartmentalizing spaces under negative pressure, such as boiler rooms, and providing make-
5920
up air for combustion.
5921
• Decoupling supply or return floor and ceiling plenums from the exterior enclosure. If these are
5922
connected, serious consequences will arise that should be considered; the exterior walls become
5923
ducts with air forced through them, potentially causing severe condensation, microbial growth
5924
and deterioration (Figure 2.2L).
5925
• Generic materials that meet the air leakage requirements stated above are as follows (Bombaru,
5926
Jutras, and Patenaude, CMHC, 1988, http://www.cmhc-schl.gc.ca/publications/en/rh-
5927
pr/tech/98109.htm)
5928
5929
5930
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 201
Return to TOP
5931
Figure 2.2-L Plenums connected to the exterior enclosure can
5932
move moist air through these assemblies (source Wagdy Anis )
5933

\*Membranes must withstand air pressures in both directions without displacement or damage.
5937
If not fully adhered, they must be sandwiched between two board materials.
5938
5939
If housewraps and other film membranes, are not fully supported on both sides, as is the case in a brick
5940
cavity wall, they cannot support negative wind loads without tearing at the staples and brick anchors or
5941
rupturing under load (Bosack and Burnett, 1998). Housewraps in brick cavity walls displace under
5942
negative wind pressure and “pump” building air into the assembly, potentially causing condensation in
5943
cold climates. While testing in Canada to pre-qualify its membrane for use as an air barrier material, a
5944
manufacturer of spunbonded polyolefin discovered that to withstand negative wind pressures, the
5945
membrane needed to be stronger, and installed with fasteners having 1” diameter plastic washers, or a
5946
brick tie must be installed every 6” (150 mm) into the stud and 16” (400mm) apart (Figure 2.2-M).
5947
Alternatively continuous strapping with a fastener every12” (300mm) may be used. Note that products
5948
sold in Canada and the US with the same name may not have the same air leakage or strength properties.
5949
5950
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 202
Return to TOP
5951
Figure 2.2-M (source CCMC)
5952
5953
It is even more difficult to make a polyethylene vapor retarder into an air barrier. It lacks structural
5954
support when it is against glass-fiber batts and has the inherent quality of displacing and stretching, even
5955
rupturing, under high wind loads. It is also difficult to seam to itself or other materials (Figure 2.2-N).
5956
Fastener holes through polyethylene can stretch and compromise its airtightness (Shaw, 1985).
5957
5958
5959
Figure 2.2-N Polyethylene air barrier ruptures in a wall with glassfiber batt insulation
5960
(source NRC-IRC Air Leakage in Buildings, Lux & Brown 1986)
5961
5962
Materials that do not qualify as air barrier materials without additional coatings are (Bombaru, Jutras
5963
and Patenaude, CMHC, 1988):
5964
• Uncoated concrete block
5965
• Plain and asphalt impregnated fiberboard
5966
• Expanded polystyrene
5967
• Batt and semi-rigid fibrous insulation
5968
• Perforated house-wraps
5969
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 203
Return to TOP
• Asphalt impregnated felt, 15 or 30 lb.
5970
• Tongue and groove planks
5971
• Vermiculite insulation
5972
• Cellulose spray-on insulation
5973
5974
Of course there are many products formulated to qualify as air barrier materials. Some of these, as well
5975
as specifications, technical help, contactor and workmen training and certification is provided by the Air
5976
Barrier Association of America, http://www.airbarrier.org
5977
5978
Air Barrier Materials
5979
The simplest approach to airtightening a wall is to select one of the layers such as the sheathing and to
5980
airtighten it using durable tapes, adhesive sheet products, liquid-applied materials or the like. Walls
5981
constructed out of materials that are very permeable to air, such as concrete block, must be airtightened
5982
using an applied elastomeric (flexible) coating, either as a specially formulated paint, or a specially
5983
formulated air barrier sheet product, or a liquid-applied spray-on or trowel-on material. Transition peel-
5984
and-stick membranes are most commonly used at window and door perimeters, or when changing
5985
materials or wall systems (Figures 14 and 15). Alternatively, a sheet membrane such as a peel-and-stick
5986
membrane can be used on the whole wall.
5987
5988
5989
Figure 2.2-O Peel-and-stick membrane trimwork and transitions
5990
being applied Georgetown Law School, Shepley Bulfinch
5991
Richardson and Abbott, Architect (photo courtesy of Wagdy Anis)
5992
5993
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 204
Return to TOP
5994
Figure 2.2-P Liquid-applied air barrier is applied to balance of wall.
5995
Same project as in figure 14 (photo courtesy of Wagdy Anis)
5996
5997
Metal backpans are often used as part of the air barrier system in the spandrel areas of curtain walls.
5998
5999
Location of the air barrier
6000
The air barrier, unlike the vapor retarder, (since its function is to stop air movement, not control
6001
diffusion), can be located anywhere in the envelope assembly. If it is placed on the predominantly warm
6002
humid side (high vapor pressure side) of the enclosure, it can control diffusion as well, and would be a
6003
low-perm vapor barrier material. In that case, it is called an “air and vapor barrier;” if placed on the
6004
predominantly cool drier side (low vapor pressure side) of the wall, it should be vapor permeable (5-10
6005
perms or greater). Figure 2.2-Q shows a few examples of air barrier system design continuity and
6006
structural integrity, excerpted from the reference details published by the Commonwealth of
6007
Massachusetts at http://www.mass.gov/Eeops/docs/dps/inf/inf\_bbrs\_brick\_veneer\_a.pdf
6008
(Change letter “a” through “f” in the above address to download the entire series)
6009
6010
6011
6012
6013
6014
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 205
Return to TOP
6015
6016
6017
6018
6019
6020
6021
6022
6023
6024
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 206
Return to TOP
6025
6026
6027
6028
6029
Figure 2.2-Q (source Commonwealth of MA)
6030
6031
Finally, the complexities of airtightening a building using the interior finish gypsum board are worth
6032
highlighting (Figure 2.2-R). The airtight drywall approach or “ADA”, as it is known in Canada, using
6033
the interior drywall as the airtight plane, (Lstiburek and Lischkoff, 1986) is useful in residential work
6034
where renovation is not expected for many years. In commercial work however, the intent of the
6035
designer will most likely be lost to renovation. Also, continual rewiring for data lines compromises the
6036
drywall’s air tightness as the contractor punches holes above the ceiling. It is a very complex, three-
6037
dimensional problem.
6038
6039
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 207
Return to TOP
6040
Figure 2.2-R (source Wagdy Anis)
6041
6042
With the “ADA”, electric back-boxes caulked to the drywall must be used, caulking the wiring as it
6043
comes through to the electric box.
6044
6045
6046
Figure 2.2-S (source Shelter Supply website)
6047
6048
Air barriers Subject to Temperature Changes
6049
Air barriers on the exterior side of the insulation are subject to thermal changes and lots of movement
6050
due to expansion and contraction; therefore these joints are more difficult to keep airtight for the life of
6051
the building due to the stresses applied to the jointing tape or sealant by the thermal cycling over time.
6052
The best joint materials for these applications should be used, such as:
6053
• Extruded silicone bedded in wet silicone.
6054
• Wet silicone applied in a “band-aid joint” across board joints.
6055
• Other liquid applied elastomeric air barriers products.
6056
• Modified asphalt peel-and-stick with surface properly primed.
6057
6058
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 208
Return to TOP
6059
Figure 2.2-T (photo courtesy of Wagdy Anis)
6060
6061
6062
Figure 2.2-U (photo courtesy of Wagdy Anis)
6063
6064
The above two photos show foam sealant applied to all insulation board edges followed by peel-and-
6065
stick modified asphalt tape on the primed insulation sheathing boards used as the air barrier
6066
Boston College Administration Building (Shepley Bulfinch Richardson and Abbott, Architect)
6067
6068
Roof air barriers
6069
The roof membrane can be considered an air barrier since it is designed to withstand wind loads if it is
6070
fully adhered or hot- or cold-mopped. Mechanically fastened and ballasted roof systems, because they
6071
displace and momentarily billow or pump building air into the system, do not perform the required
6072
functions of containing air without displacement. In those cases, another air barrier must be selected in
6073
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 209
Return to TOP
the system. Either a peel-and-stick air and vapor barrier on the inboard side of the roof system (interior
6074
conditions and weather dependent), or taped gypsum underlayment board beneath the insulation can be
6075
used in a system with adhered under-layers of thermal protection board and insulation. Those layers
6076
must be designed to withstand maximum wind loads without displacement. Because of the critical
6077
importance of continuity with the wall air barrier, a pre-construction conference on the air barrier system
6078
must include the trades involved in the air barrier system, such as the wall air barrier subcontractor, the
6079
window subcontractor, the sealant subcontractor and also the roofing subcontractor, to discuss the
6080
connection between the roof air barrier and the wall air barrier, as well as the sequence of making that
6081
airtight and flexible connection. It is also important to ensure that the materials being joined together
6082
are compatible.
6083
6084
Penetrations into roof systems, such as ducts, vents and roof drains, must be dealt with, perhaps by using
6085
spray polyurethane foam (or other sealant) or membranes to airtighten those penetrations at the targeted
6086
air barrier layer.
6087
6088
Conclusion
6089
An air barrier system is an essential component of the building enclosure so that air pressure
6090
relationships within the building can be controlled, building HVAC systems can perform as intended,
6091
and the occupants can enjoy good indoor air quality and a comfortable environment. HVAC system size
6092
can be reduced because of a reduction in the “fudge factor” added to cover infiltration and unknown
6093
factors, resulting in reduced energy use and demand. Air barrier systems in the building envelope also
6094
control concentrated condensation and the associated mold, corrosion, rot, and premature failure; they
6095
improve and promote durability and sustainability. Building codes should require air barriers systems,
6096
and building designers and builders should be aware of the negative consequences of ignoring building
6097
airtightness.
6098
6099

Controlling Convection in Enclosure Assemblies
6229
Controlling convection currents within envelope assemblies caused by connecting air on the cold side to
6230
air on the warm side of insulation or the interior air by sealing the interior (Figure 2.2-V). This is the
6231
typical mechanism of mold formation in insulated basements, where air that is adjacent to a cool
6232
concrete basement wall cools down, gets heavier and drops, pulling in warm humid air at the top of the
6233
insulated wall.

Controlling Condensation due to Diffusion
6258
Refer to ASHRAE Fundamentals 2005 Chapter 23.
6259
6260
UNDERSTANDING VAPOR BARRIERS - ASHRAE Journal August 2004
6261
By Joseph Lstiburek, PhD, P.Eng.
6262
6263
The function of a vapor barrier is to retard migration of water vapor. Where it is located
6264
in an assembly and its permeability is a function of climate, the characteristics of the
6265
materials that comprise the assembly and the interior conditions. Vapor barriers are not
6266
typically intended to retard migration of air. That is the function of air barriers. Confusion
6267
on the issue of vapor barriers and air barriers is common. The confusion arises because
6268
air often holds a great deal of moisture in vapor form. When this air moves from location
6269
to location, due to an air pressure difference, the vapor moves with it. This is a type of
6270
migration of water vapor. In the strictest sense air barriers are also vapor barriers when
6271
they control the transport of moisture- laden air.
6272
6273
An excellent discussion about the differences between vapor barriers and air barriers is in
6274
Quirouette, 1986. Vapor barriers are also a cold climate artifact that have diffused into
6275
other climates more from ignorance than need. The history of cold climate vapor barriers
6276
itself is a story based more on personalities than physics. Rose regales readers of this
6277
history. It is frightening that construction practices can be so dramatically influenced by
6278
so little research and reassuring that the inherent robustness of most building assemblies
6279
has been able to tolerate such foolishness.
6280
6281
So What is The Problem?
6282
6283
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 215
Return to TOP
Incorrect use of vapor barriers is leading to an increase in moisture related problems.
6284
Vapor barriers were originally intended to prevent assemblies from getting wet.
6285
However, they often prevent assemblies from drying. Vapor barriers installed on the
6286
interior of assemblies prevent assemblies from drying inward. This can be a problem in
6287
any air-conditioned enclosure, in any below grade space, or when a vapor barrier is also
6288
on the exterior. Additionally, this can be a problem where brick is installed over building
6289
paper and vapor permeable sheathing.
6290
6291
What Do We Really Want to Do?
6292
6293
Two seemingly simple requirements for building enclosures bedevil engineers and
6294
architects almost endlessly: Keep water out, and Let water out if it gets in. Water can
6295
come in several phases: liq uid, solid, vapor and adsorbed. The liquid phase as rain and
6296
groundwater has driven everyone crazy for hundreds of years but can be readily
6297
understood — drain everything and remember the humble flashing.
6298
6299
The solid phase also drives everyone crazy when we have to shovel it or melt it, but at
6300
least most professionals understand the related building problems (ice damming, frost
6301
heave, freezethaw damage).
6302
6303
However, the vapor phase is in a class of craziness all by itself. We will conveniently
6304
ignore the adsorbed phase and leave it for someone else to deal with. Note that adsorbed
6305
water is different than absorbed water.3 The fundamental principle of control of water in
6306
the liquid form is to drain it out if it gets in — and let us make it perfectly clear — it will
6307
get in if you build where it rains or if you place your building in the ground where there
6308
is water in the ground. This is easy to understand, logical, with a long historical basis.
6309
The fundamental principle of water control in the solid form is to not let it become solid.
6310
And, if it does, give it space. Or, if it is solid, do not let it become liquid. If it does
6311
become liquid, drain it away before it can become solid again. This is a little more
6312
difficult to understand, but logical and based on solid research. Examples of this principle
6313
include the use of air entrained concrete to control freeze-thaw damage and the use of
6314
attic venting to provide cold roof decks to control ice damming. The fundamental
6315
principle of water control in the vapor form is to keep it out and to let it out if it gets in.
6316
6317
Simple, right?
6318
6319
No chance. It becomes complicated because sometimes the best strategies to keep water
6320
vapor out also trap water vapor in. This can be a real problem if the assemblies start out
6321
wet because of rain or the use of wet materials. It becomes even more complicated
6322
because of climate. In general, water vapor moves from the warm side of building
6323
assemblies to the cold side. This is simple to understand, except we have trouble deciding
6324
what side of a wall is the cold or warm side. Logically, this means we need different
6325
strategies for different climates. We also must take into account differences between
6326
summer and winter.
6327
6328
Finally, complications arise when materials can store water. This can be both good and
6329
bad. A cladding system such as a brick veneer can act as a reservoir after a rainstorm and
6330
significantly complicate wall design. Alternatively, wood framing or masonry can act as a
6331
hygric buffer absorbing water and lessening moisture shocks. What is required is to
6332
define vapor control measures on a more regional climatic basis measures more precisely.
6333
Part of the problem is that we struggle with names and terms. We have vapor retarders,
6334
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 216
Return to TOP
vapor barriers, vapor permeable, vapor impermeable, etc. What do these terms mean? It
6335
depends on whom you ask and whether they are selling something, or arguing with a
6336
building official. In an attempt to clear up some of the confusion, the following
6337
definitions are proposed: Vapor Retarder\*:
6338
6339
The element that is designed and installed in an assembly to retard the movement of
6340
water by vapor diffusion. \* taken somewhat from 2001 ASHRAE Handbook—
6341
Fundamentals, Chapter 23. The unit of measurement typically used in characterizing the
6342
water vapor permeance of materials is the “perm.” It is further proposed here that there
6343
should be several classes of vapor retarders. (This is nothing new. It is an extension and
6344
modification of the Canadian General Standards Board approach that specifies Type I and
6345
Type II vapor retarders. The numbers here are a little different, however.).These classes
6346
are: Class I 0.1 perm or less Class II 1.0 perm or less and greater than 0.1 perm Class III
6347
10 perm or less and greater than 1.0 perm Test procedure for vapor retarders: ASTM E-
6348
96 Test Method A (the desiccant method or dry cup method).
6349
6350
Finally, a vapor barrier is defined as: Vapor Barrier: A Class I vapor retarder. The current
6351
International Building Code (and its derivative codes) defines a vapor retarder as 1.0
6352
perm or less (using the same test procedure). In other words, the current code definition
6353
of a vapor retarder is equivalent to the definition of a Class II vapor retarder proposed by
6354
the author. Continuing in the spirit of finally defining terms that are tossed around in the
6355
enclosure business, it is also proposed that materials be separated into four general
6356
classes based on their permeance (again nothing new, this is an extension of the
6357
discussion in a previous ASHRAE Journal article4):
6358
 Class I 0.1 perm or less
6359
 Class II 1.0 perm or less and greater than 0.1 perm
6360
 Class III 10 perm or less and greater than 1.0 perm
6361
 Test procedure for vapor retarders: ASTM E-96 Test Method A (the desiccant method
6362
or dry cup method)
6363
 Finally, a vapor barrier is defined as: Vapor Barrier: A Class I vapor retarder.
6364
6365
The current International Building Code (and its derivative codes) defines a vapor
6366
retarder as 1.0 perm or less (using the same test procedure). In other words, the current
6367
code definition of a vapor retarder is equivalent to the definition of a Class II vapor
6368
retarder proposed by the author. Continuing in the spirit of finally defining terms that are
6369
tossed around in the enclosure business, it is also proposed that materials be separated
6370
into four general classes based on their permeance (again nothing new, this is an
6371
extension of the discussion in a previous ASHRAE Journal article4):
6372
 Vapor impermeable: 0.1 perm or less
6373
 Vapor semi-impermeable: 1.0 perm or less and greater than 0.1 perm
6374
 Vapor semi-permeable: 10 perms or less and greater than 1.0 perm
6375
 Vapor permeable: greater than 10 perms
6376
6377
6378
Recommendations for Building Enclosures
6379
The following building assembly recommendations are climatically based (see sidebar, “Hygrothermal
6380
Regions”) and are sensitive to cladding type (brick or stone veneer, stucco) and structure (concrete block,
6381
steel or wood frame, precast concrete). The recommendations apply to residential, business, assembly,
6382
educational and mercantile occupancies. The recommendations do not apply to special use enclosures
6383
such as spas, pool buildings, museums, hospitals, data processing centers or other engineered enclosures
6384
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 217
Return to TOP
such as factory, storage or utility enclosures. The recommendations are based on the following
6385
principles:
6386
• Avoiding use of vapor barriers where vapor retarders will provide satisfactory performance.
6387
Avoiding use of vapor retarders where vapor permeable materials will provide satisfactory
6388
performance. (thereby, encouraging drying mechanisms over wetting-prevention mechanisms).
6389
6390
• Avoiding the installation of vapor barriers on both sides of assemblies, i.e., “double vapor
6391
barriers,” to facilitate assembly drying in at least one direction
6392
6393
• Avoiding installation of vapor barriers such as polyethylene vapor barriers, foil faced batt
6394
insulation and reflective radiant barrier foil insulation on the interior of air-conditioned
6395
assemblies (a practice that has been linked with moldy buildings).5
6396
6397
• Avoiding installation of vinyl wall coverings on the inside of air-conditioned assemblies (a
6398
practice that has been linked with moldy buildings).6
6399
6400
Enclosures are ventilated meeting ASHRAE Standards 62.1 or 62.2.
6401
6402
Each of the recommended building assemblies was evaluated using dynamic hygrothermal modeling.
6403
The moisture content of building materials that comprise the building assemblies all remained below the
6404
equilibrium moisture content the materials as specified in ASHRAE proposed Standard 160P, Design
6405
Criteria for Moisture Control in Buildings, under this evaluation approach. Interior air conditions and
6406
exterior air conditions as specified by 160P were used. WUFI was used as the modeling program.
6407
6408
More significantly, each of the recommended building assemblies have been found by the author to
6409
provide satisfactory performance under the limitations noted. Satisfactory performance is defined as no
6410
moisture problems reported or observed during at least a 10-year period. The only exceptions relate to
6411
the assemblies containing low density spray applied foams. The author has only been familiar with their
6412
use in assemblies less than five years old.
6413
6414

6465
CODE REQUIREMENTS FOR VAPOR RETARDERS
6466
6467
6468
6469
The proposed building code requirements are based on a combination of field experience
6470
and laboratory testing. The requirements were also evaluated using dynamic
6471
hygrothermal modeling. The modeling program used was WUFI.1 Under the modeling
6472
evaluation, the moisture content of building materials that comprise the building
6473
assemblies evaluated all remained below the equilibrium moisture content of the
6474
materials as specified in ASHRAE proposed Standard 160P, Design Criteria for Moisture
6475
Control in Buildings. Interior air conditions and exterior air conditions as specified by
6476
160P were used. Enclosures are ventilated to meet ASHRAE Standards 62.1 or 62.2.
6477
6478
The climate zones referenced are the U.S. Department of Energy climate zones as
6479
proposed for adoption in the 2006 International Residential Code (IRC) and International
6480
Energy Conservation Code (IECC). Their development is the subject of two ASHRAE
6481
papers.2,3 An accompanying map defines the climate zones. Note that vapor retarders are
6482
defined and classed using ASTM E- 96 Test Method A (the desiccant method or dry cup
6483
method) consistent with the current code language. However, exterior sheathing/cladding
6484
assemblies are defined and classed using Test Method B (the “wet cup” method) to take
6485
advantage of the ability of some sheathings to “breathe” as they are exposed to high
6486
relative humidities.
6487
6488
1. Zones 1, 2, and 3 do not require any class of vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6489
insulation in insulated assemblies (this recommendation has already been accepted by the
6490
code committee at the code hearings in Nashville, September 2003 and Kansas City, May
6491
2004).
6492
6493
2. Zone 4 (not marine) requires a Class III (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior
6494
surface of insulation in insulated wall and floor assemblies.
6495
6496
3. Zone 4 (marine) requires a Class III (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6497
insulation in insulated wall and floor assemblies where the permeance of the exterior
6498

6499

6500
6501
4. Zone 4 (marine) requires a Class II (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6502
insulation in insulated wall and floor assemblies where the permeance of the exterior
6503
sheathing/cladding assembly is less than or equal to 1.0 perm and greater than 0.1 perm
6504
as tested by Test Method B (the “wet cup” method) of ASTM E-96).
6505
6506
5. Zone 4 (marine) requires a Class III (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6507
insulation in insulated wall and floor assemblies where the permeance of the exterior
6508
sheathing is 0.1 perm or less as tested by Test Method B (the “wet cup” method) of
6509
ASTM E-96) and the interior surface of the exterior sheathing shall be maintained above
6510

6511
state heat transfer, interior air at a temperature of 70°F (21°C), at a relative humidity
6512
specified in Table 1 and exterior air at a temperature that is equal to the average outdoor
6513
temperature for the location during the coldest three months of the year (e.g., December,
6514
January and February).
6515
6516
6. Zone 5 requires a Class III (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6517
insulation in insulated wall and floor assemblies where the permeance of the exterior
6518
sheathing is greater than 1.0 perm as tested by Test Method B (the “wet cup” method) of
6519
ASTM E-96.
6520
6521
7. Zones 6 and 7 require a Class II (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6522
insulation in insulated wall and floor assemblies where the permeance of the exterior
6523
sheathing is greater than 1.0 perm as tested by Test Method B (the “wet cup” method) of
6524
ASTM E-96.
6525
6526
8. Zones 5, 6 and 7 require a Class II (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6527
insulation in insulated wall and floor assemblies where the permeance of the exterior
6528
sheathing/cladding assembly is less than or equal to 1.0 perm and greater than 0.1 perm
6529
as tested by Test Method B (the “wet cup” method) of ASTM E-96.
6530
6531
9. Zones 5, 6 and 7 require a Class II (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6532
insulation in insulated wall and floor assemblies, where the permeance of the exterior
6533
sheathing is 0.1 perm or less as tested by Test Method B (the “wet cup” method) of
6534
ASTM E-96 and the interior surface of the exterior sheathing shall be maintained above
6535
the dew-point temperature of the interior air. Under this design approach, assume steady-
6536
state heat transfer, interior air at a temperature of 70°F (21°C), at a relative humidity
6537
specified in Table 1, and exterior air at a temperature that is equal to the average outdoor
6538
temperature for the location during the coldest three months of the year (e.g., December,
6539
January and February).
6540

6542
6543
6544
6545
10. Zone 5 requires a Class III (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6546
insulation in ventilated insulated roof or attic assemblies.
6547
6548
11. Zone 5 require a Class III (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6549
insulation in unvented insulated roof or attic assemblies and the condensing surface shall
6550
be maintained above the dewpoint temperature of the interior air. The condensing surface
6551
is defined as either the interior surface of the structural roof deck or the interior surface of
6552
an air-impermeable insulation applied in direct contact to the underside/interior of the
6553
structural roof deck. “Air-impermeable” is quantitatively defined by ASTM E 283. Under
6554
this design approach assume steady-state heat transfer, interior air at a temperature of
6555
70°F (21°C), at a relative humidity specified in Table 2 and exterior air at a temperature
6556
that is equal to the average outdoor temperature for the location during the coldest three
6557
months of the year (e.g., December, January and February).
6558
6559
12. Zones 6 and 7 require a Class II (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6560
insulation in unvented insulated roof or attic assemblies and the condensing surface shall
6561
be maintained above the dew-point temperature of the interior air. The condensing
6562
surface is defined as either the interior surface of the structural roof deck or the interior
6563
surface of an air-impermeable insulation applied in direct contact to the underside/interior
6564
of the structural roof deck. “Air-impermeable” is quantitatively defined by ASTM E 283.
6565
Under this design approach assume steady-state heat transfer, interior air at a temperature
6566
of 70°F (21°C), at a relative humidity specified in Table 2 and exterior air at a
6567
temperature that is equal to the average outdoor temperature for the location during the
6568
coldest three months of the year (e.g., December, January and February).
6569
6570

13. Zones 6 and 7 require a Class II (or lower) vapor retarder on the interior surface of
6571
insulation in ventilated insulated roof or attic assemblies.
6572

6575

6576

6577
6578
15. Exceptions to the above requirements shall be allowed when assemblies are evaluated
6579
by dynamic hygrothermal modeling. The moisture content of building materials that
6580
comprise the building assembly shall remain below the equilibrium moisture content the
6581
materials as specified in 160P under this evaluation approach. Interior air conditions and
6582
exterior air conditions as specified by 160P shall be used. What This Means From a
6583
Practical Perspective Polyethylene is a Class I vapor retarder. A kraft-faced fiberglass
6584
batt is a Class II vapor retarder. Latex painted gypsum board (one coat of latex paint) is a
6585
Class III vapor retarder.

of 1 in. (25 mm) thick or thicker has a perm value of 1.0 perm or less. Film faced

6610

6611

6612

6614

6616

6617

6618
within the wall cavity and gypsum board painted with latex paint (Class III vapor
6619
retarder) is required on the interior of this assembly. If this assembly is moved to
6620
Minneapolis, a Class II vapor retarder is required on the interior (a kraft paper faced
6621
fiberglass batt).
6622

Holzkirchen, Germany.
6627

6628
energy codes and standards: part 1 – development process.” ASHRAE Transactions.
6629
3. Briggs, R.S., R.G. Lucas, and T. Taylor. 2003. “Climate classification for building
6630
energy codes and standards: part 2 – zone definitions, maps and comparisons.” ASHRAE
6631
Transactions. August 2004 ASHRAE Journal 50
6632
