IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 229
Return to TOP
6637
Strategy 2.3 – Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
6638
6639
6640
Overview
6641
Proper building pressurization is required to limit moisture and contaminant transfer across the building
6642
envelope. Building pressurization is the static pressure difference between the interior pressure and the
6643
exterior (atmospheric) pressure of a building. This static pressure difference will influence how much
6644
and where exfiltration and infiltration will occur at the building envelope. It should be noted that the
6645
static pressure difference across the envelope is not the same at all points of the building envelope.
6646
Wind direction and speed, indoor –outdoor temperature differences, differing mechanical supply, return
6647
and exhaust airflows to each space, and compartmentalization of spaces can create different static
6648
pressures at various points of the building envelope. Many HVAC systems are designed to achieve an
6649
overall building pressurization at an average of 0.02 inches (4.98 Pa) differential (across the building
6650
envelope). The target pressure differential of 0.02 inches can fluctuate due to changing weather
6651
conditions, wind load, and HVAC system operation.
6652
6653
Assessment
6654
Climatological Requirements
6655
Depending upon the climate, the pressure differential should be positive or negative. Commercial and
6656
institutional buildings should be slightly positive, on average, except for those in climate zones 7 and 8,
6657
where the building should be slightly negative, on average, when the outdoor air temperature falls below
6658
32ºF (OºC). In humid climates the building should be slightly positive. In all climates, buildings which
6659
are humidified, such as medical facilities or museums, or buildings or spaces with significant internal
6660
moisture sources such as natatoriums, spas, or indoor gardens, should be negative when the outdoor air
6661
temperature falls below the dew point temperature maintained indoors.
6662
6663
For mixed climates with extreme conditions during both summer and winter seasons, buildings can be
6664
equipped with dewpoint control. When the outdoor dewpoint rises above the indoor dewpoint the
6665
HVAC system could be set to switch from neutral pressure to positive pressure. This will help limit
6666
infiltration and an undesirable dehumidification load. For buildings with temperature controlled outdoor
6667
economizers instead of dewpoint control, the internal pressure switching point could be the outdoor dry
6668
bulb temperature that equals the maximum dewpoint indoor temperature. This is more conservative,
6669
since the outdoor dewpoint temperature might not yet be reached, and will result in more hours of
6670
positive pressure (more fan cost) than if the actual dewpoints indoor and outdoor were compared.
6671
(Humidity Control Design Guide, ASHRAE, Harriman et. al.)
6672
6673
Regional and Local Outdoor Air Quality Requirements
6674
The outdoor air quality where the building is to be located should be analyzed. If the building is in a
6675
negative pressure the outdoor air contaminants could be drawn into the building. The outdoor air quality
6676
could be affected by neighboring activities such as dry cleaner, restaurant, manufacturing process, etc. or
6677
the existence of any site or location specific pollutants or contaminants. Additional information can be
6678
found in Strategy 1.2 Select HVAC systems to Improve IAQ and Reduce the Energy Impacts of
6679
Ventilation.
6680
6681
Building Usage and Layout
6682
The building usage needs to be taken into consideration to determine the building pressure requirements.
6683
As noted under climatological requirements the usage of the building coupled with the climatological
6684
requirements can determine the building pressurization.
6685
6686
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 230
Return to TOP
The layout of building space and usage of the building space can affect the building pressurization. The
6687
uniformity of the layout from floor to floor, space usage, and construction style (atrium lobby areas or
6688
continuous slabs between all floors) affect air distribution and the degree of pressurization required.
6689
Atria and a lack of between-floor air barriers provide portals of air flow that affects multiple levels,
6690
making pressure relationships more difficult to control. The building layout should be analyzed both
6691
vertically and horizontally for its effects on pressurization. For example, a hospital might have certain
6692
areas under localized depressurization, such as laboratory spaces or areas with infectious control
6693
concerns. If the areas are placed on the perimeter of the building, infiltration through the building
6694
envelope and moisture and contaminant problems are likely to result. If such areas must be kept under
6695
localized negative pressure, they should be placed in the center of the building floor plan so that the air
6696
travels into them from conditioned adjacent spaces. This will help ensure that the air traveling to the
6697
depressurized space is conditioned, and the HVAC system has a better chance of maintaining the overall
6698
positive building pressurization. (Odom, DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 Mold and Moisture Prevention,
6699
Washington, DC: National Council of Architectural Registration Boards)
6700
6701
Compartmentalization of spaces and separate HVAC systems serving the compartmentalized spaces
6702
could be required to maintain proper building pressurization. As noted above an Atrium area could be
6703
separated architecturally form the building and a separate HVAC system could be utilized for the atrium
6704
to help maintain proper building pressure relationships. Part of the architectural requirements could be
6705
the use of two door vestibules or revolving doors.
6706
6707
During remodels and the construction process, considerations must be made for pressurization strategies.
6708
Sequencing of HVAC equipment operation or lock out during construction may be required to avoid
6709
building pressurization problems. Starting up exhaust systems before starting supply systems can draw
6710
in moisture in hot humid climates or drawn in dust and dirt particles during the construction/landscaping
6711
process.
6712
6713
Phasing of the construction and remodel process must also be taken into consideration. Typically the
6714
phased areas of construction do not line up with HVAC systems that are serving the phased areas. This
6715
can create compartmentalization issues and building pressurization issues since the HVAC system may
6716
or may not be able to be put into operation to serve the phased areas. When the HVAC system is put
6717
into operation, it can create a negatively pressurized area in the completed phase drawing moisture and
6718
contaminants from the construction areas or incomplete building openings into the completed area. The
6719
HVAC systems will have to be segregated for phasing, if possible, or the HVAC systems will have to be
6720
remodeled and/or designed to match the phasing requirements for the project. Addition information on
6721
Managing Construction to Preserve IAQ as discussed in Strategy 1.4 Manage Construction and
6722
Renovation to Preserve IAQ.
6723
6724
Building Orientation and Wind Load
6725
Address building orientation concerns and wind load that can impact the amount of outside air
6726
infiltration into a building and affect the fan capacity and therefore the amount of outside air required to
6727
provide the proper building pressurization. The static pressure distribution on the outside of a building
6728
will vary with the angle of attack of the wind. In general, the static pressure caused by the wind will be
6729
positive on the upward wind surface until the angle of attack exceeds 45º. The static pressure will be
6730
negative on the upward wind surface when the angle of attack exceeds 75º, while both positive and
6731
negative pressures exist when the angle of attack is between 45º and 75º. On the leeward side, the static
6732
pressure will always be negative. The pressure on the roof caused by wind, on average, will be negative,
6733
but roof angles steeper than 20º will have parts of or the whole upwind side at a positive pressure
6734
(ASHRAE Laboratory Design Guide). The turbulence or gustiness of the approaching wind and the
6735
unsteady character of separated flows cause surface pressures to fluctuate. Pressures utilized are time-
6736
averaged values, with an averaging period of about 600 s. This is approximately the shortest time period
6737
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 231
Return to TOP
considered to be a “steady-state” condition when considering atmospheric winds; the longest is typically
6738
3600 s. Instantaneous pressures may vary significantly above and below these averages, and peak
6739
pressures two or three times the mean values are possible. Although peak pressures are important with
6740
regard to structural loads, mean values are more appropriate for computing infiltration and ventilation
6741
rates (ASHRAE Fundamentals 2005).
6742
6743
A few examples from a field investigation of 70 buildings in the southern United States will help
6744
visualize the magnitude of these forces in typical low-rise commercial buildings (Cummings et al.
6745
1996). In this field investigation, each building was pressurized internally with a blower, so that the
6746
average pressure difference across the exterior wall was 0.2” w.c. (50 pa). Once that pressure difference
6747
was achieved, the airflow through the blower into the building was measured with a calibrated venturi
6748
nozzle. Some buildings leaked more than 50 complete air changes per hour. Others leaked less than 5
6749
air changes per hour. The average leakage for all of the buildings was about 20 air changes. A pressure
6750
difference of 0.2” w.c. (50 Pa) is created whenever the wind blows against the outside wall at a velocity
6751
of 25 m.p.h. (11.2 m/s). As noted earlier the outdoor wind pressure is never uniform around the whole
6752
building. (Humidity Control Design Guide, ASHRAE, Harriman et. al.)
6753
6754
For a more detailed discussion and calculation of the wind pressure on a building reference ASHRAE
6755
Fundamentals 2005 Chapter 16 and Chapter 27.
6756
6757
Stack Effect
6758
Stack effect is a condition that exists in tall buildings at the times when outside temperature is
6759
significantly lower than the temperature of the spaces in the building. This is the phenomenon in which
6760
the cold weather acts as a chimney with natural convection of air entering at the lower floors of the
6761
building, flowing through the building, and exiting through the upper floors. The cause of stack effect is
6762
the difference in density between the cold denser air outside the building and the warm less dense air
6763
inside the building. The pressure differential that is created by the stack effect is directly proportional to
6764
the building height and the temperature that exists between the warm inside building and cold outside
6765
building.
6766
Figure 2.2-D (source ASHRAE Design Guide for Tall Commercial Buildings page 22)
6767
6768
Figure 2.2-E from the Canadian Building Digest CBD-107 Stack Effect and Building Design; A.G.
6769
Wilson and G.T. Tamura shows the absolute values of the pressure differences that can be estimated due
6770
to temperature difference and building height. For example, the total pressure difference from stack
6771
effect for a building 600 ft high at a inside –to-outside temperature difference of 100 F deg is about 2”
6772
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 232
Return to TOP
w.c. Figure YY shows the pattern of pressures and air flow caused by stack effect for an idealized
6773
building with uniform distribution of openings in the exterior wall, through each floor, and into the shaft
6774
at each story. Measurements made on several multistory buildings have shown that up to 80 percent of
6775
the total pressure difference is taken across the outside walls and that the remainder is distributed across
6776
various interior separations. This indicates that with present construction there is a relatively low
6777
resistance to air flow from floor to floor compared with that through the exterior wall. The level of the
6778
neutral pressure plane, which depends upon vertical distribution of the openings through which air flows
6779
into, through, and out of buildings, is generally near mid-height. For a uniform distribution of openings,
6780
as in Figure 2.2-F, pressure differences from stack effect can be estimated. For example, with 80 per
6781
cent of the total pressure difference taken across the outside walls and a neutral pressure plane at mid-
6782
height, the pressure drop at the entrance is equal to 40 per cent of the total. Similarly, the pressure
6783
difference across the walls of vertical shafts at the first floor level is equal to 10 per cent of the total. In
6784
the above example, the pressure difference across the entrance is about 0.8 in. of water and 0.2 in. of
6785
water across the vertical shaft at the ground floor level (Canadian Building Digest CBD-107 Stack
6786
Effect and Building Design; A.G. Wilson and G.T. Tamura).
6787
6788
6789
Figure 2.2-E (source Canadian Building Digest CBD-107. Stack
6790
Effect and Building Design; A.G. Wilson and G.T. Tamura)
6791
6792
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 233
Return to TOP
6793
Figure 2.2-F (source Canadian Building Digest CBD-107. Stack
6794
Effect and Building Design; A.G. Wilson and G.T. Tamura)
6795
6796
When addressing buildings in hot climates, reverse stack effect can occur. The hot less dense outside air
6797
enters the upper floors of a tall building and the colder denser air of the building exits through the lower
6798
floors.
6799
6800
A thorough discussion of tall building HVAC system design and stack effect can be found in the
6801
ASHRAE Tall Building Design Guide, following are some of the strategies and recommendations from
6802
this design guide
6803
6804
1. Strategies/Recommendations
6805
a. Minimize air leakage into and out of building. Address entrances, exits, truck docks, air
6806
intakes or exhaust, and overhangs in construction with light fixtures. Internally address
6807
fire stairs, elevator shafts, and mechanical/electrical shafts for wiring, duct and piping.
6808
6809
b. Vestibules or air locks can be provided for loading docks. Utilize two door vestibules
6810
with adequate heat and make sure that doors can operate independently of each other.
6811
6812
c. Utilize revolving doors. Use these revolving doors where there is the most traffic in and
6813
out of the building such as entrances from attached parking ramps or the main employee
6814
entrance. Two door vestibules should not be used for employee entry, as a large number
6815
of people means both doors will be opened simultaneously, and this should be avoided.
6816
6817
d. Utilize good door and sill gaskets on fire stair doors.
6818
6819
e. Compartmentalization should be utilized where possible. Elevator shafts should utilize
6820
doors at the entry to the elevator banks to create an elevator vestibule on each floor.
6821
Where possible on vertical shafts, seal vertical faces to minimize airflow into the shaft
6822
that would travel vertically. This vertical compartmentalization, vertically dividing the
6823
building into separate systems, may be required to reduce the height of the indoor air
6824
column. Floor penetrations in tall buildings are required by code to be sealed with a fire
6825
seal. Some fire seals are designed to expand with heat and are not air tight before the fire.
6826
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 234
Return to TOP
Making sure that the floor penetrations are sealed air tight is required for the vertical
6827
compartmentalization. Reference figure X below on the displaying the effects of
6828
compartmentalization.
6829
6830
f. If a single AHU serves multiple floors, each floor should have mechanically operated
6831
damper on the supply and return ductwork to isolate the shaft from the floor when the
6832
AHU is off.
6833
6834
g. Provide an independent HVAC system for the lobby in high-rise and complex buildings
6835
that is tailored to meeting the HVAC requirements of the lobby that includes
6836
pressurization. This allows for proper building pressure control and maintaining the
6837
adverse effects of stack effect. Higher percentages of outside air for pressurization can
6838
be introduced through this independent HVAC system. This independent HVAC system
6839
will also allow for night set-back control strategies for the main HVAC system (the
6840
independent lobby system can maintain lobby pressurization and comfort while the main
6841
system is turned back or off). (Commissioning and Envelope Leakage: Using HVAC
6842
Operating Strategies To Meet Design and Construction Challenges by David Sellers,
6843
Hannah Friedman, Larry Luskay, and Tudi Haasl, Portland Energy Conservation, Inc.)
6844
6845
h. The outside air, exhaust air, and return air volumes need to be evaluated in the design
6846
process and field measured and verified.
6847
6848
6849
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 235
Return to TOP
6850
Figure 2.3-G (source: ASHRAE Fundamentals 2005 27.8)
6851
6852
Building Envelope
6853
The effectiveness of the building pressurization is dependent upon the leakiness of the building envelope.
6854
The design and construction of the building envelope should limit exfiltration, infiltration and
6855
leakage. Strategy 2.2 Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces of this
6856
manual provides information on the design and construction of the building envelope.
6857
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 236
Return to TOP
6858
Identify planned openings in the building envelope that may impact the ability of the HVAC system to
6859
provide overall positive pressurization by inadvertently increasing envelope leakiness. These planned
6860
openings can include mechanical planned openings such as roof vents, louvers, floor drains (traps), as
6861
well as other penetrations. Architectural planned openings such as vents, penetrations, door and window
6862
openings, and even building envelope drainage openings need to be considered when addressing
6863
envelope leakiness. Once these planned openings are identified, employ strategies to try to reduce the
6864
amount of openings if possible. Options could include providing airtight motorized dampers on elevator
6865
shaft louvers; utilize revolving doors, etc. Any planned openings that cannot be sealed will have to be
6866
considered when evaluating the amount of outside air required to pressurize the building or possible
6867
interior compartmentalization.
6868
6869
In addition to the planned openings, identify potential unplanned openings that may impact the ability of
6870
the HVAC system to provide overall positive pressurization by increasing the envelope leakiness.
6871
Estimate the envelope leakiness so that the amount of outdoor air can be determined to provide proper
6872
building pressurization. Typical air leakage values per wall unit area at 0.30 in. of water (75 Pa) are
6873
0.10, 0.30 and 0.60 CFM/ft² (500, 1500, and 3000 cm³/s-m²) for tight average and leaky walls
6874
respectively (Tamura and Shaw 1976a). At a pressure of 0.016” (4 Pa), which is a more typical building
6875
pressure that is trying to be maintained, the air leakage values per wall unit area are converted to 0.015,
6876
0.045, and 0.089 CFM/ft² (76, 227, and 454 cm³/s-m²). Keep in mind that the building pressure is not
6877
evenly distributed across the building envelope and that a differential pressure across the building
6878
envelope can exceed the 0.016” (4 Pa) at various locations of the building envelope. This will most
6879
likely occur for any tall building due to stack effect and wind loading. A more thorough discussion on
6880
envelope leakiness can be found in the AHSRAE Fundamentals 2005 F27 and Airtightness of
6881
Commercial and Institutional Buildings: Blowing Holes in the Myth of Tight Buildings; Thermal
6882
Envelopes VII Conference, Dec 6-10, 1998, Clearwater, FL. Andrew K. Persily, Ph.D.
6883
6884
6885
6886
Figure 2.2-H (source: ASHRAE Article Sept 2007
6887
– Measuring Air Tightness at ASHRAE HQ)
6888
6889
HVAC System
6890
Air Volume Considerations
6891
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 237
Return to TOP
Air volume considerations include analyzing the issues noted earlier in this section; building orientation
6892
and wind load, stack effect, building envelope leakiness (planned and unplanned), and the HVAC
6893
system air volume requirements (required outside air for ventilation and exhaust air systems). The
6894
airflow rates due to theses separate forces must be determined by adding the pressure differences
6895
together, not the airflow rates, because the airflow rate through each opening is not linearly related to the
6896
pressure difference. A thorough discussion of this calculation can be found in the ASHRAE
6897
Fundamentals 2005 Chapter 27 (ASHRAE Fundamentals 2005).
6898
6899
Due to the complexity of the pressure difference calculations and the summation of total airflows of the
6900
different sources noted above, many times a rule of thumb is used for determination of required airflow
6901
for building pressurization. For a positively pressured building, the total return air and exhaust air
6902
volume from the building should be from 80 to 90 percent of the total outside air volume. When using
6903
these values, one should use caution and make sure that noted building pressurization issues are
6904
addressed. For example, if a tall building is being considered then stack effect should be addressed,
6905
building orientation and location can determine wind load issues or the type of envelope construction
6906
could affect the envelope leakiness. For any critical applications the pressure difference across the
6907
building envelope should be determined and the air volumes calculated.
6908
6909
In addition to the proper volume of air being provided for proper building pressurization, the distribution
6910
of the air within the building spaces must be addressed. The makeup air must be provided in the correct
6911
areas or spaces to help overcome depressurization due to stack effect and/or wind effect and building
6912
compartmentalization. Direct delivery to the space, ducting the makeup air to the space, is more
6913
effective for pressurization control than indirect delivery, through door undercuts for example. Indirect
6914
delivery does not ensure that the makeup air makes it to the space of concern and the space may not be
6915
pressurized properly.
6916
6917
The toilet exhaust system and other exhaust systems should be viewed as a system for controlling odors
6918
and localized moisture only, not as a method of drawing outside ventilation air into a building or
6919
meeting a building’s ventilation code requirements. The ventilation air required for building
6920
pressurization should be environmentally conditioned and cleaned prior to being introduced into the
6921
building.
6922
6923
HVAC System Dehumidification Capacity
6924
The HVAC system that is positively pressurizing the building by supplying conditioned outside air to
6925
the building should address moisture removal (dehumidification) capacity. HVAC systems are typically
6926
more efficient at cooling than dehumidifying the air. As a result unconditioned outside air that is
6927
brought into the building is cooled to the desired temperature before it is properly dehumidified creating
6928
elevated moisture levels and microbial growth inside the building. Furthermore, because the HVAC
6929
system is controlled by temperature (thermostat) instead of humidity (humidistat) the equipment never
6930
senses the elevated moisture levels within the building space and therefore never fully removes the
6931
moisture. Any HVAC system that will provide outside air for pressurization of the building should be
6932
sized to handle the latent load requirements and employ control strategies to allow for proper
6933
dehumidification of the outdoor air. (Odom, DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 Mold and Moisture
6934
Prevention, Washington, DC: National Council of Architectural Registration Boards)
6935
6936
Building Static Pressure Monitoring and Control Strategies
6937
Following are some control strategies that can be used to maintain proper building pressurization. One
6938
main strategy to employ is to decouple the temperature control and building pressure control functions.
6939
This is especially true when the HVAC systems utilize airside economizers. (Commissioning and
6940
Envelope Leakage: Using HVAC Operating Strategies to Meet Design and Construction Challenges by
6941
David Sellers, Hannah Friedman, Larry Luskay, and Tudi Haasl, Portland Energy Conservation, Inc.).
6942
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 238
Return to TOP
For additional information on HVAC system types and descriptions and IAQ implications,
6943
reference Strategy 1.2 Select HVAC Systems to Improve IAQ and Reduce the Energy Impacts of
6944
Ventilation.
6945
6946
Natural Relief
6947
Every building has some form of natural relief. This can include cracks around doors and windows and
6948
other planned or unplanned openings. Natural relief pressurization is typically found in smaller
6949
buildings. This is technically not a control option for building pressurization as there is no actual control
6950
available. As building construction methods get better and energy efficiency becomes a concern the
6951
building envelopes are becoming tighter and natural relief is not a pressurization control option.
6952
6953
Barometric Relief
6954
Barometric relief controls positive space pressure by utilizing a non-powered gravity damper that opens
6955
upon an increase in pressure difference between the building and outdoors. This is typically used in
6956
small buildings with HVAC systems with constant volume airflow. Location of the barometric damper
6957
is critical and should be located in the conditioned space so that is senses space pressure almost directly.
6958
Proper set up of the barometric relief damper is required; the damper needs to be adjusted to start
6959
relieving air for a slightly positively pressure building. Barometric relief cannot control a building for
6960
negative pressure and limits the ability for 100% OA economizing.
6961
6962
Airflow monitoring
6963
Airflow monitoring of the outside airflow quantity and exhaust airflow quantity and maintaining the
6964
proper offset of airflow quantities can be utilized for proper building pressure. If airflow monitoring is
6965
utilized proper selection and location of the airflow measuring stations must be employed. Reference
6966
the Strategy 7.2 Continuously Monitor and Control Outdoor Air Delivery sections of this manual for
6967
additional information. This strategy works well with smaller buildings and HVAC systems that will
6968
allow for measurement of all airflows. Concerns in using the airflow monitoring strategy include the
6969
fact that many exhaust flows (e.g., toilet exhaust, electrical room exhaust) are not normally measured
6970
and the total differential of airflow cannot be determined. Second, the measurement of outside airflow
6971
and exhaust airflows could have significant errors and that the error in the difference of the two flows
6972
could equal or exceed the quantity you need to maintain set point for proper building pressure (Kettler
6973
1995).
6974
6975
Return Fan Plenum Pressure
6976
One of the more reliable control designs is to control the return fan to maintain a positive static pressure
6977
in the return plenum and modulate the relief dampers to maintain building static pressure. A pressure
6978
sensor in the return air plenum adjusts the capacity of the return fan, usually by a variable frequency
6979
drive (VFD). In addition, pressure sensors monitor the indoor and outdoor pressure difference. This
6980
pressure difference signal modulates the position of the relief damper, directly controlling building
6981
pressure. The relief damper must be controlled separately from other dampers to accommodate varying
6982
building pressure set points. As a note, the relief damper in most cases should be closed when at
6983
minimum outside air. The relief damper’s purpose is to relieve positive pressure when economizing.
6984
The return fan configuration is typically used when there is a ducted return air system or there is an
6985
unreasonable pressure drop that the supply fan must overcome. The return fan plenum pressure control
6986
typically requires set up and coordination of the return fan speed, the exhaust/relief damper position and
6987
outside air damper position at all operating conditions. Reference Table 1 for advantages and
6988
disadvantages of return fan plenum control. (ASHRAE Guideline 16P and Trane ADM-APN003-EN).
6989
6990
Relief Fan with Direct Control
6991
Pressure sensors monitor the indoor and outdoor pressures to calculate a differential pressure. This
6992
differential pressure directly controls the building pressure by adjusting the relief fan’s capacity. The
6993
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 239
Return to TOP
fan capacity control is typically by a variable frequency drive (VFD). The relief fan only operates when
6994
it is necessary to relieve the building pressure. Relief fans with direct control are typically easier to
6995
control, less expensive to install and less costly to operate than return-fan configurations. Generally, the
6996
relief-fan configuration works best in VAV systems designed with a ceiling-plenum return and when the
6997
supply fan can handle the pressure drop from the air handler’s discharge opening to its return opening.
6998
For advantages and disadvantages of relief fan direct control reference Table 1. (Trane ADM-APN003-
6999
EN).
7000
7001
Table 2.2-A (source Table 1 from Trane ADM-APN003-EN)
7002
7003
7004
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 240
Return to TOP
7005
Pressure Sensor Location
7006
If building static pressure control is utilized with measurement of indoor and outdoor static pressures,
7007
the location of measurement for indoor static pressure must be selected carefully. The indoor sensor
7008
should be located away from openings to the outside or elevator lobbies and in a large representative
7009
area shielded from wind pressure effects or drafts. The outside location must also be selected carefully,
7010
typically 10 to 15 foot above the building and oriented to minimize wind effects from all directions. For
7011
tall buildings in cold climates, and if there is a single fan system serving all floors, the inside and outside
7012
taps need to be at ground level. If the outdoor sensor is roof mounted, you create a positive pressure at
7013
the roof level and an extremely negative pressure at ground level. The discrepancy between ground and
7014
roof pressures varies with the indoor-outdoor temperature difference (stack effect). This temperature
7015
variation needs to be considered when selecting the control point, as a fixed offset will not compensate
7016
for the temperature variance. If the building has fans for the upper floors and other fans for the lower
7017
floors, the indoor and outdoor taps should be on or near the lowest floors served. For tall buildings in
7018
hot climates it would be better to have the outdoor pressure sensor at the top of the building, to help
7019
assure that all parts of the building are positive. If the building is compartmentalized for pressure
7020
control, position an outdoor pressure sensor at the elevation of each compartment with associate indoor
7021
pressure sensors. Depending on building orientation and vertical design, multiple static pressure sensors,
7022
on the exterior and interior of the building, could be utilized to help provide dynamic readings that could
7023
be constantly read and evaluated.
7024
7025
Economizer Considerations
7026
If airside economizers are utilized, then relief of the excess outdoor air supplied to the building will need
7027
to be addressed. When the economizer is enabled excess outdoor air will be supplied to the building.
7028
Without an adequate relief system the building will become over-pressurized. There are three common
7029
types of relief systems: relief dampers, relief fans and return fans. Some of the advantages to the relief
7030
dampers are lowest first cost, least amount of space required, lowest energy cost and simple and quiet
7031
operation. Limitations of the relief dampers are that they usually limited to small systems, have
7032
increased damper leakage, and are susceptible to wind effects. The relief fan systems offer advantages
7033
to return fan systems. These advantages include greater layout flexibility, lower energy costs, less
7034
complicated controls for VAV applications and lower first cost. Limitations of the relief fan systems
7035
include control instability with high pressure drop return air systems, negative building pressures at low
7036
outdoor air rates, and fluctuating building pressure controls. Advantages of the return fan system
7037
compared to relief fan systems are smaller supply fan and motor and they are preferred for constant
7038
volume systems. There are very few limitations to return fan systems however they are prone to control
7039
issues due to flawed control schemes. Signal tracking can lead to varying building pressures since the
7040
same capacity control signal is sent to the supply and return fans and fan volume tracking is not an
7041
effective means to control minimum outdoor air rates in VAV systems. Probably the most reliable
7042
control design is to control the return fan to maintain a positive static pressure in the exhaust plenum and
7043
modulate the relief dampers to maintain building static. The section on Building Static Pressure
7044
Monitoring and Control Strategies noted above and ASHRAE Guideline 16P have more information on
7045
building pressure control strategies.
7046
(Source ASHRAE Journal Sept 2000 Steven T. Taylor, P.E.)
7047
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 241
Return to TOP
7048
Figure 2.3-I (source ASHRAE Journal Sept 2000 Steven T. Taylor, P.E.)
7049
7050
Constant Volume System Considerations
7051
The following case study from the Mold and Moisture Prevention Handbook 2005 by the National
7052
Council of Architectural Registration Boards (Odom, DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 Mold and Moisture
7053
Prevention, Washington, DC) not only demonstrates the climate issues and vapor barrier issues, but also
7054
notes the constant volume toilet exhaust system that created a negatively pressurized building and
7055
infiltration of the hot humid air.
7056
7057
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 242
Return to TOP
7058
7059
Figure 2.3-J (source: Page 10 & 11 of NCARB Mold and Moisture Prevention Handbook
7060
– Odom, DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 “Mold and Moisture Prevention”, Washington, DC)
7061
7062
To help alleviate the constant volume fan issues, implement intermittent toilet exhaust systems to
7063
minimize the airflow exhausting from the building and introduce the make-up air directly to each
7064
individual room allowing for the make-up air to be distributed at the area of concern. This make-up air
7065
should be conditioned, dehumidified, cleaned, etc. by an HVAC system. Providing the make-up air into
7066
the corridors and through the door undercuts does not always guarantee that the make-up air will make it
7067
to the areas of concern
7068
7069
Centralized constant volume exhaust systems should be used minimally if possible. Utilize localized
7070
exhaust systems that will only operate when the space is occupied. This would help minimize the
7071
depressurization of spaces and the required directly ducted make-up air to the spaces. All exhaust
7072
systems should not be oversized and should be sized properly for the odor or contaminant removal of
7073
concern.
7074
7075
Variable Air Volume System Considerations
7076
If the HVAC system utilizes a Variable Air Volume System (VAV), then the outside air quantity to the
7077
building needs to be reviewed for proper airflow amounts during operation of the VAV boxes from
7078
maximum airflow to minimum airflow. The individual VAV airflow quantities needs to be coordinated
7079
with the total system supply airflow (AHU, RTU, etc.) to verify that at minimum system outside airflow
7080
and minimum VAV airflow, the proper amount of outside air is supplied to the building to help maintain
7081
proper building pressurization at various levels or wings of the building. For example; a VAV air
7082
handling unit is sized for 10,000 CFM total airflow with a required minimum outside airflow of 3,000
7083
CFM. There is a connected load of 12,000 CFM of VAV boxes at maximum airflow and 4,000 CFM
7084
with the VAV boxes at minimum airflow. When the air handling unit total supply airflow is reduced,
7085
typically with a variable frequency drive, to accommodate the VAV boxes controlling to minimum
7086
airflow the air handling unit/system must make adjustments, typically with the return air and outside air
7087
damper control, to allow for the 3,000 CFM of minimum outside airflow to be introduced into the air
7088
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 243
Return to TOP
handling unit/system. At full minimum flow the air handling unit should be providing 4,000 CFM of
7089
total supply airflow which consists of 3,000 CFM of outside airflow and 1,000 CFM of return airflow.
7090
In addition, each VAV zone should be analyzed at maximum airflow and minimum airflow. If the
7091
system utilizes an uncontrolled return airflow in the zone, then a routinely low cooling load zone could
7092
often be negatively pressurized, this could present problems in a hot humid climate. See also: Strategy
7093
7.1 Provide Appropriate Outside Air Quantities for each Room or Zone
7094
7095
Return Air Plenums
7096
If the HVAC system uses an open plenum above the dropped ceiling for returning air instead of a hard
7097
connected duct, the plenum will be in a negative pressure. This negative pressure difference can create
7098
untreated infiltration as air finds its way in unplanned construction openings. In the summertime this
7099
can create high humidity in building cavities and cold cavities in winter time creating condensation in
7100
both seasons. This infiltration of outside air is also a major energy penalty for the building and the
7101
HVAC system having to treat this unconditioned outside air. Avoid using plenum returns and utilize
7102
sealed return ducts when possible. If ducted returns are not possible, the case for providing the proper
7103
building envelope with a good air barrier would become even more important. For detailed information
7104
on building envelopes and air barriers reference Strategy 2.2 Limit Condensation within the Building
7105
Envelope and on Interior Surfaces.
7106
7107
7108
Photo courtesy W. Anis
7109
Figure 2.3-K This return air plenum was negatively pressurized with respect to the outdoors.
7110
Condensation occurred on the pipe and became a source for the mold growth.
7111
7112
Duct Leakage
7113
Excessive air leakage on either the supply or the return ductwork will cause the supply air or return air
7114
within the space to become unbalanced. If the supply ductwork is leaking air into the ceiling space the
7115
AHU may deliver less air than it returns. This imbalance can depressurize the space, resulting in
7116
infiltration of unconditioned outside air into the space or wall cavity (Figure 37A). Conversely, if the
7117
unit has a return side air leak, it will deliver more air than it returns from the space (Figure 37B). In this
7118
case, the mechanical space housing the unit and the attached wall systems would become depressurized.
7119
Depressurization could cause unconditioned outside air to infiltrate the wall cavity, even if the
7120
conditioned space were at a positive pressure. Preventing ductwork leakage is critical for controlling
7121
building pressurization. (Source: NCARB Mold and Moisture Prevention Handbook – Odom, DuBose,
7122
Scott & Nelson 2005 “Mold and Moisture Prevention”, Washington, DC)
7123
7124
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 244
Return to TOP
An example of unsealed exhaust duct is shown in Figure 16.3. In a small hotel, unsealed exhaust ducts
7125
pulled outside air through the outside wall continuously. The air carried moisture, which condensed
7126
behind the vinyl wall covering in the cool hotel rooms. The mold created the musty odors so familiar in
7127
the humid climates, and the building’s interior walls had to be pulled out and replaced to eliminate
7128
health risk from that mold. In the Cummings study described, the field investigators measured the
7129
airflows entering and leaving the one unsealed central toilet exhaust system in the business hotel. A
7130
single fan on the roof drew air from exhaust grills in 40 guest rooms. The total of all airflows entering
7131
all 40 exhaust grills was 1324 cfm. The airflow leaving the fan on the roof was 2799 cfm. In other
7132
words, the leaking exhaust duct work pulled more air from the building cavities than from bathrooms
7133
(figure 2.3-L). (Humidity Control Design Guide, ASHRAE, Harriman et. al.)
7134
7135
7136
Figure 2.3-L Unsealed Exhaust Duct
7137
7138
SMACNA guidelines for proper construction and sealing of ductwork should be followed to help
7139
eliminate excessive ductwork leakage. For additional measures the ductwork can be pressure tested for
7140
air leakage. The SMACNA Duct Air leakage Test Manual provides procedures and guidelines for leak
7141
testing ductwork.
7142
7143
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 245
Return to TOP
7144
7145
7146
7147
Figure 2.3-M and Figure 2.3-N (source: NCARB Mold and Moisture Prevention Handbook (Odom,
7148
DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 “Mold and Moisture Prevention”, Washington, DC)
7149
7150
Airflow Measurement
7151
Allow for accurate and repeatable field measurement of airflows: outside airflow, supply airflow, return
7152
airflow and exhaust airflow. To be able to evaluate the building pressurization performance one must be
7153
able to determine the actual airflow quantities into and out of the building. One of the most accurate
7154
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 246
Return to TOP
methods for determining airflow quantity is the duct traverse. For all ducted fan systems, AMCA &
7155
ASHRAE both identify an ideal duct traverse plane as 2 ½ equivalent duct diameters from condition
7156
(discharge, elbow, etc) for up to 2500 fpm, this is both upstream and downstream of the duct traverse. If
7157
the air velocity exceeds 2500 fpm, then one needs to add 1 equivalent diameter for each additional 100
7158
fpm. For rectangular duct the equivalent length EL= (4a\*b/Π)0.5 , where “a” & “b” are the duct
7159
dimensions. In addition to the duct layout, the control strategies need to be evaluated to allow for
7160
manipulation and operation of the HVAC systems to allow for fan/system operation in the critical modes
7161
of operation for airflow measurement. For example, airflow measurements should be taken for a
7162
constant volume air handling unit in minimum outside air mode and full airside economizer mode. The
7163
control sequencing can become more complex when a Variable Air Volume Air Handling system is
7164
employed with airside economizer. The system outside air minimum, full airside economizer airflow,
7165
maximum VAV airflow conditions, minimum VAV airflow conditions, building pressure control
7166
strategies (relief fan control or return fan plenum pressure control), all have to be taken into
7167
consideration to determine what airflow measurements are required and how will these measurements be
7168
obtained, both actual field location and through control manipulation.
7169
7170
The use of roof top units presents a problem for outdoor airflow measurement. Unless the intake hood
7171
for the outside air is extended with the proper duct length to allow for a duct traverse, as noted above,
7172
the outside airflow quantity on a roof top unit cannot be accurately determined. In addition, if the roof
7173
top unit supply and return ductwork is installed without the proper equivalent duct length required for a
7174
duct traverse, the supply and return airflow quantities cannot be accurately determined.
7175
7176
Many buildings utilize exhaust fans that are installed without ductwork. These are typically wall
7177
mounted or roof mounted fans exhausting mechanical rooms, janitorial rooms, electrical rooms or
7178
kitchen areas. In the majority of theses installations, there is no means available to accurately measure
7179
the airflow. Try to avoid these installations by installing ductwork or grilles to allow for airflow
7180
measurement.
7181
7182
For a description of proper design and installation considerations to allow for accurate and repeatable
7183
measurement of airflows reference the Strategy 7.2 Continuously Monitor and Control Outdoor Air
7184
Delivery sections of this document.
7185
7186
Verification
7187
1. Verify proper construction of building envelope including but not limited to proper use of air
7188
barriers and sealing of all pipe, conduit, ductwork and any other envelope penetrations.
7189
Reference Strategy 2.2 Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces.
7190
7191
2. Provide the design information for the building pressurization requirements on the contract drawings.
7192
This should include total airflows into and out of the building, required directional airflow and/or the
7193
required pressure differential for the building. A summary of the building pressurization
7194
requirements and airflow and pressure values in a tabular format is extremely helpful.
7195
7196
3. Test, adjust and balance the HVAC system and verify outdoor air, exhaust, supply and return
7197
airflows to assure that the building is maintaining the proper pressurization relationship. This
7198
includes testing the systems in minimum and maximum airflow modes. For the various modes and
7199
measurements that are required reference Building Static Pressure Monitoring and Control
7200
Strategies and Variable Air Volume System Considerations sections above. In addition reference
7201
AABC, NEBB, ASHRAE, or TABB for standards and procedures required for the testing, adjusting
7202
and balancing of HVAC systems.
7203
7204
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 247
Return to TOP
4. Perform pressure differential mapping to verify actual pressure relationships. Performing pressure
7205
measurements between inside the building and outside only can lead to misdiagnoses of building
7206
pressurization. Pressure differential measurements identify the potential for airflow between spaces.
7207
Multiple pressure readings inside the building can identify pressure differences between the spaces
7208
and wall cavities. It cannot be assumed that a wall cavity has the identical pressure gradient of the
7209
space. If the wall cavity is positively pressurized with infiltration with reference to the space, an
7210
electrical outlet could let this infiltration containing moisture and contaminants migrate into the
7211
space. These pressure readings should also include the ceiling plenum pressures to verify that they
7212
are positive in pressure with respect to the outdoors. Measurements should be taken after all HVAC
7213
systems have been tested and balanced. If possible the pressure measurements should be taken at
7214
varying system operating conditions (i.e., maximum and minimum airflows for VAV systems, room
7215
fan coil units on and off). Depending on building orientation and HVAC system configuration, the
7216
pressure readings might be required to be taken at close to peak seasonal conditions. For tall
7217
buildings this could include winter time pressure readings to determine pressurization effects due to
7218
stack effect and summer readings to determine any reverse stack effect conditions. Reference Figure
7219
60B displaying an actual mapping of pressures from a building room in central Florida (Odom,
7220
DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 Mold and Moisture Prevention, Washington, DC: National Council
7221
of Architectural Registration Boards).
7222
7223
5. If proper pressure relationships cannot be obtained utilizing the HVAC systems, then additional
7224
building envelope requirements will need to be addressed.
7225
7226
7227
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 248
Return to TOP
7228
Fig 2.3-O (Source: page 87 Odom, DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 “Mold and Moisture Prevention”,
7229
Washington, DC: National Council of Architectural Registration Boards)
7230
7231
7232
7233
7234
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 249
Return to TOP
7235
Strategy 2.4 – Control Indoor Humidity
7236
7237
7238
Principles of Indoor Condensation
7239
It may be common to say that the dew falls, but more correctly, it condenses from the surrounding air.
7240
This happens because cold air can hold less water in vapor form than hot air. Outdoors, air heats up
7241
during the day and absorbs water that changes from liquid to vapor state (evaporates). Then, when the
7242
air cools at night, it reaches the point at which it can no longer hold all the vapor, and the vapor turns
7243
back into a liquid (condenses). The point at which this happens is a temperature called the dew point.
7244
7245
Every air condition19 has a unique dew point temperature, and when the air is cooled to this temperature,
7246
condensation will result. Here is an example of how this might occur indoors, even on a surface that
7247
you might not think of as very cold.
7248
7249
The psychrometric chart (called "psych" chart for short) is a two-dimensional graph of air conditions.
7250
The horizontal axis is the familiar dry bulb temperature. The vertical axis is the absolute humidity ratio
7251
in mass water vapor / mass dry air20. Each point on the graph represents a unique condition of moist air.
7252
To fix the "moist air conditions", we must know two properties and at least one of them must be related
7253
to humidity. For instance, we may know the dry bulb and wet bulb temperature, or the dry bulb and
7254
relative humidity. Try out various conditions yourself on the psychrometric chart to determine the dew
7255
point temperature at which condensation will occur.
7256
7257
7258
7259
7260
7261
7262
7263
[REMAINDER OF PAGE LEFT INTENTIALLY BLANK]
7264
7265
19 It takes two measurements to determine the properties of moist air at a given atmospheric pressure. At least one of these
measurements must be humidity related (relative humidity, wet bulb temperature, absolute humidity, dew point, etc.)
20 In IP units, often expressed in grains/lb. or lbs./lb. There are 7,000 grains in one lb.

7266
7267
7268
7269
7270
7271
7272
7273
7274
7275
7276
7277
7278
7279
7280
7281
7282
Figure 2.4-B and Figure 2.4-C
7283

This is the saturation curve, at which air is saturated with as much water vapor as it
can hold at any given temperature. Air can only exist at conditions to the right of this
curve. Its physical molecular properties prevent it from holding more moisture (going
vertically up the graph) or being colder at a given moisture content (going
horizontally to the left) than that.
75F at 80% relative humidity is
an example of a condition that
can occur when indoor relative
humidity is not properly
controlled. (LINK to 62.1
requirement and all the text on
controlling indoor humidity.)
Moist air,at the starting condition of 75F, 80% r.h., will reach
saturation at 68F when it is cooled by approaching the cold outer
surface of the pipe insulation shown at left. It will start to release its
moisture resulting in condensation.
This air will not result in condensation
at the conditions given. It is dry
enough that it will stop cooling before
it reaches the saturation line.
75F 60% relative
humidity is a common
indoor room condition,
when indoor humidity is
well controlled. (LINK
to 62.1, Fundamentals
indoor design
conditions).
This horizontal line and the direction arrow
to the left represent what happens to air
when it cools, such as would happen when it
approaches a cold surface (such as the pipe
surface to the left). This is called "sensible
cooling" until the air reaches the saturation
curve. If the air is cooled any more, it will
roughly follow the saturation curve down
and to the left, resulting in the appearance
of liquid moisture, known as condensation.
Whether or not condensation occurs at an air
temperature of 75° F depends on the humidity
conditions (see the Psychrometric Chart above) of
the air and the temperature of surfaces (see the pipe
above) that the room air is exposed to.
The psychrometric chart (called "psych" chart for short) is a two
dimensional graph of air conditions. Each point on the graph
represents a unique condition of moist air. To know these "moist
air conditions", we must know two properties and at least one of
them must be related to humidity. For instance, we may know the
dry bulb and wet bulb temperature, or the dry bulb and relative
humidity.
Insulation systems
always result in
surface
temperatures
somewhat
different from the
surrounding air
(colder than the
surrounding air in
the case of chilled
water piping).
This is a
consequence of
the surface film
itself having a
resistance to heat
flow (typically
about R=0.7 ft2-
hr-degF/Btu). 68F
is approximately
the surface
temperature that
would result from
the conditions
shown.
The condensation could drip on the ceiling, where in combination
with organic matter (dust, drywall paper, paint, etc.) it can result in
biological growth, a bad result for indoor air quality. Furthermore, it
can wet the pipe insulation, allowing the growth of mold, degrading
its insulation properties, resulting in lower surface temperatures
and even more condensation. Unchecked, this process can soak
the insulation and even cause it to slough off the pipe.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 251
Return to TOP
7287
7288
7289
Figure 2.4-D
7290
7291
What can go wrong?
7292
It is common for air conditioning system designers to choose indoor conditions like 50 or 60% relative
7293
humidity, so how can condensation occur? Wouldn’t it take some very cold surfaces (LINK to section)
7294
to result in condensation? The answer is yes if the spaces are conditioned all the time at the peak design
7295
condition., More frequently, however, systems operate at part load conditions at which they may not do
7296
enough dehumidification (see design tips below.) Furthermore, just like outdoor daily cycles of
7297
temperature and humidity, changes in temperature and humidity indoors, such as when systems are
7298
turned off, can lead to condensation22.
7299
7300
A leaky envelope, an un-pressurized building and building materials that absorb and then emit moisture
7301
can foil the ability of systems to control indoor humidity. (Refer to building P control section for more
7302
on this.) ASHRAE Research Project (RP) 1254 found such difficulties with many packaged systems and
7303
climates at the minimum leakage rate allowed by ASHRAE Standard 90.1. ASHRAE Standard 62.1
7304
requires a vapor retarder and caulking of exterior joints, although it does not prescribe an envelope
7305
leakage rate. Humidity considerations often require tighter envelopes that the minimum. If perimeter
7306
22 All building envelopes leak to some extent, and when systems are off, the indoor conditions migrate towards outdoor
humidity conditions. If outdoor humidity is high, this can result in condensation on cold surfaces. Such surfaces (ducts and
pipes) may result when the systems are turned back on. The more leaky the envelope and the more extreme the
outdoor/indoor differential, the more potential exists for this condition. A different effect can occur if it is cold outdoors
when the system is off and the walls cool down lower than the indoor dew point.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 252
Return to TOP
spaces are depressurized, such as when systems are off or if makeup air does not keep up with exhaust,
7307
moisture problems can occur inside walls. Some buildings may require unoccupied system operation to
7308
limit indoor humidity.
7309
7310
Integrated Design Process (Indoor conditions, Loads, Special system capabilities)
7311
An integrated design process to meet multiple needs, including avoiding condensation, starts with the
7312
selection of indoor design conditions. These conditions will normally be based on comfort (LINK to
7313
section and 55) and air quality Codes23 and Standards (LINK to 62.1). If there are conditions that can
7314
lead to condensation, such as large indoor sources, unusually cold surfaces, or continuous ventilation
7315
with conditioning cycling on and off in humid climates, then additional analysis of potential
7316
condensation may be advisable. See the following graphic for an overview of the interactions among
7317
the conditioned space, humidity sources and the HVAC system.
7318
7319
7320
7321
7322
Figure 2.4-E
7323
[Editor’s Note: An arrow will be added through wall / window to show infiltration when graphic is
7324
redrawn prior to publication.]
7325
7326
Once we know all the load sources and the quantity of heat and moisture they add to the space, the
7327
common peak load calculation will provide the equipment capacity to meet the sensible and latent
7328
design loads. It is essential that the proper air flow, entering and leaving conditions of the cooling coil
7329
be selected and adhered to in the installation. Equipment capacity ratings at standard conditions usually
7330
do not correspond to the needs of spaces with humidity control challenges and system-specific
7331
23 The 2006 version of the IMC was current at the time of this writing and does not contain humidity requirements for most
occupied spaces.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 253
Return to TOP
parameters based on the load calculation (LINK to Fundamentals) must be used to rate the performance
7332
of equipment.
7333
7334
A high humidity load (low sensible heat ratio) requires a flow rate lower than the standard 400 cfm per
7335
ton; selections as low as 200 cfm per ton are not uncommon. This can be lower than the catalog rating
7336
information of packaged equipment and may require discussion with the manufacturer’s equipment
7337
applications personnel, an upgrade of the equipment selection or a change in the system type.
7338
7339
A second load calculation at a “humidity challenge” condition is required by ASHRAE standard 62.1,
7340
starting with the 2007 version. This condition requires that the designer consider the dehumidification
7341
performance of the system at a high latent, low sensible load condition. (LINK to 62.1 User’s Manual
7342
example). However, even this new requirement does not mean that special humidity control systems be
7343
added. Simpler solutions are often available, as described in the next section.
7344
7345
One definition of hot, humid climates given by Odom, DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 Mold and
7346
Moisture Prevention on p 11-14 is simple and helpful in identifying the latent/sensible issues for
7347
different climates.
7348
7349
System Design Tips
7350
Indoor humidity can be controlled in many designs without the use of reheat or a dedicated dehumidifier
7351
by using strategies such as dedicated outdoor air systems, hot gas reheat, energy recovery ventilation or
7352
variable volume control, as opposed to cycling control or temperature reset,.
7353
A dedicated outdoor air system (DOAS) decouples ventilation requirements from the conditioning space
7354
loads and can assist in controlling indoor humidity when outdoor air humidity is and high and variable.
7355
In order to do this, the DOAS itself must have the ability to deal with the humidity load at all outdoor air
7356
conditions which in many climates means it must have heating and cooling/dehumidification capability..
7357
If the discharge air temperature of the DOAS in cooling mode is kept constant, then the outdoor air it
7358
introduces will not add humidity load to the space. (PSYCH chart figure might be helpful here).
7359
However, the DOAS does not address outdoor air that infiltrates through wall cracks, open windows or
7360
doors.
7361
7362
Hot gas reheat uses recovered energy to reheat air that has been cooled and dehumidified. This is
7363
essentially the same cycle that a dedicated refrigerant cycle dehumidifier uses, but it can be incorporated
7364
into the main conditioning system. With this capability, the system can remove humidity from a space
7365
without over-cooling it sensibly. Thus it is useful at conditions of low SHR, which may, for instance,
7366
occur at part load. (Psych chart figure might be helpful.) Hot gas refers to refrigerant gas that the
7367
compressor in a refrigeration cycle discharges on its way to the condenser, where its heat will be
7368
rejected to the outdoors. In hot has reheat some of it is diverted to a separate coil downstream of the
7369
dehumidification coil. While the hot gas is recovered waste heat, the entire process is not without energy
7370
cost
7371
7372
VAV, usually thought of as a method for zone control, also works for dehumidification because less air
7373
at a low coil discharge temperature retains the moisture removal capacity of the system at part load.
7374
Temperature reset does not accomplish this. (LINK to 62 UM example). This combines the energy
7375
conservation and zone control benefits of VAV with dehumidification. Proper control of outdoor air for
7376
ventilation in VAV systems and supplying the amount of outdoor air required to balance exhaust is
7377
important and required by Standard 62.1 (Link to these sections). Designers will want to limit the
7378
amount of exhaust and consider its potential for undesirable depressurization on a space-by-space basis,
7379
particularly at the perimeter of the building in hot and humid climates. For instance, in hotel rooms,
7380
toilet exhaust can be intermittent based on occupancy (interlocked with the light switch).
7381
7382
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 254
Return to TOP
Many systems are equipped with air side economizers, which provide free cooling with outdoor air
7383
when its enthalpy (total sensible and latent heat content) is less than that of outdoor air. If the system is
7384
not designed to handle the humidity load of outdoor air that is very moist (even though it has lower
7385
enthalpy), then it may be necessary to “lock out” such economizer air based on dew point temperature.
7386
For more information about this strategy, see Harriman et al 2001 Humidity Control Design Guide.
7387
7388
Small Packaged Systems:
7389
You may need to upgrade the type of cooling unit or system to achieve the control of humidity. An
7390
ASHRAE Research Project (RP 1254) evaluated multiple system configurations and options and
7391
discussed their ability to meet dehumidification requirements in various climates and load conditions.
7392
They found numerous situations where packaged equipment could not meet the needs even at full load.
7393
It is essential to closely examine the latest packaged equipment options and their ability to meet the load.
7394
For instance, some packaged rooftop units are now available with hot has reheat capability under
7395
proprietary names (see Carrier 48HJ004-028 in references). Furthermore, catalog data (see page 128 of
7396
the referenced product brochure) shows air flow as low as 200 cfm/ton, which is even lower than the RP
7397
considered.
7398
7399
Other options, such as DOAS and VAV involve system type selections that must be made early in the
7400
HVAC design process. While VAV systems are often thought of for large multi-zone systems, they are
7401
needed for humidity control even in single zone systems. VAV can almost always be applied to
7402
packaged rooftop equipment larger than 20 tons (and may be available at even smaller sizes), chilled
7403
water air handlers and built-up DX air handlers. In order for this strategy to be effective in a DX system,
7404
the coil must run continuously cold. Therefore, part load refrigerant flow control, such as hot gas bypass,
7405
variable refrigerant volume (VRV) and variable flow compressors may be needed. Sometimes, using
7406
VAV to control humidity may be available with an upgrade of the HVAC system type to a built-up
7407
system or multi-zone unit.
7408
7409
No Humidity Controller Needed
7410
In most spaces, using one or several of the above techniques will be sufficient to achieve indoor
7411
conditions of sufficiently low humidity to avoid condensation. For instance, if the sensible heat ratio of
7412
the space at the part load conditions is above 0.5 (latent load not more than ½ of the total load), this can
7413
be achieved with VAV, but may require low coil discharge temperature and higher thermostat set points.
7414
How do you know for sure? This requires the calculation at some common and demanding part load
7415
conditions in order to determine if you can rely on open loop control of indoor humidity (no humidity
7416
sensor to feedback and control system functions). (Reference to 62 UM example – Martha suggests
7417
bringing it in).
7418
7419
Special Spaces
7420
Spaces that have large latent loads and small sensible loads, either at full load or at part load, may
7421
require dedicated humidity control systems. Spaces that may exhibit these characteristics include
7422
underground spaces, swimming and bathing areas, kitchens, or those where large amounts of
7423
unconditioned humid outdoor air enter, for instance by door opening or other forms of infiltration.
7424
7425
In the case of showers, natatoriums and cooking areas, for example, it is accepted that humidity will be
7426
high, condensation will occur and surfaces are constructed that are inorganic and cleanable. Even in a
7427
natatorium, not all the surfaces or materials may be so tolerant of very high humidity and condensation
7428
can be objectionable for other reasons. For these reasons, dehumidification is often used in natatoriums.
7429
7430
When such spaces with high humidity are in buildings with other spaces not so tolerant of the conditions,
7431
it is best to keep them at negative pressure with respect to the building; otherwise the building itself may
7432
require special consideration for humidity control.
7433
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 255
Return to TOP
7434
Some designs call for intentionally cool surfaces, such as a chilled ceiling system and (n-insulated
7435
ductwork in occupied space. It is especially important to analyze the resulting space humidity to avoid
7436
condensation in these systems.
7437
7438
Dedicated Dehumidification Systems
7439
As discussed above, certain spaces with high humidity loads in proportion to their sensible loads may
7440
require a dedicated dehumidifier,. This may be based on the refrigerant cycle or on a desiccant. The
7441
latter uses a material that is hygroscopic (attracts water) that passively removes water from an air stream
7442
and then must be regenerated by using heat to drive off the moisture. Use of waste heat (for instance
7443
from the exhaust air stream or a combustion process) can reduce the energy impact of this type system.
7444
7445
Refrigerant cycle dehumidifiers use both the cold and hot side of a refrigeration cycle first to cool and
7446
dehumidify an air stream and then to reheat it. The resulting discharge air is then both warmer and drier
7447
than the air that entered the dehumidifier. One common application for this type of dehumidification is
7448
in natatoriums, where a combined pool heating, ventilating and dehumidification unit is often used.
7449
7450
Dry desiccant dehumidifiers typically use a crystalline substance in a rotating wheel that adsorbs
7451
moisture from the air stream to the occupied space. The most typical liquid desiccant is a strong
7452
solution of lithium bromide and water, which absorbs moisture from the air stream. In all cases, the
7453
desiccant and must be regenerated with heat, which may be recovered to conserve energy.
7454
7455
Insert system graphics and psych chart w/air process.
7456
7457
Humidity Design Considerations for Specific Spaces
7458
[Note: Reference and draw key information from Harriman et al 2001 Humidity Control Design Guide.
7459
Pssible summary chart.]
7460
7461
[Note: Insert new Table here and discussion of Case Studies,]
7462
7463
7464
Case Study #1 [Editor’s Note: Case study information will be added prior to publication.]
7465
7466
A good example would be a temporary classroom with a packaged constant volume air conditioner in a
7467
humid climate. The unit cycles off after the school day. I don’t have details of an actual horror story
7468
but maybe someone does. We could invent details of the system being off, cold surfaces remaining,
7469
condensation and mold ensuing, community reactions, etc.
7470
7471
7472
Case Study #2: Assembly Room Humidity Control with VAV
7473
7474
An assembly room (note to reviewers: I have some pix of the room if you think it’s useful) that is part
7475
of a large religious and education facility has a single chilled water air handler. The room is used for
7476
meetings, parties and performances and the chilled water coil meets the full load, even at about 50%
7477
outdoor air. Conventional part load controls would throttle the chilled water valve at constant air flow,
7478
resulting in a warmer coil that would lose its ability to dehumidify unless reheat is applied. The
7479
following shows how an upgrade of the air handler to VAV control accomplishes dehumidification
7480
without the use of reheat. The upgrade also includes demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) by sensing
7481
carbon dioxide and tracking the actual outdoor air supply using dedicated minimum outdoor air inlets
7482
with pitot tube flow sensing and dampers.
7483
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 256
Return to TOP
7484
See the following chart and the accompanying psychrometric chart, which represents the load conditions
7485
at the dehumidification design conditions (LINK to Fundamentals) and not the more common peak dry
7486
bulb temperature. Note that the system can meet the low room temperature and humidity design
7487
conditions. In order to do this, the coil discharge temperature is kept low (48.50F). Note the high latent
7488
load (54% of total) and the low air flow (about 180 cfm/ton) that requires a low entering chilled water
7489
temperature (390F).
7490
7491
7492
7493
