References
7499
ASHRAE Research Project (RP) 1254 Evaluating the Ability of Unitary Equipment to Maintain
7500
Adequate Space Humidity Levels, Phase II Final Report. Note that this report used the following
7501
infiltration model when the system was off: Infiltration= (0.038 cfm/ft2) + (0.1)(WindSpeed in mph)
7502
(converted to IP units, see page 14). The moisture storage model used in this Research Project,
7503
combined with this system-off infiltration model resulted in significant humidity loads on the systems.
7504
7505
WEATHERMASTER® 48HJ004-028 48HE003-006 Single-Package Rooftop Units Gas
7506
Heating/Electric Cooling 2 to 25 Nominal Tons Form 48H-1PD, Carrier Corporation 2006. Note that
7507
page 128 of this brochure shows pressure drop of various units and shows a rating point for the 6 ton
7508
unit at about 200 cfm/ton. RP-1254 (see reference) evaluated packaged unit performance at 300-400
7509
cfm/ton (see pages 16-17 of the RP).
7510
7511
Harriman et al 2001 Humidity Control Design Guide
7512
7513
Odom, DuBose, Scott & Nelson 2005 Mold and Moisture Prevention, Washington, DC: National
7514
Council of Architectural Registration Boards
7515
7516
7517
7518
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 258
Return to TOP
7519
Strategy 2.5 – Select Suitable Materials, Equipment and Assemblies for Unavoidably
7520
Wet Areas
7521
7522
Indoor areas subject to repeated wetting
7523
Areas within buildings that may be subjected to high moisture or repeated wetting events include those
7524
where water is used frequently (such as is the case in kitchens/cafeterias, washrooms, custodial closets,
7525
counters and backsplashes around sinks, indoor pools, spas and locker rooms). Entranceway areas may
7526
also experience frequent wetting, depending on climate (hence the need for careful design of track-off
7527
systems utilizing moisture -tolerant matting.) See Strategy 3.4 Provide Track-off Systems at Entrances
7528
for more information. Mechanical rooms are an area of concern. Any flooring area subjected to wet
7529
cleaning practices must also be considered, as must the wall materials that are in contact with these floor
7530
zones. Carpeted areas have received some focus, with particular attention to minimizing the use of water,
7531
and making careful provision to ensure adequate drying. Locations at or below grade in geographic
7532
areas subject to flooding are primary targets for the use of appropriate moisture-tolerant materials.
7533
7534
Problems associated with wet materials
7535
The following problems related to building materials (and finishes / furnishings) have been associated
7536
with damp conditions (US EPA, 2008):
7537
• Colonization of building materials by molds, wood-decaying fungi, and insect pests (e.g.,
7538
termites and carpenter ants),
7539
• Warping of wooden materials,
7540
• Corrosion,
7541
• Dissolving of water-soluble building material components,
7542
• Moisture-induced reactions with latex flooring adhesives, or elevation of the pH of concrete so
7543
that applied adhesives do not cure.
7544
• Damage to brick or concrete products due to freeze-thaw cycles,
7545
• Damage to paints and varnishes
7546
7547
Materials susceptible to moisture damage
7548
Materials that are vulnerable to moisture-triggered damage have one or more of the following
7549
characteristics (US EPA, 2008).
7550
1. Nutrient-content (carbon based) that can support growth of biocontaminants (eg. paper,
7551
fiberboard, particleboard, oriented strand board, plywood, and solid wooden materials).
7552
2. Porous structure that readily absorbs water and subsequently also takes longer to dry once wet
7553
(eg. wood, particleboard, fiberboard, concrete, concrete masonry, brick and fiber-based
7554
insulations such as fiberglass, mineral wool, and cellulose).
7555
3. Composition susceptible to crumbling, dissolving, delaminating or deforming due to presence of
7556
moisture or upon drying (eg. wood, paper, wood-based composites, and some gypsum board
7557
materials).
7558
4. Lack of effective anti-microbial properties (eg. untreated paper, fiberboard, particleboard,
7559
oriented strand board, plywood, some solid wooden materials, as well as some plastics, paints,
7560
and varnishes).
7561
7562
Moisture-related damage to building materials usually requires a combination of factors to occur. For
7563
example, damage due to molds, bacteria or wood-decaying fungi will occur only if required moisture
7564
levels and duration of wetting occurs, the material contains sufficient bio-nutrients and does not possess
7565
natural anti-microbial characteristics. Other materials may tolerate wetting conditions if they possess
7566
characteristics (and conditions) that support rapid drying. A material such as concrete, though not
7567
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 259
Return to TOP
particularly susceptible to moisture damage, may contribute to the damage of materials in direct contact
7568
with it, due to concretes nature to absorb and transport moisture.
7569
7570
Porous materials that are not allowed to dry adequately following wetting episodes, or installed such that
7571
they are continuously dampened by capillary action from moisture sources in contact with them, can
7572
suffer damage. Gypsum wallboard, when coated with a moisture-impermeable vinyl surface can lead to
7573
severe problems if moisture penetrates from the back surface then becomes trapped.
7574
7575
Selection of moisture-resistant materials
7576
As is that case with most design decisions, selection of building materials involves consideration of
7577
multiple criteria. In cases where repeated wetting is a priority concern, moisture-resistance may become
7578
a dominant factor, however, this does not mean that other considerations should be ignored (see, for
7579
example, the overview to Strategy 5.1 - Control Indoor Contaminant Sources through Appropriate
7580
Material Selection). A material that successfully handles frequent wetting, but also emits significant
7581
contaminants (volatile or particulate) is not the best choice for indoor use.
7582
7583
Consideration must also be made concerning the potential source of moisture that will affect the material.
7584
Flooring materials subject to wet mopping require very different attention when compared with flooring
7585
materials overlaying concrete slabs with absorb and transfer moisture from below. A wall material that
7586
occasionally becomes wet may be satisfactory if it has properties enabling rapid drying.
7587
7588
FEMA’s guidance on Flood-Resistant Materials (1993) includes tables of common building materials
7589
ranked with a 5-point scale in terms of “flood resistance”. Separate tables are given for Flooring
7590
Materials, and for Wall and Ceiling Materials.
7591
7592
Materials such as stainless steel, copper, some stones, china, and porcelain tile are favored for use in
7593
bathrooms, kitchens, and entryways where they are likely to get wet since they contain no nutrients, do
7594
not absorb water, and are stable when wet.
7595
7596
Ceramic tile, glass, plastic resins, metals, and cement-based products are highly resistant to fungal
7597
growth and are thus commonly used in areas such as entryway floors, bathroom floors, tub surrounds,
7598
showers, locker rooms, pool and spa rooms, and kitchens.
7599
7600
Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) materials are among the most frequently used wall and floor finishing
7601
materials because they provide inexpensive, easy to- clean surfaces. They typically resist microbial
7602
growth, but (as discussed below) they may degrade in the presence of moisture (see section on PVC
7603
Materials in Strategy 5.1 - Control Indoor Contaminant Sources through Appropriate Material Selection).
7604
7605
Flooring materials considered to be moisture-resistant include clay tile, quarry tile, terrazzo, or ceramic
7606
tile as well as concrete materials such as concrete tile, or pre-cast concrete. Rubber sheets or tiles may be
7607
considered (keeping emissions properties in mind) while non-porous stone, slate, or cast stone with
7608
waterproof mortar may also be used successfully in potentially wet areas.
7609
7610
Moisture-resistant wall materials include ceramic veneer or ceramic wall tile, glass blocks or glass
7611
panels, and concrete. Latex paints will permit moisture transfer and facilitate drying of wall materials.
7612
7613
In general, the use of anti-microbial agents to increase the moisture-resistance of an otherwise unsuitable
7614
material should be discouraged in favor of strategies that utilize material assemblies capable of
7615
providing adequate moisture tolerance without additional modification.
7616
7617
7618
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 260
Return to TOP
7619
Strategy 2.6 – Consider the Impacts of Landscaping and Indoor Plants on Moisture and
7620
Contaminant Levels
7621
7622
Outdoor Plantings
7623
Transpiration is a physiological process fundamental to the life of green plants. Water molecules are
7624
absorbed from the soil via plant root hairs. Water is then transported as a column of liquid from the
7625
roots, through the stem, and into the leaves via osmosis through xylem or wood cells. Water molecules
7626
evaporate from leaves through small pores known as stomata. Investigations in the 1970s (Kozak et al.,
7627
1979, 1980) showed that occupants of homes whose roofs were heavily shaded by trees tended to have
7628
more allergies possibly due to elevated mold levels associated with damp roofs.
7629
7630
Overwatering of planter boxes located at grade level contiguous with the building envelope is known to
7631
result in capillary entry of water through porous masonry into the building interior. High water tables
7632
around buildings as well as overwatering of landscaping around building perimeters can also result in
7633
capillary entry of water into the building foundation. Builders can reduce capillary penetration of water
7634
through damp proofing of foundation materials.
7635
7636
Green roofs typically involve the installation of a porous soil to a depth of 2 to 8 inches above the roof
7637
structure. Small herbaceous plants with noninvasive root systems are typically grown in these roof
7638
gardens. In a few green roofs a thicker layer of soil up to a meter in depth is used in conjunction with
7639
the installation of shrubs and small trees. All roof gardens involve a substantial increase in weight of the
7640
roof structure (see Graham, 2006 for code implications).
7641
7642
Benefits of green roofs are thought to include energy savings associated with better thermal insulation of
7643
the roof and protection of the roof membrane from deterioration caused by atmospheric ozone.
7644
Disadvantages associated with green roofs include the increased difficulty of finding potential sources
7645
roof leaks.
7646
7647
One of the benefits of green roofs is that the garden/vegetation provides a habitat for insects, birds, and
7648
small animals in an environment that would otherwise be hostile to life. However, if air handling units
7649
are installed in the roof garden consideration should be given to elevating the height of the unit above
7650
the vegetation to reduce the entry of microorganisms (from the soil) and to reduce the possible entry of
7651
herbicides and insecticides that may be used in garden maintenance. Similar disadvantages would also
7652
apply to air handling units with grade level or below grade outdoor air inlets.
7653
7654
Very little information is available in the peer-reviewed literature on long term testing of green versus
7655
conventional roofs for leakage. A well designed and properly installed roof membrane is required to
7656
prevent leakage.
7657
7658
Green facades are trellis systems added to the envelope wall which support the growth of climbing
7659
plants (vines) rooted along the building foundation (Sharp, 2007). Several years are required for the
7660
climbing plants to grow along the trellis system and create a green façade along the entire wall.
7661
7662
Living or vegetative walls differ from green facades in that panels or planters containing vegetation are
7663
affixed onto a structural framework on the envelope wall. Shrubs, ferns, other vegetation growing in the
7664
panels can be installed along the wall in a few days creating an “instant” living wall. An irrigation
7665
system is installed in the living wall to provide moisture for the plant roots in each affixed vegetative
7666
panel.
7667
7668
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 261
Return to TOP
In both green facades and green walls plant roots do not contact the building envelope (Sharp, 2007).
7669
However, care must be taken during installation to make sure that the structural framework holding the
7670
botanical materials does not penetrate or disrupt the waterproofing integrity of the envelope wall. In
7671
addition, both green facades and living walls require attention to watering, fertilizer application and pest
7672
control. Periodic pruning is required to remove dead botanical materials and in dry climates to reduce
7673
fire risk.
7674
7675
Indoor Plantings
7676
Potted plants are commonly found in indoor environments. A moderate number of well maintained
7677
plants in a commercial building are aesthetically pleasing and compatible with a healthy indoor
7678
environment. However, if watering of the plants results in wetting of carpets, fiberboard, and other
7679
biodegradable finishing materials, microbial growth (especially fungi) will occur. Some potting
7680
materials such as wicker baskets are susceptible to water damage and mold growth (Kozak et al., 1979,
7681
1980).
7682
7683
Dense indoor vegetation is found in some buildings in atrium gardens. In general, the presence of an
7684
atrium garden is beneficial to the indoor environment as long as the garden is carefully maintained (e.g.,
7685
green vegetation produces oxygen during photosynthesis). However, mold growth can occur on moist
7686
mulch (e.g., bark and wood chips) and dead leaves present on the atrium floor. Therefore, maintenance
7687
activities in the atrium garden such as removal of moldy vegetation and mulch should be carried out
7688
with care so as to minimize dispersion of bioaerosols.
7689
7690
In buildings with attached greenhouses high levels of mold spores occur in the indoor air relative to that
7691
found outdoors (Botzenhart et al., 1984). The sources of elevated indoor mold spores include fungi
7692
growing on green leaves (e.g., Cladosporium and other leaf-sourced molds) and Aspergillus (including
7693
A. fumigatus) and Penicillium that grows on dead vegetation found on the moist greenhouse floor. The
7694
study by Botzenhart et al., (1984) suggests that having greenhouse-type indoor vegetation is not
7695
compatible with good microbial IAQ.
7696
7697
Some consideration has been given to using living walls or “active walls” as part of ventilation systems
7698
in green buildings (Wikipedia, 2007). This concept involves passage of ventilation air through living
7699
wall vegetation and is based on the belief that the plant itself or the microbes associated with the plant
7700
root system removes contaminants from the circulating air. That plants function as air cleaners neglects
7701
the extensive biochemistry literature which shows that plant metabolism itself is a significant source of
7702
VOC (e.g., terpenes) emissions (Went, 1974). In addition, the concept that living walls are biofilters is
7703
controversial because moist surfaces especially at the root-soil interface are likely to be a strong source
7704
of bioaerosols capable of degrading IAQ.
7705
7706
The belief that indoor plantings are potential air cleaners originated from NASA studies in the 1970s and
7707
1980s (see review by Levin, 1992). Green vegetation was thought to reduce levels of airborne VOCs
7708
such as formaldehyde, benzene, and trichloroethylene based on laboratory chamber experiments.
7709
However, calculations suggested that in a typical home, approximately 680 plants would be needed to
7710
achieve a modest reduction in indoor VOC levels (Levin, 1992). Additional studies suggested that the
7711
pot or potting medium itself was a strong VOC sink. At the present time research on indoor living walls
7712
and the ability of extensive indoor vegetation to enhance IAQ is limited. The study by Botzenhart et al.
7713
(1984) has shown that “greenhouse-type” vegetation indoors can degrade IAQ. Therefore, the designer
7714
should be cautious with regard to installation of living walls in commercial non-industrial buildings.
7715
7716
Plants and Moisture
7717
Engineers and architects are familiar with the term moisture content which refers to the total amount of
7718
water present in a construction or finishing material. Biologists are more familiar with the terms water
7719
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 262
Return to TOP
activity and Equilibrium Relative Humidity (ERH) relating to the ease with which free water can be
7720
extracted by living cells from the substrate in order to support growth. A piece of pinewood with a
7721
moisture content of about 17% is equivalent in biological terms to an ERH of 80% or a water activity of
7722
0.8 (on a decimal system). An ERH of 80% or water activity of 0.8 is equivalent to the ratio of the
7723
vapor pressure of water in the pores of the pine wood relative to the vapor pressure of pure water at the
7724
same temperature and pressure. At this point (water activity of 0.8) some fungi can extract moisture
7725
from the pine wood and begin the growth process.
7726
7727
Unfortunately, there is no easy way to measure water activity in the field as compared to direct reading
7728
methods for moisture content by moisture meter. This is because of the variation in the amounts of
7729
bound water (held tightly by strong chemical bonds) and free water (held less tightly by absorptive and
7730
absorptive forces) among various substrates (Flannigan, 1992; Flannigan and Miller, 2001).
7731
7732
The above authors have published instructive examples of differences between moisture content and
7733
water activity in common food items. Thus, at a constant moisture content of 14% the water activity of
7734
the following three food items is:
7735
7736
• White raisins
0.57
7737
• Wheat grains
0.70
7738
• Peanuts
0.90
7739
7740
Although the moisture content of raisins, wheat grains, and peanuts is identical, spoliation fungi are able
7741
to easily extract-free water from the peanut but not the raisin. The free water in the raisin is tightly held
7742
and no fungus can grow at a water activity of 0.57. A few dry-loving (xerophilic) fungi can grow at a
7743
water activity of 0.7 and many fungi can grow at a water activity of 0.9. In practical terms, although the
7744
moisture content is identical, the peanut becomes moldy while the raisin remains edible.
7745
7746
Flannigan (1992) and Flannigan and Miller (2001) have provided similar examples contrasting moisture
7747
content and water activity for construction materials. Thus, at a water activity of 0.8 the moisture
7748
content of the following building materials is:
7749
7750
• Softwood
17%
7751
• Wallpaper
11.3%
7752
• Cement
1%
7753
• Brick
about 0.5%
7754
7755
At the moisture contents listed above for the four building materials, some xero-tolerant (dry loving)
7756
fungi can extract free water to support growth. Although softwood has the highest moisture content, its
7757
water is more firmly bound to the substrate (wood cells) than the water in the brick or cement.
7758
7759
Nutrient and temperature also play an important role in controlling biological growth (Flannigan, 1992;
7760
Flannigan and Miller, 2001). Molds are able to more easily extract water from brick and cement that is
7761
soiled by dust and dirt because
7762
of the nutrient in the soiling and the hygroscopic nature of the dirt and dust.
7763
7764
Photo #2 shows ferns growing from carpeted flooring in a room previously damaged by a water disaster.
7765
The ferns are growing nicely even though the debris on the floor surface is dry. The roof systems of
7766
green plants require a water activity of at least 0.95 in order to extract free water from the soil. In the
7767
example in Photo #2, the root system (rhizones) of the ferns are finding adequate water for growth in the
7768
building structure below the flooring.
7769
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 263
Return to TOP
7770
Plants are, in general, less efficient than fungi in their ability to extract moisture from the substrate. The
7771
presence of plants in Photo #2 indicates with high certainty that adequate moisture is also present to
7772
support the growth of fungi and bacteria in the flooring substructure. The designer should be aware that
7773
plant growth in or on buildings such as in living walls and green roofs implies that microorganisms are
7774
also present and growing in the same building component. These microorganisms potentially can
7775
degrade building components and in the case of extensive indoor vegetation can potentially degrade IAQ.
7776
7777
7778
Figure 2.6-B. Ferns are growing on the flooring of this room previously
7779
affected by a water disaster. The growth of green plants requires a water
7780
activity of greater than 0.95 and indicates that building infrastructure where
7781
plant root systems are located is moist enough to support the growth of
7782
fungi and bacteria that contribute to building biodeterioration.
7783
7784
References
7785
1. K. Botzenhart, K. Altenhoff and Th. Leithold, Molds in the Air of Greenhouse Home, Indoor Air
7786
1984, Vol. 3, 277-281 (1984).
7787
2. B. Flannigan, Approaches to Assessment of the Microbial Flora in Buildings, IAQ 92, ASHRAE,
7788
pp. 139-145 (1992).
7789

Return to TOP
3. B. Flannigan and J.D. Miller, Microbial Growth in Indoor Environments, Chapter 2-1 in:
7790
Microorganisms in Home and Indoor Work Environments, pp. 35-67, (2001).
7791
4. M.S. Graham, Code Compliance for Green Roof Systems, Tech Today, pp. 30, 2007.
7792
5. P. Kozak, J. Gallup, L. Cummins and S. Gillman, Currently Available Methods for Home Mold
7793
Surveys II, Ann. Allergy, 45, 167-176 (1980).
7794
6. H. Levin, Can House Plants Solve IAQ Problems, Indoor Air Bulletin (2) 1992.
7795
7. R. Sharp, 6 Things You Need to Know About Green
7796
Walls, http://www.bdcnetwork.com/article/CA6459410.html?q=ca6459410 (1974).
7797
8. F.W. Went, Reflections and Speculations, Annual Review of Plant Physiology 25, 1-26 (1974).
7798
9. Wikipedia, Living Walls, http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Living\_wall (2007).
7799
7800
7801
7802
7803
7804
7805
7806
7807
7808

Return to TOP
OBJECTIVE 3 – LIMIT ENTRY OF OUTDOOR CONTAMINANTS
7809
7810
7811
Key elements in the control of outdoor contaminants are to:
7812
7813
• Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outside Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
7814
• Strategy 3.2 – Control Entry of Radon
7815
• Strategy 3.3 – Control Intrusion of Vapors from Subsurface Contaminants
7816
• Strategy 3.4 – Provide Effective Track-off Systems at Building Entrances
7817
• Strategy 3.5 – Design and Build to Exclude Pests
7818
7819
7820
7821
See also Chapter 2 Integrated Design and Construction Process for Good IAQ and these closely related
7822
Strategies:
7823
7824
• Strategy 7.3 - Provide Particle Filtration & Gas Phase Air Cleaning Consistent with Project IAQ
7825
Objectives
7826
• Strategy 2.3 - Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
7827
• Strategy 4.5 - Control Legionella in Water Systems
7828
• Strategy 2.6 - Consider Impacts of Landscaping and Indoor Plants on Moisture and Contaminant
7829
Levels
7830
7831
7832
7833
7834
7835
7836
7837
[REMAINDER OF PAGE LEFT INTENTIALLY BLANK]
7838
7839
7840
7841
7842
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 266
Return to TOP
7843
Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outdoor Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
7844
7845
7846
Overview
7847
Outside air enters a building through its air intakes. In mechanically ventilated buildings, these air
7848
intakes are part of the HVAC system, and in naturally ventilated buildings these intakes can be either
7849
operable windows or other openings in the building’s envelope.
7850
7851
Research to date has shown that the quality of the outdoor air provided in a building greatly affects the
7852
quality of the indoor air. Therefore, besides evaluating the quality of the outdoor air in the area where a
7853
building is located, it is equally important to assess the presence of any local contaminant sources and
7854
design the location of the air intakes in such a way as to minimize re-entrainment of airborne pollutants
7855
emitted by these sources.
7856
7857
Mechanical codes (such as the IMC, IPC, UMC, and UPC) have some basic requirements for locations
7858
of building intakes (see Tables 1 and 3 for a list of ASHRAE and Model Code requirements In most
7859
cases these requirements are minimums and do not reflect good design practices. For example, there is a
7860
large disparity between the 2006 International and Uniform Mechanical Codes and ASHRAE Standard
7861
62.1-2007 on separation distance between cooling tower exhaust and building intakes. The 2006 IMC
7862
and UMC allow a vertical separation of only 5 feet between a cooling tower and a building intake
7863
whereas 62.1-2007 requires a 25-foot separation. The 5 foot separation is clearly inadequate and can
7864
create an unsafe environment for building occupants
7865
7866
Building intakes should be located as far as practically possible from strong contaminant sources, such
7867
as cooling towers. For example, minimum distance of 40 feet between cooling towers and building
7868
intakes has been specified and implemented in a 1 million square foot, 5-building office complex,
7869
exceeding the 25-foot recommendation listed in Table 5-1 of ASHRAE 62.1-2007, without any
7870
additional costs to the owner (Alevantis et al, 2002).
7871
7872
ASHRAE’s Recommendations
7873
ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 (Table 5.1) lists minimum separation distances between air intakes and
7874
specific contamination sources (see Table 1). These distances are the shortest “stretched string” distance
7875
measured from the closest point of the outlet opening to the closest point of the outdoor air intake or
7876
building opening. Although the list does not cover all possible sources, it does give the designer a
7877
guiding tool. Appendix F of Standard 62.1 (see Table 2) allows the designer to calculate distances from
7878
sources other than the ones listed in Table 5.1. The distances listed in 5.1 should be considered as
7879
design minimums.
7880
7881
At this time model codes have not adopted Table 5.1. Although as mentioned above Table 5.1 does not
7882
cover all possible sources, it does cover more sources and provides for greater separation distances as
7883
can be found for example by comparing Table 5.1 and the 2001 California Mechanical Code (see Table
7884
3 for a comparison table).
7885
7886
Besides separation distances, other parameters than need to be looked at during design include velocities
7887
and orientation of exhausts (or exhaust stacks). Chapter 44 of the ASHRAE Handbook – Applications
7888
(Building Air Intake and Exhaust Design) has a detailed discussion of the subject and with expanded
7889
guidance in the Section entitled “Exhaust-to-Intake Dilution Calculations” (ASHRAE, 2007).
7890
Furthermore, Chapter 16 of the Applications Handbook (Airflow Around Buildings) provides basic
7891
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 267
Return to TOP
information for evaluating wind airflow patterns and identifying problems caused by the effects of wind
7892
on intakes and exhausts.
7893
7894
Cooling Towers
7895
Outdoor air intakes should be located at least twenty five feet away and upwind from plume discharges
7896
of cooling towers, evaporative condensers and fluid coolers. In addition, outdoor air intakes should be
7897
located at least 15 feet away from intakes or basins of cooling towers, evaporative condensers and fluid
7898
coolers (ASHRAE, 2007).
7899
7900
Buildings designed with smaller separation distances or with outdoor air intakes downwind from cooling
7901
towers could increase the risk of re-entrainment of cooling tower exhaust which could include
7902
Legionnella bacteria (which could result in Legionnaires’ Disease) as well as pollutants emitted by the
7903
chemicals used to treat the cooling tower water (which could affect health when inhaled). See Topic
7904
4.5: Control of Legionella in Water Systems for more information on Legionnaires’ Disease
7905
7906
There is strong evidence both in the scientific literature as well as in the form of recommendations by
7907
expert organizations on separation distances between cooling towers and building air intakes.
7908
ASHRAE’s 62.1-2007 recommendation on separation distances of cooling towers is paralleled by the
7909
California Department of Health Services (CDHS) in a publication entitled Minimizing the Risk of
7910
Legionnaires' Disease in Public Buildings published in 1995 which also recommends a 25 ft separation
7911
distance between cooling towers and building air intakes. This recommendation is not limited to any
7912
specific building type.
7913
7914
Laboratory Fumes Hood Exhaust Stacks
7915
For distances between building air intakes and laboratory fume hood exhausts guidance provided by
7916
NFPA 45-2004 (NFPA, 2004) and ANSI/AIHA Z9.5-2003 (AIHA, 2003) should be followed. A
7917
minimum separation distance of 30 feet is recommended by ASHRAE (2001), but the maximum
7918
possible separation is a good practice (ASHRAE, 2001; DiBernardinis, 1993). CFD and/or wind tunnel
7919
analyses may be needed in these or similar applications, depending on the constituents in the exhaust
7920
airstream.
7921
7922
Other Sources Of Contamination Including Plumbing Vents
7923
All nearby potential odor or contaminant sources (such restaurant exhausts, emergency generators) and
7924
prevailing wind conditions need to be evaluated. Location of plumbing vents in relationship to outdoor
7925
air intakes in high-rise buildings may require additional analysis.
7926
7927
Most Model Codes require a 3 to 10 foot separation distance between terminations of vents carrying
7928
non-explosive or flammable vapors, fumes or dusts and building (other than healthcare) air intakes. In
7929
the case of plumbing vents, most codes require a 10 foot separation distance.
7930
7931
Although healthcare facilities are not covered in this Design Guide, we are describing their intake
7932
separation requirements for informational purposes only. The Guidelines for Design and Construction
7933
of Health Care Facilities require separation distances of 25 feet between building intakes and plumbing
7934
vents, exhaust outlets of ventilating systems, combustion equipment stacks, areas that may collect
7935
vehicular exhaust or other noxious fumes. However, these guidelines allow the 25 foot separation
7936
distance to be reduced to 10 feet if plumbing vents are terminated at a level above the top of the air
7937
intake. The 2007 California Mechanical Code for hospitals (Section 407.2.1) requires that: “outdoor air
7938
intakes be located at least 25 feet from exhaust outlets of ventilating systems, combustion equipment
7939
stacks,… cooling towers and areas that may collect vehicular exhaust or other noxious fumes. The
7940
bottom of outdoor air intakes shall be located as high as practical, but not less than 10 feet above
7941
ground level. If installed above roof, they shall be located 18 inches above the roof …”.
7942
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 268
Return to TOP
7943
Due to wind effects around buildings and multiple other local variables, establishing minimum
7944
separation distances for each source is impossible. Each design case must be evaluated based on local
7945
conditions and variables and the designer ultimately needs to exercise professional judgment. In some
7946
cases, advanced calculations and/or modeling may be required.
7947
7948
In certain applications sewage systems may be under slightly positive pressure resulting in excessive
7949
odors at the plumbing vent outlet. If the designer determines that the resulting separation distances are
7950
excessive and impractical, such as in the case of existing installations, some alternatives do exist as
7951
described below. It is important that the designer: (a) does not use the following alternatives in lieu of
7952
plumbing vents to the outside; (b) ensures that all code requirements are met; and (c) confirms with the
7953
local building authorities that these alternatives are acceptable. In addition, attention should be paid to
7954
ensuring that build up of potentially explosive sewer gases (e.g. methane) in the building’s plumbing
7955
system is avoided when implementing the following alternatives (e.g., some plumbing vents may have
7956
to remain unrestricted at all times to avoid built up of such gases). Such alternatives include:
7957
7958
o Air Admittance Valves (AAV). AAV are pressure-activated, one-way mechanical valves. The
7959
normally closed AAV valve opens when a plumbing fixture is operated, allowing air to enter and
7960
equalize the pressure in the plumbing system for proper drainage. When the water flow stops,
7961
gravity closes the valve thus preventing plumbing odors from escaping through the valve and
7962
maintaining trap seal.
7963
o Gas-Phase Filtration: A "tub" of carbon/potassium/alumina pellets blend has been used to cap
7964
sewer vent outlets. The deep bed of pelletized sorbent provides a pressure deterrent but will
7965
control the contaminants if the building vent pressure is sufficiently positive to force the air
7966
through the bed. The open bed allows the system to breathe when the vent is operating normally
7967
and venting to the negative (Burroughs, 2008)
7968
7969
Wind Tunnel Modeling, Computer Simulations, and Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)
7970
In cases where calculations show that concentrations of chemicals from nearby exhausts are likely to be
7971
close or exceed odor or health guidelines at an intake, a range of options exist to assist the designer in
7972
proper placement of the exhausts and intakes. Wind tunnel analyses with scale models, computer
7973
simulations, and CFD analyses are some of the tools available to a designer.
7974
7975
Special Considerations for Packaged HVAC Units
7976
In packaged HVAC units, the vent of the flue (see Figure 1) should be extended at least 10 inches or
7977
more above the top of the unit (see Figure 2) to minimize flue gas re-entrainment (ARTI, 2002). In
7978
units where the intake and exhausts are in close proximity (see Figure 3), dilution of building exhaust air
7979
in the economizer mode is significantly less than the dilution of flue gas in the heating mode.
7980
Researchers reported that in one study the minimum dilution ratio in the economizer mode of a
7981
packaged HVAC unit was 9, representing a maximum building-air exhaust re-entrainment of 11 percent;
7982
for the same unit the dilution ratio for a tall vertical flue vent had a minimum dilution ratio measured
7983
was 1284, corresponding to only 0.08 percent entrainment of flue gases (ARTI, 2002). For larger
7984
HVAC packaged units, extending the intake or exhaust should be considered in order to minimize re-
7985
entrainment of the exhaust air into the intake (see Figures 5, 6, 7)
7986
7987
Packaged HVAC units should be located so that their air intakes and exhausts are directed away from
7988
large obstructions. Short-term maximum re-entrainment rates for the above-mentioned packed HVAC
7989
unit (see Figure 4) ranged from 11 percent with a wall obstruction at 15 feet from and normal to the
7990
exhaust hood to 32 percent for a wall obstruction at 5 feet from and normal to the exhaust hood (ARTI,
7991
2005).
7992
7993
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 269
Return to TOP
Table 3.1-A: Standard 62.1 TABLE 5-1 Air Intake Minimum Separation Distance Listed distances
7994
are the shortest “stretched string” distance measured from the closest point of the outlet opening to the
7995
closest point of the outdoor air intake or building opening
7996
Object
Minimum Distance, ft (m)

15 (5)

30 (10)
Vents, chimneys, and flues from combustion appliances and equipment (Note 4)
15 (5)
Garage entry, automobile loading area, or drive-in queue (Note 5)
15 (5)
Truck loading area or dock, bus parking/idling area (Note 5)
25 (7.5)
Driveway, street, or parking place (Note 5)
5 (1.5)
Thoroughfare with high traffic volume
25 (7.5)
Roof, landscaped grade, or other surface directly below intake (Notes 6 and 7)
1 (0.30)
Garbage storage/pick-up area, dumpsters
15 (5)
Cooling tower intake or basin
15 (5)
Cooling tower exhaust
25 (7.5)
Note 1: Significantly contaminated exhaust is exhaust air with significant contaminant concentration, significant sensory-irritation
intensity, or offensive odor.
Note 2: Laboratory fume hood exhaust air outlets shall be in compliance with NFPA 45-19913and ANSI/AIHA Z9.5-1992.4
Note 3: Noxious or dangerous exhaust is exhaust air with highly objectionable fumes or gases and/or exhaust air with potentially
dangerous particles, bioaerosols, or gases at concentrations high enough to be considered harmful. Information on separation criteria for
industrial environments can be found in the ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual5 and in the ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC
Applications. 6
Note 4: Shorter separation distances are permitted when determined in accordance with (a) Chapter 7 of ANSI Z223.1/NFPA 54-20027
for fuel gas burning appliances and equipment; (b) Chapter 6 of NFPA 31-20018for oil burning appliances and equipment, or (c) Chapter
7 of NFPA 211-20039 for other combustion appliances and equipment.
Note 5: Distance measured to closest place that vehicle exhaust is likely to be located.
Note 6: No minimum separation distance applies to surfaces that are sloped more than 45 degrees from horizontal or that are less than 1
in. (3 cm) wide.
Note 7: Where snow accumulation is expected, distance listed shall be increased by the expected average snow depth.
7997
7998
7999
Standard 62.1 – Appendix F
8000
8001
INFORMATIVE APPENDIX F SEPARATION OF EXHAUST OUTLETS AND
8002
OUTDOOR AIR INTAKES
8003
8004
F1 General. Exhaust air and vent outlets as defined in Table 5-1 shall be located no
8005
closer to outdoor air intakes, and operable windows, skylights, and doors, both those on
8006
the subject property and those on adjacent properties, than the minimum separation
8007
distance L specified in this section. The distance L is defined as the shortest “stretched
8008
string” distance measured from the closest point of the outlet opening to the closest
8009
point of the outdoor air intake opening or operable window, skylight, or door opening,
8010
along a trajectory as if a string were stretched between them.
8011
8012
F2 Application. Exhaust outlets and outdoor air intakes or other openings shall be
8013
separated in accordance with the following.
8014
8015
Exception: Laboratory fume hood exhaust air outlets shall be in compliance with
8016
NFPA 45-1991 and ANSI/AIHA Z9.5-1992.
8017
8018
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 270
Return to TOP
F2.1 Outdoor Air Intakes. Minimum separation distance between exhaust air/vent
8019
outlets as defined in Table 5-1 and outdoor air intakes to mechanical ventilation systems
8020
or operable windows, skylights, and doors that are required as part of natural ventilation
8021
systems shall be equal to distance L determined in accordance with Section F3.
8022
8023
Exception: Separation distances do not apply when exhaust and outdoor air intake
8024
systems do not operate simultaneously.
8025
8026
F2.2 Other Building Openings. Minimum separation distance between building
8027
exhaust air/vent outlets as defined in Table 5-1 and operable openings to occupiable
8028
spaces shall be half of the distance L determined in accordance with Section F3.
8029
Minimum separation distance between high odor intensity or noxious or dangerous
8030
exhaust air/vent outlets and operable openings to occupiable spaces shall be equal to the
8031
distance L determined in accordance with Section F3.
8032
8033
F2.3 Additional Limitations for Noxious or Dangerous Air. Minimum separation
8034
distance between exhausts located less than 65 ft (20 m) vertically below outdoor air
8035
intakes or operable windows and doors shall be equal to a horizontal separation only as
8036
determined in accordance with Section F3; no credit may be taken for any vertical
8037
separation.
8038
8039
F2.4 Equipment Wells. Exhaust air outlets that terminate in an equipment well that
8040
also encloses an outdoor air intake shall meet the separation requirements of this section
8041
and, in addition, shall either
8042
8043
a. terminate at or above the highest enclosing wall and discharge air upward at a
8044
velocity exceeding 1000 fpm (5 m/s) or
8045
b. terminate 3 ft (1 m) above the highest enclosing wall (with no minimum velocity).
8046
8047
Exception: Low contaminant or intensity air.
8048
8049
For the purpose of this section, an equipment well is an area (typically on the roof)
8050
enclosed on three or four sides by walls that are less than 75% free area, and the lesser
8051
of the length and width of the enclosure is less than three times the average height of the
8052
walls. The free area of the wall is the ratio of area of the openings through the wall,
8053
such as openings between louver blades and undercuts, divided by the gross area (length
8054
times height) of the wall.
8055
8056
F2.5 Property Lines. Minimum separation distance between exhaust air/vent outlets
8057
and property lines shall be half of the distance L determined in accordance with Section
8058
F3. For significant contaminant or odor intensity exhaust air, where the property line
8059
abuts a street or other public way, no minimum separation is required if exhaust
8060
termination is 10 ft
8061
(3 m) above grade.
8062
8063
F3 Determining Distance L. Separation distance L shall be determined using any of the
8064
following approaches:
8065
8066
a. Use the values of L in Table F-1.
8067
b. Calculate L in accordance with Equation F-1 (a or b).
8068
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 271
Return to TOP
c. Determine L using any calculation or test procedure approved by the authority having
8069
jurisdiction that shows that the proposed design will result in equivalent or greater
8070
dilution factors than those specified in Table F-2.
8071
8072
L = 0.09 · √Q · (√DF – U/400) in feet (IP) (F-1a)
8073
L = 0.04 · √Q · (√DF – U/2) in meters (SI) (F-1b)
8074
8075
where
8076
8077
Q = exhaust air volume, cfm (L/s). For gravity vents, such as plumbing vents, use an
8078
exhaust rate of 150 cfm (75 L/s). For flue vents from fuel-burning appliances, assume a
8079
value of 250 cfm per million Btu/h (0.43 L/s per Kw) of combustion input (or obtain
8080
actual rates from the combustion appliance manufacturer.
8081
8082
DF = dilution factor, which is the ratio of outside air to entrained exhaust air in the
8083
outside air intake. The minimum dilution factor shall be determined as a function of
8084
exhaust air class in Table F-2:
8085
8086
8087
TABLE F-1 Minimum Separation Distance, L, in ft (m)
8088
Significant
Contaminant
or Odor
Intensity
Noxious
or
Dangerous
Particles
15 (5)
30 (10)
8089
TABLE F-2 Minimum Dilution Factors
8090
Exhaust
Air Class
Dilution
Factor
(DF)
Significant
contaminant
or odor
intensity
15
Noxious or

particles
50\*

8091
8092
For exhaust air composed of more than one class of air, the dilution factor shall be
8093
determined by averaging the dilution factors by the volume fraction of each class:
8094
8095
DF = Σ(DFi · Qi )/ ΣQi
8096
8097
where
8098
8099
DFi = dilution factor from Table F-2 for class i air and Qi is the volumetric flow rate of
8100
class i air in the exhaust airstream.
8101
8102
U = exhaust air discharge velocity, fpm (m/s). As shown in Figure F.1, U shall have a
8103
positive value when the exhaust is directed away from the outside air intake at an angle
8104
that is greater than 45° from the direction of a line drawn from the closest exhaust point
8105
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 272
Return to TOP
the edge of the intake; U shall have a negative value when the exhaust is directed
8106
toward the intake bounded by lines drawn from the closest exhaust point the edge of the
8107
intake; and U shall be set to zero for other exhaust air directions regardless of actual
8108
velocity. U shall be set to 0 in Equation F-1 for vents from gravity (atmospheric) fuel-
8109
fired appliances, plumbing vents, and other nonpowered exhausts, or if the exhaust
8110
discharge is covered by a cap or other device that dissipates the exhaust airstream. For
8111
hot gas exhausts such as combustion products, an effective additional 500 fpm (2.5 m/s)
8112
upward velocity shall be added to the actual discharge velocity if the exhaust stream is
8113
aimed directly upward and unimpeded by devices such as flue caps or louvers.
8114
(Source: Standard 62.1-2007; Appendix F)

Note 1: Significantly contaminated exhaust is exhaust air with significant contaminant concentration, significant sensory-irritation intensity, or
offensive odor.
Note 2: Laboratory fume hood exhaust air outlets shall be in compliance with NFPA 45-19913and ANSI/AIHA Z9.5-1992.4
Note 3: Noxious or dangerous exhaust is exhaust air with highly objectionable fumes or gases and/or exhaust air with potentially dangerous particles,
bioaerosols, or gases at concentrations high enough to be considered harmful. Information on separation criteria for industrial environments can be
found in the ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual5 and in the ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC
Applications. 6
Note 4: Shorter separation distances are permitted when determined in accordance with (a) Chapter 7 of ANSI Z223.1/NFPA 54-20027 for fuel gas
burning appliances and equipment; (b) Chapter 6 of NFPA 31-20018for oil burning appliances and equipment, or (c) Chapter 7 of NFPA 211-20039
for other combustion appliances and equipment.
Note 5: Distance measured to closest place that vehicle exhaust is likely to be located.
Note 6: No minimum separation distance applies to surfaces that are sloped more than 45 degrees from horizontal or that are less than 1 in. (3 cm)
wide.
Note 7: Where snow accumulation is expected, distance listed shall be increased by the expected average snow depth.

8277
Strategy 3.2 – Reduce Entry of Radon
8278
8279
8280
Assessment
8281
Radon is a radioactive gas formed from the decay of uranium in rock, soil and groundwater. Exposure
8282
to radon decay products in residences is the second leading cause of lung cancer in the U.S. after
8283
cigarette smoking, and is responsible for about 10 to 14% of lung cancer deaths (NAS 1999). The
8284
additional cancer risk due to exposures in non-residential buildings has not been quantified. The World
8285
Health Organization (WHO) estimates that radon causes 6 to 15% of all lung cancers globally
8286
(reference).
8287
8288
Action Levels and Exposure Limits
8289
The average outdoor level of radon in the U.S. is 0.4 picocuries per liter (pCi/L) (Hopper et al. 1991).
8290
Any indoor exposure above outdoor background levels carries some increased risk of lung cancer.24
8291
8292
The U.S. Environmental Protection
8293
Agency (USEPA) recommends that
8294
action be taken to reduce radon
8295
levels in schools and homes where
8296
levels are 4 pCi/L or higher. Over
8297
19% of schools in the U.S. have at
8298
least one ground contact classroom
8299
with a level of 4 pCi/L or more
8300
(Table ?-1).
8301
8302
The U.S. Occupational Safety and
8303
Health Administration (OSHA) has
8304
set a maximum permissible level
8305
for radon in workplaces of 30 pCi/L
8306
for any 40 hours in a consecutive 7
8307
day period (29 CFR 1910.1096
8308
(c)(1) and Table 1 of Appendix B to
8309
10 CFR Part 20). However, in
8310
general OSHA recommends that
8311
exposures to any contaminant be
8312
“as low as reasonably achievable.”
8313
About 5% of measurements taken
8314
in U.S. federal workplaces were
8315
above 4 pCi/L, but only 0.1% were
8316
above 20 pCi/L.25
8317
The International Commission on
8318
Radiological Protection (1990) recommends that national authorities set action levels for workplaces in
8319
24 NAS 1999. This is based on review of cellular and molecular evidence, including “good evidence that a single alpha
particle can cause major genomic changes in a cell… [and] convincing evidence that most cancers are of monoclonal origin,
that is, they originate from damage to a single cell.”
25 Because the studies in schools and federal buildings were mostly or entirely based on measurements integrated over days
or months, they may overestimate exposures. Studies which have taken continuous measurements have found that radon
concentrations tend to be lower during occupied hours when the HVAC system is on than at night (Denman et al. 2004,
Durcik et al. 1997, Marley 2000, Marley et al. 1999, Tokonami et al. 2003). On the other hand, if the HVAC system
depressurizes part or all of the building relative to the subslab, it can pull radon into the building (Leovic et al. 1990).

the range of 500 to 1500 Bq/m3 (~13.5 to 40.5 pCi/L) and the International Atomic Energy Agency
8320
recommends an action level of 1000 Bq/m3 (~27 pCi/L). A number of European Union countries have
8321
set enforced action levels for both new and existing workplaces of 400 Bq/m3 (~10.8 pCi/L) and one
8322
(Sweden) has set an enforced action level for new workplaces of 200 Bq/m3 (~5.4 pCi/L). Several EU
8323
countries have set advisory action levels at 200 Bq/m3 for new workplaces (Akerblom 1999).
8324
8325
Several factors may cause a given radon concentration in schools or workplaces to have a different
8326
impact than the same concentration at home. On a lifetime average basis people in the U.S. spend only
8327
18% of their time in all indoor places other than residences (Klepeis et al. 2001), so a workplace
8328
concentration of the same magnitude as a home concentration will result in only about one-fourth the
8329
cumulative exposure, all other things being equal. On the other hand, the risk per unit exposure is
8330
estimated to be somewhat higher for working age people (roughly 20 to late 50s) than for younger or
8331
older people (USEPA 2003). Finally, the equilibrium factor and the fraction of radon progeny that are
8332
unattached may be different depending on aerosol generation rates, air circulation rates, filtration levels
8333
or other factors. Evidence suggests that workplaces with high concentrations of aerosols (from
8334
production processes such as wood joinery or baking, or from environmental tobacco smoke) have
8335
higher equilibrium factors but lower unattached fractions (Reichelt et al. 2000, Streil, Yu et al. 1998).
8336
The former would tend to increase the effective dose for a given radon concentration, while the latter
8337
would tend to decrease it. OTA (1995) used USEPA’s (1993) school survey data to estimate that
8338
exposure of schoolchildren to radon in schools was responsible for only about 64 additional deaths/year
8339
and that “Only in what appear to be exceptional circumstances do in-school exposures make significant
8340
contributions to lifelong radon exposures…” Based on this, when (if at all) should we recommend
8341
radon control construction techniques??)
8342
8343
Regional Radon Potential
8344
The most common way radon enters buildings is in soil gas drawn in through joints, cracks or
8345
penetrations when the building is at negative pressure relative to the surrounding soil. Negative building
8346
pressures occur due to stack effect, wind effect or an excess of mechanical exhaust relative to outdoor
8347
air supply (ASHRAE 2005).
8348
8349
USEPA has developed a map of radon zones to rank the potential for elevated radon levels in the
8350
U.S.(Figure ?-1), and recommends that architects and engineers use this map to help determine where
8351
radon control techniques might be needed in schools and other large buildings.
8352
8353
8354
Figure 3.2-B USEPA map of radon zones. Zone 1 counties have a predicted average
8355
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 282
Return to TOP
indoor radon screening level greater than 4 pCi/L and have the highest potential for
8356
elevated indoor radon levels. Zone 2 counties have a predicted average indoor radon
8357
screening level between 2 and 4 pCi/L and have moderate potential for elevated indoor
8358
radon levels. Zone 3 counties have a predicted average indoor radon screening level of
8359
less than 2 pCi/L and have low potential for elevated indoor radon levels. Larger scale
8360
maps of individual states are available at http://www.epa.gov/radon/zonemap.html
8361
8362
There is no way to determine with certainty prior to construction whether radon-resistant construction is
8363
needed for a given site. While the radon potential does exhibit regional variations related to geology, it
8364
can also vary locally by several orders of magnitude due to local variations in geology and soils. To
8365
illustrate this point, while classrooms in areas with high radon potential are more likely to have radon
8366
levels over 4 pCi/L (Table 3.2-B), almost 60% of all classrooms with levels over 4 pCi/L are in areas
8367
with medium or low radon potential (USEPA 1993).
8368
8369
The incremental cost of radon resistant construction is fairly low, especially in areas where subslab
8370
aggregate and vapor retarders are routinely used for other reasons. In addition, the cost of radon
8371
resistant new construction is substantially less than the cost to retrofit a radon mitigation system after
8372
construction.
8373
8374
In areas where elevated radon levels are more common (Zone 1 and other radon-prone areas that have
8375
been identified in Zones 2 and 3), USEPA (no date, a) recommends that new schools incorporate:
8376
• A roughed in active soil depressurization system (including a sub-slab aggregate and vapor barrier
8377
beneath all slabs, one centrally located suction pit per 100,000 sq.ft. of ground-contact floor area, a
8378
vent pipe from each pit to the roof and a junction box on the roof next to the vent pipe)
8379
• Sealing of potential radon entry routes
8380
• Post-construction radon measurements
8381
8382
With the ASD system roughed in, the suction fan(s) and controls can be added later at low cost if
8383
measured radon levels exceed recommended action levels or exposure limits.
8384
8385
Local Radon Potential
8386
Regional, state or local officials may be able to provide information about radon levels in buildings near
8387
the project site that may help the owner and design team decide whether to implement radon control
8388
techniques. In the U.S. the USEPA maintains a list of state and regional indoor environments contacts
8389
(http://www.epa.gov/iaq/whereyoulive.html).
8390
8391
Radon in Drinking Water and Building Materials
8392
In some areas, groundwater contains high levels of radon. If well water is supplied directly to a building,
8393
radon can be released into the indoor air when the water is used. In homes, water with a radon level
8394
10,000 pCi/L is estimated to contribute about 1 pCi/L to indoor air levels. In the U.S., many of the
8395
public water supplies with elevated concentrations of radon serve communities of less than 1000 people.
8396
Elevated concentrations occur primarily in New England states, Appalachian states, Rocky Mountain
8397
states and small areas in the Southwest and the Great Plains (NAS 1999). Radon in well water is not
8398
believed to be a common source of elevated indoor radon levels in commercial and institutional
8399
buildings and is not covered further in this guide.
8400
8401
In rare instances, radon may emanate from building materials such as aggregate in concrete masonry
8402
units or fill, if such materials contain radium-rich wastes. Radon in building materials is believed to be
8403
an extremely rare of elevated indoor radon levels in commercial and institutional buildings and is not
8404
covered further in this guide.
8405
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 283
Return to TOP
8406
Owner’s Risk Tolerance
8407
The design team should discuss action levels, exposure limits and percentages of buildings with elevated
8408
radon levels with the owner so that the owner can make an informed decision about the use of radon
8409
resistant construction techniques. Decisions made should be documented in the IAQ Basis of Design.
8410
8411
Synergies and Conflicts
8412
Many radon control techniques are relevant to other IAQ issues as shown in the following table (Table
8413
3.2-A).
8414
