Notes:
1. Although the general features of an active soil depressurization system are the same for radon and
vapors from site contaminants, specific requirements may be different. For a site where both vapor
intrusion and radon are concerns, the more stringent requirements should be followed.
2. Some situations require neutral or negative building pressurization. In these circumstances the active
soil depressurization system must maintain the sub-slab at a lower pressure than the adjacent neutral or
negative building areas.
8417
Active Soil Depressurization
8418
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 284
Return to TOP
Active soil depressurization (ASD)(Figure 3.2-C) is the most effective, reliable and widely applied
8419
radon reduction technique (ASTM 2007). An ASD system uses a suction fan (or fans) to create a zone
8420
of negative pressure below the slab, drawing air from the building into the soil around the foundation.
8421
This zone of negative pressure prevents radon-laden soil gas from flowing into the building.
8422
Measurements in residences have shown that ASD systems reduce indoor radon concentrations by up to
8423
99% (EPA reference).
8424
8425
(Should use of a credentialed individual (National Environmental Health Association-National Radon
8426
Proficiency Program or National Radon Safety Board) to design and install radon mitigation systems be
8427
recommended?)
8428
8429
8430
8431
Figure 3.2-C Schematic drawing of active soil depressurization (ASD) system and sealing of radon
8432
entry routes. The zone of negative pressure below the slab causes air to flow from the building
8433
into the sub-slab area, preventing flow of soil gas into the building. The radon-laden soil gas
8434
is drawn up the vent pipe and exhausted outdoors, where it is quickly diluted to ambient levels.
8435
(Source: USEPA 1994)
8436
8437
Design of Load-Bearing Elements to Facilitate ASD
8438
Each subslab area isolated by subslab walls requires a suction pit, suction vent pipe and suction fan.
8439
Minimizing barriers to sub-slab air flow therefore reduces the cost of ASD. USEPA has demonstrated
8440
that a single suction pit, vent pipe and fan can depressurize the entire subslab of a large building if
8441
barriers to subslab air flow are minimized by using one of the following foundation designs:
8442
• Post and beam construction (Figure 3.2-D)
8443
• Thickened slab footings with aggregate run continuously below the footings (Figure 3.2-E)
8444
• Conventional spread footings with interruptions such that the entire subslab is interconnected
8445
(Figure 3.2-F).
8446
8447
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 285
Return to TOP
Figure 3.2-D
Option 1: Post-and-beam
construction.
Outside walls and posts are load
bearing. Interior walls are non-
load bearing and do not extend
below slab.
Entire sub-slab is
interconnected and only one
suction pit and suction fan are
required.
Source: USEPA 1994.
8448
8449
Figure 3.2-E.
Option 2. Interior weight-bearing
walls on thickened slab footings
with aggregate run continuously
below the footings.
Entire sub-slab is interconnected
and only one suction pit and
suction fan are required.
Source: USEPA 1994.
8450
8451
Figure 3.2-F
Option 3: Interior weight-
bearing walls extending below
aggregate layer to
conventional spread footings,
but interrupted at corridor.
Entire sub-slab is
interconnected and only one
suction pit and suction fan are
required.
Source: USEPA 1994.
8452
Boundary of
depressurization zone
Boundary of
depressurization zone
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 286
Return to TOP
If conventional spread footings are used below all walls, many separate subslab zones will be created
8453
and many suction pits/vent pipes/suction fans will be needed (Figure 3.2-G). If the stem walls (frost
8454
walls) are made of concrete masonry, sufficient subslab communication can be provided by setting
8455
every other CMU in the second course below the slab on its side (Figure 3.2-H).
8456
8457
Figure 3.2-G Undesirable options. Interior weight-bearing walls extend below aggregate layer to
8458
conventional spread footings, and are continuous so that subslab aggregate is divided into separate zones.
8459
Multiple radon suction pits and suction fans are required. Source: USEPA 1994.
8460
8461
Boundaries of three
separate
depressurization
zones
Boundaries of nine
separate
depressurization
zones
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 287
Return to TOP
Figure 3.2-H. Tests by USEPA have demonstrated that turning every other CMU
8462
on Its side in the course below the slab-level course creates sufficient subslab
8463
communication to enable the pressure field to extend under the entire slab, allowing
8464
use of a single suction pit, vent pipe and suction fan (Source: USEPA 1994).
8465
8466
Sub-Slab Aggregate
8467
ASD systems include a gas-permeable layer to permit the radon exhaust fan to draw a negative pressure
8468
over as large a sub-slab area as possible. The following design criteria are recommended by USEPA
8469
(1994):
8470
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 288
Return to TOP
• 4 to 6 inches of ASTM C-33-03 size #5 aggregate (½ to 1 in. diameter with less than 10% passing
8471
through a ½ in. sieve and free void space of approximately 50%)26
8472
• Where the aggregate is placed over material with a high fine content, and compaction of the
8473
aggregate is required for code reasons, provide a geotextile fabric27 below the aggregate to
8474
exclude fines. (can we provide recommended properties?)
8475
• Provide a vapor retarder over the aggregate below the slab to keep wet concrete from filling voids
8476
in the aggregate layer. 28 The vapor retarder will also serve to slow the entry of radon through
8477
cracks in the slab that may develop later (ASTM 2007)
8478
8479
According to USEPA (1994), these design criteria will allow a single radon suction pit and suction fan
8480
to create a zone of negative pressure extending over 100,000 ft2 of ground contact area, if all other
8481
recommendations are followed.
8482
8483
If crushed aggregate is not readily available or is very expensive, it may be possible to use drainage
8484
mats29 in place of aggregate as a permeable subslab layer, but such application has not be tested for
8485
radon control in commercial or institutional buildings. ASTM 2007 provides further information on
8486
drainage mats used in residential radon mitigation systems. They note that these systems, when installed
8487
according to the manufacturer’s recommendations, have an order of magnitude less void space volume
8488
with does a layer of crushed aggregate. They further note that radon reduction systems using these mats
8489
do not always reduce indoor radon concentrations below the USEPA action level. The mats described
8490
there are installed in strips around the perimeter of the foundation, with a few additional strips parallel to
8491
the short side of the foundation footprint, thus underlying about one-fifth of the sub-slab area. Sheet
8492
drainage mats are available for use below commercial building slabs and, if used under the entire
8493
foundation, could provide a void space volume much closer to that of aggregate.
8494
8495
Assure that horizontal runs of utility pipes and conduits (water, sewer, electric, telecommuncations, etc.)
8496
are placed below the aggregate layer or above the slab, so that they do not form barriers or restrictions to
8497
air flow (see ASTM 2007).
8498
8499
Suction Pit
8500
The key feature of the radon suction pit is the large void space in which the vent pipe terminates.30 The
8501
following design criteria are recommended:
8502
• Provide a suction pit for each area isolated by subslab walls.
8503
26 In the U.S., subslab aggregate is frequently required by code. Even where not required, it is usually included in designs to
provide a drainage bed for moisture and a stable, level surface on which to pour the slab. (more specific info on code
requirements for vapor retarders?)
27 A geotextile is a permeable geosynthetic fabric used with foundations, soils, aggregates, etc. to achieve various engineering
objectives. A common use of geotextiles is to separate two materials with different particle size distribution (e.g., aggregate
and underlying soil) to prevent undesirable mixing.
28 A vapor retarder is commonly installed under the slab in many areas of the country to reduce liquid water penetration and
vapor diffusion, so does not necessarily add costs related to radon control.
29 Drainage mats are often made of geocomposites, a type of geosynthetic material. The geocomposites used for this
application are sheets or strips having a core with a very high void area (geonet or other) and a layer of geotextile attached to
one or both sides. The core has a high hydraulic transmissivity (and soil gas transmissivity) within the manufactured plane,
even under compressive stress. The geotextile is permeable to water (and soil gas), allowing it to flow into the core, but
serves as a separator and filter to exclude soil particles from the core. For further information see www.geosynthetic-
institute.org/, http://www.geosyntheticssociety.org/indexigs.htm, or http://www.infogeos.com/portal.php?setlang=2. See
http://www.arcat.com/divs/sec/sec02620.shtml for a list of some manaufacturers of architectural subdrainage. (Masterspec
95. Masterspec 04 version does not list any manufacturers)
30 Some designers have used horizontal perforated pipe connected directly to the vent pipe for collection of subslab soil gases.
The suction pit recommended here is lower cost and equally effective if all design recommendations are followed.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 289
Return to TOP
• One suction pit/vent pipe/fan should be able to depressurize at least 100,000 ft2 of continuous
8504
(non-isolated) ground contact area, if all recommendations regarding aggregate and sealing are
8505
followed.31
8506
• For large buildings, provide an interface between the void space and the aggregate of about 7 ft2
8507
(about 30 times the cross-sectional area of the vent pipe) (Figure 3.2-I & 3.2-J). For smaller
8508
buildings, a smaller suction pit is sufficient (Figure 3.2-K).
8509
8510
Follow appropriate structural design criteria for reinforced concrete poured over the radon pit.
8511
8512
8513
Figure 3.2-I. Suction pit design providing 7 sq.ft. of void – aggregate interface.
8514
3 ft x 3 ft x 12 in. suction pit with vertical sides. (Source: USEPA 1994).
8515
8516
31 The maximum area that can successfully be depressurized with one suction fan is not known. In at least some cases a
single fan has been used successfully to depressurize areas as large as 480,000 ft2.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 290
Return to TOP
8517
8518
Figure 3.2-J. Alternative suction pit design providing 7 sq.ft. of void – aggregate
8519
interface. 4 ft. x 4 ft. x 8 in. deep pit with sloped sides (Source: USEPA 1994).
8520
8521
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 291
Return to TOP
8522
8523
Figure 3.2-K. Smaller suction pit suitable for smaller buildings. Based on USEPA tests,
8524
an 8 in. deep pit with 6 in. vent pipe will exhaust at least 20,000 sq.ft., and a 6 in. deep pit
8525
with a 4 in. vent pipe and fan will exhaust about 10,000 sq. ft. as long as the vent pipe run is
8526
less than about 20 ft. and all other recommendations are followed. (Source: USEPA 2004).
8527
8528
Radon Vent Pipe
8529
Provide a radon vent pipe from each suction pit to outdoors. The following design criteria are
8530
recommended:
8531
• Use 6 inch diameter vent pipe to provide sufficient airflow to depressurize large ground contact
8532
areas without sealing expansion joints.
8533
• Run vent pipe horizontally below the slab from the centrally located suction pit to a convenient
8534
riser location (Figure 3.2-L). Pitch vent pipe at least 1/8 inch per foot toward suction pit to assure
8535
that any condensed moisture will drain back to the pit. (ASTM 2007 has higher pitches depending
8536
on pipe size and anticipated air flows. 1.5”/ft for 3” pipe, 3/8” per ft for 4” pipe)
8537
• Run vent riser vertically to vent fan above roofline. (Need to provide alternatives where this is not
8538
practicable. See for example ASTM 2007)
8539
• Terminate vent above roof, in a manner consistent with local code provisions for discharge of
8540
laboratory fume hood exhaust or other rooftop exhaust venting toxic fumes. For examples of
8541
suitable discharge configurations see: Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended
8542
Practices, 25th edition (ACGIH 2004) or ASHRAE Handbook of Applications 2007, chapter 44.
8543
(Assure that radon fan can generate sufficient static pressure to overcome pressure drop of
8544
termination).
8545
8546
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 292
Return to TOP
8547
8548
Figure 3.2-L Detail of radon vent pipe roof penetration. (USEPA 1994).
8549
8550
• The discharge can contain extremely high levels of gaseous contaminants, so terminate the vent at
8551
a height sufficient that discharge does not re-enter the building. If this configuration is not
8552
possible, choose a configuration that provides at least a 1,000 to 1 dilution ratio to the nearest air
8553
intake or operable window, calculated following the procedures in ASHRAE Handbook of
8554
Applications 2007, chapter 44. (ASTM 2007 recommends 10 ft or more away from any window,
8555
door or other opening, if the discharge point is not at least 2 ft above the top of such opening).
8556
• Seal openings between vent pipe and floor slab with a high adhesive sealant such as polyurethane.
8557
• Seal all piping joints.
8558
• Seal vent pipe penetration through roof (Figure 3.2-L).
8559
• Comply with all code requirements, including but not limited to:
8560
o piping material and fire stop details where vent penetrates fire rated wall, ceiling deck or
8561
floor deck
8562
o piping material in return plenums above suspended ceilings.
8563
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 293
Return to TOP
• Provide labeling of all radon system components to ensure that future owners or operators do not
8564
remove or defeat the system
8565
o Provide labeling of exposed vent pipe at least every 10 ft. along entire pipe run.
8566
o Provide labeling of vent termination, including statement regarding minimum distance to
8567
air intake
8568
o (ASTM 2007 requires pipe labels, membrane inspection labels, radon system labels,
8569
sump cover inspection labels, and radon maintenance provider identification labels.
8570
Some of these ideas should be added here.)
8571
8572
Sub-slab Radon Vent System:
May contain hazardous levels of radon
8573
Sub-slab vent stack may contain high levels of radon: Do
not place air intake within 25 ft.
Figure 3.2-M Suggested labeling for radon vent pipe (top) and radon vent termination (bottom).
8574
Consult local codes to determine specific minimum distance to air intake.
8575
8576
Suction Fan
8577
Provide a suction fan for each vent pipe.32 The following design criteria are recommended:
8578
• Use fan(s) manufactured specifically for outdoor installation and continuous duty in radon control
8579
systems. Tubular in-line centrifugal fans are typically used. 33
8580
• For a subslab depressurization zone on the order of 50,000 to 100,000 ft2, provide a fan rated for
8581
500 to 600 cfm at 0 in. static pressure. (This DP rating seems inadequate. ASTM 2007 calls for
8582
75 cfm at 0.75 in. wc. Lower is allowed as long as the fan maintains -0.02” wc in all parts of the
8583
gas-permeable layer. Also, consider whether higher pressure may be required for highrise
8584
buildings in cold climates where first floor pressures can be quite negative in cold weather when
8585
supply fans are off.)
8586
• Locate fan above roof. Pipe downstream of fan is under positive pressure and leaks could allow
8587
radon to enter the building. (Provide another alternative for mid- and high-rise buildings)
8588
• Connect fan in vent pipe with watertight flexible connectors.
8589
• Provide waterproof electrical service switch at fan as required by code.
8590
• Provide a warning device to alert the building operator if the radon exhaust system is not operating
8591
properly.
8592
o Warning system should have an electronic pressure sensor that activates a warning light or
8593
other alarm when the system pressure drops below the nominal operating suction pressure.
8594
This value must be determined at system start-up by determining the suction required to
8595
maintain the desired negative pressure everywhere under the slab (What allowance is
8596
32 Passive systems (without a fan) are sometimes effective in single family residential construction, but are not recommended
for commercial and institutional buildings because negative pressures from various exhaust systems, insufficient building
pressurization, stack effect due to greater building heights, and other factors are more likely to overcome a passive system. In
addition, passive sytems would require a greater number of suction pits and vent pipes to be effective and would be more
expensive than an ASD system.
33 Some manufacturers providing fans for use in radon mitigation systems include Fantech, RadonAway, KTA, AMG.
(Prefer not to list manuf but no other easy way to make it easy for users of the guide to locate product?)
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 294
Return to TOP
appropriate for variations in pressure at grade level due to stack/wind effects in commercial
8597
buildings?).
8598
o (ASTM 2007 suggests that an acceptable range of normal operating suction pressures is +/-
8599
20% of the initial nominal reading at system startup.)
8600
o Warning light or alarm should be installed in an area frequently visited by the building operator,
8601
facilities manager or other responsible person and should be clearly labeled. One option is to
8602
connect the pressure sensor to the building automation system (BAS) and generate the alarm
8603
via the BAS.
8604
8605
If there is a significant likelihood that the building will not have elevated radon levels, the venting can
8606
be capped at roof level and the fan installed after radon testing if determined necessary.
8607
8608
Sub-Membrane Depressurization of Crawl Spaces
8609
The most effective radon control technique for crawl spaces is submembrane depressurization (SMD)
8610
(USEPA 1988, Pyle & Leovic 1991) (Figure 3.2-N).34
8611
8612
8613
Figure 3.2-N. Schematic drawing of submembrane depressurization in crawl space (USEPA 1994).
8614
8615
The following design criteria are recommended by EPA (1994) for schools and other large buildings:
8616
• If soil in crawl space is not adequately permeable, provide a layer of permeable material or a
8617
perforated pipe network above the crawl space floor.
8618
• Remove large rocks, broken CMUs and other obstructions.
8619
• Provide 6 mil or thicker polyethylene sheeting as a vapor retarder and as the upper surface of a
8620
“plenum” above the crawl space floor. Overlap adjacent sheets at least one foot. Seal seams in
8621
the area near the suction point, using a sealant recommended by the manufacturer. This will
8622
increase the area of the zone that can be effectively depressurized. Where sheeting goes around
8623
support piers, seal sheeting to piers.
8624
• Provide suction pit, vent pipe and suction fan as for ASD.
8625
34 The crawl space itself can be depressurized, but it is difficult to effectively isolate it from the occupied space above.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 295
Return to TOP
• If the crawl space will be accessed periodically by maintenance staff or tradespeople, provide pads
8626
or other protective materials over the membrane to reduce the potential for damage (ITRC 2007).
8627
8628
More recent design guidance for residences (ASTM 2007) recommends that crawl spaces have a ground
8629
cover consisting of a poured concrete slab, a thin concrete slab, or a sealed 6 mil or thicker polyethylene
8630
membrane (or 3 mil or thicker cross-laminated polyethylene membraine) to seal the top of the gas-
8631
permeable layer. The type of ground cover is determined by the intended use of the crawl space.35
8632
Whichever type is used, the ASTM guidance requires that it be sealed at seams, pipe and other
8633
penetrations, and at the crawl space perimeter.36
8634
8635
Sealing of Radon Entry Routes
8636
Radon can enter buildings through innumerable routes (Figure 3.2-O). Sealing the major entry routes
8637
greatly increases the effectiveness of active soil depressurization and is an essential element of radon
8638
resistant construction.37
8639
8640
8641
Figure 3.2-O. Typical radon entry routes in poured concrete
8642
foundation walls, poured concrete floors and crawlspaces (CMHC 2007).
8643
35 A regular poured concrete slab (≥ 3.5 in.) is to be used to support heavy equipment, frequent maintenance traffic, active
storage, etc., a thin slab ((≥ 2 in.) is to be used to keep out small animals, when the space is to be used for storage of light
objects, when maintenance traffic is expected, or when a sealed polyethylene membrane will not assure a durable sealed
ground cover for the life of the building.
36 For a polyethylene ground cover, ASTM (2007) requires that seams have a minimum 12 in. overlap and be sealed, and that
edges extend at least 12 in. up the foundation walls and be sealed to the walls. All openings for posts, pipes and other
penetrations must also be sealed. When traffic or storage is possible, it is to be protected by barriers that route traffic around
it or durable walkways over it. Polyethylene membranes are also required to have a conspicuously posted label advising
periodic inspections. ASTM (2007) also requires a vapor retarder under the ground cover membrane. See ASTM 2007 for
further requirements.
37 Sealing alone (without active soil depressurization and positive building pressurization) is not an effective radon reduction
technique because it is impossible, practically speaking, to seal every opening that could allow soil gas to enter the building.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 296
Return to TOP
8644
8645
Figure 3.2-P Typical radon entry routes in concrete block foundation walls (CMHC 2007).
8646
8647
Sealing of Joints, Cracks and Penetrations
8648
The aggregate or other gas-permeable layer must be reasonably well sealed at the top, bottom and sides
8649
if the radon control system is to be effective. The following design criteria are recommended for sealing
8650
of joints, cracks and penetrations in the slab above the aggregate38:
8651
¾ Minimize cracking of slabs in contact with the ground. Follow recommended industry practices
8652
(ACI 2004a). Reinforce concrete with rebar and wire mesh, reinforce with fiber additives (ACI
8653
1996), use water-reducing admixtures (plasticizers)( ACI 2004b), cure properly, and/or use higher
8654
strength concrete.
8655
¾ Seal all joints and penetrations in slabs.
8656
o Use a sealant with strong adhesion to concrete under difficult conditions, long service life and
8657
good elasticity. Polyurethane typically meets these criteria. Avoid silicon caulks, which do
8658
not adhere well to concrete.
8659
o Prepare surfaces and apply sealant according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Proper
8660
preparation typically requires that surfaces be clean and dry and that the surface temperature be
8661
above freezing. (ASTM 2007 requires that concrete shall have cured for a minimum of 28 days
8662
before caulks or sealants are applied to it)
8663
o Seal joints between floor slab and exterior and interior load-bearing walls. Alternative
8664
methods include:
8665
38 Sealing of the sides is discussed in the next section. The undisturbed ground is usually considered to provide a sufficient
seal on the bottom, of the gas-permeable layer, unless the soil is very permeable or has been disturbed, e.g. by blasting. See
ASTM 2007 for further information.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 297
Return to TOP
• Install an expansion joint at the floor/wall joint with the top ½ to ¾ in. of the joint
8666
removable after the concrete floor sets. Seal with polyurethane caulk before floor covering
8667
is installed.
8668
• Round the slab at the floor/wall joint with an edging tool when poured. Seal with
8669
polyurethane joint compound after curing and before floor covering is installed.
8670
• (ASTM 2007 recommends pouring slabs tight to the walls and states that such joints
8671
usually do not require additional caulking or sealing).
8672
o Seal pour joints and control saw joints
8673
• Make joints large enough to seal with polyurethane caulk after the slab sets (ASTM 2007
8674
recommends that, if used, they be large enough to fill with polyethylene backer rod prior to
8675
sealing with polyurethane caulk)
8676
• Tool both sides of cold joints when poured.
8677
• Seal after curing.
8678
o Seal openings around penetrations through slab with polyurethane caulk. Penetrations
8679
requiring sealing include water lines, sewer lines, floor drains, gas lines, electrical service
8680
entries, air conditioning condensate drains, roof drains and others. (ASTM 2007 recommends
8681
pouring slabs tight to penetrating objects and states that such joints usually do not require
8682
additional caulking or sealing. If a penetration has a gap around it, e.g. because it was moved
8683
before the slab was set, ASTM states that it shall be sealed by widening the gap, inserting
8684
polyethylene backer rod, and sealing with polyurethane caulk, or filling the gap with low
8685
shrink mortar or grout. Plumbing rough-ins and other openings are to be filled with a sealant
8686
– expanded polyurethane foam or other as permitted by code. Openings in and penetrations
8687
through poured concrete are to be sealed at either the interior or exterior surface. Openings
8688
in and penetrations through hollow core walls are to be sealed at both the interior and exterior
8689
surfaces.)
8690
¾ Seal drains and sumps.
8691
o Provide water traps in all floor drains. (ASTM 2007 recommends mechanical check valves –
8692
preferred – or traps capable of holding standing water 6 in. deep. Check valves are to be
8693
placed in a sealed sump pit with removable cover to facilitate maintenance. When a trap is
8694
used, a permanent label is to be applied in a conspicuous place, directing occupants to keep
8695
the trap filled with water).
8696
o If interior footing drains extend out beneath the footing to daylight or to a sewer, use water
8697
traps to keep the drains airtight while still allowing water to drain.
8698
o If interior footing drains terminate in a sump, seal the sump hole airtight with a gasket and lid,
8699
use a submersible pump, and vent the sump to the outdoors using plastic or other piping.
8700
¾ Seal elevator shafts.
8701
o Thoroughly seal ground contact portions of elevator shafts.
8702
o Design drains and sumps to prevent flow of soil gas into elevator shaft (Provide more specific
8703
information about what works and is permissible under ASME 17.1)
8704
8705
Construction and Sealing of Below-Grade Walls
8706
The following design criteria are recommended for basement walls, stem walls, curtain walls, tunnel
8707
walls and other below-grade walls:
8708
¾ Follow recommended industry practices to minimize cracking of poured concrete walls (ACI
8709
2005) and concrete masonry walls (NCMA xxxx, ACI/ASCE xxxx) (need references).
8710
¾ Seal openings around all penetrations through walls. Use sealants, preparation and application
8711
described for sealing of slab penetrations.
8712
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 298
Return to TOP
¾ For concrete masonry walls:
8713
o Use CMUs with low air permeability if possible (It may be difficult for the manufacturer to
8714
provide CMUs with a specific permeability). (ASTM 2007 states that poured concrete walls,
8715
100% solid CUMs or solidly grouted concrete masonry are preferred).
8716
o Use solid concrete soil-gas entry barriers near the top of the wall, immediately below ledges
8717
(e.g., ledges supporting brick veneer), and at the top and bottom of door, window and other
8718
openings that are below the solid top course or interrupt it. The solid course near the top of the
8719
wall must be above the surface of the finished grade. This course may consist of a continuous
8720
course of solid concrete masonry or a continuous course of fully grouted masonry units. The
8721
solid courses for ledges and openings may consist of either of these, or of a solid concrete
8722
beam. (see ASTM 2007).
8723
o Seal the joint between the bottom course of block and the underlying footing or slab. (provide
8724
more specific information)(ASTM 2007 describes this as optional).
8725
o Seal concrete masonry walls with a suitable waterproofing treatment (Table ?-3). Neither
8726
uncoated concrete blocks nor blocks with dampproofing treatments are effective water or soil-
8727
gas barriers. The waterproofing treatment must provide: good adhesion, crack-spanning ability,
8728
flexibility and elasticity through a wide temperature range, puncture resistance, and long term
8729
chemical and structural stability in the type of environment where it will be located (e.g., in
8730
contact with soil and groundwater etc). Closely follow the manufacturer’s recommended
8731
application procedures.
8732
o If the above measures do not sufficiently reduce radon concentrations, consider depressurizing
8733
the block wall itself. Depressurizing block walls can cause basement depressurization (and
8734
backdrafting of combustion equipment) and in some cases can enhance intrusion of radon, so it
8735
should be used only where subslab depressurization or drain tile depressurization is inadequate
8736
to achieve the required radon reductions (USEPA 1993).
8737
8738
Table 3.2-C Foundation wall coatings (Are these still relevant to today’s coating materials? Should
8739
we make specific comments re various alternative materials/systems?)
8740
Wall Coating
Notes
Exterior
Cold-applied coal tar
modified polyurethane
(waterproofing)
Can be attacked by acids in groundwater, but can be defended by a
protection board.
Dries hard but has some elasticity.
Performance is limited by capabilities of the applicator. Difficult to
achieve even coats on vertical surfaces (USEPA 1991)
Cold-applied polymer-
modified asphalt
(waterproofing)
Less resistant to chemicals than coal tar modified polyurethane, but
has better elasticity, crack-spanning ability and re-sealability.
Performance is limited by capabilities of the applicator. Difficult to
achieve even coats on vertical surfaces (USEPA 1991)
Membrane
waterproofing systems
Uniform thickness.
Most are chemically stable and have good crack-spanning ability.
Concrete seams must be smooth so that membrane is not punctured.
Thermoplastic membranes are rated highly for resistance to chemicals
and longevity.
Rubberized asphalt polyethylene membranes have superior crack-
bridging ability compared to fully adhered thermoplastic membranes
(USEPA 1991).
Seams and overlaps must be carefully and completely sealed for
membranes to function as effective radon barriers.

Return to TOP
Wall Coating
Notes
Exterior
Manufacturers’ recommendations for sealant, application procedures
and safety precautions must be followed.
Bituminous asphalt
parge or spray coat
(dampproofing only)
Undependable as waterproofing treatment or to seal radon entry
routes.
Can be attacked by soil and groundwater chemicals, specifically acids
(USEPA 1991)
May lose elasticity at below-freezing temperatures.
Surface bonding cement
or mortar (dampproofing
only)
Undependable as waterproofing treatment or to seal radon entry
routes.
Some may be chemically unstable in the alkaline environment of
Portland cement (USEPA 1991).
Interior
Cementitious coatings
Inelastic, does not have good crack-spanning ability, cannot resist
hydrostatic pressure.
Masonry paint
Some can be effective as radon barriers if properly applied
(Ruppersberger 1991).
8741
(ASTM 2007 notes that post-tensioned slab foundations do not normally have below-grade walls. For
8742
these foundations, curtain walls are required around the edges to seal the edges of the gas-permeable
8743
layer. All penetrations required for the radon system, including suction piping, drain traps and check
8744
valves, etc. are to be installed before the slab is cast.)
8745
8746
Building Pressurization & Dilution Ventilation
8747
Positive pressurization of grade-level and below-grade spaces reduces radon entry and is a useful
8748
adjunct to active soil depressurization and sealing of radon entry routes. Ventilation with outdoor air
8749
also lowers indoor radon concentrations by dilution.
8750
8751
Location of Ductwork
8752
Do not place ductwork below grade (buried, in tunnels, or in crawl spaces) in radon-prone areas.
8753
Below-grade ductwork can draw radon into the air distribution system. Return ductwork leaks can
8754
create a strong negative pressure, but even supply ductwork may be negatively pressurized at the duct
8755
wall.
8756
8757
Where ground-contact mechanical rooms serve as plenums and are at negative pressure during HVAC
8758
system operation, requirements for sealing of joints, cracks and penetrations and coating of below-grade
8759
walls should be carefully followed to create an effective radon barrier. (is this sufficient?) (What about
8760
return ductwork in walls and chaseways in contact with slabs?)
8761
8762
Envelope Air-Tightness
8763
Follow recommendations on air-tightness of the building envelope in the section titled Strategy 2.2
8764
Limit Condensation of Water Vapor within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces to reduce
8765
leakiness of the building envelope and reduce the excess of outside air over exhaust air required to
8766
maintain positive building pressure.
8767
8768
Building Pressurization
8769
Follow recommendations in the section titled Strategy 2.3 Maintain Proper Building Pressure to
8770
maintain positive pressure in all ground contact rooms during occupied hours. This will reduce entry of
8771
radon as well as moisture and unfiltered contaminants in outdoor air. Pay particular attention to ground
8772
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 300
Return to TOP
contact rooms with high exhaust rates such as laboratories, commercial kitchens, and locker and shower
8773
rooms. The ASD system must impose sufficient suction on the subslab to keep the subslab is at lower
8774
pressure than these spaces, even though the spaces themselves may be negative with respect to adjacent
8775
rooms or the outdoors. (Is this reasonable for these spaces?)
8776
8777
If climate considerations, high humidity spaces or use of natural ventilation require pressure in ground-
8778
contact rooms to be neutral or negative, assure that the active soil depressurization system flow rate is
8779
adjusted to keep the subslab area more negative than the occupied space.
8780
8781
Ventilation Rates
8782
Follow recommendations in the section titled Strategy 7.1 Provide Appropriate Outdoor Air Quantities
8783
for Each Room or Zone to dilute contaminants, including radon, during occupied hours.
8784
8785
If negative building pressures overwhelm the active soil depressurization system when the HVAC
8786
system is off and draw radon into the building, it will be necessary to start HVAC operation and
8787
ventilation in advance of occupancy to establish positive pressurization and dilute radon levels that built
8788
up while the HVAC system was off.
8789
8790
Quality Assurance (QA) of Radon Control Systems
8791
8792
Design Verification
8793
Assure that the design guidance provided in this section is properly implemented in the design,
8794
including:
8795
• Design of load-bearing elements
8796
• Sub-slab aggregate material and thickness, geotextile and vapor retarder
8797
• Crawl space membrane and permeable material or perforated piping network
8798
• Location, size and construction of suction pits
8799
• Location, size, termination, sealing and labeling of radon vent pipe
8800
• Suction fan type, sizing, location and installation
8801
• Warning device sensors, alarms, location
8802
• Sealing of below grade joints, cracks and penetrations
8803
• Construction and sealing of below grade walls, especially concrete masonry walls
8804
• Ductwork location
8805
• Building air-tightness, building pressurization and dilution ventilation (covered elsewhere)
8806
8807
Submittal Review
8808
Review submittals to assure that they are consistent with design criteria, including:
8809
• Sub-slab aggregate
8810
• Geotextile
8811
• Subslab vapor retarder
8812
• Crawl space membrane
8813
• Permeable material for crawl space
8814
• Perforated piping network for crawl space
8815
• Radon vent pipe
8816
• Radon suction fan and flexible connectors
8817
• Warning device sensors and alarms
8818
• Sealants for below-grade joints, cracks and penetrations
8819
• Permeability of CMUs for below grade walls
8820
• Solid top blocks for below grade CMU walls
8821
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

Return to TOP
• Waterproofing materials for below grade CMU walls
8822
8823
Construction Coordination
8824
Meet with construction management, general contractor and relevant subcontractors to communicate:
8825
• Design intent of the active soil depressurization system and foundation sealing,
8826
• Prescriptive and performance requirements,
8827
• Construction observation and verification requirements for the active soil depressurization system
8828
and foundation sealing..
8829
8830
Assure that required construction observation items are incorporated into the construction schedule
8831
8832
Construction Observation
8833
Assure that active soil depressurization system is installed in accordance with the contract documents.
8834
Conduct appropriately timed site inspections to verify:
8835
• No unintended subslab barriers divide any design depressurization zone into more than one zone
8836
(prior to installation of aggregate)
8837
• Installation of geotextile fabric below slab (prior to installation of aggregate)
8838
• Installation of aggregate below slab to proper thickness and with no introduction of fines (prior to
8839
pouring of concrete)
8840
• Proper location and installation of suction pits, including horizontal vent pipe and
8841
support/reinforcement for concrete above suction pit (prior to pouring concrete),
8842
• Installation of vapor retarder over aggregate (prior to pouring concrete)
8843
• Installation of concrete to minimize cracking
8844
• Installation of solid top blocks on CMU walls
8845
• Installation of waterproofing/radon barrier on CMU walls (prior to backfilling (exterior) or
8846
covering (interior)
8847
• Installation of permeable material or perforated piping in crawl spaces
8848
• Preparation and sealing of floor/wall joints (prior to installation of floor covering)
8849
• Preparation and sealing of pour joints and control saw joints (prior to installation of floor
8850
covering)
8851
• Sealing of all penetrations, drains and sumps (prior to floor covering or wall surface installation,
8852
if any)
8853
• Installation and sealing of polyethylene or other ground cover in crawl space
8854
• Installation, sealing and labeling of vent pipe riser (prior to installation of gypsum board or other
8855
wall surface)
8856
• Installation and labeling of radon suction fan and vent pipe termination
8857
• Installation and labeling of warning device
8858
8859
Construction Verification
8860
Assure that radon levels during occupied hours are below exposure limits/action levels:
8861
¾ Measure radon levels during occupied hours under normal operation of the ASD and HVAC
8862
systems, at least 24 hours after ASD system operation is started (provide more
8863
guidance/references)
8864
¾ If radon levels are above exposure limits/action levels and you did not install fan(s) to activate the
8865
active soil depressurization system, install fan(s) following guidance in this section.
8866
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 302
Return to TOP
¾ If radon levels are above exposure limits/action levels with depressurization fan(s) operating,
8867
conduct tests of pressure field extension and troubleshoot problems.
8868
o Drill ¼ to ½ in. holes through the slab at various distances and directions from each suction
8869
pit, avoiding any subslab utility lines.
8870
o Using a micromanometer, measure the subslab pressure in each hole relative to the pressure
8871
above the lab with the fan off and with the fan on. If all of the guidelines presented here are
8872
followed, the subslab pressure should be at least -0.01 in. wg in all test holes with the fan(s)
8873
on.
8874
o If the pressure is not at least -0.002 in. wg in all test holes, troubleshoot the system by
8875
confirming fan operation, sealing major radon entry routes, locating potential subslab barriers,
8876
inspecting the aggregate used, and inspecting the operation of the HVAC system.
8877
o After resolution of problems, carefully seal test holes with concrete patching material.
8878
8879
Assure that warning device and labeling are installed as recommended. Test response of warning device
8880
to a reduction or reversal of negative pressure.
8881
8882
Operation and Maintenance (O&M) of Radon Control Systems
8883
Provide training to the facilities staff on the purpose and importance, location, operation and
8884
maintenance of the radon control systems.
8885
8886
The following items are recommended for inclusion in the O&M manual for the radon control system:
8887
• Describe the system, its purpose and its components.
8888
• Provide as-built drawings showing locations of suction pits, vent pipes and suction fans.
8889
• Provide results of any radon tests obtained without or with operation of the ASD system.
8890
• Describe how to read the radon system monitor, and provide manufacturer’s information on
8891
monitor installation and calibration.
8892
• Describe how to interpret the system failure warning device and what to do if the device
8893
indicates a failure.
8894
• Provide contact information for the company that should be called for radon system service.
8895
• Provide contact information for the agency in charge of the state’s radon program.
8896
• Provide radon fan warranty and installation documentation.
8897
• Recommend the following maintenance:
8898
o Weekly: (Would monthly be adequate??)
8899
• Check pressure gauge(s) in radon vent pipe(s) and system alarm to ensure that fan is
8900
maintaining adequate negative pressure to depressurize the subslab area.
8901
o Annually:
8902
• Inspect radon suction fan(s) for bearing failure or signs of other abnormal operation and
8903
repair or replace if required.
8904
• Inspect discharge location of radon vent pipe to ensure that no air intake has been located
8905
nearby and that no change in building usage has placed the exhaust near operable
8906
windows.
8907
• Check HVAC system to verify that its operation is not creating negative building
8908
pressures that could overcome the active soil depressurization system.
8909
• If building has settled, check for slab, floor or basement wall cracks and perform radon
8910
testing and additional sealing, if needed, to ensure the continued effectiveness of the
8911
system.
8912
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 303
Return to TOP
• Inspect impermeable membrane in crawl space and repair or replace if necessary? (How
8913
long will poly last in crawl space?)
8914
o When work is performed in foundation area:
8915
• Assure that any new or disturbed penetrations are sealed following the specifications for
8916
the original construction.
8917
o When work is performed on HVAC systems:
8918
• Assure that requirements for building pressurization and ventilation are maintained.
8919
8920
Training and O&M documentation on building pressurization and ventilation are covered in other
8921
sections.
8922
8923
Radon Measurements
8924
USEPA (1994) recommends that radon measurements be made in newly constructed schools with ASD
8925
systems at least 24 hours after the ASD fan is turned on, to verify that radon levels are below 4 pCi/L.
8926
8927
They also recommend that radon measurements be made in new schools in areas with high potential for
8928
radon (Zone 1 and other radon-prone areas that have been identified in Zones 2 and 3), if an ASD
8929
system was only roughed in, to determine whether suction fans should be added to activate the system
8930
(USEPA, no date, a).
8931
8932
Finally, they recommend that every existing school be tested for radon (USEPA no date, b).
8933
8934
Since radon levels can vary greatly from one room to another, EPA recommends that measurements be
8935
made in all frequently occupied ground-contact rooms39 (EPA 1993c). This includes frequently
8936
occupied rooms:
8937
• On slabs-on-grade,
8938
• Basements,
8939
• Directly above enclosed crawl spaces, and
8940
• Directly above basement spaces that are not frequently occupied.
8941
8942
Radon tests may be short term (2 to 90 days) or long term (more than 90 days). Measurements should
8943
be taken during the colder months and with windows and doors closed except for normal entry and exit.
8944
Tests of 2 to 5 days should be taken on weekdays with the HVAC systems operating normally, and
8945
should start after at least 12 hours of closed conditions, e.g., after a weekend. Short term tests should
8946
not be taken during abnormal weather. Testing also should not be conducted during structural changes,
8947
renovations or replacement of the HVAC system. (EPA 2003).
8948
8949
If a school elects to use short-term tests for initial measurements, EPA (1993c) recommends that these
8950
measurements be made in all frequently occupied ground contact rooms simultaneously. They
8951
recommend that simultaneous follow-up tests be conducted in every room with an initial short-term test
8952
result of 4 pCi/L or greater. The follow-up tests should be short-term measurements if results are
8953
needed quickly, for example, if the initial measurements are on the order of 10 pCi/L or higher. Long-
8954
term follow-up tests, preferably over an entire school year, are appropriate if radon levels are in the
8955
range of 4 to 10 pCi/L, although short-term tests may also be used..
8956
8957
39 Rooms on upper floors can also have elevated radon levels, but they are unlikely to exceed those in the ground-contact
rooms. Mitigation measures that reduce radon levels in ground contact rooms will also reduce radon levels on upper floors.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 304
Return to TOP
EPA (1993c) provides additional guidance on placing the detectors in a room, on who may conduct the
8958
testing, and on quality assurance procedures. They also note that school districts should contact their
8959
state Radon Office before testing, since some states may have their own requirements (EPA no date, b).
8960
8961
If the initial and follow-up tests are both of the short term type, EPA (1993c) recommends that action be
8962
taken to reduce radon levels if the average of the two measurements is 4 pCi/L or more. If a long term
8963
test is conducted, EPA recommends that action be taken if the long-term average is 4 pCi/L or more.
8964
8965
Beyond the first round of testing (initial plus follow-up), EPA (1993c) recommends retesting as follows:
8966
• “If no mitigation is required after initial testing (e.g., all rooms were found to have levels below
8967
4 pCi/L), retest all frequently occupied rooms in contact with the ground sometime in the future.
8968
As a building ages and settles, radon entry may increase due to cracks in the foundation or other
8969
structural changes.
8970
• “If radon mitigation measures have been implemented in a school, retest these systems as a
8971
periodic check on any implemented radon reduction measures.
8972
• “If major renovations to the structure of a school building or major alterations to a school's
8973
HVAC system are planned, retest the school before initiating the renovation. If elevated radon is
8974
present, radon-resistant techniques can be included as part of the renovation.
8975
• “Retest after major renovations to the structure of a school building or after major alterations to a
8976
school's HVAC system. These renovations and alterations may increase radon levels within a
8977
school building.” (USEPA 1993c).
8978
8979

Page 307
Return to TOP
9090
Strategy 3.3 – Reduce Intrusion of Vapors from Subsurface Contaminants
9091
9092
9093
Background
9094
Vapor intrusion is “the migration of a [chemical of concern] vapor from a subsurface soil or
9095
groundwater source into the indoor environment of an existing or planned structure" (ASTM 2008).
9096
Volatile substances in buried wastes or contaminated soil or groundwater can vaporize and move
9097
through the vadose zone40 by diffusion. Once they reach the zone of influence of a building, they are
9098
drawn through the soil and through cracks and openings in the foundation due to pressure differences
9099
between the soil and the building (USEPA 2004). A building may be at negative pressure relative to the
9100
surrounding soil due to thermal stack effect, wind effect, exhaust and combustion vent flows exceeding
9101
make-up air flows, or other factors (ASHRAE 2005).
9102
9103
The chemicals of concern for vapor intrusion are those that are sufficiently volatile to migrate through
9104
the soil as a vapor and sufficiently toxic that they may adversely affect human health41 (USEPA 2002).
9105
These include many volatile organic compounds, some semi-volatile organic compounds, and some
9106
inorganic substances such as mercury. Vapor intrusion is a concern primarily because of the potential
9107
for chronic health effects from long term exposure to low concentrations, although in extreme cases
9108
vapor concentrations can be high enough to cause acute health effects or explosion hazards (USEPA
9109
2002).42
9110
9111
Although vapors can intrude into buildings from naturally occurring subsurface gases,43 most
9112
regulations, standards and guidance are specific to vapors from environmental contaminants.44
9113
Contaminants can be present below ground due to accidental spills, improper disposal, leaking landfills
9114
or leaking underground or above ground storage tanks (Tillman & Weaver 2005).
9115
9116
Some of the most common present and past land uses associated with chemicals of concern include gas
9117
stations, dry cleaners, former industrial sites that used vapor degreasers or other parts cleaning chemicals,
9118
and manufactured gas plants (ASTM 2008). Other land uses potentially associated with chemicals of
9119
concern include landfills, circuit board manufacturing plants, electrical power facilities, military bases,
9120
etc. There are hundreds of thousands of contaminated or potentially contaminated sites in the U.S.,
9121
including Superfund (CERCLA), RCRA, underground storage tank (UST), brownfields and oil sites
9122
(USEPA 2002).45 For example, it is estimated that there are about 36,000 active dry cleaning facilities
9123
40 The zone between the land surface and the regional water table. The vadose zone is generally unsaturated (i.e., pores
contain a mixture of water and soil gas) except for the capillary fringe above the water table and any perched water tables
within the vadose zone.
41 A chemical is considered sufficiently volatile if its Henry’s Law Constant is 1 x 10-5 atm-m3/mol or greater. A chemical is
considered sufficiently toxic if the vapor concentration of the pure component poses an incremental lifetime cancer risk
greater than 10-6 or a non-cancer hazard index greater than 1. (USEPA 2002).
42 For example, intrusion of methane or carbon dioxide from landfills can create risks of explosion or suffocation,
respectively (USEPA 2005). These issues are outside the scope of this guide but should be evaluated when a building project
is on or near a contaminated site. ITRC’s (2007) guideline states that Step 1 of the screening process should evaluate
whether the site may present an acute exposure concern or emergency hazard. If so, immediate collection of indoor samples
or implementation of interim remedial measures such as evacuation of occupants or sub-slab depressurization must be
undertaken.
43 Examples of naturally occurring subsurface gases are radon, methane and hydrogen sulfide. Radon is a decay product of
uranium and is widespread. Mitigation of radon is addressed in Strategy 3.2. Naturally occurring methane includes natural
gas in geologic formations as well as methane formed from the anaerobic decay of more recent organic matter. Hydrogen
sulfide gas occurs naturally in crude oil, natural gas and volcanic gases and can also be formed from the anaerobic decay of
organic matter.
44 The exception is radon. See Section .
45 See Glossary at the end of this Strategy.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 308
Return to TOP
in the U.S., of which 75% are contaminated with volatile solvents (USEPA, cited in ITRC 2003). The
9124
GAO (2002) has estimated that 200,000 active underground storage tanks in the U.S. may be leaking.
9125
There are an estimated 3,000 to 5,000 sites of former manufactured gas plants in the U.S. (USEPA
9126
1999).
9127
9128
The potential for intrusion of vapors from these sites into buildings was not widely recognized by U.S.
9129
regulators until the 1990s (IRTC 2007) and both the science and the regulatory environment are still
9130
evolving. The U.S. EPA developed draft guidance for the evaluation of vapor intrusion in 2002
9131
(USEPA 2002). This guidance was suggested for use at RCRA Corrective Action sites, CERCLA
9132
National Priorities List and Superfund Alternative sites and Brownfields sites, but not for UST sites, for
9133
which EPA recommended continued use of an earlier directive (USEPA 1995a) (see also ASTM 2002).
9134
In 2005 EPA published guidance for evaluating landfill gas emissions with respect to both explosion
9135
hazard and vapor intrusion (USEPA 2005). Many states have developed regulations, policies or
9136
guidance for vapor intrusion investigations, are currently developing such documents, or rely on the
9137
EPA 2002 draft guidance (Buonicore 2007, ITRC 2004)46 The U.S. Department of Defense has its own
9138
guidance documents as well (U.S.A.F. 2006, U.S. Army 2006). Recently, ASTM (2008) developed a
9139
national standard for assessment of vapor intrusion on properties involved in real estate transactions.
9140
This standard addresses vapor intrusion from USTs and landfills as well as CERCLA, RCRA and
9141
brownfield sites. It defines straightforward and relatively low cost approaches to screen sites for
9142
potential vapor intrusion conditions.
9143
9144
It is uncertain how widespread vapor intrusion problems are (Tillman & Weaver 2005), though certainly
9145
a number of sites with significant vapor intrusion have been identified. In Redfield, Colorado, for
9146
example, use of solvents at a former manufacturing facility had contaminated the groundwater with 1,1
9147
dichloroethene (DCE) in a plume extending about 1.5 miles under residential properties (Folkes and
9148
Kurz 2002). Identification of this problem led to indoor air testing of nearly 800 homes and mitigation
9149
of nearly 400 (ITRC 2007). At the other extreme of scale, an investigation conducted for USEPA
9150
(2006) at a retail dry cleaner in Michigan found that perchloroethylene (PCE) contamination of the soil
9151
caused vapor intrusion in one nearby residence but no others. Based on extensive discussion with Phase
9152
I environmental consultants, the Task Group that developed ASTM E2600-08 expects that the screening
9153
methodology in Tiers 1 and 2 of the standard should eliminate vapor intrusion as a concern in the vast
9154
majority of real estate transactions (Buonicore, pers. comm. 2008).
9155
9156
Vapor intrusion can occur even though a site has undergone remediation. For example, at an industrial
9157
site in Endicott, NY, over 73,000 gallons of VOCs had been removed and over 2 billion gallons of
9158
groundwater pumped over a period of 20 years when the State directed that the potential for vapor
9159
intrusion be investigated. Testing was conducted in a sample of homes and businesses located above the
9160
300 acre groundwater plume. Elevated concentrations of tricholorethene (TCE) and other VOCs were
9161
found in indoor air even in cases where groundwater concentrations were low, and remediation was
9162
conducted in over 400 structures (McDonald and Wertz 2003, NYSDEC, 2007).
9163
9164
A few states are moving to evaluate closed cases for vapor intrusion. New York has decided that all past,
9165
current and future contaminated sites will be evaluated (NYDEC 2006). This policy includes several
9166
hundred sites already remediated and either in the long term monitoring phase or closed out (NYDEC
9167
2006, ITRC 2007). Maine has reopened selected closed sites (ITRC 2007). A few other states have
9168
investigated a few sites without formally reopening them, have reviewed old sites on a case by case
9169
basis, are evaluating their policy on this issue or note that they have the authority to re-open sites if
9170
warranted (ITRC 2007).
9171
9172
46 Links to various guidance documents are available at http://www.envirogroup.com/links.php.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 309
Return to TOP
Screening and Assessment
9173
Screening and assessment of the potential for vapor intrusion must follow the regulations, policies and
9174
guidance of the relevant jurisdiction. Obtaining guidance from the appropriate regulatory agency early
9175
in the process is the best way to avoid problems and assure a successful project. ITRC maintains a list
9176
of vapor intrusion contacts for states and EPA regions on its website47 and EnviroGroup maintains links
9177
to various state guidance documents on its website.48 It is anticipated that USEPA and ASTM will work
9178
together to educate state regulatory agencies and try to achieve consistency in response as the standard
9179
becomes a routine part of property environmental due diligence (Buonicore, pers. comm. 2008). The
9180
ASTM process is described here.
9181
9182
ASTM standard E 2600-08 uses a tiered approach to allow properties with a low risk of vapor intrusion
9183
to be screened out quickly and at relatively low cost. “The first two tiers…[are] used to determine
9184
whether a pVIC [potential vapor intrusion condition] exists and, if so, the third tier is designed to
9185
provide confirmation (i.e., a VIC exists) or reduce the level of uncertainty. The fourth tier provides
9186
general mitigation alternatives…” (ASTM 2008). The user may proceed to a Tier 3 investigation or pre-
9187
emptive mitigation (Tier 4) without conducting Tier 1 and 2 screenings if they choose to do so for
9188
reasons of timeliness, cost-effectiveness or other considerations.
9189
9190
The standard requires that vapor intrusion assessments be performed by an environmental professional
9191
as defined in ASTM E 1527-05,49 so in most cases this work will be contracted outside the primary
9192
design team. The steps are described to give the design team an understanding of the process and how it
9193
may impact the schedule and the ultimate building design.
9194
9195
ASTM Tier 1 Screening
9196
The ASTM E 2600-08 Tier 1 screening uses information that is generally collected during an ASTM E-
9197
1527 Phase I Environmental Site Assessment.50 Phase I ESAs are conducted as a standard part of
9198
environmental due diligence in most commercial real estate transactions in the U.S. Information
9199
required for Tier 1 screening includes:
9200
• Information on the existing or planned use of the property,
9201
• Existing and planned types of structures on the property,
9202
• Characteristics of the surrounding area,
9203
• Government records that identify known or suspected sources of contaminants of concern on the
9204
property itself and surrounding areas,
9205
• Historical records related to prior use of the property and surrounding areas,
9206
• Physical setting information including topographic, hydrologic, geologic, hydrogeologic and soils
9207
data,
9208
• Natural and man-made features that may provide preferential paths for vapor movement,
9209
47 http://www.itrcweb.org/vaporintrusion/
48 http://www.envirogroup.com/links.php
49 ASTM E 1527-05 references and repeats the requirements of 40 CFR §312.10(b). Under this regulation a Professional
Engineer or Geologist must have the equivalent of three years of full time relevant experience and others must have up to ten
years of relevant experience to conduct these assessments. 40 CFR §312.10(b) states that, “Relevant experience, as used in
the definition of environmental professional in this section, means: participation in the performance of all appropriate
inquiries investigations, environmental site assessments, or other site investigations that may include environmental analyses,
investigations, and remediation which involve the understanding of surface and subsurface environmental conditions and the
processes used to evaluate these conditions and for which professional judgment was used to develop opinions regarding
conditions indicative of releases or threatened releases (see Sec. 312.1(c)) to the subject property.”
50 In the past there was potentially some ambiguity as to whether the Phase I ESA itself required investigation of the potential
for vapor intrusion (Buonicore 2007), although the vast majority of Phase I ESAs did not include such an investigation. New
ASTM standard E 2600-08 clarifies that vapor intrusion assessment is outside the scope of an ASTM E-1527 ESA, though it
may be conducted concurrently as a supplemental scope item.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 310
Return to TOP
• Specialized knowledge that the user (typically the prospective purchaser) may have, and
9210
• Any other information from the Phase I ESA, if the VIA is conducted in conjunction with an ESA.
9211
9212
This information is reviewed to evaluate whether a potential vapor intrusion condition exists. The
9213
screening assesses:
9214
1. Whether there are known or suspected contaminated sites within specified minimum search
9215
distances of the property,
9216
2. Whether chemicals of concern are likely to be present at these sites,
9217
3. Whether the contaminated plume of groundwater, soil gas or LNAPL51 from these sites is within
9218
the critical distance of the nearest existing or planned building on the property (or the nearest
9219
property boundary, if there are no existing or planned buildings), and if it is, whether risk-based
9220
concentrations for vapor intrusion are exceeded.
9221
9222
The first two of these tests are mandatory, while the third is conducted only if data on the extent and
9223
depth of the contaminated plume are available at the time of the Tier 1 screening.
9224
9225
The standard specifies the approximate minimum search distances to which government records must be
9226
reviewed to identify known or suspect contaminated sites. For some types of contaminant sources the
9227
search covers only the property itself, while for other types it extends as far as a mile from the property’s
9228
boundaries (Table 3.3-A).52 The standard lists typical chemicals of concern for vapor intrusion in an
9229
informative appendix (see Table 3.3-B).53 The environmental professional uses existing records and
9230
data and familiarity with various types of contaminant sources (e.g., service stations, dry cleaners) to
9231
determine whether chemicals of concern are likely to be present.
9232
9233
Table 3.3-A Approximate minimum search distances. Government records must be reviewed for
9234
contaminated sites of various types that are within these distances (miles) from the nearest boundary of
9235
the property undergoing transaction. (ASTM 2008).

51Light non-aqueous phase liquid: LNAPLs are liquids that are not readily soluble in water and are less dense than water. As
a result, they may pool on top of the water table. Oil and gasoline are examples.
52In assessing the existence of known or suspected contaminated sites, historical records of past land uses must also be
searched to distances consistent with those in Table X-1. Standard historical sources that must be reviewed include fire
insurance maps, local street directories, aerial photographs, USGS topographical maps and other credible sources.
53 Many states have their own lists which must be used for sites in their jurisdiction. For example the California EPA (2005)
started with the USEPA list but added fuel oxygenates and two PCB congeners.
