71556 1,1,1-Trichloroethane
75150 Carbon disulfide
60571 Dieldrin
78933 Methylethylketone (2-
butanone)
79016 Trichloroethylene
56235 Carbon tetrachloride
115297 Endosulfan
108101 Methylisobutylketone
75694 Trichlorofluoromethane
57749 Chlordane
106898 Epichlorohydrin
80626 Methylmethacrylate
96184 1,2,3-Trichloropropane
126998 2-Chloro-1,3-
butadiene
(chloroprene)
60297 Ethyl ether
91576 2-Methylnaphthalene
95636 1,2,4-Trimethylbenzene
108907 Chlorobenzene
141786 Ethylacetate
1634044 MTBE
108678 1,3,5-Trimethylbenzene
109693 1-Chlorobutane
100414 Ethylbenzene
108383 m-Xylene
108054 Vinyl acetate
124481 Chlorodibromomethan
e
75218 Ethylene oxide
91203 Naphthalene
75014 Vinyl chloride
(chloroethane)
75456 Chlorodifluoromethane
97632 Ethylmethacrylate
104518 n-Butylbenzene
\*The ASTM (2008) list is drawn from USEPA (2002), deleting those chemicals that USEPA indicated do not meet both the
9241
toxicity and volatility criteria, and adding polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs). The approach used by USEPA to develop the
9242
original list is described in Appendix D of USEPA 2002. Although USEPA 2002 excludes petroleum releases from
9243
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 312
Return to TOP
underground storage tanks from the scope of its guidance, the many of the key chemicals of concern for petroleum leaks are
9244
nevertheless included in their list.
9245
\* As noted previously, intrusion of methane can create an explosion hazard. This is outside the scope of this IAQ guide, but
9246
should be considered when evaluating properties near contaminated sites that may be associated with subsurface methane.
9247
9248
If data on the location of the contaminated plume are available, an evaluation can be made as to whether
9249
the plume is within the critical distance.54 The critical distance from the plume to the nearest existing or
9250
planned building on the property (or the nearest property boundary, if there are no existing or planned
9251
buildings) is a “stretched string” distance that takes into account the depth to the plume as well as its
9252
overland distance from the property. The critical distance is 100 feet for non-petroleum hydrocarbon
9253
chemicals of concern and LNAPL, and 30 feet for dissolved petroleum hydrocarbon chemicals of
9254
concern. The shorter distance for dissolved petroleum hydrocarbons reflects the fact that they are
9255
biodegraded in the vadose zone in the presence of oxygen.
9256
9257
If data are available on the concentrations of chemicals of concern in the contaminated plume, an
9258
evaluation can be made as to whether these exceed risk-based concentrations (RBCs) for vapor intrusion.
9259
Some states have established their own generic RBCs for screening purposes, while others use the RBCs
9260
from USEPA (2002). The USEPA draft guidance, as an example, sets target indoor air concentrations to
9261
satisfy both the prescribed risk level (for cancer) and the target hazard index (for non-cancer risk). It
9262
then establishes target shallow soil gas, deep soil gas and groundwater concentrations (RBCs)
9263
corresponding to these target indoor air concentrations, based on “reasonable worst case” attenuation
9264
factors from soil gas to indoor air and partitioning between the water table and soil gas governed by
9265
Henry’s law.55
9266
9267
According to a survey of state regulators (ITRC 2004), over two thirds of states now differentiate
9268
between residential and non-residential occupancies in setting screening levels for contaminants of
9269
concern. The non-residential screening levels are generally somewhat higher than the residential levels
9270
because the lifetime hours of exposure are assumed to be lower. In addition some jurisdictions use a
9271
child adjustment factor for carcinogens in residential settings but not in non-residential settings (see, for
9272
example, NJDEP 2005). Screening levels for schools, daycare facilities, hospitals, nursing homes or
9273
other occupancies with sensitive populations are typically lower than those for other workplaces and
9274
may be the same as those for residences. The target indoor air concentrations for vapor intrusion in
9275
workplaces are generally based on current estimates of cancer risk levels and non-cancer hazard
9276
quotients and are much lower than OSHA occupational levels.
9277
9278
If a known or suspect source of contamination exists within the specified minimum search distance, and
9279
if chemicals of concern are suspected to be present at that site, the ASTM standard requires that a
9280
potential vapor intrusion condition be presumed to exist if no further data are available. If data on the
9281
location and depth of the contaminated plumes associated with these sites are available as well, a
9282
potential vapor intrusion condition must be presumed to exist only if at least one plume or LNAPL is
9283
within the critical distance of existing or planned buildings on the property (or of the property boundary
9284
if there are no existing or planned buildings). If data on contaminant concentrations in these plumes are
9285
also available, and if the state allows use of risk-based concentrations, a potential vapor intrusion
9286
54 In some cases this evaluation can be made without detailed information on the groundwater plume. For example, if there is
surface water between the contaminant source and the site under consideration, extension of the plume past the surface water
can be ruled out. If the groundwater flow direction is known, and if the contaminant source is downgradient of the site under
consideration, the critical distance is measured from the source to the nearest existing or planned building (or property
boundary), and information on the depth of the plume is not required.
55 The USEPA (2002) draft guidance allows the site risk manager to “choose to select media-specific target concentrations
for screening at three cancer risk levels: 10-4, 10-5 and 10-6, or a hazard quotient of 1 for non-cancer risk, whichever is
appropriate…” and thus provides three different tables of generic screening levels using the three different cancer risk levels.
This provides as much as a 100:1 latitude in the RBCs to be used for screening.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 313
Return to TOP
condition must be presumed to exist only if at least one plume is within the critical distance and has
9287
concentrations at the closest point in the plume which exceed the risk-based concentrations. If a
9288
potential vapor intrusion condition is identified, the client working with his or her environmental
9289
consultant must decide how to proceed. For example, it may make sense to proceed to a Tier 2
9290
investigation. Alternatively, a client may decide it makes sense to proceed directly to pre-emptive
9291
mitigation (Tier 4).
9292
9293
If it can be determined that there are no known or suspect contaminated sites within the minimum search
9294
distance, or if there are such sites, that the distance to their contaminated plumes or LNAPL is greater
9295
than the critical distance, or if the contaminated plume is within the critical distance but the plume
9296
concentrations are lower than the applicable risk-based concentrations, then it can be presumed unlikely
9297
that a potential vapor intrusion condition will occur in an existing or planned building on the property.
9298
If no potential vapor intrusion condition exists, no further action is required. However, the user might
9299
still decide to install pre-emptive mitigation if, for example, it appears possible that a migrating plume
9300
may reach the property at some time in the future, or if there is a risk of future leaks or spills.
9301
9302
ASTM Tier 2 Screening
9303
The Tier 2 screening may be based on existing or new data. If “extent of contamination” reports
9304
(“Phase II” reports) exist at the appropriate regulatory agency for all potentially contaminated sites
9305
within the minimum search distance, then the Tier 2 screening can be based on existing information
9306
alone. The reports are reviewed to determine the size and behavior of the contaminated plume(s), the
9307
specific contaminants and their concentrations, and the status of remediation. The environmental
9308
professional evaluates this information to determine whether the contaminated plume is within the
9309
critical distance and whether the concentrations at the nearest point exceed risk-based concentration
9310
levels, as described earlier. It appears likely that in many cases environmental professionals will
9311
combine the Tier 1 screening and the non-invasive portion of the Tier 2 screening (i.e., the portion based
9312
on existing data) into a single scope of work (Buonicore, pers. comm. 2008).
9313
9314
If there are known or suspect contaminated sites within the minimum search distance for which detailed
9315
plume data are not available, Tier 2 screening allows for sampling of soil, soil gas and/or groundwater, if
9316
such sampling may prove useful in assessing the potential for a vapor intrusion condition.
9317
9318
The criteria for deciding whether a potential vapor intrusion condition exists or is not likely to exist at
9319
the property are essentially the same as described for the Tier 1 screening. While the decision at the Tier
9320
1 stage is sometimes based only on the presence of known or suspect sources of contaminants within the
9321
minimum search distance (which in the worst case can be up to a mile), the more complete data
9322
available at the conclusion of Tier 2 should at least allow the decision to be made based on whether a
9323
plume is within the critical distance (which at worst can be 100 feet), and may allow the decision to be
9324
based on whether contamination in those plumes exceeds risk-based concentrations.
9325
9326
ASTM Tier 3 Assessment
9327
If the Tier 1 and Tier 2 screenings do not screen out the potential for a vapor intrusion condition, the
9328
prospective owner may choose to conduct a more sophisticated (and likely more expensive) Tier 3
9329
assessment. Because current policies vary widely, the ASTM standard defines the assessment only in
9330
general terms and requires the environmental professional to review and consider applicable federal and
9331
state regulation, policy or guidance. The standard suggests that in jurisdictions which do not provide
9332
vapor intrusion guidance, users should rely on the USEPA (2002) draft guidance or the guidance
9333
prepared by the Interstate Technology & Regulatory Council (ITRC 2007).56 It also notes that the Tier 3
9334
56 ITRC is “a state-led, national coalition of personnel from the environmental regulatory agencies of some 46 states and the
District of Columbia, three federal agencies, tribes, and public and industry stakeholders. The organization is devoted to
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 314
Return to TOP
assessment requires specialized expertise and may be beyond the capability of the environmental
9335
professional conducting the Phase I ESA.
9336
9337
The specific investigative approach(es) selected for a Tier 3 assessment (Table 3.3-C) may depend on
9338
regulatory requirements or on other considerations. ITRC (2007) and ASTM (2008) provide
9339
considerable informative guidance and references on sampling design, sample collection and analysis
9340
methods, quality assurance and interpretation. Both recommend that multiple lines of evidence be used
9341
in drawing conclusions.
9342
9343
Table 3.3-C Approaches for vapor intrusion investigations (ITRC 2007)
9344
Medium investigated
Evaluation method\*
Key issues
Groundwater
Prediction of indoor air concentrations
based on attenuation factor or site-
specific modeling.
Lack of precision in attenuation factors or
modeling requires use of very conservative
assumptions. Henry’s law must be corrected
for groundwater temperature.
Soil gas
Prediction of indoor air concentrations
based on attenuation factor or site-
specific modeling.
Fewer pathway assumptions than for
groundwater. More difficult to assure
accuracy and representativeness of
measurements.
Sub-slab soil gas
Prediction of indoor air concentrations
based on attenuation factor or site-
specific modeling. Attenuation factor
may be measured using a naturally
occurring tracer such as radon.
Fewest pathway assumptions. Intrusive.
Attenuation factors may still be conservative
for some buildings.
Indoor air
Direct measurement of indoor air
concentrations
Intrusive. Background sources may make it
difficult to determine contribution of vapor
intrusion. There may be seasonal variations
in concentrations.
\*In all cases the estimated indoor air concentration is used in a health risk assessment to determine whether a vapor intrusion
9345
condition exists.
9346
9347
If groundwater or soil gas data are collected (e.g., in the case of new construction), and if concentrations
9348
exceed generic screening levels, modeling is typically used to estimate indoor air concentrations. The
9349
USEPA spreadsheet version of the Johnson and Ettinger model is most commonly used (see USEPA
9350
2004). 57 This version includes a component programmed by EPA that calculates human health risk
9351
based on the estimated indoor concentrations. The Johnson and Ettinger model is based on certain
9352
assumptions that may not always be appropriate (ITRC 2007):
9353
o Steady state conditions
9354
o An infinite source of contamination
9355
o Uniform air mixing in the building
9356
o No preferential pathways
9357
o No biodegradation of contaminants in the vadose zone
9358
o Homogeneous lateral distribution of contaminants beneath the building
9359
o Entry of contaminant vapors primarily through cracks in the foundation and walls
9360
o Buildings constructed on slabs or with basements
9361
o Constant ventilation rates and soil gas flow into the building
9362
9363
According to ITRC (2007), “the … model is most suitable under homogeneous site conditions
9364
with uniform building construction features. Conversely, the model is weakest under variable
9365
reducing barriers to, and speeding interstate deployment of better, more cost-effective, innovative environmental techniques”
(ITRC 2007).
57 Use of any model should be approved by the relevant regulatory agency. According to ITRC, regulatory agencies typically
customize the USEPA spreadsheet by changing some default values and limiting which parameters can be changed with site-
specific data.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 315
Return to TOP
conditions and cannot evaluate significant preferential pathways; wet basements; substantial
9366
lateral transport of soil gas; tall buildings in cold climates; large buildings with potential
9367
localized sources of vapors beneath them; and shallow, fractured-bedrock conditions. Each of
9368
these conditions has the potential to significantly increase the rate of vapor intrusion beyond the
9369
model’s predictions…” Obviously the model should only be used when the assumptions implicit
9370
in it reasonably match the real world conditions being modeled.
9371
9372
When indoor air samples are collected rather than groundwater or soil gas samples, determining the
9373
contribution due to vapor intrusion can be difficult because many of the chemicals of concern have other
9374
sources in building materials, heating fuels, consumer products, ambient outdoor air and so on, and
9375
because regulatory screening levels are close to background levels for many compounds. ITRC (2007)
9376
therefore recommends that (a) indoor air sampling be conducted only after identifying the buildings
9377
most likely to be affected, to minimize false positives, (b) background sources be eliminated prior to
9378
sampling whenever possible (e.g., cigarette smoke and recently dry cleaned clothes), and (c) outdoor
9379
ambient samples be taken whenever indoor samples are collected.
9380
9381
The conclusion of a Tier 3 assessment may be that a vapor intrusion condition exists, that no vapor
9382
intrusion condition exists, or that there is not enough information to reach a clear conclusion, in which
9383
case a potential vapor intrusion condition must still be presumed to exist.
9384
9385
Synergies and Conflicts
9386
Many vapor intrusion mitigation techniques are relevant to other IAQ issues (Table 3.3-D).
9387
9388

Notes:
1. Although the general features of an active soil depressurization system are the same for radon and vapors from subsurface
contaminants, specific requirements may be different. For a site where both vapor intrusion and radon are concerns, the more
stringent requirements should be followed.
2. Some situations (e.g., natatoriums) require neutral or negative building pressurization to minimize moisture transfer from the
building into wall cavities. In these circumstances an active soil depressurization system must maintain the sub-slab at a lower
pressure than the adjacent neutral or negative building areas, and a building pressurization system would not be suitable for
vapor intrusion mitigation.
9391
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 316
Return to TOP
Remediation and Mitigation Options
9392
9393
Source Removal or Treatment
9394
In the long term, the best remedy for contaminated sites is to remove the contaminant source or to
9395
perform treatments in place to reduce contaminant levels. Remediation technologies include such
9396
options as excavating and removing contaminated soil, pumping and treating contaminated groundwater,
9397
in situ chemical oxidation, in situ bioremediation and soil vapor extraction and treatment. These
9398
measures are outside the scope of this guide.
9399
9400
Removal of contaminated soil and soil vapor extraction have the potential to reduce or eliminate soil gas
9401
migration substantially in a relatively short time, rendering building mitigation measures unnecessary
9402
(ITRC 2007). Some other technologies, such as groundwater pumping and treating, are longer term
9403
strategies that may take many years to reduce the contaminant source to a level where vapor intrusion is
9404
no longer a concern, as at the Endicott, NY site described earlier. Where these longer term clean-up
9405
strategies are used, institutional controls and/or building mitigation are necessary as interim strategies
9406
until clean-up is complete. In still other cases, cleanup may be on hold pending legal decisions
9407
regarding responsibility, availability of cleanup funds, or completion of investigations, feasibility studies
9408
and remediation designs. In these cases, too, institutional controls and/or building mitigation are
9409
necessary as interim strategies.
9410
9411
Institutional Controls
9412
Institutional controls are “legally enforceable conditions placed on a property to reduce the likelihood of
9413
exposure to unacceptable levels of contaminants…” (ASTM 2008). They may include such measures as
9414
zoning restrictions, prohibitions on excavation, advisories about groundwater contamination,
9415
requirements that vapor intrusion mitigation systems be preemptively installed in new construction,
9416
requirements that source remediation systems or building mitigation systems be periodically inspected
9417
and/or requirements that contaminant levels be periodically monitored (ITRC 2007).
9418
9419
Establishment of institutional controls is outside the scope of this guide, but the design team should
9420
assure that they are aware of and fully understand any institutional controls that may be in place on the
9421
property where a project is undertaken. In some circumstances an owner or developer could seek to
9422
incorporate institutional controls as an alternative to mitigation. For example, use restrictions that
9423
would assure that a facility could not be used as a day care or school could allow higher action levels to
9424
be applied and possibly eliminate the need for a mitigation system.
9425
9426
Building Mitigation
9427
Vapor intrusion mitigation systems should be designed and installed by parties who meet the
9428
qualifications of the regulatory agency having jurisdiction and who have relevant expertise. Very few
9429
design teams or contractors have this expertise in-house, so most rely on specialized environmental
9430
consultants and mitigation contractors. Since vapor intrusion is a relatively new issue, the developer
9431
should review consultants’ and contractors’ experience carefully to assure that they will be able to
9432
provide effective solutions. Regulatory agencies may be able to provide information regarding
9433
environmental consultants and contractors who have worked on vapor intrusion within their jurisdiction.
9434
9435
Requirements for design, installation and performance testing of vapor intrusion mitigation systems vary
9436
widely by jurisdiction. It is therefore important to involve the appropriate regulatory agency early in the
9437
design process.
9438
9439
Where the need for vapor intrusion mitigation is a possibility, it is cheaper and more effective to
9440
incorporate the system in the original design and construction, rather than retrofit it after the building is
9441
built and vapor concentrations are tested. The need for vapor intrusion control should be considered
9442
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 317
Return to TOP
early in design. During conceptual design, it may be possible to reduce the potential for vapor intrusion
9443
by changing the placement of a building on a property, changing the location of the portions of a
9444
building having basements, locations of elevator shafts, and the like (ITRC 2007). Later design
9445
decisions may affect the cost of vapor intrusion mitigation systems or preclude some mitigation options.
9446
For example, the type and design of load bearing elements may affect the number of suction pits
9447
required.
9448
9449
ITRC (2007) summarizes vapor intrusion mitigation technologies (Tables 3.3-E and 3.3-F).58 The
9450
methods used are largely adapted from radon control strategies described and tested by USEPA (USEPA
9451
1993, 1994a, 1994b, 1995b) and are therefore similar to those described to Strategy 3.2 Control Entry of
9452
Radon. The discussion in this section mainly highlights differences in application to vapor intrusion, so
9453
the section on radon should also be reviewed in designing vapor intrusion mitigation systems.
9454
9455
Bringing indoor vapor concentrations below action levels commonly requires reductions of two or three
9456
orders of magnitude (and sometimes more), in contrast to the one to two orders of magnitude typically
9457
required to bring radon concentrations below action levels. To achieve these larger reductions, it may be
9458
necessary to combine several mitigation techniques or to design and install systems to exacting
9459
standards.
9460
9461
Active soil depressurization (sub-slab or sub-membrane) is generally the most reliable vapor intrusion
9462
control technology. ASD systems should be combined with sealing of joints, cracks and penetrations,
9463
including sealing of below grade walls. In new construction, ASD is typically combined with a venting
9464
layer and gas vapor barrier to further increase its effectiveness, potentially allowing the number of
9465
suction pits/vents/fans to be reduced or allowing greater concentration reductions to be achieved with a
9466
given number of pits/vents/fans. Where the amount of vapor intrusion that will occur is uncertain, it can
9467
make sense to install a passive venting system with gas vapor barrier (similar to ASD but without the
9468
fan) and add the fan(s) to upgrade to ASD if post-construction testing shows it to be necessary. Passive
9469
systems should always be designed to be readily converted to active systems. In general, use of barriers
9470
alone without passive venting is not recommended.
9471
9472
Building pressurization may be the best choice for sites with wet or low permeability soils where ASD is
9473
not effective. It may also be a viable alternative to ASD for other buildings if the HVAC system has the
9474
capability to reliably maintain positive pressures in all ground-contact rooms. However, pressurization
9475
for vapor intrusion control requires a level of quality assurance in design, construction, operation and
9476
maintenance that goes beyond that associated with pressurization control for most other purposes.59 It is
9477
also likely to increase energy use more than an ASD system. In cases where the building owner is not
9478
the party legally responsible for mitigation, the owner may be reluctant to employ the HVAC system for
9479
vapor intrusion mitigation control. For these reasons, building pressurization has not been widely used
9480
as a vapor intrusion mitigation strategy to date (Folkes, pers. comm.. 2008).
9481
9482
Positive sub-slab pressurization, indoor air treatment and “intrinsically safe building design” may be
9483
viable alternative or supplemental mitigation techniques in certain circumstances.
9484
9485
Much of the ITRC (2007) mitigation guidance is based on USEPA research in single family dwellings
9486
and on vapor intrusion experience in single family dwellings. Application of this guidance to
9487
commercial buildings is reasonable, however, since the observed concentrations of contaminants of
9488
58 The Interstate Technology Regulatory Council’s (2007) vapor intrusion guideline was prepared by a team that included
representatives of environmental agencies in 21 states, the EPA, US Army, Navy, and a number of environmental consulting
and industry groups.
59 An exception is clean rooms and other spaces with exacting HVAC system performance requirements.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 318
Return to TOP
concern in commercial buildings often are not greatly different from concentrations in residences
9489
(Folkes, pers. comm. 2008). Some factors can make commercial buildings moderately less susceptible
9490
to vapor intrusion. These include somewhat higher outside air exchange rates in some commercial space
9491
types, somewhat greater indoor air dilution volumes in some cases, a larger footprint relative to the size
9492
of some vapor plumes, some degree of building pressurization in typical commercial designs, and higher
9493
action levels for some commercial space types. The impact of these differences on the suitability of
9494
various mitigation systems is likely to be greater for sites remote from the contaminant source (e.g., over
9495
a groundwater plume some distance away) than for contaminated sites themselves. In the latter case,
9496
where the projected indoor contaminant concentrations in an unmitigated building may be 1000 times
9497
the action level, modest differences in air change rates, etc. are not sufficient to affect the choice of
9498
mitigation techniques. In the former case, where projected concentrations in unmitigated buildings may
9499
be much lower, the normal differences between commercial and residential buildings may in some cases
9500
be sufficient to allow simpler mitigation techniques such as passive venting & barrier systems to
9501
succeed. As noted previously, however, passive systems should always be designed to allow ready
9502
conversion to active systems if necessary.
9503
9504
Note that the mitigation technologies described here are designed to reduce intrusion of contaminants in
9505
the vapor phase. If contaminants enter the building in contaminated groundwater or by capillary
9506
wicking, it is necessary to first solve the problem causing water entry, and then address vapor migration.
9507
9508
Table 3.3-E. Comparison of vapor intrusion mitigation methods (adapted from ITRC 2007).
9509

Typical applications
Challenges
Pros
Cons
Sub-slab
depressurization
(SSD)
• New and existing buildings
• In new construction,
typically combined with
venting layer and gas vapor
barrier to maximize extent
of sub-slab negative

with each suction pit, and

required.

depressurize sumps, drain

foundations, if present
• Performance limited where
soils are wet or have low
permeability.
• Can be used in both
existing and new buildings.
• Most reliable, cost
effective and efficient
technique in majority of
cases.
• Documented effectiveness
in reducing vapor intrusion:
90 to 99% reductions
typical in single family
homes (USEPA 1993),
with reductions of 99.5%
or more possible with well-
designed and constructed
systems (Folkes 2002).
• Adaptable to a wide range
of site conditions and
geology.
• Variations in air
temperature, wind,
atmospheric pressure do
not influence effectiveness.
• Performance can be
monitored with simple
pressure gauges.
• Venting layers and gas
vapor barriers do not need
to be as robust as for
passive systems.
• Requires periodic
maintenance.
• Higher operating and
maintenance cost than passive
systems.
• In existing buildings,
conditions may limit options
for location of suction pits,
risers and fans.
• More difficult to effectively
depressurize large areas with
each fan if soils are wet or
have low permeability.
• Will not work if the venting
layer becomes saturated due to
high water tables or flooding.
• In some jurisdictions and
situations, may require air
emission permit or exhaust gas
control.
Sub-membrane
depressurization
(SMD)
• Crawl spaces
• Dirt or bedrock floors in
existing building
basements
• Sealing to foundation wall,
penetrations
• Potential for damage to
membrane over time by
trades people or
maintenance staff
accessing crawl space
• Similar to SSD
• Most effective mitigation
system for crawl spaces.
Properly installed SMDs
have resulted in
concentration reductions of
up to 99.5% in residences
(Folkes & Kurz 2002).
• Can be combined with SSD
in buildings that have both
slabs or basements and
• Similar to SSD.
• Membranes must be sealed
well at foundation wall,
footings and penetrations to
prevent leaks.
• Membranes can easily be
damaged by persons accessing
crawl space.
• System must be inspected
periodically to confirm that no
significant leakage has

Technology
Typical applications
Challenges
Pros
Cons
crawl spaces
developed due to damage or
loss of seals. Monitoring,
inspection and repair costs
may be higher than for SSD.
• In some jurisdictions and
situations, may require air
emission permit or exhaust gas
control.
Sealing joints,
cracks and
penetrations in
the building
envelope
• Cracks and penetrations in
existing buildings
• Access to all openings for
sealing.
• Lack of permanence.
• Low effectiveness by itself.
• In existing buildings sealing
alone reduces levels only 0 to
50%. (ITRC 2007).
Gas vapor
barriers
• New buildings.
• Crawl spaces and dirt or
bedrock floors in existing
buildings..
• Often combined with
passive venting or
SSD/SMD and with sealing
of joints, cracks,
penetrations, drains etc. in
foundation to increase
effectiveness. Not
generally recommended by
itself.
• Preventing tears and holes.
• Ensuring that seams,
penetrations, etc. are
effectively sealed.
• May not be sufficient by
itself unless only small
reductions in vapor
intrusion rates are needed.
• Some states do not accept
gas vapor barrier alone.
• Lack of standard criteria
for minimum thickness,
puncture resistance, tear
strength of barrier
materials.
• Low to moderate capital
cost.
• No mechanical parts.
• Low operating and
maintenance costs.
• Not applicable to existing
buildings except for crawl
spaces and dirt or bedrock
floors.
• Even small holes, tears or
incomplete sealing of seams or
of edges at footings, walls,
columns or penetrations can
render barrier alone
ineffective.
• Not likely to be effective by
itself.
• In exposed applications (e.g.
crawl spaces) trades people or
maintenance staff may
accidentally penetrate barrier
during building maintenance
work.
• High concentrations of some
chemicals could potentially
degrade membrane or seam
seals.
Passive venting
• New buildings.
• Sites with low soil gas flux.
• Should be combined with
gas vapor barrier having no
significant leaks.
• Should be designed to
enable conversion to active
system if passive system
performance is inadequate.
• Relies on air flow induced
by wind and stack effects,
which do not assure
venting at all times.
• Air flow rates and suction
usually much less than
achieved by active systems.
• Performance limited where
soils are wet or have low
permeability.
• Can be applied where
vapor intrusion is a
possibility but not yet
certain to occur (e.g., new
construction), and
converted to an active SSD
system later if determined
necessary.
• Low operating and
maintenance costs.
• Not as effective as active SSD.
EPA (1993) found variable
performance for passive
systems in new construction in
single family homes, with
maximum reductions no
greater than 90%.
• Upgrade to active SSD likely
to be required even in new
construction if large
concentration reductions are
needed.
• Not suitable for existing
buildings unless the required
reduction in contaminant
concentrations is fairly small.
EPA (1993) found reductions
of 30 to 70% from passive
systems in existing single
family homes..
• Wind and stack effect do not
assure venting at all times.
• Will not work if venting layer
becomes saturated due to high
water tables or flooding.
Building
pressurization
• New or existing large
commercial buildings.
• Buildings with sensitive
occupants.
• Requires regular air
balancing [and/or
automated control] and
maintenance.
• Will not maintain positive
pressure when air handlers
are off.
• Applicable to both new and
existing buildings.
• May be the most effective
technology at preventing
vapor intrusion.
• May be the most cost-
effective option if existing
• Generally more costly than
other techniques if existing
system is not already capable
of pressurization.
• Incremental energy cost can
be substantial.. Automated
control may minimize energy
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 320
Return to TOP
Technology
Typical applications
Challenges
Pros
Cons
• Can increase energy costs
due to need to bring in
sufficient outside air to
assure pressurization.
HVAC system is capable
of maintaining positive
pressures.
• Can be implemented in
buildings with wet or very
low permeability soils that
prevent sub-slab
depressurization.
costs.
• Typically keeps building
pressurized only during
occupied hours. Build-up of
contaminants when HVAC
system is off and length of
time elevated concentrations
persist after system comes on
must be evaluated in
determining effectiveness.
• Regular maintenance and air
balancing required to assure
that positive pressure is
reliably maintained. Pressure
tests and monitoring should be
incorporated into the design to
assure sufficient positive
pressure in all areas of
building potentially subject to
vapor intrusion.
• Effective only if building is
relatively tight (e.g., not in
warehouses with large loading
dock doors).
Indoor air
treatment
• Specialized cases only.
• Can be effective when
combined with other
techniques to achieve
required concentration
reductions in problem
rooms.
• Can be difficult to
effectively capture
contaminants.
• Energy intensive.
Significant operating,
maintenance and
monitoring burden.
• Typically generates waste
disposal stream.
• Removes and disposes of
contaminants rather than
simply redirecting them
outside building
• Less susceptible to
malfunction or leaks than
most other technologies
• Can be implemented in
buildings with wet or very
low permeability soils that
prevent sub-slab
depressurization.
• Less effective than other
control methods, where they
are applicable
• Expensive compared to other
methods: Costly to install (see
next column) and to operate.
Annual operating expenses of
$15 to $20,000 not uncommon
(ITRC 2003).
• Very maintenance-intensive.
• Collects contaminant vapors
within the building, so is
dependent on uninterrupted
performance to protect
occupants.
• If system leaks occur, they
may result in higher exposures
than if there were no control.
Sub-slab
pressurization
• Same as SSD.
• Most applicable to highly
permeable soils
• Less effective than SSD
• Higher energy costs than
SSD
• Potential for short-
circuiting through cracks
• Does not require soil gas to
be collected within the
building.
• May be more efficient than
SSD in highly permeable
soils
• Generally less effective than
SSD.
• Uses more energy than SSD.
• Cracks or penetrations can
allow air forced into subslab to
re-enter building, carrying
contaminants May not be
appropriate for tight soils
9510
Table 3.3-F Key elements of vapor intrusion mitigation systems (ITRC 2007).
9511
Sub-slab depressurization
• Most widely applied and effective systems for vapor intrusion control
• Applicable to new and existing construction
• One or two suction pits adequate in most existing single-family homes
• Typically combined with venting layer and passive barrier in new construction
• Performance may be limited by low permeability subsoils
• May be combined with drain tile or block wall depressurization
Sub-membrane
depressurization
• Most widely applied and effective systems for crawl spaces
• Applicable to new and existing construction
• Suction field extension (e.g., perforated pipe) may be required for tight soils
• Liners should be sealed to foundation walls and footings
• Liners should be protected against damage where access (e.g., to service furnaces or
plumbing) is expected
• Performance may be limited by low-permeability subsoils
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 321
Return to TOP
• May be combined with SSD, drain tile, and/or block wall systems
Passive barrier [alone]
• Do not expect complete elimination of vapors
• Select barriers that are thick enough to withstand normal construction abuse
• Include thorough quality control procedures to minimize barrier damage
• Inspect barrier seals at all edges, penetrations, and seams
• Test barrier integrity and performance after installation
• Have contingencies to enhance passive barriers [with venting or other measures] if not
adequate
Passive venting
• Generally not adequate for existing structures (except crawl spaces)
• Do not expect complete elimination of vapors
• Should be combined with sound passive barrier
• Venting layer must be highly permeable with distributed collection pipe system
• Allow for addition of fan if necessary to meet performance objectives
• Test system integrity and performance after installation
Building pressurization
• Generally practicable only in commercial buildings
• Requires relatively “tight” buildings to limit airflow and energy costs
• Only the bottom floor (e.g., basement) requires pressurization
• May be cost-effective if existing HVAC equipment can be used without significant
modifications
• Energy costs typically increased due to higher replacement-air flow rates
Sub-slab pressurization
• Generally less effective than SSD systems
• May be appropriate in high-permeability subsoils
• Higher energy costs than SSD systems
9512
Active Soil Depressurization
9513
Active soil depressurization (ASD) systems (sub-slab depressurization (SSD) and sub-membrane
9514
depressurization (SMD)) are the most widely used, versatile and effective systems to reduce vapor
9515
intrusion.
9516
9517
Folkes (2002) and Folkes and Kurz (2002) documented reductions in indoor vapor concentrations of
9518
99.5% or more from carefully designed and installed ASD systems in residences in Redfield, Colorado.
9519
These systems were installed in existing buildings and so did not include gas vapor barriers or sub-slab
9520
venting layers (e.g. gravel), but they did include sealing of accessible cracks and penetrations. About
9521
three quarters of the SSD systems and two-thirds of the SMD systems were able to bring concentrations
9522
below action levels for the contaminant of concern without modifications to the conventional radon
9523
mitigation system design. The remaining SSD systems required such modifications as enlargement of
9524
the suction pit, additional suction pits, and/or replacement of the fan with a larger fan. The remaining
9525
SMD systems required such modifications as sealing of small remaining gaps between the liner and
9526
foundation wall, addition of more perforated pipe to extend the suction field under the liner, and/or
9527
installation of a larger fan.
9528
9529
ASD systems for radon mitigation in commercial buildings are described in the section titled Strategy
9530
3.2 Control Entry of Radon, which should be consulted in designing ASD systems for mitigation of
9531
vapor intrusion.
9532
9533
In new construction, use of venting layers and gas vapor barriers can increase the effectiveness of ASD
9534
systems, potentially allowing the number of suction pits/vents/fans to be reduced or allowing a higher
9535
level of performance to be achieved with a given number of pits/vents/fans.60 A highly permeable
9536
venting layer facilitates lateral movement of soil gases to collection points. When a gas vapor barrier
9537
(see below) is used, the venting layer should consist of rounded materials that will minimize the risk of
9538
puncturing the barrier. ITRC (2007) recommends sand or fine rounded gravel (pea gravel). These
9539
60 Gas vapor barriers are also used as part of sub-membrane depressurization systems in crawl spaces in existing buildings.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 322
Return to TOP
materials are finer than the #5 aggregate recommended in the section on radon mitigation and may have
9540
lower permeability, preventing suction pits from depressurizing as large an area. ITRC notes that
9541
permeable non-woven geotextiles or geogrids can be used for the venting layer as long as care is taken
9542
to avoid clogging the pores with fine-grained soils or concrete.
9543
9544
Sealing of Vapor Intrusion Routes
9545
In existing buildings, ASD systems should be combined with sealing of joints, cracks and penetrations,
9546
including sealing of below grade walls. Sealing recommendations are described in the section
9547
titled Strategy 3.2 Control Entry of Radon, which should be consulted in designing ASD systems for
9548
mitigation of vapor intrusion.
9549
9550
Gas Vapor Barriers
9551
Gas vapor barriers are materials placed below a building to physically block the intrusion of vapors.
9552
They can increase the efficiency of ASD systems, reducing the number of collection points, vent risers
9553
and fans required, or providing greater concentration reductions for a given number of collection points.
9554
Gas vapor barriers should be used together with ASD systems in new construction where vapor intrusion
9555
is a concern and in crawl spaces in existing construction. They are unlikely to be effective by
9556
themselves unless only small reductions in vapor intrusion rates are needed, so their use alone is not
9557
generally recommended (ITRC 2007).
9558
9559
ITRC (2007) recommends use of thick (e.g., ¼-inch) layers of spray-on rubberized asphalt emulsions or
9560
thick sheet membranes (e.g., 60–100 mil high-density polyethylene [HDPE] or similar materials). They
9561
recommend use of cushioning materials above and/or below the membrane, such as geotextiles, sand, or
9562
fine rounded gravel (pea gravel) to minimize the risk of puncturing the barrier and rendering it
9563
ineffective. ITRC cautions against use of thin polyethylene films or moderately thick (e.g., 10–20 mil)
9564
polyvinyl chloride membranes below concrete slabs because experience has shown that they are too
9565
easily damaged during construction. They further recommend that the chemicals of concern at a given
9566
site be evaluated to assess whether they could degrade the integrity of the gas vapor barrier material or
9567
seals.
9568
9569
To create a gas-tight system, sheet membranes must be welded at the seams and at penetration points.
9570
Penetration boots, attachment bars and fasteners are needed to attach the liner to penetrations. Spray-
9571
applied membranes should be sprayed onto a base carrier (e.g. geotextile fabric) having at least a 6 inch
9572
overlap at seams. A cant strip must be installed around the base of each penetration, and the membrane
9573
sprayed at least 3 inches onto the penetrating components (piping, wiring, footings, posts, etc.).
9574
9575
If the barrier is to be effective, the project must include carefully planned and executed quality control
9576
procedures. These procedures must assure that the membrane is not damaged during installation and
9577
verify that it is carefully sealed at penetrations, seams and edges. They must assure that it is not
9578
damaged during placement of the overlying rebar and concrete or other construction activities. They
9579
must include testing of performance after the slab is placed, for example by smoke tests, pressure tests
9580
or measurement of indoor vapor concentrations.
9581
9582
The thick barrier and cushioning recommended for vapor intrusion go beyond the recommendations for
9583
the subslab vapor retarder in radon mitigation systems (see Strategy 3.2 Control Entry of Radon). The
9584
retarder recommended there is intended only to exclude wet concrete from the venting layer (aggregate),
9585
not to serve as a barrier against soil gas flow.
9586
9587
Passive Venting (no Fan) with Barrier as a Provisional Strategy
9588
Passive venting consists of a venting layer below the slab and a passive vent stack to exhaust the soil gas.
9589
The primary driving forces for passive venting are wind and stack effect, and these may be low, zero or
9590
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 323
Return to TOP
reversed under some weather conditions, so a passive venting system should be combined with a highly
9591
effective gas vapor barrier with no significant leaks (ITRC 2007).
9592
9593
Passive venting is not as effective as active systems and may not be adequate where large reductions in
9594
indoor vapor concentrations are needed. Its first cost is generally higher than that of active systems for
9595
roughly comparable performance because of the need for a more highly permeable venting layer, more
9596
suction pits, a network of soil gas collection piping and a highly effective gas vapor barrier. Moreover,
9597
some jurisdictions may not allow the use of passive systems. For all these reasons, it may be preferable
9598
to proceed directly to an active system. However, in new construction where the amount of vapor
9599
intrusion that will occur is uncertain, it can make sense to install a passive system as a provisional
9600
strategy, and add the fan(s) required to upgrade to an active system if testing shows it to be necessary.
9601
9602
Testing methods may include, for example, measurement of subslab vapor concentrations and estimation
9603
of indoor concentrations based on conservative attenuation factors, direct measurement of indoor vapor
9604
concentrations, adjusted for background concentrations, or injection of tracer gases into the venting
9605
layer and measurement of their concentrations inside the building.
9606
9607
Building Pressurization
9608
Building pressurization may be an efficient and cost-effective alternative to SSD for buildings where the
9609
HVAC system has the capability to reliably maintain positive pressures in all ground-contact rooms.
9610
This is more likely to be the case in commercial and institutional buildings than in the residences. EPA
9611
(1994) reports that only small (<0.001 in. w.g.) positive pressures are required to prevent vapor intrusion.
9612
Building pressurization can also be used in some cases where ASD is not applicable, such as where wet
9613
or low permeability soils prevent ASD from creating large depressurization fields below the slab.
9614
9615
The section on Strategy 2.3 Maintain Proper Building Pressurization provides design guidance for
9616
building pressurization. Several additional considerations must be taken into account when using
9617
building pressurization to mitigate vapor intrusion:
9618
• HVAC systems can only maintain positive pressure when they are running and when outside air is
9619
being provided. When they are off (e.g., nights and weekends) or when the outside air dampers are
9620
closed, vapor intrusion may occur and allow contaminant concentrations to rise in indoor air.
9621
HVAC systems must be started and outdoor air introduced long enough before occupancy to dilute
9622
accumulated contaminants, including those that may have sorbed onto building and furnishing
9623
surfaces. The time required to accomplish this must be evaluated on a building-specific basis.
9624
• Some contaminants of concern may have very low action levels, requiring more consistent and
9625
reliable pressure control than is needed to meet other objectives (such as control of condensation in
9626
building cavities, control of outdoor air contaminants, or control of drafts for occupant comfort).
9627
• Buildings and systems must be designed, balanced, tested, operated and maintained to provide
9628
positive pressure in all ground contact rooms under all weather conditions and building operating
9629
conditions. Particular issues of concern may include, among others:
9630
o Stack effect in cold climates, including the potential need to compartmentalize the
9631
ground floor from higher floors and/or provide separate HVAC systems to enable
9632
efficient pressurization;
9633
o Wind effect;
9634
o Balancing and control of outdoor air flows, relief flows, exhaust fan flows, and gravity
9635
relief flows,
9636
o Adequate allowance for building envelope leakage in determining the excess of outdoor
9637
air flow over exhaust flow required for pressurization,
9638
o Inability to pressurize buildings with large leakages, such as warehouses with large doors
9639
that are frequently open,
9640
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 324
Return to TOP
o Zone level balancing of supply, return and exhaust in ground-contact rooms, including
9641
zones (such as commercial kitchens) with large exhaust flows,
9642
o Variations in outdoor air flow and overall or zone-level building pressurization due to
9643
demand controlled ventilation, variable volume operation or other factors;
9644
o Proper and representative location of the indoor and outdoor taps for building pressure
9645
sensors, pressure sensor calibration and drift;
9646
o Negative pressures in return ducts, tunnels, plenums and mechanical rooms in contact
9647
with the ground;
9648
o The possibility of elevators “pumping” soil gas into the building.
9649
9650
• It is critical that the design intent to use building pressurization to control vapor intrusion be clearly
9651
documented in the operating and maintenance manuals and addressed in operator training. Many
9652
future changes have the potential to undermine the effectiveness of this vapor intrusion mitigation
9653
strategy, including operator reductions in outside air volumes or shortening of morning purge times
9654
to reduce energy costs, addition of demand-controlled ventilation, addition of exhausts, tenant
9655
turnover and fit-out, rebalancing by operators or contractors unfamiliar with the system design intent,
9656
and other actions. Because building pressurization is provided by the HVAC system, it may be more
9657
vulnerable to performance degradation by future O&M and remodeling changes than a dedicated
9658
vapor intrusion mitigation system such as an ASD system.
9659
• Use of building pressurization to control vapor intrusion may entail higher equipment costs and
9660
energy costs to condition sufficient minimum outside air volumes and to purge contaminants each
9661
day before the first occupants arrive.
9662
• Building pressurization could deflect contaminated soil gas into adjacent, unprotected spaces, such
9663
as other tenant spaces within a mall building, a situation which should be avoided..
9664
9665
Other Approaches
9666
Sub-Slab Pressurization
9667
In highly permeable soils where SSD systems are unable to adequately depressurize the area below the
9668
slab, use of positive sub-slab pressurization (SSP) may be more effective (EPA 1993). In SSP, air
9669
(usually from indoors) is forced into the venting layer or the soil below the slab rather than being drawn
9670
out. This creates a zone of slightly elevated pressure, causing the vapor-laden soil gases below to flow
9671
to the sides of the building where they are released into the ambient air. SSP systems use more energy
9672
than SSD systems. Moreover, cracks and penetrations in the slab or foundation wall may allow the air
9673
injected into the subslab to re-enter the building, potentially carrying vapors with it.
9674
9675
Where SSP appears to be the appropriate choice, the injected air should be filtered to prevent clogging
9676
of the system. Pits should be provided at the injection point(s) (EPA 1993).
9677
9678
Indoor Air Treatment
9679
Indoor air treatment is not widely used to reduce concentrations of intruding vapors because it is both
9680
less effective than other methods and more costly to install and operate. However, it can be useful to
9681
augment other mitigation technologies to further reduce concentrations in “problem rooms.”
9682
9683
The section on Strategy 7.4 Provide Particle Filtration and Gas Phase Air Cleaning Consistent with
9684
Project IAQ Objectives provides further information on sorbent media, filter cartridge types, efficiencies,
9685
capacities, pressure drop and selection criteria.
9686
9687
Intrinsically Safe Building Design
9688
ASTM Standard E 2600-08 suggests that vapor intrusion can be avoided through “intrinsically safe
9689
building design,” such as use of open air parking or well ventilated underground parking between the
9690
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 325
Return to TOP
ground and the first occupied floor. ITRC (2007) does not discuss this alternative. Where this approach
9691
is consistent with the overall architectural design goals of a project, the effectiveness should be carefully
9692
evaluated. Considerations include, among others:
9693
9694
• The potential for elevator shafts serving underground parking garages or open air parking levels to
9695
provide a route for intrusion of vapors into the occupied floors,
9696
• The potential for other unintended air flow paths (e.g., around column enclosures) to allow vapors to
9697
be drawn into occupied floors,
9698
• The potential for original or retrofit CO control of parking exhaust to radically reduce garage
9699
ventilation and vapor dilution,
9700
• The potential for stack effect to overwhelm underground parking exhaust,
9701
• The potential for underground parking exhaust to increase soil gas flux if not properly balanced by
9702
makeup air.
9703
9704
Effect of Chemicals of Concern on Mitigation System Design
9705
According it ITRC (2007), the particular chemicals of concern at a given site affect system design in
9706
three ways:
9707
1. Potentially combustible vapors require intrinsically safe blowers and monitoring or alarm
9708
systems.
9709
2. Some vapors can degrade barrier membranes, pipes or the solvents used to join pipes or seal
9710
membrane seams.
9711
3. Contaminant action levels affect the concentration reductions required and the mitigation
9712
technologies likely to be successful. Passive systems may be able to achieve reductions or 80%
9713
or less, but higher reductions will usually require ASD or pressurization systems (USEPA 1993).
9714
Standard designs for radon control, if carefully executed, are generally sufficient for reductions
9715
of up to 95%, while enhancements may be required to achieve greater reductions (Folkes and
9716
Kurz 2002).
9717
9718
QA of Vapor Intrusion Mitigation Systems
9719
Most states that have regulations or policies related to vapor intrusion require at least one post-
9720
installation test to verify that mitigation systems meet performance objectives. In some cases indoor air
9721
testing is not required, with post-mitigation testing of the suction field produced by ASD systems
9722
considered sufficient to demonstrate their performance. Tests typically must be performed by a
9723
qualified environmental consultant.
9724
9725
The environmental consultant is typically also responsible for design verification, submittal review and
9726
construction observation related to the mitigation system. The regulatory agency may also participate in
9727
these activities. It is critical that rebar and concrete contractors and the general contractor understand
9728
the design intent of any gas vapor barrier, the importance of maintaining its integrity, and procedures for
9729
notification of the environmental consultant or barrier contractor if the barrier is punctured.
9730
9731
If concentrations exceed action levels, performance of a passive system may be improved by adding a
9732
fan to convert it to ASD. Performance of ASD systems may be improved by increasing fan size,
9733
enlarging suction pits, installing additional suction pits and associated venting, performing additional
9734
sealing, or other strategies.
9735
9736
O&M and Monitoring of Vapor Intrusion Mitigation Systems
9737
Most states that have regulations or policies related to vapor intrusion require a formal management plan
9738
to assure that the mitigation system will be operated and maintained in a manner that provides ongoing
9739
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 326
Return to TOP
effective control of vapor intrusion. The management plan is a form of institutional control. Typically,
9740
O&M is the responsibility of the party responsible for the contamination.
9741
9742
Many states also require follow-up testing of indoor air and/or system indicators (e.g. suction pressures)
9743
at some regular interval until such time as the mitigation system is no longer necessary. Regulators may
9744
allow the frequency of testing to be reduced over time if tests show that performance is consistently
9745
maintained. The testing requirement is also a form of institutional control.
9746
9747
Once long-term cleanup efforts reduce contamination to a degree that reduces vapor intrusion below
9748
action levels, systems can be deactivated with the approval of the regulatory agency, though some
9749
owners may choose to continue their operation.
9750
9751
Glossary
9752
Brownfields: Section 101 of CERCLA defines brownfields as “real property, the expansion,
9753
redevelopment, or reuse of which may be complicated by the presence or potential presence of a
9754
hazardous substance, pollutant, or contaminant” and estimates that there are more than 450,000
9755
brownfields in the U.S.
9756
(http://www.epa.gov/swerosps/bf/index.html, http://www.epa.gov/swerosps/bf/about.htm)
9757
9758
CERCLA (42 U.S.C. 9601 et seq.): Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation, and
9759
Liability Act (CERCLA), enacted in 1980 and amended by the Superfund Amendments and
9760
Reauthorization Act of 1986, the Asset Conservation, Lender Liability, and Deposit Insurance
9761
Protection Act of 1996 and the Small Business Liability Relief and Brownfields Revitalization Act of
9762
2001. This Act is commonly known as the Superfund Act. CERCLA “gives the Federal government
9763
the authority to respond to chemical emergencies and to clean up uncontrolled or abandoned hazardous
9764
waste sites. The Superfund program addresses both short and long term risks, from toxic chemical spills
9765
and threats to the permanent cleanup and rehabilitation of abandoned hazardous waste sites. Superfund
9766
also provides authority for the prosecution of those responsible for the releases of hazardous waste and a
9767
trust fund to subsidize cleanup when no responsible party can be identified.” As of October 26, 2007
9768
there were 1245 final sites and 66 proposed sites on the National Priorities List where long term
9769
remediations can be conducted under CERCLA
9770
(http://www.epa.gov/ebtpages/cleasuperfund.html; http://www.epa.gov/superfund/policy/cercla.htm; htt
9771
p://www.epa.gov/superfund/sites/query/queryhtm/npltotal.htm ).
9772
9773
RCRA (42 U.S.C.6901 et seq.): Resource Conservation and Recovery Act, enacted in 1976 and
9774
amended by the Hazardous and Solid Waste Amendments of 1984. RCRA regulates the management of
9775
solid waste, hazardous waste and underground storage tanks for petroleum products and certain
9776
chemicals. RCRA solid waste means “any garbage, or refuse, sludge from a wastewater treatment plant,
9777
water supply treatment plant, or air pollution control facility and other discarded material, including
9778
solid, liquid, semi-solid, or contained gaseous material resulting from industrial, commercial, mining,
9779
and agricultural operations, and from community activities.” RCRA hazardous waste includes waste
9780
that is ignitable, corrosive, reactive (e.g., explosive) or contains certain amounts of toxic chemicals.
9781
EPA has developed a list of more than 500 specific hazardous wastes. Hazardous wastes are generated
9782
by businesses such as dry cleaners, automobile repair shops, hospitals, exterminators, photo processing
9783
centers, chemical manufacturers, electroplating companies and oil refineries
9784
(http://www.epa.gov/epaoswer/osw/basifact.htm#solidwaste; http://www.epa.gov/epaoswer/hazwaste/da
9785
ta/index.htm#rcra-info ). Information about hazardous waste handlers including generators, transporters,
9786
treatment, storage and disposal facilities and others is available from EPA’s RCRAInfo database through
9787
the Hazardous Waste Query Form (http://www.epa.gov/enviro/html/rcris/rcris\_query\_java.html )
9788
9789
Superfund. See CERCLA.
9790
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 327
Return to TOP
9791
Underground storage tank: EPA defines an underground storage tank system (UST) as “a tank and any
9792
underground piping connected to the tank that has at least 10 percent of its combined volume
9793
underground.” Subtitle I of RCRA required EPA to adopt regulate USTs and to require owners and
9794
operators to prevent, detect and clean up releases. The statute and regulations apply only to USTs
9795
storing either petroleum or hazardous substances as defined in CERCLA.
9796
9797
Vadose zone: The zone between the land surface and the regional water table. The vadose zone is
9798
generally unsaturated, with pores containing a mixture of water and soil gas, except for the capillary
9799
fringe above the water table and any perched water tables within the vadose zone.
9800
9801

9971
MECHANICAL SYSTEMS

9979

9980

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

10002
Strategy 4.1 – Control Moisture and Dirt in Air Handling Systems
10003
10004
10005
Overview
10006
Moisture can enter HVAC systems from varied sources including (a) snow and rain intrusion through
10007
outdoor air inlets; (b) water leaks into ducts and (c) humidifiers located in HVAC systems. Temperature
10008
changes in HVAC equipment associated with the air-conditioning process result in the production of
10009
liquid water in the coil section and moisture saturated air and damp surfaces in supply air ducts
10010
downstream of cooling coils. In some HVAC systems, moisture is intentionally added to the air stream
10011
by humidifiers located in the air-handling units or in the air supply ductwork.
10012
10013
Microorganisms including fungi and bacteria can grow in liquid water and damp/moist niches along
10014
airstream surfaces in HVAC systems. Reviews on microbial growth in buildings discuss requirements
10015
for growth including moisture, temperature, and nutrients (1, 2, and 3). As noted above, wet/damp
10016
niches can and do occur in HVAC equipment and while moisture especially in air-conditioning
10017
equipment, can be reduced by good design, elimination of wet/damp air stream surfaces is seldom
10018
possible. Temperature is generally not limiting for microbial growth in HVAC equipment as a wide
10019
variety of fungi and bacteria can grow on wet/damp surfaces at refrigeration temperature (e.g., growth of
10020
the common mold Cladosporium cladosporioides on refrigerator gaskets, reference 3). The third factor
10021
that potentially limits microbial growth in HVAC equipment, namely the presence of nutrients, is often
10022
overlooked by HVAC design professionals. Dust and dirt accretions on surfaces can readily support
10023
microbial growth on wet/damp HVAC materials such as steel even though this structural material alone
10024
is non-biodegradable. Thus, attention to design of a dust or dirt-free HVAC system is important in
10025
limiting fungal and bacterial growth associated with the presence of damp niches in HVAC equipment.
10026
Limiting the amount of liquid water and damp niches in HVAC equipment is also important in control
10027
of fungal and bacterial growth. Because some microorganisms are very proficient in obtaining nutrient
10028
from a wide variety of man-made materials the HVAC designer is encouraged where ever possible to
10029
use non-biodegradable materials in wet or damp HVAC equipment niches.
10030
10031
Outdoor Air Intakes and Outdoor Air Inlet Areaways
10032
There is considerable discussion in Section 5.6.2, 5.6.3, and 5.6.4 of ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 on
10033
rain and snow intrusion into HVAC equipment. It is mentioned in Section 5.6.4 that suitable access
10034
doors shall be required to permit cleaning and presumably to remove snow (melt water) that may
10035
penetrate into the intake. No mention is made of below grade outdoor inlets which in practice, are
10036
accumulation sites for dirt and debris including botanical materials like leaves, which are rich growth
10037
sites for fungi such as Aspergillus fumigatus. Outdoor air entering below grade and grade-level inlets is
10038
often contaminated by various pollutants such as mold spores from decaying leaves, pesticides, and
10039
fertilizers, none of which are mentioned in Table 4-1 of Standard 62.1-2007. Practical actions to protect
10040
outdoor inlets from contamination and to remove water that may enter the inlet include the following:
10041
• Recognize during the design process that below grade and grade level outdoor air inlets are most
10042
susceptible to the entry of landscape pollutants such as leaves, pesticides, and fertilizers.
10043
Provision during design should be made for removal of leaves, dirt, and debris that accumulate
10044
near inlets, within wells associated with below grade inlets, or downstream of the inlet bird
10045
screens. In order to maintain outdoor air inlets and outdoor air intake areaways in a dirt-free
10046
condition, provision during design must be made for adequate access so that maintenance
10047
persons can enter these spaces with vacuum cleaners to remove accumulated dust and debris.
10048
• Materials that are used to construct the airstream surfaces of outdoor air inlet plenums should be
10049
smooth, resistant to corrosion, and readily cleanable.
10050
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 334
Return to TOP
• Outdoor air inlets constructed above grade should be located as far as reasonably possible from
10051
external pollutant sources (see minimal separation distances in Table 5.1 of ASHRAE Standard
10052
62.1-2007).
10053
• Information on design of outdoor air inlets to minimize rain and snow entry is found on pages 5-
10054
16 to 5-18 of the ASHRAE User’s Manual for Standard 62.1-2007.
10055
10056
Filters relating to Microbial Growth in HVAC Equipment
10057
Highly efficient filters provide an important design tool for reducing the amount of fine dust that might
10058
otherwise settle out on airstream surfaces in HVAC equipment. Fine dust that can settle out in HVAC
10059
equipment contains an abundance of carbonaceous-rich components such as lint and textile fragments,
10060
soil and silt particles, skin scales, outdoor botanical (e.g., pollen), and microbial (e.g., phylloplane-leaf
10061
sourced fungi) particles that are nutrients for microbial growth under damp-wet conditions.
10062
10063
It is important to realize that a wet filter is a poor filter. The dust cake of a wet filter provides a nutrient-
10064
rich site for microbial growth. A wet filter thus becomes a microbial growth site.
10065
10066
Design considerations for filters that potentially affect HVAC microbial contamination include:
10067
• Keep filters dry regardless of filter location. Provide for an O&M inspection program to verify
10068
that filters are periodically checked to verify a dry condition.
10069
• Use the most highly efficient filters as possible (see Strategy 7.4 Provide Particle Filtration and
10070
Gas Phase Air Cleaning) to keep fine dusts out of damp or wet HVAC niches such as drain pans
10071
and cooling coil sections.
10072
• Discard wet filters.
10073
10074
Drain Pans and Moisture Carry-Over From Cooling Coils
10075
The purpose of the drain pan beneath the cooling coil section is to collect water that condenses on the
10076
coils and to quickly remove this water from the air-handling unit. Photo 1 provides an example of poor
10077
design and subsequent poor maintenance of an air handling unit drain pan. Water stagnated in the pan,
10078
fine dust accumulated over time in the pan and a thick biofilm (gelatin-like mass containing bacteria,
10079
fungi, and protozoa) grew in the inside metal pan surfaces eventually filling-up much of the pan.
10080
ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 (Section 5.11.1) and Section 10.4.4.3 of the ACGIH Bioaerosols Book
10081
(1999) contain guidance on making drain pans self-draining. Thus, it is important that the drain hole for
10082
the pan be flush with the bottom of the pan. It is important to make certain that when the air-handling
10083
unit is mounted in a mechanical room that allowance be made for mounting the drain line at the very
10084
bottom of the pan. It is equally important to make certain that the trap on the drain line is deep sealed
10085
(see Section 5.11.3 of Standard 62.1-2007 and Section 10.4.4.3 of ACGIH, 1999) so as to allow water
10086
from the pan to drain even under maximum negative pressure created by the fan. Pages 5-27 to 5-28 of
10087
the ASHRAE User’s Manual for Standard 62.1-2007 contains additional design information for drain
10088
pans and deep sealed traps.
10089
10090
It is important during design of the air handling unit to provide the owner/operator with a
10091
maintenance/inspection plan for the drain pan assembly. Key elements of this plan include periodic
10092
inspection of the pan for self-drainage and for biofilm development. If the wet surfaces of the pan feel
10093
gelatin-like or slimy to the touch, this is a certain sign of biofilm development. Physical cleaning of the
10094
pan is required for biofilm removal; biocide treatment is not a substitute for physical cleaning.
10095
10096
Moisture carryover from cooling coils, which can result in wetting of downstream surfaces and
10097
microbial growth on these surfaces, can be minimized by guidance in Section 5.11.4 of Standard 62.1-
10098
2007 and Section 10.4.4.2 of the ACGIH Bioaerosols Book (1999). Thus, if the air velocity is too high
10099
over a part of the coil section, water droplets may wet downstream surfaces.
10100
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 335
Return to TOP
10101
Smooth and Cleanable Surfaces
10102
While microorganisms can grow on smooth but dirty surfaces in HVAC equipment, growth will usually
10103
be greatest on porous airstream surfaces where dust and dirt (nutrient) accumulation is highest. Photo 2
10104
shows that the most dense area of mold growth occurred on porous airstream surfaces downstream of the
10105
cooling coil section.
10106
10107
Designers should consider the following in order to minimize moisture and mold problems near the
10108
plenum housing cooling coils:
10109
• Specify airstream surfaces that are as smooth, readily cleanable, and not subject to corrosion or
10110
deterioration. (See ACGIH 1999, Section 10.4.4.5)
10111
• Design main air supply ducts for easy access for periodic inspection and future cleaning as
10112
necessary.
10113
• Reduce reliance on antimicrobials; emphasize maintenance of a dust-free or minimal condition in
10114
this portion of the HVAC system. Excellent filtration is necessary to minimize dust
10115
accumulation.
10116
10117
While mold growth will occur readily on dusty, porous, airstream surfaces, growth can also occur on
10118
relatively smooth metallic surfaces in air-conditioning ductwork. Photo 3 shows dust and debris present
10119
on the metal airstream surface of an externally insulated supply air duct. Photo 4 shows mold growth
10120
(magnification about 400x) that has occurred on the dust and debris shown in Photo 3. Note the
10121
abundant presence of spores and hyphae in Photo 4. This illustrates the importance of maintaining all
10122
airstream surfaces of HVAC equipment in a dust-free or minimal dust condition, especially in damp-wet
10123
locations.
10124
10125
Residual oils and lubricants found on metal surfaces in newly fabricated ductwork and plenums
10126
accumulate dust deposits (because it is sticky) and which can become a nutrient source for microbial
10127
growth in wet conditions. Residual oils and lubricants should therefore, be removed from metallic
10128
airstream surfaces prior to equipment commissioning.
10129
10130
10131
Figure 4.1-B Some of the porous air stream surface (about 5 ft. downstream from cooling coils) is
10132
heavily colonized by visible mold growth. (Photo courtesy of Phil Morey)
10133
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 336
Return to TOP
10134
10135
10136
Figure 4.1-C Dust and dirt are present on the metallic airstream surface of this externally insulated
10137
supply air duct. (Photo courtesy of Phil Morey)
10138
10139
10140
Figure 1.1-D Mold Growth (spores and hyphae) has taken place on the dust and dirt on the airstream
10141
surfact. Remember, dust and dirt in a damp/wet environment is food (nutrient) for molds! (Photo
10142
courtesy of Phil Morey)
10143
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 337
Return to TOP
10144
Impact of Humidifier Moisture on Airstream Surfaces
10145
Humidifiers, if used in HVAC systems, are usually incorporated into air-handling units or main air
10146
supply ducts. Humidifiers that emit water droplets may draw their water supply directly from a potable
10147
cold water pipe or directly from a sump containing water that is recirculated. The potential for microbial
10148
contamination of humidifier water droplets is directly related to the contamination in the supply water
10149
that is aerosolized (ACGIH,1999; Section 10.4.4.4). It is well known that water droplets aerosolized
10150
from sumps containing recirculated water are heavily colonized by various microorganisms including
10151
actinomycetes, Gram-negative bacteria such as Flavobacterium, and yeasts.
10152
10153
Thus, it is a desirable practice to use humidifiers that work on the principle of aerosolization of water
10154
molecules (absence of carry-over of microbes) instead of water droplets (where microbial components
10155
may be carried over).
10156
10157
If steam is used in humidifier operation, it is important to determine if the steam source contains
10158
corrosion inhibitors. Corrosion inhibitors which may be present in boiler steam can carry-over into
10159
humidifier aerosol, and cause IAQ complaints from building occupants.
10160
10161
Regardless of the kind of humidifier used in an air-handling unit or in supply ducts, it is important
10162
during HVAC design to verify that moisture emitted by the humidifier is completely absorbed by the
10163
ventilation airstream within the absorption distance recommended by the humidifier manufacturer
10164
(ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007; Section 5.13.2). The use of airstream surface materials that are smooth
10165
and easily cleanable is desirable within the moisture absorbing distance recommended by humidifier
10166
manufacturer.
10167
10168
O&M procedures prepared by the designer should include periodic inspection of HVAC components
10169
containing humidifiers. Water present on airstream surfaces near humidifiers in air handling units or in
10170
supply ducts is a certain indication of moisture failure and possible microbial growth. Filters that maybe
10171
present in air handling units near humidifiers should not be wetted by humidifier moisture.
10172
10173
Page 5-31 of the ASHRAE User’s Manual for Standard 62.1-2007 contains additional design
10174
information for humidifiers used in HVAC equipment.
10175
10176
Table 4.1-A Moisture and Dirt in HVAC Resource Guide
10177

Standard 62.1-2007; Section 5.6.4

Properly sized access panels allow removal of water,
dirt, and debris in the outdoor air inlet plenum

Preferable to located above grade at a location
minimally impacted by water and air pollutants
Preventing HVAC Filters From Becoming Wet

Verify that filters cannot be wetted by water from
outdoor sources or sources in the air-handling unit.
ACGIH 1999; Section 10.4.4.1.

maintained in a dry condition.

Standard 62.1-2007; Section 5.11.3
ACGIH 1999; Section 10.4.4.3

conditioning season
Moisture Cary Over from Coiling Coils; Smooth and Cleanable Surface

Standard 62.1-2007; Section 5.11.4 and ACGIH 1999;
Section 10.4.4.2
Accumulation of dust and debris
