reduces accumulation of dust and debris; ACGIH
1999; Section 10.4.4.5
O&M program for airstream surfaces
O&M program should include dust removal from
smooth and non-smooth surfaces especially in
locations downstream from cooling coils
Reliance on antimicrobials
Antimicrobials should be avoided because
microorganisms can grow on dirt accretions that
accumulate on treated surfaces
Residual oils from airstream surfaces
Manufacturers can provide information on the
removal of sticky residual oils from sheet metal
surfaces.
Impact of Humidifier Moisture on Airstream Surfaces\*
Water droplet or water molecule emissions
ACGIH 1999; Section 10.4.4.4
Humidifiers that emit water droplets from a sump
should be avoided
Humidifier water source
Standard 62.1-2007; Section 5.13.1
Boiler steam with corrosion inhibitors should be
avoided.
Complete entrainment of humidifier moisture
Standard 62.1-2007; Section 5.13.2
Smooth air stream surfaces
Preferable to use smooth materials on air stream
surfaces within moisture absorbing distance.
\*See also ASHRAE 62.1 User’s Manual for Standard 62.1-2007.
10178
10179
References
10180
ACGIH, Bioaerosols Assessment And Control, Chapter 10 on Prevention and Control of Microbial
10181
Contamination, 10-1 to 10-13, Cincinnati, (1999).
10182
10183
Microorganisms in Home and Indoor Work Environment, Eds., B. Flannigan, R. Samson and D. Miller,
10184
Taylor & Francis, London 490 pp., (2001).
10185
10186
AIHA, Field Guide for the Determination of Biological Contaminants in Environmental Samples,
10187
Fairfax, VA (2005).
10188
10189
10190
10191
10192
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 339
Return to TOP
10193
Strategy 4.2 – Select and Install Airstream Surfaces that are Resistant to Erosion and
10194
Deposition
10195
10196
Introduction
10197
Duct systems are used to transport and distribute air to and from building spaces. This air is typically
10198
forced through the duct systems by the fans associated with various air handling equipment. Enclosed
10199
duct systems can actually be constructed of any solid surface; but, typically, modern duct systems are
10200
constructed of sheet metal (ferrous metals or aluminum), rigid board made from fiberglass or other fiber
10201
materials, and petroleum based piping materials. Ductwork made from these materials can be either rigid
10202
or flexible. Air can also be distributed through chases, plenums and other cavities of the building
10203
structure itself.
10204
10205
Unfortunately, the surface area of any air distribution system has the potential to collect and then pollute
10206
the traveling airstream with dust, dirt, moisture and microbial contaminants. The design and installation
10207
of any duct or other air transport system should always be done in such a way as to minimize the
10208
potential for contamination. In fact, Section 5.5 of ASHRAE Standard 62.1, Ventilation for Acceptable
10209
Indoor Air Quality, states that “All airstream surfaces in equipment and ducts in the heating, ventilating,
10210
and air-conditioning system shall be designed and constructed” to provide “Resistance to Mold Growth”
10211
and “Resistance to Erosion”.
10212
10213
Duct Surface Materials
10214
The selection of the material from which the air distribution is constructed is, typically, based on
10215
parameters and considerations other than indoor air quality and contaminant control. However, in any
10216
case, the surface area of this material should be as smooth and free of obstructions as possible. Using
10217
ductwork constructed of sheet metal or plastic piping materials is, therefore, recommended since these
10218
materials are inherently smooth and rigid. The internal surfaces of fiberboard duct and the surface areas
10219
of building structure plenums and chases typically are much more rough and contain many more places
10220
where dirt, dust and other polluting materials can collect. Additionally, the use of flexible ducts should
10221
be minimized, or eliminated if possible. Flexible ducts have an irregular interior surface that, again, can
10222
be a trap for unwanted particles and microbes.
10223
10224
Duct systems should be constructed and installed to minimize the areas where particles can accumulate.
10225
Joints and connections should be sealed internally as well as externally. Smooth transition fittings
10226
should be use for all changes in air direction and these all fittings should be installed per the procedures
10227
and guidelines in the latest edition of the Sheet Metal and Air Conditioning Contractors National
10228
Association’s (SMACNA) HVAC Duct Construction Standards and the project design specifications. As
10229
much as possible and feasible round or flat oval duct should be used, since the corners of rectangular
10230
duct can collect dirt and dust.
10231
10232
Duct Liner
10233
Metal, and sometimes plastic, ducts are typically thermally insulated with either an external or internal
10234
insulation. Various types of insulation are used, including fiberglass-based rigid board, fiberglass wrap,
10235
flexible fiberglass-based wraps and closed cell foam insulations. While both internal and external
10236
insulations can provide the necessary thermal protection to minimize heat transfer and prevent
10237
condensation of moisture, the internal duct insulation (better known as duct liner) is often used to also
10238
better control the acoustic noise in a fan and air distribution system. However, installing insulation
10239
internally in a duct system exposes the air stream to an even greater potential for introducing unwanted
10240
particles, fungi and bacteria.
10241
10242
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 340
Return to TOP
The most common duct liners are typically constructed from fiberglass. In theory, if kept clean and dry
10243
and protected from erosion or deterioration of the binder, fiberglass might be an acceptable liner
10244
material. However, the face of duct liner material is typically fibrous and not as smooth as the sheet
10245
metal surface to which it is mounted. Therefore, it is normally not practical to achieve clean and dry duct
10246
liner with a fibrous or rough surface over the life of the building with typical, or even above average,
10247
airstream filtration. Also, in high humidity environments and elsewhere when the duct liner might be
10248
exposed to high humidity, duct liners with fibrous or rough surfaces present a greater potential for mold
10249
growth since the dirt that accumulates on the surface promotes the retention of moisture.
10250
10251
It is, therefore, recommended that the use of duct liner be avoided. The acoustic noise control of an air
10252
distribution system should be completed with a more holistic approach that includes equipment selection,
10253
sizing, and location and proper air distribution design which includes sizing, velocity, fabrication
10254
integrity, and diffuser selection. If necessary, the services of an acoustical engineer should be engaged.
10255
10256
If, however, the use of internal duct liner is required and unavoidable the following procedures and
10257
cautions should be followed:
10258
• Investigate the use of manufactured, double-walled ductwork. While more expensive than
10259
conventional single-layer sheet metal duct, the liner in this product is completely enclosed,
10260
reducing the potential for liner deterioration and the direct exposure of the liner to the airstream.
10261
The inner wall of most double wall ductwork is also perforated which provides a better
10262
acoustical control.
10263
• Where possible, use closed cell foam type insulation for duct liner. While the binding and face of
10264
fiberglass-based duct liners have tremendously improved, there is still a potential for
10265
deterioration that is not present in closed cell insulations. Also, the closed cell typically has a
10266
smoother surface than fiberglass based products, potentially reducing the trapping and
10267
accumulation of particles. Please note, however, that the closed cell non-porous or non-
10268
fibrous liner products do not, typically, have the flammability ratings or the acoustical
10269
performance of the fibrous glass products
10270
• Be sure that the installed liner products are treated with anti-microbial coatings. Many
10271
manufactured duct liners now include anti-bacteria and anti-fungal coatings on the surfaces.
10272
There are also a number of anti-microbial coating products that can be applied in the field.
10273
• Install all liner per the design specifications, the guidelines of SMACNA and the North America
10274
Insulation Manufacturers Association (NAIMA), and the manufacturer’s instructions. Assure
10275
that there are no gaps or cavities in the installation and that all adjacent sections are properly
10276
connected. Seal and protect all edges of the insulation from the flow of the airstream.
10277
10278
Commissioning, Operation and Maintenance
10279
In order to assure that the airstream surfaces do not contribute to the lack of indoor air quality in a
10280
building, the procedures outlined above for the design and installation of duct systems should be
10281
included as a part of the building commissioning process. The selection, installation and cleaning of duct
10282
and insulation materials should be addressed as part of the commissioning design review and
10283
construction monitoring processes.
10284
10285
The building air distribution systems should also be designed and installed so that clean duct surfaces
10286
can be properly maintained. Certainly, the air handling unit/fan filters should be changed regularly and
10287
the other components of the air handling unit should be cleaned. Ducts are part of total air delivery
10288
system, and there is a direct correlation between increased MERV rating on filters and decreased
10289
deposition of dirt and particulates on duct surfaces. Additionally, however, provisions for access to the
10290
internal surfaces of the duct system should be designed and installed into the system so that ductwork
10291
can be cleaned internally without damaging the system materials.
10292
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 341
Return to TOP
10293
References
10294
Note: The list of references will be added prior to publication.
10295
10296
10297
10298
10299
10300
10301
10302
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 342
Return to TOP
10303
Strategy 4.3 – Control Moisture Associated with Piping, Plumbing Fixtures, and
10304
Ductwork
10305
10306
Overview
10307
Condensation and mold growth can occur on the insulation jacket of cold water piping for a number of
10308
reasons, including (a) an inadequate R-value of the installed insulation; (b) a failed vapor retarder; and
10309
(c) an unanticipated high dew point temperature in the microenvironment where the piping is located.
10310
Photo #1 shows water droplet formation on cold water piping in an unventilated ceiling location where
10311
the dew point temperature was elevated. Mold growth occurred not only on the pipe jacket (Photo #1),
10312
but also on ceiling tiles and paper-faced wallboard wetted by the water drops. This type of moisture-
10313
mold problem can be hidden from view (e.g., in a wall cavity) and can result in a significant mold
10314
growth problem.
10315
10316
It is important, therefore, for the designer to anticipate dew point temperatures in microenvironments
10317
such as ceiling spaces, wall cavities, mechanical equipment rooms, and utility chases where cold water
10318
piping and cold air supply ducts will be located. Additionally, it is important to specify an adequate
10319
insulation R-value and a continuous vapor retarder to prevent condensation.
10320
10321
Minimizing Condensation
10322
Condensation and mold problems associated with cold water pipe and cold air supply duct insulation
10323
jackets can be exacerbated where insulation is subject to minimum or moderate physical activity in
10324
locations like mechanical equipment rooms and utility tunnels where O&M personnel must crawl over
10325
or walk on pipe or duct insulation jackets. In these locations, the designer should specify a PVC or
10326
aluminum jacket for purposes of protecting the insulation and vapor retarder.
10327
10328
Minimizing Leaks
10329
Mold growth problems associated with plumbing leaks can occur in wall cavities, utility chases, ceilings,
10330
and other interior spaces. The mold-moisture problem associated with plumbing leaks may be small
10331
(e.g., occasional leak beneath a sink) or massive (e.g., slow leaks in plumbing in wall cavities in stacked
10332
restrooms in high-rise buildings including hotels). Paper-faced wallboard, composite wood products,
10333
and ceiling systems are especially susceptible to damage from plumbing leaks. Materials in occupied
10334
spaces may also be damaged by plumbing leaks.
10335
10336
The designer should take actions as follows to minimize plumbing leaks. These actions include testing
10337
of water lines according to Section 312.5 of the International Plumbing Code. It is important for the
10338
designer to specify a construction schedule with adequate time for water line testing while the plumbing
10339
is still open for inspection. In addition, it is important to test the integrity of water-holding assemblies
10340
including whirlpools and shower pans before occupancy.
10341
10342
In addition to the need for appropriate insulation on cold water piping and cold air supply ducts, there
10343
are additional items that fall under plumbing design where control of moisture and mold growth should
10344
be considered. These additional considerations include the following:
10345
• Horizontal storm water piping above ceilings should be properly insulated to minimize
10346
condensation.
10347
• Vertical piping (e.g., storm water, condensate, etc.) should be appropriately insulated in risers
10348
and wall cavities subject to infiltration in order to minimize condensation.
10349
• Consideration should be given to minimize the location of domestic water valves above ceilings
10350
in order to reduce water damage to ceiling systems.
10351
• Carefully consider the location of in-line humidifiers because of the potential for water leaks.
10352
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 343
Return to TOP
In building microenvironments where chronic leaks and floods will likely occur over time (e.g., stacked
10353
restrooms in hotels; piping readily subject to corrosion, joint failure, and physical damage), the designer
10354
and the owner should specify use of moisture tolerant construction and finishing materials such as
10355
cement-based products, ceramics, plastics, metals, etc. The use of paper-faced wallboard and composite
10356
wood products should be minimized in these problematic wet microenvironments.
10357
10358
Plumbing System O&M Guide
10359
An important aspect of controlling mold growth associated with cold water piping and plumbing
10360
systems, is the assembly of O&M information in one location for the benefit of future users. Appendix
10361
G of the EPA Moisture Guide contains details on the types of documents that should be assembled.
10362
Appendix G of the EPA Moisture Guide also contains a Plumbing Operations and Maintenance
10363
Checklist that should be followed for conducting inspections designed to minimize plumbing leaks.
10364
10365
Table 4.3-A – Mold Growth Associated with Piping & Plumbing Resource Guide
10366
Minimizing Condensation
Dew Point Conditions in Piping Locations
Plumbing System Design Goal #2, pp. 57, EPA Moisture
Guide
Insulation R-value and vapor Retarder
Plumbing System Design Goal #2, pp. 59, EPA Moisture
Guide
PVC or aluminum jackets on exposed pipe surface
Minimizing Leaks
Testing of water lines, fixtures, and assemblies
Section 312.5 of International Plumbing Code
Testing when plumbing lines are open for inspection Plumbing System Design Goal #1, pp. 57, EPA Moisture
Guide.
Materials for pipe chases and locations susceptible
to leaks (high moisture tolerance)
Plumbing System Design Goal #3, pp. 59, EPA Moisture
Guide
Plumbing System O&M Guide
Locating the O&M information
Appendix G, pp. 140, EPA Moisture Guide
Inspection Procedures
Appendix G, pp. 140-142, EPA Moisture Guide
10367
10368
10369
10370
10371
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 344
Return to TOP
10372
Strategy 4.4 – Facilitate Access to HVAC Equipment for Inspection, Cleaning and
10373
Maintenance
10374
[Editor’s Note: Strategy not yet updated from 60% Draft]
10375
10376
Access for periodic inspection, routine maintenance, and cleaning of air handling equipment is essential
10377
for maintaining acceptable indoor air quality. Assessing “soiling” of the HVAC system, monitoring the
10378
effectiveness of air-cleaning, and validating the performance of monitors and sensors has become an
10379
increasingly important aspect of building operation and maintenance. It is important to conserve
10380
(assure) access to HVAC system components just like access to the electrical panels and components are
10381
protected. Failure to design and construct systems that facilitate access may significantly compromise
10382
indoor air quality. It is well recognized that routine maintenance can be significantly compromised by
10383
above ceiling installations or locations requiring ladders or other equipment for access. In addition,
10384
systems that can be accessed without special tools are more likely to receive the appropriate inspection
10385
and maintenance.
10386
10387
Access in Design Documents
10388
Access considerations begin with the design of the mechanical room to accommodate the AHU and
10389
other HVAC system components. Design and selection of the HVAC system itself can limit access and
10390
maintenance problems. For example, choosing a central system rather than a multiple fan-coil system
10391
reduces the components needing access for inspection and maintenance. The mechanical room design
10392
must position the HVAC system components to assure ease of access. Low ceilings, cramped spaces, or
10393
difficult access will inhibit appropriate access and maintenance. This must be avoided. It is also
10394
important recognize that access doors to the mechanical room can impact performance by allowing the
10395
introduction of local contaminants. Access doors should remain closed except as required.
10396
10397
AHSRAE Standard 62.1-2007 provides fairly comprehensive requirements for assuring appropriate
10398
access to all components of the HVAC system including the air-handling unit, air distribution system,
10399
controllers, and sensors. In addition, most manufacturers provide guidance on the required service
10400
clearances. It is essential that such clearances take into account door swings and space for personnel to
10401
access, stand, and move tools and materials out. Failure to provide appropriate access can result in
10402
inadequate maintenance of the system.
10403
10404
Locations that Hinder Access
10405
Even with the best designs, appropriate access can be compromised during the construction and
10406
installation of any or all of the HVAC system components. In addition, installation of other building
10407
systems, such as plumbing, electrical systems, and telecommunication systems, can result in obstruction
10408
that either complicate or obstruct direct access. Careful stewardship during the construction phase is
10409
essential to assuring that clear access is maintained.
10410
10411
Another risk associated with providing access to the HVAC system is the potential of air leakage from
10412
or into the system. Leakage of cooled air from the system can result in condensation, which can result
10413
in microbial growth within the HVAC system or on other building materials. Conversely, leakage on
10414
the negatively pressurized sections of the system can result in the infiltration of contaminants from the
10415
surrounding spaces and these contaminants may degrade the indoor air quality in the occupied spaces
10416
serviced by the system.
10417
10418
Any modifications or addition of access doors may also impact long-term performance and indoor air
10419
quality. For example, if new access doors are required for the remediation of mold growth within the
10420
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 345
Return to TOP
system, these doors must be installed in conformance with SMACNA requirements to assure adequate
10421
prevention of leakage.
10422
10423
Equipment Clearance
10424
Ventilation equipment shall be installed with sufficient working space for inspection and routine
10425
maintenance.
10426
10427
Manufacturer’s literature generally gives the required service clearances. Such clearances should take
10428
into account door swings and space for personnel to stand and move tools and materials out.
10429
10430
Electrical Code28 covers specific service workspace requirements for electrical equipment that may be
10431
part of or near ventilation equipment.
10432
10433
Ventilation Equipment Access
10434
Access doors, panels, or other means shall be provided and sized to allow convenient and unobstructed
10435
access sufficient to inspect, maintain, and calibrate all ventilation system components for which routine
10436
inspection, maintenance, or calibration is necessary. Ventilation system components comprise, for
10437
example, air-handling units, fan-coil units, controllers, and sensors.
10438
Section 6.3.4 Casing Access Doors in SMACNA’s HVAC Duct Construction Standards – Metal and
10439
Flexible: Unless required to permit replacement of larger equipment such as a fan motor, access door
10440
size should be limited to approximately 20 inches (508mm) wide by 54 inches (1372mm) high. They
10441
state that this will provide adequate size for personnel and most equipment. Larger doors should be
10442
avoided since they will break the continuity of the wall reinforcing. DOORS SHOULD OPEN
10443
AGAINST THE AIR PRESSURE. This arrangement utilizes the air pressure rather than the door
10444
latches to force the door against the sealing gasket.
10445
10446
Air Distribution System
10447
Access doors, panels, or other means shall be provided in ventilation equipment, duct-work, and
10448
plenums, located and sized to allow convenient and unobstructed access for inspection, cleaning, and
10449
routine maintenance of the following:
10450
• Outdoor air intake areaways or plenums
10451
• Mixed air plenums
10452
• Upstream surface of each heating, cooling, and heat-recovery coil having a total of four rows or
10453
less
10454
• Both upstream and downstream surface of each heating, cooling, and heat-recovery coils having
10455
a total of more than four rows and air washers, evaporative coolers, heat wheels, and other heat
10456
exchangers
10457
• Air cleaners
10458
• Drain pans and drain seals
10459
• Fans
10460
• Humidifiers
10461
10462
Figure 2-10 Duct Access Doors and Panels in SMACNA’s HVAC Duct Construction Standards – Metal
10463
and Flexible provides specification for sizing and securing access doors and panels. They also state that
10464
construction and airtightness must be suitable for the duct pressure class used.
10465
10466
10467
10468
10469
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 346
Return to TOP
10470
Strategy 4.5 – Control Legionella in Water Systems
10471
10472
10473
Lukewarm water (30-40°C) in cooling towers, evaporative condensers, and potable water service
10474
systems provides places for the growth of Legionella, which are gram-negative bacteria. At present,
10475
there are approximately 48 species known in the genus Legionella (3) and many of the species have
10476
been associated with human illness. Some Legionella species can be distinguished into serogroups and
10477
approximately 70 serogroups are known (3). For example, L. pneumophilia serogroups 1, 3, and 6 may
10478
be found in a water sample. Legionella pneumophila is the species that caused the outbreak of
10479
Legionnaire’s disease in Philadelphia in 1976 in which 34 people died. Legionnaire’s disease is an
10480
infection (the lung of a susceptible person is the target organ) that results in pneumonia, which may be
10481
fatal. Legionella may also cause a milder form of illness known as Pontiac Fever, which is
10482
nonpneumonic and similar to a severe flu. Legionnaire’s disease and Pontiac Fever differ in the clinical
10483
reaction, incubation time, and attack rate (4-6). Legionnaire’s disease is a pneumonia with a 2-10 day
10484
incubation, and occurs in about 5% of cases of community-acquired pneumonia. Further, there are
10485
specific risk factors for Legionnaire’s disease, such as immune deficiencies, underlying illnesses,
10486
diabetes mellitus, smoking, and the elderly and young children are at greater risk. Pontiac Fever is
10487
nonpneumonic, with symptoms that resemble influenza with onset about 36 hours after exposure. The
10488
reason for the differing health effects of Legionella exposure is unknown.
10489
10490
Legionella pneumophila is the species that most frequently causes legionellosis. Further, there are
10491
approximately 15 serotypes of L. pneumophila which are distinguished by their reactivity to diagnostic
10492
antibodies (6). Identification of the environmental source of a Legionellosis outbreak occurs when the
10493
species/serotype of Legionella from the infected person matches the species/serotype of Legionella in
10494
the environmental reservoir. So, it is important to determine the species and the serotype of both clinical
10495
and environmental isolates when attempting to identify the environmental source of an infection or
10496
outbreak. Molecular typing (alone or in combination with monocional antibody subtyping) is necessary
10497
to make a definitive match.
10498
10499
10500
Control of Legionella in Cooling Towers
10501
Proper siting (Building siting; Mists; Building Openings)
10502
Cooling towers should be located as far as possible from building openings including HVAC system
10503
outdoor air inlets (See Section 7.6.5 of ASHRAE Guidelines 12-2000; See Figure 4.5-B). When
10504
choosing the location for cooling towers on or near buildings consideration should be given to the
10505
direction of prevailing winds and location of nearby areas where people may congregate. The designer
10506
should choose cooling tower locations with an objective of minimizing the carry-over of drift or mist
10507
(from the tower) to outdoor occupancy areas (see case study). In situations where cooling tower drift or
10508
mist is likely to impact populated outdoor areas, the designer should carefully evaluate the efficiency of
10509
drift eliminators (ASHRAE Guideline 12-2000; Section 7.3).
10510
10511
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 347
Return to TOP
10512
Figure 4.5-B - HVAC outdoor air intakes and other building openings should be
10513
located as far as possible from cooling towers. The HVAC inlet in the photo is located
10514
within 4 meters of a double cell cooling tower. Under certain atmospheric conditions,
10515
drift or mist from the towers can enter the outdoor air inlet. (Photo courtesy of P. Morey)
10516
10517
Operation And Maintenance Of Cooling Towers
10518
The designer should provide the owner/building operator with a complete set of operation and
10519
maintenance information for the cooling tower including appropriate biocide, scale, and corrosion
10520
control procedures. This information includes cooling tower start-up procedures and procedures for
10521
start-up undrained systems. Information on cooling tower system operation and maintenance should be
10522
placed in a secure location that is readily accessible to facilities operation and maintenance personnel as
10523
well as to building inspectors. Section 7.6.1 - 7.6.3 of ASHRAE Guideline 12-2000 contains a helpful
10524
overview of cooling tower operation and maintenance procedures.
10525
10526
Section 5.3.2 of the WHO Legionella guide (page 80) provides designers with additional
10527
recommendations useful in upgrading the maintenance of cooling towers, namely designing cooling
10528
towers to be:
10529
 easy to clean; use non-porous materials.
10530
 easy to access; interior surfaces should be readily accessible for purposes of cleaning.
10531
10532
10533
Control of Legionella in Potable Water Systems
10534
Proper Storage Temperatures in Hot Water Tanks
10535
Table 1 (pp. 695) in Fields and Moore (2006) summarizes recommendations for storage and point of
10536
discharge (faucets, showerheads) temperatures in potable water systems. In general, there is consensus
10537
that a hot water storage temperature of 60°C (140°F) and a point of discharge temperature of 50°C
10538
(122°F) are appropriate. Section 4.1.6 of ASHRAE Guideline 12-2000 and Section 4.4.1 of the WHO
10539
2007 Guidelines contain similar recommendations. It should be noted however, that maintaining such
10540
temperatures will not insure a Legionella-free system.
10541
10542
10543
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 348
Return to TOP
10544
Design Considerations For Potable Water Systems
10545
Sections 4.1.1-4.1.4 of ASHRAE Guideline 12-2000 provide information for designers on minimizing
10546
the potential for Legionella amplification in potable water systems. These design considerations include
10547
the following: (a) minimize the use of rubber washers and fittings in plumbing systems; (b) reduce
10548
dead-end or stagnant plumbing lines; (c) and reduce dirt and debris in water lines entering the buildings
10549
as these materials provide nutrient for Legionella growth. Section 4.3 of the WHO 2007 Legionella
10550
Guide (see pp. 63) includes a discussion risk factors associated with construction materials used in
10551
plumbing systems.
10552
10553
Control of Legionella in Heated Spas, Architectural Fountains, Misters and Other Water Systems
10554
The growth of Legionella can occur in other water systems including heated spas, architectural fountains
10555
and waterfalls, misters, air washers and humidifiers. ASHRAE Guideline 12-2000 provides a
10556
comprehensive review of this subject. As with cooling towers and potable water systems, the potential
10557
for Legionella growth is affected by water temperature and the presence of nutrients and biofilms. The
10558
risk of disease is also affected by droplet size in aerosol emitted from these water features.
10559
10560
Emergency Disinfection of Water Systems
10561
Corrective actions to reduce Legionella amphilication in water systems including thermal eradication,
10562
monochloramine, hyperchlorination, copper-silver ionization, chlorine dioxide treatments, etc., are
10563
reviewed elsewhere (4,7-12).
10564
10565
Section 7.6.4 of ASHRAE Guideline 12-2000 briefly mentions procedures that may be used for
10566
emergency disinfection of cooling towers. It is noted that emergency disinfection procedures for
10567
cooling towers are highly corrosive and are not to be used routinely. The Cooling Technology Institute
10568
(2002) provides an example of an emergency disinfection procedure for cooling towers. An additional
10569
example of emergency disinfection for cooling towers is provided on pp. 82 of the WHO (2007)
10570
Legionella Guide.
10571
10572
Section 4.1.6 of ASHRAE Guideline 12-2000 provides information on procedures for emergency
10573
thermal disinfection hot water distribution systems. In order to carry out thermal disinfection, a hot
10574
water temperature of 71-77°C (160-170°F) is required (minimum flush time 5 to 30 minutes). Fields
10575
and Moore (2006) summarized emergency thermal disinfection recommendations for potable water
10576
systems based on other guidelines (See Table 1, pp. 695). If thermal disinfection of a hot water system
10577
is not possible, then other means of emergency disinfection are available (Section 4.1.6 of ASHRAE
10578
Guideline 12-2000).
10579
10580
Case Study #1 (additional details for overview example):
10581
10582
Three office workers in building A developed Legionnaires disease caused by Legionella pneumophila
10583
serogroup 1. The implicated source of L. pneumophila was a three-celled cooling tower located about
10584
100 meters away on the roof of building B. The three workers took breaks located outside and at grade
10585
level around building A. Prevailing winds tended to blow drift (mist) from the cooling tower on the roof
10586
of building B toward the grade level area at building A.
10587
10588
As mentioned earlier (Section 3) the water in the cooling tower appeared to be clean (see Figure 4.5-C)
10589
and had been treated with biocide. Water samples from the tower contained about 3,000 Legionella
10590
pneumophila serogroup 1 - the disease causing agent. In this case study, “good maintenance” practices
10591
alone were insufficient to control Legionella within this tower. The only reliable way to determine if
10592
this or other water systems pose a risk for transmission of Legionella is culture sampling from the tower
10593
water basin (See next topic).
10594
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 349
Return to TOP
10595
10596
Figure 4.5-C- Relatively clear water occurs in the basin of a “well maintained”
10597
cooling tower on the roof of Bujilding B that was implicated as the source of Legionella
10598
that caused the disease outbreak among occupants at Building A. (Photo courtesy of J. Stout)
10599
10600
Environmental Monitoring for Culturable Legionella
10601
Routine sampling of building water systems for culturable Legionella is considered controversial
10602
because disease may occur in the absence of positive sampling results and positive sampling results do
10603
not imply that disease has or will occur.
10604
10605
In a recent review, Fields and Moore (2006) point out that scientific basis for determining an
10606
“acceptable” number of Legionella in water systems does not currently exist for a number of reasons
10607
including differences in occupant susceptibilities and building designs as well as variation in the
10608
virulence of Legionella species and serotypes. However, it has been suggested that Legionella risk is
10609
greatest when a high percentage of building water samples are positive for culturable Legionella,
10610
expecially Legionella pneumophila serogroup #1 (7).
10611
10612
Several documents review the microbial ecology of Legionella in cooling towers, hot water systems, and
10613
other water reservoirs (4,5,7-10). Protocols for collection of Legionella from these sources are well
10614
known (3,5). Analysis of water samples for culturable Legionella is the preferred method used in field
10615
studies. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) methods, while providing a quick turn around time for sample
10616
analysis in the laboratory, cannot distinguish between culturable and dead Legionella. PCR methods for
10617
analysis are discouraged because only live (culturable) Legionella can cause infection.
10618
10619
4.5-A Legionella Resource Guide
10620

Proper O&M Programs

Section 5.3.2 of WHO 2007 Guide

OBJECTIVE 5 – LIMIT CONTAMINANTS FROM INDOOR SOURCES
10657
10658
10659
Specific strategies described in this Objective are:
10660
10661
• Strategy 5.1 – Control Indoor Contaminant Sources through Appropriate Material Selection
10662
• Strategy 5.2 – Employ Strategies to Limit the Impact of Material Emissions
10663
• Strategy 5.3 – Minimize IAQ Impacts Associated with Cleaning and Maintenance
10664
• Strategy 5.4 – Consider Upper Room Ultraviolet Germicidal Irradiation in Relevant Applications
10665
10666
10667
10668
See also these closely related Strategies and Objectives:
10669
10670
• Strategy 1.3 – Establish a Project Schedule that Facilitates Achievement of Good IAQ
10671

10672
• Strategy 4.5 – Control Legionella in Water Systems
10673
• Objective 6 – Capture and Exhaust Emissions from Building Equipment and Activities
10674
• Objective 7 – Reduce Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation and Air Cleaning.
10675
10676
10677

10679

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 352
Return to TOP
10681
Strategy 5.1 – Control Indoor Contaminant Sources through Appropriate Material
10682
Selection
10683
10684
A few key principles are essential to understanding and thus successfully making appropriate material
10685
selections leading to reduced indoor contaminant levels. The brief overview presented here is intended
10686
to provide a basis for interpretation of product labeling information and the specifics for individual
10687
building materials. More detailed information is available at the end of this section (see Contaminant
10688
Emissions: Essential Concepts and Emissions Testing: Methods and Results).
10689
10690
Definitions of major terms related to material emissions are provided below in Table 5.1-A.
10691
10692
Table 5.1-A Definitions of terms related to emissions.
10693
Term
Agency/Organization;
Report/Publication; (LINK)
Definition
VOC (Volatile
Organic
Compounds)
ASTM; D1356 Standard
Terminology Relating to Sampling
and Analysis of Atmospheres
(ASTM D1356)
An organic compound with a saturation vapor pressure greater than 10−2 kPa at
25°C (where an “organic chemical” = a carbon-based compound in which the
element carbon is attached to other carbon atom(s), hydrogen, oxygen, or other
elements in a chain, ring, or three dimensional structure)
ISIAQ – Glossary of the Indoor Air
Sciences, 1st Edn (March 2006)
Organic compounds with boiling points ranging from a lower limit between 50
oC and 100 oC, and an upper limit between 240oC and 260oC, where the upper
limits represent mostly polar compounds
U.S. Environmental Protection
Agency; Code of Federal
Regulations (40 CFR 51.100(s))
(US-EPA VOC Defn)
Any compound of carbon, excluding carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, carbonic
acid, metallic carbides or carbonates, and ammonium carbonate, which
participates in atmospheric photochemical reactions (with dozens of exceptions
for compounds “determined to have negligible photochemical reactivity.”)
SVOC
ASTM; D1356 Standard
Terminology Relating to Sampling
and Analysis of Atmospheres
(ASTM D1356)
Semi-Volatile Organic Compounds: An organic compound with a saturation
vapor pressure between 10−2 and 10−8 kPa at 25°C.
VVOC
WHO
Very Volatile Organic Compounds: compound with boiling point in range from
below 0oC to between 50 and 100oC
TVOC
ASTM; D1356 Standard
Terminology Relating to Sampling
and Analysis of Atmospheres
Total Volatile Organic Compounds: The summed concentration of all the
individual volatile organic compounds (VOCs) quantifiable in an air sample by
both a precisely specified sampling protocol and a precisely defined analytical
method.
PM10
US-EPA
Particulate Matter with an aerodynamic diameter of up to 10 μm.
Corresponds to “Thoracic” fraction.
PM3.5
Corresponds to “Respirable” fraction: i.e. particles that penetrate/deposit
exclusively into the gas-exchange region or pulmonary region of the deep lung
PM2.5
US-EPA
Particulate Matter with an aerodynamic diameter of up to 2.5 μm, referred to as
the “fine” particle fraction.
PM0.1 or UFP
US-EPA
Particulate Matter with an aerodynamic diameter of up to 0.1 μm, referred to as
the “ultrafine” particle fraction.
LOAEL
WHO; Air Quality Guidelines for
Europe, 2nd Edition (2000)
Lowest-observed-adverse-effect level: Lowest concentration or amount of a
substance, found by experiment or observation, which causes an adverse
alteration of morphology, functional capacity, growth, development or life span
of the target organism distinguishable from normal (control) organisms of the
same species and strain under the same defined conditions of exposure.
NOAEL
WHO; Air Quality Guidelines for
Europe, 2nd Edition (2000)
No-observed-adverse-effect level: Greatest concentration or amount of a
substance, found by experiment or observation, which causes no detectable
adverse alteration of morphology, functional capacity, growth, development or
life span of the target organism under defined conditions of exposure. Alterations
of morphology, functional capacity, growth, development or life span of the
target may be detected which are judged not to be adverse.
ef
Emission Factor: The rate of contaminant release per unit of material surface
(typically exposed surface area, A), eg. μg/m2/h
ER
Emission Rate: The rate of contaminant release, expressed as mass/time, eg
mg/h (ER = ef \* A)
10694
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 353
Return to TOP
The indoor contaminants found in non-industrial environments, typically complex mixtures of 100’s of
10695
individual compounds, have been characterized in many studies (including Wallace, 1987; Tsuchiya,
10696
1988; Girman etal., 1999; Hodgson and Levin 2003; Alevantis et al; 2006a; 2006b). In some cases, the
10697
“fingerprints” of the materials used in building construction, furnishing and interior finishes are clearly
10698
evident. Emission rates (i.e. source strengths) can vary enormously between individual materials, so to
10699
can the complexity of their emissions (i.e. number of individual compounds emitted). Lumping all
10700
emissions together as a single value and report this as Total VOC (or “TVOC”) is not a good indicator
10701
of a product’s impact on health (including comfort and irritation). Since individual VOCs have highly
10702
variable effects, it is important to quantify emission rates of specific “target” compounds of concern.
10703
VOCs are not the sole compounds of interest: SVOCs, particulate emissions, and secondary by-products
10704
formed by reaction with ozone have IAQ impact. SVOCs, “Secondary” Emissions, and Fine
10705
Particulates provides discussion of non-VOC emissions of interest. Further information regarding
10706
TVOC and the selection of target emission compounds is provided in section: Total VOC and Target
10707
VOCs.
10708
10709
10710
Figure 5.1-C Air sample from building material emissions test:
10711
each peak represents a separate VOC (courtesy NRCC)
10712
10713
Information regarding the IAQ impact of selected individual compounds is available from a variety of
10714
sources (and summarized in section on Contaminant Irritancy, Odor and Health Impact). It is
10715
important to note that in the complex mixtures typical of product emissions, effects may be synergistic
10716
or cumulative in nature, so that compound-by-compound analysis may not reflect true impact.
10717
10718
In non-industrial environments, strict regulation concerning contaminant levels is almost non-existent.
10719
While tempting to apply a factor to an existing occupational guideline value (eg 1/10 TLV), this
10720
approach is not recommended due to the complexity of the contaminant mixtures, as well as to the
10721
nature of the exposed occupants in these buildings (see IAQ Guidelines, Standards and Specifications)
10722
10723
There are two major classes of labeling systems used to rate indoor materials: “content” based and
10724
“emissions” labels. Two major factors distinguish these broad categories. First, content-based labels
10725
typically assess the VOC level of a product based on the mass % of VOCs present in the material. No
10726
attempt is made to measure the actual emissions of VOCs from the product, so true impact on indoor air
10727
is not assessed. Secondly, many content-based labels have a primary focus on environmental
10728
sustainability and ambient air quality (not indoor), and often use the US EPA definition of “VOC’ which
10729
deals specifically with compounds involved in photochemical reactions leading to smog formation. As
10730
such, many VOCs (per the ASTM or ISIAQ definitions for example) are excluded even though they
10731
may have significant occupant impact indoors. A “VOC-free” or “low-VOC” product may thus actually
10732
contain significant quantities of compounds that may adversely influence occupant comfort, irritation or
10733
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 354
Return to TOP
health. In addition, “green” is an over-used and occasionally misleading term (hence “GreenWashing”):
10734
caution should be taken in interpreting green claims (see TerraChoice, 2007).
10735
10736
Finally, emissions testing (typically in small stainless steel chambers) is a very complex process with
10737
multiple steps, each of which can significantly affect reported emissions results for a given product.
10738
These include specimen collection and handling, conditioning prior to testing, mounting in chamber
10739
(including use of specimen “substrates” and/or holders), chamber environmental conditions (temperature,
10740
humidity, air velocity, pressure), air sample collection and analysis, sampling frequency and duration,
10741
data analysis and interpretation (estimation of resulting indoor concentrations) and reporting. The
10742
various “emissions” labels are not consistent in their handling of these parameters, so interpretation of
10743
individual findings must be made carefully. Details are provided in the section: Emissions Testing:
10744
Methods and Results.
10745
10746
Selection of key contaminants of concern is a critical first step toward the effective source management.
10747
Only then can individual products be assessed via reliable methods to determine their emissions source
10748
strength (emission rates) of these identified compounds and thus their true impact on indoor
10749
environments. A particular product’s final exposed (emitting) surface area, it’s total installed mass, and
10750
it’s emission rate/decay profile for contaminants of concern must also be known. Failure to consider
10751
any of these factors may result in poor material choices.
10752
10753
Careful selection of building materials and interior finishes and furnishings can be a very successful
10754
aspect of an overall IAQ design approach as demonstrated by the following case study.
10755
10756
10757
Case Study #1: Capital Area East End Complex
10758
10759
• Capitol Area East End Complex, Sacramento Calif: 5-bldg sustainable office complex built in 2002-
10760
03 with total area of 140,000m2
10761
• Emissions testing of majority of interior finishing materials required (per Section 01350
10762
10763
10764
Figure 5.1-D Schematic source:
Figure 5.1-E photo courtesy Leon Alevantis
10765
http://www.eastend.dgs.ca.gov/AboutTheProject/default.htm
10766
10767
Overall, concentrations of the common chemicals measured at the CAEEC shortly after initial
10768
occupancy and for several months thereafter were comparable to those reported in the BASE study with
10769
only few chemicals at the CAEEC being higher than the BASE study, indicating that careful selection of
10770
building materials provides indoor air concentrations of most chemicals comparable to existing
10771
buildings, thus reducing exposures to high levels of numerous chemicals usually associated with new
10772
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 355
Return to TOP
buildings. This is particularly noteworthy since the CAEEC buildings were new when the VOC
10773
measurements were made and the BASE study buildings were at least several years old
10774
10775
This study has shown that requiring emissions testing from manufacturers appears to have helped
10776
achieve better than average indoor air quality at the CAEEC. The concentration targets established for
10777
this project were not exceeded in the majority of the locations. Concentrations of the common chemicals
10778
measured at the CAEEC were comparable to those reported in the BASE study with only few chemicals
10779
at the CAEEC being higher than the BASE study. Therefore, as expected, careful selection of building
10780
materials during a building’s design appears to result in lower concentrations of VOCs during the initial
10781
months of a newly-constructed building (Alevantis, etal, 2006)
10782
10783
10784
Case Study #2 : EPA Waterside Mall (Washington, DC)
10785
10786
• Mixed-use building, first phase completed in 1970, occupied by EPA in 1971, additions in 1980s
10787
• Major renovation in 1987, included installation of 22,600 m2 of new carpeting
10788
• Of more that 5100 EPA employees, ~880 reported health effects attributed to the renovation.
10789
• Although problem likely due to a number of factors (including HVAC system complexity, design,
10790
capacity and maintenance; occupant crowding; density of office equipment), the carpeting was
10791
eventually replaced with a low-odor alternative.
10792
• Estimated cost: ~$4M including carpet replacement, HVAC renovations, IAQ investigations, sick
10793
leave, labor to address IAQ issues, compensation claims, etc plus litigation costs.
10794
10795
10796
Emissions Data: Available Information
10797
10798
Manufacturer-Supplied Information
10799
The first step in selecting materials with low emissions of priority contaminants is to seek information
10800
directly from product manufacturers. The information supplied can range from Material Safety Data
10801
Sheet (MSDS) summaries of hazardous ingredients contained in the product, to detailed emissions test
10802
data conducted by 3rd party testing agencies (see Emissions Testing, Section 4.4.2.2).
10803
10804
MSDS information is required in the U.S. by OSHA under Hazard Communication regulation, and in
10805
Canada under Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System (WHMIS) guidelines according to
10806
the Hazardous Products Act, Part II and the Controlled Products Regulations. The information provided,
10807
is thus geared to occupational environments (as are ACGIH TLVs and OSHA PELs). Information is
10808
seldom given on the complete chemical composition of the product, and chemical emission information
10809
is rarely provided. Chemicals deemed as propitiatory are not reported. In addition, there is no
10810
enforcement of accuracy standards for MSDSs.
10811
10812
10813
“Information from MSDS to evaluate potential emissions from indoor material and products
10814
should be used with caution. Limited reviews of submitted MSDS are conducted. MSDS for
10815
many products are incomplete. Also, changes in manufacturing processes and chemicals can
10816
occur before MSDS are corrected. MSDS data may not reflect changes in the designated
10817
hazardous material list or the most current health hazard information. In summary, users of
10818
MSDS should carefully review the information and use it with care.”
10819
10820
- Tichenor, 2007
10821
10822
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 356
Return to TOP
In view of these limitations, a preferred option is to request manufacturers to submit chemical contents
10823
and emissions test results and to identify carcinogens, teratogens, and mutagens listed by recognized
10824
authorities (Table 5.3-?). If the required information is not readily available, materials testing may be
10825
required (conducted according to recognized standards and protocols).
10826
10827
Labels: “Green” Products / “Green” Buildings
10828
Whether targeting individual products or entire buildings, “Green” labeling systems have the laudable
10829
intent of promoting environmental sustainability. This focus on global ecology does mean that some
10830
caution must be observed when using certain “Green” information to assess IAQ impact. In stressing
10831
sustainability, green products may promote use of relatively high proportions of recycled materials.
10832
Depending on the nature of these recycled materials and the processing used to incorporate them, this
10833
can sometimes contribute to emissions of harmful compounds to the indoor environment. Note that this
10834
is not necessarily the case, and several “green” rating systems also consider the toxicity of emitted
10835
contaminants as a priority, such that green products with substantial recycled content can have relatively
10836
low emissions (Alevantis et al. 2003). Product specifiers must also consider that green labels such as
10837
EcoLogo or Green Seal (Table 5.1-A certify a product as being “low-VOC” based on the US-EPA
10838
definition of VOC. As discussed previously, this can be misleading when applied to IAQ concerns. No
10839
emissions testing is performed, product certification is based on VOC content, based on weight % of the
10840
final product per US EPA (2000) Method 24. Both EcoLogo and Green Seal attempt tighter control on
10841
VOC release by listing specific compounds that must be excluded from product formulation. These
10842
restrictions are product-dependent, so careful attention must be paid to the specific certification standard
10843
employed. [Note: Insert discussion of SCS here.] Additional resources related to Green products are
10844
provided in Table 5.1-C.
10845
10846
Table 5.1-B: Summary of product labeling systems – VOC Content Labels
10847
Program Name (Region, Developer/Sponsor, Date
Established; Link) General Comments
Test Protocol & Compounds Specified
Environmental Choice EcoLogo (Canada, Environment
Canada - TerraChoice Environmental Marketing, Inc.,
1988, www.environmentalchoice.ca )
• > 7000 certified products in 120 categories with individual
Certification and Criteria Documents (CCD)
• Requires detailed instruction by manufacturer regarding:
o proper application so as to minimize health concerns and
maximize performance; and
o proper disposal methods
• No emissions testing
• VOC ratings based on wt %
• Product-specific limits on VOCs, eg.
• Adhesives: 2 general purpose adhesives certified under CCD-046
which:
• bans use of aromatic or halogenated solvents, formaldehyde,
borax, Hg, PB, Cd, Cr;
• VOC content < 5% by weight

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

Return to TOP
Program Name (Region, Developer/Sponsor, Date
Established; Link) General Comments
Test Protocol & Compounds Specified
Green Seal (USA, Green Seal, 1989; www.greenseal.org )
• 32 environmental certification standards issued to date, of
which four include an IAQ component:
o Paints (GS –11)
o Commercial Adhesives (GS-36)
o Industrial & Institutional Cleaners (GS-37)
o Industrial & Institutional Floor-Care Products (GS-40)
• 19 “Choose Green Reports” published to date by Green Seal,
including six that deal with IAQ issues (but discussion of VOC
emissions is limited):
o Carpet
o Floor Care Products – Finishes and Strippers
o Office Furniture
o Office Supplies
o Particleboard and Medium-Density Fiberboard
o Wood Finishes and Stains
• No emissions testing
• Composition-based analysis of VOC content (EPA definition)
specified as weight %
• Product-specific limits on VOCs
o E.g. for paint: excluded VOCs from formulation: methylene
chloride, 1,1,1-trichloroethane, benzene, toluene
(methylbenzene), ethylbenzene, vinyl chloride, naphthalene, 1,2-
dichlorobenzene, di (2-ethylhexyl) phthalate, butyl benzyl
phthalate, di-n-butyl phthalate, di-n-octyl phthalate, diethyl
phthalate, diethyl phthalate, dimethyl phthalate, isophorone,
antimony, cadmium, hexavalent chromium, lead, mercury,
formaldehyde, methyl ethyl ketone, methyl isobutyl ketone,
acrolein, acrylonitrile
• Prohibitions on carcinogens (per IARC and NTP)
• Prohibitions on reproductive toxicants (per California’s Prop. 65)
GreenSpec (USA, Building Green Inc.,
www.buildinggreen.com)
• subscription-based online resource
• GreenSpec Directory listing products based on available
information concerning such factors as recycle content,
resource conservation, indoor air emissions, etc.
• No quantitative criteria used for product evaluation
• No testing performed.
10848
Table 5.1-C: Green product resources.
10849
Agency
Document (LINK)
ASHRAE
ASHRAE Green Guide: The Design, Construction and Operation of Sustainable Buildings (2003)
California Integrated Waste
Management Board
Sustainable (Green) Building Green Building Materials.
(http://www.ciwmb.ca.gov/GreenBuilding/Materials/#IAQ)
NCARB
Sustainable Design
NIST
NISTIR 6916 BEES® 3.0 Building for Environmental and Economic Sustainability - Technical Manual
and User Guide
US-EPA
Whole Building Design Guide Federal Green Construction Guide for Specifiers
(http://www.wbdg.org/design/epa\_greenspec/096800.pdf )
GreenSpec
GreenSpec-Listed Green Building Products
(http://www.buildinggreen.com/menus/index.cfm? )
10850
Several whole building strategies exist for the design and operation of Green Buildings. In 1993, Natural
10851
Resources Canada (NRCan) introduced the C-2000 Program for Advanced Commercial Buildings
10852
(http://www.sbc.nrcan.gc.ca/buildings/c2000\_e.asp ) for the design of high performance, low-energy
10853
buildings having minimal environmental impact. Indoor performance specifications to provide health,
10854
comfort and productivity of the building occupants were based on a philosophy of avoiding generation
10855
of any contaminant or problems as a first priority, and second, eliminating the contaminant or problem at
10856
the source, and finally, to use dilution of the contaminant to some acceptable level if the first two
10857
strategies do not achieve the desired result.
10858
In the same year, the US Green Building Council (USGBC) created the LEED (Leadership in
10859
Environmental and Energy Design) Guideline and Rating System. The Canadian Green Building
10860
Council (CaGBC) was established in 2002. The LEED system gives credits for building design related
10861
to product emissions, primarily through EQ Credit 4 – Low-Emitting Materials.
10862
10863
Labels: Low-Emission Products
10864
Broader spectrum emission labeling systems (formaldehyde plus additional VOCs and/or TVOC) have
10865
proliferated throughout the world since their introduction in the 1970’s and 80’s (Blue Angel in
10866
Germany in 1977; Environmental Choice Eco-Logo in Canada in 1988). Major European labels
10867
include the Nordic Swan (Scandinavia, 1989), Indoor Climate Label (Denmark and Norway, 1995), the
10868
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 358
Return to TOP
Finnish M-1 M-2 system (1995). Industry-specific European labels include Germany’s GuT system
10869
established in 1990 by the Association of Environmentally-Friendly Carpets, and the GEV EMICODE
10870
system developed by German manufacturers of flooring installation products. In an effort to harmonize
10871
labeling strategies, the European Union (EU) introduced The Flower Eco-label.
10872
10873
Green Seal was the first labeling system introduced in the U.S. (in 1989 by an independent non-profit
10874
organization of the same name). Green Label is an industry-designed and administered program
10875
introduced in 1992 by the Carpet and Rug Institute (CRI), while GreenGuard is a privately-run 3rd-party
10876
certifier of products that grew from the 1996 AQSpec List.
10877
10878
Driven by a growing focus on LEED certification of buildings and more arduous product emission
10879
specifications mandated in California (California 01350 - Special Environmental Requirements, 2000;
10880
and the Collaborative for High Performance Schools (CHPS) Best Practices Manual: Material
10881
Specifications: 2001), more recent US labeling schemes have introduced more demanding criteria.
10882
These include Green Label Plus (introduced by CRI in 2004), GREENGUARD for Children &
10883
Schools, and a series of third-party labels introduced by Scientific Certification Systems (SCS): Indoor
10884
Advantage, Indoor Advantage Gold and FloorScore (introduced by the Resilient Floor Covering
10885
Institute in collaboration with SCS). In addition, several specification requirements developed
10886
exclusively for office furniture systems exist, including the California Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)
10887
Specifications For Open Panel Office Furniture and the Building and Institutional Furniture
10888
Manufacturers Association’s BIFMA M7.1-2007 specification (adopted as an ANSI standard). [Update:
10889
add Calif “Gold Standard” for carpet.]
10890
10891
These diverse labeling/specification systems are summarized in Table 5.1-D. Global efforts at
10892
harmonization of these systems (such as the establishment of an ASTM testing protocol) have not been
10893
successful due to strong commercial interests. However, if these efforts are successful in the future they
10894
will hopefully lead to greater consistency along scientifically sound test methods and evaluation criteria
10895
in terms of test conditions, number/ selection of individual VOCs quantified, time point(s) of testing, as
10896
well as frequency of testing (ie requirement for annual re-certification). The Emissions Testing section
10897
outlines key factors essential to the development of emissions testing leading to prediction of true IAQ
10898
impact of building materials/finishes/furnishings.
10899
10900
As can be seen from Table 5.1-D, labeling systems for rating product emissions can vary greatly in their
10901
sophistication. They may address relatively few compounds (such as formaldehyde, styrene, 4PC), or
10902
they may include far greater detail in terms of the specified compounds for certification. European
10903
systems tend to give greater attention to odor aspects of material emissions. This is an important
10904
consideration, valuable in terms of occupant’s perceptions of IAQ, but it should not take precedence
10905
over toxicological impact.
10906
10907
Formaldehyde-only product emission specifications include those mandated in the US by the
10908
Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD). In Canada, formaldehyde emissions from
10909
wood products are self-regulated by industry to meet HUD specifications. Additional details (including
10910
formaldehyde-based Environmentally Preferable Product certification of wood-based materials) can be
10911
found in the Composite Wood and Agrifiber Materials section.
10912
10913
Table 5.1-D: Summary of product labeling systems – VOC Emissions Labels
10914
Program Name (Region, Developer/Sponsor, Date Established;
Link) General Comments
Emissions Test Protocol and
Specified Compounds
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 359
Return to TOP
Program Name (Region, Developer/Sponsor, Date Established;
Link) General Comments
Emissions Test Protocol and
Specified Compounds
BIFMA M7.1-2007 (USA, Building and Institutional Furniture
Manufacturers Association, 2005)
• Standard Test Method For Determining VOC Emissions From Office
Furniture Systems, Components And Seating
• Chamber air change rate specifications:
o 0.65 to 1.09 ac/h for complete workstations (33 m3 chamber)
o 0.9 to 1.5 ac/h for workstation components (6 m3 chamber)
o 1.0 ac/h for workstation materials (0.05 to 0.1 m3 chamber)
• Emissions @ 72h & 168h
• Conditions:
o 23oC
o 50% RH
o 1 m2/m3
• Requires the analysis of:
o Formaldehyde
o VOCs classified according to chemical classes including:
- Aliphatic hydrocarbons (Alkanes),
- Aromatic hydrocarbons,
- Halogenated hydrocarbons (Halocarbons),
- Terpenes,
- Esters,
- Aldehydes and ketones (excl. formaldehyde), and
- Other
o Identification of any peaks whose area is greater than
10% of the total area of the peaks in its class.
o Quantification of:
- any VOCs from ECA-TVOC list (1997)
- 10 most abundant compounds among the identified
VOCs
- individual VOCs of specific interest (e.g., as required
by a certification program)
BIFMA X7.1 (USA, Building and Institutional Furniture
Manufacturers Association)
Proposed Standard for Formaldehyde and TVOC Emissions of Low-
emitting Office Furniture Systems and Seating
• deals specifically with Formaldehyde and TVOC as
opposed to “M7.1” which examines specific individual
VOCs.
Blue Angel (Germany, Federal Ministry of the Environment, Nature
Protection and Nuclear Safety, administered/sponsored by Federal
Environmental Agency, 1977, www.blauer-engel.de/englisch/)
• Original Environmental label, now covers nearly 4000 products in about
80 categories
• Includes low-emission requirements for certain products:
o Composite wood panels (particleboard, fiberboard, plywood)
o Wood products and wood based products (flooring, residential
furniture, office furniture, wood panels)
o Floor covering adhesives and other installation materials
o Upholstery
o Wall paints
• Emissions @ 1d & 28d
• Test Conditions:
o 23oC
o 45% RH
o 1 ac/h
o 1 m2/m3
• Product-specific limits on VOCs
o Wood products: analysis for 141 target VOCs, specific
concentration limits for Formaldehyde; low MW VOCs
(BP 50-250°C); high MW VOCs (BP > 250° C); and
CMT compounds
California 01350 (California “Special Environmental Requirements,
Specifications”, 2000,
www.ciwmb.ca.gov/GreenBuilding/Specs/Section01350/)
and Standard Practice for the Testing of Volatile Organic Emissions from
Various Sources Using Small-Scale Environmental Chambers
(CA/DHS/EHLB/R-174) http://www.cal-
iaq.org/VOC/Section01350\_7\_15\_2004\_FINAL\_PLUS\_ADDENDUM-
2004-01.pdf
requires:
• Specific procedures for specimen receiving, handling, and preparation
o Conditioning of test specimens for 10 days at 23 +/- 2oC and 50 +/-
10% RH, followed by a 96-hr test
o Sample collection, 24, 48 and 96 hr, following completion of 10-day
conditioning period, based on small chamber tests as per ASTM
Standard D5116-97
• Calculated emissions factors of the identified chemicals of concern then
used to calculate the “modeled” indoor air concentrations for a standard
office space or a classroom application using default ventilation rates,
quantities (surface area, fault length, or units) of the material to be
installed, and space volumes.
• Emissions @ 14 d (10d conditioning + 96h chamber)
• Compounds on California’s Office of Environmental Health
Hazard Assessment (OEHHA) list of chemical with non-
cancer Chronic Reference Exposure Levels (CREL)
(http://www.oehha.ca.gov/air/chronic\_rels/AllChrels.html )
o Modeled results must not exceed 50% of the CREL
value after 96h (plus 10d conditioning)
o Formaldehyde: no single product’s modeled
concentration can contribute > half of a total maximum
concentration limit of 33µg/m3 (27 ppb)
o Acetaldehyde: no single product’s modeled
concentration can contribute > 100% of a total maximum
concentration limit of 9µg/m3
• Carcinogens and reproductive toxicants on California’s
Proposition 65 list – need to be reported
• Compounds on the California Air Resources Board (CARB)
list of Toxic Air Contaminants (TAC) – need to be reported
• The 10 most abundant VOCs not included on any of the
above lists need to be reported.

Page 360

Program Name (Region, Developer/Sponsor, Date Established;
Link) General Comments
Emissions Test Protocol and
Specified Compounds
California Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) Specifications For Open Panel
Office Furniture
http://www.cal-iaq.org/VOC/Spec/CA\_FurnitureSpec-IAQ\_2007-04-
12.pdf
California Target VOC list (32 VOCs): products emissions
compared to maximum allowable concentrations in a standard
office scenario based on emission factors from a standardized
furniture configuration; concentrations for 10 most abundant
VOCs not listed above must also be reported
Collaborative for High Performance Schools, CHPS (California, 2001,
www.chps.net)
• Follows California 1350 specification
• Emissions @ 14 d (10d conditioning + 96h chamber)
• Materials satisfying criteria published
at http://www.chps.net/manual/lem\_table.htm
FloorScore (USA, Resilient Floor Covering Institute - RFCI,
www.rfci.com)
• Developed by RFCI in collaboration with SCS
• SCS serves as 3rd party certifier
• California 1350-based certification of flooring materials including tile,
sheet vinyl, linoleum or rubber products for residential and commercial
flooring
• Emissions @ 14 d (10d conditioning + 96h chamber)
• Cal Section 01350
GEV EMICODE (Germany, GEV, 1990, www.emicode.com/)
• GEV = Association for the Control of Emissions in Products for
Flooring Installation
• Industry-managed, covers flooring installation products (including
primers, leveling compounds, screed materials, adhesives, surface
sealing compounds, underlays).
• Three TVOC-based emissions classes: EC 1 (very low), EC 2 (low), EC
3 (not low) with separate levels for Liquid, Powder-based, Pasty, Ready-
to-use underlays etc, and joint sealant products
• Test per ISO 16 000 part 6
• Test Conditions:
o 23oC
o 50% RH
o 0.5 ac/h
o 0.4 m2/m3
Green Label (USA, Carpet and Rug Institute – CRI, 1992)
• Industry-designed and administered, applies only to carpets, carpet
adhesives, and carpet cushions.
• Emissions @ 24 h
• Carpet: formaldehyde, 4-PC, styrene, TVOC
• Adhesive: formaldehyde, 2-ethyl-1-hexanol, TVOC
• Cushion: formaldehyde, BHT, 4-PC, TVOC
Green Label Plus (USA, Carpet and Rug Institute – CRI,
2004, www.carpet-rug.com/index.cfm )
• Revised version of Green Label program developed to satisfy
California's CHPS Criteria
• Emissions @ 1d and 14 d
• future testing at 1d only if 1st test finds good correlation
• Carpet:
o <1/2 of the current OEHHA CREL values
o TVOC, Acetaldehyde, Benzene, Caprolactam, 2-
Ethylhexanoic Acid, Formaldehyde, 1-Methyl-2-
Pyrrolidinone, Naphthalene, Nonanal, Octanal, 4-
Phenylcyclohexene, Styrene, Toluene, Vinyl Acetate
Greenguard (USA, Greenguard Environmental Institute - GEI,
1996; www.greenguard.org/)
• Certification programs developed and administered by Greenguard
Environmental Institute (GEI)
• Emissions testing conducted by AQS
• Results are confidential
• Emissions typically @ 168 h (7d)
• Conditions:
o 23oC
o 50% RH
o air change rate not specified
o 0.4 - 1 m2/m3
• Product-specific, GEI-established “allowable emission
levels” (in ppm or mg/m3) based on the material/product
being used in a room volume of 32 m3 with an outdoor air
change rate of 0.8 AC/h (product loading not specified) for
certain VOCs (formaldehyde, total aldehydes, 4-
Phenylcyclohexene, and styrene), TVOCs, and, where
applicable, respirable particles, ozone, and other pollutants,
• Carcinogens and reproductive toxins found in California
Proposition 65, US NTP or IARC,
• US EPA’s national ambient air quality standards,
• 1/10 of ACGIH 8 hour TWA-TLV value
Greenguard Children & Schools (USA, Greenguard Environmental
Institute - GEI, 2005; www.greenguard.org/)
• revised version of Greenguard certification, developed to meet 01350
and CHPS specifications
• same as Greenguard, except:
o 1/100 TLV,
o ½ OEHHA CREL,
o plus limits on TVOC, formaldehyde, aldehydes,
phthalates, and particles ≤10um

Link) General Comments
Emissions Test Protocol and
Specified Compounds
GuT (Germany, Association of Environmentally-Friendly Carpets,
1990)
• Based on the ECA-18-system
• Emissions @ 72 h (3d)
• Includes odor testing
• Uses the LCI (Lowest Concentration of Interest) table
published by AgBB (http://www.umweltdaten.de/daten-
e/agbb.pdf )
• Prohibits carcinogens vs. EU list Classes 1 and 2
Indoor Advantage (USA, Scientific Certification Systems –
SCS, www.scscertified.com/iaq/ )
• Designed to meet indoor emissions limits defined by the LEED program
for furniture and seating; paints and coatings; adhesives and sealants.
Indoor Advantage Gold (USA, Scientific Certification Systems -
SCS, www.scscertified.com/iaq/ )
• Designed to meet the indoor emissions limits required by the California
01350 program.
• Applies to any nonflooring product generally used within an enclosed
indoor environment such as wall coverings, systems furniture, casework,
and insulation.
Product-dependent protocols
Indoor Climate Label (Denmark and Norway, 1995, www.dsic.org)
• Normative bodies:
o Danish Society of Indoor Climate
o Norwegian Forum of Indoor Climate Labelling
• Assessment protocols developed for:
o Wall and ceiling systems
o Carpets
o Interior doors and folding partitions
o Windows and exterior doors
o Resilient floors, wood-based floors and laminated floors
o Oils for wood-based floors
o Kitchen, bath and wardrobe cabinets
o Interior building paint
o Furniture
• Emissions @ 28 d (in cells or conventional chambers)
• Includes sensory odor testing
• Threshold values for odour and irritation used are those
given in VOCBASE
• “indoor-relevant time-value” calculated = time required for
most slowly emitting individual substances to fall below
their odour and irritation thresholds.
M-1, M-2 (Finland, 1995, www.rts.fi/english.htm )
• Developed by the Finnish Society of Indoor Air Quality and Climate
(FiSIAQ)
• Carcinogens identified vs. IARC
10915
Emissions Databases
10916
In addition to published reports on emissions data from building materials (eg. Hodgson and Girman,
10917
1983; Tichenor and Mason, 1988; Cinalli etal 1993; Clausen etal, 1996; Yu and Crump, 1998;
10918
Alevantis etal 2003), several limited databases containing product emission information do currently
10919
exist (Table 5.1-E). They vary greatly in the type and detail of the information provided. Most contain
10920
data for single conditions of temperature and humidity. Since emissions may be strongly dependent on
10921
environmental factors, the data should not be used to predict impact for conditions falling beyond those
10922
employed during testing (this includes materials subject to direct solar gains, radiant heating, and air
10923
velocity conditions in the case of “wet-applied” products were evaporation is key to initial emission
10924
rates). Product variability is also a priority concern when using emissions data: this aspect includes
10925
batch-batch variability of product, homogeneity of specimens, as well as the changing nature of product
10926
formulations and manufacturing processes can significantly influence emissions characteristics and
10927
render older data obsolete. Careful attention needs to be paid to both the currency of the data and the
10928
test conditions employed. For design purposes, preference is always to obtain test specimens directly
10929
from manufacturers of product specimens that will actually be used in the building’s construction.
10930
10931
The EPA source-ranking database contains information on a wide range of products, but with limited
10932
detail for any given material. In contrast, IRC’s IA-QUEST database and simulation tool (Fig. 5.1-F)
10933
provides detailed emissions data for a limited set of generically identified products. IA-QUEST provides
10934
designers and manufacturers with a tool with which to assess product impact and facilitate development
10935
of low emission alternatives. It can quickly estimate the impact of multiple products on the indoor
10936
concentration of individual VOCs for either constant or changing ventilation conditions. As guideline
10937
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 362
Return to TOP
values develop, this capability will support evaluation of product acceptability for different indoor
10938
conditions and thus support labeling systems. Analysis of the impact of adjusting ventilation rates,
10939
while meeting acceptable guideline values, is possible with the tool in support of ASHRAE Standard’s
10940
62.1-2007 IAQ Procedure.
10941
10942
NIST’s ContamLink tool makes use of both the EPA and IRC databases, linking the contained data for
10943
access by their Contam multi-zone model. The CHPS Low-emitting materials table provides emissions
10944
data for products that have been tested according to the 01350 protocol, while the Building Materials
10945
Emissions Study reports results for 77 materials (containing both recycled and non-recycled materials).
10946
10947

Figure 5.1-F Screen shots from IA-QUEST emissions database/VOC concentration simulation
10952
10953
10954
[Editor’s Note: The following sections on individual products are still in bullet format, and will be
10955
edited to text for next version. The more detailed material presented here will be moved into an
10956
Appendix as background info, not true “Guidance.”]
10957
10958
Priority Materials/Finishes/Furnishings
10959
• Many factors in selection of appropriate materials (structural strength, durability, cleanability,
10960
flame resistance). Resistance to microbial contamination may be of concern for certain
10961
applications/situations, but in general, widespread use of treatments or use of materials
10962
specifically formulated to prevent microbial growth (including isothiazolinines, azoles, and
10963
pyrethiones) should be discouraged. Materials that are well designed for a given indoor
10964
application and that receive proper care and maintenance will not likely be subject to problematic
10965
biocontamination. The treatments employed, often seemingly unnecessarily, may have negative
10966
impact both in terms of release of contaminants indoors, and in development of microbial
10967
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 364
Return to TOP
resistance. Triclosan for example, added as an antifungal agent, is persistent in the environment,
10968
bioaccumulative, and has been identified as a possible endocrine disruptor.
10969
10970
Architectural Coatings
10971
•
“Architectural Coatings” covers a broad class of products including sealers, primers, paints,
10972
enamels, clear wood finishes (lacquers, varnishes, etc), shellacs, stains, and fire-retardant
10973
coatings,
10974
•
Use water based, low-VOC products where possible
10975
•
Important to note that, as with other materials, VOC emissions from the applied coating product
10976
is not the sole factor in determining overall product related emissions. Durability is an important
10977
consideration from two aspects: a long-lasting product requires less frequent touch-up or re-
10978
application, hence lower emissions, and a finished surface that requires less cleaning and/or re-
10979
surfacing will contribute fewer emissions due to the use of cleaning agents or waxes, sealers, etc.
10980
•
Paints – history (lead, …. VOCs): from MPI website: “Historically the world of paint and
10981
coatings was not an environmentally friendly one! Some paints contained mercury, some arsenic
10982
and most of us know about paints that contained lead. Paints (today) contain both organic and
10983
inorganic compounds or materials, some of which may adversely impact our environment by
10984
releasing solvents or other toxic materials at various stages of the product life-cycle. Some
10985
reports indicate that almost 4% of the ozone depleting substances in the U.S. are a result of VOC
10986
emissions from paint & coating products”.
10987
o “VOC-Free” paints – EPA definition (vs IA-QUEST data?)
10988
o Paint VOC content often based on “base” paint only, high VOC content of added
10989
pigments is not considered – need to test the final tinted paint
10990
o Substrate impact – paint on drywall; stain on maple vs oak?
10991
10992

South coast rule
10994
•
From Cal.DHS Building Materials Emission Study: 1350Products advertised as low or no VOC
10995
are regulated under the Clean Air Act. The definition of VOCs under the Clean Air Act excludes
10996
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 365
Return to TOP
numerous compounds of concern for indoor air quality simply because they are not reactive and,
10997
therefore, do not contribute significantly to the formation of photochemical smog. However, in
10998
indoor environments, all VOCs that have a potential impact on human health, comfort or
10999
performance are important to consider. Therefore, the definition of VOCs under the Clean Air
11000
Act is not appropriate for indoor air considerations.
11001
11002
Table 5.1-G
11003
Acrolein
Diethyl phthalate
Formaldehyde
Methylene Chloride
Acrylonitrile

Asbestos
Di-n-octyl phthalate
Lead
1,1,1 –trichloroethane
Benzene
1,2 –dichlorobenzene

Butyl benzyl phthalate Di (2-ethylhexyl)
phthalate
