Methyl isobutyl ketone Free Crystalline Silica
11004
•
Emissions highly substrate-dependent (eg wood stain from maple vs oak), testing according to
11005
standardized protocols essential, test on intended substrate if possible
11006
•
189P: . Paints and coatings used on the interior of the building (defined as inside of the
11007
weatherproofing system and applied on-site) shall comply with the following criteria:
11008
o Architectural paints, coatings and primers applied to interior walls and ceilings: VOC
11009
content shall be determined according to and meet the limit requirements of Green Seal
11010
Standard GS-11.
11011
o Clear wood finishes, floor coatings, stains, sealers, and shellacs: VOC content shall be
11012
determined according to and meet the limit requirements of SCAQMD Rule 1113.
11013
I-BEAM:
11014
•
Establish a protocol for painting and insure that the protocol is followed by both in-house
11015
personnel and by contractors.
11016
o Use low VOC emission, fast drying paints where feasible.
11017
o Paint during unoccupied hours.
11018
o Keep lids on paint containers when not in use.
11019
o Ventilate the building with significant quantities of outside air during and after painting.
11020
Insure a complete building flush prior to occupancy.
11021
o Use more than normal outside air ventilation for some period after occupancy.
11022
o Avoid spraying, when possible.
11023
•
Give example of “VOC-Free” paint from IA-QUEST
11024
11025
Flooring Materials
11026
•
General comments:
11027
o Types: Resilient tile, laminate, hardwood, linoleum, carpet

o Consider impact of any required adhesives and/or maintenance products that will be

o emissions from carpet of styrene and of 4-phenylcyclohexene (4-PCH), a by-product of
11095
the styrene butadiene rubber (SBR) latex manufacturing process, would rapidly react with
11096

11097

11098
•
linoleum: Ozone also reacts with the oils found in linseed oil resulting in the very strong and
11099
persistent odor of linoleum floor covering. These oils, composed primarily of esters of linolenic,
11100
linoleic, and oleic acids, have also been found by other researchers to form the aldehydes and
11101
odorous compounds emitted from linoleum and other products using linseed oil in their
11102
formulation (Morrison and Nazaroff, 2002).

11120
fiberboard (MDF), plywood, wheatboard, strawboard, panel substrates and door cores. Materials

Structural plywood conforming to PS1 and PS2 and oriented strand board shall be made with
11130

11131

•
Composite Panel Asociation: Environmentally Preferable Product (EPP) Grademark Program
11134
(http://www.pbmdf.com/Index.asp?bid=1087 ). Deals with emissions of formaldehyde only. No
11135
other VOC emissions are addressed.
11136
o FORMALDEHYDE EMISSIONS REQUIREMENT
11137
o Unfinished Particleboard. Formaldehyde emissions from unfinished particleboard must
11138
be less than or equal to 0.20 ppm using the Large Chamber Test Method (ASTM E1333).
11139
Particleboard products will be evaluated at the typical loading rate for particleboard of
11140
0.13 ft2/ft3. Particleboard that uses a bonding system other than Urea Formaldehyde,
11141
may qualify for “Exempted” status under section 6.3 of the EPP Grademark Manual. One
11142
exception to this requirement is for Grade LD of ANSI A208.1-1999 (Door Core)
11143
products. Grade LD is allowed a loading ratio of 0.04 ft2/ft3 as per section 3.4 of ANSI
11144
A208.1-1999.
11145
o Unfinished MDF. Formaldehyde emissions from unfinished MDF must be less than or
11146
equal to 0.20 ppm using the Large Chamber Test Method (ASTM E1333). MDF products
11147
will be evaluated at the typical loading rate for MDF of 0.08 ft2/ft3. Special arrangements
11148
will be made for MDF manufacturers who wish to have the MDF tested at the higher
11149
loading ratio of 0.13 ft2/ft3. MDF that uses a bonding system other than Urea
11150
Formaldehyde, may qualify for “Exempted” status under section 6.3 of the EPP
11151
Grademark Manual.
11152
o Hardboard. Formaldehyde emissions from unfinished hardboard must be less than or
11153
equal to 0.20 ppm using the Large Chamber Test Method (ASTM E1333). There are no
11154
specifications in the three relevant hardboard standards (ANSI A135.4, ANSI A135.5,
11155
ANSI A135.6) that require or recommend a loading ratio for hardboard products.
11156
Hardboard is most similar to MDF and will be tested with the loading ratio of MDF at
11157
0.08 ft2/ft3. Hardboard that uses a bonding system other than Urea Formaldehyde, may
11158
qualify for “Exempted” status under section 6.3 of the EPP Grademark Manual.
11159

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

Return to TOP
o Purchasing pressed wood products such as particleboard, MDF, or hardwood plywood for
11176
construction or remodeling of homes, or for do-it-yourself projects, that are labeled or
11177
stamped to be in conformance with American National Standards Institute (ANSI)
11178
criteria. Particleboard should be in conformance with ANSI A208.1-1993. For
11179

11180
the panel. MDF should be in conformance with ANSI A208.2-1994; and hardwood
11181
plywood with ANSI/HPVA HP-1-1994. These standards all specify lower formaldehyde
11182
emission levels.
11183
o Purchasing furniture or cabinets that contain a high percentage of panel surface and edges
11184
that are laminated or coated. Unlaminated or uncoated (raw) panels of pressed wood
11185
products will generally emit more formaldehyde than those that are laminated or coated.
11186
o Using alternative products such as wood panel products not made with urea-
11187
formaldehyde glues, lumber or metal.

11253
11254
PVC Materials
11255
Polyvinyl chloride (“PVC or “vinyl”) is found in thousands of common synthetic materials. Data from
11256
the American Plastics Council (2002) indicate that approximately 75% of the annual North American
11257
PVC production (>14 billion pounds) is used in the manufacture of construction materials (including
11258
PVC-based flooring and vinyl-covered wallboards). A wide range of VOCs and SVOCs are emitted
11259
from PVCs, including plasticizers, solvent residues, unreacted monomers, and secondary degradation
11260
products (Järnström etal 2008). Phthalates are added as plasticizers, primarily to give flexibility to the
11261
products. DBP (Di-n-butyl-phthalate), DIBP (Di-iso-butyl-phthalate), DEHP (Di(2-ethylhexyl)-
11262
phthalate) were commonly used in PVC until ~2000, now DINP (Di-iso-nonyl-phthalate) is increasingly
11263
used (Pohle 1997; Lorz etal 2002). Several phthalates, especially DEHP, are suspected to have
11264
significant adverse health impact, including carcinogenic and teratogenic effects and soft PVC used in
11265
wall coverings may contain more than 30% phthalic acid esters (Wensing etal 2005). Damp PVC
11266
flooring has been associated with asthma development in children (Bornehag et al 2002), apparently due
11267
to phthalate hydrolysis (see section on Indoor Chemistry).
11268

Contaminant Emissions: Essential Concepts
11401
[Editor’s Note: The bulk of this material will be moved into an Appendix as background info, not true
11402
“Guidance”]
11403
11404
Material emissions impacting the indoor environment may be released as volatile chemicals or as
11405
particulates (or both as in the case of chemicals adsorbed onto particulate surfaces). A basic
11406
understanding of the key terms associated with emissions is required to be able to interpret the
11407
information available: definitions are provided in Table 5.1-A with links for more detailed descriptions.
11408
11409
Total VOC and Target VOCs
11410
• general VOC definition
11411
• EPA (ambient focus, excluded compounds) vs IAQ definitions of VOC
11412
• Envtl choice CCD-146: "volatile organic compound" or "VOC" means any organic compound
11413
which participates in atmospheric photochemical reactions to create smog. It excludes those
11414
organic compounds which the ECP designates as having negligible photochemical reactivity
11415
found in Appendix 3. The methods to calculate VOC content are:
11416
• EPA Method 24-24A, 40 C.F.R., Part 60, Appendix A (1991),
11417
• Method 18,48 Federal Register 48, no. 202, October 18, 1983,
11418
• Method 1400 NIOSH Manual of Analytical Methods, Volume 1, February 1984,
11419
• Environmental Protection Agency Method 8240 GC/MS Method for Volatile Organics,
11420
September 1986; or
11421
• demonstrated through calculation from records of the amounts of constituents used to make the
11422
product where volatile means vapour pressure >0.01 KPa at 20°C.
11423
11424
TVOC vs “TVOC” eg (20) compound sum per Mølhave)
11425
• attractive due to apparent simplicity – but it is not simple
11426
• Include:
11427
o early use of TVOC;
11428
o Mølhave L, Nielsen, GD (1992) caution about limitations of VOC
11429
o Mølhave in 2003
11430
o ASHRAE 62.1
11431
11432
Effects on comfort, irritation, and health of building occupants are highly dependent on the properties of
11433
individual compounds. Lumping the emissions together and reporting total emissions as a single value
11434
thus makes little sense. Additionally, TVOC estimation is reliant on the particular analytical method
11435
employed: different methods will lead to different TVOC estimations.
11436
11437
11438
“There is insufficient evidence that TVOC measurements can be used to predict health or
11439
comfort effects. In addition, odor and irritation responses to organic compounds are highly
11440
variable. Furthermore, no single method currently in use measures all organic compounds that
11441
may be of interest. Setting target concentrations for TVOCs is not recommended. Setting target
11442
concentrations for specific VOCs of concern is preferred.”
11443
11444
-ANSI/ASHRAE 62.1: Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality
11445
11446
11447
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 378
Return to TOP
11448
11449
“TVOC air concentrations are approximations and are typically determined by summing the
11450
areas of all gas chromatographic peaks derived from test methods such as D 5466 or D 6196.
11451
The TVOC air concentration values so derived depend on the type of air sampler; the type of
11452
GC detector and how it is calibrated; the collection, retention, and recovery efficiencies of the
11453
sorbent trap, canister, or other sampling device; the efficiency of transfer to the gas
11454
chromatographic (GC) column; the type and size of the GC column; the GC temperature
11455
program and other chromatographic parameters; how the concentration is derived from the
11456
peak area (for example, whether single or multiple internal standards are used, as well as the
11457
types of reference standards); and the composition of the air sample (for example, the relative
11458
abundances of hydrocarbon, halogenated, or oxygenated compounds).”
11459
11460
- ASTM D1356: Standard Terminology Relating to Sampling and Analysis of Atmospheres
11461
11462
Due to this ambiguity in both analytical terms and in occupant impact, Mølhave in 2003 stated that the
11463
usefulness of the TVOC concept for prediction of health effects of chemicals mixtures is undocumented
11464
and cannot be used for risk assessment. In spite of this growing criticism, TVOC continues to be
11465
(mis)applied in IAQ characterization and building material labeling schemes.
11466
11467
Some confusion also arises due to the use of ambient air quality-based definitions of VOCs which focus
11468
exclusively on compounds leading to photochemical reactions associated with smog formation and
11469
ozone depletion (see Table xxxxx for US-EPA definition of VOC). This EPA VOC definition is
11470
misleading when applied to indoor conditions or to ratings of “VOC-free” products since several
11471
compounds of concern from an IAQ standpoint are excluded.
11472
11473
Semi-Volatile Organic Compounds (SVOCs)
11474
Semi-volatile compounds (including phthalates, flame-retardants and pesticides); very volatile
11475
compounds (including formaldehyde); and “secondary” emissions (including transient, reactive chemical
11476
species and/or fine particulates formed by hydrolysis or oxidation by agents such as ozone) are
11477
increasingly recognized as key factors influencing IAQ. With the exception of formaldehyde, they often
11478
fall outside the analytical window considered by most existing product labeling systems.
11479
11480
SVOCs are by definition organic compounds with saturation vapor pressures between 10−2 and 10−8 kPa
11481
at 25°C (ASTM D1356, 2005). Common SVOCs found in indoor air include plasticizers such as
11482
phthalic acid esters (phthalates) and organophosphate flame retardants (used in indoor materials such as
11483
fabrics/ textiles, plastics and wood-based materials). Their lower volatility relative to “VOCs” means
11484
that SVOCs are typically emitted at slow rates for long time periods. Indoor exposure to SVOCs is
11485
therefore characterized by chronic exposures to low levels. Their impact on health can be significant,
11486
with several SVOCs implicated as carcinogens or in the development of asthma.
11487
11488
Indoor Chemistry – Secondary Emissions:
11489
Primary emissions from building products and materials, whether VVOC, VOC, SVOC or particulate
11490
are not the only IAQ concern. Under conditions that may readily occur in indoor environments, these
11491
initial contaminants may react to form “secondary” products that may be more important in terms of
11492
occupant irritation and/or health. The reactions involved are the domain of “indoor chemistry”
11493
(Morrison 2007) and typically occur at material surfaces (hence “surface” or “interfacial” chemistry).
11494
As stated by Weschler and Shields (1997): “From a chemical perspective, the indoor environment is a
11495
reaction vessel … many of the resulting products are more reactive and/or irritating that their
11496
precursors”
11497
11498
Oxidation or hydrolysis of primary emissions are common reactions. Ozone is one oxidizing agent,
11499
which on reacting with unsaturated compounds such as terpenes from wood-based materials results in
11500
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 379
Return to TOP
the formation of aldehydes (e.g. formaldehyde – a know carcinogen) as well as strongly irritating
11501
compounds such as organic acids, carbonyls and dicarbonyl compounds (believed to be especially
11502
irritating). These secondary compounds, formed at typical indoor ozone levels, have been shown to
11503
occur at levels sufficient to result in health/comfort concerns (Weschler 2004). Once promoted as
11504
beneficial in reducing indoor VOC levels, ozone-generators are now banned by the California Air
11505
Resources Board (ARB) due to their unintentional formation of harmful by-products, including the
11506
generation of fine particulates.
11507
11508
NOx released indoors from combustion appliances, or brought into a building from exterior sources is
11509
another oxidizing agent associated with secondary emissions. Hydrolysis reactions may also occur: for
11510
example the hydrolysis of DEHP plasticizer (refer to section on PVC Materials). DEHP hydrolysis
11511
occurs more readily when catalyzed by basic conditions provided by certain concrete flooring and
11512
gypsum board surfaces, and leads to the formation of by-products (including 2-ethyl-1-hexanol) linked
11513
to asthma (Norback etal 2000).
11514
11515
Solutions to reducing the impact of secondary emissions on IAQ include reducing indoor ozone levels
11516
(for example through the use of active carbon, “AC”, filtration of outdoor air), through direct venting of
11517
indoor combustion sources to remove NOx, through moisture control to reduce hydrolysis reactions, and
11518
through reduction of the feedstock “primary” emissions through source control via careful selection of
11519
building products, materials and furnishings.
11520
11521
Recognition of the involvement of indoor ozone in the formation of irritating by-products through
11522
secondary reactions with indoor VOCs has resulted in the use of ozone generators as “air cleaning”
11523
devices being discouraged.
11524
11525
Certain cleaning products used indoors have been highlighted as significant contributors to indoor
11526
chemistry. Those that contain terpenes, glycols, etc in their formulation are of particular concern
11527
(CARB Report, 2006). Refer to the section of this guide dealing with building cleaning (Minimize IAQ
11528
Impacts Associated with Cleaning and Maintenance) for more detailed discussion of this aspect.
11529
11530
Ref Hoang 2008 report (Corsi) – 2o emns from ceiling tile in presence of O3
11531
 Poppendieck et al. [34] focused on by-product formation from 24 materials during and after a
11532
disinfection period in which the materials were exposed to 1000 ppm of ozone. The largest
11533
total by-product mass releases were observed for three materials - paper, office partitioning
11534
material, and medium density fiberboard. Some by-products were observed to persist for
11535
months after ozonation. (Poppendieck, D.G., et al., 2007; Formation and emissions of
11536
carbonyls during and following gas-phase ozonation of indoor materials. Atmospheric
11537
Environment, 41 (35): 7614-7626)
11538
 Building ozonation by-products (BOBPs)

RoC, published biennially, is an informational scientific and

circumstances that may pose a hazard to human health by virtue
of their carcinogenicity. Current version (11th) contains 246
entries.

IAQ Guidelines, Standards and Specifications
11594
The first step in effectively reporting on, or rating product emissions, is to identify specific “target”
11595
emissions of concern. The criteria for their selection should include known or potential impact on
11596
occupant health or comfort; presence (known or expected) within materials used in building construction,
11597
finishing or furnishing; and ability by current analytical means for their detection and accurate
11598
quantification. Resulting “target” lists will be dynamic: depending on current knowledge regarding
11599
physiological impact, analytical capabilities, and the continuous development of new materials and
11600
technologies. On-going evaluation of target compounds is a necessary exercise (eg. Wolkoff etal 1997).
11601
11602
For outdoor air or indoor air in industrial/occupational settings (see Table 5.1-N), specific regulations
11603
exist governing allowable concentrations of specific compounds (e.g. NAAQS, ACGIH, OSHA). This is
11604
not the case for indoor environments in commercial or institutional buildings, where no legislation
11605
currently exists for contaminant levels. Non-industrial indoor environments are also distinct in terms of
11606
both the occupants themselves and the nature and extent of contaminant exposures. For these reasons,
11607
the use of occupational levels (or arbitrary arithmetic fractions of occupational levels) is inappropriate
11608
(see text box below).
11609
• Many “guideline” IAQ standards are TVOC-based, with the exception of formaldehyde, very little
11610
specific guidance is available concerning specific compounds
11611
•
Alevantis, L. (1999) Reducing Occupant Exposure to Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) from Office
11612
Building Construction Materials: Non-Binding Guidelines – Appendix E4.
11613
(www.dhs.ca.gov/ps/deodc/VOC/VOCapp.PDF ).
11614
• "endocrine disruptor" means an exogenous substance or mixture that alters function(s) of the
11615
endocrine system and consequently causes adverse health effects in an intact organism, or its progeny,
11616
or (sub)populations. Candidate endocrine disruptors are listed in Appendix 1 of Towards the
11617
Establishment of a Priority List of Substances for Further Evaluation of Their Role in Endocrine
11618
Disruption prepared for the European Union; (Source: ECP CCD-148)
11619

OSHA sets enforceable permissible exposure limits (PELs) to
protect workers against the health effects of exposure to
hazardous substances. PELs are regulatory limits on the amount
or concentration of a substance in the air based on an 8-hour

11627
“Caution must be used in directly extending the ACGIH TLVs® or other workplace guidelines to
11628
spaces covered by this standard and to population groups other than workers. Industrial health
11629
practice attempts to limit worker exposure to injurious substances at levels that do not interfere
11630
with the industrial work process and do not risk the workers' health and safety. There is not an
11631
intention to eliminate all effects, such as unpleasant smells or mild irritation. Further, the health
11632
criteria are not uniformly derived for all contaminants. Irritation, narcosis, and nuisance or other
11633
forms of stress are not uniformly considered as the basis for the concentration limits. This is
11634
because different organizations use different end points and different contaminants have more
11635
or less information available on diverse end points of interest. The target population is also
11636
different from the occupants found in the spaces covered by this standard. Healthy industrial
11637
workers tend to change jobs or occupations if an exposure is intolerable. In contrast, workers in
11638
commercial environments such as offices do not expect to have elevated concentrations of
11639
potentially harmful substances, nor are monitoring programs in place, as may be the case with
11640
industrial contaminants. In addition, the general population may have less choice about where
11641
they spend most of their time and includes those who may be more sensitive, such as children,
11642
asthmatics, allergic individuals, and the elderly”
11643
11644
- ANSI/ASHRAE 62.1: Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality

11649
Setting targets is a two-step process: identification of the specific compounds of concern followed by
11650
establishment of guidance concentrations for individual contaminants. Nielsen etal (1997) and Seifert
11651
etal (1999) have discussed the establishment of guidelines for indoor contaminants. Specific regulation
11652
in non-industrial indoor environments remains extremely sparse, although some guidance can be found:
11653
Table 5.3-? summarizes several key sources.
11654
11655
Formaldehyde can be used as an example of the discrepancy between occupational levels and proposed
11656
indoor guidance. OSHA and ACGIH regulations (0.75 and 0.3 ppm, Table 5.1-N) can be compared to
11657
the CREL recommendation of 0.002 ppm. Additional comparisons can be made using the NRCC Target
11658
List Table provided in the Appendix. This list of VOC targets for emissions from building materials
11659
was developed in 2003 by NRCC in collaboration with Health Canada and a variety of academic and
11660
government partners. The table summarizes available OSHA, ACGIH, and CREL values for the 90
11661
VOCs selected based on review of information. Odor and irritancy values are also reported. A report
11662
released by Won etal (2005a), summarizes the development of the list. Results for 69 tests of building
11663
materials using these 90 target compounds are reported in Won etal (2005b) and included in the publicly
11664
released IA-QUEST database (refer to the section on Emissions Databases and Data Analysis for
11665
additional details regarding this tool).
11666
11667
Independently, and during the same time period, a 121-compound VOC list was developed in California
11668
and released in the 2003 State of California - Dept. of Health Services report: Building Materials
11669
Emissions Study (64 of these compounds also appeared in the NRCC list).
11670
11671
In 1997, the European Commission – Joint Research Centre – Environment Institute, released their
11672
report on TVOC in IAQ Investigations which contained a list of 63 compounds to be included in any
11673
TVOC estimation. This list was used by BIFMA to help set targets for VOC emissions from office
11674
furniture.
11675
11676
Once target compounds have been identified, the next step in setting emissions guidelines involves the
11677
establishment of concentration levels for non-industrial indoor environments. Several guideline level
11678
documents exist (Table 5.1-O). Most provide recommendations for TVOC levels only and mention
11679
certain specific compounds of concern without setting target levels. ASHRAE 62.1 includes a brief
11680
discussion of existing guidelines. Charles etal (2005) recently conducted a brief review of IAQ
11681
guidelines and standards.
11682
11683

Page 387
Return to TOP
If emission rates and building ventilation conditions are known, then resulting occupant exposures can
11684
be estimated, but establishment of target or guideline concentrations is difficult for several reasons.
11685
Short-term (or acute) high level emissions of contaminants, while key to any exposed building
11686
occupants and potentially significant if porous materials in the area may result in their absorption and
11687
subsequent re-emission, may have lower overall effect than long-term (chronic) exposure to much lower
11688
levels. Some understanding of the kinetics of emission behavior is therefore vital in assessing impact on
11689
building occupants. In addition, occupants are generally exposed to complex mixtures of contaminants.
11690
Effects of individual compounds may not reflect the synergistic impact that mixtures can trigger.
11691
Mixtures also lead to formation of “secondary” products through chemical reactions, which may have
11692
irritation or health impacts different from the parent compounds. Some of these interactions are
11693
becoming understood (for example the formation of irritating compounds via oxidation of terpenes by
11694
ozone).
11695
11696
Investigations of the levels and nature of contaminants found in indoor environments have become
11697
increasingly detailed as analytical techniques advance. Coupled with growing knowledge related to the
11698
health impact (including comfort, odor tolerance and membrane irritation) of indoor contaminants, our
11699
focus on indoor contaminants of concern has shifted from TVOC to specific compounds.
11700
11701
California released a “Sustainable (Green) Building Modular Office Furniture Specification” in 2000,
11702
which included testing requirements for VOC emissions using the ECA 63 compound list. This
11703
specification set concentration limits for grouped classes of compounds (e.g. alkanes, aromatics,
11704
terpenes, aldehydes/ketones, etc). Furniture acceptance under this specification required emission testing
11705
followed by estimation of VOC concentrations for set building scenarios.
11706
11707
In 2007, the State of California’s DGS Procurement Division, in cooperation with numerous California
11708
State environmental agencies, as part of their Air Quality (IAQ) Specifications For Open Panel Office
11709
Furniture, advanced this process: requiring compliance for specific concentration limits set for an
11710
updated list of 32 target VOCs based on CREL values (with the exception of 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone).
11711
Further details can be found in the section on Office Furniture Systems.
11712
11713
Emissions Testing: Methods and Results
11714
Concerns regarding chemical emissions from building materials date back more than 30 years (eg.
11715
Mølhave, 1982). Matthews (1987); Tichenor (1989); Levin and Hodgson (1996); Wolkoff (1999) and
11716
Saarela etal (2002) have described protocols for determining VOC emissions from building materials.
11717
ASTM D 5116 serves as a basis for development on emissions test protocols. Tichenor prepared a
11718
comprehensive review in 2007 for the US-EPA entitled “Criteria for Evaluating Programs that Assess
11719
Materials/Products to Determine Impacts of Indoor Air Quality” (available
11720
at http://www.epa.gov/iaq/pdfs/tichenor\_report.pdf ). This document evaluates emissions testing criteria,
11721
data analysis, labelling systems, and existing US test laboratories. A summary of existing
11722
methods/protocols for conducting emission testing of building materials and products is provided in
11723
Table 5.1-P. It indicates the continuous evolution of emissions testing, as increasingly sophisticated
11724
protocols develop to identify products with low “COC” emissions.
11725
11726

11736
Test Protocols – Emissions Chambers
11737
Fundamental to valid emissions evaluation is the product test protocol from start to finish, including all
11738
aspects of product selection, specimen variability, specimen conditioning, testing (including chamber
11739
design/consruction, precise control of environmental conditions, test duration and sampling frequency),
11740
air sampling and analysis methods, and data interpretation and reporting. Emission chambers can vary
11741
considerably in size (from “cells” with internal volumes less than 1L to “full-scale” or “large” chambers
11742
that are room-size and can accommodate complete office workstations or building assemblies). Test
11743
duration and sampling frequency can be critical in order to discern fast decaying emissions from slow,
11744
long-term emitters that may in the end have greater impact on indoor air.
11745
11746
11747
Figure 5.1-P 50L Emissions Chamber (photo courtesy NRCC)
11748
11749
11750
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 390
Return to TOP
11751
Figure 5.1-Q Emissions Test: Glue-down carpet assembly. Example VOCs showing
11752
relatively high initial concentrations with steady decay (Source: CMEIAQ 2005)
11753
11754

11756

11757
lower initial concentrations but very slow decay rates (Source CMEIAQ 20)
11758
11759
Test Protocols – Material Assemblies
11760
Emission tests of individual materials is very informative and enables direct product-product
11761
comparisons, but may not accurately reflect their true impact on indoor air if they will eventually be
11762
installed as part of an assembly of materials. The real impact of selecting carpeting as an office flooring
11763
material, for example, cannot be determined by testing the carpet alone, if adhesives are required for the
11764
installation. The complete system of carpet + recommended adhesive product + concrete slab (which
11765
may absorb some of the VOCs from the adhesive then re-emit gradually) must be tested as a unit.
11766
Individual tests of the components are still needed to attribute sources, but emissions impact cannot be
11767
gauged unless the assembly test is performed.
11768
11769
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 391
Return to TOP
11770
Figure 5.1-S
Figure 5.1-T
11771
Carpet/ Adhesive/ Concrete Assembly
Linoleum/ Adhesive/ Plywood tested as Assembly
11772
(Source National Research Council Canada)
11773
11774
Whenever possible, materials should be tested in a manner that simulates their intended installation
11775
conditions. This applies as well to “edge effects” of materials: in many cases, emission rates from a cut
11776
edge of a product vary signicantly compared with the normally exposed surfaces. Typically to avoid
11777
this problem, holders or edge sealing techniques are employed to limit the edge emissions during testing.
11778
Of course if the material will be installed with exposed edges, sealing them during an emissions test
11779
would be inappropriate.
11780
11781
Test “assemblies” may also include the “substrate” employed on which a product is supported or applied.
11782
Examples include the use of gypsum wallboard as substrate for testing paint emissions. Here, the choice
11783
of wallboard type (water resistant, fire-retardent, standard?) may impact on the emissions observed, as
11784
will decision to seal the wallboard with a primer coat prior to application of the paint product to be
11785
tested. Again, substrate selections that mimic reality are preferred.
11786
11787
Emissions Testing Laboratories
11788
Certification systems appear to exist for some aspects of emissions testing (such as the chemical analysis
11789
of air samples collected from emissions chambers). Labs certified by the American Industrial Hygiene
11790
Association laboratory accreditation program (http://www.aiha.org) are able to conduct analyses for
11791
hazards at levels of concern for industrial workplaces. They may not necessarily be accredited,
11792
organized, or able to perform analysis for chemicals and particulate matter at concentrations of concern
11793
for indoor air. In addition, no current accreditation process exists in North America for labs conducting
11794
complete material emissions testing (i.e. the complete process of sample collection, handling,
11795
preparation, chamber operation, emissions testing, air sampling, data collection and interpretation).
11796
11797
Guidance documents do exist that recommend procedures, but these are not defined “methods” per se. In
11798
a report funded by the US EPA, Tichenor (2007) reviewed various emissions testing programs and
11799
recommended criteria for their evaluation. Tichenor also reviewed US labs currently offering emissions
11800
testing services.
11801

12010
4 Based on the current 1-hr Acute Reference Exposure Level (REL) of 76 ppb (94 µg/m3), an exposure level of 27 ppb (33 µg/m3) can be

extrapolated based on an 8-hr exposure period (OEHHA, 1999). The current CREL of 3 µg/m3 was not used.

Control of Emissions through use of VOC Barriers
12030
• Where possible, seal edges/unseen exposed surfaces of materials using effective VOC barrier
12031
• Do not leave gypsum wallboard unpainted since it can become sink and then a source of VOCs.,
12032
• Composite Panel Association technical Bulletin: VOC Emission Barrier Effects of Laminates,
12033
Overlays and Coatings for Particleboard, Medium Density Fiberboard (MDF) and Hardboard
12034
(http://www.pbmdf.com/CPA30/files/ccLibraryFiles/Filename/000000000738/TB%20VOC.pdf )
12035
o Barrier types:
12036
 Thick laminates:
12037
 High pressure laminates (HPL)
12038
 Phenolic impregnated backer sheets (20 mils+)
12039
 vinyls (6 mils+)
12040
 Thin laminates:
12041
 Low pressure laminates (LPL)/Resin saturated papers
12042
 Melamine low pressure laminates (Thermally fused melamine)
12043
 Polyester low pressure laminates (Polyester saturated paper)
12044
 Thin vinyls (2-5 mils)
12045
 Low basis weight papers
12046
 Foils
12047

12048

12049

12053

12054

12055
o Problem with barriers, may extend emissions – and problem (vs fast decay)
12056
o By providing barrier to one target compound (eg. formaldehyde), the barrier itself may become a
12057
source of other VOCs of concern
12058
o Effective barriers can reduce VOC emissions from particleboard and MDF products by up to
12059
95% (CPA 2003).
12060
o Barry, Alpha ; Corneau, Diane (2006), Effectiveness of barriers to minimize VOC emissions
12061
including formaldehyde.(volatile organic compounds), Forest Products Journal (Sept.2006)
12062
 Preliminary study indicated:
12063
 epoxy powder coating reduced formaldehyde and TVOC emissions from MDF by more than
12064
90%
12065
 phenolic, vinyl, and melamine paper applied on particleboard panels also reduced
12066
formaldehyde emissions by more than 90%, but were less effective on VOC emissions, with
12067
reductions of 88, 66, and 85 percent, respectively
12068

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 404
Return to TOP
Material Conditioning
12072
• Emissions from materials that are installed near the end of buildinging completion (for example final
12073
finishing materials and furnishing products) will typically have their highest emission source
12074
strengths during the period immediately following manufacture. In an attempt to reduce potenital
12075
impact on early occupancy IAQ, efforts should be made to “condition” these materials (ie air out the
12076
materials at a clean location separated or isolated from the final occupied space of the finished
12077
building). Caution should of course be observed that contamination of the materials by construction
12078
dirt/dust or by any strong-source agents related to building construction occur and negate the
12079
conditioning effort (refer to scheduling section). Unwrapping/ventilating new office partition panels
12080
only to have them become soiled by their conditioning environment is one obvious example to be
12081
avoided.
12082
• Use a location that is well-ventilated or not inside the building to remove and dispose of packaging
12083
materials and ‘Condition” materials/products known to have early emission peaks before installing
12084
them.
12085
• http://www.epa.gov/iaq/schooldesign/controlling.html#Air Out and Flush Out :
12086
” Air out is when completed products that produce objectionable emissions are removed from
12087
packaging and unrolled or spaced apart in a well-ventilated warehouse so that fresh air can easily
12088
flow in and around the products, thereby quickly removing any pollutants emitted from the products.
12089
Drawers and doors of cabinetry should also be open, and electrical products such as computers and
12090
printers should be turned on. Because the products are being aired out in a well-ventilated warehouse,
12091
the pollutants are not emitted within the school building, thus reducing the chances that the pollutants
12092
will be adsorbed onto other building materials or finishes, or that occupants will be affected. The
12093
types of products most applicable for air out include rolls or tiles of synthetic flooring products, and
12094
products such as cabinets, shelving, desks, and work surfaces made from particle board, plywood, or
12095
medium density fiberboard (MDF).”
12096
• Tichenor papers on airing of building materials (?) – Barney has papers (Bayer paper – Hal has it)

need for timely availability of clean conditioning facilities (within or exterior to th enew
12106
building) to accommodate the materials.

Building Bake-Out:
12136
• Building "bake-outs", i.e., when temperatures are increased up to 100F in order to "artificially age"
12137
building materials, are not recommended. Their effectiveness has not been proven, and they may in
12138
fact damage parts of the HVAC system or building components.
12139
• Bldg in SanFran & in East Bay. Warped wood, cracked foundations etc.
12140
• Should be avoided – problematic: minmimal effect on volatiles, increases SVOC out-gassing-
12141
subsequently (Charlene Bayer published on this - shifting sources)
12142
• Girman paper from Indoor Air 1990.
12143
• California Department of Health Services (1996) Reducing Occupant Exposure to Volatile Organic
12144
Compounds (VOCs) from Office Building Construction Materials: Non-binding Guidelines
12145
 building bake-out: process designed to "artificially age" building materials and products by
12146
elevating the temperature of an unoccupied, newly constructed or remodeled building while
12147
supplying a fixed amount of ventilation, and flushing out the building with the maximum
12148
possible ventilation after completion of the bake-out.
12149
 3.3 Building Bake-outs
12150
 The purpose of a bake-out is to "artificially age" building materials and products by
12151
accelerating emissions of residual solvents. A building bake-out involves elevating the
12152
temperature of an unoccupied, newly constructed or remodeled building to between 95 and
12153
102EF (35 and 39EC) while supplying a fixed amount of ventilation, and flushing out the
12154
building with the maximum possible ventilation after completion of the bake-out. According
12155
to Girman et al. (1987), an increase from 74EF (23EC) to between 90 to 102EF (32 and
12156
39EC) should increase diffusion of solvents through materials by approximately 10 percent
12157
and should increase the vapor pressure of solvents by approximately 200 percent.
12158
 Various researchers have conducted a number of field trials of bake-out in actual buildings
12159
(Girman, 1989; Girman et al., 1987, 1989, 1990; Offermann et al., 1993; Hicks et al., 1990).
12160
However, the results of these studies have been mixed. In some cases, concentrations of
12161
certain compounds were significantly reduced, while in other cases, concentrations either
12162
remained unaffected or increased after a bake-out. In one case, VOC concentrations were
12163
substantially decreased immediately after a bake-out but within two weeks returned to the
12164
concentrations measured prior to the bake-out (Offermann et al., 1993).
12165
 Based on current knowledge, the building bake-out procedure is neither recommended or
12166
discouraged. The problems often associated with this process include: (a) technical
12167
difficulties such as being able to raise significantly the temperature of all VOC sources while
12168
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 406
Return to TOP
providing sufficient ventilation as is discussed in Appendix D; (b) its questionable
12169
effectiveness; (c) the potential of material damage due to elevated temperatures; and (d) the
12170
potential of VOC adsorption and subsequent re-emission by porous surfaces. The bake-out
12171
procedure can be used in some situations as long as the building owners and/or building
12172
professionals are aware of the limitations and risks associated with this technique. The
12173
technical aspects of building bake-outs are discussed in Appendix D.
12174
 Instead of the bake-out procedure, the guidelines recommend the following: (a) select low-
12175
VOC-impact products and materials; (b) develop a ventilation protocol during and after
12176
construction (a method known as building flush out); and (c) delay occupancy until VOC
12177
concentrations have been decreased to an acceptable level (see Section 2).
12178
 APPENDIX D: Technical Considerations During Building Bake-Outs
12179
 The following is a discussion of problems associated with building bake-outs
12180
that are based on the limited experience of CDHS researchers in applying the
12181
bake-out procedure in several buildings (as reported by Girman et al., 1987;
12182
1989; and 1990).
12183
 1. Achieving bake-out temperatures: HVAC systems are sized for specific
12184
thermal and cooling loads which depend on the number of occupants, office
12185
equipment, ventilation rate, and outdoor conditions. Thus, attempting to use a
12186
building's HVAC system for a bake-out may be problematic. Increased
12187
temperatures may be achievable by modifying an HVAC system's temperature
12188
controls because most HVAC systems are slightly oversized. Additional heat
12189
sources also can be brought into a building (e.g., portable electric heaters),
12190
however, kerosene or other unvented combustion heaters should be avoided. A
12191
building's lights also can be used as additional heat sources. The final air
12192
temperature also will depend on outdoor conditions. If a bake-out is
12193
conducted in winter when outdoor temperatures are close to the "design"
12194
temperature, it may not be possible to achieve the desired bake-out
12195
temperatures. In addition, the fluctuation of a building's air temperature
12196
between day and night time must also be taken into consideration. Adjustment
12197
of window shades and curtains can maximize solar heat gain during the day
12198
and minimize heat loss during the night.
12199
 2. Ventilation during bake-out: A certain amount of mechanical ventilation
12200
must be provided during a bake-out to minimize adsorption of VOCs by
12201
porous materials. The need for increased ventilation must be balanced against
12202
the need to achieve and maintain high temperatures. Usually minimum
12203
outdoor air quantities are utilized during a bake-out (with maximum
12204
recirculation to ensure adequate air mixing throughout the area being baked-
12205
out), thereby minimizing the heating load due to ventilation, so that the
12206
highest possible indoor temperatures can be achieved. However, Tichenor et
12207
al. (1991) caution that a bake-out conducted with minimal ventilation may
12208
result in a re-distribution of VOCs from some building materials to others.
12209
 3. Duration of bake-out: Ideally a bake-out should be conducted over an
12210
extended period of time. However, financial constraints usually limit such a
12211
procedure to a few days. It is worth noting that, depending on the thermal
12212
mass of a building, it takes at least a day to achieve target air temperatures at
12213
the beginning of a bake-out and at least a day to decrease indoor
12214
temperatures to comfortable levels after the end of a bake-out. Thus, a total of
12215
four days must be allocated to allow two days or less of actual bake-out time.
12216
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 407
Return to TOP
 4. Effectiveness of bake-out on various sources: According to Levin (1992b), a
12217
bake-out will not affect all VOC-emitting materials and products in the same
12218
manner. Thin materials, with large surface-tovolume ratios (i.e., a large
12219
fraction of the mass close to the surface such as paints, varnishes, and waxes),
12220
are more likely to be affected by a bake-out than thick materials, with small
12221
surface-to-volume ratios (i.e., only a small fraction of the mass close to the
12222
surface, such as particleboard). Another important parameter in determining
12223
the effectiveness of a bake-out is the temperature of the various materials
12224
during this procedure. Material temperatures depend on a material's thermal
12225
mass and on the duration of the bake-out (see Item 3 above), where the
12226
thermal mass of a material is determined by multiplying the specific heat of
12227
the material by its density. For example, to raise the temperature of a carpet
12228
and carpet adhesive sufficiently, the temperature of the sub-floor also must be
12229
increased due to the large thermal mass of the carpet assembly. Therefore,
12230
materials with large heat capacities are unlikely to be affected by bake-outs of
12231
short duration. California Department of Health Services Reducing Occupant
12232
Exposure to Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) from Office Building
12233
Construction Materials: Non-binding Guidelines D2
12234
 5. Cost of bake-outs: There are certain costs involved in a building bake-out
12235
besides energy costs. These costs include HVAC modifications, personnel
12236
involved with planning and execution of the bake-out, continuous monitoring
12237
and adjustment of building temperatures during the bake-out especially in
12238
cases when building emergency systems are overridden, etc. In addition,
12239
replacement cost of any building materials damaged as a result of the bake-
12240
out must be considered. For example Girman et al. (1990) reported that in
12241
one of five baked-out buildings there was some material damage consisting of
12242
buckled vinyl flooring in one room and a cracked window pane (out of a total
12243
of 225 double-glazed windows). The authors also reported that the building
12244
with the material damage was the one most extensively baked-out [this
12245
building was heated for 102 hr to temperatures up to 100EF (38EC).] The
12246
overall cost for this building bake-out was higher than that of the four other
12247
buildings, i.e., $0.23/ft2 ($2.5/m2), but negligible when compared to the cost
12248
of the building. Finally, the costs associated with delayed occupancy also
12249
must be considered.
12250
 6. Other issues: Some of the fire and safety alarms may have to be overridden
12251
during a bake-out. In addition, because during a bake-out a building operates
12252
well above design temperatures it is important to consider all the related legal
12253
and insurance issues. During a bake-out: (a) access to the building should be
12254
limited to HVAC maintenance/monitoring personnel; and (b) respirators
12255
capable of removing VOCs should be offered to those entering the building.
12256
• Girman, J.R. 1989. "Volatile Organic Compounds and Building Bake-Out." In: Occupational
12257
Medicine: State of the Art Reviews. J.E. Cone and M.J. Hodgson, Editors. Philadelphia,
12258
Pennsylvania: Hanley & Belfus, Inc. 4(4):695-712.
12259
• Girman, J.R.; Alevantis, L.E.; Petreas, M.X.; and Webber, L.M. 1990. "Building Bake-Out Studies."
12260
Proceedings of the Fifth International Conference on Indoor Air Quality and Climate, Toronto,
12261
Canada, July 29 - August 3, 3:349-354.
12262
• Levin, H., Editor. 1992b. "Building-Bake-Outs." Indoor Air Bulletin, 2 (7): 1-6.
12263
• Offermann, F.J.; Loiselle, S.A.; Ander, G.; and Lau, H.L. 1993. "Indoor Contaminant Emission Rates
12264
Before and After a Building Bake-out." Proceedings: IAQ 93 - Operating and Maintaining Buildings
12265
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 408
Return to TOP
for Health, Comfort and Productivity, Atlanta, Georgia: American Society of Heating, Refrigerating,
12266
and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc., pp. 157-163.

12271
12272
Building Flush-out
12273
• regardless of the diligence applied to careful selection of materials with low emissions, conditioning
12274
of materials prior to installation, etc, the materials/finishes and furnishing that become part of the
12275
building interior will emit contaminants to some degee. In certain cases, “in-place curing” of
12276
materials/components will of necessity take place. Emissions during this . To the extent possible,
12277
installation of porous materials should have been scheduled to occur after this process has occurred.
12278
• A pre-occupancy “flush-out” of the building, in which increased ventilation rates above expected
12279
building norms are maintained for an extended period, is a recommended practice to deal with this
12280
early emission period. Effective “Flush-out” can be difficult to achieve for a number of reasons.
12281
Depending on the geographical region and climate, coupled with the season in which building
12282
completion occurs (see scheduling), increasing the intake of hot/humid or dry/extremely cold outdoor
12283
air can be extremely costly, and may be impossible in cases where building economizers are not used
12284
(refer to HVAC design). Failure to fully consider the potential moisture control impacts on building
12285
of conducting “flush-out” operations can have serious consequences (ref moisture control and/or
12286
fungal contamination sections).
12287
• post construction: building flush-out:
12288
o NOTE – review/incorporate concepts from IEQ\_TC\_CHPSBPMVIII TRACK CHANGE 2008-
12289
03-25 v3-Flush out.doc
12290
o LEED (NC 2.2 EQ Credit 3.2: Construction IAQ Management Plan) recommends a total air
12291
volume of 14,000 cu.ft. of outdoor air per sq.ft. of floor area (while maintaining an internal
12292
temperature of at least 60 degrees F and relative humidity no higher than 60%)
12293
o .100% outdoor air 24h/d for 14d with HVAC supply fans at max flows
12294
o Ability to employ building flush-out depends on climatic region: not appropriate in hot/humid
12295
climates where condensate drains in HVAC systems typically cannot handle volume if run at
12296
100% OA – need to coordinate with project scheduling. To prevent 100%OA-induced humidity
12297
problems, flush-out period may require severe extension – not financially viable. Gives support
12298
to Material selection strategies leading to low emissions, and elimination or reduction of need
12299
to conduct flush-out.
12300
o Flush-out should be conducted after the punchlist (?) else completion of punchlist items may
12301
result in re-introduction of contaminants
12302
o All post-construction cleaning within the building (which should avoid use of strong solvents)
12303
must be completed prior to flush-out
12304
o Quantify the # of air changes, not the time. Note if you don’t have an economizer can take a
12305
long time
12306
o LEED – building commissioning – 10 days 24 h/day 100% OA system (?) – good graphics?
12307
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 409
Return to TOP
o Hal – report to State on flush out
12308
o Tied in w moisture control (dual purpose: flush-out moisture from new construction as well as
12309
VOC contaminants)
12310
o Consider outdoor envt activities during flush-out activities (eg example of landscaping activities
12311
in full swing during flush-out – dirt/dust drawn into bldg)
12312
o http://www.epa.gov/iaq/schooldesign/controlling.html#Air Out and Flush Out :
12313
“Flush out is when large amounts of outdoor air are forced through a recently completed
12314
building for a period of 3 to 90 days so that the majority of pollutant emissions from building
12315
materials, finishes, and furnishings can be removed from the building before occupancy. The
12316
recommended minimum volume of outdoor air needed for flush out is the amount needed to
12317
ventilate the full school at least once each hour (1 ACH, or air change per hour), 24 hours a day,
12318
7 days a week. At a minimum, all mechanical ventilation systems should be set to provide the
12319
largest amount of outdoor air as practical from the final construction stages when floor products
12320
and paints are applied through the first few days of occupancy. Based on a 90-day flush out of
12321
two different office buildings, the State of Washington now requires a minimum 30-day flush
12322
out period for all its new public buildings. California's building standards also require a 1 hour
12323
daily flush out prior to normal building occupancy to reduce contaminants that may build up
12324
when the ventilation is off overnight.”
12325
o California Interagency Working Group on Indoor Air Quality - Indoor Air Quality Info Sheet -
12326
ADVISORY ON RELOCATABLE AND RENOVATED CLASSROOMS http://www.cal-
12327
iaq.org/adv\_4pg.htm
12328
"Flush-out" all newly constructed, remodeled, or acquired classrooms.
12329
 Prior to use of any new relocatable units by staff or students, operate HVAC systems at their
12330
maximum outdoor air intake rate continuously for several days. Similarly, provide maximum
12331
flush-out by HVAC (or open windows) for newly renovated classrooms and offices. Start this
12332
"flush-out" as soon as the HVAC system is operational, and continue after furniture
12333
installation. During this period, do not recirculate return air.
12334
 "Flush-out" not "Bake-out"
12335
 Continue "flush-out" ventilation during periods of first use
12336
 Efforts to minimize exposures to school children and staff should continue in the weeks
12337
following project completion. Emissions of VOCs from building materials still pose problems
12338
during this time, when they are at their highest. This is done by delaying occupancy in
12339
renovated rooms and portable classroom for several weeks, and utilizing maximum outdoor
12340
air ("flush-out") ventilation during these periods and for the first weeks of use. Flush out
12341
periods of 1-2 weeks are recommend, although longer periods may be required. For the first
12342
days to weeks of occupant use, continue to operate HVAC systems at the maximum outdoor
12343
air setting. Finally, monitor occupants' comfort, and follow-up complaints to identify
12344
problems early.
12345
 LEED EQ Credit 3.2: Construction IAQ Management Plan: Before Occupancy (1 Point)
12346
• Intent: Reduce indoor air quality problems resulting from the construction/renovation
12347
process in order to help sustain the comfort and well-being of construction workers
12348
and building occupants.
12349
• Requirements: Develop and implement an Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) Management Plan
12350
for the pre-occupancy phase as follows:
12351
• OPTION 1 — FLUSH-OUT
12352
• After construction ends, prior to occupancy and with all interior finishes installed,
12353
perform a building flush-out by supplying a total air volume of 14,000 cu.ft. of
12354
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 410
Return to TOP
outdoor air per sq.ft. of floor area while maintaining an internal temperature of at
12355
least 60ºF and relative humidity no higher than 60%.
12356
• OR
12357
• If occupancy is desired prior to completion of the flush-out, the space may be
12358
occupied following delivery of a minimum of 3,500 cu.ft. of outdoor air per sq.ft.
12359
of floor area to the space. Once a space is occupied, it shall be ventilated at a
12360
minimum rate of 0.30 cfm/sq.ft. of outside air or the design minimum outside air
12361
rate determined in EQ Prerequisite 1, whichever is greater. During each day of the
12362
flush-out period, ventilation shall begin a minimum of three hours prior to
12363
occupancy and continue during occupancy. These conditions shall be maintained
12364
until a total of 14,000 cu.ft./sq.ft. of outside air has been delivered to the space.
12365
12366
In-Place Curing
12367
• ref Tichenor report on carpet emissions decays vs conditioning env’t (link wi conditioning)
12368
12369
Ventilation Rates and HVAC Operation Schedules
12370
 during construction (link to Wayne’s section on IAQ during construction)
12371
o provide increased ventilation during installation of high emission materials
12372
(unavoidable), during painting/sealing activities, during renovations
12373
o during dust generation activities, close off returns, provide temporary exhaust paths
12374
for ventilation air
12375
 Morning/weekend purge. When the energy penalty is not too severe or where heat recovery
12376
ventilators are in place, run a system purge during morning start-up and/or during
12377
maintenance and cleaning. This should be performed from time to time, mainly during spring
12378
and fall when energy penalties are slight. (ref: City of New York, Dept. of Design and
12379
Construction (1999): High Performance Building Guidelines)
12380

12424
Strategy 5.3 – Minimize IAQ Impacts Associated with Cleaning and Maintenance
12425
12426
12427
The impact of building cleaning on IAQ is increasingly being examined in detail (eg CARB 2006, 2007).
12428
Cleaning a building can be an extremely expensive component of overall cost (approaching that of the
12429
energy costs to operate the building). Cleaning may also release large quantities of chemicals into the
12430
indoor air. As an example, the VOC emissions due to a single stripping/ rewaxing of a resilient flooring
12431
material may exceed the emissions released during the initial installation of the material (when its
12432
emission rates are at their highest). Over the lifetime of a material, the cleaning-related emissions can
12433
thus be extremely large. Efforts to minimize the required cleaning frequency and intensity can be highly
12434
rewarding from both financial and IAQ perspectives.
12435
12436
The simplest and most effective means of controlling the negative impact that cleaning products may
12437
have on the indoor environment is to reduce the need for their use. This is best accomplished by
12438
designing effective barriers to the entry of dirt into the building (see Objective 3 – Limit Entry of
12439
Outdoor Contaminants for discussion of the effective use of “Track-Off” systems at building entrances,
12440
as well as for a detailed examination of particle filtration equipment associated with building HVAC
12441
systems to control outdoor particulate sources).
12442
12443
Select durable materials/finishes that are simple to clean and maintain
12444
Installing finishes/surfaces within the building that may be effectively and efficiently cleaned with
12445
relatively benign agents can also reduce the IAQ impact of cleaning. Consider, of course, the emission
12446
properties of the material itself (see Strategy 5.1 Control Indoor Contaminants through Appropriate
12447
Material Selection). Select materials with low dirt trapping properties, for example, consider the use of
12448
terrazzo or decorative poured concrete as flooring options where appropriate. Consider as well any hard
12449
surface maintenance requirements such as polishing, stripping, or wet process cleaning and the
12450
emissions characteristics of any products used in these actions. Rubber or natural linoleum may be
12451
resilient flooring alternatives that do not require stripping/waxing but examine their detailed emissions
12452
properties carefully before selecting.
12453
When using carpets, select styles with low dirt trapping characteristics and easy cleaning prtocols. Use
12454
carpet tile in preference to wall-to-wall in order to facilitate replacement of small damaged/dirty sections
12455
rather than need to replace much larger areas. During cleaning, be careful to avoid over-wetting of
12456
carpet (can lead to biocontamination). “Deep” cleaning should be periodically implemented effectively
12457
remove deep dust, thereby reducing exposure of building occupants to allergens and other pollutants
12458
(Roberts etal 2004).
12459
12460
In restrooms, use large-dimension tile to reduce the amount of grout lines that may be difficult to clean
12461
and require use of strong cleaning agents. Avoid use of white grout that will show stains easily and thus
12462
require more frequent cleaning. Specify non-staining surfaces that are easy to clean. “Stainless” steel
12463
may in fact not be, and may require periodic application of polishing compounds (eg in elevator
12464
interiors). Polished granite, vitreous tiles, synthetic stones and certain solid-surface plastics may be
12465
preferable materials (EBN 2005).
12466
12467
Where possible, keep the number of different types of surfaces within a building to a minimum. This
12468
will reduce the number of distinct cleaning agents that must be used, and also minimize the risk that
12469
cleaning products will be inappropriately used, leading to reduced longevity of interior finishes.
12470
12471
Microbial resistance of materials used indoors can be a consideration with regards to surface cleaning. In
12472
general, however, it is probably wise to focus on easily cleanable surfaces amenable to common
12473
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 413
Return to TOP
disinfectant treatment where required (coupled with control of indoor/surface temperature and moisture
12474
conditions) rather than on utilization of specific inhibitors of microbial growth.
12475
12476
Select cleaning products with minimal emissions
12477
The volume of cleaning products used on commercial and institutional buildings is extremely large.
12478
Efforts to reduce consumption and to select products with relatively low emissions of contaminants can
12479
thus have a significant impact on IAQ. The classes of cleaning agents used indoors are diverse, and
12480
include: general-purpose cleaners; bathroom cleaners (incl. disinfectants, sanitizers, antiseptics and
12481
disinfectant-cleaners); drain cleaners, carpet cleaners; glass cleaners; degreasers; stain removers;
12482
deodorizers (including air “fresheners”), polishes, waxes, and sealers.
12483
12484
Cleaning products used indoors include caustic acids and alkalis, antimicrobial agents, as well as
12485
assorted solvents of organic nature (including hydrocarbons, glycols, and glycol ethers). Aerosol-based
12486
cleaners typically contain light hydrocarbon gases. Disinfectants contain alcohols and aldehydes.
12487
Degreasers and spot cleaners may release chlorinated hydrocarbons into indoor air.
12488
12489
According to the US EPA (April 2000 EPP Update from EPA's Office of Pollution Prevention and
12490
Toxics): "Traditional cleaning products can contain chemicals associated with cancer, reproductive
12491
disorders, eye or skin irritation, and other human health issues." The table below summarizes the
12492
potential health impacts due to compounds commonly found in cleaning agents.
12493
Table 5.3-A Chemicals found in cleaning products.
12494

products

sanitizers
• repeated exposure can lead to occupational asthma, allergies, or
skin sensitization

12495
There are a number of sources of information for guidance on selection of cleaning agents that will have
12496
minimal IAQ impact. The table below provides a summary.
12497

12499

products

JP4
12500
Certain cleaning agents contain compounds that, when exposed to ozone at levels that can occur indoors,
12501
react to form irritating contaminants (see section on “indoor chemistry” in Strategy 5.1 Control Indoor
12502
Contaminants through Appropriate Material Selection). Pine oil cleaners (POC), for example, are
12503
sources of terpenoids including α-terpinene, d-limonene, terpinolene, and α-terpineol (Singer etal 2006;
12504
CARB Report 2006) that have been shown to interact with ozone to create secondary products including
12505
formaldehyde, hydroxyl radicals, and secondary organic aerosols.
12506
12507
In a survey of commonly available cleaning products, Nazaroff etal (2006) found that many contained
12508
ethylene-based glycol ethers (primarily 2-butoxyethanol) at levels up to 10% (mass fraction) while 12
12509
out of 21 products tested contained terpenes and other ozone-reactive compounds at levels up to 26%.
12510
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 415
Return to TOP
When tested in realistic room cleaning scenarios in the presence of typical indoor ozone levels, in some
12511
cases the use of these products resulted in the formation of both formaldehyde and fine particulates at
12512
levels exceeding California air quality standards. OH radical formation was also noted. Concentrations
12513
of glycol ethers could reach levels of concern with use of some products. Careful selection of cleaning
12514
products is thus recommended.
12515
12516
The use of air fresheners should be discouraged as they are associated with the formation of irritating
12517
secondary emissions (Singer etal 2006).
12518
12519
Provide appropriate storage for cleaning products
12520
Even with careful selection of appropriate cleaning products for use indoors, janitor’s closets should be
12521
properly vented (direct exhaust, no recirculation) as a means of source control. These storage locations
12522
should also be well lit (to facilitate proper preparation of cleaning agents/equipment) and contain
12523
properly sloped floor drains that are designed to capture any chemical spills and simplify subsequent
12524
clean up.
12525
12526
Easy to use dispensers should be installed for all stock cleaning agents. It is important to purchase
12527
cleaning agents as concentrates in order to reduce packaging requirements, but care must be taken to
12528
ensure correct preparation of cleaning solutions while also minimizing risk of spills/excessive use/waste
12529
and staff injury. Clear instructions for cleaning agent preparation and use should be posted along with
12530
available MSDS information on cleaning products and clear warnings regarding any hazards associated
12531
with their use.
12532
12533
Adopt cleaning protocols that will have minimal IAQ impact
12534
Cleaning strategies, which focus on efficiency combined with use of relatively benign cleaning products
12535
to lessen overall impact on IAQ, should be adopted. As part of building Operations and Maintenance
12536
practices, clear instructions re cleaning protocols should be prepared including preparation/use/storage
12537
of cleaning agents. These should also include clear warnings regarding the dangers associated with the
12538
mixing of cleaning product classes. The advice of custodial/janitorial staff regarding optimal cleaning
12539
protocol implementation should be sought early in the design process.
12540
12541
Cleaning should be scheduled after business hours when building is largely unoccupied to minimize
12542
occupant exposure. Adequate ventilation must be provided during/after cleaning: building ventilation
12543
and custodial operations schedules must be properly coordinated. Careful control of cleaning practices
12544
and materials to ensure adherence to specified cleaning protocol/products must be maintained
12545
(especially when the cleaning service is contracted out). Complete cleaning records (what, when and
12546
how) must be maintained.
12547
12548

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

12591
Strategy 5.4 – Consider Upper Room Ultraviolet Germicidal Irradiation in Relevant
12592
Applications
12593
12594
UVGI and Infection Control
12595
UVGI is thought to inactivate airborne microorganisms by damaging the DNA necessary for replication
12596
and the initiation of the infection process. As such, irradiation does not physically remove the microbial
12597
agent including its allergens and toxins; irradiation makes the agent unable to replicate.
12598
12599
Mycobacterium tuberculosis and other agents such as measles and influenza can enter the indoor air as
12600
droplet nuclei which are the residuals of respiratory droplets. These particles settle from air very slowly
12601
and thus can be transmitted through a room or throughout a building. The greatest risk of exposure to
12602
infective agents such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis occurs in crowded rooms or buildings containing
12603
infected people such as in homeless shelters, correctional facilities, etc., especially when outdoor air
12604
ventilation is poor.
12605
12606
In the late 19th century and early 20th century, large amounts of outdoor air ventilation were
12607
recommended to reduce disease from infectious agents such as Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
12608
Approximately 15cfm of outdoor air per occupant appears to be near the minimum ventilation rate
12609
necessary to reduce transmission of infectious agents. Filtration can also capture droplet nuclei but this
12610
intervention may not be practical in all cases (eg., correctional facilities; homeless shelters). Adding
12611
UVGI to the upper room air space or in the ventilation air stream can increase the equivalent outdoor air
12612
change rate at least for invective bacterial (M. tuberculosis) and viral (measles) agents (Nardell &
12613
Macher, 1999).
12614
12615
Factors such as the type of microorganism and relative humidity can also affect the ability of UVGI to
12616
damage microbial DNA. Thus, coronavirus is more susceptible to UVGI than the adenovirus (Walker et
12617
al., 2007). UVGI is less effective against Micrococcus luteus (a human shed bacterium) at high relative
12618
humidity (Gorsuch et al., 1998). Stachybotrys chartarum appears more resistant to UVGI than
12619
Aspergillus versicolor (Green et al., 2005). It should be noted that UVGI has been shown to be effective
12620
in controlling the spread of Mycobacterium tuberculosis and measles in actual field studies in buildings
12621
(Nardell and Macher, 1999; Nardell, 2002).
12622
12623
UVGI in Buildings
12624
UVGI fixtures may be placed in air handling units, usually in the mixed air plenum where the irradiation
12625
directly falls on the coils. Filtration of the mixed air ventilation is necessary to keep dust and dirt from
12626
accumulating on the lamp surface and thereby lessening the germicidal efficiency of the fixture
12627
(Bernstein et al., 2006).
12628
12629
Culturable bacteria and fungi are eliminated on irradiated surfaces within the air handling unit (Menzies
12630
et al., 1999). However, culturable microorganisms cannot be eliminated from non-irradiated crevices
12631
and non-irradiated air stream surfaces in the air handling unit (Kowalski & Bahnfleth, 2002).
12632
12633
Ultraviolet emitting lamps can also be placed within stand alone air cleaners. Kujundzic et al.,(2006)
12634
reported that the lamp in some units may discharge ozone. Additional studies by the same authors found
12635
no additional air cleaning effect associated with the presence of the lamp in the unit.
12636
12637
Upper room UVGI fixtures are typically installed in rooms where many people (including some
12638
occupants disseminating infectious microbial agents) may assemble and where outdoor air ventilation is
12639
inadequate. The UVGI fixtures are installed above eye level along the upper portion of room walls (see
12640
Photo #1). Parallel louvers with non-reflective surfaces in the fixtures direct the irradiation in a slightly
12641
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 418
Return to TOP
tilted upward horizontal plane (Dumyahn & First, 1999). Upper room UVGI can lower exposure to
12642
Mycobacterium tuberculosis equivalent to 12 to 35 air changes per hour of outdoor air cleaning
12643
(Lighting Research center, 2003). Other factors such as irradiation intensity, time of droplet nuclei
12644
exposure, and relative humidity can affect the equivalent outdoor air cleaning achieved by UVGI.
12645
12646
Safety with UVGI
12647
Nardell (2002) used the term “dangerous” to refer to the painful eye irritation and skin erythemia that
12648
can occur when people are exposed to unshielded UVGI. The ACGIH (2007) TLV for 254NM UVGI is
12649
6.0µj/cm2. Trevisan et al. (2006) reported a case where students in an autopsy room were exposed to
12650
700µj/cm2 UVGI (254NM) irradiation for 90 minutes due to absence of an interlock system (UV lights
12651
shut off when occupants enter room). In less than a minute occupants were exposed to a dose of
12652
irradiation exceeding the ACGIH TLV, resulting in both ocular and skin symptoms. Talbot et al. (2002)
12653
described another case of occupant exposure to unshielded UVGI. These authors emphasized the need
12654
for “fail-safe” interlock devices to switch off the UVGI fixture when occupants enter any area where
12655
unshielded UV lamps may be present. Designers must be aware that this includes UVGI systems that
12656
may be installed in HVAC system components.
12657
12658
UVGI Effects on IAQ
12659
Only a few field studies have been published on the effect of UVGI in occupied buildings. Bernstein et
12660
al (2006) showed that UVGI installed in the air handling units in homes of asthmatics reduced asthma
12661
symptoms. However, no significant reduction in airborne molds or allergens occurred in these
12662
residences as a result of UVGI operation.
12663
12664
In a study of office workers, Menzies et al (1999) found that airborne mold and bacteria were unaffected
12665
by UVGI. Menzies et al (1999) also found that environmental satisfaction parameters among office
12666
workers were in general, not affected by whether the UVGI in the HVAC system was on or off. Thus,
12667
more skin rash and irritation occurred with the UVGI “on”, while less headaches, difficulty with
12668
concentration, and eye irritation occurred at the same time. Airborne thermotolerant fungal and bacterial
12669
concentrations were unaffected by the UVGI. Increases in total dust levels were associated with UVGI
12670
(Menzies et al., 1999).
12671
12672
Designers should consider that UVGI has been shown to reduce tuberculosis infection (Mycobacterium
12673
tuberculosis attacks macrophages in alveoli of the lung). UVGI does not, as far as we know, affect
12674
allergic sensitivity, irritation caused by microbial particulate, and possible toxic effects (eg., endotoxins,
12675
glucans, mycotoxins) all of which are independent of the infection process. More research is needed to
12676
determine if UVGI in air handling units has a positive effect on IAQ. However, there is no doubt that
12677
upper room UVGI in some situations reduces the levels of infectious droplets such as with
12678
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and possibly with other viral agents such as influenzas.
12679
12680
References
12681
ACGIH, 2007, TLVs® and BEIs®. pp. 146-149.
12682
12683
Bernstein, J.A. et al., 2006, Jour. of Asthma, 43, 255-262.
12684
12685
Dumyahn, T. and M. First, Amer. Ind. Hyg. Assoc. Jour., 60, 219-227.
12686
12687
Gorsuch, E. L. et al., 1998, Intern. Jour. Of Occup. Safety And Ergonomics, 4, 287-297.
12688
12689
Green, C.F. et al., 2005, Can. Jour. Microbiology, 51, 801-804.
12690
12691
Kowalski, W.J. and W.P. Bahnfelth, 2000, Heating Piping and AC, January, 100-110.
12692
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

12693
Kujundzic, E. et al., 2006, Jour. Occ. & Env. Hygiene, 3, 536-546.
12694
12695
Lighting Research Ctr., 2003, Controlling Tuberculosis Transmission with Ultraviolet Irradiation,
12696
Rensseler Polytechnic Institute, 7, pp.
12697
12698

12699
12700

12701
12702

12703
12704

OBJECTIVE 6 – CAPTURE AND EXHAUST EMISSIONS FROM BUILDING EQUIPMENT
12717
AND ACTIVITIES
12718
12719
12720
Specific Strategies described in this Objective are:
12721
12722
• Strategy 6.1 – Properly Vent Combustion Equipment
12723

12724

12725
Air Distribution Systems
12726

12727
12728
12729
12730
See also these closely related Strategies:
12731
12732
• Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outside Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
12733
• Strategy 1.2 – Select HVAC Systems to Improve IAQ and Reduce the Energy Impacts of Ventilation
12734
• Strategy 2.2 – Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surface
12735
• Strategy 2.3 – Maintain Proper Building Pressurization
12736
• Strategy 3.2 – Control Entry of Radon
12737
• Strategy 3.3 – Control Intrusion of Vapors from Subsurface Contaminants
12738
12739
12740
12741
12742
12743
12744
12745
12746
12747

IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final

12753
Strategy 6.1 – Properly Vent Combustion Equipment
12754
12755
12756
Introduction
12757
In simple terms, combustion occurs whenever a source of fuel combines with oxygen, is heated and
12758
burned. Within the enclosed spaces of a building there are many potential uses of combustion operations,
12759
and, consequently many potential sources of combustion products. The sources can include:
12760
• Open flame processes and equipment such as numerous industrial processes, laboratory burners,
12761
cooking appliances, kerosene space heaters and gas-fired laundry equipment, fireplaces and
12762
wood burning heating stoves;
12763
• Dedicated combustion equipment such as furnaces, boilers, water heaters and generators
12764
12765
During combustion - depending on the type of fuel and the completeness of the combustion process - a
12766
number of gases and particles are released as by-products. Some of these by-products are potentially
12767
harmful to human health. The most prevalent of these gases are:
12768
• Carbon Dioxide (CO2) - A colorless, odorless gas that is also exhaled during breathing by
12769
humans and animals. In large quantities, however, carbon dioxide can cause dizziness, headaches
12770
and fatigue.
12771
• Carbon Monoxide (CO) – Also a colorless and odorless gas. However, carbon monoxide is
12772
poisonous. Breathing carbon monoxide reduces the ability of the bloodstream to carry oxygen.
12773
The symptoms of carbon monoxide poisoning include dizziness, fatigue, flu-like symptoms, and
12774
confusion. Consumed in large quantities, carbon monoxide can cause loss of consciousness and
12775
even death.
12776
• Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) – A colorless gas with a distinct odor. Exposure to nitrogen dioxide
12777
causes irritation in the eyes, throat and lungs that could lead to respiratory system infection.
12778
12779
To avoid exposure to these harmful products, combustion equipment used within a building must be
12780
designed and installed with a proper system of supply and exhaust ventilation.
12781
12782
Capture and Exhaust of Combustion Products
12783
Any situation of indoor combustion requires a properly designed and installed venting and exhaust
12784
system. Depending on the fuel, process and type of equipment being vented, there are several different
12785
types of exhaust capture systems.
12786
12787
Chimneys (Non-mechanical, natural exhaust)
12788
Chimney systems, sometimes referred to as natural draft systems, operate by creating a differential
12789
pressure between warmer conditions at the chimney inlet and cooler conditions at the chimney outlet.
12790
The resulting difference in pressures causes the combustion gases to rise up and out of the building. The
12791
amount of pressure differential is determined by the height of the chimney and the difference in
12792
temperature form the inlet to the outlet of the chimney.
12793
12794
Chimney systems are typically used to ventilate fireplaces, most wood burning stoves, smaller (less than
12795
