100 gallon) gas-fired water heaters, and some open flame cooking appliances. Chimney systems should
12796
always be selected and designed so that the temperature of the gases within the chimney is always above
12797
dew point. If water vapor is allowed to condense, it could flow back into, and cause damage to, the
12798
combustion equipment. Condensed water vapor can also combine with the sulfur that is produced during
12799
combustion to form corrosive sulfuric acid on the walls of the chimney.
12800
12801
12802
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 422
Return to TOP
Induced Draft (powered, negative pressure exhaust)
12803
These type of exhaust is a mechanical system that uses one or more fans to pull combustion gases out of
12804
the building. The fan is typically located at the exit of the exhaust system where the resulting movement
12805
of air by the fan causes an induced negative pressure within the exhaust duct.
12806
12807
Furnaces and boilers that utilize atmospheric pressure gas or oil burners use induced draft fans for
12808
exhaust. Most industrial ventilation exhaust, laboratory exhaust systems and range hoods over gas-fired
12809
cooking appliances also use induced draft exhaust. Intakes for an induced draft system, if they cannot be
12810
connected directly to the combustion equipment, should be located as close to the source of combustion
12811
as physically possible. When designing an induced draft system, since the fan is typically in the
12812
airstream of the exhaust gas, it is important to select fan equipment that can withstand both the expected
12813
temperatures of the exhaust gases as well as any potential reactions with the chemical composition of
12814
those gases.
12815
12816
Forced Draft (powered, positive pressure exhaust)
12817
This type of exhaust is a mechanical system that uses one or more fans to push fresh air for combustion
12818
into the equipment. The combustion chamber of the equipment is, consequently, pressurized and the
12819
resulting combustion gases are forced out through the exhaust system.
12820
12821
Most larger combustion equipment, including boilers, generators, and even some residential furnaces,
12822
use a forced draft burner and exhaust system. In any combustion system that is under positive pressure if
12823
is important that the equipment combustion chamber and the connected exhaust duct system are
12824
designed and constructed to be air tight. Since the exhaust system is transporting harmful combustion
12825
products out of the building, the system must be sealed to prevent any leakage of these products into the
12826
surrounding building spaces.
12827
12828
Design and Installation
12829
Regardless of the type of system used, all components of any capture and exhaust system must be
12830
designed and installed to adequately remove the products of combustion. Fan and duct materials must be
12831
selected to not be damaged by the temperature or chemical makeup of the gases being exhausted. Duct
12832
systems should be sealed air tight to prevent any leakage that may affect the system operation or
12833
contaminate the spaces in the building.
12834
12835
Exhaust systems should always be installed per equipment manufacturer’s instructions and any
12836
applicable building codes, but the location of exhaust outlets and discharge is extremely important.
12837
Exhaust outlets should never be located near building windows or other outside air intakes to ensure that
12838
combustion products cannot be reintroduced into the building.
12839
12840
Supply Air for Combustion
12841
Equally as important as the size and operation of the exhaust system to remove combustion products is
12842
the availability of an adequate supply of fresh supply air for combustion. An inadequate amount of
12843
supply air means incomplete combustion, which results in an increase of the harmful products of
12844
combustion. An inadequate amount of supply air can also cause a negative pressure at the equipment
12845
burner. This negative pressure condition interferes with the proper operation of the exhaust system and
12846
may result in a “backdraft” of air coming back down the exhaust duct.
12847
12848
Combustion processes and equipment that are connected to natural draft (chimney) or mechanical
12849
induced draft systems typically use the available air in the room for combustion. Therefore, the design of
12850
the room space must include provisions for continually introducing a new supply of air to make up for
12851
the air being used in combustion. This make up supply is normally provided through grilles, or
12852
additional ductwork, connecting the room to adjacent spaces or to the outside. This fresh air supply is
12853
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 423
Return to TOP
introduced in the room in at least two separate locations (typically high and low) in order to eliminate
12854
the effects of stratification and the natural convective movement of the air due to temperature
12855
differentials.
12856
12857
Much of the forced draft burner equipment also uses the available room air for combustion supply..
12858
Therefore, make up air must be continually provided to the areas near the burner. Connections to an
12859
outside supply of air, similar to the systems described above for induced draft exhaust, must be designed
12860
as part of the equipment installation.
12861
12862
Newer, more efficient combustion systems now use a direct vent system for supply air. This system
12863
connects the equipment directly to the outside through a supply air duct. The advantage of this system is
12864
that no air is pulled from the room in which the equipment is located, and there are no resulting room
12865
pressure problems. When designing and installing direct vent systems, always locate the outside inlet
12866
away from any potential contaminants.
12867
12868
Proper Operation and Maintenance of Equipment
12869
Even if the exhaust and combustion supply air systems are properly designed and installed, there is still
12870
a great potential for contamination from harmful combustion products if the combustion equipment
12871
itself is not functioning correctly. Always ensure that the equipment is installed per manufacturer’s
12872
instructions.
12873
Additionally, the systems described here should also be designed and installed so that all associated
12874
equipment can be properly maintained. Perform regular inspections of the equipment condition and
12875
operations, and repair or replace any defective equipment as required.
12876
12877
Commissioning
12878
In order to assure that contamination from combustion equipment is prevented, it is recommended that
12879
the procedures outlined above for the design and installation of equipment and exhaust and supply duct
12880
systems be included as a part of the building commissioning process.
12881
12882
12883
References
12884
[Note: References will be added prior to publication.]
12885
12886
12887
12888
12889
12890
12891
12892
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 424
Return to TOP
12893
Strategy 6.2 – Provide Local Capture and Exhaust for Point Sources of Contaminants
12894
12895
12896
Overview
12897
The effective capture and exhaust of contaminants generated within the occupied zones of facilities
12898
addressed by this guide can minimize the impact of these contaminants on the occupants in the area in
12899
which the contaminant is generated and surrounding occupied spaces. ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007,
12900
Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality, defines a contaminant as follows: “An unwanted
12901
airborne constituent that may reduce acceptability of the air.” Examples of spaces in which
12902
contaminants are generated in the occupied space are as follows: laboratories (commercial and school),
12903
commercial kitchens, copy/printing facilities, beauty and nail salons, food courts in shopping malls,
12904
technology shops in schools, and maintenance facilities.
12905
12906
Exhaust methodologies at the point of generation and system design strategies for some of these
12907
applications are addressed in other publications and are not repeated in this guide. For additional
12908
information, please see the following publications:
12909
12910
Commercial Kitchens:
12911
ASHRAE, 2007 HVAC Applications Handbook, Chapter 31
12912
National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) Standard 96
12913
Applicable Model Codes for Project Site (such as International Mechanical Code and
12914
International Fuel Gas Code)
12915
Laboratories:
12916
ASHRAE, 2007 HVAC Applications Handbook, Chapter 14 (includes listing of resource
12917
materials for various laboratory applications)
12918
12919
In all of these applications, there are three primary considerations in order to minimize the impact of the
12920
contaminants on the occupants in the immediate area and surrounding areas: (1) exhaust the
12921
contaminants as close to the source as possible, directly to the outdoors, (2) maintain the area in which
12922
these contaminants are generated at a negative pressure relative to the surrounding spaces to minimize
12923
the potential impact on occupants in adjacent spaces, and (3) enclose and exhaust the areas where
12924
diffuse contaminants are generated. Additional information concerning the relative pressures of
12925
adjacent spaces can be found in Strategy 6.4 Maintain Proper Pressure Relationships between Spaces.
12926
Also, there are often specific requirements in model building codes, as well as recommendations for
12927
these areas in ASHRAE standards and governmental publications.
12928
12929
While maintaining a negative pressure in the spaces where contaminants are generated, it is important to
12930
ensure that the overall building is maintained at a positive pressure relative to the outdoor environment
12931
and additional information on this subject is summarized in Strategy 6.4 Maintain Proper Pressure
12932
Relationships between Spaces.
12933
12934
The following information will address a few of the specific areas where point source of contaminants
12935
can present challenges in providing acceptable indoor air quality.
12936
12937
Exhaust Contaminants as Close to the Source as Possible and Directly to the Outdoors
12938
There are numerous examples of applications where exhausting contaminants close to the source and
12939
discharging directly to the outdoors provides effective minimization of the impact of the contaminants
12940
on occupants and surrounding spaces. One prevalent example is beauty and nail salons.
12941
12942
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 425
Return to TOP
Beauty and nail salons use chemicals extensively in the processes common to these facilities. As
12943
mentioned above, exhaust air at the source of the contaminants is the most effective method for
12944
minimizing the effect of these contaminants on occupants of the space. This may be accomplished by
12945
the use of a hood or snorkel over the treatment area or, in the case of nail treatment, exhaust at table
12946
height of the workstation. An example of a localized exhaust at the worktable is shown in Figure 1.
12947
12948
12949
Figure 6.2-B (Source: US EPA No. 744-F-07-001)
12950
12951
Table 403.3 of the International Mechanical Code, 2006 Edition, and ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 also
12952
address minimum exhaust requirements for these facilities. Per Table 403.3 of the IMC, exhaust of 50
12953
CFM intermittent or 20 CFM continuous is required per station (with a general ventilation requirement
12954
of 25 CFM per person in a beauty salon) with additional requirements that recirculation of air from this
12955
space is prohibited and the contaminants and orders shall be captured at the source. Also, in ASHRAE
12956
Standard 62.1-2007, there is a general exhaust recommendation of 0.6 CFM/sq. ft. for beauty and nail
12957
salons in Table 6-4.
12958
12959
In addition, there are recommendations concerning exhaust systems in this application prepared by the
12960
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, summarized in EPA No. 744-F-7-001 dated March, 2007. This
12961
guide recommends the following:
12962
Nail salons should have one or a combination of the following:
12963
-
Work table with an exhaust vent embedded in it that is vented to the
12964
outdoors.
12965
-
Ceiling or wall-mounted exhaust system with exhaust intake suspended
12966
above the work table.”
12967
12968
The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) also has published recommendations,
12969
which reiterate the code requirements indicated above.
12970
12971
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 426
Return to TOP
Another example of the need for exhaust close to the source is commercial laundry facilities. These
12972
facilities utilize chemicals extensively, which heightens the need to exhaust close to the source. In
12973
addition, in the drying area of these facilities, the exhaust is heavily moisture-laden. The failure to
12974
effectively exhaust the dryers close to the source and direct to the outdoors will increase the potential for
12975
moisture problems inside the space and the resulting mold and/or mildew.
12976
12977
In any application where diffuse contaminants are generated, a direct connection at the source is the
12978
preferred method of exhaust. A direct connection will limit spread of the contaminants within the space,
12979
minimizing the effects of the contaminants on other occupants and furnishings inside of this zone, as
12980
well as reducing the possibility of the contaminants spreading to surrounding spaces.
12981
12982
One of the occupancy categories in which direct connection is recommended is automobile repair
12983
rooms. ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007, Table 6-4, Minimum Exhaust Rates suggests an exhaust air
12984
quantity of 1.5 CFM per square foot and also denotes that repair stands where engines are operated for
12985
testing and repair “shall have exhaust systems that directly connect to the engine exhaust and prevent
12986
escape of fumes.”
12987
12988
Maintain the area in which these contaminants are generated at a negative pressure relative to the
12989
surrounding spaces
12990
Proper space pressurization is required to minimize contaminant transfer across adjacent spaces. Space
12991
pressurization is the static pressure difference between the adjacent spaces of a building. This static
12992
pressure difference will influence where exfiltration and infiltration will occur across the adjacent spaces.
12993
Additional information about space pressurization can be found in Strategy 6.4 Maintain Proper Pressure
12994
Relationships between Spaces.
12995
12996
The design intent for any area where diffuse contaminants are generated is to maintain this area at a
12997
negative pressure relative to the surrounding spaces in order to minimize the migration of contaminants
12998
to any of the adjacent spaces. To maintain the space at a negative pressure, the total amount of exhaust
12999
air must exceed the amount of supply air delivered to the space. The balance of the supply air for the
13000
exhausted space will be accomplished by transfer air from the adjacent spaces. In evaluating the total
13001
building, the designer must be careful to maintain a positive pressure in the building relative to the
13002
outdoors to minimize infiltration of unfiltered, untreated outdoor air directly into the occupied spaces.
13003
13004
One example of the need to maintain pressure differentials of different areas is health-care facilities,
13005
such as hospitals and medical care facilities. The 2007 ASHRAE Handbook – HVAC Applications
13006
includes specific tabular data (Chapter 7, Tables 3 and 6) about the recommended pressure relationships
13007
to adjacent spaces for hospitals, outpatient facilities, and nursing facilities. Table 6 from this publication
13008
is shown below as an example of the information provided for this particular application. Also, there is
13009
additional design guidance for this application in the American Institute of Architects publication
13010
entitled “Guidelines for Design and Construction of Health Care Facilities.”
13011
13012
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 427
Return to TOP
13013
Table 6.2-A Pressure Relationships and Ventilation of Certain Areas of Nursing Facilities
13014
13015
Source 2007 ASHRAE Handbook – Applications
13016
13017
Another application where specific recommendations are provided for spaces to be maintained at a
13018
negative pressure is natatorium design, due to the utilization of chemicals to maintain an acceptable
13019
quality of the water for the swimmers. Chapter 4 of 2007 ASHRAE Handbook – HVAC Applications
13020
states: “Pool and spa areas should be maintained at a negative pressure of 0.05 to 0.15 in. of water
13021
relative to adjacent areas of the building to prevent chloramine odor migration.” The chloramine
13022
compounds are not only objectionable in odor, but corrosive in nature, which can be detrimental to
13023
construction components and furnishings in the adjacent areas. Figure 3 below shows the direct exhaust
13024
of a typical natatorium area to the outside, as well as maintaining a negative pressure relative to adjacent
13025
spaces.
13026
13027
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 428
Return to TOP
13028
Figure 6.2-C Natatorium Design
13029
Note: Figure will be modified to show negative pressure needed at door of adjacent space.
13030
13031
Similarly, there are specific code requirements for ventilation air quantities in applications of duplication
13032
and printing workrooms. Per Table 430.3 of the International Mechanical Code (2006), there is a
13033
minimum ventilation requirement of 0.5 CFM/sq. ft. for these areas. In ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007,
13034
this same 0.5 CFM/sq. ft. is recommended as a minimum exhaust rate for copy and printing rooms.
13035
13036
There are many other areas where ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 recommends a minimum exhaust rate,
13037
due to the potential impact of contaminants on the space of utilization and adjacent spaces. Examples of
13038
some of these additional areas of concern are as follows: art classrooms, copy/printing facilities,
13039
kitchenettes or breakrooms, photography darkrooms, educational science labs, parking garages, and
13040
woodworking shops.
13041
13042
Enclose and exhaust areas where diffuse contaminants are generated
13043
In addition to exhausting as close to the contaminant source as possible and maintaining the area in
13044
which the contaminants are generated under a negative pressure, enclosing the area where the
13045
contaminants are generated can assist in minimizing the migration of the contaminants into adjacent
13046
spaces. It is recommended that enclosing the space should be utilized in conjunction with the other
13047
strategies in this chapter, not in lieu of these strategies.
13048
13049
If enclosing the space in which contaminants are generated is utilized exclusively in an attempt to
13050
control the contaminants, the potential pressure differential between adjacent spaces will possibly allow
13051
the contaminants to migrate to other areas of the facility. If utilizing space enclosure is the primary
13052
strategy to reduce migration of contaminants to adjacent spaces, it is important to ensure that all
13053
penetrations of the enclosure walls and openings into the room are properly sealed to minimize any
13054
leakage into the surrounding areas.
13055
13056
13057
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 429
Return to TOP
13058
Strategy 6.3 – Design Exhaust Systems to Prevent Leakage of Exhaust Air into
13059
Occupied Spaces or Air Distribution Systems
13060
13061
Overview
13062
The effective capture and exhaust of odors and contaminants generated within the occupied zones of
13063
facilities is addressed in the section titled Strategy 6.2 Provide Local Capture and Exhaust for Point
13064
Sources of Contaminants in this guide. After the effective capture of the odors and/or contaminants, the
13065
exhaust air must be conveyed to the outdoors. It is important to take steps to minimize the potential for
13066
leakage of the odors and contaminants into other areas of the building or air distribution systems prior to
13067
discharge to the outdoors and, when discharged, also minimize the potential for the exhaust air to be
13068
entrained into ventilation air intakes for the subject building or adjacent facilities. Areas which require
13069
exhaust include toilet rooms, soiled laundry storage rooms, pet shops (animal areas) and spaces where
13070
contaminants are generated as a part of such processes as cooking, scientific procedures and
13071
experimentation, generation and reproduction of paper materials, personal nail treatments, woodworking
13072
and metal shop procedures, and areas where chemicals are utilized extensively (such as natatoriums). In
13073
addition, if the building design requires the use of smoke control system(s), then the potential impact of
13074
leakage of the exhaust system can have health or life safety consequences.
13075
13076
Exhaust methodologies at the point of generation and system design strategies for some of these
13077
applications are addressed in other publications and are not repeated in this guide. For additional
13078
information, please see the following publications:
13079
13080
Commercial Kitchens:
13081
ASHRAE, 2007 HVAC Applications Handbook, Chapter 31
13082
National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) Standard 96
13083
Applicable Model Codes for Project Site (such as International Mechanical Code and
13084
International Fuel Gas Code)
13085
Laboratories:
13086
ASHRAE, 2007 HVAC Applications Handbook, Chapter 14 (includes listing of resource
13087
materials for various laboratory applications)
13088
13089
In each of the applications addressed in this guide, there are three primary considerations in order to
13090
minimize the possibility of impacting other occupied spaces and/or air distribution systems: (1)
13091
effectively seal ductwork to minimize potential for leakage from the duct system, (2) provide outdoor
13092
discharge position and configuration to minimize entrainment of exhaust air into fresh air ventilation
13093
systems or adjacent buildings, and (3) maintain exhaust ducts in plenum spaces under a negative
13094
pressure.
13095
13096
Strategy 6.4 Maintain Proper Pressure Relationships between Spaces should be reviewed for additional
13097
information concerning exhaust systems and adjacent spaces. Also, there are often requirements in
13098
model building codes, as well as recommendations for these areas in ASHRAE standards and
13099
governmental publications, that address specific issues such as hazardous exhaust, flammable exhaust,
13100
etc.
13101
13102
The following information will address a few of the specific design and installation considerations
13103
where exhaust air routing and termination can present challenges in providing acceptable indoor air
13104
quality.
13105
13106
13107
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 430
Return to TOP
Effectively seal ductwork to minimize potential for leakage from the duct
13108
Exhaust duct systems should be effectively sealed in order to minimize the potential for leakage from
13109
the ductwork. A wide variety of sealing methods and products exist for ductwork in the construction
13110
industry. For more specific information about the sealing methods and products, refer to the following
13111
publication: SMACNA HVAC Duct Construction Standards. For purposes of this guide, we will focus
13112
on the potential effects of improper sealing of the exhaust ductwork.
13113
13114
The leakage rates for unsealed ductwork vary considerably with the type of fabrication utilized, the
13115
method of assembly, and the installation workmanship. The resulting leakage rates of specific
13116
installations indicate the potential for issues with exhaust airstreams in an occupied facility. In Chapter
13117
35 of the 2005 ASHRAE Handbook - Fundamentals, test results are quoted which indicate that the
13118
leakage rate for unsealed, longitudinal seams in ductwork averages from 0.08 to 0.16 CFM per foot of
13119
seam length at 1-inch w.g., depending on the duct construction method. Also, the test results indicate
13120
that this leakage rate equates to approximately 10-15% of the total duct leakage.
13121
13122
ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007, Paragraph 5.3 states the following concerning sealing of exhaust ducts:
13123
“Exhaust ducts that convey potentially harmful contaminants shall be negatively pressurized relative to
13124
spaces through which they pass, so that exhaust air cannot leak into occupied spaces; supply, return, or
13125
outdoor air ducts; or plenums.” The following exception is stated for this requirement: “Exhaust ducts
13126
that are sealed in accordance with SMACNA Seal Class A.” This seal class is defined in the SMACNA
13127
HVAC Duct Construction Standards publication.
13128
13129
Table 8A of the 2005 ASHRAE Handbook – Fundamentals recommends a “B” seal class for exhaust
13130
ductwork installed indoors, either in unconditioned or conditioned spaces. This seal class requires that
13131
all transverse joints and longitudinal seams be effectively sealed. Seal class “A”, which requires the
13132
sealing of duct wall penetrations in addition to sealing of joints and seams, may be advantageous if the
13133
exhaust duct is not routed in a chase isolated from the occupied space or if the exhaust system is part of
13134
a smoke control system for the facility. These seal classes are consistent with the SMACNA HVAC
13135
Duct Construction Standards publication.
13136
13137
The following information concerning duct leakage tests is excerpted from the 2005 ASHRAE
13138
Handbook – Fundamentals:
13139
13140
“Leakage tests should be conducted in compliance with SMACNA’s HVAC Air Duct Leakage Test
13141
Manual (1985) to verify the intent of the designer and the workmanship of the installing contractor.
13142
Leakage tests used to confirm leakage class should be conducted at the pressure class for which the duct
13143
is constructed. Leakage testing is also addressed in ASHRAE Standard 90.1.
13144
13145
Limited performance standards for metal duct sealants and tapes exist. For guidance in their selection
13146
and use refer to SMACNA’s HVAC Duct Construction Standards (1995). Fibrous glass ducts and their
13147
closure systems are covered by UL Standards 181and 181A. For fibrous glass duct construction
13148
standards consult NAIMA (1997) and SMACNA (1992). Flexible duct performance and installation
13149
standards are covered by UL 181, UL 181B and ADC (1996). Soldered or welded duct construction is
13150
necessary where sealants are not suitable. Sealants used on exterior ducts must be resistant to weather,
13151
temperature cycles, sunlight, and ozone.”
13152
13153
It should be noted that since the publication of the 2005 ASHRAE Handbook – Fundamentals,
13154
SMACNA’s HVAC Duct Construction Standards publication was updated in 2005, which is now the
13155
current edition.
13156
13157
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 431
Return to TOP
Any leakage of the installed ductwork can be minimized by effectively sealing the ductwork as indicated
13158
above. The amount of leakage, based on observations of installed systems, will be significantly greater
13159
if the ductwork is eliminated and a chase constructed of shaft wall or drywall is utilized to convey the
13160
exhaust air from the space to the outdoors. This practice should be avoided to minimize the potential for
13161
leakage from the exhaust system into occupied spaces.
13162
13163
13164
Figure 6.3-B (to be added)
13165
13166
Provide outdoor discharge position and configuration to minimize entrainment of exhaust air into
13167
fresh air ventilation systems or adjacent buildings
13168
After effectively sealing the ductwork to prevent leakage of the exhaust air and associated contaminants,
13169
the air must be discharged to the building exterior. The position and configuration of the exhaust
13170
termination will have a direct impact on minimizing the potential for entrainment of the exhaust
13171
airstream into fresh air ventilation systems or adjacent buildings.
13172
13173
Table 5-1 of ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 provides recommended minimum separation distances
13174
between air intake locations and various types of exhaust airstreams (see Table 6.3-A). Some of these
13175
separation distances exceed the current requirements of the model codes (such as the International
13176
Mechanical Code), which does not differentiate separation distances based on the type of exhaust
13177
airstream. The current edition of the IMC requires a minimum of 10 feet of separation between exhaust
13178
and intake locations or, alternatively, an intake opening located a minimum of two (2) feet below the
13179
contaminant source.
13180
13181
In addition to a minimum separation distance as required by the applicable building code and/or
13182
recommended by ASHRAE Standard 62.1, Chapter 44 of the 2007 ASHRAE Handbook – HVAC
13183
Applications includes recommendations for the stack discharge of exhaust airstreams.
13184
13185
Previous ASHRAE research indicates that stacks terminating below the level of adjacent walls and
13186
architectural enclosures frequently do not effectively reduce roof-level exhaust contamination. To take
13187
full advantage of their height, stacks should be located on the highest roof of a building. Architectural
13188
screens used to mask rooftop equipment adversely affect exhaust dilution, depending on porosity,
13189
relative height, and distance from the stack. Prevailing wind conditions should also be evaluated to
13190
determine the potential impact on the exhaust plume.
13191
13192
Photo of installation exhibiting significant duct leakage
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 432
Return to TOP
13193
Table 6.3-A Air Intake Minimum Separation Distance
13194
Object
Minimum Distance
ft (m)
Significantly contaminated exhaust (Note 1)
15 (5)
Noxious or dangerous exhaust (Notes 2 and 3)
30 (10)
Vents, chimneys, and flues from combustion appliances and equipment (Note 4)
15 (5)
Garage entry, automobile loading area, or drive-in queue (Note 5)
15 (5)
Truck loading area or dock, bus parking/idling area (Note 5)
25 (7.5)
Driveway, street, or parking place (Note 5)
5 (1.5)
Thoroughfare with high traffic volume
25 (7.5)
Roof, landscaped grade, or other surface directly below intake (Notes 6 and 7)
1 (0.30)
Garbage storage/pick-up area, dumpsters
15 (5)
Cooling tower intake or basin
15 (5)
Cooling tower exhaust
25 (7.5)
Note 1: Significantly contaminated exhaust is exhaust air with significant contaminant concentration, significant sensory-irritation
intensity or offensive odor.
Note 2: Laboratory fume hood exhaust air outlets shall be in compliance with NFPA 45-1991 and ANSI/AIHA A9.5-1992
Note 3: Noxious or dangerous exhaust is exhaust air with highly objectionable fumes or gases and/or exhaust air with potentially
dangerous particles, bioaerosols, or gases at concentrations high enough to be considered harmful. Information on separation criteria for
industrial environments can be found in the ACHIH Industrial Ventilation Manual and in the ASHARE Handbook –HVAC Applications.
Note 4: Shorter separation distances are permitted when determined in accordance with (a) Chapter 7 of ANSI A233.1/NFPA 54-2002 for
fuel gas burning appliances and equipment, (b) Chapter 6 of NFPA 31-2001 for oil burning appliances and equipment, or (c) Chapter 7 of
NFPA 211-2003 for other combustion appliances and equipment.
Note 5: Distance measured to closest place that vehicle exhaust is likely to be located
Note 6: No minimum separation distance applies to surfaces that are sloped more than 45 degrees from horizontal or that are less than 1
inch (3cm) wide.
Note 7: Where snow accumulation is expected, distance listed shall be increased by the expected average snow depth.
Source: Table 5-1 ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007
13195
13196
In addition, adjacent structures or terrain close to the emitting building can adversely affect stack
13197
exhaust dilution, because the emitting building can be within the recirculation flow zones downwind of
13198
these nearby flow obstacles. Also, an air intake located on a nearby taller building can be contaminated
13199
by exhausts from the shorter building. Wherever possible, facilities emitting toxic or highly odorous
13200
contaminants should not be located near taller buildings or at the base of steep terrain.
13201
13202
Stacks should be vertically directed and uncapped. Stack caps that deflect the exhaust jet have a
13203
detrimental effect on the exhaust plume. Small conical stack caps often do not completely exclude rain.
13204
Periods of heavy rainfall are often accompanied by high winds that deflect raindrops under the cap and
13205
into the stack. A stack exhaust velocity of about 2500 fpm prevents condensed moisture from draining
13206
down the stack and keeps rain from entering the stack. For intermittently operated systems, protection
13207
from rain and snow should be provided by stack drains, rather than stack caps.
13208
13209
High stack exhaust velocity and temperatures increase plume rise, which tends to reduce intake
13210
contamination. Exhaust velocities, in general, should be maintained above 2000 fpm to provide adequate
13211
plume rise. Velocities above 2000 fpm may result in objectionable noise and vibration from exhaust
13212
fans. A nozzle on the stack discharge can be used to increase exhaust velocity and plume rise. If the
13213
exhaust system utilizes variable-volume fans, the stack exhaust velocity calculation must be based on
13214
the minimum total flow rate from the system.
13215
13216
An exception to these exhaust velocity recommendations may be when corrosive condensate droplets
13217
are discharged. In this case, a velocity of 1000 fpm in the stack and a condensate drain are recommended
13218
to reduce droplet emission. At this low exhaust velocity, a taller stack may be needed to counteract
13219
downwash caused by low exit velocity. Downwash occurs where low-velocity exhausts are
13220
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 433
Return to TOP
pulled downward by negative pressures immediately downwind of the stack.
13221
13222
For unique exhaust applications, additional detailed calculation methodologies for estimating stack
13223
height and exhaust-to-intake dilution are available in Chapter 44 of the 2007 ASHRAE Handbook –
13224
HVAC Applications.
13225
13226
Maintain exhaust ducts in plenum spaces under a negative pressure
13227
As noted above, ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007, Paragraph 5.3 indicates that exhaust ducts shall be
13228
negatively pressurized relative to spaces through which they pass, so that exhaust air cannot leak into
13229
occupied spaces; supply, return, or outdoor air ducts; or plenums. In addition, the model building codes
13230
also address this application.
13231
13232
For example, the International Mechanical Code states under a paragraph titled “Contamination
13233
prevention” requires that exhaust ducts under positive pressure, chimneys, and vents shall not extend
13234
into or pass through ducts or plenums.
13235
13236
To achieve this objective, the exhaust fan on any system that requires routing of the ductwork through a
13237
plenum space must be located at the end of the duct run closest to the building exit point. This should
13238
not alter the methodology of sealing of the exhaust duct as discussed above.
13239
13240
In Strategy 6.2 Provide Local Capture and Exhaust for Point Sources of Contaminants, the importance
13241
of exhausting as close to the source of contaminant is emphasized. However, in applying this
13242
philosophy, if the system design or type of contaminant dictates the need for the fan location closer to
13243
the location of the source contaminant, then it is important to avoid routing of the ductwork through a
13244
plenum space. Sealing of the ductwork (as described in the section above) becomes even more
13245
important to minimize the risk of leakage of the contaminant-laden exhaust airstream into the plenum
13246
space.
13247
13248
Figure 6.3-C Maintain Exhaust Ducts in Plenum Spaces under a Negative Pressure
13249
13250
13251
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 434
Return to TOP
13252
Strategy 6.4 – Maintain Proper Pressure Relationships between Spaces
13253
13254
13255
Proper space pressurization is required to minimize moisture and contaminant transfer across adjacent
13256
spaces. Space pressurization is the static pressure difference between the adjacent spaces of a building
13257
with the air moving from higher pressure spaces to lower pressure spaces. This static pressure
13258
difference will influence where exfiltration and infiltration will occur across the adjacent spaces.
13259
Maintaining proper pressure relationships between adjacent spaces is critical to ensure airflow in the
13260
proper direction, from clean spaces to dirty spaces. Many HVAC systems are designed to achieve space
13261
pressurization at an average of 0.05 inches (12.45 Pa) differential.
13262
13263
Space Usage
13264
The space usage needs to be addressed so that proper design considerations may be applied. All
13265
contaminants and moisture levels require to be identified so that contamination of adjacent spaces will
13266
be minimized. In addition, it needs to be identified if the space will be positively or negatively
13267
pressurized. In some space usage and applications it is required to Strategy 6.2 Provide Local Capture
13268
and Exhaust for Point Sources of Contaminants
13269
13270
Following is a listing of some common spaces that should be identified for proper space pressurization.
13271
13272
1. Remodels and phased constructions and considerations during construction
13273
a. During construction apply pressurization strategies to keep the moisture and contaminants
13274
from being drawn from the building exterior or areas under construction into the occupied or
13275
finished areas. This can include sequencing HVAC equipment operation or lock out during
13276
construction to avoid negatively pressurizing the finished areas (operating exhaust fans with
13277
no ventilation air) or positively pressurizing the areas under construction. As noted
13278
in Strategy 1.4 Managing Construction and Renovation to Preserve IAQ, avoid utilizing
13279
permanent HVAC equipment during construction and remodel.
13280
2. Medical Office Buildings and Surgery Spaces
13281
a. Medical office buildings will require special attention to space pressurization. Most surgery
13282
spaces are required to be positively pressured to adjacent spaces while certain lab areas will
13283
require to be negatively pressurized to the adjacent spaces. Standards for surgery spaces and
13284
other medical related spaces and their application are discussed by the American Institute of
13285
Architects (AIA 2001), ASHRAE Applications Manual. The Joint Commission on Accreditation of
13286
Healthcare Organizations (JCAHO) also enforces HVAC system design and construction standards
13287
listed in the AIA guidelines. In addition to these regulations, standards and guidelines published by
13288
ASHRAE, the AIA, American Society of Hospital Engineers (ASHE), Centers for Disease Control
13289
and Prevention (CDC), National Institute of Health (NIH), and National Institute of Occupational
13290
Safety and Health (NIOSH) define the expectation of care and services in health care.
13291
3. Janitor and chemical storage spaces
13292
a. These spaces should be negatively pressured with respect to adjacent spaces to contain the
13293
chemical odors and contaminants.
13294
4. Natatoriums
13295
a. The natatorium space should be negatively pressured with respect to adjacent spaces to
13296
contain the associated pool odors.
13297
5. Kitchens
13298
a. The kitchen space should be negatively pressured with respect to adjacent spaces to contain
13299
the associated cooking odors and particulate.
13300
13301
13302
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 435
Return to TOP
6. Laboratories
13303
a. Lab spaces should ensure airflow in the proper direction, from clean area to dirty area. A
13304
chemistry lab located in a school should be negatively pressurized to adjacent spaces to
13305
contain the chemicals and particulate utilized in the lab space. Reference the ASHRAE
13306
Laboratory Design Guide for specific requirements on laboratory design.
13307
7. Parking Garages
13308
a. Parking garages that are connected to an office building structure should be kept in a
13309
negative pressure with respect to the office building to keep the moisture and contaminants
13310
from entering the building from the parking garage. Revolving doors are a good option for
13311
the building entrance to help limit the infiltration and compartmentalize to two spaces.
13312
8. Mixed Used Facilities
13313
a. In multi-family residential buildings there are typically multiple types of space usage. There
13314
could be a restaurant, beauty salon, dry cleaner and the residences all located in the same
13315
building. There should be a clean space to dirty space pressure relationship for these mixed
13316
uses utilizing proper space pressurization and directional airflow.
13317
9. Core and Shell Construction with Future Tenant Finish
13318
a. The space pressurization cannot be addressed during initial building design since the space
13319
usage has not been defined and adjacent spaces to the space of concern can constantly be
13320
changing. Evaluation and coordination of the complete building and the existing tenants
13321
should be performed every time a tenant is to be added. This evaluation should include
13322
tenant location, adjacent tenant usage, and the pressurization and airflow affects the new
13323
tenant will have on the existing tenants and the building as a whole (effects on total building
13324
pressure).
13325
13326
Space Layout
13327
Select proper space layout within the building for the most advantageous location of rooms and spaces if
13328
moisture or contaminants are a concern. Consider the location of spaces in exterior zones versus interior
13329
zones. If a space requires being negative in pressure to adjacent spaces, consider locating this space on
13330
an interior zone to prevent the negative space creating a lower pressure in relationship to the exterior to
13331
alleviate any possible infiltration of unconditioned air into the space from the exterior of the building.
13332
Review the space usages and the moisture and contaminant concerns and locate the adjacent spaces
13333
trying to make the proper airflow from clean space to dirty space easily obtainable.
13334
13335
In some space layout situations an anteroom might be required. The anteroom is a transition room
13336
between areas of substantially different pressures or that is used to gain access to a room that must
13337
maintain its pressure even during disturbances such as opening a door. The use of anterooms provides
13338
assurance that pressure relationships are constantly maintained and air remains flowing from clean to
13339
dirty, and they reduce the need for the HVAC control system to respond to large disturbances (ASHRAE
13340
Laboratory Design Guide).
13341
13342
The pressure relationships between spaces need to be determined in the planning phase. A preliminary
13343
pressure map should be developed identifying the space usage/layout and listing the supply airflow
13344
quantity, return airflow quantity, and exhaust airflow quantity. Once the space airflows are listed, the
13345
directional airflow between spaces can be shown with arrows and airflow values. Figure XX displays
13346
the pressure mapping process.
13347
13348
In addition, consider the air pressures in unoccupied spaces as well as occupied areas of the building. If
13349
the air pressure in crawlspaces, basements, or underground ducts falls below the air pressure in the soil,
13350
radon and other soil gases could be drawn into those spaces and into the building. If you are concerned
13351
that depressurization of ground contact spaces could draw radon (or other contaminants in soil gas) into
13352
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 436
Return to TOP
the building, Strategy 3.2 Control Entry of Radon and the U.S. EPA offer guidance on radon-resistant
13353
design.
13354
13355
13356
Figure 6.4-B Pressure Mapping Diagram
13357
13358
Space Envelope
13359
The effectiveness of the space pressurization is dependent upon the leakiness of the space envelope.
13360
The design and construction of the space envelope should limit exfiltration, infiltration and
13361
leakage. Strategy 2.2 Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces
13362
provides information on the design and construction of the space envelope. Identify planned openings
13363
in the space envelope that may impact the ability of the HVAC system to provide overall proper
13364
pressurization by inadvertently increasing envelope leakiness. These planned openings can include roof
13365
vents, louvers, floor drains (traps), conduit penetrations, electrical outlets, lights, and ductwork
13366
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 437
Return to TOP
penetrations. Architectural planned openings such as doors and windows need to be considered. This
13367
would include the undercut height of the door serving the space.
13368
13369
In addition to the planned openings, identify potential unplanned openings that may impact the ability of
13370
the HVAC system to provide overall proper pressurization by increasing the envelope leakiness.
13371
Verification of proper construction material usage and proper installation techniques during the
13372
construction process should take place. Jobsite visits making inspections and taking photographs can
13373
help confirm that the space envelope will meet or exceed design requirements.
13374
13375
Space Compartmentalization
13376
If space pressurization is not an option, sealing and type of construction to compartmentalize space and
13377
sources of contaminants and moisture should be utilized. This includes proper use of construction
13378
materials such as sealants, wall coverings, air barriers and vapor barriers. Reference Strategy 2.2 Limit
13379
Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior Surfaces for a more complete discussion on
13380
proper envelope construction.
13381
13382
HVAC System
13383
Air Volume Considerations
13384
Space pressurization is a method to reduce infiltration and airflow from dirty and contaminated areas to
13385
cleaner areas. This is usually accomplished by exhausting a different volume of air than is supplied. If
13386
more air is exhausted than supplied, the space is negatively pressurized and if less air is exhausted than
13387
supplied, the space is positively pressurized. A similar approach is usually applied within a room where
13388
design of the air distribution system is made to have the air flowing from cleaner areas to
13389
dirtier/contaminated areas. However, movement of personnel and opening of doors disturbs the flow
13390
pattern and this strategy should not be used where it is critical that airborne contaminants are not spread
13391
to sensitive areas within the room. (ASHRAE Laboratory Design Guide Page 191).
13392
13393
Air classification and recirculation can be classified as noted in ASHRAE Standard 62.1 section 5.17.
13394
This air classification can help identify the clean to dirty relationship and pressurization or directional
13395
airflow requirements.
13396
13397
A room’s differential airflow rate is often called offset flow, which is the sum of all leakage flows (in or
13398
out) in the room. Figure 7 shows the relationship between leakage flow rates at a specific pressure
13399
differential across an opening. Each curve on the chart represents a different leakage area.
13400
Once a leakage area along a doorframe is estimated, then leakage through the crack while the door is
13401
closed can be calculated based on the pressure difference across the door. Room air tightness is the key
13402
element in the relationship between the room’s flow offset value and the resulting pressure differential,
13403
and each room air tightness is unique and unknown unless tested. (ASHRAE HVAC Applications
13404
Manual) Air tightness of the room is dependent on the space envelope design and construction.
13405
13406
As a basic rule of thumb, if the space requires being negative in pressure in relationship to the adjacent
13407
spaces to contain the moisture or contaminants, the exhaust airflow from the space should exceed the
13408
supply airflow into the space by 10 to 20 percent. If the space requires being positive in pressure to the
13409
adjacent spaces to keep contaminants from entering the space, the supply airflow from the space should
13410
exceed the exhaust airflow into the space by 10 to 20 percent. The goal is to maintain a 0.05” (12.45 Pa)
13411
pressure differential between adjacent spaces. For more critical contaminant control, reference the
13412
ASHRAE Laboratory Design Guide.
13413
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 438
Return to TOP
13414
13415
Airflow Monitoring and Control
13416
Following are methods, adapted from the AHSRAE HVAC Applications Manual, used to pressurize
13417
spaces relative to adjacent spaces.
13418
13419
Constant Volume Differential Airflow
13420
There is technically no monitoring or control of a constant volume differential airflow setup. The
13421
HVAC system is designed with a constant airflow offset to maintain proper space pressurization. The
13422
airflows are tested and balanced to the designed supply and exhaust airflow values to maintain to obtain
13423
the airflow offset and the proper space pressure. These exhaust and supply airflow systems operate at a
13424
constant airflow value. An example of a constant volume airflow differential system is a kitchen located
13425
in a school. The exhaust airflow through the kitchen exhaust hoods would exceed the supply airflow
13426
into the kitchen. This excess exhaust airflow would create a negatively pressurized kitchen with respect
13427
to the adjacent areas. An adjacent area or areas to the kitchen would provide the make-up offset airflow
13428
quantity for the exhaust hood airflow.
13429
13430
Differential static-pressure control
13431
Differential static-pressure control uses a pressure differential sensor to measure the pressure difference
13432
between a controlled room and an adjacent space (e.g., a corridor). Differential-static pressure control is
13433
suitable for a tightly constructed room with limited traffic. It basically ignores the specific offset airflow
13434
value as required; instead, it directly controls the airflow control devices (e.g., variable air volume boxes
13435
or air valves) to achieve the required pressure differential between the controlled room and an adjacent
13436
space. The location of measurement for the static pressure must be selected carefully, away from drafts
13437
or diffusers and in a representative area. A door switch is recommended to trigger a reduced pressure-
13438
differential set point if the door opens.
13439
13440
13441
13442
Figure 6.4-C Flow Rate through Leakage Area under Pressure Differential
(Source: ASHRAE HVAC Applications Manual Page 16.6
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 439
Return to TOP
Differential flow tracking control
13443
Differential flow tracking control assumes an offset value based on intuitive guesswork; this value is
13444
then used as a volumetric or mass flow difference between entering and leaving airflows through their
13445
airflow control devices (e.g., variable air volume boxes or air valves). This method is suitable for open-
13446
style rooms or rooms with frequent traffic. Differential flow tracking control normally maintains the
13447
same offset value throughout operation to keep pressurization constant. A constant-percentage offset
13448
value is sometimes used, but this creates a weaker pressurization at a lower flow.
13449
13450
Hybrid control or cascaded control
13451
Hybrid control or cascaded control combines the pressure accuracy of differential static-pressure control
13452
and the stability of differential flow tracking control. The offset value is resettable based on the pressure
13453
differential reading. The offset value reset schedule is predetermined, and the controller’s parameters are
13454
fixed manually in field.
13455
13456
Return Air Plenums
13457
If the HVAC system uses and open plenum above the dropped ceiling for returning air instead of a hard
13458
connected duct, the plenum will be in a negative pressure. This negative pressure difference can create
13459
untreated infiltration as air finds its way in unplanned construction openings and draws air from areas
13460
that may contain contaminants and moisture that is unwanted in the HVAC system. These contaminants
13461
and moisture can then be distributed in the building through the HVAC system. If there are spaces in
13462
the building that have high contaminant and moisture levels, i.e. lab rooms, kitchens, copier rooms,
13463
avoid using open return air plenums and utilize sealed return ducts when possible.
13464
13465
If the mechanical room is the return air plenum and/or mixing plenum for the HVAC equipment, then
13466
consideration needs to be made with regard to the negative pressure of the plenum/space. This “return
13467
air plenum” will be drawing air form the adjacent spaces and this could contaminate the return air to the
13468
HVAC system. In addition, storage of materials and chemicals should not take place in this
13469
plenum/space.
13470
13471
13472
Figure 6.4-D This return air plenum was negatively pressurized with respect
13473
to the outdoors. The moist outside air infiltrated the plenum and condensed
13474
on the ceiling tiles creating a breeding ground for mold. (Photo courtesy W. Anis)
13475
13476
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 440
Return to TOP
Duct Leakage
13477
If ductwork is located in unconditioned spaces, leaks in supply ducts waste energy and complicates
13478
pressure management. The leaking supply airflow from the ductwork will infiltrate the space and
13479
possibly create a positive space pressure in the space of concern. This could force moisture or
13480
contaminants to other spaces of lower pressure creating mold or contamination in unwanted areas. The
13481
same is true for exhaust ductwork, just the reverse pressure issue. Any leakage in the exhaust or return
13482
ductwork could create a negatively pressured space drawing in moisture or contaminants. In addition,
13483
this leaking airflow is uncontrolled and the paths the airflow takes cannot be determined and wall
13484
cavities, ceiling plenums, etc. can become the path of least resistance drawing in the moisture and
13485
contaminants and bacterial growth can occur in areas that are not exposed for visual inspection. Leaky
13486
return ducts could also draw in unwanted moisture and contaminants that could then be supplied
13487
throughout the building through the HVAC air handling system.
13488
13489
SMACNA guidelines for proper construction and sealing of ductwork should be followed to help
13490
eliminate excessive ductwork leakage. For additional measures the ductwork can be pressure tested for
13491
air leakage. The SMACNA Duct Air leakage Test Manual provides procedures and guidelines for leak
13492
testing ductwork.
13493
13494
Airflow Measurement
13495
Allow for accurate and repeatable field measurement of airflows: supply airflow, return airflow and
13496
exhaust airflow. To be able to evaluate the space pressurization performance one must be able to
13497
determine the actual airflow quantities into and out of the space. One of the most accurate methods for
13498
determining airflow quantity is the duct traverse. For all ducted fan systems, AMCA & ASHRAE both
13499
identify an ideal duct traverse plane as 2 ½ equivalent duct diameters from condition (discharge, elbow,
13500
etc) for up to 2500 fpm, this is both upstream and downstream of the duct traverse. If the air velocity
13501
exceeds 2500 fpm, then one needs to add 1 equivalent diameter for each additional 100 fpm. For
13502
rectangular duct the equivalent length EL= (4a\*b/Π)0.5 , where “a” & “b” are the duct dimensions. A
13503
flow hood could also be utilized to measure space airflow volumes at the individual outlets. Careful
13504
judgment must be utilized with the flow hood as in some cases an airflow factor must be established for
13505
the flow hood depending on the type of outlet being measured, which affects the air flow patterns and air
13506
velocities being measured. This air flow factor is normally established utilizing a duct traverse,
13507
comparing the traverse airflow measurement to the flow hood measurement.
13508
13509
For a description of proper design and installation considerations to allow for accurate and repeatable
13510
measurement of airflows, reference Strategy 7.2 Continuously Monitor and Control Outdoor Air
13511
Delivery.
13512
13513
Verification
13514
1. Verify proper construction of the space envelope including but not limited to proper use of air
13515
barriers and sealing of all pipe, conduit, ductwork and any other envelope penetrations.
13516
Reference Strategy 2.2 Limit Condensation within the Building Envelope and on Interior
13517
Surfaces.
13518
13519
2. Provide the design information for the space pressurization requirements on the contract
13520
drawings. This should include total airflows into and out of the spaces, required directional
13521
airflow and/or the required pressure differential for the spaces. A summary of the space
13522
pressurization requirements and airflow and pressure values in a tabular format is extremely
13523
helpful.
13524
13525
3. Test, adjust and balance the HVAC system and verify outdoor, exhaust, supply and return
13526
airflows to assure that the spaces are maintaining the proper pressurization relationship. This
13527
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 441
Return to TOP
includes testing the systems in minimum and maximum airflow modes. Reference AABC,
13528
NEBB, ASHRAE, or TABB for standards and procedures required for the testing, adjusting and
13529
balancing of HVAC systems.
13530
13531
4. Perform pressure differential mapping to verify actual pressure relationships. Pressure
13532
differential measurements identify the potential for airflow between spaces. Multiple pressure
13533
readings inside the building can identify pressure differences between the spaces and wall
13534
cavities. It cannot be assumed that a wall cavity has the identical pressure gradient of the space.
13535
If the wall cavity is positively pressurized with infiltration, or leaky ductwork, with reference to
13536
the space, an electrical outlet could let this infiltration containing moisture and contaminants
13537
migrate into the space. These pressure readings should also include the ceiling plenum pressures
13538
to verify that they are positive in pressure with respect to the outdoors. Measurements should be
13539
taken after all HVAC systems have been tested and balanced. If possible the pressure
13540
measurements should be taken at varying system operating conditions (i.e., maximum and
13541
minimum airflows for VAV systems, room fan coil units on and off). Reference Figure 6.4-B in
13542
the Space Layout section above which displays the pressure mapping process (Odom, DuBose,
13543
Scott & Nelson 2005 Mold and Moisture Prevention, Washington, DC: National Council of
13544
Architectural Registration Boards).
13545
13546
5. If proper pressure relationships cannot be obtained utilizing the HVAC systems, then additional
13547
space envelope requirements will need to be addressed.
13548
13549
13550
13551
13552
13553
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 442
Return to TOP
OBJECTIVE 7 - REDUCE CONTAMINANT CONCENTRATIONS THROUGH
13554
VENTILATION AND AIR CLEANING
13555
13556
13557
Specific strategies described in this section are to:
13558
13559
• Strategy 7.1 – Provide Appropriate Outdoor Air Quantities for Each Room or Zone
13560
• Strategy 7.2 – Continuously Monitor and Control Outdoor Air Delivery
13561
• Strategy 7.3 – Distribute Ventilation Air Effectively
13562
• Strategy 7.4 – Provide Particle Filtration and Gas Phase Air Cleaning Consistent with Project IAQ
13563
Objectives
13564
• Strategy 7.5 – Provide Comfort Conditions that Enhance Occupant Satisfaction
13565
13566
13567
13568
See also these closely related Strategies:
13569
13570
• Strategy 1.2 – Select HVAC systems to Improve IAQ and Reduce the Energy Impacts of Ventilation
13571
• Strategy 3.1 – Locate Outside Air Intakes to Minimize Introduction of Contaminants
13572
• Strategy 1.4 – Manage Construction and Renovation to Preserve IAQ
13573
• Objective 8 – Apply More Advanced Ventilation
13574
13575
13576
13577
13578
13579
13580
13581
13582
13583
[REMAINDER OF PAGE LEFT INTENTIALLY BLANK]
13584
13585
13586
13587
13588
13589
13590
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 443
Return to TOP
13591
Strategy 7.1 – Provide Appropriate Outdoor Air Quantities for Each Room or Zone
13592
13593
13594
History
13595
Outdoor air has been provided to indoor rooms for centuries. Initially the outdoor air was makeup air
13596
for fireplaces or to let cool air into rooms during hot weather. Makeup of outdoor air or ventilation
13597
changed with the advent of electricity and the ability to mechanically provide ventilation to buildings
13598
without relying on natural drafts.
13599
13600
Basic Theory
13601
The simplified case is shown in Figure 7.1-B. Where:
13602
V is ventilation rate (volume/time)
13603
C is concentration (mass/volume)
13604
S is generation rate (mass/time)
13605
Ci
Source
Co
13606
Figure 7.1-B
13607
13608
In the room a source emits pollutants at a rate S. Ventilation is provided at rate V.
13609
The resulting concentration Ci is calculated by the following equation:
13610
13611
Ci = S/V
13612
13613
Or for a given target concentration and source emission rate, the ventilation rate is calculated by:
13614
13615
V=S/Ci
13616
13617
This is the case if
13618
1. There is only one source
13619
2. The source strength is known
13620
3. The target concentration is known
13621
4. The air in the room is perfectly mixed (the concentration is the same everywhere in the room)
13622
5. The source generation rate is constant
13623
6. Steady state conditions are reached
13624
7. The concentration outdoors is zero (Co = 0)
13625
13626
Fundamentals of the Ventilation Rate Procedure
13627
In real rooms in buildings
13628
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 444
Return to TOP
1. There are multiple sources
13629
2. For many sources, the source strength is not known
13630
3. For many indoor compounds an appropriate target concentration is not readily available
13631
4. The air in the room is not perfectly mixed
13632
5. The source generation rate varies
13633
6. Some times because of variation, steady state conditions are not approached
13634
7. There is a concentration of pollutants in the outdoor air
13635
13636
Room 1
Room 2
Room 3
Room 4
Breathing Zone
Breathing Zone
Breathing Zone
Breathing Zone
13637
Figure 7.1-C
13638
13639
Because of the lack of information to calculate ventilation rates precisely for all applications in general,
13640
current ASHRAE standard 62 minimum ventilation rates for the ventilation rate procedure were
13641
established by a consensus process considering information from:
13642
• Laboratory tests of sensory perception,
13643
• Scientific literature analyzing field data from surveys of perception and health effects related to
13644
differing ventilation rates, and
13645
• Reports of engineers’ experience with past ventilation rates in “typical” buildings.
13646
13647
On a national basis, current minimum ventilation rates are published in ANSI/ASHRAE 62.1-2007. At
13648
the local level, there are local building codes. One must also evaluate the minimum ventilation required
13649
by local code. Some codes refer to ASHRAE 62 as meeting the code and some codes incorporate the
13650
ventilation rates from ASHRAE 62. Local building codes that specify rates must be followed if rates are
13651
greater than those in ANSI/ASHRAE 62.1-2007.
13652
13653
Occupancy category
13654
Occupancy category accounts for the type of space and the activities are expected in the space. The
13655
factor Ra is intended to provide minimum ventilation for all of the non-people sources in the room
13656
Values of Ra vary from 0.06 to 0.48 cfm per square foot depending on occupancy category. Note that in
13657
rooms 2 and 3 of Figure 7.1-C the furniture quantities do not match. In the office this would include the
13658
furnishings, walls, floors, ceilings, and “typical” office equipment and accessories. It does not matter in
13659
terms of the calculation if the office is densely or sparsely furnished as the standard and the calculations
13660
do not go into that degree of complexity. One should take care in situations that are not typical to
13661
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 445
Return to TOP
determine if there is enough ventilation air. As illustrated above, zone 4 is not “typical” of an office.
13662
The emissions from minor processes and chemical compounds are different from a typical office, so
13663
even if room 4 is in an “office” building, it would be inappropriate to use the equation 6-1 calculation
13664
for “office” occupancy. Further analysis is required.
13665
13666
Boundaries for Zones and Corresponding Area
13667
In design, rooms with similar functions often are grouped together and treated as zones. Occasionally
13668
there are enough differences in conditions in a large room that it is divided into more than one zone. For
13669
the simple one-room case the room is a zone. Some very large rooms with different activities may have
13670
multiple zones. ASHRAE 62.1-2007 defines a ventilation zone as
13671
Zone: one occupied space or several occupied spaces with similar occupancy category (see
13672
Table 6-1), occupant density, zone air distribution effectiveness (see Section 6.2.2.2), and zone
13673
primary airflow (see Section 6.2.5.1) per unit area.
13674
Note: A ventilation zone is not necessarily an independent thermal control zone; however, spaces
13675
that can be combined for load calculations can often be combined into a single zone for
13676
ventilation calculations.
13677
13678
In Figure 7.1-C, rooms 2 and 3 may be combined into one zone.
13679
13680
Quantify occupants and activities
13681
Ventilation is required per person and is quantified by the factor Rp. The maximum number of people
13682
expected to occupy the zone must be determined. In the illustration, the emissions from a person
13683
engaging in heavy activity in room 4 are different from the emissions from people who are more
13684
sedentary (rooms 2 and 3) The values of Rp vary from 5 to 20 cfm per person depending on expected
13685
activity.
13686
13687
Calculate Minimum Ventilation for Each Zone
13688
The minimum ventilation rate for a zone is a function of the type of occupancy, the maximum number of
13689
people expected to occupy the space, and the area of the space. In some countries there are multiple
13690
levels of ventilation specified for different levels of acceptability. In the US only minimum rates are
13691
specified. Different levels of acceptability may be addressed using the IAQ procedure.
13692
13693
From ASHRAE 62.1-2007, Ventilation Rate Procedure: The design outdoor airflow required in the
13694
breathing zone of the occupiable space or spaces in a zone, i.e., the breathing zone outdoor airflow (Vbz),
13695
shall be determined in accordance with Equation 6-1.
13696
13697
Vbz = Rp · Pz + Ra · Az (6-1)
13698
13699
Where:
13700
Az = zone floor area: the net occupiable floor area of the zone m2 (ft2)
13701
Pz = zone population: the largest number of people expected to occupy the zone during typical
13702
usage.
13703
Rp = outdoor airflow rate required per person as determined from Table 6-1
13704
Note: These values are based on adapted occupants.
13705
Ra = outdoor airflow rate required per unit area as determined from Table 6-1
13706
13707
Adjusting Outdoor Air Quantities
13708
13709
Consider Increased Outdoor Air Quantities when Outdoor Air Quality is Good
13710
When outdoor air quality is good (or acceptable as determined by section 4 of ASHRAE 32.1-2007),
13711
increased ventilation may be beneficial for the following reasons:
13712
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 446
Return to TOP
1. Increased ventilation is related to decreased health effects. Rates greater than the current
13713
minimums are associated with decreases in symptoms.61
13714
2. Increased ventilation is correlated with increased productivity in the office and the classroom62
13715
3. Increased ventilation is related to increased acceptability of the air as reported by visitors to a
13716
room.
13717
4. Increased ventilation can save energy during air-side economizer operation depending on
13718
weather conditions.
13719
13720
Temporary Decreases in Outdoor Air Quantities
13721
During short term episodes of poor outdoor air, ventilation can be temporarily decreased using a short
13722
term conditions procedure from 62.1-2007. The design may be based on average conditions over a time
13723
T=3v/Vbz where v is the volume of the space.
13724
13725
If this process is considered, first:
13726
1. Determine outdoor air quality and extent of problem whether time of day, seasonal, or annual.
13727
See Consider Regional/Local Air Quality Issues in Stratagey 1.2 Select HVAC Systems to
13728
Improve IAQ for more information.
13729
2. Provide minimum required filtration. See Strategy 7.4 Provide Particle filtration and Gas
13730
Phase Air Cleaning for more information.
13731
3. Consider providing additional improvements to ventilation air using the IAQ procedure. See
13732
Strategy 8.5 Use the ASHRAE Standard 62.1 IAQ Procedure where Appropriate for more
13733
information.
13734
After considering the information above, if short term reduction in ventilation is warranted, then
13735
determine the allowable operating parameters for reducing introduction of pollutants into the space.
13736
13737
Optional - Calculate Benefits of Additional Ventilation Air
13738
Enhanced design of ventilation for a room requires a more sophisticated analysis. If Vbz cfm of air for a
13739
room is the minimum, it is not clear that 1.3 x Vbz cfm makes the air quality in the room 30% better. In
13740
some cases (such as untreated polluted outdoor air) the air quality in a room ventilated at 1.3 x Vbz may
13741
be worse than the air quality in a room ventilated to Vbz cfm.
13742
13743
Increasing ventilation must also consider the occupancy. There is little to no benefit for increased
13744
ventilation of a seldom used corridor. There may be a benefit to increasing ventilation in a room that is
13745
either occupied for a long time or a room that is occupied by susceptible individuals.
13746
13747
Exposures that relate to adverse health effects and people’s perception of odor are both functions of
13748
concentration. Ventilation is one of many tools used to control or limit concentrations of substances in a
13749
room.
13750
13751
The graph below illustrates the general case of CO2 concentration in a room at steady state conditions.
13752
This shows the relationship of CO2 concentration in ppm versus ventilation rate in cfm/person given
13753
certain assumptions. This theoretical graph is intended to illustrate the relationship between ventilation
13754
and concentration. This graph should not be used for Demand Controlled Ventilation (DCV)
13755
calculations or other purposes. See Strategy 8.2 Use Demand Controlled Ventilation where
13756
Appropriate for more information.
13757
13758
61 Seppanen, O., Fisk, W. J., Mendell, M. J., “Ventilation Rates and Health”, ASHRAE Journal, August 2002
62 Fisk, W. J. “How IEQ affects Health, Productivity”, ASHRAE Journal, May 2002
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 447
Return to TOP
Ventilation vs C O2 C oncentration
0
2000
4000
6000
8000
10000
12000
0
5
10
15
20
25
30
35
40
45
Ventilation rate (cfm/person)
C oncentration ppm
13759
Figure 7.1-D
13760
13761
At 1 cfm per person, the CO2 concentration is 10400 ppm. This exceeds the 8-hour time weighted
13762
average OSHA TLV of 5000 ppm for an occupied workspace. To significantly reduce the concentration
13763
requires doubling the cfm to 2 cfm per person. This would reduce the concentration to 5400 ppm. At 5
13764
cfm per person under the assumed conditions the CO2 concentration will be 2400 ppm (roughly half of
13765
the TLV). Increasing the ventilation to 10 cfm per person reduces the concentration to 1400 and
13766
doubling that to 20 cfm per person reduces it to 900 ppm. Doubling the cfm from 20 to 40 decreases the
13767
concentration from 900 to 650 ppm. There are diminishing returns from doubling ventilation. From 1
13768
to 2 cfm per person changes the concentration by 5000 ppm while changing from 20 to 40 cfm per
13769
person only changes the concentration by 350 ppm,
13770
13771
Further doubling of ventilation will slowly reduce the concentration toward 400 ppm which is the
13772
assumed outdoor concentration in this example. No amount of ventilation can reduce the concentration
13773
below that which is introduced in the outdoor air.
13774
13775
Conclusions
13776
1. Doubling ventilation will have different effects on total concentration depending on the starting
13777
point on the ventilation concentration curve. The answer to the question “Will doubling
13778
ventilation reduce the CO2 concentration by 500 ppm?” is it depends.
13779
2. As one approaches the concentration in the outdoor air, increasing ventilation is ineffective.
13780
13781
There is a process to determine the effect of changing ventilation on concentration and it is the IAQ
13782
Procedure. See Strategy 8.5 Use the ASHRAE Standard 62.1 IAQ Procedure where Appropriate
13783
for more information.
13784
13785
Enhanced ventilation design must include
13786
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 448
Return to TOP
• An evaluation of occupancy
13787
• An evaluation of benefits of increased ventilation
13788
• Quantification of the reduction in concentration using the IAQ method
13789
13790
Documentation of the occupancy evaluation, statement of benefits of increased ventilation, and
13791
quantification of the reduction in concentration of contaminants of concern using the IAQ method is
13792
enhanced ventilation design.
13793
13794
References:
13795
ASHRAE 62.1-2007
13796
13797
Final Report: ASHRAE URP 1443
13798
13799
EN 13779:2004
13800
13801
13802
13803
13804
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 449
Return to TOP
13805
Strategy 7.2 – Continuously Monitor and Control Outdoor Air Delivery
13806
13807
13808
Overview
13809
Accurate monitor and control of outdoor air intake at the air handler is an important aspect of providing
13810
the correct amount of outdoor air to a building. It has been a long practice for designers to design fixed
13811
minimum outdoor air dampers. A study of 100 existing US buildings (BASE) found that in the majority
13812
(88%) of the buildings minimum outdoor airflow was based on fixed minimum outdoor air dampers
13813
without any continuous monitoring (Persily and Gorfain, 2004; Persily et al., 2005). The same study
13814
found that in the majority of the buildings without continuous monitoring and control, over-ventilation
13815
based on existing occupancy was prevalent. It should be noted that a key reason for the over-ventilation
13816
was that the actual occupancy was lower than the design value rather than poor control of outdoor air
13817
flow.
13818
13819
It is estimated that the current amount of energy for ventilating US buildings could be reduced by as
13820
much as 30%63 if the average minimum outdoor rate is reduced to meet the current standards (Fisk et al.,
13821
2005).
13822
13823
Accurate monitor of outdoor air intake rates at the air handler is difficult. However, the potential for
13824
wasted energy with over-ventilation and the risk of poor indoor air quality with under-ventilation justify
13825
considerably increased attention to address this problem.
13826
13827
It should also be noted that continuous monitoring of the outdoor rates at the air handler does not
13828
guarantee that the proper amount of ventilation is delivered locally within the building. Poor air mixing,
13829
especially in large buildings, can result in parts of a building receiving less than the minimum amount of
13830
required ventilation. In addition, infiltration especially in buildings with leaky envelopes could provide
13831
large local variations in ventilation rates.
13832
13833
Accurate measurements of airflows in ducts require careful design, proper commissioning, and ongoing
13834
calibration. Under carefully controlled laboratory conditions, commercially-available airflow sensors
13835
are very accurate. However, laboratory conditions and accuracies cannot be duplicated in the field .
13836
13837
Straight Ducts
13838
The most accurate location to measure airflows is in straight duct runs. Unfortunately, this presents a
13839
challenge to the designer because in most HVAC systems there is insufficient straight duct length due to
13840
space and architectural constraints. ASHRAE (2005) recommends that measuring points be located at
13841
least ~7.5 hydraulic diameters downstream and ~3 hydraulic diameters upstream from any disturbance.
13842
Duct diameter can be calculated based on the following equation:
13843
13844
Dh = 4A/P
13845
13846
Where:
13847
Dh: hydraulic diameter, in
13848
A: duct cross sectional area, in2
13849
P: duct wetted perimeter, in
13850
13851
ASHRAE Fundamentals, Chapter 35, Table 2 lists hydraulic equivalents for rectangular ducts.
13852
13853
63 This is a first order estimate of savings potential
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 450
Return to TOP
Straightening vanes installed 1.5 duct diameters upstream of measurement points help improve
13854
measurement precision.
13855
13856
HVAC Systems with Economizers
13857
In HVAC systems with outdoor air ductwork sized for use with an economizer cycle, accurate
13858
measurement of minimum outside airflow rates is difficult due to low airflow velocities. Therefore, it is
13859
recommended that the maximum outdoor air intake be separated from the minimum outdoor air intake
13860
and that continuous airflow measurement at the minimum outdoor air intake be provided (ASHRAE RP
13861
980, 1999). Researchers have reported that in such systems measurement errors of “reasonably accurate
13862
measurements” are between 10 and 30% (Fisk et al., 2005). Although accuracies better than 10% to 30%
13863
can be achieved with some technologies and careful placement conditions (Dougan, 2003), they are not
13864
practical in most real-world applications. A long term study of five recently constructed office buildings
13865
with continuous monitoring and control of the outdoor air flow rates reported that in 9 of the 16
13866
measurement scenarios the average airflow rates measured in each building’s HVAC systems were
13867
within 30% of the design values; however, in 7 of the 16 cases the measured air change rates ranged
13868
from 35 to 110% above their design values, indicating that despite the fact that the HVAC systems were
13869
locked in their minimum outdoor airflow setting, these buildings were ventilated above the design
13870
airflow rates under certain conditions (Alevantis et al. 2006).
13871
13872
For built up systems with economizers, a dedicated outdoor air intake fan is recommended along with a
13873
