separate intake for minimum outdoor air. Large packaged HVAC systems with air economizers can be
13874
ordered with separate intake for minimum outdoor air as well as with monitoring and control of the
13875
minimum outdoor air.
13876
13877
Small Packaged HVAC Systems
13878
Small packaged units typically do not have continuous measurement of outdoor airflows. This suggests
13879
the need for even greater attention to confirmation of the delivery of design air flow rates through
13880
balancing, commissioning, and periodic re-commissioning.
13881
13882
It is recommended that for small packaged HVAC systems, straight runs of ductwork are designed and
13883
installed both in supply and return airstreams thus allowing accurate measurements of airflows in these
13884
airstreams. Assuming that there is no exhaust (or relief) in the HVAC system, outdoor airflows can then
13885
be estimated by deducting the return airflow rate from the supply airflow rate. If possible, ductwork
13886
should be added on the unit’s outdoor air intake to allow for a traverse of outdoor air.
13887
13888
Placement of Airflow Sensors
13889
Design velocities of outdoor air intake louvers are low in order to minimize entrainment of rain and
13890
snow. Fisk et al. (2005) reported that maximum air velocities within the “free area” of these louvers is
13891
between 700 and 2500 ft/min64. For an HVAC system with economizer and a single outdoor air intake,
13892
velocities at minimum outdoor air conditions are about 20% of the maximums, i.e., 140 to 500 ft/min at
13893
the free area of the louver. Because the cross sectional area inside the louver is less than the nominal
13894
face of the louver, velocities upstream and downstream of the louver could be 30 to 50% of the
13895
velocities in the free area of the louver. The resulting low velocities (and velocity pressures in the
13896
thousands of in H2O) are too low to measure accurately in the field with pressure-based velocity sensors
13897
and challenging for some types of electronic velocity sensors. Furthermore, the air velocity profile
13898
between outdoor air louvers and controlling dampers can be spatially non-uniform and in some cases
13899
large eddies can develop (see Figure 7.2-B).
13900
13901
64 Upper range of most common louvers is about 1250 fpm; however in areas with strong wind-driven rain, louvers with
higher maximum velocities are specified and installed
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 451
Return to TOP
Research has shown that in general better accuracies can be expected when sensors are placed in the
13902
following locations: (a) between the fixed louver blades where the air speeds are more uniform
13903
compared to air speeds downstream of the louvers; or (b) downstream of the louvers and upstream of the
13904
dampers provided that an airflow straightening device is installed between the louvers and the airflow or
13905
pressure sensors as shown in Figure 7.2-C (Fisk et al., 2005a). In order to avoid airflow backwards
13906
through the outdoor air damper during fully or substantially open conditions, a minimum pressure
13907
difference of 0.04 in. H2O should be maintained across the outdoor air dampers.
13908
13909
13910
13911
Figure 7.2-B Illustration of airflow patters at the outdoor air intake (Fisk et al., 2005b)
13912
Source: ASHRAE Transactions, Volume 111, pages 456-463, 2005
13913
13914
13915
13916
Figure 7.2-C Airflow measurement consisting of an airflow straightener
13917
and airflow monitoring blades (Fisk et al., 2005a)
13918
Source: ASHRAE Transactions, Volume 111, pages 443-455, 2005
13919
13920
Accuracy and Calibration of Airflow Sensors
13921
Regardless of whether or not airflow sensors are factory or field-installed, accuracy of these sensors
13922
must be verified with appropriately calibrated equipment at start-up and during occupancy on regular
13923
time intervals.
13924
13925
It is very important that SMACNA and ASHRAE procedures are implemented during field-based
13926
calibration and checking of measurement systems. These procedures are described in SMACNA’s
13927
HVAC Systems (SMACNA, 2002) and TAB Procedural Guide (SMACNA, 2003) as well as in Chapter
13928
14 of the ASHRAE (2005) Fundamentals
13929
13930
Table 1 lists the characteristics of the various airflow measurement methods. The accuracies reported in
13931
Table 1 were developed under controlled laboratory conditions. Actual accuracies in measurement of
13932
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 452
Return to TOP
outdoor airflow rates can be far less because air speeds and directions are highly variable and not easily
13933
predicted.
13934
13935
As can be seen from Table 1, most airflow sensors have limitations on the airflow ranges that can be
13936
applied. A common mistake is measurement of low airflows using pressure based (i.e., Pitot) sensors.
13937
Sensor manufacturers’ specifications and field conditions must be considered before a sensor is selected.
13938
13939
Airflow sensors should be capable of measuring flow within an accuracy of ±15% of the minimum
13940
outdoor airflow rate.
13941
13942
Indirect Methods of Measuring Minimum Outdoor Airflows
13943
(not recommend but included for informational purposes only)
13944
Indirect methods for measuring minimum outdoor airflows include plenum pressure control and CO2
13945
concentration balance (ASHRAE RP 980, 19xx).
13946
13947
The plenum pressure control strategy involves measuring and maintaining a constant pressure drop
13948
across a fixed orifice, such as the outdoor air damper and louver. This requires that a dedicated
13949
minimum outdoor air damper be used to create a fixed orifice. For systems with economizers, using a
13950
minimum damper position to create the fixed orifice is usually not accurate due to lack of repeatability
13951
of the damper assembly (damper, actuator, and linkage). Therefore, a separate duct for the minimum
13952
outdoor air is recommended. The pressure drop must be large enough so it can be accurately measured
13953
but not large enough to create a significant energy penalty. Proper selection of the differential pressure
13954
transmitter used to measure the pressure drop across the outside air damper is essential.
13955
13956
The CO2 method uses the concentrations of the outdoor air, return air, and supply air to determine the
13957
percent of outdoor airflow in the supply air using the following equation (Bearg, 1993):
13958
13959
% OA= (CO2-RA – CO2-MA)/(CO2-RA – CO2-OA) x 100
13960
13961
Where:
13962
% OA = percent of outdoor air in supply stream
13963
CO2-RA = CO2 concentration in return air stream
13964
CO2-MA = CO2 concentration in mixed air stream
13965
CO2-OA = CO2 concentration in outdoor air stream
13966
13967
The accuracy of this method is reduced as the difference in the CO2 concentrations of the return and
13968
outdoor air becomes small and as the outdoor air becomes a smaller fraction of the supply airflow.
13969
Furthermore, accurate measurement of the supply air concentration is difficult due to poor air mixing
13970
downstream of the mixing chamber. Repeatability can be a significant source of error when multiple
13971
CO2 sensors are used. Using a central measuring device with extraction of the sample by tubing
13972
connected to the sampling point reduces the potential for error that can occur when independent sensors
13973
are used in each location.
13974
13975
Temperature, instead of CO2, can also be used to estimate the amount of outdoor airflow. ASHRAE
13976
(2007) recommends that the temperature difference between outside and return airstreams be greater
13977
than 20 °F. An equation similar to the one shown above for CO2 can be used to calculate percent of
13978
outdoor air in the supply airstream. The potential sources of error described above for estimating
13979
percent of outdoor air based on CO2 concentrations are also applicable for temperature.
13980
13981
% OA= (tRA – tMA)/(tRA – tOA) x 100
13982
13983
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 453
Return to TOP
Where:
13984
% OA = percent of outdoor air in supply stream
13985
tRA = air temperature of the return air stream
13986
tMA = air temperature of the mixed air stream (before any conditioning)
13987
tOA = air temperature of the outdoor air stream
13988
13989
Due to the accuracy problems associated with the indirect methods discussed above, none is
13990
recommended. Instead, direct methods are encouraged.
13991
13992
Design Issues: Commissioning, Operation and maintenance
13993
The designer needs to make provisions for measurement and verification of the minimum outdoor
13994
airflows during the initial commissioning as well during the ongoing commissioning of a building (see
13995
Table 2 for Section 8.4.1.8 of ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007). Such provisions include easy access to
13996
the airflow sensors, software that can detect sensor (e.g. airflow) and equipment (damper motor)
13997
malfunctions, etc. In addition, the design criteria and occupancy assumptions need to be listed in a clear
13998
format in the operations and maintenance manual. The building maintenance staff should be encouraged
13999
to adjust the minimum amount of outdoor air on regular intervals (e.g., annually) based on actual
14000
maximum occupancy data. Adjustment of the minimum airflows should be made easier by the provision
14001
of convenient access and effective adjustment mechanisms; also, it is very helpful if the occupancy data
14002
is displayed on the BMS and can be modified.
14003
14004
14005

Strategy 7.3 – Distribute Ventilation Air Effectively
14101
14102
14103
Zone Air Distribution Effectiveness
14104
Voz = Vbz/Ez (6-2)
14105
14106
Where
14107
Voz is the quantity of ventilation air delivered to the occupied zone (cfm)
14108
Vbz is the quantity of ventilation air delivered to the breathing zone (cfm)
14109
Ez is zone air distribution effectiveness
14110
14111
Efficient Distribution
14112
Ez ranges from 1.0 to 1.2
14113
14114
• Ceiling supply of cool air
14115
• Ceiling supply of warm air and floor return
14116
• Ceiling supply of warm air less than 15°F (8°C) above space temperature and ceiling return provided
14117
that the 150 fpm (0.8 m/s) supply air jet reaches to within 4.5 ft (1.4 m) of floor level.
14118
• Floor supply of cool air and ceiling return provided that the 150 fpm (0.8 m/s) supply jet reaches 4.5
14119
ft (1.4 m) or more above the floor
14120
• Floor supply of cool air and ceiling return, provided low- velocity displacement ventilation achieves
14121
unidirectional flow and thermal stratification
14122
• Floor supply of warm air and floor return
14123
14124
Adequate Distribution
14125
Ez = 0.8.
14126
14127
• Ceiling supply of warm air 15F or more above space temperature and ceiling return
14128
• Makeup supply drawn in on the opposite side of the room from the exhaust and/or return
14129
14130
Inefficient Distribution
14131
Ez < 0.8
14132
14133
• Floor supply of warm air and ceiling return
14134
• Makeup supply drawn in near to the exhaust and/or return location
14135
14136
Table 7.3-A Zone Air Distribution Effectiveness
14137
Air Distribution Configuration
Ez
Ceiling supply of cool air
1.0
Ceiling supply of warm air and floor return
1.0
Ceiling supply of warm air 15°F (8°C) or more above space temperature and ceiling return.
0.8
Ceiling supply of warm air less than 15°F (8°C) above space temperature and ceiling return provided that the 150
fpm (0.8 m/s) supply air jet reaches to within 4.5 ft (1.4 m) of floor level. Note: For lower velocity supply air, Ez=
0.8.
1.0
Floor supply of cool air and ceiling return provided that the 150 fpm (0.8 m/s) supply jet reaches 4.5 ft (1.4 m) or
more above the floor. Note: Most underfloor air distribution systems comply with this proviso.
1.0
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 461
Return to TOP
Air Distribution Configuration
Ez
Floor supply of cool air and ceiling return, provided low- velocity displacement ventilation achieves
unidirectional flow and thermal stratification
1.2
Floor supply of warm air and floor return
1.0
Floor supply of warm air and ceiling return
0.7
Makeup supply drawn in on the opposite side of the room from the exhaust and/or return
0.8
Makeup supply drawn in near to the exhaust and/or return location
0.5
1. “Cool air” is air cooler than space temperature.
2. “Warm air” is air warmer than space temperature.
3. “Ceiling” includes any point above the breathing zone.
4. “Floor” includes any point below the breathing zone.
5. As an alternative to using the above values, Ez may be regarded as equal to air change effectiveness determined in accordance with
ASHRAE Standard 12916for all air distribution configurations except unidirectional flow.
Source: ASHRAE 62.1-2007
14138
14139
Ducted or Non-ducted?
14140
Ducted
14141
With a ducted air system, the air is distributed directly to the zone. Ducts need to be properly
14142
constructed and sealed. Provisions need to be made in design for proper balancing. Once a properly
14143
designed and constructed ducted system is balanced, one is assured that the air is efficiently delivered.
14144
14145
Non Ducted
14146
Non Ducted – Plenum Dump: In the past, some systems were designed to introduce air into a plenum
14147
under the assumption that the air would somehow mix and be distributed into the building. Such
14148
“dumping” systems no longer meet current standards. Current research demonstrates that air does not
14149
mix or distribute using this strategy. 65
14150
14151
Non Ducted – Designed: There are special cases with designed non-ducted systems such as underfloor
14152
air distribution (UFAD) and dual plenum systems. These systems require special attention to
14153
maintaining the integrity of the plenum. Many recently installed systems have suffered from leaks. The
14154
area that must be sealed is usually far greater than that of a ducted system.
14155
14156
Many systems have also suffered from inadequate airflow at the furthest distances from supply air
14157
introduction. In these cases, increasing fan speeds/sizes to provide adequate air compounds problems by
14158
increasing energy usage, increasing the volume of leaks, increasing system noise, and increasing noise at
14159
nearby diffusers because of increased pressure.
14160
14161
Provisions for proper balancing must be made in the design. Because the pathways are numerous and
14162
sometimes unknown at the beginning of design, techniques such as pressure regain that work on duct
14163
designs, may not work for non-ducted systems.
14164
14165
14166
14167
65 “A Study of Multiple Space Effects on Ventilation System Efficiency in Standard 62.1 – 2004 and Experimental Validation
of the Multiple Spaces Equation”
Final Report for ASHRAE Research Project 1276, David P. Yuill, Grenville K. Yuill, Andrew H. Coward
March 15, 2007, ASHRAE, Atlanta, GA
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 462
Return to TOP
14168
Strategy 7.4 – Provide Particle Filtration and Gas Phase Air Cleaning Consistent with
14169
Project IAQ Objectives
14170
14171
This discussion is not intended to inform the reader about the fundamentals (such as mechanics, physics,
14172
and chemistry) of filtration and gaseous air cleaning media, as these subjects are well covered in
14173
appropriate ASHRAE Fundamentals Chapters (Reference noted here). The NAFA Air Filtration Guide
14174
(Reference noted here) also provides an excellent resource for this discussion. Instead, this discussion is
14175
intended to provide the designer with a basic working understanding of extraction efficiency, testing
14176
methods, equipment selection, life cycle costing, and application of FAC. This will result in a fuller
14177
understanding of the filtration and air cleaning (FAC) equipment evaluation, selection, and application
14178
process.
14179
14180
FAC Equipment Selection and Specification Guidance
14181
The following table provides general selection guidance that combines and balances the project need or
14182
strategy with appropriate extraction efficiency requirements, contaminants of concern, and life cycle
14183
cost/value. The details of each design strategy are detailed in the section following the chart. The
14184
general performance characteristics of FAC equipment are incremental providing the designer with
14185
flexibility of selection dependent upon the constraints of space, pressure drop, and cost. Thus, the
14186
recommendations provide a range of selection options dependent upon varying degrees of need and
14187
expectation. The resulting outcomes can be attained incrementally without the necessity of resorting to
14188
"clean room" performance levels when they are unwarranted. The Table denotes outcomes that range
14189
from "Good," which represents an enhancement well beyond the minimum standard/code to "Best" that
14190
represents superior performance, benefit, and/or value for that specific strategy.
14191
14192
General Selection Guidelines
14193
Table 7.4-A. General Selection Guidance
14194
Design Strategy/
Problem or Situation
Recommendation/
Solution Range
Outcome/Benefit
3.6.1.1. Keep heat
exchange and
distribution system
surfaces cleaner
Apply MERV 8-13
8=Good
13=Best value
Avoids excessive coil fouling; maintains heat
exchange efficiency; reduces energy losses; lowers
cleaning and maintenance costs; maintains system
performance; lessens opportunity for mold growth by
reducing nutrient and moisture retention
3.6.1.2. Keep
conditioned space
cleaner
Apply MERV 8-13
8=Good
13=Best value
Reduces nuisance dust; lowers housekeeping cost
3.6.1.3. Control of viable
or pathogenic particles
Apply MERV 14-16
14=Good
16=Best
Lowers occupant exposure to airborne pathogens;
decreases negative health effects; decreases
absenteeism; decreases related productivity cost
3.6.1.4. Treatment of
excessively polluted
outdoor air
Apply MERV 11-14
11=Good
14-Best
Apply Gas Phase ME
Assures code/standard-of-care compliance; lowers
occupant exposure to external sources of particles,
odors, and irritants; lowers generation of cross-
contamination from building exhausts; reduces
generation of undesirable products of chemical
reaction from ozone and indoor chemicals
3.6.1.5. Control of
specific contaminants of
concern, including those
associated with criminal
intent
Apply MERV 13-17
13=Good
17=Best (HEPA)
Apply HE-HEGA
HE=Good
HEGA=Best
Reduces risk of highly detrimental or criminal
incidents of particulate or gas phase contamination to
processes, products, people, or their related activities
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 463
Return to TOP
Design Strategy/
Problem or Situation
Recommendation/
Solution Range
Outcome/Benefit
3.6.1.6. Reduce outdoor
air ventilation rates using
the IAQ Procedure from
Standard 62.1
Apply MERV 11-13
11=Good
13=Good
Apply ME-HE
ME=Good
HE=Best
Reduces latent load from outdoor air; reduces
contaminant load from polluted outdoor air; reduces
HVAC load and capacity;; reduces capital cost;
reduces energy consumption and operating cost
14195
The terms and acronyms employed in this table are described or discussed more fully in later detailed
14196
discussion of each of the strategies, but cursory definitions are: MERV=Minimum Efficiency Reporting
14197
Value; HEPA=High Efficiency Particulate Arrestor; ME=Medium Efficiency range partial bypass gas
14198
phase air cleaner; HE=High Efficiency solid bed gas phase air cleaner; HEGA=High Efficiency Gas
14199
Adsorber solid bed gas phase air cleaner with specialized war gas carbon media.
14200
14201
Equipment Selection Discussion by Design Strategy
14202
A full range of particulate and gas phase filters is available from a wide selection of manufacturers. The
14203
filters vary widely in frame methodology and quality; seal mechanisms; filter/sorption media; cartridge
14204
depth and size; loading characteristics and capacity; surface area configurations; airflow and pressure
14205
drop; and cost. The efficiency or removal performance represents only one of these final selection
14206
criteria. Thus, all the other factors listed below that influence the overall performance and total value of
14207
the FAC system should be included in the selection and specification process.
14208
14209
The application of filtration and air cleaning is a balance of control and extraction requirements; the
14210
physical/mechanical limitations of the air handling system; characteristics of the contaminants of
14211
concern; and the features of the FAC equipment. The higher the efficiency requirements, generally, the
14212
greater the space, energy, operating and maintenance cost requirements. Because of the latter constraints,
14213
the final selection of equipment should go beyond removal efficiency to include; capacity and life cycle,
14214
maintenance requirements, and life-long pressure drop factors (See section on Life Cycle Optimization-
14215
Link). Because of these factors, life-cycle cost, including energy requirements, should drive the selection
14216
rather than first cost. The following discussion provides further details and rationale for the selection of
14217
specific extraction efficiencies that are outlined in Table XX General Selection Guidance summary
14218
(LINK). In the case of multiple objectives or strategies, the more stringent selection will prevail and
14219
satisfy the lesser requirements.
14220
14221
Particulate Filter Selection Recommendations: 1,2--MERV 8-13: These design targets are important
14222
areas because they address advanced air quality application to a broad segment of indoor environments.
14223
Both anecdotal and applied field research6,7 indicates that the minimum filtration requirement of
14224
Standard 62-2007 (MERV 6) does not keep coils or systems sufficiently clean to avoid particulate
14225
accumulation over time, even though the MERV 6 is a substantial improvement over the previously used
14226
fiberglass disposable panel filters. The reason that the MERV 6 is not adequate to keep the system clean
14227
is that air handler and distribution system contamination comes mainly from fine particulate matter
14228
contained in polluted outdoor air. Figure NX illustrates the difference between mass and particle counts.
14229
Although the large particles in outdoor air are the large segment of the airborne mass (therefore EPA
14230
PM-10 data), it is the small sub-micron sized particles that dominate, consisting of over 98% of the
14231
count. These superfine particles are very small and difficult to filter out. They are the cause of the black
14232
soot-like smudging accumulation on wet surfaces and air pathway surfaces.
14233
14234
14235
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 464
Return to TOP
14236
Figure 7.4-B Particle Size Distribution in the Atmosphere
14237
(Source: Figure 1.4 from NAFA Manual)
14238
14239
The optimal selection for these target areas is MERV 137 extended media filters applied in gasketed
14240
front loading filter retainers based on field research (Burroughs X). This provides an optimal balance of
14241
efficiency, capacity, pressure drop, and both first and operating cost. When space or pressure drop limits
14242
prevail and restrict the selection, the lower MERV levels of 8-12 are available in 2" and 4" filters that
14243
can be accommodated by most unitary equipment with integral filter racks. These are substantial
14244
improvements over the minimal MERV 6 with only marginal increase in cost or pressure drop. Cleaner
14245
HVAC systems are critical to sustained air quality because particulate accumulation yields nutrient
14246
build-up and moisture retention that enables mold growth in the air handler and distribution system. This
14247
same MERV 13 efficiency level provides adequate extraction to generally improve space cleanliness
14248
and to support the IAQ Procedure.
14249
14250
14251
Photo courtesy of The Filtration Group
14252
Figure 7.4-C Typical Extended Media Pocket Filter of MERV 13 Efficiency
14253
14254
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 465
Return to TOP
Particulate Filter Design Strategy Recommendation 4,5,6,7--MERV 13-17: Because of their relatively
14255
small size (0.3 to 1 microns) and higher related exposure risk, the control of viable, pathogenic, or
14256
respirable particles requires higher efficiency control, greater than MERV 13. Dependent upon virility,
14257
concentration, and dosage limitation, MERV 14 is normally applied. However, up to MERV 16 level
14258
may be required, though this level is normally applied in healthcare and high-risk settings. The MERV
14259
15 and 16 levels offer very high efficiencies at the sub-micron size range and yet operate with
14260
acceptable velocities and pressure drops. In these higher MERV levels, seal and gasketing, as well as
14261
special retainer systems, are extremely critical to successful performance. However, this design selection
14262
area is highly particle size dependent and the final MERV selection should be driven by the target
14263
particle size of concern and the reduction targets required.
14264
14265
The designer should also be aware that the outdoor air is a primary source of smaller respirable sized
14266
particles (less than 2.5 micron), primarily because of condensation nuclei from internal combustion
14267
engines, chemical reaction, or phase change (evaporation of aerosols). The range of commercial filters
14268
through MERV 16 will also accommodate for most accidental and naturally occurring contaminants.
14269
However, the heightened risk of criminal or terrorist induced threats may require HEPA (High
14270
Efficiency Particulate Arrestor is MERV 17 having a 99%+ efficiency @ 0.3 micron particles) for
14271
sustained control and ultimate life safety protection. When progressing into the HEPA level of filtration,
14272
an entirely different class of requirements are imposed, including reduced airflow (250 fpm approach
14273
velocity), increased pressure drop (up to 3 iwg at termination), increased seal requirement, increased
14274
monitoring, and increased footprint space for access and service. These equate to increased first cost,
14275
increased service cost, and increased energy requirements.
14276
14277
For whatever reason, when the higher end of the MERV spectrum is applied, the IAQ Procedure should
14278
be considered because of the inherent energy savings potential and since the investment in filtration and
14279
related operating capacity is already funded from other motivation. (LINK See Section XXX Using the
14280
Indoor Air Quality Procedure to…..)
14281
14282
(Caution: At this higher MERV level, both pressure drop and life cycle can vary dramatically within a
14283
MERV class. This variance between filter styles depends upon surface area, media selection, airflow
14284
ratings, and filter configuration. The designer should carefully compare, select, and specify the
14285
preferable cartridge configuration in addition to the desired MERV level.)
14286
14287
14288

14290
14291
14292
Photo courtesy of The Filtration Group
14293
Figure 7.4-D Typical High-capacity HEPA Cartridges
14294
14295
Gas Phase Air Cleaner Selection Design Recommendations: 4,5,6—ME: These general objectives can
14296
be fulfilled by the Medium Efficiency range of gas phase filters. Dependent upon the air cleaner
14297
selection, MERV particulate control requirements may also be fulfilled in a single filter stage using a
14298
common retainer. Most gas phase air cleaners in this efficiency range are pleated media filters (from 2 to
14299
4 inches in depth) that can be applied wherever pleated particulate filters are applied. Deeper versions
14300
(12" in depth) require appropriate tracking, access depth and/or special filter retainer banks or side load
14301
units similar to extended media particulate cartridges. In this gas phase version, the sorption media is
14302
imbedded within a fiber matrix that retains the media particles without binders or other techniques that
14303
negate sorption capacity. Because of the fabric component, filter modules exhibit MERV 6-12
14304
particulate filtration capability and some filters carry MERV designations. There are various filtration
14305
sorption media options available in this version, such as both carbon or alumina/potassium
14306
permanganate, enabling the designer to respond to varying requirements. However, this lighter duty
14307
filter in normally applied for light odors loads using the carbon component. In this setting, the ME type
14308
cartridge is serviced and changed out on a timed routine. This is because the limited sorbent content
14309
combined with the particulate arrestance and accumulation yields limited life cycle, usually 3-4 months
14310
in normal office space consistent with the change cycle of the particulate control component. One
14311
particularly useful application of the ME gas phase cartridge is the treatment of outdoor air
14312
contaminated with ozone in non-attainment areas.
14313
14314
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 467
Return to TOP
14315
Photo courtesy of The Filtration Group
14316
Figure 7.4-E Typical Medium Efficiency light duty gaseous cartridges
14317
14318
Gas Phase Air Cleaner Design Recommendation 5--HE-HEGA (High Efficiency Gas Absorber): When
14319
dealing with critical gas phase contaminants of concern, the designer must be cognizant of the full
14320
nature of the contaminant, including identity and chemical properties; concentration; and targeted
14321
control attainment levels. Molecular control is relatively "high-tech" and requires knowledgeable
14322
selection of the appropriate sorbent, HEGA cartridge, and retainer system. These High Efficiency air
14323
cleaner systems require higher levels of pressure drop capacity, space for installation and service,
14324
accommodation for weight, and rigorous maintenance attention. They must also be monitored rigorously
14325
using either gas phase monitoring equipment, testing coupons, or sorbent sampling for factory analysis.
14326
14327
The HE (High Efficiency) employs solid media beds or trays varying from 3/8" to 3" in depth that are
14328
filled with pelletized sorption media. These containers are then configured into an extended V-cell that
14329
both lowers the pressure drop and controls the velocity of the air through the media bed. Much higher
14330
single pass efficiencies and longer service life are attained by these solid bed gaseous filters as compared
14331
to the ME light duty version. This is because of the total weight of sorbent content as well as the longer
14332
dwell time that is engineering into the cartridge. The designer can also select the appropriate sorption
14333
media dependent upon the requirements and properties of the contaminants of concern. Specific sorbents
14334
can be selected for specific chemical control properties, such as carbon for high molecular weight VOC;
14335
alumina/KmO4 for acid gasses and formaldehyde; and zeolite molecular sieves for ammonia. Physical
14336
blends of sorbent pellets can also be employed for general mixed airstreams, like occupancy odors.
14337
14338
The HEGA gas phase filter cartridge is designed to maximize single pass efficiency and, minimize
14339
breakthrough, thus providing optimal control of hazardous chemicals with life threatening risk. The unit
14340
employs deeper media beds, longer dwell times, greater media content, and specialized media treated
14341
selected to treat specific known toxic gasses. It is applied in commercial buildings that are considered to
14342
be vulnerable to criminal or terrorist acts or in commercial space where highly toxic airborne
14343
contaminants are a health threat to occupants. This area of application is a specialized technical "art-
14344
form" and requires the designer to seek experience and dedicated expertise to properly select, design,
14345
and apply HEGA systems.
14346
14347
Service life of gas phase filters is highly dependent upon contaminate concentration and operating
14348
exposure time. Unlike particulate filters, their life cycle cannot be monitored in situ or real time, unless
14349
by specialized chemical sensing equipment that is not currently practical for most commercial
14350
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 468
Return to TOP
applications. However, if the contaminant(s) of concern are odorous or irritating, then recurring
14351
occupant complaints becomes a reliable indicator of service life termination. Test coupons can be
14352
installed concurrently in the airstream that can provide useful guidance when analyzed by the equipment
14353
manufacturer. In the case of bulk media, especially alumina/KMnO4, small bulk samples can be
14354
periodically withdrawn for analysis by the manufacturer to provide indication of remaining service life.
14355
14356
14357
14358
Photo courtesy of The Filtration Group
14359
Figure 7.4-F Typical High Capacity HEPA cartridge
14360
14361
Air Capture and Seal
14362
A number of factors influence the performance, efficiency, and overall effectiveness of the filtration and
14363
air cleaning (FAC) system. The most significant and often overwhelming factor is air capture, or
14364
conversely expressed--air by-pass. For the air filtration or extraction system to perform, the air must
14365
pass through the filter media. Thus, the first and most important step in attaining properly filtered air is
14366
control of air capture and bypass. By-pass can occur in multiple ways:
14367
14368
• Through pressure imbalance and envelope flaws, air can enter the conditioned space through
14369
infiltration, bypassing the entire air handling system. This is also an issue regarding moisture
14370
control and is discussed more fully in Section "n(link)regarding negative building pressures and
14371
infiltration.
14372
• Air can by-pass the filter section in the air handler because of openings in the air handling
14373
system housings, or flawed, inadequate, or failed seals and gaskets in the doors, access panels,
14374
and fabrication seams.
14375
• Air can by-pass the filter cartridge because of flawed seal within the retainer/tracking system
14376
• Air can by-pass the filter around the frame of the filter because of lacking or inadequate and/or
14377
failed gasketing and seal between filters, between the filter and the retainer, between the filter
14378
and the access door, or between the filter and the access tracking.
14379
14380
Recent research activities (field research1, modeling2, and in-duct laboratory measurement3) have
14381
demonstrated that even small visual gaps on a single plane of the filter can result in significant loss of
14382
efficiency. In field evaluation, Burroughs1 demonstrated that adding 1/8" neoprene gasketing between
14383
the mating vertical header frames of MERV13 (link to 52.2 discussion) bag filters mounted in side load
14384
tracking increased measured efficiency by 17% at 1 micron particle size (see Table N). Seigle2 modeled
14385
the bypass phenomenon based upon the crack infiltration formula in ASHRAE Fundamentals. In
14386
controlled laboratory research, Kosar, et al3 demonstrated similar impact of bypass measured in a
14387
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 469
Return to TOP
Standard 52.2 test rig. This amount of by-pass can equate to the effective loss of two to three MERV
14388
levels of efficiency. Thus, to optimize the performance of any enhanced filtration and air cleaning
14389
system:
14390
14391
• Properly balance the building to avoid unfiltered air infiltrating into the conditioned space; See
14392
Strategy 2.3 Maintain Proper Building Pressurization for more information.
14393
• Apply line-of-sight or light penetration inspection to avoid visually obvious bypass pathways
14394
around FAC system; around filter frames and retainer systems; and at access doors:
14395
• Seal all mating metal surfaces with caulk or appropriate mastic, including ductwork joints,
14396
adjoining frames, and AHU seams;
14397
• Gasket the seal plane(s) of filter frames or headers with resilient, nonporous gasketing, such as
14398
neoprene foam;
14399
• Gasket all filter frame mating surfaces with resilient, nonporous gasketing, such as neoprene
14400
foam (suggestion: specify desired gasketing as factory installed on the filter cartridge at the
14401
appropriate or desired seal plane);
14402
14403
Table 7.4-C An intervention evaluation applying 1/8th inch neoprene gasket tape on the vertical mating
14404
surfaces of MERV 13 frames yielded the efficiency increases noted in this table2.
14405

0.5 micron
1 micron

1177633
26862
2347
Count Subsequent to Gasketing

21637
1941
Average Increase in Efficiency
15.0%
19.5%
17.3%

14407
14408
(Graph 5 from NAFA Air Media here.)
14409
Figure 7.4-G In laboratory in-duct research reported by University of Illinois/Chicago,
14410
this graph shows the fall off in efficiency of a MERV 14 filter experiencing the by-pass
14411
made more visually obvious in the following photo. As installed, the MERV 14 filter is
14412
most likely behaving as a MERV 8.
14413

Return to TOP
14416

14417
has fallen from a poorly sized filter track and has over two inches of
14418
visible gap at the access door, which can bypass up to 50% of the air.
14419
14420
14421
14422
Figure 7.4-I The gasketing on this filter access door has eroded
14423
and disappeared. Thus, the door no longer seals properly.
14424

14426
Figure 7.4-J This corrosion penetration within this air handler
14427
allows outdoor air to bypass around the filter bank.
14428
14429
FAC System Location
14430
The following figure illustrates the various locations that can be employed for the filtration bank. The
14431
various location regimes provide the designer further versatility to locate the filtration function where it
14432
best suits the specific needs of the project for performance, access, and life cycle.
14433
14434
14435
Figure 7.4-K. There are numerous optional locations for the location of filter banks dependent
14436
upon the needs and intent of the design strategies, contaminate sources, and space usage. The
14437
return air (Alternate 1--Design strategy 3.6.1.1-3) and outdoor air (Alternate 2--Design strategy 3.6.1.4-
14438
5) can be filtered independently allowing for different filter selection; or combined
14439
with return air (Alternate 3--Design strategy 3.6.1.1.-6); or can be located upstream with or
14440
without prefiltration (Alternate 4 &4a--Design strategy 3.6.1.1-6); or downstream of the coil
14441
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 472
Return to TOP
with or without prefiltration (Alternate 5 &5a--Design strategy 3.6.1.1-6); downstream of
14442
the blower (Alternate 6--Design strategy 3.6.1.1-6); terminal to the distribution system
14443
(Alternate 7--Design strategy 3.6.1.1-6); and treating the exhaust air to avoid atmospheric
14444
pollution or cross contamination occurrence (Alternate 8--Design strategy 3.6.1.4).
14445
14446
Using the IAQ Procedure
14447
Since advanced IAQ design and enhanced filtration are part of the project scope, the IAQ Procedure of
14448
Standard 62.15 provides the designer an excellent opportunity to provide both advanced IAQ conditions
14449
as well as energy savings. The filtration and air cleaning (FAC) employs contaminant removal
14450
(extraction) rather than dilution (ventilation) to achieve indoor contaminant control. Both are
14451
"fractional" in the amount of delivered clean air with FAC being much more cost effective during hot
14452
and humid outdoor conditions because of the capacity and operating cost of treating the latent content of
14453
outdoor air. Once the make-up air and pressurization requirements are met, the remaining dilution can
14454
be provided by filtration and air cleaning. Since up to 40% of the HVAC energy demand is for treating
14455
the outdoor air ventilation component, this approach awards the owner with compelling capacity
14456
(capital) and operating cost (energy) savings far in excess of the cost of operating and maintaining the
14457
filtration system. A fuller discussion of the overall requirements of the IAQ Procedure is contained
14458
in Strategy 8.5 Using the ASHRAE Standard 62.1 IAQ Procedure where Appropriate.
14459
14460
The IAQ Procedure is best and easiest applied with filtration when the following conditions are met that
14461
affect the usage of FAC to fulfill ventilation compliance:
14462
• When outdoor air is polluted, of low quality, and/or in non-attainment of the EPA National
14463
Ambient Air Quality Standards. The IAQ Procedure enables avoidance of bringing in
14464
contaminated outdoor air that is not acceptable for dilution purposes. This is particularly
14465
applicable in ozone non-attainment areas.
14466
• When contaminants of concern are identified and quantifiable, both in outdoor air or return air.
14467
This enables the selection of appropriate filtration and/or gaseous air cleaning tactics and
14468
equipment; the determination of targets of acceptability and attainment; and guides methods of
14469
measuring and monitoring the systems during operation.
14470
• When the condition of the outdoor ambient air is either designated as "hot and humid" or when
14471
outdoor air is seasonally hot and humid. This enables the reduction of latent load and enhances
14472
moisture management within the conditioned space. This benefits design capacity and first cost,
14473
as well as operating expense during peak loads.
14474
• When enhanced filtration is already a design component, which may be because of known
14475
contaminant generation or advanced requirements in the conditioned space. Once the investment
14476
is made in filtration that is better than minimum requirements, the enhancement may be
14477
sufficient to satisfy the IAQ Procedure requirements to yield related operating energy savings
14478
from ventilation reduction.
14479
• When the building occupant population density is high and/or widely diverse, requiring large
14480
quantities of outdoor ventilation air during peak occupancy. Particularly valuable in public
14481
assembly and conference spaces, thereby avoiding the excess capacity that occupancy driven
14482
ventilation rates require--when in fact, the space is transiently occupied and vacant for high
14483
percentages of operating cycle times.
14484
• When motivated owners recognize the economic value of the IAQ Procedure in first cost;
14485
operating cost; and positive environmental impact. Further, they are willing to support the
14486
conscientious monitoring and maintenance of the HVAC system to sustain the advanced quality
14487
of the indoor environment.
14488
14489
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 473
Return to TOP
Though not widely applied in commercial buildings, Burroughs has reported on a number of
14490
installations7 in selected markets that have successfully exploited the IAQ Procedure since its
14491
incorporation into Standard 62-1981 in its current form.
14492
14493
(Verification: The use of the IAQ Procedure requires additional rigor and rationale from the designer to
14494
provide mass balance calculations or other compliance measures to demonstrate the control of
14495
contaminants of concern and/or air testing to quantify contaminants of concern. Verification of
14496
attainment can also be provided by FAC suppliers or IAQ consultants through air testing to demonstrate
14497
control. This is an additional first cost to demonstrate attainment and the operation and maintenance of
14498
the filtration equipment adds to operational cost as well. However, the return ratios in energy savings
14499
range from 5 : 1 to 10 : 1 when comparing energy savings to filter cost, far out-weighing the cost of
14500
filtration and additional engineering rigor. See also Strategy 8.5 Using the ASHRAE Standard 62.1
14501
IAQ Procedure where Appropriate.)
14502
14503
Design Process Protocol
14504
The application of enhanced filtration and air cleaning is a well-established technology in industrial
14505
process control, clean rooms, healthcare, pharmaceuticals, and special usage buildings, such as museums
14506
and laboratories. However, it has not been widely used in general commercial buildings. Thus, the
14507
following regime is provided to familiarize the designer to the routine of applying enhanced filtration
14508
and air cleaning.
14509
14510
Table 7.4-D
14511
Task
Rationale and Explanation of Process
Investigate and characterize ambient
(external) contaminants of concern
Establishes quality and reliability of outdoor air as
diluent. Identifies external sources of odor, particulates,
pollutants, & potential chemical risks for exposure or
reaction. Predicts need and timing for outdoor air
treatment/avoidance. Guides the selection of control
methods and efficiency requirements.
Identify and characterize internal
contaminants of concern (CoC)
Provides basis for quality of return air for determining
Class and reuse options. Predicts needs and levels of air
treatment. Contaminant identity guides selection of
proper control method--particulate or gas--and efficiency
targets.
Select FAC efficiency and methods to
control identified CoC
Determines efficiency selection (MERV level) and gas
phase filter type. Establishes targets of contaminant
reduction and control.
Perform life cycle analysis and select
filter cartridge within efficiency bracket
Consider life cycle, capacity, durability, labor, space,
pressure drop and factors beyond efficiency and cost in
final selection.
Based upon filter selection and ambient
air condition, evaluate IAQ Procedure as
energy management option
Since enhanced filtration may be specified, the
investment in first cost and operating cost is covered.
Dependent on outdoor air contamination and seasonal
latent load--IAQ Procedure may be indicated for
operating cost savings and favorable payback.
Establish targets of acceptability
Provides opportunity of risk analysis for owner. Guides
removal efficiency requirements. Provides mechanism
for assessing and measuring attainment for IAQ
Procedure.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 474
Return to TOP
Task
Rationale and Explanation of Process
Determine location of filter banks
Guides the need and specific treatment requirements of
different air sources--outdoor air vs return air vs mixed
air.
Establish seal, gasketing, and retainer
needs
Specify seal and gasket requirements in AHU,
distribution, and filter bank. Specify retainer system.
Without guidance, this critical element will be neglected
by contractors.
Select air filter cartridge and framing to
maximize life cycle and durability
Provides for maximum life cycle at lowest operating cost
by selecting the least cost filter type. Assures that the
filter frame will sustain the filter media throughout an
extended life cycle period.
Provide appropriate monitoring
instrumentation
Filters should be monitored and serviced based upon
pressure drop. Provide for proper sensors; read-out; and
linkage to Building Automation Systems.
Specify filtration requirements during
construction.
Most systems are precontaminated during the
construction and start up procedures. Specified filtration
level should be installed upon start-up to protect systems
during this high concentration generation period.
Carefully evaluate the need for
prefiltration.
With filters of MERV 13 and less, prefiltration can add
cost, labor, and energy demand without compensating
added value to final filter life cycle. Prefilter > MERV 14
and only high efficiency HE/HEGA cartridges unless
heavy lint or dirt-load is expected.
Provide for additional change(s) of
specified filters for post construction
Without this, the contractor will ignore change-out or
purchase on price alone.
Provide for compliance verification of
IAQ Procedure as component of
commissioning
Establishes conformity and attainment of target CoC
concentrations through air testing or panel. See IAQP
compliance discussion. (LINK)
Provide the owner with filter selection
rational and specifications along with
full documentation, as well as training
for operating personnel.
Without guidance, maintenance personnel will buy on
habit, prior knowledge, or ignorance. Training is
especially important when filtration is fulfillment of the
IAQ Procedure and is mandatory.
14512
Performance Evaluation and Consideration of FAC Alternatives
14513
14514
Particulate Filter Efficiency Selection (MERV and ASHRAE Standard 52.2)
14515
In particulate filtration selection, specific efficiency guidance is based upon the minimum efficiency
14516
determination using ASHRAE Standard 52.2-20074 and its related "MERV" designations. The term
14517
MERV stands for Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value and is a derivative of the Composite Efficiency
14518
Curve (CEC), which is the full data product of Standard 52.2. This Method of Test (MOT) was first
14519
published in 1999 and has since superceded the archaic Average Atmospheric Dust Spot Test Method of
14520
Std. 52.1 (the current '07 version incorporates errata but does not change the MOT). This standard
14521
provides the designer with comparative efficiency data for filters under laboratory conditions using a
14522
controlled test aerosol (KCl) over their entire loading cycle and over a full range of 12 particle size
14523
fractions from 0.3 to 10 microns. This size spectrum covers the range of particle sizes that are
14524
considered to be important in providing high quality indoor environmental conditions. Filter efficiency
14525
is dependent upon particle size, increasing with larger particles (See Figure NX for the particle sizes of
14526
common airborne agents).
14527
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 475
Return to TOP
14528
14529
14530
Figure 7.4-L Particle Size Range of Common Airborne Agents
14531
(Source: Graphic Figure 1 from NIOSH document)
14532
14533
Filter efficiency also changes over the loading cycle, generally increasing as the filter loads with an
14534
accumulated layer of particulate matter, although some types of media can lose efficiency during
14535
loading. Standard 52.2 provides minimum efficiency data during loading, while providing minimum
14536
levels at specific particle sizes. These data are combined and reported as the Composite Efficiency
14537
Curve (CEC) that indicates this minimum efficiency for each of 12 size fractions at whatever loading
14538
stage that it occurs. Table (N1) illustrates the data from multiple stages of loading with ASHRAE test
14539
dust, including an initial stage that serves as a conditioning step intended to relieve static charge in
14540
media types that rely on this vehicle for efficiency enhancement.
14541
14542
14543
14544
Figure 7.4-M Sample Air Cleaner Performance Summary
14545
(Source: ASHRAE 52.2 2007 - from Figure C-2 (b) Std 5.2 pg 36)
14546
14547
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 476
Return to TOP
The CEC is then integrated into three micron-size bands (E1 0.3 to 1;E2 1.0 to 3.0; E3 3.0 to 10.0) and
14548
the efficiencies in each band are averaged and bracketed with minimum thresholds to derive the MERV
14549
designation. Being simpler, easier, and quicker than the CEC, the MERV can be used for broad
14550
screening and category selections. For critical applications, however, especially in the higher MERV
14551
designations when minimum performance is more critical, the CEC should be examined by the designer
14552
for precise minimum efficiencies at specific size fractions of concern. Upon request, most filter
14553
equipment manufacturers will furnish third party test reports showing the CEC curve and the related
14554
MERV designation on their products. For quick screening, increasingly higher MERV levels indicate
14555
improved minimums and improved efficiencies at smaller particle sizes. For example, MERV 6 has a
14556
measured efficiency of not less than 35% in the 3.0 to 10 micron size range and its efficiency against
14557
less than 1 micron particles is not even reported. On the high end, MERV 16 has a measured efficiency
14558
of not less than 95% against the 0.3-1 micron size range. The MERV designation progresses upward
14559
through this efficiency spectrum, allowing the designer to select the desired bracket of efficiency at the
14560
smallest particle size of concern.
14561
14562
After several years of experience with the new MOT, the SSPC52.2 committee determined that the
14563
conditioning step in the original procedure was not sufficiently rigorous to replicate the efficiency drop
14564
experienced by certain media, specifically synthetic "electret" media that demonstrates an inherent
14565
charge. When exposed to ambient pollution constituents, such as superfine particles and diesel exhaust,
14566
this charge can be relieved bringing about a dip in the performance curve that is not predicted by
14567
the original conditioning step. Research sponsored by SSPC 52.2 has yielded guidance on an enhanced
14568
conditioning loading procedure that employs a superfine KCl aerosol replacing the ASHRAE dust
14569
previously used. This revised conditioning method has been through public review and will be published
14570
as an appendix to the Standard providing an optional alternate conditioning step. Thus, designers and
14571
owners desiring the more robust conditioning criterion should specify the desired MERV designated
14572
with "KCl conditioning" and iterate the specific targeted minimum efficiency from the CEC and the
14573
testing velocity. For example, a specification could read:"MERV 14 (KCl conditioned per Appendix
14574
XX) 95% @ 1.0 micron @ .93m3/s."(Caution: Efficiency and related MERV designation are only one
14575
aspect of final filter selection. See the discussion of filter selection for guidance on other performance
14576
criterion LINK)
14577
14578
14579
14580
Figure 7.4-N Composite Efficiency Curves (Source: ASHRAE 52.2 2007)
14581
14582
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 477
Return to TOP
The curves in Figure 7.4-N represent an array of CEC (Composite Efficiency Curves) indicating precise
14583
minimum efficiency data points at each of 12 particle sizes. The three bands (E1-.3 to 1; E2-1 to 3; and
14584
E3-3 to 10) are merged to create the MERV represented here by Filter A, which is a MERV14; Filter B,
14585
which is a MERV11; Filter C, which is a MERV 9; Filter D, which is a MERV 8; and Filter E, which is
14586
a disposable panel filter with insufficient efficiency to be qualified by the 52.2 MOT.
14587
14588
Gas Phase Air Cleaner Efficiency Selection: Standard 145-P
14589
The testing of gas phase air cleaners is much more of an "art form" than the particulate testing, since
14590
there has been no published industry consensus standards for the testing of gas phase filters. However,
14591
ASHRAE Standard Project Committee 145 has been has been successful in developing a standard
14592
method of test that has attained consensus approval. The standard, designated as 145.1, covers the
14593
testing of sorbent media at laboratory bench scale and is approved for publication. A companion
14594
standard, 145.2, will establish the efficiency performance of a full sized cartridge in a laboratory test
14595
duct that is adapted from the Standard 52.2 testing system. It is expected to publish within the year.
14596
14597
Currently, however, performance data is also available from selected manufacturers who have
14598
established proprietary test methods that are generally based upon or similar to the 145P standard. The
14599
efficiency data is generally reported using a variety of metrics: Single Pass Efficiency (SPE) for specific
14600
contaminants under defined conditions; time to break-through; micrograms/mol of contaminant retained
14601
vs unit/mass of sorbent; or some combination of these. The performance data can be performed on
14602
single gas challenges or may employ blended gases to represent typical chemical families.
14603
14604
Generally, the current forms of gaseous filter cartridges are either based upon a fabric/fibrous matte that
14605
incorporates a modest amount of sorbent, or a solid bed or tray of bulk sorbent media in pelletized form.
14606
The combined matte/sorbent version is pleated for enhanced capacity and lower pressure drop and used
14607
for lighter concentration loading. These units are generally referred to as "medium efficiency" (ME) and
14608
may have up to a MERV 12 particulate efficiency designation. The more robust version of gas phase air
14609
cleaner is based on bulk pelletized media poured into a tray that is then incorporated into a larger V-
14610
bank or extended media cell. It is applied for higher concentration, capacity, and/or efficiency
14611
requirements and referred to as "solid bed"; "total retention;" or "high efficiency gas adsorber" (HE).
14612
The latter is different from the medium efficiency type--being much higher in performance, capacity,
14613
life cycle--but also higher pressure-drop, weight, and cost. When specialized media is incorporated as
14614
the active fill media for specialized high performance applications, such as anti-terrorism, the unit is
14615
called HEGA (High Efficiency Gas Adsorber). (Caution: A number of factors can influence the
14616
selection of the appropriate gas phase air cleaning, because efficiency is influenced by a number of
14617
factors having to do with chemical properties of the sorbent and the contaminant of concern, as well as
14618
the behavior of molecules in the gaseous state.)
14619
14620
14621
Case Study #1
14622
Energy Savings with Acceptable IAQ in Renovated Office Space
14623
14624
This 20-story building is located within a high-rise office complex that is a typical tenant leased property
14625
that contains predominantly state agency departments, It is approximately 35 years old with original air
14626
handling equipment. Each floor is an autonomous zone served by a single air handler. Outdoor air is
14627
supplied from a common shaft from the roof to each mechanical room that serves as the return plenum
14628
from the ceiling plenum return system. At the beginning of a lease term, the tenant entity required the
14629
landlord's management company to bring the property up to the then current ASHRAE Standard 62
14630
version, which imposed outdoor air levels beyond the capacity of the existing air handlers. The
14631
mechanical rooms are central to the building with no external access and, further, have no extra space to
14632
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 478
Return to TOP
accommodate for unit replacement or upgrades. The management team applied the Indoor Air Quality
14633
Procedure to replicate the improvement of indoor environmental acceptability through filtration and air
14634
cleaning rather than additional ventilation air. This avoided the introduction of additional outdoor air
14635
that would have overwhelmed the existing system with hot and humid air during summer cooling
14636
conditions. The unit was limited to side access 2" filter tracks previously devoted to 2" disposable
14637
fibrous glass prefilters. The upgrade and fulfillment of the IAQ Procedure was accomplished through the
14638
usage of special medium efficiency (ME) gas phase pleated filters. These combination filter cartridges
14639
employ a MERV 8 rated particulate filter media, imbedded with sorption pellets of KMnO4/alumina that
14640
are retained within the strata of filter media. The upgraded filtration system has demonstrated a 3-month
14641
life cycle that was established by capacity analysis by the filter manufacturer.
14642
14643
14644
Figure 7.4-O
14645
14646
The energy savings experienced by the building manager equals $26,409 less the cost of the filtration
14647
maintenance of $16,747 for net savings of $9,662 per year over the comparable cost of treating an
14648
equivalent amount of outdoor air. The installation of the filtration equipment did not require further
14649
capital expenditure beyond the filter cost, thus, the payback was virtually immediate and the tenant has
14650
been completely satisfied with the indoor air quality conditions. Thus, the filtration upgrades not only
14651
fulfilled the compliance requirement of Standard 62; it also facilitated the completion of a long-term
14652
lease opportunity.
14653
14654
Energetic Type Filters
14655
The above discussion refers to FAC that have reported efficiencies using the industry consensus
14656
Standard 52.2-2007. There are other filtration or air cleaning devices that employ variations of active
14657
electrical fields that are claimed to provide enhanced particle and molecular capture. Unfortunately,
14658
many of these cannot be tested using 52.2 or 145-P. Thus, there is no recognized industry standard or
14659
Method of Test to evaluate or rate these devices that claim contaminant control or extraction through
14660
this energetic means. The second concern regarding energetic devices is that electrical fields generally
14661
create ozone as a by-product, which is a Contaminant of Concern (CoC) in the indoor environment
14662
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 479
Return to TOP
because of potential irritation from direct exposure, as well as potential by-products of reaction with
14663
indoor pollutants.
14664
14665
EAC –. The more established and better known energetic device is the electrostatic precipitator (ESP),
14666
also call an Electronic Air Cleaner (EAC). This device employs an electrical field to charge the small
14667
airborne particles and incorporates a deposition plate that attracts and captures the particles, extracting
14668
them from the airstream. The deposition plate is periodically cleaned to renew the capture efficacy. This
14669
device is especially effective against small micron-sized particulate matter that responds well to electron
14670
charging and deposition. The EAC category of air cleaner is not tested using the 52.2 MOT because
14671
there is no declared "end point" for filter service, which negates the ability to predict "minimum"
14672
efficiency. Unlike arrestance filters, the efficiency of this device is best when totally clean and drops
14673
over time to an end point of zero efficiency. The inability to predict efficiency drop-off and appropriate
14674
cleaning/service cycles impairs the usability of this device in critical commercial applications. More
14675
recent research (Hanley X Reference) sponsored by EPA has shown that the charging wires of the EAC
14676
can also become blinded with depositions of airborne silicates. Over time, these depositions sheath the
14677
wires and decay the ability of the unit to be sufficiently cleaned to be able to return to original efficiency
14678
levels after the cleaning process.
14679
14680
Combination Devices: Some newer devices employ various combinations of energetic sources with both
14681
arrestance and gaseous sorption components and make strong claims regarding enhanced air cleaning or
14682
purifying through ionization or other electron manipulation. Many of these devices are residential in
14683
marketing motivation, but they are appearing in the commercial market. Generally speaking, if a claim
14684
sounds too good to be true, it usually isn't. Even if the system or device is based on sound technology
14685
and the claims are based upon anecdotal testimonies lacking in credible third party research or
14686
documentation, then the claims can be suspect. Even if the device does not lend itself to the 52.2 Method
14687
of Testing, upstream versus downstream particle counts or contaminant concentration comparisons
14688
performed by a credible third party researcher will provide an accurate indicator of performance. The
14689
evaluation should contain a life cycle or capacity evaluation as well. If the device is claimed to act upon
14690
the space rather than upstream/downstream of the controller, then in situ site or chamber testing under
14691
controlled conditions over time can provide similar validation of performance under actual operating
14692
conditions.
14693
14694
Ultraviolet Germicidal Irradiation (UVGI): The use of UVGI as an energetic device for airborne
14695
pathogen control in upper room air is well-established especially in healthcare facilities and TB wards.
14696
The more recent IAQ concerns about microbial growth, especially mold in HVAC systems, as well as
14697
9/11 related concerns for microbiological-based weapons of mass destruction, have reawakened interest
14698
in this time-proven technology. Though not a filter, per se, the device is being claimed as an "air
14699
purifier". The original application of UVGI was in high-bay rooms where air exposure times were long
14700
in relatively slow moving air streams. (See Strategy 5.4 Consider Upper Room Ultraviolet
14701
Germicidial Irradiation) When applied in an HVAC system, the dwell times are very short and the
14702
operating conditions of the device are substantially altered from the earlier successful applications.
14703
Currently, UVGI bulbs are being installed into air handlers to treat the wet coil and the related surfaces
14704
of the air handler. However, some suppliers claim microbial control effects in the moving air stream.
14705
Because of the increased interest and the widespread promotion of UVGI for application in HVAC
14706
systems, current research is being performed to examine the effectiveness of UVGI under various
14707
operating conditions inside HVAC equipment. A study sponsored by ARTI (Air Conditioning and
14708
Refrigeration Technical Institute) and performed by Foarde and VanOsdell (reference) provides
14709
guidance on the significant issues affecting UVGI performance. The primary factors influencing the
14710
effectiveness of UVGI are listed below to assist the design team in their consideration and evaluation of
14711
this emerging application area.
14712
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 480
Return to TOP
• Installation in a high velocity HVAC system is much more complex than the traditional and
14713
historical applications in high ceiling or laminar flow airstreams because of the dramatically
14714
reduced dwell times involved with a fast moving airstream.
14715
• There is strong anecdotal evidence that UVGI can be effective in retarding the fungal growth that
14716
can occur on wet surfaces that are in direct irradiance pathway and where contact dwell time is
14717
long and constant.
14718
• UVGI irradiance is a direct line-of-sight phenomenon; thus, targeted surfaces must be in direct
14719
contact view, although reduced irradiance effect can rebound from polished surfaces.
14720
• Because of the vagaries of microbe resistance and confounding factors in ductwork installation,
14721
control of microbial contaminants in a moving airstream is fractional and varied.
14722
• Control (Kill % ratio) is highly dependent upon irradiance Dosage, which in turn is dependent
14723
upon multiple factors, such as bulb characteristics and operating behavior, bulb age and
14724
cleanliness, airflow rate, temperature, humidity, installation location and methodology, and
14725
reflectivity of duct components.
14726
• Issues such as soiling of bulb surfaces and reflective surfaces, sub-cooling of bulb surfaces, and
14727
bulb age can all negatively affect the irradiance performance of bulbs over time.
14728
• The technology works best against vegetative bacteria as this technology was established with
14729
the original experience in TB wards. Unfortunately, the behavior is worst with spore-forming
14730
bacteria and fungi where the marketplace claims are apparently focused.
14731
• Because of the variables and the indicated resistance to inactivation by sporulating bacteria, care
14732
should be exercised in making or accepting blanket claims for either pathogenic control or for
14733
“building security” performance without substantial testing under actual operating conditions.
14734
• The early experience with UVGI was in conjunction with hospital level (MERV 14-16) and
14735
HEPA installations (MERV 17-20). The synergy with high efficiency particulate filtration can
14736
address the issues of particulate fouling of bulbs and reflecting surfaces. Concurrent filtration
14737
also resolves the issue that UVGI does not “cleanse” the air because the particulate
14738
contamination remains in the airstream unless it is extracted by good filtration. The latter
14739
approach also solves the issue that airborne fungal spores can affect human response whether
14740
they are viable or non-viable because of mycotoxin content.
14741
• In light of the variables of design and application, designers and installers should apply safety
14742
factors to their designs. Further, they should avoid relying solely on design equations or factory
14743
recommendations, but perform actual testing with contaminants of concern using conditions
14744
specific to the application site.
14745
14746
Maximizing the Value and Performance of FAC
14747
14748
Life Cycle Analysis
14749
The dominant factor influencing life cycle cost of filtration has little to do with the first cost of the filter,
14750
although this is often the prevailing purchasing criterion. These comments can apply equally to both
14751
particulate and gas phase filtration. The real cost to the owner is How long does it last? and How much
14752
energy does it cost to operate? To illustrate, the entire life cycle analysis of FAC includes all of the
14753
following:
14754
• Filter Delivered Cost. The cost of freight is a significant factor in shipping filters because of bulk
14755
and rate penalty.
14756
• Warehousing, handling, and storage. The cubage of filtration requires disproportionate
14757
warehouse space.
14758
• Labor of installation/replacement. Ease of access, gasketing, & location of units influence man-
14759
hours expended.
14760
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 481
Return to TOP
• Use of prefilters to protect downstream filters. Can extend the life of more expensive final filters
14761
of higher efficiency and cost, but requires additional space, cost and energy.
14762
• Life cycle of filters. Change cycle dictates cost per unit time.
14763
• Pressure drop requirement and characteristic pressure drop curve over loading.
14764
• Disposal of expended cartridges. This also includes the labor, handling, and freight cost of solid
14765
waste disposal.
14766
14767
This entire life cycle equation is dominated by two factors--the life cycle of the filter and the pressure
14768
drop loss during installation. For example, if the life service cycle of the final filter is doubled, then first
14769
cost, freight, labor, handling and storage, and disposal costs are halved automatically. In the case of
14770
pressure drop, the cost of an additional.25 IWG ΔP over one year with an air handler running 24/7 with
14771
a moderately efficient motor with power costing 6 cent/KWh will be approximately $64/year. If a
14772
typical MERV 14 filter requires .6 IWG initial pressure drop, it is obvious that the energy cost of
14773
operating far exceeds the cost of any MERV 14 filter. If a filter of an identical MERV designation
14774
operates at .25 IWG less, then that selection will pay for the filter regardless of its first cost. (See Table
14775
NX for illustration of filter operating cost comparison)
14776
14777
How to Maximize the Life Cycle and Performance of FAC
14778
Since life cycle and pressure drop are the critical cost factors, the designer has the opportunity to design
14779
into the building, long term life cycle operating advantages that will provide the owner with lifetime
14780
return on investment. The following recommendations provide insight into the control life cycle and
14781
operating cost.
14782
• Examine the initial and final pressure drop features of filter selections. As illustrated in Table
14783
NX, the same MERV filter in different configuration can provide totally different energy demand.
14784
Select the filter of the required MERV having the lowest initial pressure drop--regardless of the
14785
initial cost of the filter.
14786
• Specify high quality framing materials that are sturdy and moisture resistant. This is because
14787
extended media filters have longer installed life, even months or years. Low grade pasteboard or
14788
light duty beverage board typically used in less expensive filter frames will not retain integrity,
14789
hold dimensions, or properly support the filter media over the potential life cycle. Low quality
14790
framing will degrade before the capacity of the filter media is fully exploited, resulting in shorter
14791
life cycles than possible with quality framing materials due to the degradation.
14792
• Avoid prefiltration when applied solely to protect downstream final filters since the prefilter adds
14793
life cycle cost, energy demand, and does not proportionately extend the life cycle value of the
14794
final filter as illustrated in Table NXX. Exceptions apply for downstream HEPA; HE/HEGA gas
14795
phase filtration; and greater than MERV 14 level particulate filters; or also, when there are
14796
unusual loads of large mass particles, such as blowing sand.
14797
• Understand the role of approach or duct velocity. When applied to filtration equipment, the rule
14798
of squares is not precisely applicable because of turbulence factors at the face of extended media
14799
filters. However, the influence is far greater than a linear relationship--meaning that
14800
disproportionate gains can be attained by de-rating filters. Most manufacturers publish pressure
14801
drop data over various approach velocities. As illustrated in Figure NX, a 20% reduction in
14802
approach velocity from 500 fpm to 400 fpm can drop the initial pressure drop by one/half and
14803
also increase the operating life cycle by 50 to 200%. To attain this reduction within engineered
14804
systems, simply increase the duct cross-section by over-sizing the filter bank. For example
14805
increase a 4 X 5 configuration to a 5 X 5 configuration. There is no impact on floor space and the
14806
reduction in pressure drop is significant. When this is not possible, consider media velocity as
14807
discussed below.
14808
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 482
Return to TOP
• Understand the role of media velocity or dwell time within the media. Likewise, the rule of
14809
squares does not apply precisely, but lowering media velocity also yields disproportionate gain in
14810
pressure drop. This is done within the filter by selecting filters with higher surface area: meaning
14811
higher pleat count with pleated filters; deeper pleats, from 2" to 4" for example; deeper pockets
14812
in the case of bag filters; and filter configurations that provide superior surface area. Within the
14813
targeted MERV level, select the filter with the most surface area possible, consistent with the
14814
physical constraints of the system or select equipment that can support installation and access of
14815
higher surface area FAC.
14816
• The reduction of velocity also yields increased performance in other ways. Especially in gas
14817
phase filters, the lower media velocity increases single pass efficiency. In particulate filters, the
14818
dirt holding capacity is enhanced and life cycle is extended both from the standpoint of dirt
14819
holding as well as lower pressure drop and extended time before attaining change points due to
14820
diminished airflow.
14821
• Maximizing life cycle can also be attained by monitoring the pressure drop progression of
14822
particulate filter systems using precise manometer equipment. Most modern air handling
14823
equipment is equipped with variable speed drives, meaning that the system operates at part load
14824
condition much of the time. This effectively lowers the pressure drop and enables even greater
14825
life cycle extension if monitored and change-out is not on an arbitrary time schedule. This also
14826
allows the prescription of the change out point, which should be no greater that double the
14827
original initial pressure drop. Although this is contrary to most suppliers' literature that denote
14828
the failure point or final test point of the filter, it represents the most economical range of the
14829
filter life cycle. Most extended media filters exhibit a relatively flat pressure drop performance
14830
curve for most of their life cycle, until the final fraction of loading when most curves abruptly
14831
rise and terminate. By changing ahead of this rise, most of the filter life cycle will have been
14832
attained at the lowest possible range of energy demand.
14833
14834
Table 7.4-E
14835
