14836
This table provides analysis for comparing two different filter types having the same MERV designation.
14837
When pressure drop and related energy expense is combined with life cycle expectation, the lower initial
14838
cost filter is the poor decision from a long-term life cycle perspective. Whatever the MERV range, the
14839
lowest life-cycle cost filter is the one with the most surface area and the lowest pressure drop,
14840
regardless of first cost.
14841
14842
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 483
Return to TOP
14843
Figure 7.4-P Effect of Underrating and Overrating Filter Life
14844
The role of velocity is illustrated with these rating curves. When filters
14845
are underrated by as little as 20%, the gain can be as much as 50% in
14846
life cycle with reduction of up to one half in operating pressure drop.
14847
(Source: Illustration credit to NAFA – Figure 13.1)
14848
14849
Prefiltration Considerations
14850
Historically, filter suppliers have recommended prefiltration protection of higher efficiency, higher cost
14851
final filters using less expensive "sacrificial" panel filters. The theory was that prefilters add a modest
14852
level of extraction efficiency and keep larger mass particles from flooding the final filter, yielding longer
14853
life on the more valuable final filter. The increase of cost of labor, the increase of the use of higher
14854
efficiency pleated filters for prefilters, the decrease of cost of final filters, and the increase of energy
14855
costs have altered this paradigm. The following Table X illustrates and compared the impact of the cost
14856
of prefiltration vs the savings through life extension of the final filter being prefiltered. For this analysis,
14857
energy cost is ignored. The first column denotes the master list price of the filter type for comparison
14858
purposes (prior to routine discounts for volume or customer contracts). The second column adds a
14859
nominal labor fee for change out labor and handling and reports the annual cost of the filter based on
14860
factory recommended change cycles. In the case of the higher MERV filters, one-year life was assumed
14861
as the baseline. Assume the addition of prefiltration to attain life extension beyond the one-year baseline.
14862
From the mathematics, it is easy to see that the cheap disposable filter is one of the most expensive
14863
annual costs because of short life and high labor cost. Thus, assume that the MERV 6 pleat is used to
14864
prefilter the MERV 13 bag filter and one additional year is attained through the prefiltration inclusion.
14865
The result is a two-year expenditure of $56 for prefiltration to gain an additional year on the final filter
14866
at a value of $20.43. If even longer life is attained, the multi-year cost of prefiltration exceeds the annual
14867
value gain of life extension by a multiplier of 6. Only the most expensive filters, MERV 14 and higher,
14868
deserve prefiltration consideration and, only then, after careful consideration of the nature of the
14869
prefiltered air; the additional energy demand of the prefilter; and the related gain of life in the final filter.
14870
(Remember, if energy were considered in this discussion, 0.025 IWG pressure drop costs up to $64/year,
14871
which is over twice the annual installed cost of the pleated filters that operate at that average pressure
14872
drop range.)
14873
14874
14875

14901
Strategy 7.5 – Provide Comfort Conditions that Enhance Occupant
14902
Satisfaction .
14903
14904
Introduction
14905
Thermal comfort has links both to occupant perceptions of air quality and studies now suggest that there
14906
are links to occupant productivity as well. Dry bulb temperature is only one parameter out of many that
14907
interact in a complex manner to produce occupant satisfaction. Others include humidity, radiant
14908
temperature, air movement (including dynamic elements that mimic natural breezes) and factors even
14909
more remote from thermal conditions, such as occupant proximity to windows, sight lines to them and
14910
the level of control offered to occupants, whether they use it or not.
14911
14912
Occupant expectations play a huge role as well. For instance, occupants will accept a much wider range
14913
of thermal conditions in spaces that are naturally rather than mechanically conditioned and/or when
14914
individuals have greater control over their own environments.
14915
14916
Links to:
14917
(a) Studies and references showing how natural conditioning expands range of occupant acceptance
14918
vs. mechanical cooling (b) how occupant control of operable windows and other aspects that
14919
affect indoor conditions improves occupant satisfaction, (c) Studies about occupant controlled
14920
personalized ventilation and commercial systems to apply it, (d) ASHRAE 55 and other studies
14921
showing temperature for peak in occupant satisfaction, (e) Links to 55 and studies showing the
14922
complex interaction among dry bulb, radiant temperature, humidity, air movement, dynamic air
14923
movement (Zhu from Tsinghua), (f) studies showing the benefit of windows in sight of the
14924
workspace, (g) lower temperatures enhance satisfaction with non-thermal aspects of air quality,
14925
(h) ASHRAE 55 Thermal Comfort Tool (i) studies in hot / humid climates showing expansion of
14926
comfort range with environments open to the outdoors and the heavy use of fans.
14927
14928
Often in traditional designs, the HVAC designer’s role in achieving comfort conditions begins and ends
14929
at the selection of an indoor design condition and the sizing of a system to provide these at the peak
14930
outdoor conditions. This is certainly a prerequisite in mechanically conditioned buildings, yet it leaves
14931
very little latitude to designers to improve thermal comfort control beyond the norm. Depending upon
14932
the particular design, there are several additional considerations and actions available to a designer
14933
beyond this prerequisite that can enhance occupant satisfaction.
14934
14935
Basic Thermal Comfort
14936
Thermal comfort is highly individual and cannot be predicted with accuracy for a particular person.
14937
Nevertheless, there are tools for predicting the proportion of people likely to be satisfied by the
14938
combination of comfort factors that include temperature, humidity, air velocity and radiant heat transfer.
14939
The most commonly known tool in the U.S. is the Thermal Comfort Tool, a computer program that is
14940
part of ASHRAE Standard 55. In order to use this tool, the amount of clothing worn by occupants and
14941
their level of metabolic activity must be known (in units of Clos and Mets, respectively). Accounting
14942
for variations among individuals in Clos and Mets are not part of any automatic temperature control
14943
system and this is a reason for providing, when possible, more zones and greater individual control by
14944
occupants. One aspect of individual control that occupants may have, depending on their work
14945
environment, is the ability to add or remove clothing to adapt to their thermal comfort sensation. To the
14946
extent that this can be allowed, it will enhance thermal comfort and reduce energy consumption.
14947
14948
Zoning and Occupant Control
14949
Use smarter zones and more of them. Rooms and areas having loads that vary over time in patterns that
14950
are significantly different from areas that surround them should have their own conditioning control
14951
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 486
Return to TOP
loops and thermostats. Each person in control over their own environment is the ideal situation and
14952
personal control has been shown to increase satisfaction and reduce SBS symptoms. However, this is
14953
not always possible due to limitations such as first cost, architectural layout and system complexity.
14954
When quantities of zones are limited by such considerations, strategic distribution of zones can
14955
maximize their impact. For instance, ten single occupancy offices with similar glazing on the same
14956
façade of a building can be on one zone, but the conference room with variable occupancy and a corner
14957
office exposed to two compass directions probably warrant separate zones. The sales area of an in-line
14958
store with glazing and frequent door openings probably warrants a different zone from the stock and
14959
merchandise preparation area with equipment such as steamers. (A graphic would be useful here).
14960
14961
Since people often have different expectations for thermal comfort and may not understand how HVAC
14962
systems and automatic controls work, so-called “thermostat wars” can occur, where people passively or
14963
openly struggle over set points. For instance, a person who feels warm may set the cooling set point 10
14964
degrees lower than normal, which can result in significant sub-cooling if the system has the capability to
14965
achieve this. Anger and open arguments have been known to occur and In some cases, removal of
14966
occupant direct control by giving it to the building operator alone may be a reasonable option.
14967
14968
The strategy has sometimes been employed of installing dummy thermostats that are not connected to
14969
the automatic control, on the theory that mere thermostat “twiddling” will satisfy occupants. This has the
14970
risk of further alienating occupants, creating a mistrustful situation between system operators and
14971
occupants and distancing occupants from the reality of what HVAC systems can and cannot do.
14972
14973
Part load humidity and velocity control
14974
The control of humidity at part load is a comfort goal as well as one for reducing the possibility of
14975
condensation. For more information on part load control of humidity, see the section on this subject,
14976
Controlling Indoor Humidity.
14977
14978
Systems that vary the air volume in response to thermal load reductions can have advantages in energy
14979
conservation and humidity control at part load. However, air diffusion must be selected so that
14980
minimum air velocity conditions in occupied zones are maintained at low air flow. This can be done by
14981
selecting diffusers that achieve this over the entire range of flow rate, using fan-powered variable
14982
volume boxes or otherwise enhancing air movement.
14983
14984
Operational strategy and design implications
14985
Most automatic temperature control systems do not have measure or respond to thermal environmental
14986
factors such as humidity, radiation and air movement and the only parameter they control is the dry bulb
14987
temperature set point. Many building operators have discovered by trial and error that the ideal dry bulb
14988
conditions for occupant satisfaction are 210C in the winter and 220C in the summer, which is different
14989
from typical indoor design conditions for which 240C. Lower temperatures, such as 200C have been
14990
shown to improve occupant satisfaction with IAQ, independent of perceived thermal comfort (reference
14991
the handbook with latest indoor design data, research that supports the optimum temps – can’t place the
14992
name of the paper at the moment and research that supports the thermal – IAQ link).
14993
Designers can take this into account in their designs by avoiding thermostats that have fixed rather than
14994
variable set points and sometimes by allowing sufficient system capacity to achieve the “ideal”
14995
temperatures. If this results in excess capacity, then part load control and efficiency considerations are
14996
important (reference tools for part load control in design). In addition, choose thermostat locations that
14997
best represent the conditions that occupants will experience and that are not confounded by solar
14998
radiation or other heat sources. When no single location meets this criterion, consider providing multiple
14999
averaging temperature sensing locations.
15000
15001
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 487
Return to TOP
Reference system selection section here, including the potential benefits of radiant heating and cooling,
15002
tight humidity control, separating the thermal conditioning and ventilation functions.
15003
15004
Thermal Radiation
15005
The design team should consider the significant role thermal radiation may play in thermal comfort and
15006
adjust the design accordingly by (1) mitigating the negative impacts of radiation, (2) taking them into
15007
account in system capacity and control and (3) utilizing the beneficial techniques radiation can play in
15008
comfort. For instance, glazing improves the indoor environment by introducing natural light and giving
15009
people a visual connection to the outdoor environment but can also degrade thermal comfort by
15010
introducing additional heat and if people are exposed to direct radiation from the sun. Glazing in cold
15011
climates can have the opposite effect. Selecting glazing that transmits visible light but not thermal
15012
radiation can mitigate these effects. (reference to tool here on selection of glazing). Cooking equipment
15013
(especially when exposed to customer areas) can also be a significant thermal radiator that impacts
15014
indoor comfort. ASHRAE Standard 55 discusses and accounts for further variations such as radiation
15015
asymmetry.,
15016
15017
Radiant heating and cooling can be used to enhance comfort in an expanded temperature envelope.
15018
Graphic here of (1) a restaurant seating area showing operable shading on overhead glazing to limit heat
15019
gain and radiant ceiling panels used in heating season and (2) a warehouse with overhead gas fired
15020
radiant heating.
15021
15022
If radiant heating is used, be aware that an unintended side effect can be increased off gassing of
15023
contaminants from the heated materials, including those surfaces that receive radiant heat from the
15024
primary heated surface. If radiant cooling is used, the potential for condensation must be considered
15025
(see section on maintaining indoor humidity conditions.
15026
15027
Air Movements
15028
In traditional overhead cooling designs, diffuser selection and layout should be done to achieve velocity
15029
near the occupants (sometimes referred to as terminal velocity) of about 50 fpm (.25 m/s). Discuss other
15030
design velocities for UFAD and displacement. (Reference Std 55 and include graphic. Discuss velocity
15031
turndown on VAV, and increased stratification from overhead heating). Reference design tools for
15032
diffuser selection, Rock book on room air distribution.) The use of supplemental fans can expand the
15033
thermal comfort envelope and may be considered in cases where full conditioning is not practical or to
15034
improve energy efficiency.
15035
15036
Consider a graphical example here illustrating, for example an outdoor unconditioned seating area in
15037
Singapore (C. Sekhar has presented something on this) that both protects people from the radiation
15038
effects of the sun and provides air movement through the open sides. Also could show use of ceiling
15039
fans to provide air movement and south Asian office with fans (nceub material) and limited conditioning.
15040
15041
In the heating mode, drafts can occur under windows and other significant sources of heat loss. This can
15042
be counteracted by heat sources under windows or by overhead forced air or radiant heating. Overhead
15043
forced air has height and temperature limitations. Overhead supply of air for heating that is much hotter
15044
than the room or from a very high ceiling will not reach the occupied level and will not counteract the
15045
drafts. It will also not mix properly if it is the source of ventilation air (Reference to section of ASHRAE
15046
62.1).
15047
15048
15049
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 488
Return to TOP
OBJECTIVE 8 – APPLY MORE ADVANCED VENTILATION APPROACHES
15050
15051
15052
15053
Specific Strategies described in this section are:
15054
15055
• Strategy 8.1 – Effectively Distribute Ventilation Air for Multiple Spaces
15056
• Strategy 8.2 – Use Demand-Controlled Ventilation Where Appropriate
15057
• Strategy 8.3 – Use Energy Recovery Ventilation Where Appropriate
15058
• Strategy 8.4 – Use Natural or Mixed Mode Ventilation Where Appropriate
15059
• Strategy 8.5 – Use the ASHRAE Standard 62.1 IAQ Procedure Where Appropriate
15060
15061
15062
15063
Before considering advanced approaches, it is important to understand the basic ventilation and air
15064
cleaning issues described in Objective 7 – Reduce Contaminant Concentrations through Ventilation
15065
Filtration, and Air Cleaning.
15066
15067
15068
15069
15070
15071
15072
[REMAINDER OF PAGE LEFT INTENTIALLY BLANK]
15073
15074
15075
15076
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 489
Return to TOP
15077
Strategy 8.1 – Effectively Distribute Ventilation Air for Multiple Spaces
15078
15079
15080
Multiple Zone Recirulating Systems
15081
ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007 provides methodology for calculating the quantity of outdoor air required
15082
at the outdoor air intake. These calculations are a part of design of an HVAC system. The first step in
15083
ventilation system design is to provide appropriate outside air quantities for each room or zone. Within
15084
each room or zone it is important to distribute ventilation air effectively.
15085
15086
HVAC system design is beyond the scope of this document. The following discussion is limited to
15087
identifying types of systems and critical factors and does not delve into design calculation details which
15088
can become increasingly complex as multiple airflow paths are introduced in system design. The reader
15089
is advised to refer to ASHRAE for standards, additional technical information, and training resources.
15090
15091
Constant Air Volume
15092
A constant (air) volume system (CAV) provides a fixed amount of air to each room or zone. Thermal
15093
comfort is maintained by varying the temperature of the air. For all multiple zone systems, the system
15094
ventilation efficiency Ez determines the minimum volume of outdoor air required at the outdoor air
15095
intake (Vot).
15096
15097
Vot = Vou/Ev (6-8)
15098
15099
Ev = minimum (Evz) (A-3)
15100
15101
The value of Ev is a function of the discharge outdoor air fraction (Zd) for the critical zone(s) and the
15102
average outdoor air fraction (Xs) of the HVAC system. In order to perform the calculations one must
15103
determine, the supply airflow to each zone (Vpz), the zone outdoor airflow for each zone (Voz) and the
15104
system primary airflow (Vps).
15105
15106
A simple method for determining Ev in a constant volume system is the calculate the air fraction for all
15107
the zones to determine the critical zone using the following equation for each zone.
15108
15109
Zp = Voz/Vpz (6-5)
15110
15111
where Vpz is the zone primary airflow, i.e., the primary airflow to the zone from the air handler including
15112
outdoor air and recirculated return air.
15113
15114
Once the maximum Zp is determined, Ev can be determined by looking it up in table 6.3 from ASHRAE
15115
62.1-2007.
15116
15117
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 490
Return to TOP
15118
Table 8.1-A System Ventilation Efficiency
15119
Max (ZP)
Ev
≤0.15
1.0
≤0.25
0.9
≤0.35
0.8
≤0.45
0.7
≤0.55
0.6
> 0.55
Use Appendix A
1. “Max Zp” refers to the largest value of Zp, calculated using Equation 6-5, among all the zones served by the
system.
2. For values of Zp between 0.15 and 0.55, one may determine the corresponding value of Evby interpolating the
values in the table. 3. The values of Evin this table are based on a 0.15 average outdoor air fraction for the system
(i.e., the ratio of the uncorrected outdoor air intake Vou to the total zone primary airflow for all the zones served
by the air handler). For systems with higher values of the average outdoor air fraction, this table may result in
unrealistically low values of Ev and the use of Appendix A may yield more practical results.
15120
Variable Air Volume
15121
A variable air volume system (VAV) provides a variable amount of air to each room or zone. Thermal
15122
comfort is usually maintained by varying the volume of the air. For all multiple zone systems including
15123
VAV, the system ventilation efficiency (Ez) determines the minimum volume of outdoor air required at
15124
the outdoor air intake (Vot).
15125
15126
The difference in VAV and CAV systems is that the airflow varies depending on the load. Reducing the
15127
volume of air reduces the load on the fan and saves energy. For VAV systems the zone primary airflow
15128
(Vpz) is the minimum expected primary airflow for design purposes (sometimes noted as Vpzmin).
15129
Often airflow during heating is different from airflow during cooling. If this is the case, then both
15130
conditions (heating and cooling) must be calculated.
15131
15132
Secondary Recirculation
15133
Some systems have fan systems that create additional recirculation beyond that of the primary air
15134
handler. The efficiency of these systems can be dramatically better than “single supply” systems. The
15135
calculation of the system efficiency using the following equation is more complex because of the
15136
multiple recirculation paths.
15137
15138
From ASHRAE 62.1-2007:
15139
15140
General Case Evz = (Fa + Xs · Fb – Zd · Fc)/Fa (A-2)
15141
15142
Or in terms of E values:
15143
15144
Explanation of all applications and use of this equation is beyond the scope of this document. The
15145
reader is referred to ASHRAE (www.ASHRAE.org) for additional resources including the 62.1 User’s
15146
Manual, archives of the ASHRAE Journal, and several courses offered by the ASHRAE Learning
15147
Institute for more information.
15148
15149
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 491
Return to TOP
Parallel Fan-Powered Box
15150
This system is a VAV system for cooling. When heat is required, a fan tunes on and pulls tempered air
15151
from the plenum and/or provides heat from heating strips or coils. Since this system operates in two
15152
modes, there will be at least two calculations required for system efficiency and a potential for multiple
15153
operating conditions.
15154
15155
Parallel fan powered box
plenum
supply air
return air  from plenum
or return from room
(ducted)
ceiling
15156
Figure 8.1-B Parallel Fan Powered Box
15157
15158
Series Fan-Powered Box
15159
This system is a VAV system for cooling and also recirculates air from the room (ducted return) or the
15160
plenum (plenum return). This system provides better mixing in the zone and may improve comfort.
15161
The box may or may not provide heating. If this system operates in two modes, there will be at least
15162
two calculations required for system efficiency.
15163
15164
Series fan powered box
plenum
supply air
return air  from plenum
or return from room
(ducted)
ceiling
15165
Figure 8.1-C Series Fan Powered Box
15166
15167
Ducted vs Plenum Return
15168
Depending on the configuration, a ducted return may perform as a “single supply” system. There are
15169
some IAQ advantages to ducted return with regard to building pressurization and other concerns, but the
15170
plenum return may have a ventilation system efficiency advantage.
15171
15172
Transfer Fan
15173
In some cases a fan may be used to transfer air from an adjacent (or nearby) space to increase the airflow
15174
in a room such as a conference room. This can improve system ventilation efficiency depending on the
15175
quality of the air from the transfer space.
15176
15177
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 492
Return to TOP
Transfer Fan
plenum
ceiling
15178
Figure 8.1-D Transfer Fan
15179
15180
15181
15182
Other Systems (less commonly used)
15183
15184
Changeover Bypass VAV
15185
Changeover bypass VAV is a special case where the primary fan airflow is constant and the zone
15186
airflow varies.
15187
15188
Dual Fan – Dual Duct
15189
As the name implies there are two primary fans and two ducts in this system – one for cooling and one
15190
for heating. Outdoor air is usually provided through the cooling system. In this case the primary
15191
heating fan recirculates some unused outdoor air improving the system efficiency. Temperature is
15192
controlled in the zone by mixing air from the two ducts to provide the temperature needed to offset the
15193
heating or cooling load.
15194
15195
Induction Unit
15196
This system uses high pressure primary air to “induce” a secondary airflow in the room across heating or
15197
cooling coils.
15198
15199
Additional design resource:
15200
“Single-Path Multiple-Zone System Design” ASHRAE Journal January 2005
15201
15202
15203
15204
15205
15206
15207
15208
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 493
Return to TOP
15209
Strategy 8.2 – Use Demand-Controlled Ventilation where Appropriate
15210
15211
15212
Overview
15213
Demand-controlled ventilation (DCV) is a control strategy that varies the amount of ventilation by
15214
resetting the design outdoor air intake flow (Vot) setpoint and design zone outdoor air flow (Voz) set
15215
point to an occupied space based on the changing number of occupants. The goal is to avoid under
15216
ventilation (thus poor indoor air quality), as well as over ventilation (thus wasted energy). It has been
15217
estimated that in U.S. commercial buildings, DCV has the potential to reduce heating and cooling loads
15218
by as much as 20% (Roth et al., 2005; Brandemuehl and Braun, 1999) or $0.05 to more than $1 per ft2
15219
annually (FEMP, 2004). However, actual savings can vary widely depending on climate, variability in
15220
population density and occupancy schedule, type of building, whether or not the HVAC system has an
15221
economizer, etc.
15222
15223
The simplest approach to DCV is by controling outdoor air rate in an on-off manner based on a signal
15224
from a room occupancy sensor, time clock, or a light switch. A more sophisticated approach uses a
15225
signal that is proportional to the number of persons in a space to automatically modulate the amount of
15226
outdoor air. Examples of approaches for estimating occupancy include people-counters and carbon
15227
dioxide (CO2) sensors.
15228
15229
Measurement and control of indoor CO2 concentration has been the most popular DCV method because
15230
CO2 sensors and associated controllers are relatively inexpensive and under in carefully controlled
15231
environments (such as environmental chambers) has been shown to correlate well with people-related
15232
contaminant levels. A number of packaged HVAC equipment manufacturers now offer CO2 sensors and
15233
controllers as an option to their equipment. This method is based on the fact that when the
15234
indoor/outdoor CO2 concentration difference is at steady state, the rate of CO2 generation indoors by
15235
occupants is proportional to the number of occupants and their activity level (Appendix C of ASHRAE
15236
Standard 62.1-2007 has an expanded discussion on this topic). The CO2-generation rates listed in
15237
ASHRAE Standard 62.1 are for adults and therefore should not be used for children.
15238
15239
Besides the CO2-based DCV other methods for estimating occupancy variations exist. These methods
15240
include occupancy schedules by time-of-day, direct count of occupants, or estimation of occupancy. In
15241
addition, new technologies are emerging, such as dynamic infrared imaging, enabling automated and
15242
direct count of occupants entering and exiting a space thus may avoid some of the calibration and
15243
accuracy problems associated with CO2 sensors. Some practitioners believe that CO2 is considered a
15244
more direct indicator of the pollutant that the ventilation system is trying to control (i.e., bioeffluents) so
15245
it inherently takes into account the time delays that occur due to space volume when people enter or
15246
leave a space. However, other practitioners believe that ventilation in a space should be increased
15247
immediately as soon as it is occupied.
15248
15249
The DCV controls described above are used to adjust the occupant component of the ventilation rate, the
15250
component intended to dilute pollutants generated by occupants (bioeffluents) and their activities. They
15251
cannot be used to adjust the area-based building component, which is intended to dilute pollutants off-
15252
gassed from building materials, furnishings, cleaning products, etc. In theory it may be possible to
15253
adjust the area-based building component by measuring the concentrations of target building-related
15254
pollutants. However for non-industrial environments, there is no consensus on a list of target building-
15255
related pollutants and associated maximum allowable concentrations that can be used as an indication of
15256
“acceptable” or “healthy” indoor air. Measuring total volatile organic compounds (TVOCs) has been
15257
proposed as an alternative to CO2, but many indoor air quality studies have concluded that there is
15258
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 494
Return to TOP
insufficient evidence that TVOC concentrations can be used to predict health or comfort effects. Until
15259
another indicator of building related pollutant concentration can be developed, the building component
15260
cannot be reduced below design rates.
15261
15262
Appropriate Applications for DCV
15263
DCV is most appropriate in densely occupied spaces [greater than or equal to 25 people per 1000 ft2 (40
15264
square foot per person) at peak] with intermittent or variable population. For these spaces, DCV offers
15265
the potential for both energy savings and improved indoor air quality. The benefit of DCV increases
15266
with the level of density, transiency and cost of energy.
15267
15268
Occupancy categories appropriate for DCV include theaters, auditoriums/public assembly, gyms, some
15269
classrooms, restaurants, office conference rooms, etc. Due to health concerns, densely occupied spaces
15270
with people-related pollutants other than normal bio-effluents, such as waiting areas of health care
15271
facilities, are inappropriate for DCV despite their intermittent or variable population. Densely and
15272
continuously occupied office spaces, such as call centers, are also inappropriate for DCV.
15273
15274
Although the energy conserving benefits of DCV are small in general office building applications and
15275
therefore should not be used in such applications, certain aspects of DCV controls may be beneficial to a
15276
building in ensuring that the proper amount of ventilation is supplied under all operating conditions. For
15277
example, continuous measurement of outdoor airflow rates and indoor CO2 levels can help building
15278
personnel make adjustments to the HVAC system set points thus avoiding large amounts of over
15279
ventilation or under ventilation relative to the applicable code requirements.
15280
15281
Design Considerations for DCV
15282
Section 6.2.7 (Dynamic Reset) of 62.1-2007 allows designers to reset outdoor air intake flow Vot and/or
15283
zone outdoor airflow Voz as operating conditions change based on “variations in occupancy or
15284
ventilation airflow in one or more individual zones for which ventilation airflow requirements will be
15285
reset”. Additionally, Section 6.4.3.8 of ASHRAE Standard 90.1-2004 requires that systems with design
15286
outdoor air capacities greater than 3000 cfm serving areas with average design occupancy densities
15287
exceeding 100 people per 1000 ft2 be equipped with DCV systems.
15288
15289
Section 403.3.1 of the 2006 International Mechanical Code allows that the minimum flow rate of
15290
outdoor air shall be permitted to be based on the actual number of occupants present.
15291
15292
A number of considerations need to be made during the design of any DCV system. These
15293
considerations include:
15294
1. Ventilation for DCV zones: The minimum outdoor airflow required in the breathing zone
15295
supplied for all variable occupancy levels and load conditions must be no less than the sum of
15296
the people total outdoor airflow rate for the zone (Rp\*Pz) plus the area total outdoor airflow rate
15297
(Ra\*Az) as determined from Table 6-1 of 62.1-2007 .
15298
15299
Where:
15300
Rp = people outdoor air rate, determined from Table 6-1
15301
Ra = area outdoor air rate, determined from Table 6-1
15302
Pz = zone population
15303
Az = zone floor area
15304
15305
Diversity (D) cannot be used in determining the number of people. See Appendix A of the 62.1
15306
User’s Guide for more information.
15307
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 495
Return to TOP
2. Minimum Ventilation for DCV zones. The minimum outdoor airflow rate supplied to a space
15308
during regular business hours (irrespective of occupancy) should be no less than:
15309
o Ra times the floor area
15310
o The exhaust airflow prescribed in Section 6.2.8 of 62.1-2007 for single zone and 100%
15311
outdoor air systems. (For multiple-zone systems, outdoor air intake flow must be no less
15312
than the total system exhaust airflow for all zones, to minimize infiltration. More about
15313
multiple-zone systems in DCV in Multiple Zone Systems below.)
15314
o The corrected amount for the calibrated precision of the measuring and controlling
15315
methods employed, as verified by factory calibration to a recognized national reference
15316
standard. Additionally, field calibration should be required to adjust the set points to
15317
account for precision.
15318
3. Pressure controls of DCV systems. DCV systems should be capable of providing stable space
15319
pressurization control and should operate in concert with changes in the ventilation rate as
15320
conditions internal and external to the building change (Dougan, 2004).
15321
4. Variable Air Volume Systems: Utilize continuous measurement and proper control of outdoor
15322
airflow rates to ensure that sufficient ventilation air is supplied to the occupied zone (but may not
15323
be delivered at breathing level if there is poor mixing within the space).
15324
5. Single Zone Constant Volume Systems: Provide a continuous measurement and control of the
15325
outdoor airflow rates or provide a simple damper control as described in Appendix A of the
15326
ASHRAE 62.1 User’s Manual. If a continuous measurement of the outdoor airflow rates is
15327
provided, it should be capable of measuring flow within an accuracy of ±15% of the minimum
15328
outdoor airflow rate.
15329
6. Documentation: As with any other HVAC component, it is important for the designer to develop
15330
written description of the equipment, methods, control sequences and the intended operational
15331
functions necessary for commissioning, reporting, and maintenance of DCV systems.
15332
Commissioning agents need to implement requirements and document the results during building
15333
and IAQ commissioning; maintenance personnel need written guidance on how to properly
15334
maintain the DCV after completion of construction; and health and safety staff need guidance on
15335
how to validate that the proper amount of ventilation is supplied under all variable occupancy
15336
levels and load conditions.
15337
15338
CO2-Based DCV
15339
As mentioned previously, CO2-based DCV is the most popular method of DCV. This is due in part to
15340
their low cost and in part to the fact their control simplicity (since outdoor airflow requirements in
15341
earlier versions of the IMC and 62.1 were prescribed in cfm/person, and since indoor minus outdoor
15342
CO2 concentration, at steady state, is directly proportional to outdoor airflow rate per person if
15343
occupancy is stable, a single CO2 set point could be used to maintain a constant outdoor airflow rate per
15344
person – this approach satisfied earlier versions of the IMC and 62.1). A number of packaged HVAC
15345
equipment manufacturers now offer CO2 sensors and controllers as an option to their equipment. A
15346
USDOE-funded study published in 2004 lists about a dozen manufacturers of CO2 sensors and controls
15347
(FEMP, 2004).
15348
15349
Relationship between Outdoor Air Flow Rate and Steady State CO2 Concentration
15350
The outdoor airflow rate per person can be calculated based on the following equation (ASHRAE,
15351
2007a)
15352
15353
Vo = N/(Cs − Co)
15354
15355
15356
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 496
Return to TOP
Where:
15357
Vo = outdoor air flow rate per person
15358
N = CO2 generation rate per person
15359
Cs = CO2 concentration in the space
15360
Co = CO2 concentration in outdoor air
15361
15362
For sedentary (1.2 mets) adult persons, N=0.31 L/min. Therefore based on a target Vo, a trigger value
15363
for Cs − Co can be set in the CO2 control system.
15364
15365
It is worth pointing out that the generation rate of N=0.31 L/min does not apply to children, or to non-
15366
sedentary adults and therefore it should not be used for these types of occupants. For adults, Figures C.2
15367
and C.3 of ASHRAE Standard 62.1 show the relationship between CO2 generation rate, breathing rate,
15368
and physical activity. The entire Appendix C is shown in Table 1 at the end of this section. Appendix A
15369
of the ASHRAE 62.1-2007 User’s Manual offers guidance on the equations that can be applied for CO2-
15370
based ventilation.
15371
15372
Code and Green Building Requirements for CO2-based DCV
15373
CO2-based DCV is required by some building codes. For example, the California Energy code (Title
15374
24) (California Energy Commission, 2005) requires CO2-based DCV in single-zone high occupancy
15375
areas (≤40 ft2/person) served by HVAC system(s) with outdoor air economizer (a number of exceptions
15376
are listed such as classrooms). Similarly, the Oregon Energy Code requires that all systems with
15377
ventilation capacities of 1,500 cfm or more, serving areas with an average occupant load factor of 20
15378
ft2/person or less include a means to automatically reduce outdoor air intake below design rates when
15379
spaces are partially occupied (Oregon State Energy Office, 2003 and 2007). Also, the US Green
15380
Building Council gives points in its Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED) rating
15381
system for: (a) measuring CO2 concentrations in HVAC systems serving either densely occupied spaces
15382
(25 persons per 1000 ft2 or 40 ft2/person) or all other non-densely spaces; and (b) generating an alarm
15383
when concentrations exceed a pre-specified concentration (US Green Building Council, 2007).
15384
15385
CO2 Sensor Technology, Accuracy and Drift
15386
Most CO2 sensors available on the market today are based on non-dispersive infrared (NDIR)
15387
photometric principle. CO2 absorbs infrared light at 4.26 μm and the amount of absorption increases
15388
with the CO2 concentration (ASHRAE, 2005). However, aging of the infrared light source leads to
15389
sensor drift. In order to address the drifting of the sensors, manufacturers of CO2 sensors developed
15390
“self-calibrating” sensors using various technologies and self calibrating algorithms. These technologies
15391
include using the lowest reading over a period of time, measuring light transmissions at a wavelength
15392
other than that where CO2 and other common air components absorb light, or using a second light source
15393
or splitting the light source to assess aging of the primary light source. A limited multi-month
15394
evaluation of three of these self-calibrating sensors indicated considerable drift for one of the three
15395
sensors (House, 2007; Apte, 2006).
15396
15397
Despite the relatively low cost and short payback of CO2-based DCV, the market has not grown as had
15398
been expected (FEMP, 2004). This is partially due to the limited data on the long-term performance of
15399
these sensors (Apte, 2006). In 2001, NIST researchers published a review of available literature on
15400
CO2-based DCV and also identified knowledge gaps (Emmerich and Persily, 2001). Also in 2006/2007,
15401
LBNL researchers published the results of a pilot study of the accuracy of 44 CO2 sensors located in 9
15402
commercial buildings (Fisk et al., 2007). These studies have indicated that there are numerous issues
15403
that need to be addressed with further research. Some of the reported issues with the CO2 sensors relate
15404
to the accuracy of the sensors while others relate to maintenance/calibration and to the inherent sensor
15405
lag times (time required for concentration to rise to a pre-determined level before ventilation is
15406
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 497
Return to TOP
increased; time constants of rise and decay of occupant-generated CO2 concentrations vary from many
15407
minutes to several hours).
15408
15409
With funding from the California Energy Commission and the Iowa Energy Center, researchers at Iowa
15410
State University are conducting a rigorous multi-month assessment of 15 different models of CO2
15411
sensors over ranges of pressure, temperature, humidity and CO2 concentration that are representative of
15412
HVAC applications. Testing will include an assessment of performance characteristics such as accuracy,
15413
repeatability, linearity and hysteresis (Maxwell, 2007).
15414
15415
Lag between Occupancy and CO2 Concentration
15416
Some practitioners believe that lag times are not a problem if the CO2 tracks the bioeffluents. However,
15417
other practitioners suggest that ventilation be increased immediately after occupancy. Because sensors
15418
responses are typically very slow, and therefore the sensed CO2 value is much lower than the actual
15419
bioeffluent level, resulting in a DCV control system that is probably unstable. Systems with sensor
15420
response time constants in units of “minutes” may be satisfactory, but systems with sensor response time
15421
constants in units of “hours” would not work at all. This is a control system design issue, which must be
15422
addressed during design. Figure 1 below depicts a graphical representation of the lag issue for an
15423
assembly space during varying occupancies. The difference between the “instantaneous ventilation
15424
requirement” and the “approximate ventilation rate for CO2 control”
15425
15426
15427
Figure 8.2-D Lag between Occupancy and Minimum Ventilation
15428
(courtesy of Martha Hewett)
15429
15430
Design Considerations for CO2-Based DCV Systems
15431
It is recommended that the designer makes the following considerations during the design of a CO2-
15432
based DCV system:
15433
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 498
Return to TOP
1. In HVAC systems with open plenum returns, CO2 sensors should be located in the room at the
15434
breathing level and not in return air plenums.
15435
2. In HVAC systems with open plenum returns, a sufficient number of sensors should be placed
15436
within a space in order to get an accurate average space CO2 concentration.
15437
3. In HVAC systems with ducted returns, CO2 sensors may be placed in the return air duct, if the
15438
designer can provide calculations showing that the CO2 measurement in the return duct is
15439
equivalent to breathing level average measurements.
15440
4. In all rooms with CO2 sensors, DCV controls should maintain CO2 concentrations (with respect
15441
to the outdoor air CO2 concentration) between the maximum level expected at design population
15442
and the minimum level expected at minimum population.
15443
5. Outdoor air CO2 concentration should be measured continuously using a CO2 sensor located
15444
within four feet of the outdoor air intake. Alternatively, outdoor air CO2 concentration can be
15445
assumed to constant, provided the constant level is conservatively high and based on recent
15446
historical data for the area where the building is located, e.g., 400 ppm or higher (ASHRAE,
15447
2007b).
15448
6. CO2 sensors should be certified by the manufacturer to have an accuracy of no less than 50 ppm
15449
for concentration ranges typically found in HVAC applications (e.g., 400 to 2000 ppm), be
15450
factory and field calibrated, and be certified by the manufacturer to require calibration no more
15451
frequently than once every 5 years while operating under typical field conditions [limited
15452
research to date indicates that field-based calibration should be performed once every one to two
15453
years (Fisk, 2008)]
15454
7. Design provisions should be provided for periodic maintenance and calibration. This will assist:
15455
(a) maintenance personnel in properly maintaining the DCV; and (b) health and safety personnel
15456
in validating that the proper amount of ventilation is supplied under all variable occupancy levels
15457
and load conditions
15458
15459
Alternatives to CO2-based DCV
15460
In certain limited applications, such as classrooms, where occupancy is either zero or nearly 100%, the
15461
control of outdoor air rate in an on-off manner based on a signal from a room occupancy sensor, time
15462
clock, or a light switch is a practical and energy-saving solution. Other simple forms of DCV may
15463
include direct count of occupants or estimation of occupancy and programming the ventilation supply
15464
accordingly. However, some energy codes, such as the California Title 24, specifically exclude these
15465
types of DCV controls for non-classroom applications (classrooms are exempt from the DCV
15466
requirements of California Title 24). Stanke (2006) has a detailed description of the various DCV
15467
options that a designer can chose for a single zone variable occupancy application.
15468
15469
More sophisticated forms of DCV are based on technologies that can count the number of persons
15470
entering and exiting a space and adjust ventilation accordingly (Apte, 2006). New advances in sensing
15471
and micro computing technologies may automate DCV. Dynamic infrared imaging hardware and
15472
software are emerging technologies with some significant potential advantages. These technologies are
15473
already available for people counting and are used for marketing, security, and facility management
15474
applications. Some of the potential advantages of these technologies may include reduced signal delay
15475
problems (as discussed above) or drifts typically found in chemical sensors. To date these technologies
15476
have not been tested with DCV but a number of research proposals have been written and it is likely that
15477
research in this area will be done in the near future.
15478
15479
15480
15481
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 499
Return to TOP
DCV in Multiple-Zone Systems
15482
Application of DCV in single-zone systems is fairly straight forward. Neither Standard 62.1-2007 nor
15483
the associated User’s Manual offer any advice on the design and operation of DCV for systems that
15484
serve multiple spaces. Stanke (2008) has listed a number of multiple-zone system design and part-load
15485
challenges, including assumptions about occupant/visitor movement (zone to zone, or entering/leaving
15486
the system), occupant diversity, etc. In addition, Warden (2004) has proposed supply air-based CO2 for
15487
multiple space systems
15488
15489
ASHRAE in coordination with designers and researchers will be addressing these multiple-zone system
15490
dynamic reset issues in the future. Further research maybe needed to develop design equations and any
15491
associated new technology for multiple-zone DCV.
15492
15493
The California Energy Commission is proposing to expand its requirement for DCV for single zone,
15494
high occupancy spaces to multiple-zone systems. This proposal does not present the same challenges as
15495
listed above, because Title 24 defines treats outdoor air and recirculated air equally at the zone level.
15496
Therefore if the minimum supply airflow rate meets Title 24’s ventilation rate requirements (either per
15497
person or by area, whichever is greater) then the ventilation requirements are met regardless of the
15498
percentage of outdoor air in the supply air.
15499
15500
15501
15502
Table 1 – Informative Appendix C
15503
Rationale for Minimum Physiological Requirements for Respiration Air Based on
15504
CO2 Concentration
15505
15506
Oxygen is necessary for metabolism of food to sustain life. Carbon and hydrogen in
15507
foods are oxidized to CO2 and H2O, which are eliminated by the body as waste products.
15508
Foods can be classified as carbohydrates, fats, and proteins, and the ratio of carbon to
15509
hydrogen in each is somewhat different. The respiratory quotient (RQ) is the volumetric
15510
ratio of carbon dioxide produced to oxygen consumed. It varies from 0.71 for a diet of
15511
100% fat to 0.8 for a diet of 100% protein and 1.00 for a diet of 100% carbohydrates (see
15512
Reference C-l). A value of RQ = 0.83 applies to a normal diet mix of fat, carbohydrate,
15513
and protein.
15514
15515
The rate at which oxygen is consumed and carbon dioxide is generated depends on
15516
physical activity. These relationships are shown in Figure C.2 (see Reference C-2). The
15517
breathing rate is shown also. A simple mass balance equation gives the outdoor air flow
15518
rate needed to maintain the steady-state CO2 concentration below a given limit.
15519
15520
Vo = N/(Cs − Co) (C-1)
15521
15522
where
15523
Vo = outdoor air flow rate per person
15524
Ve = breathing rate
15525
N = CO2 generation rate per person
15526
Ce = CO2 concentration in exhaled breath
15527
Cs = CO2 concentration in the space
15528
Co = CO2 concentration in outdoor air
15529
15530
15531
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 500
Return to TOP
15532
15533
For example, at an activity level of 1.2 met units (1.0 met = 18.4 Btu/h·ft2),
15534
corresponding to sedentary persons, the CO2 generation rate is 0.31 L/min. Laboratory
15535
and field studies have shown that with sedentary persons about 15 cfm (7.5 L/s) per
15536
person of outdoor air will dilute odors from human bioeffluents to levels that will satisfy
15537
a substantial majority (about 80%) of unadapted persons (visitors) to a space C-3, C-4, C-5, C-
15538
6, C-7. If the ventilation rate is to be held to 15 cfm (7.5 L/s) per person, the resulting
15539
steady-state CO2 concentration relative to that in the outdoor air is,
15540
15541
Cs - Co = N/Vo
15542
= 0.31/(7.5 × 60 s/min)
15543
= 0.000689 liters of CO2 per liter of air
15544
≈ 700 ppm
15545
15546
Thus, maintaining a steady-state CO2 concentration in a space no greater than about 700
15547
ppm above outdoor air levels will indicate that a substantial majority of visitors entering a
15548
space will be satisfied with respect to human bioeffluents (body odor). A more detailed
15549
discussion of this relationship between CO2 concentrations and the perception of
15550
bioeffluents, as well as the use of indoor CO2 to estimate building ventilation rates, is
15551
contained in ASTM Standard D6245.C-8
15552
15553
CO2 concentrations in acceptable outdoor air typically range from 300 to 500 ppm. High
15554
CO2 concentrations in the outdoor air can be an indicator of combustion and/or other
15555
contaminant sources.
15556
15557
Figure C.3 shows the outdoor air flow rate required as a function of physical activity and
15558
steady-state room concentration. If the activity level is greater than 1.2 met, the required
15559
ventilation must be increased to maintain the same carbon dioxide level.
15560
15561
Also the decrease in oxygen content of the room air can be found from Equation C-l
15562
when oxygen concentration is substituted for carbon dioxide concentration.
15563
15564
Co − Cs = N/Vo (C-2)
15565
15566
The term N now has a negative value with respect to its use in Equation C-1 since oxygen
15567
is consumed rather than generated.
15568
15569
Cs = Co − N/Vo (C-3)
15570
15571
15572
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 501
Return to TOP
15573
15574
The oxygen consumption rate is 0.36 L/min when the activity level is 1.2 met. For
15575
ventilation at a rate of 15 cfm (429 L/m) and an activity level of 1.2 met units, the room
15576
oxygen level will be reduced from an outdoor concentration to 20.9%. Thus the oxygen
15577
content of the room is reduced from 21% to 20.9%, a change of only 0.5%. The carbon
15578
dioxide is raised from the background of 0.03% to 0.1%, a change of 230%. Thus
15579
dilution of carbon dioxide is clearly more significant than replacing oxygen.
15580
15581
