15674
Strategy 8.3 – Use Energy Recovery Ventilation where Appropriate
15675
15676
15677
Required by Energy Standards
15678
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 90.1-2007 requires energy recovery for certain large outdoor air systems.
15679
Systems that are designed to provide more than 5000 cfm of supply air with a minimum of 70% outdoor
15680
air are required to have an energy recovery system that is at least 50% effective. Additional
15681
requirements for air-to-air energy recovery are in the process of being added to 90.1. Please refer to the
15682
latest version and addenda.
15683
15684
In addition, many other buildings can benefit from reduction in energy costs by applying air-to-air
15685
energy recovery.
15686
15687
Types of Air to Air Energy Recovery Devices
15688
In general there are two types of devices: total energy recovery ventilators (ERV) that transfer heat and
15689
moisture between incoming and exhaust air and heat recovery ventilators (HRV) that do not transfer
15690
moisture. Air-to-air energy recovery equipment is tested in accordance with ASHRAE Standard 84 and
15691
rated and certified in accordance with ARI 1060. (Residential ERV’s and HRV’s are rated by the Home
15692
Ventilating Institute.) Certified ratings should be used to select and design systems with energy recovery
15693
ventilation. All devices require proper design and application and as with any mechanical equipment, all
15694
require cleaning and maintenance.
15695
15696
General Design Considerations:
15697
1. Provide appropriate filtration for the airstreams. Just like ductwork coils and other parts of the
15698
HVAC system, energy recovery devices must be kept clean to work properly and avoid dirt and
15699
moisture related problems.
15700
2. Controls must be designed for the application. Different energy recovery equipment requires
15701
different controls. Different climates require different controls. Most devices require some
15702
controls to change efficiencies and to change operation during differing weather conditions. If
15703
you have an economizer, the energy recovery device can work against energy savings without a
15704
properly devised control sequence. Certain applications may require bypass dampers for
15705
adequate control
15706
3. Size equipment properly to maximize benefit, meet ventilation requirements, and avoid
15707
pressurization problems. Different devices have different pressure capabilities. Take into
15708
account the effect of unbalanced flows and leakage on airflow and effectiveness. Also note that,
15709
for a given flow, a larger device will recover more energy. In addition, a larger device will
15710
exhibit lower pressure loss. Higher pressure loss uses additional fan energy; selecting a smaller
15711
component will increase fan power and decrease recovered energy.
15712
4. Condensation must be dealt with for almost all equipment (except wheels) in almost any climate.
15713
Severe climates may require frost protection, depending on the frosting threshold, which varies
15714
with the device and application. Frost avoidance is usually handled with controls, manufacturer’s
15715
guidance should be followed.
15716
5. Fouling and corrosion should be considered but are factors usually related to industrial
15717
applications.
15718
6. Selection of the type of energy recovery device should take into account its impact on the
15719
sensible heat ratio of the load. If a large portion of the outside air load is latent, for example, use
15720
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 505
Return to TOP
of a sensible only device will shift the SHR and potentially result in a mismatch between the load
15721
and the capacity of the system.
15722
7. Reduced loads due to the use of energy recovery should be reflected in heating and particularly
15723
in cooling equipment sizing. In addition to the obvious economic implications, failure to resize
15724
may result in oversized systems with shorter run times, decreased energy efficiency and possible
15725
loss of humidity control.
15726
8. In addition to reducing humidity loads and cooling requirements, ERV’s also reduce
15727
requirements for humidification in cold dry climate applications.
15728
15729
Energy Recovery Wheel
15730
Rotating energy recovery wheels work by passing a porous wheel through the outdoor air and exhaust
15731
airstreams transferring heat energy stored on the wheel surfaces. A desiccant coating adsorbs and
15732
desorbs moisture. A total energy recovery wheel is efficient in transferring both heat (sensible energy)
15733
and moisture (latent energy). IAQ considerations include the effects of cross leakage. In most
15734
applications the only impact is to account for leakage in sizing to meet ventilation requirements. If
15735
necessary, there are a variety of methods for dealing with cross leakage, including fan placement,
15736
differential pressures or a purge section. Cross leakage from leakage and carryover is quantified in
15737
ratings of the ERV. As with any other rotating device, structural integrity during installation is very
15738
important to proper operation.
15739
15740
Fixed Plate with Latent Transfer
15741
Another energy recovery ventilator is a fixed plate with latent transfer. In this device a permeable
15742
membrane separates the airstreams. Heat is transferred through the membrane. The membrane is
15743
designed to let water pass through but block other molecules from passing through. Different
15744
membranes may pass different molecules.
15745
15746
Fixed Plate
15747
A fixed plate heat recovery ventilator (HRV) transfers sensible energy only and will have little or no
15748
crossover between airstreams as the air is separated by metal plates that transfer the heat energy from the
15749
separated airstreams. If it is designed to be condensing, it is important that it drains properly and that air
15750
is well filtered to avoid fouling or clogging. If the application is non-condensing, the device is very
15751
reliable having no moving parts.
15752
15753
Heat Pipe
15754
A heat pipe transfers sensible energy only and works with a fluid that evaporates and condenses at
15755
temperatures consistent with the application. Similar to air conditioning and refrigeration, there are
15756
fixed temperature limits for different fluids. In comfort cooling and heating applications, heat pipes
15757
need a tilt control to change over from heating to cooling. The tilt control can also be used in the frost
15758
control scheme as well as to heat exchange in the wrong direction during transition seasons.
15759
15760
Runaround Loops
15761
Coil energy recovery or runaround loops transfer sensible energy only and consist of a coil in the
15762
outdoor airstream and another coil in the exhaust airstream. A fluid is pumped through the coils to
15763
transfer heat energy. A three way valve is used for control. This device is also very simple and uses
15764
common HVAC components, coils, pipe, valves and a pump.
15765
15766
15767
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 506
Return to TOP
15768
Strategy 8.4 – Use Natural or Mixed Mode Ventilation where Appropriate
15769
15770
[This section draws heavily on the CIBSE publication “National Ventilation in Non-Domestic
15771
Buildings” (AM10), which is the principal guidance document on the application of natural
15772
ventilation. More information on AM10 is available at www.cibse.org/. The document is also
15773
available at the ASHRAE bookstore at www.ashrae.org. Material based on this document is identified
15774
below with the following symbol – CIBSE AM10.]
15775
15776
Natural / Mixed Mode Ventilation System
15777
Design principles
15778
Natural ventilation systems are intended to provide sufficient outside air to achieve appropriate
15779
standards of air quality and to provide cooling when needed. Since the cooling capacity of natural
15780
ventilation is limited by the temperature of outdoor air, a key design challenge is to limit heat gains
15781
through good solar control and careful management of the internal gains. Naturally conditioned
15782
buildings do not aim to achieve constant environmental conditions, but take advantage of, and adapt to,
15783
dynamic outdoor conditions to provide a comfortable, controllable indoor environment for the occupants.
15784
This deviation from the accepted norm will require the buildings owner’s approval and possibly
15785
education of building operating staff and end users.
15786
15787
A key aspect of natural ventilation is generally to empower the occupant to make suitable adjustments to
15788
window opening, shading devices, etc. to maintain personal comfort without diminishing the comfort of
15789
others or wasting energy. This may mean that automatic control strategies are carefully integrated with
15790
user behavior.
15791
15792
There are many different types of natural ventilation strategies. The most appropriate strategy depends
15793
on the type of space (i.e. open plan, cellular), use of the space (i.e. classroom, gym, office, etc.) and
15794
whether wind or buoyancy forces are likely to predominate.
15795
15796
Naturally ventilated buildings are becoming more common in North America in part because of the
15797
increase in the cost of energy and the desire to reduce greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions. Having stated
15798
this, care needs to be taken in the design and application of natural ventilation systems to ensure that the
15799
desired energy and GHG emission reduction are achieved. For example, it may be necessary to design
15800
automatic heating and/or cooling interlock control on ventilation openings to prevent higher than
15801
required outside air quantities, with uncontrolled space heating/ cooling, consequently causing an
15802
increase in energy consumption as compared to a convention mechanically ventilated building.
15803
15804
As global climate change progresses, and outside temperatures experience greater variation, achieving
15805
good summertime thermal comfort with low energy consumption will become increasingly challenging
15806
for both naturally and mechanically ventilated buildings. Regardless of how the climate changes, natural
15807
ventilation should continue to play an important role as a lead strategy, particularly in locations with
15808
more moderate climate. As the climate warms, a mitigating factor could be that the occupants will adapt
15809
to the warmer outside temperatures and find warmer inside temperatures more acceptable.
15810
15811
A naturally ventilated and cooled building can provide benefits to its users, its owners, and to society as
15812
a whole. The following provides a summary of some benefits and the goals that this strategy can help to
15813
achieve.
15814
• With the threat of climate change, many local, state, and national governments are taking action
15815
to reduce GHG emissions. Using natural ventilation cooling in lieu of a traditional mechanical
15816
system can reduce the energy required to operate a building and hence reduce GHG emissions.
15817
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 507
Return to TOP
• An entirely passive natural ventilation system can eliminate the energy for cooling normally
15818
associated with fans, chillers, cooling towers, and pumps, while a hybrid system requires some
15819
energy to supplement outdoor air delivery and/or cooling under extreme conditions.
15820
• Naturally ventilation buildings can provide a superior living or working environment for building
15821
users, by giving occupants greater control over their comfort.
15822
• Such buildings can provide a healthy and stimulating working or living space with reduced
15823
operating and maintenance costs.
15824
15825
Comfort expectations
15826
Despite the fact that naturally ventilated and cooled buildings cannot maintain as tight a temperature
15827
control as a typical mechanical system, studies have shown high levels of occupant satisfaction. This is
15828
because occupants are given control over their surroundings and have ample access to fresh, clean air.
15829
Recent research in this area is incorporated into the ASHRAE 90.1-2004 Adaptive Comfort model.
15830
If it is too hot or stuffy occupants can open a window, and then close the window when the temperature
15831
drops. A certain level of user buy-in is required; when building occupants have direct access to ambient
15832
weather conditions they tend to dress appropriate to the natural climate. In the summer they might wear
15833
short sleeves, and in the winter they might put on a sweater. This adaptation allows a naturally ventilated
15834
and cooled building to expand the normal indoor temperature range without sacrificing comfort levels.
15835
In an environment where occupant dress is dictated by policy, it becomes more critical that occupants be
15836
able to control their environment. This can be achieved by avoiding automated window controls and
15837
allowing the occupants to decide when to open and close windows, or with the inclusion of a hybrid
15838
system that can maintain space temperatures via mechanical cooling.
15839
15840
A key criterion when assessing overheating is to define the thermal comfort conditions that are
15841
considered acceptable. Thermal comfort is a complex mix of physiology, psychology and culture. What
15842
is deemed acceptable will depend on activity and clothing level as well as temperatures, air speeds and
15843
humidity. Occupants who have more control over clothing, activity level and air speed will find a
15844
broader range of temperature and relative humidity acceptable. a quote from the CIBSE Application
15845
Manual
15846
15847
It is clear that a naturally ventilated building will deliver a more variable temperature than an air
15848
conditioned building, but that does not necessarily mean that occupants will be less comfortable. In
15849
summer, increased air movement from large openings can provide an enhanced perception of thermal
15850
comfort, but consideration should be given to nuisance draughts, such as those that might blow papers
15851
off desks.
15852
15853
Good natural conditioning design will also provide occupants with so-called adaptive opportunities to
15854
adjust their situation in response to changing conditions. This might involve:
15855
• using blinds or other moveable shading to cut out direct solar radiation
15856
• providing the opportunity to increase air movement by opening windows or the use of desk fans.
15857
15858
Coupled with less formal dress codes, these opportunities allow occupants to adjust their environment to
15859
suit their own preferences, allowing greater freedom of choice than is usually practical in an air
15860
conditioned environment. However, this does require careful design of the windows, their position and
15861
opening mechanisms, such that occupants can readily adapt their environment.
15862
15863
Another important factor is that recent research has shown that in prolonged spells of warm weather,
15864
people's expectations for comfort change.
15865
15866
Figure 8.4-E shows the effect of air speed on thermal comfort.
15867
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 508
Return to TOP
15868
15869
Figure 8.4-E Effect of Air Speed
15870
(Source: CIBSE Application Manual AM10 Fig 2.4)
15871
15872
Integrated design
15873
An integrated design approach is essential for successful implementation of natural ventilation, since it
15874
crosses traditional boundaries of design responsibility and impacts virtually all disciplines including
15875
architectural, mechanical, electrical, interior design, and acoustics. Natural ventilation design also
15876
requires an integrated approach to the division of responsibility among members of the design team,
15877
component suppliers and system installers.
15878
15879
It is vital to involve all design team disciplines in these discussions. Successful natural ventilation and
15880
cooling systems often involve cost trade-offs between disciplines. For example, the mechanical system
15881
will likely be smaller and less expensive, but the structural budget might increase in order to provide
15882
more thermal mass, or the need for operable windows might increase the architectural costs. The earlier
15883
in the process the entire design team has a common understanding of the intent of the building, the
15884
easier it will be to incorporate the necessary elements into the design at the lowest overall cost.
15885
15886
Before design begins it is of utmost importance to acquire buy-in from the building owner/operator. The
15887
design team and client need to agree on comfort standards for the occupants of the buildings. For
15888
example, if building occupants are given control over operable windows and are allowed to adapt their
15889
dress to weather conditions, usually the acceptable comfortable temperature range can be expanded
15890
without sacrificing occupant satisfaction. If possible, choose a standard, such as Section 5.3 or ASHRAE
15891
55-2004 or the Bounding Comfort Parameters from LEED-NC 2.1 to guide the thermal comfort
15892
performance requirements for the design.
15893
It is also important that the building owner understand the design intent of the active and passive
15894
building systems and that the building users and maintenance staff are educated on the proper use of the
15895
systems to maintain comfort (i.e. opening and closing solar shades, opening windows for night flushing,
15896
closing windows in the winter to reduce heating requirements).
15897
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 509
Return to TOP
It is a good idea to gather and analyze typical local weather data before design begins, including hourly
15898
ambient temperature, humidity, and wind speed and direction. Combining this knowledge with the
15899
thermal comfort criteria can lead to an initial assessment regarding the necessity of supplementary
15900
cooling.
15901
15902
Applications for Natural Ventilation Cooling
15903
The following flow chart from CIBSE Application Manual AM10 Fig 2.8 shows how critical design
15904
related issues/ constraints need to be considered during the design of a naturally ventilated and cooled
15905
building;
15906
15907
15908
Figure 8.4-F Flow chart that takes the user through a broad-brush
15909
decision tree to identify the most appropriate forms of ventilation.
15910
(Source: CIBSE Application Manual AM10 Fig 2.8)
15911
15912
Even in a well designed passively-cooled building there may be a few times a year when outdoor
15913
temperatures remain elevated for a prolonged period and thus indoor temperatures rise above the
15914
comfort range. The building owner and design team must decide if it is acceptable to tolerate these
15915
infrequent “peak” periods, or if a hybrid system should be installed instead to add mechanical cooling
15916
during the peak periods. Thermal modeling software can help predict the amount of time that elevated
15917
temperatures occur and to assist in this decision and building design in general.
15918
One of the greatest advantages of natural ventilation is its simplicity. Although it is possible to design a
15919
complex, fully automated system, often the best designs rely on the building occupants to control their
15920
own conditions. From an operations and maintenance standpoint there is minimal effort required: no
15921
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 510
Return to TOP
cooling towers, pumps, fans, piping, or duct-work. Again, a hybrid system would include some of this
15922
equipment, but overall it would be a relatively simple system to maintain.
15923
Finally, a natural ventilation and cooling system may open new design avenues not previously available.
15924
There is no duct work to hide in soffit or drop-ceilings and less mechanical space required. This may
15925
allow for more usable space, greater floor-to-ceiling height, or no ugly cooling towers to be tucked away
15926
on the roof or on the grounds.
15927
The following sections describe when and where natural ventilation and cooling systems are applicable
15928
and their interactions with other aspects of the building design.
15929
15930
Appropriate Climatic Conditions
15931
Natural ventilation and cooling systems are most effective in climates where ambient temperatures and
15932
humidity levels naturally fall into comfortable ranges. Additionally, consideration of wind pattern,
15933
diurnal temperature swings and outdoor pollution should be considered. In less humid environments
15934
outdoor air typically drops sufficiently at night to enable a night-time cooling effect to “flush-out” the
15935
heat that has been stored inside a building and pre-cool it for the next day.
15936
Additionally, so called “micro-climate” issues should be considered during design. These can vary from
15937
the shading due to foliage to the wind effect of surrounding buildings.
15938
The combination of favorable climate and location makes much of the Pacific Northwest and other
15939
coastal regions, as well as some higher altitude mountain areas in the West ideal locations for the
15940
implementation
15941
of natural ventilation and cooling.
15942
15943
Appropriate Building Programming
15944
It is important to know the intended building programming before the design begins. Building
15945
programming in this sense refers to the periods of occupancy in a building and the corresponding
15946
intended use. For example, the natural ventilation and cooling strategy for an office building with only
15947
daytime use would be very different from a 24-hour fitness facility. Nearly any type of building can take
15948
advantage of natural ventilation and cooling, but there are some conditions that greatly benefit or hinder
15949
a properly functioning system.
15950
The following list of programming conditions should be considered and mitigated in the design and use
15951
of a naturally ventilated and cooled facility:
15952
• Ventilation to maintain adequate levels of indoor air quality
15953
• Highly concentrated cooling loads (i.e. data centers): Typically, if these are in the design, an air
15954
conditioning unit will be included to serve only these spaces. Another option uses an exhaust fan
15955
to pull cooler air in from adjacent spaces. Such spaces may offer an opportunity for heat
15956
recovery.
15957
• Security issues: If relying on using operable windows during unoccupied hours (i.e. night
15958
flushing), it is important to take into account security concerns. Typically 1st floor windows
15959
should not be left open at night, thus the design may require louvers at lower building elevations
15960
or may not make use of those openings.
15961
• Occupant dress code: It is most beneficial if the users of the building are able to wear
15962
“seasonally appropriate” attire to maximize their comfort (i.e. short sleeves, sweaters, etc.). If
15963
uniform requirements make this unacceptable, extra attention needs to be paid to giving the
15964
occupants a high level of control over their indoor environment. This can mean easy access to
15965
operable windows or using a hybrid system to more tightly control temperatures.
15966
• High occupancy periods: For areas such as a conference room or dining area where, for short
15967
periods of time the space is fully occupied but that is not the typical condition, there may be a
15968
“recovery” period required for the space to flush out the built-up cooling load before it can be
15969
fully occupied again. If this is not possible, supplementary cooling may be required. If
15970
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 511
Return to TOP
incorporated properly into the overall space design, a ceiling fan can make a space feel much
15971
more comfortable without adding mechanical cooling.
15972
• Noise issue: To maximize the effectiveness of a natural ventilation and cooling system it is
15973
important to allow for free air flow across spaces, facilitated by unobstructed paths from one side
15974
of a space to the other. Depending on the floor layout and design this may mean there can be
15975
noise pollution from adjacent spaces or the outside environment. Sometimes a little “ambient
15976
noise” can be beneficial as with no mechanical system humming away in the back ground
15977
occupants may be more sensitive to surrounding conversations or noises. But, naturally
15978
ventilated buildings often include large areas of exposed concrete in order to increase the thermal
15979
capacity of the space. Such large areas of hard surface will require careful attention to achieve a
15980
satisfactory internal acoustic environment.
15981
• Indoor air quality (IAQ): Typically IAQ improves with a naturally ventilated and cooled building,
15982
due to the significant increase in fresh air. However, the design team should be aware of the
15983
surrounding environmental conditions, such as idling vehicles or allergenic-pollen producing
15984
foliage when planning a naturally ventilated building. Typically it is not possible to practically
15985
filter outdoor air in a natural ventilation and cooling system without a fan-assist.
15986
• Smoke control: since smoke can follow natural ventilation paths, the integration of the fire safety
15987
strategy must be an important part of design for natural ventilation.
15988
• Health and safety: many natural ventilation openings will be at significant heights above floor
15989
level and so the proposed Work at Heights Regulations will be particularly relevant.
15990
• When existing buildings are being renovated one should consider the form, structure and siting
15991
of the building. They may prove to be either a constraint or an opportunity for natural ventilation.
15992
For example, many older buildings have high-mass structural components – masonry, concrete
15993
and brick. These provide thermal mass that can potentially enhance the effectiveness of a natural
15994
ventilation design. On the other hand, challenges are introduced if the existing building is
15995
oriented on a north/south axis such that the east and west façades experience large afternoon
15996
solar heat gains during the summer.
15997
• Facilities with daytime occupancy only and with cooler nighttime temperatures can be ideal
15998
candidates for natural ventilation since they can take advantage of night flush out. Areas that are
15999
primarily unoccupied during the night can be flushed out and pre-cooled for the following day.
16000
However, if there are work spaces that are occupied around-the-clock, supplemental cooling may
16001
be required during the day, as flush out during the night may cause discomfort if temperatures
16002
drop too low. A significant advantage of 24-hour facilities is that security concerns may be
16003
lessened, particularly if there is always some one “on-duty.” This should be evaluated on a case-
16004
by-case basis.
16005
16006
Space Conditioning: Control of summer overheating
16007
Perhaps the single biggest issue that influences the technical viability of natural ventilation for the
16008
purpose of space conditioning is summertime temperatures. The cooling potential of natural ventilation
16009
is limited by the outdoor temperature, and in some prevailing climates, by occupant expectations of
16010
thermal comfort. In suitable climates, and with adaptive occupant expectations, as a rule of thumb, it is
16011
generally agreed that natural conditioning systems can meet total heat loads averaged over the day of
16012
around 30-40 W/ m2 (i.e. solar plus internal gains).
16013
16014
In most cases, achieving acceptable summer conditions requires three main features in the design and
16015
use of the building:
16016
• good solar control to prevent excessive solar gains entering the occupied space
16017
• modest levels of internal gains (people, small power loads and lighting loads)
16018
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 512
Return to TOP
• acceptance that during peak summer conditions, temperatures in the space will exceed 25 ˚C for
16019
some periods of time; air temperatures may be higher still, but in a well-designed building, such
16020
higher air temperatures will be offset by cooler mean radiant temperatures and enhanced air
16021
movement.
16022
16023
Acoustic performance
16024
The presence of significant external noise sources can inhibit the application of natural ventilation.
16025
There are a number of solutions to this problem.
16026
• Place the ventilation inlets on the sides of the building away from the principal noise sources. If
16027
the noise source is road traffic, this has the added benefit of locating the ventilation inlets away
16028
from the source of pollutants.
16029
• Baffle the external noise by the use of suitable vegetation and/ or physical acoustic barriers.
16030
16031
The other important acoustic issue is that of absorbing internally generated noise in spaces with large
16032
areas of hard surface, which are often associated with thermally massive buildings. The use of absorbent
16033
partitions or decorative hangings is one approach; another is to use carefully profiled ceilings that result
16034
in acoustic focus points below the absorbent floor level.
16035
16036
IAQ Considerations in System Selection
16037
The principal role of ventilation is to provide an appropriate level of indoor air quality (IAQ) by
16038
removing and diluting airborne contaminants. Higher rates of ventilation may be provided than proposed
16039
in the applicable ventilation standard, and this may enhance the perception of freshness, but in most
16040
cases this will come at a price because energy costs will increase correspondingly.
16041
16042
[Note: Guidance required on achieving adequate levels of IAQ (to avoid mold growth and health
16043
hazards]
16044
16045
A three-stage strategy is as follows:
16046
1. Extract ventilation: to remove pollutants at source, with the extracted air being replaced with
16047
outside air.
16048
2. Dilution ventilation: (supply and extract): to disperse and dilute other pollutants.
16049
3. Purge ventilation: to aid removal of high concentrations of pollutants released from occasional
16050
activities such us painting, or the accidental release via spillages, etc. Purge ventilation is
16051
typically an order of magnitude greater than background ventilation. As well as helping to
16052
remove high levels of contaminants, purge ventilation can also help to remove excess heat from
16053
the space, thereby assisting thermal comfort in summer.
16054
16055
The dilution ventilation rates in U.S. codes and standards typically vary from about 5 to 20 CFM per
16056
person (ref to 62.1 and IMC). Since naturally ventilated buildings cannot provide a constant ventilation
16057
rate, it is necessary to consider what level of dilution ventilation and what overall air quality has been
16058
provided.
16059
16060
[Question: Can we discuss the lowest ventilation rates that will be achieved by the system, what levels
16061
are appropriate, what is allowed by code and by Standard 62, etc. In other words, how do you set a
16062
design criterion for NV or MMV?]
16063
16064
The level of dilution ventilation achieved by the natural ventilation system in actual operation may be
16065
compared to that provided using a constant ventilation rate. One way of doing this is to use the CO2
16066
level in the space as a proxy for ventilation rate. By calculation, the CO2, levels in the occupied space
16067
can be determined based on a constant ventilation rate of 20 CFM per person during occupied hours.
16068
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 513
Return to TOP
The naturally ventilated design could be evaluated on the basis of the average CO2, concentration during
16069
occupied hours compared to that achieved by the mechanically ventilated design,
16070
16071
In a similar way, if the volume of the space is sufficiently large, then the pollutants from the activities in
16072
the space will affect the IAQ in the occupied zone more slowly, especially if a pure displacement type
16073
ventilation strategy is adopted, with the pollutants being concentrated in a stratified layer above
16074
occupant level. As an illustration, consider ventilating a theatre, where there the design occupancy is
16075
1000 people. This occupancy will only last for the duration of the performance, and will build up to that
16076
peak for the hour or two preceding 'curtain-up'.
16077
16078
Control of ventilation
16079
If natural ventilation is adopted, the system must be able to provide controllable ventilation rates across
16080
a wide range, from say 0.5 to 5 ACH or even more. Indeed, it should be possible to shut down the
16081
ventilation rate to near zero when the building is unoccupied, especially if occupancy is the principal
16082
source of pollutants. The wide range of flow rate that is required means that the different modes of
16083
ventilation (whole-building, purge, etc.) are likely to be provided via different devices such as trickle
16084
ventilators, opening windows and/or purpose provided ventilators. Natural and mixed mode ventilation
16085
designs require a high degree of integration between the facade design and the control strategy.
16086
16087
As well as providing the required ventilation rates, the ventilators should be designed so as to minimize
16088
discomfort from draughts, especially in winter. In office-type buildings, this usually involves placing the
16089
inlets at high level, typically 1.7 m or more above floor level.
16090
16091
Complementary Design Techniques
16092
There are many synergies between natural ventilation and cooling systems and other building design
16093
techniques. Often it is possible to take advantage of “double-duty” design to get several functions out of
16094
one building element, thus reducing costs and increasing functionality.
16095
16096
The following list is of design techniques that mesh with a natural ventilation and cooling design. It will
16097
not always be possible to include them all in a building, but as many as possible should be considered as
16098
part of the design:
16099
• Operable windows: The key feature of naturally ventilated and cooled spaces is occupant control of
16100
windows (including operable louvers/dampers). Operable windows on opposite sides of a space
16101
allow for cross ventilation when a breeze blows. High and low combinations of operable windows
16102
create stack-effect ventilation, caused by temperature stratification and pressure differentials. Ideally
16103
a space should have both cross and stack ventilation.
16104
• Radiant heating: Because there is no mechanical air delivery there are limited options to condition
16105
the space during the heating season. It is often reasonable to use radiant slab heating. If needed,
16106
supplementary perimeter heating can be added. Minimum ventilation air can be directed through a
16107
heating coil or radiator before entering the space. There can be significant cost savings in using
16108
radiant heating (either in-slab or over-head) to condition large open spaces (such as fire station
16109
garages) because rather than heating all of the air in the space (including the exhaust and make-up
16110
air), heat is provided directly to the occupants and work areas. Another advantage of in-slab radiant
16111
heating is that because there is no ductwork or exposed mechanical equipment overhead or on the
16112
garage floor there is minimal risk of trucks or fire-fighting equipment colliding with the mechanical
16113
system. Garage space comfort requirements are also typically less stringent than other regularly
16114
occupied spaces. This enables the designer to design for a lower winter set-point temperature saving
16115
energy. In addition, providing a uniform floor temperature is less critical. These together allow the
16116
designer to specify less total embedded hydronic tubing in the floor slab (i.e. wider spaced tubing),
16117
reducing construction costs.
16118
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 514
Return to TOP
• Radiant cooling (hybrid systems only): In a hybrid system most of the cooling will still be passive,
16119
but during peak times radiant cooling can keep the spaces comfortable. If a radiant slab or panel is
16120
used for heating it can be switched over during the summer months to provide cooling, though it has
16121
to be closely controlled to prevent condensation. Climates well situated for chilled slabs or panels
16122
typically have low humidity levels in summer, thus allowing slab or panel temperatures to remain
16123
lower and more cooling effect to be provided without risking condensation. Chilled slabs are very
16124
effective when used to prevent solar load striking a floor from heating the space. Figure 18.4-G
16125
illustrates the use of hybrid natural ventilation and a chilled slab system.
16126
16127
16128
Figure 8.4-G Hybrid system combining natural ventilation
16129
with chilled slab (Courtesy of Stantec)
16130
16131
• Shading: The best way to keep a space cool is to never allow it to get hot in the first place.
16132
Optimized active or passive shading can help counteract the increased solar gain caused by windows,
16133
and is crucial in any natural ventilation and cooling design.
16134
• Thermal mass: This is one of the oldest yet most effective passive building design techniques.
16135
Buildings with heavy construction and exposed thermal mass (concrete, brickwork, rock, etc.) can
16136
greatly reduce their peak cooling loads by allowing the material in the building to store energy.
16137
During the night, the building cools off (especially if assisted with night flushing), and then during
16138
the day it slowly absorbs the heat, resulting in a space that does not feel as hot. After a long spell of
16139
hot days, the effect of thermal mass may become minimal if the building never gets a chance to cool
16140
down.
16141
• Daylighting: This technique uses the natural sunlight, rather than electric lighting to provide light to
16142
a space. It can save significantly on electricity usage and reduce cooling loads. On the surface it
16143
seems that daylighting is counter-productive to a natural ventilation and cooling system. Daylighting
16144
requires sunlight, while natural ventilation and cooling prefer to have it blocked out. In reality these
16145
strategies can co-exist and benefit each other. This is because a key element of daylighting is glare
16146
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 515
Return to TOP
control, meaning preventing direct sunlight from striking a surface. This also prevents direct solar
16147
gain. Often shading devices can double as daylighting features by allowing in diffuse light, or
16148
bouncing light off of the ceiling. Figure 8.4-H illustrates the combination of daylighting with proper
16149
shading and natural ventilation.
16150
16151
Figure 8.4-H Illustration of daylighting used in combination with
16152
shading and natural ventilation (Courtesy of Stantec)
16153
16154
Selecting a Natural Ventilation concept
16155
If on the basis of the above considerations it is concluded that natural ventilation is viable, either as the
16156
sole strategy or as a major component of a mixed mode approach, the next stage is to develop the design
16157
concept. Three basic steps need to be considered.
16158
1. To define the desired air flow pattern from the ventilation inlets through the occupied space to
16159
the exhaust: this will be a function of the form and organization of the building, which in turn
16160
will depend on its intended use and the details of the site. For example, if a heavily congested
16161
road bounds one side of the site, then it would be inadvisable to draw air into the building from
16162
that side because of the problems of pollution from vehicle exhausts.
16163
16164
2. To identify the principal driving forces which will enable the desired\* pattern to be achieved:
16165
certain strategies tend to be driven by wind pressures, others by temperature differences. In some
16166
cases, these natural forces will need to be supplemented with fans. Good design will ensure that
16167
the dominating driving forces are in sympathy with the intended flow distribution.
16168
16169
3. To size and locate the openings such that the required air flow rates can be delivered under all
16170
operating regimes: this is in itself a three-stage process:
16171
a. The flow rates have to be determined based on the air quality and thermal comfort
16172
requirements defined in the brief.
16173
b. The openings have to be sized and located to deliver those flows under design conditions.
16174
c. A control system must be specified to maintain the required flowrates under varying
16175
weather and occupancy conditions. Section 4 of the CIBSE AM10; 2005 manual gives
16176
details of the calculation methods that can be used at this stage of design.
16177
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 516
Return to TOP
16178
Design Options
16179
The first consideration is to plan the flow path through the building. This involves much more than
16180
drawing a series of arrows on a sectional view of the building. The form of the building has to be
16181
designed to facilitate the chosen strategy; the strategy then has to be engineered to ensure the air can
16182
flow along the chosen path at the required flow rates under the naturally available driving pressures. It
16183
should be appreciated that the building will need to operate in a number of different modes. In the
16184
heating season, driving forces will tend to be strong and the building will need to operate in 'minimum
16185
fresh air' mode. During mid-season, the building will move into a cooling mode in which outside air can
16186
be used to provide the cooling but driving forces will reduce. During peak summer conditions, outside
16187
air temperatures will exceed internal temperatures; this will reverse the direction of the stack-induced
16188
pressures but will also change the ventilation effect from cooling to heating. Therefore the building may
16189
need to revert to operating in minimum fresh air mode.
16190
16191
Fresh air must flow from outside, through the occupied areas and then to the exhaust point. The pattern
16192
of air flow must be considered for all operational regimes, winter and summer, as well as special
16193
operating modes such as cooling by night ventilation. This must include consideration of the impact of
16194
variations in weather conditions. For example, although one wind direction might predominate, the
16195
strategy will have to be sufficiently robust to work in all likely weather conditions. As well as the
16196
general pattern of air movement through the building, the needs of the occupants and the way in which
16197
the building is to be used.
16198
16199
Floor plan depth can be one of the most important consideration that affects the potential for
16200
daylighting, exterior views and natural ventilation. Floor plans with relatively narrow wings, such as I-,
16201
H-, U-, or T-shaped plans, ensure that most interior spaces have good access to natural light and winds.
16202
Courtyards and atria can also be used to bring light and air to surrounding narrow spaces.
16203
16204
The area of interior space that can be daylight using windows depends on both building depth and floor-
16205
to-ceiling height. Single-story buildings and the top floors of multi-story buildings can be top lit using
16206
skylights, roof monitors or light wells.
16207
16208
Since useful daylight from typical windows can only reach 15 to 25 ft. into spaces with 8 or 9 ft. floor-
16209
to-ceiling heights, floor plans deeper than ~ 56 ft. (two rooms flanking a double-loaded corridor) will
16210
require constant electric lighting. Redirecting daylight with light shelves, prismatic glazing and other
16211
reflective systems can extend naturally lit interior space to 30 to 35 ft. deep.
16212
16213
An occupant’s view of the exterior depends on the distance from the window, the visible transmissivity
16214
of the glazing, and obstructions to light. To ensure good views for most occupants:
16215
•
Limit the maximum distance of workstations from the building exterior to 20 to 25 ft.
16216
•
Use atria and outdoor courtyards to increase the variety and number of views.
16217
16218
A well-designed natural cooling strategy can be as effective as mechanical air-conditioning, but its
16219
potential is also greatly influenced by floor plan depth.
16220
16221
Narrow floor plans increase the potential for effective cross-ventilation: bringing outdoor air into one
16222
side of a space and exhausting it on an adjacent or opposite side. Cross-ventilation can move air
16223
effectively over deep floor plans, but air temperature increases and air quality drops as it moves across
16224
the room. The practical limit for the length of the airflow path is five times the ceiling height (~50 ft. for
16225
a ten ft. ceiling).
16226
16227
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 517
Return to TOP
Single-sided ventilation, where only one exterior wall has operable windows or vents, is also possible
16228
but less effective, since air speed (with its cooling effect) is typically lower than in cross-ventilation
16229
situations.
16230
16231
With a single operable window or vent, natural ventilation relies on wind turbulence and buoyancy,
16232
instead of the higher pressures available from wind. In single-sided ventilation, air flows in the bottom,
16233
is heated within the space, and flows out at the top of the same opening. The larger the height between
16234
the top and bottom, and the higher the temperature change, the greater the airflow.
16235
16236
Single-sided, single-opening natural ventilation is effective to a depth of approximately two times the
16237
ceiling height. This implies a maximum room depth of approximately 18 to 20 ft., for 9 to 10 foot
16238
ceiling height with a window approximately five foot high.
16239
16240
Where separate high and low openings are used, warm air leaves through the upper vent, inducing
16241
inflow through the lower vent. In this situation, if the vertical separation between the openings is
16242
approximately 5 ft., ventilation is effective for up to 2.5 times the ceiling height. This gives a maximum
16243
room depth of 23 to 25 ft.
16244
16245
Cautions
16246
•
Energy savings from day lighting depend on skylights and windows and electric lighting controls
16247
that must be properly commissioned.
16248
•
Energy savings from natural cooling require that supplementary air-conditioning systems are
16249
turned off when windows are open.
16250
16251
Unacceptable noise and indoor air pollution are possible when openings are close to major traffic routes.
16252
Traffic noise and pollution diminish significantly above the third floor.
16253
16254
16255
Figure 8.4-I
16256
Source: Figure from Santa Monica Green Building Program
16257
http://greenbuildings.santa-monica.org/index.html
16258
16259
The layout and shape of the interior determine the effectiveness of daylighting and natural ventilation.
16260
The depth of daylight distribution is a function of window height relative to the working plane.
16261
Increased ceiling height gives more flexibility, allowing devices such as light shelves to bounce light
16262
deeper into the interior.
16263
•
Design ceiling heights 9 ft. 6 in. or greater to permit the use of taller windows.
16264
•
Separate the window into upper and lower portions, to independently control daylight, natural
16265
ventilation and view.
16266
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 518
Return to TOP
•
Use light colors on interior surfaces, especially walls and ceilings, to increase the daylight that
16267
reaches areas remote from windows.
16268
•
Use splayed, light-colored window sills and reveals to reduce contrast and glare.
16269
•
Align interior partitions perpendicular to windows to avoid blocking daylight.
16270
•
Shape the ceiling and use secondary reflecting surfaces to further diffuse daylight.
16271
16272
Cross-ventilation depends on a continuous airflow path. Open-plan layouts are ideal, but where spaces
16273
are subdivided, rooms must be designed to allow airflow in and out, between rooms and through
16274
occupied areas.
16275
•
Locate larger spaces on the windward side of the building. This provides improved air
16276
distribution in all linked interior spaces.
16277
•
Use false ceilings above corridors and passageways to create breezeways for airflow between
16278
rooms on either side of the passage.
16279
•
Use operable transom windows in walls to permit a free flow of air and daylight.
16280
•
Use high ceilings to allow heated air to rise out of occupied zones.
16281
16282
Noise within the building from occupants, HVAC equipment and plumbing is a common irritant and is
16283
usually caused by poor sound isolation in floors and walls, or by poor equipment selection.
16284
The integrity of acoustical partitioning can be achieved by:
16285
•
Extending partitions above the suspended ceiling.
16286
•
Eliminating holes for ducts, pipes, conduits, cables, etc., and sealing around necessary ones.
16287
•
Minimizing sound transmission through air ducts passing through the partition with baffles &
16288
duct liners.
16289
•
Isolating piping from the structure.
16290
16291
Cautions
16292
•
Avoid creating stagnant zones with little airflow.
16293
•
Continuous air paths can reduce acoustic privacy.
16294
•
Although changing airflow direction can lead to more even distribution, the air tends to slow
16295
down and lose some of its cooling effect.
16296
16297
Natural ventilation and mixed-mode
16298
The above paragraphs give an overview of the factors that need to be considered when assessing
16299
whether natural ventilation is appropriate to the building under consideration. It should be recognized
16300
that not all parts of a building have to be treated in exactly the same way. Different strategies may be
16301
applied to different parts of a building, or at different times. This is the so-called 'mixed mode' approach,
16302
which is discussed in greater detail in CIBSE AM13 (22). The various approaches to mixed-mode are as
16303
follows. These approaches are not mutually exclusive, in other words, several of them can be combined
16304
in a single building.
16305
1. Contingency mixed-mode: where flexibility of space is required, then it is important to 'design-
16306
in' the potential to upgrade the services so that additional cooling can be installed to meet tenant
16307
requirements or the changing climate. This provision will include space allowances for
16308
additional distribution systems incorporated into floor and/or ceiling voids. The cost of the
16309
additional flexibility will need to be set against the savings in initial and operating costs accruing
16310
from the avoidance of unnecessary air conditioning.
16311
16312
2. Zoned mixed-mode: recognizes that different parts of any building will have different uses. Air
16313
conditioning is provided only in certain parts of the building that have the greater need. For
16314
instance in areas of lower heat gain, or those that are occupied for short periods, heating and
16315
natural ventilation only could be provided. Such an approach relies on the requirements of the
16316
individual spaces being reasonably constant over the life of the building. Such an approach can
16317
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 519
Return to TOP
also create tensions, if one group of occupants feels that another group has been provided with
16318
what they believe is a better working environment.
16319
16320
3. Changeover mixed-mode: recognizes that the cooling requirements of any space will vary from
16321
season to season. An example of changeover mixed-mode would be to use mechanical
16322
ventilation in extreme weather conditions (hot and cold), but rely on natural ventilation in milder
16323
weather. This reduces the problem of cold draughts in winter, and allows the use of mechanical
16324
night ventilation for precooling in hot summer periods.
16325
16326
4. Concurrent mixed-mode: provides mechanical ventilation and natural conditioning
16327
simultaneously. The mechanical system is designed to provide the fresh air requirement, with
16328
additional ventilation by opening windows to provide summer cooling. The mechanical system
16329
can also provide night ventilation with the windows closed, which avoids the associated security
16330
problem. If mechanical cooling is also provided, care should be taken to avoid excess ventilation
16331
in very hot weather.
16332
16333
Design Process and Strategies
16334
The following sections outline key concepts that need to be addressed both before and during the design
16335
process. For thorough design guidelines, please refer to Further Resources section.
16336

16343
Design Tools and Calculations
16344
In a traditional HVAC design, compliance with thermal comfort criteria can be demonstrated through
16345
peak load calculations, unit capacities and temperature set points. Thermal comfort compliance in a
16346
natural ventilation and cooling design can be more difficult to prove, as indoor temperatures are a
16347
function of frequently changing factors such as wind speed and direction, cloud cover, outdoor
16348
temperature, and occupancy levels. Some comfort criteria, such as ASHRAE 55-2004 are based on peak
16349
indoor operative temperatures, while others, such as the LEED-2.1 Bounding Comfort Parameters refer
16350
to the number of annual hours allowed at different temperature thresholds.
16351
16352
16353
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 521
Return to TOP
Design Calculations
16354
There are two main types of passive natural ventilation to aid in airflow: cross ventilation and stack
16355
ventilation. Cross ventilation techniques use high and low pressure zones created by wind to draw fresh
16356
air through a building. Stack ventilation uses high and low pressure zones created by rising heat, causing
16357
convection currents. This technique can be implemented by designing an exit vent that draws warm air
16358
out of the top of the building and a vent near the lower levels of the building to allow cool air to enter.
16359
16360
16361
16362
Figure 8.4-J [Note: Diagrams to be redrawn]
16363
16364
For basic natural ventilation design purposes, a class of tools known as 'explicit envelope flow models'
16365
are the most appropriate. They allow basic dimensioning of the system components. It then explains
16366
how other, more sophisticated tools (such as implicit envelope flow models, combined thermal and
16367
ventilation models, computational fluid dynamics and physical scale models) can be used to check the
16368
performance of the sized system under a variety of operating modes.
16369
16370
Because implicit envelope flow models are the most useful tool to the designer, this aspect is developed
16371
in depth in the CIBSE AM10; 2005 Natural Ventilation in non- domestic buildings document, showing
16372
how the basic textbook equations can be manipulated to provide solutions to most design problems.
16373
16374
The best way to demonstrate compliance with the design comfort criteria is to conduct thermal modeling
16375
for those portions of a building that are naturally ventilated. This requires software capable of
16376
performing dynamic hourly load, air flow and temperature calculations, including the effects of operable
16377
windows, and natural air flow, with the ability to use location appropriate weather files.
16378
16379
Thermal modeling is useful for much more than simply demonstrating compliance. It can be used to test
16380
different options, or iterated to optimize the design. For example, the thermal modeling software can be
16381
used to find the optimal quantity, size and location of operable windows, or evaluate the effect of
16382
changing building materials.
16383
16384
(Source: Stantec design manual and CIBSE Application Manual AM10 Fig 2.1.5)
16385
16386
Cost Benefit Analysis
16387
The following is an example from an actual project of capital and annual energy costs for natural
16388
ventilation designs as compared to traditional systems. Dollar values in the examples should be
16389
considered an indicator of relative costs as not all of the data is current.
16390
16391
Capital Costs
16392
Table XXX, following, shows a sample capital cost comparison (in $/ft2) of a passive natural ventilation
16393
cooling system vs. a hybrid system vs. a traditional ducted VAV system. It is taken from a report for a
16394
classroom design of a university building in the Puget Sound region and was created by Stantec in May
16395
2006.
16396
16397
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 522
Return to TOP
The comparison illustrates some of the cost trade-offs involved with natural ventilation and cooling
16398
design. Notably, in this project, the architectural costs are higher but the mechanical costs are lower. In
16399
this case the hybrid system not only includes mechanical cooling, but also fully automated operable
16400
windows controlled by the DDC system. This is why the architectural elements in the hybrid system are
16401
more expensive than in the passive system.
16402
16403
One opportunity to further reduce costs of naturally ventilated high performance buildings that is not
16404
represented in Table 3 are local utility, municipal, state, federal and other green incentive programs.
16405
Many programs exist that can reduce the initial investment cost of energy conserving features in
16406
buildings, including natural ventilation.
16407
16408

16409
(including automated window control) vs. traditional systems (in $/ft2)

16411
Annual & Life-Cycle Costs
16412
For the example in Table 3, overall construction costs on a square foot basis are lowest for the passive
16413
natural ventilation and cooling design coupled with perimeter baseboard heating. This system is
16414
expected to have the lowest annual energy costs, even though heating costs may be slightly higher than
16415
the hybrid natural ventilation case which has the more efficient radiant floor heating system. Conversely,
16416
energy costs are highest for the conventional system, primarily because of the fan energy.
16417
16418
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 523
Return to TOP
Because of its lower capital and energy costs the passive natural ventilation design has easily the lowest
16419
life cycle cost of the three options. For those spaces where a passive system can be effectively
16420
implemented it therefore becomes the most economic choice.
16421
16422
For spaces that may need some hybrid mechanical cooling on the warmest days the choice between a
16423
conventional ducted overhead VAV system (initial cost = $34.74/ft2) and the hybrid natural ventilation
16424
system (initial cost = $40.07/ft2) becomes one of choosing between lowest first cost and lowest
16425
estimated life cycle cost.
16426
16427
The life cycle cost calculation may be applied as follows. For a classroom, the baseline annual energy
16428
use intensity for spaces with a traditional ducted overhead VAV design could be about 50 kBtu/ft2.
16429
Using current utility rates for natural gas and electricity in the Puget Sound region, this comes out to
16430
$1.06/ft2 annually.
16431
16432
Provided the same classroom is designed instead to maximize natural ventilation cooling benefits, those
16433
spaces served by the hybrid natural ventilation system have typically been found to have energy cost
16434
savings of at least 25%. This equates to annual energy cost savings relative to the traditional design of
16435
approximately $0.32/ft2. Assuming fuel escalation averages 10% annually and a discount rate of 6%,
16436
the internal rate of return (IRR) on the additional capital cost investment for the hybrid natural
16437
ventilation system is 10.9% and positive cash flow occurs after about 10 years.
16438
16439
Glossary
16440
Comfort criteria: Agreed upon interior design conditions for project, such as maximum temperature or
16441
annual hours above temperature thresholds
16442
16443
Cross ventilation: Taking advantage of natural breezes to draw air across a space, requires operable
16444
windows on opposing facades of the building and unobstructed paths for air flow
16445
16446
Daylighting: The use of natural sunlight to provide light to a space rather than electric light, typically
16447
most effective when light is diffuse rather than direct-beam sunlight
16448
16449
Hybrid system: In this context refers to a natural ventilation system with supplementary mechanical
16450
cooling, usually used only to meet peak loads
16451
16452
Night flush: Opening up windows and dampers at night to purge hot air from building and allow fresh
16453
air to pre-cool building structure for next day, most effective when used in combination with thermal
16454
mass
16455
16456
Natural ventilation: Strictly, this only refers to providing fresh air to a building’s occupants without the
16457
use of mechanical means (i.e. fans), often it is incorrectly used to imply natural cooling
16458
16459
Natural cooling: Keeping a space from overheating by introducing cooler outside air without the use of
16460
mechanical means (i.e. fans, chillers, etc.), often requires preventing air from entering spaces if outside
16461
temperature exceeds inside temperature, also known as natural conditioning or passive cooling
16462
16463
Operable windows: Refers to glazing or dampers that can be opened to allow outside air to enter a space
16464
16465
Operative temperature: The temperature that an occupant “experiences” in a space, including the
16466
effects of air temperature, air movement, and radiant temperature
16467
16468
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 524
Return to TOP
Radiant heating/cooling: A cool surface will radiantly “pull” heat away from an occupant thereby
16469
cooling them, a hot surface will give up heat to a person radiantly, this allows for heating and cooling
16470
without mechanically conditioning air
16471
16472
Stack ventilation: Using the natural buoyancy of hot air to create vertical air movement, typically
16473
involves high level venting through chimneys, vent shafts, or atriums
16474
16475
Thermal mass: Dense building materials such as concrete, brickwork, or stone that moderates space
16476
temperatures by absorbing heat during hot periods and releasing it during cool periods
16477
16478

16508
Strategy 8.5 – Use the ASHRAE Standard 62.1 IAQ Procedure where Appropriate
16509
16510
16511
History of the Indoor Air Quality Procedure (IAQP)
16512
This methodology was first introduced in the original ASHRAE Standard 62 in 1973 and again in 62-
16513
1981 as a portion of the recirculation discussion. These early versions allowed the treatment of
16514
recirculated conditioned air using enhanced FAC to reduce the outdoor ventilation requirement to as low
16515
as 5 CFM. Since the '81 version of Standard 62 was not ANSI approved, the procedure was not widely
16516
applied other than in the several local jurisdictions accepting that version, such as Atlanta and Seattle.
16517
The IAQP was reiterated and formalized in Standard 62-89 as an alternate path of compliance to attain
16518
acceptable indoor air quality. However, the IAQP has not been widely used by designers because of its
16519
complexity and the additional engineering rigor required as compared to the Ventilation Rate Procedure
16520
(VRP), such as calculations, analysis, and/or testing, along with the related liability. The occasional
16521
usage of the procedure was predominately focused in the South because of the high outdoor humidity
16522
and heat loads. Further, it has been most widely applied in buildings having high density and wide
16523
diversity of occupancy, such as arenas, schools, auditoriums, theaters, convention centers, and hotels. In
16524
these spaces, the economic advantages provide compelling returns on the additional design and
16525
equipment investment,.
16526
16527
The current 2007 version of the Standard 62 VRP alternative requires a more complex engineering
16528
calculation for minimum ventilation values providing for both occupancy sources and building derived
16529
sources. It also requires more design rigor when dealing with part load conditions and VAV systems. It
16530
has established a minimum filtration efficiency of MERV 6 (Link MERV discussion Appropriate Filter
16531
Efficiency Selection). It requires scrutiny of the outdoor air pollutant levels and application of enhanced
16532
FAC of outdoor air when the site is in a non-attainment area, with special focus on "ozone" as the CoC.
16533
It includes chapters on Start-up and Maintenance, which implies additional engineering rigor in those
16534
areas that concern system access, cleanability, and operating efficiency. These additional levels of
16535
engineering rigor combined with spiraling energy costs have created a much more attractive economic
16536
platform for considering the application of the IAQP. (See ASHRAE Standard 62-2007 User's Manual
16537
for more information.) The FAC manufacturing industry has also put in place improved efficiency
16538
testing standards; developed new fibers and media designed specific for air filtration applications; and
16539
have developed a wide range of filter sizes, styles, and efficiencies of FAC. This broad selection base
16540
provides designers multiple options to incrementally maximize life cycle, value, and performance while
16541
minimizing pressure drop and energy demand.
16542
16543
When to Use the IAQP
16544
The IAQ Procedure is easiest and most effectively applied under one or more of the following
16545
conditions. The overwhelming driver of the IAQP is economic because of the compelling potential
16546
savings in large high occupancy projects. However, several of the denoted conditions impose additional
16547
engineering rigor upon the design team when employing the simpler Ventilation Rate Procedure (VRP).
16548
Thus, in several conditions marked with "\*", this additional engineering rigor is already expended to
16549
meet the current level of compliance to the VPR of Standard 62.1-2007. This minimizes the additional
16550
requirements necessary to evaluate and apply the IAQP alternative.
16551
16552
16553
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 526
Return to TOP
16554
Table 8.5-C Optimal Conditions for IAQP Deployment
16555
Condition
Outcome
\*Outdoor air is non-attainment for
NAAQS or otherwise polluted
Reduces burden of external sources of particulate and gaseous
pollutants
\*Components selected for low out-
gassing source contribution
Allows outdoor reduction through use of mass balance equations
to document comparability to dilution

Once identified, CoC concentrations can be controlled, evaluated,
and verified through mass balance equations and/or testing

high requiring excessive ventilation

conjunction with normal VRP rates, eliminating peak excess
ventilation

by code, standard, or authority

and potential targets of acceptability

limitations

equipment relying on IAQP for supplemental contaminant control
without additional HVAC capacity
Involvement of motivated and
knowledgeable owner
Involves owner who appreciates and gains from the economic
advantages of the IAQP and is committed to maintenance of FAC
16556
Applying the IAQP
16557
Figure X outlines the criterion for compliance to the IAQP as cited from Standard 62.1. The following
16558
discussion provides further information and guidance on the alternative paths.
16559
16560
Mass Balance equations are provided by Standard 62.1 in Appendix D and are reproduced herein for the
16561
convenience of the reader.
16562
16563
16564
16565
16566
[Note: Insert Table D-1 from Standard 62 here and reproduce the code Quantities and Subscripts as
16567
part of the illustration]
16568
16569
16570
16571
Figure 8.5-C Required Outdoor Air or Space Contaminant
16572
Concentration with Recirculation and Filtration
16573
16574
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 527
Return to TOP
16575
Figure 8.5-C provides eight different permutations of system arrangements to enable the determination
16576
of steady state conditions applicable to each. These calculations provide the outdoor airflow and
16577
resulting space contaminant concentration comparing the effect of dilution with filtration removal or
16578
extraction efficiency. Other tools are also available to the designer to provide similar data, for example,
16579
contaminant modeling programs, such as "CONTAM".
16580
16581
Successful Like-kind Buildings provide local authorities assurance of the successful history of the
16582
application of the IAQP under similar conditions. Although there are a number of buildings that have
16583
successfully employed the IAQP, they represent a small percentage of the building stock. Further, the
16584
successful applications tend to follow the hot and humid zone and to fall into the class of buildings
16585
experiencing high occupancy and/or wide diversity of occupancy density and/or activity. Yet, the energy
16586
savings and related air quality improvements from the successful usage of the IAQP provide powerful
16587
incentives and benefits as illustrated in the following case study. Because of the recurring urgency of
16588
energy prices, the stories of these buildings are emerging along with the reports of their energy savings
16589
(Burroughs1, Stanley2). Their influence provides high value in supporting the usage of the IAQP and
16590
warrants the effort to seek out the justification of this method.
16591
16592
Case Study #1
16593
16594
[Note: Photo to be added]
16595
16596
Figure 8.5-D This high-rise hotel was built in a major Southeastern city, using an atrium-style tower.
16597
The structure is approximately 40 years old and has been subjected to increasing heat loads due to a
16598
combination of aging mechanical equipment and the additional load of solar reflections from more
16599
recently constructed high rise office buildings now surrounding the glass tower of the hotel. The hotel
16600
operating personnel experienced increasing difficulty in sustaining comfort and humidity conditions in
16601
the upper strata of the tower atrium. As an energy conservation measure and to enhance cooling
16602
capacity, four recirculation units were installed on each quadrant of the hotel roof. The four recirculation
16603
air handlers collect adjacent building room exhaust from the hotel tower rooms and treat the air with
16604
high efficiency solid-bed gas phase filtration equipment. The filter systems are also equipped with
16605
MERV 6 filters installed upstream and downstream of the gas filter cartridges. Two of the 20,000 cfm
16606
air handlers (south and west units) are also equipped with cooling coils to augment the cooling capacity
16607
of the building. All of the units free blow into the top of the tower atrium to replace a total of 80,000 cfm
16608
of previously required outdoor make-up air. The calculated cost savings to replace this amount of make
16609
up air totals $111,909 per annum, less the annual FAC maintenance and operating cost of $6,808,
16610
yielding a net saving of $105,101 per year.
16611
16612
16613
Contaminant Air Monitoring or Testing is required during the evaluation period when the CoC are not
16614
easily determined, or even if known, their concentration or source strength or generation rate is not
16615
known. Other than this initial usage of air testing, monitoring or air testing may also be used to verify
16616
the delivered concentration after treatment with FAC. This verifies that the product of the IAQP is equal
16617
to or better than the expectation of the VRP. The process of air testing can be costly because it requires
16618
trained operating personnel and, oftimes, specialized monitoring equipment. Thus, care should be taken
16619
to be precise and focused in the selection of critical CoC for testing and evaluation to minimize the
16620
testing costs. This alternate also contains a requirement for the setting and verification of acceptability
16621
limits for the perceived indoor air quality. For example, if acceptance level is set at 80%, then an
16622
untrained panel of occupants is to be used to verify that more than 80% of the group renders judgement
16623
affirming the acceptability of the indoor air quality after no more than a 15 second exposure to the
16624
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 528
Return to TOP
subject space. Either the air testing or the perceived air quality evaluation can be performed as an
16625
alternate method of compliance.
16626
16627
16628
16629
Figure 8.5-D
16630
16631
A South Western Texas school district applied the IAQP to a high school that was experiencing high
16632
humidity, IAQ complaints, and high energy costs. After selection of contaminants of concern and related
16633
air testing, selection of concentration limits, setting of perceived IAQ acceptance level, the IAQP was
16634
implemented. The system employed enhanced particulate air filters (MERV 8) and medium efficiency
16635
gas phase filtration to reduce the outdoor air component by 9760 CFM. Energy savings were
16636
documented at 68,897 kWh; 4,091 therms; and $11,900 in cost, including 72 tons of CO2 emissions.
16637
Concurrently, the school reported elimination of IAQ complaints from teachers and a reduction of
16638
student usage of asthma inhalers by 50%.
16639
16640
Combination with VRP enables the usage of the IAQP in conjunction with the more routine Ventilation
16641
Rate Procedure. For example, if the change of usage of a space converts a low-density office space into
16642
a high density conference room, the IAQP can be applied to this sub-zone to provide acceptable indoor
16643
air quality in that sub-zone without increasing the outdoor air quantity to the entire space. When specific
16644
CoC would require additional outdoor air to create sufficiency dilution, likewise the IAQP can be
16645
employed through the addition of enhanced filtration to control that CoC in conjunction with the normal
16646
VRP rate. Other authoritative influences, such as LEED, may encourage the general premise of up to
16647
30% outdoor air additional to the normal VRP rate to meet their performance criterion. The IAQP can
16648
provide this additional increment through FAC rather than additional outdoor air. This can be
16649
particularly crucial if the outdoor air is hot and humid or is of marginal quality due to pollution.
16650
16651
Process for Applying the IAQP
16652
The discussion in Chapter II provides the verbal description of the steps of the design process. The
16653
following Figure X is a visual description of the process taken from the User's Manual of Standard 62.1-
16654
2007, which illustrates the design process. (Chart to come from ASHRAE 62.1 UM page 6-47)
16655
16656
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 529
Return to TOP
IAQ Procedure Design Flow Chart
16657
16658
16659
16660
16661
16662
16663
(to be added)
16664
16665
16666
16667
16668
16669
16670
16671
16672
16673
16674
16675
Figure 8.5-E IAQ Procedure Design Flow Chart
16676
16677
Selection of CoC
16678
The selection of the appropriate Contaminants of Concern (CoC) has been the primary area of
16679
discomfort to designers in the consideration of the IAQP and, thus, deserves further elaboration. In new
16680
construction projects, designers have traditionally been wary of the IAQP because of potential liability
16681
as well as the cost of additional engineering effort. This fear regarding risk is well-founded when the
16682
base building design is a generic and/or speculative office building without full knowledge of the
16683
environmental conditions or the intended occupant density or activity. This does not allow the design
16684
team sufficient knowledge to develop the CoC or definitive target acceptability criterion, since the
16685
occupant(s) are unknown. These open questions expose the designer to greater risk because of vague
16686
information and too many assumptions. When the project is a building renovation with an existing and
16687
known owner, having an existing operating history, located in an established site setting with
16688
environmental history data, and having an established occupancy quantity and activity, it is more
16689
feasible to consider and apply the IAQP. In many instances, the IAQP is far more cost effective than the
16690
VRP to design, construct, and operate. As an example, the building may have been built to prior
16691
ventilation standards and to bring the facility to current code would require substantially increased
16692
HVAC capacity. If replacement of this equipment is not required as part of the renovation, then
16693
compliance to current improved IAQ acceptability may be attained using the IAQP without upgrading or
16694
replacing the existing operative equipment. The only equipment modification that is required is to
16695
accommodate for the addition of enhanced particulate and/or gas phase filtration.
16696
16697
When there is direct contact with the owner/occupant of a project, this facilitates the designation and
16698
selection of the CoC, their concentration strengths, and target acceptability targets for both the specific
16699
CoC targets as well as the perceived air quality. The following chart illustrates the technique of CoC
16700
selection that was performed during the project described in the above school case study. They
16701
designated as potential contaminant sources--the building, the occupants, and the outside air--and then
16702
selected specific contaminants to profile and replicate these sources. Table X1 illustrates the selected
16703
contaminants and Table X2 provides the related Standards and Guidelines from which the target
16704
attainment data were derived.
16705
16706

from Stanley paper)

16731
Documentation
16732
The thorough documentation of the IAQP process is critical to assure acceptance by local authorities.
16733
This is also is critical to assist in the proper commissioning and operational training to properly maintain
16734
the system(s). Each step of the process should be documented and provided to the the owner. The
16735
documentation should consist of not less than the following:
16736
• The rationale for selecting the IAQP and the compliance method;
16737
• The contaminants of concern (CoC)used in the process;
16738
• The sources and sources strengths of the CoC both in the outdoor and and inside;
16739
• The target concentration limits and their related source of cognizant authority;
16740
• An iteration and details about successful similar buildings along with the basis for concluding
16741
that the design approach was successful and that the they are similar in design to the proposed
16742
usage; and
16743
• If contaminat testing and monitoring and occupant satisfaction evalution were employed to
16744
demonstrate compliance, then include the evaluation details along with results.
16745
16746
16747
16748
16749
16750

16752
