Appendix B Environmental Monitoring
16757
16758
16759
Note to reviewers: This is intended to provide content to solicit reactions from the PC members
16760
and is only a first pass at developing the content for this section.
16761
16762
16763
Key points regarding environmental monitoring of indoor air quality:
16764
16765
1. Never measure anything unless you know what you are going to do with the results. The purpose of
16766
environmental monitoring should be clearly established and the target pollutants identified in order
16767
to design an effective monitoring program.
16768
16769
2. Short term sampling represents the conditions only at the place and time the sample is collected and
16770
cannot be assumed to represent the building more generally. Results obtained from air sampling are
16771
like snapshots, representing only what exists at the time it is taken.
16772
16773
3. Airborne concentrations of indoor source pollutants are strongly dependent on concurrent outdoor air
16774
ventilation rates. Only by simultaneously measuring ventilation and calculating source strengths can
16775
information be obtained that can be compared with samples collected at other time points.
16776
16777
“In matters related to indoor environmental quality, research is usefully guided by this
16778
quote, commonly attributed to Albert Einstein: "make everything as simple as possible,
16779
but not simpler" (http://www.onedegree.ca/2005/04/08/making-einstein-simple). A.
16780
Bejan recommends scale analysis as the means to "obtaining the most information per
16781
unit of intellectual effort" (Convection Heat Transfer, Wiley, New York, 1984, p. 18). RP
16782
Schwarzenbach et al. remind us that, "a model is an imitation of reality which stresses
16783
those aspects that are assumed to be important and omits all properties considered to be
16784
nonessential" (Environmental Organic Chemistry, Wiley, New York, 1993, p. 547). Both
16785
doing the right research and doing the research right require careful attention to the
16786
importance of the information sought and the efficiency of the means of acquiring it.”
16787
16788
- William W Nazaroff, 2006, personal communication (by permission)
16789
16790
Indoor air is a complex mixture
16791
The mixtures of chemicals that are commonly present in indoor air often contain scores or even
16792
hundreds of compounds at widely varying concentrations from parts per trillion (1 in a trillion
16793
molecules) to parts per thousands. The two main constituents of air at sea level up to a couple miles
16794
above sea level are nitrogen (about 78%) and oxygen (about 21%). More precisely, it contains roughly
16795
(by molar content/volume) 78.08% nitrogen, 20.95% oxygen, 0.93% argon, 0.038% carbon dioxide,
16796
trace amounts of other gases, and a variable amount (around 1%) of water vapor.
16797
16798
Categories of indoor air pollutants
16799
Monitoring IAQ generally refers to measurements of the constituents of air – chemicals, particles, and
16800
microbial components.
16801
16802
Each of these can be broken down into more detailed categories as shown in Table EM-1.
16803
16804
16805
16806
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 533
Return to TOP
Table EM-1 Broad Categories of Indoor Air Pollutants
16807
Constituent
Detailed Categories
Chemicals \*
Organic
Volatile
Semi-Volatile
Inorganic
Particles – defined by
size
Total Suspended Particles (TSP) < 10 µm mass median
aerodynamic diameter
Respirable Suspended Particles (RSP)
Fine Particles < 2.5 µm mass median aerodynamic diameter
Biological Aerosols
(Bio Aerosols)\*
Fungi, Mold
Bacteria
Viruses
Pollen
\* Chemicals may be in the solid (condensed) phase or the gaseous phase. Gases can be molecules in the air or on the
16808
surfaces including the surfaces of airborne or settled dust and other particles.
16809
\* Bioaerosols may be viable or non-viable
16810
16811
Most indoor air pollutants are present at very low concentrations
16812
Chemicals commonly considered as important indoor air pollutants like formaldehyde and carbon
16813
monoxide are not usually found at concentrations greater than 1 or 2 parts per million (0.0001 or
16814
0.0002%). Most common volatile organic chemicals (VOCs) found indoors are at levels less than 10 ppb
16815
or 0.000001%. Generally most compounds found in most buildings are usually reported at
16816
concentrations less than 1 ppb. Typically, there may be between one and five or ten compounds at
16817
concentrations between 1 and 10 ppb. It is unusual to find concentrations of most VOCs above 10 ppb in
16818
most buildings. The exceptions are when building materials or furnishings are new or when occupant
16819
activities or equipment are strong emitters. 66
16820
16821
Health implications of indoor air quality
16822
Even though most compounds occur at relatively low concentrations, some indoor air pollutants can be
16823
short or long-term health hazards and cannot be totally ignored. Approximately ¼ of the highest
16824
concentrations fo VOCs measured in offices during the period 1990 – 2001 were measured at
16825
concentrations that approached 1/10th of the lowest guideline value for odor, irritation, or acute
16826
toxicity.67 Additionally, a few compounds found indoors are carcinogens, the most notorious of which is
16827
formaldehyde.
16828
16829
Total human exposure to pollutants is strongly affected by indoor air
16830
It is often thought that human exposure to pollutants is dominated by what we eat and drink. However,
16831
for many if not most pollutants, this is a myth. In spite of the low concentrations by mass or molar
16832
fraction of air, the total exposure to humans can be significant through inhalation, skin absorption, or
16833
inadvertent ingestion of indoor air pollutants. Since the average adult inhales about 20 cubic meters of
16834
air per day, the total mass of air consumed is far more than the total mass of water and food taken
16835
66 Hodgson, A.T.; Levin, H. Volatile organic compounds in indoor air: A review of concentrations measured in North
America since 1990. LBNL-51715. http://eetd.lbl.gov/IED/pdf/LBNL-51715.pdf
67 Hodgson, A.T.; Levin, H.. Classification of measured indoor volatile organic compounds based on noncancer
health and comfort considerations.. LBNL-53308. http://eetd.lbl.gov/IED/pdf/LBNL-53308.pdf.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 534
Return to TOP
intentionally. So contamination of air that is inhaled can be an important source of significant exposure
16836
to environmental pollutants. For most pollutants, it is a more important pathway of exposure than eating
16837
and drinking. An adult typically inhales between 10 and 20 cubic meters of air per day, a volume equal
16838
to the air in a small bedroom, with a mass equal to 12 to 24 kg or 27 to 54 lbs per day.
16839
16840
Use environmental monitoring sparingly
16841
Many experts agree that investigating indoor air quality problems should involve environmental
16842
monitoring only as the third stage of the process. Interviews and a walkthrough and establishment of the
16843
history of the problem(s) and the purpose of the investigation are the first step. Then, if necessary,
16844
review and evaluation of building plans and specifications, operational logs, and establishment of initial
16845
hypotheses regarding the potential problem causes and pathways. Finally, environmental monitoring
16846
including ventilation system performance and outdoor air delivery, thermal conditions, and a set of
16847
target pollutants may be warranted.
16848
16849
Samples representative of occupant exposures are difficult to obtain
16850
Indoor air sampling presents challenge in terms of obtaining samples that reflect or represent occupant
16851
exposures because sample collection too close to the occupant will be influenced by the occupant’s
16852
activities and metabolic products while sample collection too far from the occupant will not capture the
16853
occupant’s actual pollutant exposures since these are generally dominated by the occupant’s activities.
16854
See Figures EM-2 and EM-3 at the end of this draft.
16855
16856
16857
“The influence of personal activity sources on exposure to indoor contaminants is
16858
defined and demonstrated using data from occupational and residential studies. The
16859
ratios of measurements from personal exposure monitoring to those made by micro-
16860
environmental exposure monitors are summarized to be typically 3 to 10 for
16861
occupational settings and 1.2 to 3.3 for residential settings. The ratios are shown to
16862
be log-normally distributed, and dependant primarily on the proximity of the source
16863
to the receptors. Current models are reviewed for possible application to the
16864
prediction of indoor concentration gradients and future model development and
16865
validation studies are suggested.
16866
Source: Charles E. Rodes, Richard M. Kamens, and Russell W. Wiener, 1991. The
16867
Significance and Characteristics of the Personal Activity Cloud on Exposure
16868
Assessment Measurements for Indoor Contaminants. Indoor Air. 2.123-145.
16869
16870
Time Period of Monitoring
16871
Long term sampling – hours to days – with results given in Time weighted averages (TWAs) give a
16872
time-integrated exposure over the total duration of the monitoring and are useful for assessing
16873
cumulative exposure. They fail to capture variations in concentrations or source strengths during the
16874
time covered.
16875
16876
Short-term samples such as so-called “grab samples” collect air over a very short period of time and can
16877
capture a peak exposure if their timing is coincident with the peak concentrations. However, since
16878
source strengths and ventilation vary over time and space, a grab sample can distort average, long-term
16879
exposure in either direction.
16880
Coming in and getting a grab sample of the air is like telling the energy guy to go look at the electrical
16881
meter for 10 seconds to understand building energy performance.
16882
16883
A compromise has been proposed that takes short samples at regular intervals. This combines the
16884
advantages and the disadvantages of both the long-term sampling and the grab sampling.
16885
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 535
Return to TOP
16886
Building air quality varies greatly
16887
Building air quality can vary greatly from one space to another and from one time point to another both
16888
in concentration and in the pollutants present. These variations can be as large as 1,000- or even 10,000-
16889
fold differences in concentrations. The substances that will be found can vary greatly within and
16890
between buildings. There are substances that will be found only rarely in any building and others that
16891
will be found commonly but not in all buildings. There are also a few substances that will be found in
16892
almost all modern buildings. These facts make generalizations about sampling and analysis difficult and
16893
dangerous. This has led to warnings against concluding that any building is free of hazards based on
16894
environmental monitoring and to term the chemicals that are present but not detected as “stealth”
16895
pollutants.68 69
16896
16897
Humans can detect chemicals that cannot be detected by environmental monitoring
16898
There are pollutants and odors that can be detected by humans that will not be easily detected or even
16899
detected at all by air sampling. Nontheless, human sensitivity to chemical pollutants varies greatly from
16900
individual to individual and chemical to chemical.
16901
16902
Sampling methods determine and limit what can be measured
16903
Sampling and analysis can only find what the method is capable of detecting. Most methods are
16904
relatively limited and have interferences and biases that must be considered when interpreting results.
16905
16906
Some sampling methods that are more general than specific in terms of what they measure can be useful
16907
for comparisons where there is a need to know if anything has changed. Examples are so-called “total
16908
volatile organic compounds” (or TVOCs) and total colony forming units (CFUs) which are each the sum
16909
of those compounds or organisms that the method used is capable of detecting. However, no methods
16910
are capable of collecting all TVOCs or CFUs. Furthermore, the lack of information about the species of
16911
organic compounds or micro-organisms prevents the results from being useful for understanding
16912
potential health or comfort implications of exposure. Only by identification and quantification of
16913
specific compounds or organisms can there be any health or comfort assessment of the monitoring
16914
results.
16915
16916
A similar principle applies to particle sampling which is usually reported in mass per unit volume or
16917
number per unit volume. But particle sizes found indoors vary by several orders of magnitude and
16918
behave very differently from each other depending on size. Furthermore, the chemical and/or biological
16919
make-up of particles can be highly variable, even within a single environment.
16920
16921
All organic chemical sampling is dependent on the methods used, and the results obtained with different
16922
methods should not be compared without abundant caution.
16923
16924
Even the very best sampling technology is incapable of detecting some organic compounds for which
16925
there is evidence of very short-term existence or for which there is only indirect evidence that they exist
16926
at all.
16927
16928
Short term sampling can capture a peak exposure which may be very much higher than the average
16929
exposure which is better captured by longer sampling collection periods. Effects may be cause by a very
16930
68 Weschler, Charles J., 2001. Reactions Among Indoor Pollutants: What’s New. Paper # 291 Proceedings of the Annual
Meeting of the Air and Waste Management Association, Orlando, FL June, 2001
69 Charles J. Weschler, J. R. Wells, Dustin Poppendieck, Heidi Hubbard, and Terri A. Pearce, 2005. Indoor Chemistry and
Health - A Workshop: Summary. doi:10.1289/ehp.8271 (available at http://dx.doi.org/) Online 3 November 2005
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 536
Return to TOP
high short term (peak) exposure that would not be recognized by a longer term (average) exposure
16931
measurement.
16932
16933
Knowing what to do with results
16934
Monitoring should never be done unless there is knowledge about what will be done with the results.
16935
This presents challenges for indoor environmental monitoring because of the lack of sufficient
16936
information about most indoor pollutants. There is an important deficiency in terms of standards and
16937
guidelines regarding safe or acceptable concentrations of indoor air pollutants. Due to the absence of
16938
regulation, there has been insufficient research into the health effects of most common indoor pollutants
16939
to establish the concentrations at which various health effects can be expected or avoided. Some of the
16940
available guidelines and standards are documented in Appendix B of ASHRAE Standard 62.1-2007.
16941
16942
An alternative to comparing concentrations to health and safety regulations, guidelines or standards is to
16943
compare them to what is commonly found in similar buildings. A source for data useful for such
16944
comparisons is Hodgson and Levin, 2003, LBNL Report 51715, downloadable from the LBL.gov
16945
website. Further guidance on exposure limits can be found in a companion paper by the same authors,
16946
LBNL Report 53308, also available from the same web site.
16947
16948
Pollutants with outdoor sources
16949
Some indoor pollutants have primarily or only outdoor sources, some have only or predominantly indoor
16950
sources, and most have both indoor and outdoor sources. For those that have both indoor and outdoor
16951
sources, simultaneous measurements must be made in both locations in order to determine the portion
16952
that is of indoor origin since ventilation rates and changes in outdoor source strengths will change the
16953
contribution of outdoor sources to the indoor concentrations.
16954
16955
Carbon dioxide – the most measured gas in indoor air
16956
Carbon dioxide is not a pollutant per se when measured at typical indoor concentrations. It can be used
16957
as a surrogate for the adequacy of ventilation in relation to human occupancy when compared with
16958
outdoor concentrations measured simultaneously. Carbon dioxide builds up over time after occupants
16959
enter a building and is most reliable as an indicator when measured after long periods (hours) of stable
16960
occupancy and ventilation system operation. When measured shortly after occupants enter, it will
16961
overestimate outdoor air ventilation when compared with outdoor concentrations. A difference of
16962
approximately 650 ppm between the stable indoor concentration and the outdoor concentration
16963
represents a ventilation rate of approximately 15 cfm/person. Consult papers by Mudarri and by Persily
16964
for more details on interpretation of CO2 measurements. 70 71
16965
16966
Evaluation of Mold Species Concentrations
16967
In the case of mold species that have both indoor and outdoor sources, the indoor concentrations are
16968
generally compared to the outdoor concentrations to determine whether conditions in the building are
16969
contributing to an increase in the species’ concentrations indoors. It is also commonly recommended
16970
practice to compare the rank order of species found outdoor and indoors as a way of evaluating the
16971
concentrations found indoors. However, many authorities including the AIHA Field Guide recommend
16972
against air sampling for mold unless there is evidence of allergic responses among building occupants.72
16973
Generally, investigations focus on identifying sources of moisture or evidence of moisture intrusion or
16974
moisture accumulation that could support mold growth. The conditions that are necessary for an
16975
70 Persily, A. K. (1997). "Evaluating Building IAQ and Ventilation with Indoor Carbon Dioxide." ASHRAE Transactions 103
(2): 193-204.
71 ASTM (2007). D6245 Standard Guide for Using Indoor Carbon Dioxide Concentrations to Evaluate Indoor Air Quality
and Ventilation, American Society for Testing and Materials, Philadelphia, PA.
72 AIHA “Field Guide for the Determination of Biological Contaminants in Environmental Samples” American Industrial
Hygiene Association.
IAQ-DG 90% Draft - Final
Page 537
Return to TOP
increased indoor concentration are the presence of moisture and nutrients to support the growth and
16976
reproduction of the organisms indoors.
16977
16978
Temperature and Relative Humidity
16979
Relative humidity is an indicator of potential for moisture activity on surfaces. Moisture activity levels
16980
on surfaces, largely determined by air humidity and the moisture content of materials, are critical to the
16981
growth of mold.
16982
In general, moisture itself is not considered a pollutant. However, excess moisture accumulation on
16983
surfaces or in materials has been strongly associated with building occupants’ respiratory health effects
16984
even more strongly the presence of mold73 Therefore,
16985
16986
Relation of Source Strength, Ventilation, and Concentration
16987
The most fundamental of relationships relevant to assessing indoor air quality is the equation C= EF/Q
16988
Where:
16989
C = pollutant concentration
16990
EF = pollutant source strength units per hour, either per unit area or per concentrated or total
16991
sources in a space
16992
Q = ventilation rate in volume of air introduced into a space per hour or air changes per hour
16993
16994
16995
16996
Figure EM-1 Relationship of Source Strength, Ventilation, and Pollutant Concentration
16997
73 Institute of Medicine, National Academy of Sciences, 2004. Damp Indoor Spaces and Health, Washington, DC: National
Academy Press.
World Health Organization, 2007; Guidelines for Indoor Air Quality: Dampness and mould.
Fisk et al, 2007. Meta-analyses of the Associations of Respiratory health Effects with Dampness and Mold in Homes, Indoor
Air Journal, Volume 17, pp. 284-295.
Bornehag et al, 2004. Dampness in Buildings as a risk factor for health effects, EUROEXPO: a multidisciplinary review of
the literature (1998-000) on dampness and mite exposure in buildings and health effects; Indoor Air Journal, Volume 14,
pp. 243-257.
AIHA, “Field Guide for the Determination of Biological Contaminants in Environmental Samples; Second Edition.” Fairfax,
VA: American Industrial Hygiene Association.

16998

16999

17000
17001
Location of Monitoring
17002
Monitoring intended to characterize human exposure always must assess the trade-off between getting
17003
too close to a single individual to obtain results with broader applicability (e.g., average values for a
17004
space or building) and getting too far away from any individual to obtain data that reflects the
17005
importance of personal activity and sources associated with an individual.
17006
17007
Monitoring can be environmental – in the same space but apart from individuals, micro-environmental –
17008
the space immediately around an individual, or personal – in the breathing zone of an individual.
17009
17010
